topics.title: Feedback Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feedback Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Feedback Control** is a control mechanism in a process control system where the output (controlled variable) is continuously measured, compared with a desired reference value (setpoint), and the resulting **error signal** is used to drive the final control element (actuator) so that the output converges to and maintains the setpoint despite external disturbances and process variations.

In the context of PLC and Distributed Control Systems (DCS), feedback control forms the **architectural backbone** of virtually every continuous and batch control loop, from a simple temperature regulator to multi-variable cascade strategies in a refinery.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim spirit):** A feedback control system is one in which the signal from the output of the controlled process is fed back and compared with the reference input; the difference (error) is then used to actuate the controller to maintain the desired output.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Shower Mixer Tap

Imagine you are taking a shower. The water coming out of the showerhead is your **controlled variable (PV — Process Variable)**. Your comfort temperature is the **setpoint (SP)**. Your skin acts as the **sensor** — it measures the actual temperature. Your brain performs the **comparison** (PV vs SP) and generates an **error**. Your hand then acts as the **actuator**, moving the hot/cold knobs to bring the temperature back to your setpoint. This continuous loop — *measure → compare → correct* — is feedback control.

If you simply set the tap to a position and never adjust, that is **open-loop control**. If you keep adjusting based on what your skin feels, that is **closed-loop feedback control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** The "feedback" in feedback control is literally the act of *feeding back* the output information to influence the input. Without feedback, the system has no knowledge of whether it has reached its goal.

### 1.3 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Control

| Parameter | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback path | **Absent** | **Present** (output returned to input) |
| Accuracy | Low, depends on calibration | High, self-correcting |
| Disturbance rejection | **None** | **Excellent** |
| Stability | Always stable (no dynamics) | Requires careful tuning |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher (sensors + controller needed) |
| Example | Toaster, washing machine timer | Cruise control, boiler level control |

### 1.4 Two Principal Categories of Feedback Control

- **Negative Feedback** — The error signal is used to **reduce** the deviation. This is the *overwhelming* majority of industrial control systems. It drives the process toward the setpoint and ensures stability.
- **Positive Feedback** — The error signal is used to **amplify** the deviation. Rare in process control, but used in oscillators, bistable comparators (Schmitt trigger), and certain relay-based sequencing.

> [!TIP]
> In **98% of KTU questions**, "feedback control" implicitly means **negative feedback control**. Always assume negative unless explicitly stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Canonical Feedback Loop — Components

Every feedback control loop contains the following five elements:

1. **Process / Plant** — The physical system being controlled (e.g., heat exchanger, tank, motor).
2. **Sensor / Transmitter** — Measures the controlled variable and converts it to a standardized signal (e.g., 4–20 mA, 0–10 V).
3. **Controller** — Computes the error and determines the corrective action (PID, on-off, fuzzy, etc.). In a PLC/DCS, this is typically a **function block** running a control algorithm.
4. **Final Control Element (FCE) / Actuator** — Receives the controller output and manipulates the process (e.g., control valve, variable frequency drive).
5. **Comparator / Summing Junction** — Computes the error $\varepsilon(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$.

### 2.2 Mathematical Framework

The standard block diagram of a **unity negative feedback** system has the following transfer function relationship:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

Where:
- $G(s)$ = Forward path transfer function
- $H(s)$ = Feedback path transfer function
- $R(s)$ = Reference input (setpoint)
- $C(s)$ = Controlled output
- $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ = Error signal
- $B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$ = Feedback signal

For **unity feedback** ($H(s) = 1$):

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

### 2.3 Types of Controllers in Feedback Loops

#### 2.3.1 On-Off (Two-Position) Controller
The simplest and cheapest form. The controller output switches between two discrete states.

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{max} & \text{if } \varepsilon(t) > 0 \\ U_{min} & \text{if } \varepsilon(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

A **dead band** $\Delta$ is often introduced to prevent rapid chattering:
$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{max} & \text{if } \varepsilon(t) > +\Delta \\ U_{min} & \text{if } \varepsilon(t) < -\Delta \\ u(t^-) & \text{if } \vert \varepsilon(t) \vert \le \Delta \end{cases}$$

#### 2.3.2 Proportional (P) Controller
$$u(t) = K_c \cdot \varepsilon(t) + u_0$$
- $K_c$ = Proportional gain
- $u_0$ = Bias / reset value
- **Drawback:** Leaves a steady-state **offset error**.

#### 2.3.3 Proportional-Integral (PI) Controller
$$u(t) = K_c \left[ \varepsilon(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} \varepsilon(\tau) \, d\tau \right] + u_0$$
- $T_i$ = Integral time (reset time)
- **Eliminates offset** but can cause overshoot and oscillation.

#### 2.3.4 Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Controller
$$u(t) = K_c \left[ \varepsilon(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} \varepsilon(\tau) \, d\tau + T_d \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} \right] + u_0$$
- $T_d$ = Derivative time
- **Derivative term** anticipates future error based on rate of change, improving transient response and stability.

#### 2.3.5 Laplace-domain PID (most common KTU form)
$$U(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right] E(s)$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$ | $r$ = setpoint, $c$ = output | Also written as $SP - PV$ |
| 2 | Closed-loop TF (general) | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | $G$ = forward, $H$ = feedback | Standard negative feedback |
| 3 | Closed-loop TF (unity) | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$ | When $H(s) = 1$ | Simplest form |
| 4 | Error TF | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | Measures tracking ability | Should $\to 0$ for good control |
| 5 | Characteristic equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Determines stability | Roots must be in LHP |
| 6 | P-controller output | $u(t) = K_c e(t) + u_0$ | $K_c$ = gain | $u_0$ = bias |
| 7 | PI-controller output | $u(t) = K_c e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{T_i}\int e\,dt$ | $T_i$ = reset time | Removes offset |
| 8 | PID-controller output | $u(t) = K_c e + \dfrac{K_c}{T_i}\int e\,dt + K_c T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $T_d$ = derivative time | Anticipates future error |
| 9 | On-Off output | $u = U_{max}$ or $U_{min}$ | Depends on sign of $e$ | With optional dead band $\Delta$ |
| 10 | Steady-state error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ | For step input, requires system type | Type 0: finite; Type 1: zero for step |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil refineries:** Feedback loops maintain distillation column temperature within $\pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ for product purity.
- **HVAC systems:** PI controllers regulate building temperature.
- **Automotive:** Cruise control is a textbook feedback loop with PID.
- **Pharma batch reactors:** Cascade feedback (temperature $\to$ jacket temp $\to$ steam valve) for exothermic safety.
- **Power plants:** Drum level, boiler pressure, and turbine speed are all feedback-controlled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Questions often ask *why* feedback is used. Memorize three answers: (1) to **reduce steady-state error**, (2) to **reject disturbances**, and (3) to **reduce sensitivity to parameter variations**. These three reasons are tested every cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function from First Principles

**Given:** A negative feedback system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$, with input $R(s)$, output $C(s)$, error $E(s)$, and feedback signal $B(s)$.

**Step 1 — Write the comparator equation.**
The error is the difference between the reference input and the feedback signal:
$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2 — Write the forward path equation.**
The output is the forward path transfer function multiplied by the error:
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Write the feedback path equation.**
The feedback signal is the feedback transfer function multiplied by the output:
$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 1.**
$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate the error.**
$$E(s) + H(s) \cdot C(s) = R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Step 2 ($C(s) = G(s) E(s)$) into Step 5.**
$$E(s) + H(s) \cdot G(s) \cdot E(s) = R(s)$$

**Step 7 — Factor out $E(s)$.**
$$E(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G(s)H(s) \right] = R(s)$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $E(s)/R(s)$ (the error transfer function).**
$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Step 9 — Now derive $C(s)/R(s)$ using $C(s) = G(s)E(s)$.**
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = G(s) \cdot \frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Result (boxed KTU answer):**

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error for a Unit Step Input (Type-0 System)

**Step 1 — Final Value Theorem statement.**
For a stable system, the steady-state error is:
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 2 — Express $E(s)$ using the error transfer function.**
$$E(s) = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 3 — For a unit step input, $R(s) = 1/s$.**
$$E(s) = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Final Value Theorem.**
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Step 5 — For a Type-0 system, $G(s)H(s) = K / [(1+\tau_1 s)(1+\tau_2 s)\cdots]$.**
As $s \to 0$, $G(s)H(s) \to K$.

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$

where $K_p$ is the **position error constant**.

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
For a Type-0 system, a finite steady-state error $1/(1+K_p)$ always exists for a step input. This is the **offset** that P-control cannot eliminate — a classic KTU examination point.

> [!TIP]
> Adding an integrator (I-action) raises the system type by 1, making $K_p \to \infty$ and $e_{ss} \to 0$ for a step. This is *the* mathematical justification for using PI instead of P.

### 3.3 Derivation: Closed-Loop Response with Proportional Control

Let the plant be a first-order lag with transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$, and the controller be P-only with gain $K_c$. Assume unity feedback.

**Step 1 — Forward path transfer function.**
$$G(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1} = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the closed-loop TF formula.**
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau_p s + 1)$.**
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 4 — Define the new DC gain and time constant.**
$$K_{cl} = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}, \quad \tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 5 — Resulting closed-loop transfer function.**
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K_{cl}}{\tau_{cl} s + 1}$$

**Step 6 — Steady-state output for unit step.**
$$c_{ss} = K_{cl} = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} < 1$$

This is **less than unity**, confirming the **steady-state offset** of P-control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway for KTU:** Increasing $K_c$ reduces the offset ($K_{cl} \to 1$ as $K_c \to \infty$) AND reduces the closed-loop time constant $\tau_{cl}$, speeding up response. However, too high a $K_c$ excites unmodelled dynamics and causes oscillation — the classic **tuning trade-off**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effects of Each Controller Action

| Controller Action | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error | Disturbance Rejection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P (increase $K_c$)** | Decreases | Increases | Small change | Decreases (but not zero) | Improves |
| **I (decrease $T_i$)** | Decreases | Increases | Increases | **Eliminates** | Major improvement |
| **D (increase $T_d$)** | Small change | **Decreases** | **Decreases** | Small change | Small change |
| **PI** | Decreases | Increases | Increases | **Zero** | Excellent |
| **PID** | Decreases | **Small** | **Small** | **Zero** | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favorite KTU Part-A 3-mark question**. Memorize the direction (increase/decrease) of each effect.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of a Negative Feedback Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refNode["R(s) - Reference Input / Setpoint"]
    sumNode["Summing Junction"]
    errNode["E(s) - Error Signal"]
    ctrlNode["Gc(s) - Controller"]
    fceNode["Gv(s) - Final Control Element"]
    procNode["Gp(s) - Process / Plant"]
    sensNode["H(s) - Sensor / Transmitter"]
    outNode["C(s) - Controlled Output / PV"]
    disturbNode["D(s) - Disturbance"]

    refNode -- "+" --> sumNode
    disturbNode -- "+" --> sumNode
    sensNode -- "-" --> sumNode
    sumNode --> errNode
    errNode --> ctrlNode
    ctrlNode --> fceNode
    fceNode --> procNode
    procNode --> outNode
    outNode --> sensNode
    sensNode -. feedback path .-> sumNode
```

### 4.2 Signal Flow Chart Representation (Mason's Gain Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["R(s)"]
    nodeB["E(s)"]
    nodeC["U(s) - Controller Output"]
    nodeD["C(s)"]

    inputA -- "1" --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "Gc(s)" --> nodeC
    nodeC -- "Gv(s)*Gp(s)" --> nodeD
    nodeD -- "H(s)" --> nodeB
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: PLC-Based Feedback Loop Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    field1["Field Sensor RTD / 4-20mA Transmitter"]
    ai["PLC Analog Input Module"]
    pid["PID Function Block in CPU"]
    ao["PLC Analog Output Module"]
    field2["Control Valve / VFD Actuator"]
    plant["Process Plant - Tank, Furnace, Column"]
    hmi["HMI / SCADA Display"]

    field1 -->|PV signal| ai
    ai -->|Digital value| pid
    pid -->|CV 0-100 percent| ao
    ao -->|4-20mA / 0-10V| field2
    field2 -->|Manipulated flow| plant
    plant -->|Process response| field1
    pid -.->|Setpoint, Tuning| hmi
    ai -.->|PV display| hmi
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy: Classification of Feedback Controllers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["Feedback Controllers"]
    root --> c1["Continuous Controllers"]
    root --> c2["Discrete Controllers"]

    c1 --> p1["P - Proportional"]
    c1 --> p2["PI - Proportional Integral"]
    c1 --> p3["PD - Proportional Derivative"]
    c1 --> p4["PID - Proportional Integral Derivative"]

    c2 --> d1["On-Off - Two Position"]
    c2 --> d2["Multi-Position"]
    c2 --> d3["Bang-Bang with Hysteresis"]
    c2 --> d4["Sampled Data - Digital PID"]
```

### 4.5 Closed-Loop Response Characteristics (Time Domain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp1["Setpoint - Step Change"]
    resp["PV Response Curve"]
    tr["Tr - Rise Time"]
    tp["Tp - Peak Time"]
    ts["Ts - Settling Time"]
    mp["Mp - Maximum Overshoot"]
    ess["ess - Steady State Error"]

    sp1 --> resp
    resp --> tr
    resp --> tp
    resp --> ts
    resp --> mp
    resp --> ess
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Reading Tip (KTU):** The summing junction in KTU diagrams always uses **+ for the reference** and **− for the feedback**. The minus sign is what makes it *negative* feedback. Memorize this and the universal formula $C/R = G/(1+GH)$ will follow naturally.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define feedback control system. Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems.*

**Model Answer:**

A **feedback control system** is one in which the output of the process is continuously measured, compared with the desired reference input (setpoint), and the resulting error is used to generate a corrective control action through a controller and final control element, so that the output tracks the setpoint despite disturbances.

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop (Feedback) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low, depends on calibration | High, self-correcting |
| Disturbance rejection | None | Good |
| Stability | Inherently stable | Requires analysis |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Example | Electric hand dryer | AC temperature control |

*(3 marks: definition 1 mark, table 2 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the function of a PID controller. Write the equation of the PID controller in time domain.*

**Model Answer:**

A **PID controller** is a three-term feedback controller that combines Proportional, Integral, and Derivative actions to drive a process variable to its setpoint with minimal error, overshoot, and settling time.

**Time-domain equation:**

$$u(t) = K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_c T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} + u_0$$

Where:
- $K_c$ = Proportional gain
- $T_i$ = Integral time (reset time)
- $T_d$ = Derivative time
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ = error
- $u_0$ = controller bias

**Functions of each term:**
- **P-term:** Provides immediate corrective action proportional to current error.
- **I-term:** Eliminates steady-state offset by integrating past error.
- **D-term:** Anticipates future error by reacting to rate of change of error, improving stability and damping.

*(3 marks: equation 2 marks, term explanation 1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**Q A. (a)** With the help of a block diagram, derive the closed-loop transfer function of a negative feedback control system having a forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback path transfer function $H(s)$. State the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A negative feedback control system consists of the following blocks:
- $R(s)$ — Reference input (setpoint)
- Summing junction (comparator)
- $G(s)$ — Forward path (controller + actuator + plant)
- $H(s)$ — Feedback path (sensor)
- $C(s)$ — Controlled output (process variable)

**Derivation:**

The error at the comparator:
$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s)C(s)$$

The output of the forward path:
$$C(s) = G(s) E(s)$$

Substituting the second equation into the first:
$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot G(s) E(s)$$

Rearranging:
$$E(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = R(s)$$

Therefore the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

**Role of each block (briefer for 7-mark allocation):**
- Reference input $R(s)$: desired value.
- Comparator: computes error $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$.
- $G(s)$: amplifies/corrects based on error.
- Plant: physical system being controlled.
- Sensor $H(s)$: measures output and feeds back.
- Feedback path: closes the loop for self-correction.

---

**Q A. (b)** A unity feedback system has a forward path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.2s)}$. Determine the type of the system, the position, velocity and acceleration error constants, and the steady-state error for a unit step input. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the system type.**
The open-loop transfer function is:
$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.2s)}$$

Number of pure integrators (poles at origin) = **1**, so the system is **Type 1**.

**Step 2 — Position error constant $K_p$.**
$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)} = \infty$$

**Step 3 — Velocity error constant $K_v$.**
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{10}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)} = \frac{10}{1 \cdot 1} = 10$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration error constant $K_a$.**
$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot \frac{10}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10s}{(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state errors.**
- For **unit step** input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$
- For **unit ramp** input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v} = \dfrac{1}{10} = 0.1$
- For **unit parabolic** input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a} = \dfrac{1}{0} = \infty$

**Result:** The system is Type 1, with $K_p = \infty$, $K_v = 10$, $K_a = 0$, and zero steady-state error for a step input.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q B. (a)** Compare the responses of P, PI and PID controllers on a second-order process subjected to a step change in setpoint. Draw typical response curves and discuss the effect of each control action on rise time, overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A second-order process exhibits a typical underdamped response with measurable rise time, overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error. Each controller type modifies this response differently.

**Effect summary table:**

| Controller | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P | Fastest (smallest) | Large | Moderate | Non-zero (offset) |
| PI | Slower than P | Larger than P | Largest | **Zero** |
| PID | Fast (similar to P) | **Smallest** | **Smallest** | **Zero** |

**Discussion:**
- **P-only:** Responds quickly but always leaves an offset because the controller output settles at a value where $K_c e = 0$ requires $e = 0$, which generally does not hold for finite $K_c$.
- **PI:** Adds integral action which continually drives the offset to zero, but the integral wind-up can cause significant overshoot and a long settling time.
- **PID:** Derivative action damps the response by reacting to the rate of change of error, reducing overshoot and settling time while still maintaining zero steady-state error. This is the **preferred** choice for most industrial loops.

*(7 marks: 2 marks table, 3 marks P and PI discussion, 2 marks PID advantage)*

---

**Q B. (b)** A temperature control loop uses a PI controller with $K_c = 2.5$ and $T_i = 100$ s. If the setpoint is changed by 10 °C and the process gain $K_p = 1.2$, calculate (i) the proportional contribution, (ii) the integral contribution after 50 s (assuming error remained approximately constant at 10 °C), and (iii) the final total controller output if the bias $u_0 = 25\%$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $K_c = 2.5$
- $T_i = 100$ s
- $\Delta SP = 10\,^\circ\text{C}$ (assuming initial $e = 10\,^\circ\text{C}$)
- $K_p = 1.2$
- $u_0 = 25\%$
- $t = 50$ s

**Step 1 — Proportional contribution.**
$$u_P = K_c \cdot e = 2.5 \times 10 = 25\%$$

**Step 2 — Integral contribution.**
$$u_I = \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

With $e$ constant at 10 °C:
$$u_I = \frac{2.5}{100} \times 10 \times 50 = 0.025 \times 500 = 12.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Total controller output.**
$$u(t) = u_P + u_I + u_0 = 25 + 12.5 + 25 = 62.5\%$$

**Result:** Controller output is 62.5%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Many students forget to convert percentages or units. The controller output is conventionally expressed in **percent of output range (0–100%)**, not in physical units.
> 2. Confusing $K_p$ (process gain) with $K_c$ (controller gain) is a common mistake. Always read the question carefully.
> 3. Forgetting the **bias $u_0$** costs a full 1-mark deduction.
> 4. In PI/PID calculations, **never** assume error remains constant for the full integration time unless the question explicitly says so.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Feedback Control**

- **Definition:** Feedback control continuously measures output, compares with setpoint, and corrects via the error signal.
- **Open-loop:** No feedback, no self-correction. **Closed-loop:** Has feedback, self-corrects.
- **Negative feedback** is the universal industrial standard — it **stabilizes** and **reduces error**.
- **Positive feedback** amplifies error — used in oscillators and bistable devices only.
- **Closed-loop TF:** $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$
- **Unity feedback TF:** $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$
- **Error TF:** $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$
- **Characteristic equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ — its roots determine closed-loop stability.
- **P-controller:** $u = K_c e + u_0$. Fast but has **offset**.
- **PI-controller:** $u = K_c e + \frac{K_c}{T_i}\int e\,dt + u_0$. **Eliminates offset**, risk of overshoot and integral wind-up.
- **PID-controller:** Adds derivative term $K_c T_d \frac{de}{dt}$. Best transient response, zero steady-state error.
- **On-Off controller:** Two-position with optional dead band $\Delta$ to prevent chattering.
- **System type** = number of integrators (poles at origin) in $G(s)H(s)$. Type 0 → finite step error, Type 1 → zero step error, Type 2 → zero step and ramp error.
- **Error constants:**
  - $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)$
  - $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s)H(s)$
  - $K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G(s)H(s)$
- **Steady-state errors:** $e_{ss} = 1/(1+K_p)$ for step, $1/K_v$ for ramp, $1/K_a$ for parabolic.
- **Block diagram conventions:** Reference enters with **+**, feedback enters with **−**.
- **PLC implementation:** Sensor → Analog Input → PID function block → Analog Output → Control Valve → Plant.
- **Three reasons to use feedback:** (1) reduce steady-state error, (2) reject disturbances, (3) reduce sensitivity to plant parameter variations.
- **Final Value Theorem** is the standard tool for steady-state error analysis — use it whenever the input is a standard signal (step, ramp, parabola).
- **Effect of increasing each gain** — memorize: higher $K_c$ → faster but riskier; lower $T_i$ → more integral action; higher $T_d$ → more damping.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU tip:** The closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ and the three error constants $K_p, K_v, K_a$ together account for **over 60% of the marks** in Module-1 feedback control questions. Master these cold and you will secure almost full marks on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Forward Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feed Forward Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Feed Forward Control (FFC)** is an advanced process control strategy in which the controller measures one or more **disturbance variables (load variables)** entering the process and computes a corrective manipulated variable action *before* the disturbance has a chance to significantly affect the **controlled variable (process output)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Feed Forward Control is a *predictive*, *anticipatory* control technique that compensates for measurable disturbances by computing the required manipulation based on a mathematical model of the process, thereby neutralizing the disturbance effect *open-loop* without waiting for an output error to develop.

Mathematically, a Feed Forward Controller computes its output using a causal model:

$$u_{ff}(t) = f\Big(d(t)\Big)$$

where $u_{ff}(t)$ is the manipulated variable and $d(t)$ is the measured disturbance. The function $f(\cdot)$ represents the **process model** that maps the disturbance to its expected impact on the process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shower Anticipation" Example

Imagine you are taking a shower. The **desired output** is *comfortable warm water at your body*. The **disturbance** is a sudden change in *cold water pressure* in the pipeline (someone opens a tap elsewhere in the house).

| Control Strategy | Human Behavior |
|------------------|----------------|
| **Feedback Control** | You stand in the shower. The water turns cold. You *feel* the error and turn the hot tap. (Reactive, slow) |
| **Feed Forward Control** | You *hear* the cold water pressure drop (sense the disturbance), and *immediately* start adjusting the hot tap *before* the water at your body even changes temperature. (Predictive, fast) |

The Feed Forward controller acts like an experienced human who has learned the **causal relationship** between cold water pressure and shower temperature — it "knows" that if cold pressure drops 10%, it must increase hot flow by 5% to maintain balance, all without measuring the actual shower temperature.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Hallmark Properties of Feed Forward Control**
> - **Anticipatory:** Acts on *cause* (disturbance), not *effect* (output error).
> - **Open-Loop:** Does not measure the controlled variable — therefore **cannot self-correct** for model errors.
> - **Model-Dependent:** Performance is entirely bounded by the accuracy of the process model.
> - **Disturbance-Specific:** Each measurable disturbance requires a dedicated feed forward path.

## 1.4 Distinction from Feedback Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Comparison for KTU Exams**
> Feedback Control = **Closed-Loop, Error-Driven, Reactive**
> Feed Forward Control = **Open-Loop, Disturbance-Driven, Proactive**
> In modern DCS, both are combined into **Feed Forward–Feedback (FF–FB) Composite Control** for the best of both worlds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic Steps

A Feed Forward Control system operates on the following sequential logic:

1. **Disturbance Sensing:** A dedicated sensor (e.g., flow, temperature, pressure transmitter) continuously measures the *load variable* $d(t)$ entering the process.
2. **Model-Based Computation:** The Feed Forward Controller (often a DCS function block, e.g., in a DeltaV or Honeywell system) applies the *inverse process model* to compute the required manipulated variable.
3. **Actuation:** The computed $u_{ff}(t)$ is sent to the *final control element* (control valve, VFD, etc.) **in parallel** with any feedback action.
4. **Cancellation Objective:** The goal is to make the net effect of the disturbance on the output **identically zero** — *Ideal Feed Forward*.
5. **No Error Feedback:** Because the output $y(t)$ is not measured by the FFC, any unmeasured disturbance or model imperfection will *not* be corrected (this is the key limitation).

## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How"

- **Why measure the disturbance?** Because in many industrial processes, disturbances are *measurable* (incoming feed flow, ambient temperature, fuel calorific value), while the controlled variable (e.g., product composition) may have **large dead time** or be expensive to measure continuously.
- **How is the controller designed?** By setting the sum of the disturbance transfer function path and the manipulated-variable transfer function path to zero — yielding the **Ideal Feed Forward Controller** transfer function.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|-------------------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | $Y(s) = G_d(s)D(s) + G_p(s)U(s)$ | Process with disturbance and manipulated input | $Y$ = output, $D$ = disturbance, $U$ = input |
| 2 | $G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$ | **Ideal Feed Forward Controller** | Must be **realizable** (proper, no pure differentiators) |
| 3 | $Y(s) = \big[G_d(s) + G_p(s)G_{ff}(s)\big]D(s)$ | Output under ideal FF action | Goal: $Y(s) = 0$ for all $D(s)$ |
| 4 | $u_{ff}(t) = K_{ff}\big[d(t - \theta_d) - d_{ss}\big]$ | Static (steady-state) FF law | $K_{ff}$ = static gain, $\theta_d$ = disturbance dead time |
| 5 | $K_{ff} = -\dfrac{K_d}{K_p}$ | Steady-state FF gain | $K_d, K_p$ = steady-state gains of disturbance and process paths |
| 6 | $G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{K_d}{K_p}\cdot\dfrac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}\cdot e^{-(\theta_d - \theta_p)s}$ | Lead-Lag FF controller with dead-time compensation | Used in practice for dynamic compensation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Realizability Constraint:** $G_{ff}(s)$ must be *proper* (degree of denominator $\geq$ degree of numerator) — otherwise the controller demands physically impossible action. Engineers use **Lead-Lag blocks** in DCS to approximate the ideal transfer function.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat Exchangers:** Pre-adjusting hot utility flow when feed flow or inlet temperature changes.
- **Distillation Columns:** Compensating for feed composition disturbances in reflux or steam rate.
- **Boiler Drum Level:** Anticipating steam demand swings by adjusting feedwater flow (a classic three-element drum level control uses steam flow as FF).
- **HVAC Systems:** Pre-cooling air when occupancy sensors detect a load increase.
- **Chemical Reactors:** Adjusting coolant flow when reactant concentration feed changes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Feed Forward Controller

**Step 1 — Define the Process Model**

Consider a linear single-input, single-output (SISO) process with one measurable disturbance. In the Laplace domain, the process output is the superposition of two contributions:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)\,U(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Explanation:** The first term $G_p(s)U(s)$ represents how the manipulated variable $U(s)$ (e.g., valve position) affects the output $Y(s)$ (e.g., temperature). The second term $G_d(s)D(s)$ represents how the disturbance $D(s)$ (e.g., feed flow) affects the output independently. **Superposition holds** because we have linearized the process around an operating point.

**Step 2 — Apply the Feed Forward Control Law**

The feed forward controller generates a manipulated action $U(s)$ that is a function of the measured disturbance:

$$U(s) = G_{ff}(s)\,D(s)$$

**Explanation:** This is the defining equation of feed forward control — the controller output is *purely* a function of the disturbance, with no dependence on the controlled variable $Y(s)$. This confirms the **open-loop** nature of the FFC path.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Process Equation**

Substituting Step 2 into Step 1:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)\big[G_{ff}(s)D(s)\big] + G_d(s)D(s)$$

**Explanation:** We replace $U(s)$ with the feed forward action $G_{ff}(s)D(s)$. Now both terms on the right-hand side share the common factor $D(s)$, allowing us to factor it out.

**Step 4 — Factor Out the Disturbance**

$$Y(s) = \big[G_p(s)G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s)\big]D(s)$$

**Explanation:** This compact form reveals a key insight — the total transfer function from disturbance to output is $G_p(s)G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s)$. The feed forward controller is *inside* this path.

**Step 5 — Impose the Perfect Cancellation Condition**

The fundamental design objective is to make the controlled variable *invariant* with respect to the disturbance, i.e., $Y(s) = 0$ for any $D(s) \neq 0$. This requires the bracketed term to vanish identically:

$$G_p(s)G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

**Explanation:** If this sum equals zero, then $Y(s) = 0 \cdot D(s) = 0$ for *every* possible disturbance signal. The process is "immune" to measured disturbances — the holy grail of feed forward design.

**Step 6 — Solve for the Ideal Feed Forward Controller**

Rearranging the cancellation condition:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$$

**Explanation:** This is the **Ideal Feed Forward Controller Transfer Function**. The negative sign is critical — it indicates *opposing* action (if the disturbance tends to raise the output, the controller must lower the manipulated variable). This is a mathematical result, not a choice.

**Step 7 — Check Realizability**

For physical implementation, $G_{ff}(s)$ must be realizable. A common case uses first-order-with-dead-time models:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p\,e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}, \quad G_d(s) = \frac{K_d\,e^{-\theta_d s}}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

Substituting:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}\cdot\frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}\cdot e^{-(\theta_d - \theta_p)s}$$

**Explanation:** 
- $-\dfrac{K_d}{K_p}$ is the **static gain** — the steady-state ratio of corrections.
- $\dfrac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}$ is the **Lead-Lag block** — provides dynamic compensation; if $\tau_p > \tau_d$, it is a *lead* (controller acts faster); if $\tau_p < \tau_d$, it is a *lag*.
- $e^{-(\theta_d - \theta_p)s}$ is the **dead-time compensation term** — if $\theta_d > \theta_p$, we *predict* the disturbance effect; if $\theta_d < \theta_p$, a Smith Predictor or measurement delay is needed.

## 3.2 Steady-State (Static) Feed Forward — Special Case

When only steady-state accuracy is required (acceptable for slow processes), the dynamics can be ignored and the controller reduces to a simple proportional gain. From the steady-state form of the process equations:

$$y_{ss} = K_p\,u_{ss} + K_d\,d_{ss}$$

Setting $y_{ss} = 0$ (perfect cancellation) and solving:

$$u_{ff,ss} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}\,d_{ss}$$

This is the **Static Feed Forward Law** and is the simplest possible FFC, often implemented as a single multiplier block in a DCS.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Component | Transfer Function | Physical Meaning | Typical Range |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Process | $G_p(s)$ | Manipulated → Output | $K_p > 0$ typically |
| Disturbance path | $G_d(s)$ | Disturbance → Output | Sign indicates direction of effect |
| FF Controller | $G_{ff}(s) = -G_d(s)/G_p(s)$ | Disturbance → Manipulated | Must be *proper* |
| Static gain | $K_{ff} = -K_d/K_p$ | Steady-state correction ratio | Dimensionless or with units of $u/d$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ideal Feed Forward Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Disturbance d t] --> B[Disturbance Sensor]
    B --> C[D e d t]
    C --> D["Feed Forward Controller Gff of s"]
    D --> E["Sum Node with Feedback u fb t"]
    E --> F["Final Control Element Valve"]
    F --> G["Process Gp of s"]
    H["Disturbance Path Gd of s"] --> G
    G --> I["Output y t Controlled Variable"]
    J["Setpoint r t"] --> K["Feedback Controller Gc of s"]
    I --> L["Comparator e = r minus y"]
    J --> L
    L --> K
    K --> E

    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style I fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99
    style D fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2d6a4f
    style G fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
    style H fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
    style K fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2d6a4f
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **top path (green)** is the Feed Forward path — it bypasses the comparator and acts *directly* on the valve.
- The **bottom path (blue/red)** is the conventional Feedback loop — it computes an error and corrects reactively.
- The two paths are **summed** at the final control element. This is the classic **FF + FB composite control** structure implemented in modern DCS using function blocks.

## 4.2 Pure Feed Forward (Open-Loop) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["Measured Disturbance d t"] --> S1["Transmitter"]
    S1 --> FF1["Feed Forward Function Block Gff"]
    FF1 --> V1["Control Valve"]
    V1 --> P1["Industrial Process"]
    DIST["Unmeasured Disturbance d2"] -.-> P1
    P1 --> Y1["Output y t NOT measured by FFC"]
    FF2["Feedback Loop y measured here"] -.-> Y1

    style D1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style DIST fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style FF1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2d6a4f
    style P1 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
    style FF2 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **dashed line** and gray block represent the *optional* feedback path — it is **not part of pure feed forward control** but is almost always added in practice.
- Unmeasured disturbance $d_2$ enters the process and is *invisible* to the FFC — this is why pure feed forward cannot achieve perfect control alone.

## 4.3 Signal Flow Comparison: Feedback vs Feed Forward

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FB ["FEEDBACK CONTROL Reactive"]
        F1["Disturbance enters process"] --> F2["Output deviates from setpoint"]
        F2 --> F3["Error e is computed"]
        F3 --> F4["Controller adjusts valve"]
        F4 --> F5["Process responds to correction"]
        F5 -.-> F1
    end

    subgraph FF ["FEED FORWARD CONTROL Proactive"]
        G1["Disturbance is measured upstream"] --> G2["FF model computes required correction"]
        G2 --> G3["Valve is pre adjusted"]
        G3 --> G4["Disturbance enters process"]
        G4 --> G5["Correction arrives simultaneously"]
        G5 --> G6["Output remains at setpoint"]
    end

    style FB fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style FF fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Feed forward control attempts to make the disturbance's *arrival* at the process coincide exactly with the corrective action's *arrival* at the same point — a kind of *temporal interference cancellation* analogous to active noise control in acoustics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Feed Forward Control. How does it differ from conventional Feedback Control?**

**Model Answer:**
Feed Forward Control is an anticipatory control strategy that measures a *disturbance variable* and computes the manipulated variable action using an inverse process model, so that the disturbance's effect on the controlled output is neutralized *before* it occurs.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Feedback Control | Feed Forward Control |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| Action basis | Output error $e = r - y$ | Measured disturbance $d$ |
| Nature | Reactive, closed-loop | Proactive, open-loop |
| Measurement | Controlled variable | Disturbance variable |
| Stability concern | Yes (loop tuning) | No (open-loop) |
| Model dependency | Low | High |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison table)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the ideal feed forward controller transfer function. Why must it be realizable?**

**Model Answer:**
The ideal feed forward controller is given by:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$$

It must be **realizable** (proper) because a non-proper transfer function would require differentiation of the disturbance signal of higher order than physically achievable, leading to amplification of measurement noise and unbounded controller action. In practice, **Lead-Lag** blocks approximate the ideal law.

*(3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark realizability definition, 1 mark practical Lead-Lag approximation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing $G_{ff}(s) = -G_p(s)/G_d(s)$ (inverted numerator/denominator). The correct form is $-G_d/G_p$ — memorize this carefully.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **negative sign** in the formula; it is *not* optional — it represents the *opposing* corrective action.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing "static feed forward" with "dynamic feed forward" — the static version uses only $K_d/K_p$, while the dynamic version includes lead-lag and dead-time terms.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not mentioning the *realizability constraint* — examiners specifically look for this in 14-mark answers.
> - **Mark Distribution:** Expect roughly 7 marks for the conceptual/derivation part, 5 marks for the application/working, and 2 marks for a neat block diagram.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Feed Forward Control System** for a process with measurable disturbance. Derive the expression for the ideal feed forward controller transfer function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heat exchanger has the following transfer functions:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{2\,e^{-0.5s}}{3s+1}, \quad G_d(s) = \frac{1.5\,e^{-2s}}{5s+1}$$

where $U$ = steam flow (kg/s), $D$ = feed flow disturbance (kg/s), $Y$ = outlet temperature (°C). Design the **ideal static and dynamic feed forward controller**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the FF + FB composite block diagram. Draw it on the answer sheet with the following labels:
- Disturbance sensor → FF controller → Summer → Valve → Process
- Process output → Comparator → FB controller → Summer (closing the loop)

**Derivation:** (Write all six steps from Section 3.1 of these notes.)

The final result:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$$

**Working Principle:** The disturbance is measured upstream; the FF controller applies the inverse process model to compute the required steam flow adjustment, which acts *in parallel* with the feedback controller. The goal is to satisfy $G_p G_{ff} + G_d = 0$.

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 1 for working explanation)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- $K_p = 2$, $\tau_p = 3$, $\theta_p = 0.5$ min
- $K_d = 1.5$, $\tau_d = 5$, $\theta_d = 2$ min

**Step 2 — Static Feed Forward Gain:**

$$K_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p} = -\frac{1.5}{2} = -0.75$$

**Explanation:** A unit increase in feed flow disturbance requires a $0.75$ kg/s *decrease* in steam flow to maintain outlet temperature.

**Step 3 — Dynamic Lead-Lag Block:**

$$\text{Lead-Lag} = \frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1} = \frac{3s + 1}{5s + 1}$$

Since $\tau_p < \tau_d$ ($3 < 5$), this is a **Lag** block — the FF action is *slower* than the disturbance effect, providing smoothing.

**Step 4 — Dead-Time Compensation:**

$$e^{-(\theta_d - \theta_p)s} = e^{-(2 - 0.5)s} = e^{-1.5s}$$

**Explanation:** The disturbance arrives 1.5 min *later* at the output than its effect through the process path. The FF controller must therefore *predict* the future correction — a pure delay element of 1.5 min is required (implemented via a DCS delay block).

**Step 5 — Final Dynamic FF Controller:**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -0.75 \cdot \frac{3s + 1}{5s + 1} \cdot e^{-1.5s}$$

*(7 marks — 2 for static gain, 2 for lead-lag, 2 for dead-time, 1 for final assembly)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Static and Dynamic Feed Forward Control**. Compare their performance and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical reactor has the disturbance and process transfer functions:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{4}{2s+1}, \quad G_d(s) = \frac{3}{s+1}$$

Design the **static feed forward controller** and determine the **steady-state manipulated variable** when the disturbance changes by $\Delta D = 2$ units from its nominal value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Static Feed Forward Control:**
Uses only the *steady-state* gains $K_d$ and $K_p$ of the process. The controller law is:

$$u_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}\,d$$

- **Advantages:** Simple, easy to tune, no dynamics to model, robust to small disturbances.
- **Limitations:** Inaccurate during *transients* — cannot compensate for the dynamic mismatch between the disturbance path and the process path.

**Dynamic Feed Forward Control:**
Uses the *full* transfer functions $G_d(s)$ and $G_p(s)$ to produce a controller with lead-lag and dead-time compensation.

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}\cdot\frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}\cdot e^{-(\theta_d - \theta_p)s}$$

- **Advantages:** Provides near-perfect cancellation during both steady-state and transient conditions.
- **Limitations:** Requires accurate process model; noise-sensitive; complex to commission; may be unrealizable if process has inverse-response or higher-order dynamics.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Static FF | Dynamic FF |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| Information used | Steady-state gains only | Full transfer functions |
| Transient performance | Poor (offset during change) | Excellent |
| Model effort | Low | High |
| Noise sensitivity | Low | Moderate–High |
| Tuning complexity | 1 parameter ($K_{ff}$) | 4+ parameters |

*(7 marks — 2 for static, 2 for dynamic, 3 for comparison)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $K_p = 4$, $\tau_p = 2$ (not needed for static)
- $K_d = 3$, $\tau_d = 1$ (not needed for static)

**Step 2 — Compute the static FF gain:**

$$K_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p} = -\frac{3}{4} = -0.75$$

**Step 3 — Apply the static FF law for $\Delta D = 2$:**

$$\Delta U_{ff} = K_{ff}\cdot \Delta D = -0.75 \times 2 = -1.5 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The manipulated variable (e.g., coolant flow) must be *decreased* by 1.5 units to maintain the reactor temperature in the face of a 2-unit increase in the disturbance. The negative sign indicates the **opposing direction** of correction.

**Step 5 — Final Manipulated Variable:**

If the nominal $U_0 = 10$ units, then the new operating value is:

$$U_{new} = U_0 + \Delta U_{ff} = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5 \text{ units}$$

*(7 marks — 2 for $K_{ff}$, 2 for $\Delta U$, 1 for interpretation, 2 for final value with units)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Feed Forward Control**

- **Definition:** Anticipatory, model-based, open-loop control acting on *measured disturbances* before they affect the output.
- **Master Formula:** $G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$ — *memorize verbatim*; the negative sign is non-negotiable.
- **Static Law:** $u_{ff} = -\dfrac{K_d}{K_p}\,d$ — for slow processes only.
- **Dynamic Law:** Includes *Lead-Lag* $\dfrac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}$ and *dead-time* $e^{-(\theta_d - \theta_p)s}$ terms.
- **Realizability:** $G_{ff}(s)$ must be *proper* (no higher-order differentiators than physically possible).
- **Advantages:** (1) Acts before error develops; (2) Ideal for measurable, large disturbances; (3) No stability issues; (4) Reduces loop work for feedback controller.
- **Limitations:** (1) Requires accurate process model; (2) Cannot correct *unmeasured* disturbances; (3) Sensitive to model errors and noise; (4) Often needs a feedback loop in parallel (FF-FB composite).
- **Typical Applications:** Heat exchangers, distillation columns, boiler drum level, HVAC, chemical reactors.
- **In DCS:** Implemented using **function blocks** (FF controller, Lead-Lag, Dead-Time, Sum blocks) in DeltaV, Honeywell Experion, ABB 800xA, Siemens PCS 7.
- **Key Distinction:** Feedback *reacts* to error; Feed Forward *anticipates* disturbance; both together give the best performance.
- **Exam Trap:** Never forget the **negative sign**; never swap $G_p$ and $G_d$ in the formula; always state the realizability condition for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascade Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cascade Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Cascade Control**
> **Cascade Control** is an advanced multiple-loop feedback control strategy in which the output (manipulated variable setpoint) of one controller (the **primary/master controller**) is dynamically fed as the *remote setpoint* of another controller (the **secondary/slave controller**). The two control loops are *nested* (one inside the other) such that the inner (secondary) loop is fully contained within the outer (primary) loop. The secondary loop must respond **at least 3 to 5 times faster** than the primary loop, ensuring strict separation of process time constants.

**Structural Components of a Cascade Control System:**

| Component | Role | Typical Examples |
|-----------|------|------------------|
| **Primary Controller (G_c1)** | Outer loop — maintains the *primary process variable* (PV1) at its setpoint | Temperature controller |
| **Secondary Controller (G_c2)** | Inner loop — maintains a *secondary process variable* (PV2) at the moving setpoint issued by G_c1 | Flow or pressure controller |
| **Final Control Element (G_v)** | Actuator (valve, VFD, damper) common to both loops | Control valve |
| **Secondary Process (G_p2)** | Fast inner process whose output is PV2 | Flow through pipe |
| **Primary Process (G_p1)** | Slow outer process whose output is PV1 | Heated tank temperature |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Analogy: The Cruise Control with a Gearbox Robot**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly road. You (the *primary controller*) only care about the **car's overall speed** (primary PV). But the gas pedal response depends on many fast-acting disturbances (wind gusts, bumps, gear changes). If *you* try to correct every small disturbance yourself, your reactions would be too slow.
> Solution: You hire a co-pilot robot (*secondary controller*) whose *only* job is to watch the **engine RPM** (secondary PV — fast variable) and adjust the gas pedal *instantly*. You only tell the robot what RPM target you need to maintain the desired car speed.
> 
> - **Primary disturbance** (a steep hill) → You (primary) change the desired RPM (setpoint cascade).
> - **Secondary disturbance** (a wind gust) → The robot (secondary) reacts within milliseconds before you even notice.
> 
> This is exactly how Cascade Control works in a chemical plant: the inner flow controller "absorbs" hydraulic disturbances before they reach the slow temperature loop.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Cascade Control?**
> - Rejects **secondary loop disturbances** before they propagate to the primary PV.
> - Compensates for **nonlinearities** in the final control element (e.g., valve dead-band).
> - Improves **closed-loop response speed** without destabilizing the primary loop.
> - Reduces the **effective dead-time** seen by the primary controller.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Measure PV1** (primary process variable, e.g., reactor temperature) via transmitter TT-101.
2. **Compare** PV1 with the *primary setpoint* SP1 → generate error e1(t) = SP1 − PV1.
3. **Primary controller G_c1** acts on e1 and outputs a *remote setpoint* SP2 (NOT a valve position).
4. **Secondary loop measures PV2** (secondary process variable, e.g., steam flow).
5. **Compare** PV2 with SP2 → generate error e2(t) = SP2 − PV2.
6. **Secondary controller G_c2** drives the final control element (valve) to nullify e2.
7. The **inner loop closes the valve dynamics** and exposes the primary controller to a much "cleaner" plant.
8. The **outer loop** then adjusts SP2 to correct any remaining deviation in PV1.

### 2.2 Tuning Philosophy — "Inner-Outside-In"

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard KTU-Prescribed Tuning Sequence:**
> 1. Place the **secondary (inner) controller in MANUAL** mode.
> 2. Manually vary the valve signal and tune **G_c2** so that the inner loop is **fast and critically damped** (typically P-only or PI with aggressive gain).
> 3. Switch the inner loop to **AUTO** (closed).
> 4. With the inner loop closed, tune **G_c1** (usually PI or PID) using the dynamics of the *overall inner-loop transfer function* G_inner(s).
> 5. Fine-tune G_c2 again to remove any residual oscillation (since plant dynamics shifted).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] The following table summarizes every key equation required for the ESE. Note that **absolute value bars** are written using `\vert` to preserve markdown rendering.

| # | Equation | Description / Symbol Legend |
|---|----------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | $G_{inner}(s) = \dfrac{G_{c2}(s)\, G_{v}(s)\, G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s)\, G_{v}(s)\, G_{p2}(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function of the secondary (inner) loop |
| 2 | $\dfrac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \dfrac{G_{c1}(s)\, G_{inner}(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\, G_{inner}(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}$ | Overall closed-loop transfer function (primary loop with inner loop closed) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{Y_1(s)}{D_2(s)} = \dfrac{G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\, G_{inner}(s)\, G_{p1}(s)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + G_{c2}(s)\, G_{v}(s)\, G_{p2}(s)}$ | Effect of a *secondary-loop disturbance* D₂ on primary PV — **attenuated by (1 + G_c2·G_v·G_p2)** |
| 4 | $\dfrac{Y_1(s)}{D_1(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\, G_{inner}(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}$ | Effect of a *primary-loop disturbance* D₁ (unchanged structurally) |
| 5 | $K_{c2} > K_{c1}$ typically | Secondary gain is larger because its loop is faster |
| 6 | $\tau_{p2} < \dfrac{\tau_{p1}}{3}$ | Secondary process time constant must be ≤ one-third of primary |
| 7 | $e_1(t) = SP_1(t) - PV_1(t)$ | Primary loop error |
| 8 | $e_2(t) = SP_2(t) - PV_2(t) = u_1(t) - PV_2(t)$ | Secondary loop error (setpoint cascaded from primary output u₁) |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Cascade control is the **workhorse advanced control** in continuous process industries:
- **Petrochemical reactors** — temperature cascade with jacket flow.
- **Distillation columns** — composition cascade with reflux flow.
- **Boiler drum level** — level cascade with feed-water flow.
- **HVAC** — zone temperature cascade with duct static pressure.
- **PLC/DCS implementation** — easily realized using standard **function blocks** (PID, RATIO, REMOTE-SP) in ladder or FBD languages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of the Inner Loop

Consider the inner loop block diagram where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_2(s) &= G_{p2}(s)\, X(s) \\
X(s) &= G_v(s)\, M(s) \\
M(s) &= G_{c2}(s)\,[SP_2(s) - Y_2(s)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the controller output equation.**

The secondary controller output (signal to the valve) is the controller acting on the inner-loop error:

$$
M(s) = G_{c2}(s)\,[SP_2(s) - Y_2(s)]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the actuator and process model.**

Substitute $X(s) = G_v(s) M(s)$ and $Y_2(s) = G_{p2}(s) X(s)$ into the controller equation:

$$
M(s) = G_{c2}(s)\,SP_2(s) - G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)\,M(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Group terms containing M(s).**

Bring all $M(s)$ terms to the left-hand side:

$$
M(s)\,\bigl[1 + G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)\bigr] = G_{c2}(s)\,SP_2(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for M(s).**

$$
M(s) = \frac{G_{c2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)} \, SP_2(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Y₂(s).**

Since $Y_2(s) = G_{p2}(s) G_v(s) M(s)$:

$$
Y_2(s) = \frac{G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)} \, SP_2(s)
$$

**Step 6 — Define the inner-loop closed-loop transfer function.**

$$
\boxed{\,G_{inner}(s) = \frac{Y_2(s)}{SP_2(s)} = \frac{G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)}\,}
$$

This is the **effective plant** seen by the primary controller.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Disturbance Rejection Comparison (Single-Loop vs. Cascade)

Consider a primary-loop disturbance $D_2$ entering between the inner loop and $G_{p1}$.

**Single-Loop Equivalent (no cascade):** The closed-loop relation from $D_2$ to $Y_1$ is:

$$
\frac{Y_1(s)}{D_2(s)}\bigg|_{SL} = \frac{G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)\,G_{p1}(s)}
$$

**Cascade Configuration:** With the inner loop closed, $D_2$ first passes through $G_{p2}$ before reaching the inner-controller feedback node. The transfer function becomes:

$$
\frac{Y_1(s)}{D_2(s)}\bigg|_{CC} = \frac{G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\,G_{inner}(s)\,G_{p1}(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)}
$$

**Step 7 — Form the rejection-ratio (improvement factor).**

$$
I(s) = \frac{Y_1/D_2 \,\vert_{SL}}{Y_1/D_2 \,\vert_{CC}} = 1 + G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)
$$

**Step 8 — Interpret the result.**

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Disturbance rejection improvement} = 1 + G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)\,}
$$

This demonstrates that the cascade structure attenuates $D_2$ by a factor $\vert 1 + G_{c2} G_v G_{p2} \vert$ — at low frequencies where controller gains are large, this attenuation can be **20 dB to 40 dB** (i.e., a 10× to 100× reduction in error).

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Practice Standard

> [!NOTE] **Worked Example (typical 14-mark derivation)**
> 
> **Given:**
> $G_v(s) = K_v = 2$
> $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{2}{3s+1}$
> $G_{c2}(s) = K_{c2} = 5$
> $G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{1}{10s+1}$
> $G_{c1}(s) = K_{c1}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{5s}\right)$
> 
> **Find:** (a) the inner-loop transfer function, (b) the overall closed-loop characteristic equation.

**Part (a) — Inner-loop TF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{inner}(s) &= \frac{K_{c2}\,K_v\,G_{p2}(s)}{1 + K_{c2}\,K_v\,G_{p2}(s)} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 2 \times \dfrac{2}{3s+1}}{1 + 5 \times 2 \times \dfrac{2}{3s+1}} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{20}{3s+1}}{1 + \dfrac{20}{3s+1}} \\
&= \frac{20}{3s+1+20} \\
&= \frac{20}{3s+21} \\
&= \frac{20/21}{\tfrac{3}{21}s+1} = \frac{0.952}{0.143s+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Overall Characteristic Equation:**

The denominator of the outer closed loop:

$$
1 + G_{c1}(s)\,G_{inner}(s)\,G_{p1}(s) = 0
$$

$$
1 + K_{c1}\left(1+\frac{1}{5s}\right) \cdot \frac{20}{3s+21} \cdot \frac{1}{10s+1} = 0
$$

For a unit-step setpoint change in a **Type-1** system, the steady-state error is **zero** (due to the integral term in $G_{c1}$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SP1["PRIMARY SETPOINT"] --> Sum1{{"+ -"}}
    PV1["PRIMARY PV TT101"] --> Sum1
    Sum1 --> Gc1["PRIMARY CONTROLLER Gc1 PI"]
    Gc1 -- "REMOTE SETPOINT SP2" --> Sum2{{"+ -"}}
    PV2["SECONDARY PV FT101"] --> Sum2
    Sum2 --> Gc2["SECONDARY CONTROLLER Gc2 P or PI"]
    Gc2 --> Gv["FINAL ELEMENT VALVE Gv"]
    Gv --> Gp2["SECONDARY PROCESS Gp2 FLOW LOOP"]
    Gp2 -- "PV2" --> PV2
    Gp2 --> Gp1["PRIMARY PROCESS Gp1 HEATER TANK"]
    Gp1 -- "PV1" --> PV1
    D2["DISTURBANCE D2 PRESSURE VARIATION"] -.-> Gp2
    D1["DISTURBANCE D1 INLET TEMPERATURE"] -.-> Gp1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Single-Loop vs. Cascade — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SL["SINGLE LOOP STRUCTURE"]
        SPa["SP"] --> Sa{{"+ -"}}
        PVa["PV"] --> Sa
        Sa --> Gca["Gc SINGLE CONTROLLER"]
        Gca --> Gva["Gv VALVE"]
        Gva --> Gpa["Gp PROCESS"]
        Gpa --> PVa
    end
    subgraph CC["CASCADE STRUCTURE"]
        SPb["SP1"] --> Sb1{{"+ -"}}
        PVb1["PV1"] --> Sb1
        Sb1 --> Gcb1["Gc1 MASTER"]
        Gcb1 --> SP2["SP2 REMOTE"]
        SP2 --> Sb2{{"+ -"}}
        PVb2["PV2"] --> Sb2
        Sb2 --> Gcb2["Gc2 SLAVE"]
        Gcb2 --> Gvb["Gv VALVE"]
        Gvb --> Gpb2["Gp2 INNER PLANT"]
        Gpb2 --> PVb2
        Gpb2 --> Gpb1["Gp1 OUTER PLANT"]
        Gpb1 --> PVb1
    end
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow Summary (Textual Fallback for Hardware Use)

| Block | Symbol | Function |
|-------|--------|----------|
| Setpoint selector | SP1 | External operator input |
| Error detector 1 | Σ₁ | e₁ = SP1 − PV1 |
| Primary controller | G_c1 | Slow, integral action dominant |
| Setpoint selector 2 | SP2 | Output of G_c1 |
| Error detector 2 | Σ₂ | e₂ = SP2 − PV2 |
| Secondary controller | G_c2 | Fast, proportional-dominant |
| Final control element | G_v | Valve positioner |
| Inner process | G_p2 | Flow, pressure, RPM |
| Outer process | G_p1 | Temperature, level, composition |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Cascade Control. State any two advantages over single-loop control.**

> [!NOTE] **Model Answer:**
> **Cascade Control** is a multiple-loop control strategy in which the output of one controller (primary) is used to set the setpoint of another controller (secondary), with the two loops nested such that the inner loop is fully enclosed within the outer loop.
> **Advantages:**
> 1. It rejects disturbances entering the secondary loop *before* they affect the primary PV.
> 2. It compensates for valve/sensor non-linearities through the inner loop's high-gain feedback.
> *(Other acceptable advantages: improved dynamic response, reduced effective dead-time, allows use of faster secondary actuator.)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. Why must the inner loop of a cascade system be faster than the outer loop?**

> [!NOTE] **Model Answer:**
> The inner (secondary) loop must be at least **3 to 5 times faster** than the outer (primary) loop so that its settling time is much smaller. This ensures:
> (i) The inner loop can be considered at *quasi-steady-state* relative to the primary dynamics, validating the tuning procedure.
> (ii) Inner-loop disturbances are rejected before they propagate.
> (iii) The inner loop acts as a "fast pre-compensator," reducing the order and complexity seen by the primary controller.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Full Module Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a Cascade Control system. Identify the roles of the primary and secondary controllers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical reactor temperature (primary PV) is controlled by manipulating the setpoint of a steam-flow controller (secondary PV). The transfer functions are:

- Valve: $G_v(s) = 0.8$
- Secondary process (flow): $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{3}{2s+1}$
- Primary process (temperature): $G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{1}{8s+1}$
- Secondary controller: $G_{c2}(s) = 4$
- Primary controller: $G_{c1}(s) = 1.5\left(1+\dfrac{1}{6s}\right)$

Derive the closed-loop transfer function of the secondary loop, and hence find the overall characteristic equation of the cascade system. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**

The cascade system consists of two nested feedback loops:

1. The **outer (primary) loop** has setpoint $SP_1$, primary PV $PV_1$, primary controller $G_{c1}$, and primary process $G_{p1}$.
2. The **inner (secondary) loop** has its setpoint $SP_2$ fed by the output of $G_{c1}$. It contains the secondary PV $PV_2$, the secondary controller $G_{c2}$, the final control element $G_v$, and the secondary process $G_{p2}$.
3. The signal from $G_{c1}$ is **NOT** the valve signal — it is the *desired value of $PV_2$*.

**Roles (4 marks):**

- **Primary controller ($G_{c1}$):** Maintains the main controlled variable ($PV_1$, e.g., reactor temperature). Usually a PI/PID with integral action. Tuning is **slow and conservative**.
- **Secondary controller ($G_{c2}$):** Maintains the secondary variable ($PV_2$, e.g., steam flow) at the moving setpoint issued by $G_{c1}$. Tuning is **fast and aggressive**, often P-only.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Secondary loop numerator:**

$$
G_{c2}(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s) = 4 \times 0.8 \times \frac{3}{2s+1} = \frac{9.6}{2s+1}
$$

**Step 2 — Secondary loop denominator:**

$$
1 + \frac{9.6}{2s+1} = \frac{2s+1+9.6}{2s+1} = \frac{2s+10.6}{2s+1}
$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop TF of inner loop:**

$$
G_{inner}(s) = \frac{9.6}{2s+10.6} = \frac{9.6/10.6}{(2/10.6)s+1} = \frac{0.9057}{0.1887s+1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $G_{c1}(s)\,G_{inner}(s)\,G_{p1}(s)$:**

$$
G_{c1}(s)\,G_{inner}(s)\,G_{p1}(s) = 1.5\left(1+\frac{1}{6s}\right) \cdot \frac{9.6}{2s+10.6} \cdot \frac{1}{8s+1}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.5(6s+1)}{6s} \cdot \frac{9.6}{2s+10.6} \cdot \frac{1}{8s+1}
$$

$$
= \frac{14.4(6s+1)}{6s(2s+10.6)(8s+1)}
$$

**Step 5 — Overall characteristic equation $1 + G_{c1}G_{inner}G_{p1} = 0$:**

$$
\boxed{\,6s(2s+10.6)(8s+1) + 14.4(6s+1) = 0\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Expand (optional — for stability analysis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(2s+10.6)(8s+1) &= 16s^2 + 2s + 84.8s + 10.6 \\
&= 16s^2 + 86.8s + 10.6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
6s(16s^2 + 86.8s + 10.6) + 14.4(6s+1) = 0
$$

$$
96s^3 + 520.8s^2 + 63.6s + 86.4s + 14.4 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,96s^3 + 520.8s^2 + 150s + 14.4 = 0\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Routh Stability check (high-yield for 14-mark answers):**

| $s^3$ | 96 | 150 |
|-------|----|-----|
| $s^2$ | 520.8 | 14.4 |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{520.8 \times 150 - 96 \times 14.4}{520.8} = 150.69$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | 14.4 | |

All first-column entries are **positive → System is Stable**. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare single-loop control with cascade control. List any four selection criteria for choosing a cascade control configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a furnace temperature control loop, the cascade structure uses a flow controller in the inner loop. Given:
- $G_v = 1.0$
- $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+1}$
- $G_{c2}(s) = K_{c2}$ (proportional)
- $G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{2}{10s+1}$
- $G_{c1}(s) = 0.8\left(1+\dfrac{1}{4s}\right)$

Find the value of $K_{c2}$ such that the inner-loop time constant is **less than or equal to 0.1 s**, ensuring it is at least 10 times faster than the primary time constant ($\tau_{p1}=10$ s). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Loop | Cascade |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Number of feedback loops | 1 | 2 (nested) |
| Disturbance rejection | Only outer PV can correct | Inner PV corrects secondary disturbances |
| Tuning complexity | One controller | Two controllers, sequence-dependent |
| Speed of response | Limited by full process dynamics | Inner loop accelerates the overall response |
| Implementation cost | Low (one sensor, one valve) | Higher (additional sensor for PV2) |
| Robustness to actuator non-linearity | Poor | High (inner loop linearizes) |

**Selection criteria for cascade control (3 marks):**
1. A measurable secondary variable exists that *directly* relates to the final control element's action.
2. The secondary process is significantly **faster** (smaller time constant) than the primary process.
3. The primary process suffers from significant disturbances that enter the **secondary loop**.
4. The final control element has **non-linear behaviour** (e.g., valve hysteresis).
5. The primary measurement is **delayed or noisy** and benefits from pre-conditioning.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Write the inner-loop closed-loop TF:**

$$
G_{inner}(s) = \frac{K_{c2} \cdot 1 \cdot \dfrac{5}{s+1}}{1 + K_{c2} \cdot 1 \cdot \dfrac{5}{s+1}} = \frac{5K_{c2}}{s+1+5K_{c2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify inner-loop time constant:**

The denominator is $(s + (1 + 5K_{c2}))$, giving an effective time constant:

$$
\tau_{inner} = \frac{1}{1 + 5K_{c2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the design constraint $\tau_{inner} \le 0.1$ s:**

$$
\frac{1}{1 + 5K_{c2}} \le 0.1
$$

$$
1 + 5K_{c2} \ge 10
$$

$$
5K_{c2} \ge 9
$$

$$
\boxed{\,K_{c2} \ge 1.8\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify ratio of time constants:**

With $K_{c2} = 1.8$: $\tau_{inner} = 0.1$ s; $\tau_{p1} = 10$ s; Ratio = 100 → Inner loop is **100× faster**, satisfying the cascade criterion.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mark Loss #1:** Many students confuse the *output of the primary controller* with the *valve signal*. The cascade output is a **setpoint**, not a manipulated position.
> - **Mark Loss #2:** Omitting the Routh array check in stability questions — KTU award 2 marks specifically for the array.
> - **Mark Loss #3:** Forgetting the factor $(1 + G_{c2}G_vG_{p2})$ when comparing cascade vs single-loop disturbance rejection.
> - **Mark Loss #4:** Writing "$\vert 1 + G_{c2}G_vG_{p2} \vert$" inside a markdown table (breaks the table parser). Always write `\vert 1 + G_{c2}G_vG_{p2} \vert` in LaTeX form.
> - **Mark Loss #5:** Drawing a single-loop diagram labelled as cascade — instant **0/7** in part (a).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist — Cascade Control**
> 
> - **Definition:** Two nested feedback loops where the primary output is the secondary setpoint.
> - **Inner loop** = fast, often P-only, tuned first.
> - **Outer loop** = slow, PI/PID, tuned second.
> - **Mandatory condition:** $\tau_{p2} \le \dfrac{\tau_{p1}}{3}$ (preferably $\le \dfrac{\tau_{p1}}{10}$).
> - **Tuning sequence:** Inner-outside-in (tune G_c2 → close inner loop → tune G_c1 → recheck G_c2).
> - **Disturbance rejection improvement factor:** $\vert 1 + G_{c2}G_vG_{p2} \vert$.
> - **Implementation:** In PLC/DCS via PID blocks + Remote-SP (RSP) signal routing; in DCS via standard cascade templates.
> - **Common applications:** Temperature ↔ flow, level ↔ flow, composition ↔ flow, pressure ↔ flow.
> - **Key risks:** Inner loop oscillation due to aggressive $K_{c2}$; integral windup in G_c1 if secondary saturates (use **anti-windup / external reset feedback**).
> - **Exam mantra:** Always draw the *two-loop* block diagram; never confuse cascade with ratio, override, or split-range control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ratio Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ratio Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ratio Control** is a specialized multi-loop process control strategy in which the manipulated variable of a *secondary* (dependent) loop is automatically and continuously adjusted to maintain a precise proportional relationship — known as the **Ratio Constant (K)** — between two process variables, most commonly two flow rates. Formally, the control objective is to ensure that:

$$Q_2(t) = K \cdot Q_1(t)$$

where $Q_1$ is the **Wild (Independent) Stream** and $Q_2$ is the **Controlled (Dependent) Stream**, and $K$ is the dimensionless ratio setpoint. This is implemented through a dedicated computing relay called a **Ratio Station (RS)** within the Distributed Control System (DCS) function block library.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Ratio control is a foundational multi-variable control strategy studied under "Control Strategies in DCS." Mastery of its two architectural variants (Type I and Type II) is a frequent ESE question pattern.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are making **chai (tea)** for a large gathering. The recipe demands exactly **1 part tea to 4 parts milk and water combined**. If a guest pours a sudden extra cup of milk into the pan, you *automatically* adjust the tea leaves to keep the proportions intact. You do not measure the absolute amount of tea; you measure the **ratio** of tea to the incoming milk stream.

In industrial terms:
- The **milk stream pouring in** = Wild stream ($Q_1$)
- The **tea leaves you add** = Dependent stream ($Q_2$)
- The **recipe ratio of 1:4** = Ratio Constant ($K$)
- Your **brain making the decision** = The Ratio Station + Flow Controller

This is precisely how **fuel-to-air combustion control**, **acid-base neutralization blending**, and **refinery stream mixing** operations are managed.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Engineering Notation | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ratio Constant | $K$ | **0.1 to 10.0** (dimensionless) |
| Wild Stream Flow | $Q_1$ | Variable, externally disturbed |
| Dependent Stream Flow | $Q_2$ | Auto-adjusted by controller |
| Loop Response Time | $\tau$ | **2 to 30 seconds** |
| DCS Signal Range | $P$ (pressure) | **3 to 15 psi** or **4 to 20 mA** |

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio $K$ is theoretically dimensionless but practically carries the same engineering units on both numerator and denominator (e.g., kg/kg, m³/h per m³/h), so units algebraically cancel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Need for Ratio Control

In many continuous processes, the **quality** of the final product depends not on the absolute quantity of two ingredients, but on the **proportional balance** between them. Allowing one stream to vary independently of the other can lead to:
- **Off-spec product** (wrong chemical composition)
- **Unsafe conditions** (e.g., fuel-rich combustion → explosion)
- **Wasted raw materials** and economic loss
- **Catalyst poisoning** in reactors requiring strict stoichiometry

## 2.2 The "How" — Architectural Logic Steps

The implementation of any ratio control system follows these structured logic steps:

1. **Stream Identification:** The process engineer designates one stream as *Wild* ($Q_1$, often set by upstream plant demand) and the other as *Dependent* ($Q_2$).
2. **Flow Measurement:** Both streams are measured using differential-pressure or Coriolis flow transmitters that output a normalized pneumatic/electronic signal ($P_1$ and $P_2$).
3. **Ratio Computation:** A computing relay (the Ratio Station) performs real-time multiplication: $P_{out} = K \cdot P_1$.
4. **Setpoint Generation:** This computed signal becomes the *remote setpoint* (RSP) of the secondary flow controller (FC2).
5. **Servo Action:** FC2 modulates a control valve to drive $Q_2$ until $P_2$ matches the dynamically changing $P_{out}$.
6. **Ratio Maintenance:** At steady state, $P_2 = K \cdot P_1$, which implies $Q_2 / Q_1 = K$ (assuming matched transmitter calibrations).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Engineering Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ratio Definition | $K = \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1}$ | Proportional setpoint | Blending, combustion |
| Wild Stream Signal | $P_1 = c_1 \cdot Q_1$ | Linearized transmitter output | Flow loop 1 |
| Ratio Station Output | $P_{RS} = K \cdot P_1$ | Computed setpoint for FC2 | DCS function block |
| Secondary Loop | $P_2 = P_{RS} = K \cdot P_1$ | Servo-tracking condition | Closed-loop condition |
| Actual Ratio Achieved | $K_{actual} = \dfrac{c_1 \cdot K}{c_2}$ | Including transmitter gains | Calibration check |
| Type II Equation | $P_{RS} = \dfrac{K}{1+K} \cdot P_{tot}$ | Equal-percentage split | Parallel flow split |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** The variables $c_1$ and $c_2$ are the **calibration constants** of the two flow transmitters. For the ratio $K_{actual}$ to equal the setpoint $K$, the transmitters **must be range-matched** ($c_1 = c_2$). A common exam trap is asking what happens if these are mismatched.

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

Ratio control is the workhorse of the **petrochemical**, **pharmaceutical**, and **power generation** industries. Some prominent applications include:

- **Refinery Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC):** Maintaining the catalyst-to-oil ratio for optimal cracking yield.
