## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any six basic building blocks of a computer-controlled system and state the function of each in one line.*

**Model Answer (6 points, ½ mark each):**
1. **Process/Plant** – The physical system whose variable is to be controlled.
2. **Sensor/Transmitter** – Converts the physical process variable into a standardized electrical signal (typically 4–20 mA).
3. **Signal Conditioner** – Amplifies, filters, and isolates the transducer output to match the A/D input range.
4. **A/D Converter** – Samples and quantizes the analog signal into a digital code for the CPU.
5. **Digital Computer/CPU** – Executes the control algorithm (e.g., PID) and supervises the loop.
6. **D/A Converter with Zero-Order Hold** – Converts the digital control output back into a piecewise-constant analog signal.
7. **Actuator/Final Control Element** – Modifies the process in response to the control signal.
8. **HMI** – Provides operator visibility and command interface.
9. **Communication Network** – Enables data exchange between distributed subsystems.

*(Any six well-explained lines fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is the 4–20 mA current loop preferred over a 0–10 V voltage signal in industrial instrumentation? Mention three reasons.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Noise immunity** – Current signals are unaffected by voltage drops across long cable runs and induced electromagnetic noise. *(1 mark)*
2. **Live-zero fault detection** – The non-zero lower limit of 4 mA allows immediate detection of a wire break, sensor failure, or open circuit (any reading < 3.8 mA flags a fault). *(1 mark)*
3. **Intrinsic safety** – Limited current and the live-zero allow safe operation in hazardous (Ex-rated) areas. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For Module 1 (Computerized Control) answers, students routinely lose marks for: (1) **forgetting to mention the Zero-Order Hold** after the D/A block — a 2-mark penalty is common; (2) writing the PID in *analog* form when the question asks for the *digital* form (a 3–4 mark penalty); (3) omitting the **live-zero advantage of 4–20 mA** when asked about industrial signal standards; and (4) drawing block diagrams without arrows showing the *direction* of signal flow.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **basic building blocks of a computer-controlled system** and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **mathematical relation between the 4–20 mA current signal and the process variable** for a temperature transmitter with range **0 °C to 200 °C**. What current corresponds to a measured temperature of **125 °C**? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The block diagram of a computer-controlled system comprises the following functional units, arranged to close the feedback loop digitally:

1. **Process/Plant** – The physical system (e.g., furnace, tank, chemical reactor) whose variable is being regulated. The variable of interest is the *controlled variable* $y(t)$.

2. **Sensor / Transducer** – A primary element (RTD, thermocouple, strain gauge, orifice plate) that senses the physical state of the process.

3. **Transmitter / Signal Conditioner** – Standardizes the transducer output to the industrial 4–20 mA range, amplifies weak signals, and filters high-frequency noise.

4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** – A low-pass filter (cutoff $f_c \le f_s/2$) that prevents high-frequency content from folding back into the sampled signal.

5. **A/D Converter** – Performs two operations: **sampling** (discretizing time at $t = kT_s$) and **quantization** (discretizing amplitude into $2^n$ levels).

6. **Digital Computer (CPU)** – Executes the chosen control law (e.g., PID), runs alarms, and stores data. In a DCS, this is the *Control Processor*.

7. **D/A Converter with Zero-Order Hold** – Holds each digital output word constant for the duration of one sampling period, reconstructing a piecewise-constant analog signal.

8. **Actuator / Final Control Element** – A control valve, variable-speed drive, or heater that physically influences the process.

9. **HMI / Operator Station** – Displays trends, alarms, and accepts operator commands (setpoint, mode, tuning).

10. **Communication Network** – Fieldbus / Industrial Ethernet links the controller with I/O, peers, and higher-level systems (SCADA, ERP).

A block diagram is drawn (refer to **Section 4.1**). The signal flows: **Plant → Sensor → Conditioner → A/D → CPU → D/A → Actuator → Plant** (forward path), with the HMI and Communication Network acting as parallel supervisory layers.

*(Marks: 1 mark for the diagram, 6 × 1 mark for explaining six key blocks.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Derivation:** A linear relation $I = a \cdot PV + b$ is assumed.

Apply boundary conditions:
- $PV = 0$ °C $\Rightarrow I = 4$ mA
- $PV = 200$ °C $\Rightarrow I = 20$ mA

Solving gives $a = 16/200 = 0.08$ mA/°C and $b = 4$ mA.

$$\boxed{\,I(\text{mA}) = 4 + 0.08 \cdot PV(^{\circ}\text{C})\,}$$

**Numerical evaluation at $PV = 125$ °C:**

$$I = 4 + 0.08 \times 125 = 4 + 10 = 14 \text{ mA}$$

**Verification:** 125 °C is 62.5 % of the 200 °C span. The current span is 16 mA, so 62.5 % × 16 = 10 mA, and 4 + 10 = 14 mA. ✓

*(Marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for substitution, 2 for correct numerical answer with units.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the **sampling process** in a computer-controlled system. State and explain the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, and compute the minimum sampling rate for a signal with maximum frequency component of **75 Hz**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A computer-controlled temperature loop uses a **12-bit A/D converter** with a full-scale input voltage of **10 V**. Calculate the **(i)** quantization resolution (LSB size) and **(ii)** the theoretical signal-to-noise ratio. Comment on the suitability of this resolution for an industrial furnace control application. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Sampling process:** The continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is measured at discrete instants $t = kT_s$, producing a sequence of samples $x[k] = x(kT_s)$. The interval $T_s$ is the *sampling period* and $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the *sampling frequency*. The Analog-to-Digital Converter is the hardware block that physically performs this discretization.

**Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited signal containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be uniquely and completely reconstructed from its samples **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

The minimum acceptable rate $f_{N} = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**. Sampling below this rate produces *aliasing* — high-frequency components fold into the baseband and become indistinguishable from genuine low-frequency content.

**Numerical computation:** With $f_{max} = 75$ Hz:

$$f_{s,min} = 2 \times 75 = 150 \text{ Hz} \quad ; \quad T_{s,max} = \frac{1}{150} \approx 6.67 \text{ ms}$$

In practice, an **oversampling factor of 5–10** is recommended, giving $f_s \approx 750$ Hz and $T_s \approx 1.33$ ms.

*(Marks: 2 for explanation of sampling, 3 for theorem statement & meaning, 2 for numerical result.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**(i) Quantization Resolution (LSB size):**

The number of discrete levels for a 12-bit converter is:

$$N = 2^{12} = 4096$$

The LSB voltage (resolution) is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.441 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio:**

For an ideal A/D converter, the SNR is:

$$SNR = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Comment on suitability:** A resolution of **2.441 mV** on a 10 V span corresponds to **0.024 %** of full scale — more than adequate for a typical industrial furnace (where temperature spans are wide, e.g., 0–1000 °C, giving a per-bit temperature of ~0.24 °C). The SNR of 74 dB exceeds the dynamic range of common industrial thermocouples and RTDs. Hence, a 12-bit A/D is **fully suitable** for furnace control; **16-bit** converters are reserved for high-precision applications such as analytical instruments or strain-gauge-based load cells.

*(Marks: 2 + 2 for the two computations, 3 for the engineering comment.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 A **Computer-Controlled System** is a closed loop with a **digital computer** inside the feedback path.
- 🔹 The canonical **9 (or 10) building blocks** are: **Plant → Sensor → Transmitter → Signal Conditioner → Anti-Aliasing Filter → A/D → CPU → D/A + ZOH → Actuator**, plus **HMI** and **Communication Network**.
- 🔹 **4–20 mA is the industrial standard** — it is current-based, noise-immune, intrinsically safe, and supports **live-zero fault detection**.
- 🔹 **Nyquist theorem** mandates $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$; failure causes **aliasing**.
- 🔹 **A/D resolution** $= V_{FS}/2^n$; **SNR** $= 6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- 🔹 **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is mandatory after the D/A — its transfer function is $(1-e^{-sT_s})/s$.
- 🔹 **Position-form PID** computes absolute $u[k]$; **velocity-form PID** computes $\Delta u[k]$ and is preferred in PLC/DCS for **bumpless transfer**.
- 🔹 **DCS hierarchy**: Level 0 (Plant) → Level 1 (Field) → Level 2 (Control: PLC/DCS) → Level 3 (SCADA) → Level 4 (ERP).
- 🔹 **Rule of thumb**: control cycle time ≪ dominant process time constant (typically $\le 10\% \cdot \tau$).
- 🔹 Always show the **direction of signal flow** with arrows in any block diagram; examiners deduct marks for undirected lines.
- 🔹 When asked for the "digital" form of a controller, **never** write the analog differential equation — write the difference equation.
- 🔹 For **live-zero calculations**, memorize the linear conversion $I = 4 + 16 \cdot (PV - PV_{min})/(PV_{max} - PV_{min})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable Logic Controller: Architecture of PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Programmable Logic Controller: Architecture of PLC ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** > A **PLC** is defined by the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)** as a *digitally operating electronic apparatus which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of instructions for implementing specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations* to control, through digital or analog input/output modules, various types of machines or processes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **IEC 61131-1 Definition:** A PLC is a *digitally operating, electronic, programmable, user-oriented system designed for use in industrial environments* that executes logical, sequential, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations through input/output (I/O) interfaces to control machines and processes. In simple words, a PLC is a **ruggedized industrial mini-computer** built specifically to survive harsh factory environments — heat, dust, vibration, and electrical noise — while replacing the old hard-wired relay logic panels that used to control manufacturing equipment. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Human Brain of a Machine"** > Think of a PLC as the **brain of a washing machine** — but a very smart one used in a factory. > > - **Sensors (eyes/ears)** = Push buttons, limit switches, temperature sensors feed information to the PLC. > - **Brain (CPU)** = Processes "IF temperature > 80°C THEN turn OFF heater" rules. > - **Muscles (Outputs)** = Motors, solenoid valves, indicator lamps are switched ON/OFF based on the brain's decision. > - **Memory (Notebook)** = Stores the user-written program and intermediate data. > > Unlike a normal PC, a PLC is designed to run 24×7 for **10+ years** in a factory with no fans, no hard disk, and **no crashes**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU-Relevant Metrics of a PLC:** > - **Scan Time:** Typically **1–25 ms** per cycle (must be ≤ process requirement). > - **I/O Capacity:** Ranges from **32 I/O** (micro PLC) to **over 8000 I/O** (large modular PLCs). > - **Operating Temperature:** **0 °C to 55 °C** (industrial grade). > - **Memory Types:** Volatile (RAM) and Non-volatile (ROM, EEPROM, Flash). > - **Power Supply:** Typically **24 V DC** (sensor side) and **120/230 V AC** (load side). ### 1.4 Why PLCs? — Evolution Timeline | Era | Technology | Limitations | |---|---|---| | Pre-1960s | Electromechanical Relays | Bulky, rewiring nightmare, hard troubleshooting | | **1968** | **First PLC — Bedford Associates (Modicon 084)** | Solved automotive plant rewiring problem | | 1970s | Ladder Logic + Boolean instructions | Limited math | | 1980s | Analog I/O, PID, Networking | Emerging standard | | **Today** | IEC 61131-3 languages, Ethernet, Fieldbus | Multi-language, IoT-ready | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** The question "Define PLC" and "Draw the block diagram" are **frequently repeated 7-mark and 14-mark questions** in OERUT722. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Architecture of a PLC — Block Diagram Breakdown A PLC is structurally a **modular system** with five fundamental building blocks. Every commercial PLC (Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Schneider Modicon, Mitsubishi MELSEC) follows this architecture. ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ PROGRAMMING DEVICE │ │ (PC / Handheld HMI / Console — Offline) │ └──────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘ │ Upload/Download ▼ ┌────────────┐ ┌─────────────────────┐ ┌────────────┐ │ INPUT │ │ CPU │ │ OUTPUT │ │ MODULES │◄─┤ ┌───────────────┐ ├─►│ MODULES │ │ (DI / AI) │ │ │ Processor │ │ │ (DO / AO) │ └─────┬──────┘ │ ├───────────────┤ │ └──────┬─────┘ │ │ │ Memory (RAM/ │ │ │ │ │ │ ROM/Flash) │ │ │ │ │ └───────────────┘ │ │ │ └──────────┬──────────┘ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ POWER SUPPLY UNIT (PSU) │ │ (110/230V AC → 5V, 24V DC isolated) │ └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.2 Detailed Subsystem Analysis #### A. Central Processing Unit (CPU) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **CPU** is the heart of the PLC. It contains a **microprocessor** (often 16-bit or 32-bit — e.g., Intel 80C186, ARM Cortex), an **arithmetic logic unit (ALU)**, and a **system bus controller**. **Functions of CPU:** 1. Reads the **ladder logic program** from memory. 2. Executes the program **line-by-line** (or rung-by-rung). 3. Performs **boolean, arithmetic, and data transfer** operations. 4. Manages **scan timing**, interrupts, and error diagnostics. 5. Communicates with the programming device and HMI. #### B. Memory Architecture Memory is **physically partitioned** into distinct regions — a high-yield KTU topic. | Memory Area | Volatility | Content | Typical Size | |---|---|---|---| | **System Memory (ROM / Flash)** | Non-volatile | Operating system, firmware | 256 KB – 8 MB | | **User Program Memory (RAM/Flash)** | Retentive | Ladder / FBD / ST user program | 64 KB – 16 MB | | **Data Memory (RAM)** | Volatile (battery-backed) | Process variables, timers, counters | 4 KB – 1 MB | | **I/O Image Tables** | Volatile | Snapshot of inputs and outputs | Fixed by I/O count | > [!NOTE] > **Retentive Memory:** Critical data is held in **lithium-backed CMOS RAM** so it survives power loss — used for **counters, timers, and process recipes**. #### C. Input / Output (I/O) Section This is the **interface between the digital/electronic world inside the PLC and the noisy industrial world outside**. **Discrete (Digital) I/O Modules:** - **Digital Input (DI):** 24 V DC, 120 V AC, 230 V AC modules. Examples: Push buttons, limit switches, proximity sensors, photo-electric sensors. - **Digital Output (DO):** Relay (2 A), Transistor (0.5 A, fast switching), Triac (for AC loads). Examples: Solenoid valves, motor contactors, indicator lamps. **Analog I/O Modules:** - **Analog Input (AI):** 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, ±10 V, RTD, Thermocouple. Examples: Temperature, pressure, flow, level transmitters. - **Analog Output (AO):** 4–20 mA, 0–10 V. Examples: Variable speed drives, control valves. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Opto-Isolation:** Every I/O module contains **opto-isolators (optocouplers)** to electrically separate the field side (high voltage) from the logic side (5 V) — protects the CPU from surges up to **1500 V RMS**. #### D. Power Supply Unit (PSU) > [!NOTE] > The PSU is NOT just a transformer. It must be a **regulated, isolated, short-circuit protected DC power supply** with **EMI/RFI filtering** for industrial noise immunity. - **Input:** 120/230 V AC, 50/60 Hz. - **Output rails:** **+5 V** (logic), **+24 V** (sensor power), and isolated rails for analog. - **Hold-up time:** Typically **20–30 ms** during power dips to complete the scan. #### E. Programming Device Originally a dedicated handheld console (Allen-Bradley T3, Siemens PG); today a **laptop/PC** with vendor software (RSLogix 5000, TIA Portal, SoMachine). ### 2.3 The PLC Scan Cycle — Operating Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scan Time** is the single most important KTU concept. It is the total time the CPU takes to **read inputs, execute the program, update outputs, and perform housekeeping**. $$T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$$ Where: - $T_{input}$ = time to scan all input image table (e.g., 0.5 ms) - $T_{program}$ = time to execute one complete ladder scan (depends on program length; typically 1–10 ms per 1 K instructions) - $T_{output}$ = time to refresh output image table to physical outputs - $T_{housekeeping}$ = diagnostics, communication servicing (e.g., 2–5 ms) > [!NOTE] > **Critical Rule:** The total scan time **must be less than** the smallest process time constant. If a temperature loop has a time constant of 2 s, a 50 ms scan is acceptable. For high-speed packaging, scans of **1 ms or less** are required. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Scan Time | $T_{scan}=T_{in}+T_{prog}+T_{out}+T_{house}$ | ms (milliseconds) | Process control responsiveness | | 2 | Program execution time | $T_{prog} = N \times t_{instr}$ | ms | $N$ = # instructions, $t_{instr}$ = µs/instruction | | 3 | I/O count | $N_{I/O}=N_{DI}+N_{DO}+N_{AI}+N_{AO}$ | Channels | Sizing the PLC | | 4 | Resolution of Analog I/O | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | V or mA | $n$ = ADC bit depth (typically 12, 14, 16) | | 5 | Memory size estimate | $M_{prog} = N_{instr} \times b_{instr}$ | Bytes | $b_{instr}$ = avg. bytes/instruction | | 6 | Throughput | $\text{I/O throughput} = \dfrac{N_{I/O}}{T_{scan}}$ | channels/s | Bus capacity check | | 7 | Opto-isolation voltage | $V_{iso} \geq 1500$ | V RMS | Surge protection | | 8 | Operating temperature | 0 to 55 | °C | Industrial environment | | 9 | Power supply rails | +5 V, +24 V, isolated analog | V DC | Standard PLC voltages | | 10 | Communication baud | 9.6 kbps – 100 Mbps | bps | Ethernet/IP, Profinet, Modbus | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Applications of PLC Architecture Knowledge:** > - **Automotive Assembly:** Body welding robots sequenced by PLCs. > - **Power Plants:** Boiler drum level and turbine sequencing. > - **Water Treatment:** Chemical dosing and pump alternation. > - **Food & Beverage:** Bottle-filling, capping, labelling lines. > - **Oil & Gas:** SCADA-integrated PLCs for wellhead control. Understanding the architecture allows a control engineer to: 1. **Right-size** the PLC (I/O count, memory, scan rate). 2. **Diagnose** hardware faults (blown fuse in DI module, CPU stop, watchdog trip). 3. **Design noise-immune** wiring (separation of 24 V DC and 230 V AC, shielded cables for analog). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Total Scan Time **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A PLC has the following execution parameters per scan: - Input scan: $T_{in} = 0.8$ ms - Program execution: $T_{prog} = 4.5$ ms - Output scan: $T_{out} = 0.4$ ms - Housekeeping + communication: $T_{house} = 2.3$ ms Calculate the **total scan time** and determine whether the PLC is suitable for a process with a critical time constant of **15 ms**. #### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Write the governing equation. $$ T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values. $$ T_{scan} = 0.8\ \text{ms} + 4.5\ \text{ms} + 0.4\ \text{ms} + 2.3\ \text{ms} $$ **Step 3:** Add the terms sequentially. $$ T_{scan} = (0.8 + 4.5)\ \text{ms} + (0.4 + 2.3)\ \text{ms} $$ $$ T_{scan} = 5.3\ \text{ms} + 2.7\ \text{ms} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{scan} = 8.0\ \text{ms}} $$ **Step 4:** Decision rule. $$ T_{scan} \stackrel{?}{\leq} \tau_{process} $$ $$ 8.0\ \text{ms} \leq 15\ \text{ms} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The PLC is suitable. The scan time consumes ~53% of the available process time, leaving a comfortable margin of **7 ms** for I/O processing and any expansion. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Program Execution Time **Problem Statement:** A PLC executes instructions at an average rate of **0.6 µs per instruction**. The user program contains **3500 ladder instructions** (rungs). Calculate the **program execution time** in ms. #### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Governing equation. $$ T_{prog} = N_{instr} \times t_{instr} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute values. $$ T_{prog} = 3500 \times 0.6\ \mu s $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate. $$ T_{prog} = 2100\ \mu s $$ **Step 4:** Convert to ms. $$ T_{prog} = 2.1\ \text{ms} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{prog} = 2.1\ \text{ms}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Analog Input Resolution **Problem Statement:** A **0–10 V analog input module** uses a **14-bit ADC**. Calculate the **voltage resolution** $\Delta V$ per LSB. #### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Formula. $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $n = 14$. $$ \Delta V = \frac{10}{2^{14}} $$ **Step 3:** Compute denominator. $$ 2^{14} = 16384 $$ **Step 4:** Final value. $$ \Delta V = \frac{10}{16384} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta V \approx 0.610\ \text{mV per LSB}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This resolution means a process variable spanning **0–10 V** can be digitized into **16 384 discrete levels** — adequate for most industrial process loops (temperature, pressure) but would require a **16-bit ADC** for high-precision weighing or analytical instrumentation. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of I/O Module Types | Module | Signal Type | Example Field Device | Isolation | Speed | |---|---|---|---|---| | DC Sinking/Sourcing DI | 24 V DC | Proximity sensor, push button | Opto-isolated | < 1 ms | | AC DI | 110/230 V AC | Limit switch on motor starter | Opto-isolated | < 10 ms | | Relay DO | Dry contact, 2 A | Solenoid valve, lamp | Mechanical isolation | 10–20 ms | | Transistor DO | 24 V DC, 0.5 A | DC motor, LED indicator | Opto-isolated | < 0.5 ms | | Triac DO | 230 V AC, 0.5 A | AC solenoid | Opto-isolated | < 10 ms | | Analog AI (4–20 mA) | Current loop | Pressure transmitter | Galvanic isolation | 16-bit ADC | | Analog AO (4–20 mA) | Current loop | Control valve positioner | Galvanic isolation | 12-bit DAC | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 PLC Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Field Sensors
Push buttons, Limit switches,
RTDs, Pressure Tx] --> B[Input Module
DI / AI + Opto-isolation] B --> C[CPU Core] C --> D[Memory Subsystem
ROM + RAM + Flash] C --> E[Output Module
DO / AO + Opto-isolation] E --> F[Actuators
Motors, Valves, Lamps] G[Programming Device
Laptop / HMI] <--> C H[Power Supply Unit
230V AC to 5V/24V DC] --> B H --> C H --> D H --> E ``` ### 4.2 PLC Scan Cycle — Sequential Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A([Power ON / Start]) --> B[Self-test &
Diagnostics] B --> C[Read All Inputs
into Image Table] C --> D[Execute User Program
Rung-by-Rung] D --> E[Update Outputs
from Image Table] E --> F[Housekeeping
Comm, Watchdog] F --> G{Stop
Request?} G -- No --> C G -- Yes --> H([CPU STOP]) ``` ### 4.3 I/O Addressing Convention (Modular PLC) ```mermaid flowchart TB Rack["Rack 0 / Slot 0 (CPU)"] Slot1["Slot 1: DI Module 16-ch"] Slot2["Slot 2: DO Module 16-ch"] Slot3["Slot 3: AI Module 8-ch"] Slot4["Slot 4: AO Module 4-ch"] Rack --- Slot1 Rack --- Slot2 Rack --- Slot3 Rack --- Slot4 Slot1 --> Addr["Addresses:
I:0/0 to I:0/15"] Slot2 --> Addr2["Addresses:
O:0/0 to O:0/15"] Slot3 --> Addr3["Addresses:
I:0.0 to I:0.7 (Word)"] Slot4 --> Addr4["Addresses:
O:0.0 to O:0.3 (Word)"] ``` ### 4.4 Memory Architecture Map ```mermaid flowchart TB MEM[PLC Memory] MEM --> SYS["System Memory
(ROM / Flash)
Holds: OS, Firmware"] MEM --> USR["User Program Memory
(Flash / Battery-backed RAM)
Holds: Ladder, FBD, SFC"] MEM --> DAT["Data Memory
(RAM, Retentive)
Holds: Timers, Counters,
Process Variables"] MEM --> IO["I/O Image Tables
(RAM, Refreshed every scan)
Holds: Input Table, Output Table"] DAT --> BATT["Lithium Battery
Backup: 5-10 years"] ``` ### 4.5 Signal Flow from Field to CPU and Back ```mermaid flowchart LR FB[Push Button
in Field] -->|24V DC| FILT[Input Filter
RC Debounce] FILT --> OPTO[Opto-isolator] OPTO --> IM[Input Image Table
Bit I:0/0] IM --> CPU[CPU executes Ladder:
XIC I:0/0 OTE O:0/0] CPU --> OM[Output Image Table
Bit O:0/0] OM --> OPTO2[Opto-isolator] OPTO2 --> DRV[Transistor / Relay Driver] DRV -->|24V DC / 230V AC| LOAD[Contactor Coil
in Field] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Define a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) as per NEMA.** **Model Answer:** > According to NEMA, a PLC is a *digitally operating electronic apparatus* that uses a *programmable memory* to internally store user-defined instructions for implementing **logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic** functions, and controls various types of machines and industrial processes through its **digital and analog input/output modules**. *(3 marks — full marks for NEMA reference + 4 key features.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **List any four advantages of PLCs over hard-wired relay control.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Flexibility:** Program modification without rewiring. 2. **Reliability:** Solid-state with no moving parts; long MTBF. 3. **Compact:** One PLC replaces hundreds of relays. 4. **Diagnostics:** Built-in LEDs, fault codes, and remote monitoring. 5. *Communication:* Easily networked with SCADA / HMI. 6. *Cost-effective* for complex sequential logic. *(3 marks — any four advantages.)* --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a PLC**. Label all blocks and describe the function of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **PLC scan cycle** in detail. How is the **total scan time** determined? Why is scan time critical in high-speed processes? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — Block Diagram of PLC Architecture (7 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 above. The textual answer: > A PLC architecture consists of **five major blocks**: > > 1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Contains the microprocessor, ALU, control unit, and system bus interface. It fetches, decodes, and executes user program instructions sequentially. It also handles interrupts, communication, and watchdog operations. > > 2. **Memory Unit:** Divided into System Memory (ROM/Flash — contains OS), User Program Memory (Flash/EEPROM — contains ladder logic), Data Memory (RAM, battery-backed — holds timers, counters, process variables), and I/O Image Tables (RAM — refreshed every scan). > > 3. **Input Module:** Receives signals from field sensors (push buttons, limit switches, RTDs, transmitters). Provides **opto-isolation**, signal conditioning, and converts field signals (24 V DC, 230 V AC, 4–20 mA, 0–10 V) to logic-level voltages for the CPU. Analog inputs use 12–16-bit ADCs. > > 4. **Output Module:** Receives logic signals from CPU and drives field actuators (contactors, solenoid valves, VFDs). Available in **relay, transistor, and triac** variants for digital outputs, and **4–20 mA / 0–10 V** DAC-based for analog outputs. Provides opto-isolation and short-circuit protection. > > 5. **Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Converts **110/230 V AC mains to regulated +5 V (logic) and +24 V (sensor) DC rails** with isolation, EMI filtering, and short-circuit protection. Hold-up time of 20–30 ms ensures scan completion during brownouts. > > 6. **Programming Device:** Modern laptop/PC with vendor software (e.g., TIA Portal, RSLogix) used to write, upload, download, and debug the ladder/FBD/ST programs. > > *(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 5 for description.)* --- **Model Solution (b) — PLC Scan Cycle (7 marks):** > The PLC operates in a **continuous, cyclic scan mode** consisting of four phases: > > **Phase 1 — Input Scan (Read):** The CPU reads the **status of all physical inputs** (limit switches, sensors) into the **Input Image Table** in RAM. This happens **once per scan**, regardless of how many times the program refers to that input. > > **Phase 2 — Program Scan (Execute):** The CPU executes the user program **rung-by-rung** (or instruction-by-instruction) using the values from the Input Image Table. **Outputs are NOT updated immediately** to the physical world; they are first written to the **Output Image Table**. > > **Phase 3 — Output Scan (Write):** The contents of the **Output Image Table** are transferred to the **physical output modules** simultaneously (latch and driver circuits energize contactors, lamps, etc.). > > **Phase 4 — Housekeeping:** Includes communication servicing (HMI, SCADA), diagnostic checks, watchdog reset, and timer/counter updates. > > **Total Scan Time Equation:** > > $$T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$$ > > **Why critical in high-speed processes:** > - The input signal may change between scans and be **missed** if scan time > pulse width. > - Process variables may have **time constants** smaller than $T_{scan}$, causing instability. > - For **packaging machines, safety interlocks, and motion control**, scan times of **1–5 ms** are essential. > - Worst case: $T_{scan}$ must be $\leq 0.1 \times \tau_{process}$ for stable control. > > *(7 marks — 3 for cycle phases, 2 for formula, 2 for criticality.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper 2]** **(a)** Explain the **types of I/O modules** used in a PLC with examples. What is the role of **opto-isolation**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PLC program has **2500 ladder instructions**, each taking **0.4 µs** to execute. Input scan = 0.5 ms, output scan = 0.3 ms, housekeeping = 1.5 ms. Calculate the **total scan time**. Is it suitable for a process with $\tau = 12$ ms? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — Types of I/O Modules (7 marks):** > **1. Discrete (Digital) Input Modules:** > Accept binary ON/OFF signals. Available in **24 V DC, 110 V AC, and 230 V AC** versions. Use **opto-isolators** to convert the field voltage to logic level. Examples of field devices: **push buttons, limit switches, proximity sensors, selector switches, photo-electric sensors**. > > **2. Discrete (Digital) Output Modules:** > Switch field loads ON/OFF. > - **Relay output:** Dry contacts rated up to 2 A; slow (10–20 ms) but universal for AC/DC loads. > - **Transistor output:** 24 V DC, 0.5 A, fast switching (< 0.5 ms), long life. > - **Triac output:** For 230 V AC loads, 0.5 A. > Examples of field devices: **contactor coils, solenoid valves, indicator lamps, relays**. > > **3. Analog Input Modules (AI):** > Accept continuously variable signals: **4–20 mA, 0–10 V, ±10 V, RTD, thermocouple**. Use a **multiplexer + ADC** (12, 14, or 16-bit) to convert to digital counts. Examples: **temperature, pressure, flow, level transmitters**. > > **4. Analog Output Modules (AO):** > Provide continuously variable signals (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) using a **DAC**. Examples: **variable frequency drives (VFDs), control valve positioners, analog meters**. > > **5. Special I/O Modules:** > - **High-Speed Counters (HSC):** for encoders (> 100 kHz). > - **Motion Control Modules:** for servo drives. > - **Communication Modules:** Profibus, Profinet, Modbus, Ethernet/IP. > > **Role of Opto-Isolation:** > - **Galvanic separation** between field side (often 230 V AC) and logic side (5 V DC). > - Protects the CPU from **voltage transients, surges, and ground loops**. > - Typical isolation voltage: **1500 V RMS** for 1 minute. > - Prevents **EMI/RFI** from the field side corrupting the CPU. --- **Model Solution (b) — Scan Time Calculation (7 marks):** > **Step 1: Program execution time** > > $$T_{prog} = N \times t_{instr}$$ > > $$T_{prog} = 2500 \times 0.4\ \mu s = 1000\ \mu s = 1.0\ \text{ms}$$ > > **Step 2: Total scan time** > > $$T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$$ > > $$T_{scan} = 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.3 + 1.5\ \text{ms}$$ > > $$\boxed{T_{scan} = 3.3\ \text{ms}}$$ > > **Step 3: Suitability check** > > $$T_{scan} \stackrel{?}{\leq} 0.1 \times \tau_{process}$$ > > $$3.3\ \text{ms} \leq 0.1 \times 12\ \text{ms} = 1.2\ \text{ms} \quad \text{??}$$ > > The PLC scan time **exceeds the 10% rule** (3.3 ms > 1.2 ms), so the PLC is **NOT suitable for tight control** of this process. Possible solutions: > - Use a **faster CPU** (shorter instruction time). > - **Optimize the program** — break into subroutines, remove unused rungs. > - Use a **task-based execution** with a high-priority fast task for the critical loop. > > **Verdict:** The PLC is suitable for **monitoring and slow control** but not for fast, high-bandwidth control of a 12 ms time-constant process. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing the scan cycle with the program execution only.** Total scan = 4 phases, not just program scan. (-1 mark) > 2. **Forgetting to label the I/O image tables** in the block diagram. (-0.5 mark) > 3. **Mixing up sinking vs. sourcing** when describing DC input wiring. (-1 mark) > 4. **Using wrong units** in the scan time equation (e.g., µs vs. ms). (-0.5 mark) > 5. **Not mentioning opto-isolation** as a feature of I/O modules. (-1 mark) > 6. **Writing the formula for scan time without explaining its significance** for closed-loop control. (-1 mark) --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Definition:** PLC = industrial, digitally operating, programmable controller per **NEMA / IEC 61131-1**. - ✅ **Five blocks:** CPU, Memory, Input Module, Output Module, PSU (+ Programming Device). - ✅ **Memory types:** ROM (OS), Flash/EEPROM (program), RAM (data, I/O image), Battery-backed (retentive). - ✅ **I/O types:** DI, DO (Relay/Transistor/Triac), AI, AO. Always **opto-isolated**. - ✅ **Scan time equation:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$. - ✅ **Rule of thumb:** $T_{scan} \leq 0.1 \times \tau_{process}$ for stable control. - ✅ **Typical values:** Scan 1–25 ms, I/O 32–8000+, Temp 0–55 °C, Isolation ≥ 1500 V. - ✅ **ADC resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^{n}$ (e.g., 14-bit → ~0.61 mV for 0–10 V). - ✅ **Industrial buses:** Modbus, Profibus, Profinet, Ethernet/IP, DeviceNet. - ✅ **Inventor:** Dick Morley (Bedford Associates, 1968, Modicon 084). - ✅ **Programming languages (IEC 61131-3):** Ladder (LD), FBD, ST, IL, SFC. ---

topics.title: Types of PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of PLC

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a ruggedized, solid-state industrial digital computer specifically designed to control manufacturing processes and automation machinery in harsh industrial environments. It uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of user-oriented instructions that implement specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations to control, through digital or analog input/output modules, various types of machines or processes.

According to **NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) Standard ICS3-1978 (Part ICS3-304)**, a PLC is defined as:
> "A digitally operated electronic apparatus which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of instructions for implementing specific functions, such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic, to control, through digital or analog input/output modules, various types of machines or processes."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of PLCs is the foundational gateway to Module 1. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to *differentiate* between categories based on **size (nano/micro/small/medium/large)**, **architecture (compact vs modular)**, and **output switching element (relay, transistor, triac)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Industrial Brain"

Imagine a modern **automatic washing machine**. It has sensors (water level, temperature, lid open/close), a control panel (knobs/buttons), and actuators (motor, water inlet valve, drain pump, heater). When you select "Wash → 30 min", the internal circuitry orchestrates the motor, water flow, and heater in a precise, timed sequence.

A **PLC** is essentially that internal "brain," but scaled up to factory level:
- **Inputs (Sensors/Switches)** = The senses (water level, temperature).
- **CPU + Memory** = The decision-making brain.
- **Ladder Logic Program** = The pre-written "wash cycle" instructions.
- **Outputs (Contactors/Valves)** = The muscles (motor, heater, pump).
- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** = The control knobs and display.

Just as washing machines come in **semi-automatic, fully-automatic, and commercial sizes**, PLCs also come in different *types* to match the scale and complexity of the industrial job. The classification into "types" simply answers: *"How big is the brain, and how many arms and legs does it have?"*

### 1.3 Why PLCs are Classified into Types

Different industries face different challenges:
- A **traffic signal controller** needs only 8 inputs and 8 outputs → a **compact (brick) PLC** is enough.
- A **petrochemical refinery** needs thousands of I/O points with redundancy and networking → a **modular rack-mounted PLC** is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "type" of PLC is NOT chosen by industry fashion, but by the **I/O count, computational complexity, environmental ruggedness, expandability, and cost** of the target application.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics for PLC Sizing

The following metrics are universally used to classify a PLC:

| Metric | Symbol/Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **I/O Count** | 8 → 8000+ points | Defines physical wiring capacity |
| **Scan Time** | 0.1 ms → 10 ms per KB | Speed of executing ladder logic |
| **Memory Size** | 1 KB → 64 MB+ | Program/data storage |
| **Operating Temp.** | 0 °C to 60 °C (industrial grade) | Environmental tolerance |
| **Communication Protocols** | Modbus, Profibus, Ethernet/IP | Network integration |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Framework

### 2.1 Primary Classification Criteria

KTU Module 1 organizes the "Types of PLC" along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Physical Architecture** → Compact (Brick) vs. Modular (Rack-mounted).
2. **Output Switching Element** → Relay, Transistor (Sinking/Sourcing), Triac.
3. **Application Scale / Size** → Nano, Micro, Small, Medium, Large.

### 2.2 Axis 1: Classification by Physical Architecture

#### A. Compact / Integrated / Brick PLC

- The **CPU, power supply, input module, and output module** are all housed in **one single enclosure**.
- Has a **fixed number of I/O points** (typically 8 to 128).
- May have limited expansion capability via add-on I/O modules.
- **Lower cost**, compact footprint, simple to install.

#### B. Modular / Rack-Mounted PLC

- Composed of **separate plug-in modules** (Power Supply, CPU, Input card, Output card, Communication card) that slide into a common **backplane/rack/chassis**.
- Each rack has multiple **slots**; each slot holds one module.
- I/O can be expanded to **thousands of points** by adding more racks and modules.
- **Higher cost**, flexible, used in complex plants.

#### C. Hybrid / Semi-Modular PLC

- A compromise: a base unit with fixed I/O, but with **expansion slots** or **expansion ports** for additional I/O cards.
- Example: **Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1400**, **Siemens S7-1200**.

### 2.3 Axis 2: Classification by Output Switching Element

This is the most KTU-examined classification because it directly affects the type of load the PLC can drive.

#### A. Relay Output PLC
- Uses **electromechanical relay contacts (SPDT/DPDT)** as the switching element.
- Can switch **AC and DC loads** of moderate current (**typically 2A to 10A per point**).
- **Slow** (mechanical switching, ~10 ms response).
- **No leakage current** when OFF.
- Subject to **contact wear** (mechanical life ~ 100,000 to 1,000,000 operations).

#### B. Transistor Output PLC
- Uses a **solid-state transistor (BJT or MOSFET)** as the switching element.
- **DC loads only**. Extremely **fast** (μs switching).
- **Two polarities exist:**
  - **Sourcing (PNP):** PLC output supplies positive voltage to the load.
  - **Sinking (NPN):** PLC output connects the load to ground (0V).
- **Long life** (no moving parts), but **leakage current** (~0.1 mA to 1 mA) exists even when OFF.
- Sensitive to **inductive kickback** — requires flyback diodes.

#### C. Triac Output PLC
- Uses a **TRIAC (bidirectional thyristor)** for switching.
- **AC loads only**. Solid-state, fast, silent operation.
- Used for dimming, heater control, and small AC solenoids.
- **Leakage current** exists when OFF — problematic for low-current AC circuits.
- Cannot handle **high inrush currents** (e.g., large motor starting).

### 2.4 Axis 3: Classification by Size / Application Scale

| Class | I/O Range | Typical Memory | Example Models | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nano PLC** | < 16 I/O | < 1 KB | Siemens LOGO!, Crouzet Millenium 3 | Smart home, small HVAC, simple machines |
| **Micro PLC** | 16 – 128 I/O | 1 – 4 KB | Siemens S7-200, AB MicroLogix 1000 | Standalone machines, conveyors |
| **Small PLC** | 128 – 512 I/O | 4 – 16 KB | Siemens S7-300, AB SLC-500 | Small process lines, packaging |
| **Medium PLC** | 512 – 2048 I/O | 16 – 64 KB | Siemens S7-400, AB ControlLogix (mid) | DCS integration, large machines |
| **Large PLC** | > 2048 I/O | > 64 KB | Siemens S7-400H (redundant), AB ControlLogix | Refineries, power plants, paper mills |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Decision Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While PLC selection is largely qualitative, two **quantitative formulas** are critical for KTU numerical problems.

#### Formula 1: PLC Scan Time

The total scan time of a PLC is the sum of four sequential phases:

$$T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{input}$ | Time to read all input statuses | 0.1 – 1 ms |
| $T_{program}$ | Time to execute the user logic | 1 – 50 ms |
| $T_{output}$ | Time to update all output modules | 0.1 – 1 ms |
| $T_{housekeeping}$ | Diagnostics, comms, self-checks | 1 – 5 ms |

#### Formula 2: Effective Program Execution Time

For a ladder logic program with $N$ instructions, each taking average execution time $\bar{t}_{instr}$:

$$T_{program} = N \times \bar{t}_{instr}$$

> **Engineering Utility:** The scan time formula is used to verify that a PLC is **fast enough** for time-critical applications (e.g., a bottling line running at 200 bottles/min requires $T_{scan} < 300$ ms).

#### Decision Rule for Output Type Selection

$$
\text{Output Selection} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Relay}, & \text{if load is AC or DC, low switching frequency, cost-sensitive} \\[4pt]
\text{Transistor (Sink/Source)}, & \text{if load is DC, high switching freq, fast response} \\[4pt]
\text{Triac}, & \text{if load is AC, silent switching needed, dimming/phase-angle} \\[4pt]
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines (Toyota, Ford):** Use **modular large PLCs (Allen-Bradley ControlLogix)** networked via **EtherNet/IP** to synchronize robotic arms, conveyors, and vision systems.
- **Building Automation (HVAC, lighting):** Use **nano/micro PLCs (Siemens LOGO!)** as standalone smart controllers.
- **Food & Beverage (Coca-Cola bottling):** Use **relay-output small PLCs** for slow, high-current valve control mixed with **transistor outputs** for high-speed counting.
- **Power Plants (NTPC, KSEB):** Use **redundant large PLCs** with **triac and relay outputs** for circuit breaker control and generator synchronization.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Why Different Output Types Exist

A PLC's output card must act as a **controlled switch** between the load and the power source. Let us model the requirements mathematically.

#### Step 1: Define the Load Profile

A load is characterized by three parameters:
- **Voltage type:** $V_{DC}$ (DC) or $V_{AC} = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} \sin(\omega t)$ (AC).
- **Current magnitude:** $I_{load}$ (typically 0.1 A to 10 A).
- **Switching frequency:** $f_{sw}$ (cycles/hour or Hz).

#### Step 2: Define the Switching Element Constraints

A "switch" must have:
- **Low on-state resistance** (or voltage drop): $V_{drop} \ll V_{supply}$.
- **High off-state impedance** (to block leakage).
- **Fast switching**: transition time $t_{tr} \ll 1/f_{sw}$.
- **Long life**: cycle rating $N_{cycles}$.

#### Step 3: Derive the Choice of Element

For a **DC load**, an AC-switching element (triac) fails because triacs do not latch in DC (no zero-crossing to commutate).

For a **high-frequency DC load** (e.g., a PWM solenoid at 1 kHz), a relay fails because:
$$
t_{relay} \approx 10 \text{ ms} = 10^{-2} \text{ s}
$$
$$
f_{max,\,relay} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{relay}} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

But the load requires $f_{sw} = 1000$ Hz. Since $f_{sw} \gg f_{max,\,relay}$, a relay **chatters and welds** — therefore a **transistor** is mandatory.

For an **AC load** with phase-angle control (e.g., dimming a heater), a triac is the only element that supports synchronous firing at the AC zero-crossing.

#### Step 4: Verify Leakage Current Effect

A transistor or triac, when OFF, still passes a small leakage current $I_{leak} \approx 0.1 \text{ mA} \to 1 \text{ mA}$. For a high-impedance load, this current may be sufficient to falsely trigger it. A relay has $I_{leak} = 0$ (mechanical gap), making it safe for sensitive instrumentation loads.

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix

#### Matrix 1: Compact vs. Modular PLC

| Parameter | Compact (Brick) PLC | Modular (Rack-Mount) PLC |
|---|---|---|
| **I/O Count** | Fixed (8 – 128) | Expandable (up to 8000+) |
| **Flexibility** | Low (cannot swap CPU separately) | High (mix & match modules) |
| **Cost** | Low (USD 100 – 800) | High (USD 2000 – 50,000) |
| **Footprint** | Small (DIN-rail mount) | Large (sub-cabinet) |
| **Maintenance** | Replace whole unit | Replace single faulty card |
| **Hot-Swap** | No | Yes (in redundant systems) |
| **Programming** | Ladder + Function Block | All IEC 61131-3 languages |
| **Networking** | Limited (Modbus RTU) | Full (EtherNet/IP, Profinet) |
| **Application** | Standalone machines, OEM | Process plants, DCS layer |
| **Example** | Siemens LOGO!, AB MicroLogix 1100 | Siemens S7-1500, AB ControlLogix |

#### Matrix 2: Relay vs. Transistor vs. Triac Output

| Parameter | Relay Output | Transistor Output | Triac Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Type** | AC or DC | DC only | AC only |
| **Switching Speed** | ~10 ms (slow) | ~0.1 – 1 μs (fast) | ~10 μs (fast) |
| **Mechanical Life** | ~1 × 10⁶ ops | ∞ (solid-state) | ∞ (solid-state) |
| **Electrical Life** | ~1 × 10⁵ (at full load) | ~1 × 10⁸ | ~1 × 10⁶ |
| **Leakage Current (OFF)** | 0 mA | 0.1 – 1 mA | 1 – 5 mA |
| **Voltage Drop (ON)** | ~0 V (closed contact) | 0.2 – 1.5 V | 1.5 – 2 V |
| **Inrush Handling** | Excellent (motor starters OK) | Poor (use freewheel diode) | Moderate |
| **Cost per Point** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Audible Noise** | Yes (click) | No | No |
| **Isolation** | Galvanic (1500 V) | Opto-coupled (1500 V) | Opto-coupled (1500 V) |
| **Typical Use** | Motor starters, solenoid valves | DC solenoids, indicators, PWM | Heaters, lamps, AC dimming |

#### Matrix 3: Nano / Micro / Small / Medium / Large PLC

| Class | I/O | CPU Power | Cost (USD) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nano** | < 16 | 8-bit equivalent | 50 – 200 | Smart building, lab automation |
| **Micro** | 16 – 128 | 16-bit | 150 – 1500 | OEM machines, conveyors |
| **Small** | 128 – 512 | 32-bit | 1500 – 10,000 | Packaging lines, water treatment |
| **Medium** | 512 – 2048 | 32/64-bit | 10,000 – 50,000 | DCS supervisory, automotive lines |
| **Large** | > 2048 | 64-bit, redundant | 50,000 – 500,000 | Power plants, refineries, paper mills |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Scan Time Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Model Question Pattern (Dec 2023 style):** A PLC is reading 256 digital inputs, executing a 12 KB ladder program with average instruction time of 0.8 μs/instruction, updating 256 outputs, and performing housekeeping. Compute the total scan time. Is the PLC suitable for a process that requires response within 5 ms?

#### Step 1: Input Update Time

Typical per-point input update time: $t_{in} = 1 \mu s$ per point.

$$
T_{input} = 256 \times 1 \, \mu s = 256 \, \mu s = 0.256 \, ms
$$

#### Step 2: Program Execution Time

Convert KB to number of instructions (assuming 1 instruction = 1 byte for average):

$$
N = 12 \, \text{KB} = 12 \times 1024 = 12288 \text{ instructions}
$$

$$
T_{program} = 12288 \times 0.8 \, \mu s = 9830.4 \, \mu s \approx 9.83 \, ms
$$

#### Step 3: Output Update Time

$$
T_{output} = 256 \times 1 \, \mu s = 256 \, \mu s = 0.256 \, ms
$$

#### Step 4: Housekeeping Time

Given as $T_{housekeeping} = 1.2 \, ms$.

#### Step 5: Total Scan Time

$$
T_{scan} = 0.256 + 9.83 + 0.256 + 1.2
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 11.542 \, ms
$$

#### Step 6: Conclusion

Since $T_{scan} = 11.54 \, \text{ms} > 5 \, \text{ms}$, **this PLC is NOT suitable** for the time-critical 5 ms process. The student should recommend:
- Upgrading to a **faster CPU** (lower $\bar{t}_{instr}$),
- Or splitting the program into multiple PLCs,
- Or selecting a PLC with $T_{program} < 3 \, ms$ capability.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between a compact PLC and a modular PLC with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Compact PLC | Modular PLC |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | CPU, I/O, and power supply in **one integrated unit** | Separate **plug-in modules** on a common rack/backplane |
| **I/O Count** | Fixed (8–128) | Expandable to **thousands** |
| **Flexibility** | Limited; cannot mix I/O types | High; mix DI, DO, AI, AO as needed |
| **Cost** | Low (₹8,000 – ₹60,000) | High (₹1.5 L – ₹40 L) |
| **Example** | Siemens LOGO! 8, AB MicroLogix 1100 | Siemens S7-1500, AB ControlLogix 5580 |
| **Application** | Standalone machines, OEM equipment | Process plants, DCS, large assembly lines |

A **compact PLC** is chosen for small, cost-sensitive applications with fixed I/O requirements, while a **modular PLC** is used where high I/O count, redundancy, and complex networking are needed.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Why is a relay output PLC preferred over a transistor output PLC for motor starter contactor coils?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Load compatibility:** Contactor coils are typically **24 VDC or 230 VAC**; relay outputs switch **both AC and DC**, while transistor outputs are **DC only**.
2. **Inrush tolerance:** Contactors draw 5–10× inrush current; relay contacts handle inrush well, whereas transistors may be damaged without protection.
3. **Galvanic isolation:** Relay outputs provide inherent **1500 V galvanic isolation** through the coil-contact separation, protecting the PLC electronics from coil back-EMF.

Therefore, relay outputs are the standard choice for switching inductive loads like contactor coils and solenoid valves.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]:**

**(a)** Classify PLCs based on **physical architecture** and **output switching element**. Draw the block diagram of a modular PLC and label all major modules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLC has 512 digital inputs (update time = 0.5 μs/point), executes a 32 KB ladder program (avg. 0.6 μs/instruction), updates 512 outputs (0.5 μs/point), and takes 2 ms for housekeeping. Calculate the total scan time and comment on its suitability for a packaging line requiring a 20 ms response. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Classification & Block Diagram — 7 marks**

**Classification by Physical Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Compact / Brick PLC** — All components in a single enclosure. Fixed I/O. Example: Siemens LOGO!.
- **Modular / Rack-mounted PLC** — Separate modules on a rack. Example: Siemens S7-1500.
- **Hybrid PLC** — Fixed I/O with expansion ports. Example: Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1400.

**Classification by Output Switching Element (2 marks):**
- **Relay Output** — Electromechanical, AC/DC, slow, robust.
- **Transistor Output** — Solid-state, DC only, fast (sinking NPN or sourcing PNP).
- **Triac Output** — Solid-state, AC only, used for dimming.

**Block Diagram of Modular PLC (2 marks):** (Refer to **Section 4.2** Mermaid diagram; student should draw the rack with slots for Power Supply, CPU, DI, DO, AI, AO, and Communication cards feeding Sensors, Actuators, and SCADA.)

---

**(b) Scan Time Calculation — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Input Update Time**

$$
T_{input} = 512 \times 0.5 \, \mu s = 256 \, \mu s = 0.256 \, ms
$$

**Step 2: Program Execution Time**

$$
N = 32 \, \text{KB} = 32 \times 1024 = 32768 \text{ instructions}
$$

$$
T_{program} = 32768 \times 0.6 \, \mu s = 19660.8 \, \mu s \approx 19.66 \, ms
$$

**Step 3: Output Update Time**

$$
T_{output} = 512 \times 0.5 \, \mu s = 256 \, \mu s = 0.256 \, ms
$$

**Step 4: Housekeeping**

$$
T_{housekeeping} = 2 \, ms
$$

**Step 5: Total Scan Time**

$$
T_{scan} = 0.256 + 19.66 + 0.256 + 2
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{scan} \approx 22.17 \, ms}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion (1 mark)**

Since $T_{scan} = 22.17 \, \text{ms} > 20 \, \text{ms}$ (required response), the PLC is **borderline unsuitable**. The program execution time dominates (89% of total). The student should recommend:
- Splitting the program across two CPUs,
- Or using a faster CPU ($\bar{t}_{instr} \le 0.4 \, \mu s$ would yield $T_{program} = 13.1 \, ms$).

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]:**

**(a)** Explain the **three types of PLC outputs (relay, transistor, triac)** with neat sketches of their internal switching circuits. Compare them on the basis of load type, speed, life, and leakage current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify PLCs based on **size/application** (Nano, Micro, Small, Medium, Large). For each class, give the I/O range, one example model, and one industrial application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Three Output Types — 7 marks**

**1. Relay Output (2 marks)**

A relay output consists of a **coil** driven by the logic circuit (via an optocoupler) and a **mechanical contact** (SPST or SPDT) that opens/closes the load circuit. When the CPU outputs a logic 1, the coil energizes, the armature moves, and the contact closes, allowing current to flow through the load.

- **Load:** AC or DC.
- **Speed:** ~10 ms.
- **Life:** ~1 million mechanical operations.
- **Leakage:** 0 mA (true galvanic break).

**2. Transistor Output (2 marks)**

A transistor output uses a **BJT (NPN sinking) or MOSFET (PNP sourcing)** as the switch. The logic level drives the base/gate via an optocoupler; the transistor then conducts, completing the DC load circuit.

- **Load:** DC only.
- **Speed:** 0.1 – 1 μs (extremely fast).
- **Life:** > 100 million operations (solid-state).
- **Leakage:** 0.1 – 1 mA (residual).

**3. Triac Output (2 marks)**

A triac output uses a **bidirectional thyristor (TRIAC)** that conducts in both AC half-cycles when triggered by a gate pulse. It is the solid-state equivalent of an AC relay.

- **Load:** AC only.
- **Speed:** 10 μs.
- **Life:** ~1 million operations.
- **Leakage:** 1 – 5 mA.
- **Special use:** Phase-angle control for dimming and heater regulation.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):** (Refer to **Matrix 2** in Section 3.2.)

---

**(b) Classification by Size/Application — 7 marks**

| Class | I/O Range | Example Model | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nano PLC** | < 16 | Siemens LOGO! 8, Crouzet Millenium 3 | Smart building, lab temperature control |
| **Micro PLC** | 16 – 128 | AB MicroLogix 1100, Siemens S7-200 | Small conveyors, packaging machines |
| **Small PLC** | 128 – 512 | Siemens S7-300, AB SLC-500 | Water treatment plant, bottling line |
| **Medium PLC** | 512 – 2048 | Siemens S7-400, AB CompactLogix 5380 | Automotive assembly, HVAC for large buildings |
| **Large PLC** | > 2048 | AB ControlLogix 5580, Siemens S7-400H | Oil refinery, thermal power plant, paper mill |

For **large PLCs**, redundant CPUs (e.g., S7-400H) and **hot-swappable I/O** are mandatory for process-critical industries where downtime costs millions per hour.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "sourcing" and "sinking":** Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the student cannot clearly state *which terminal* of the load receives the current in each configuration. Always state: *Sourcing = PLC supplies +V; Sinking = PLC switches to 0V.*
> 2. **Mixing up classification axes:** A student may list "relay output" under "compact PLC" — these are **orthogonal classifications**; the warning is to keep them separate.
> 3. **Forgetting the scan-time formula** in numerical questions: KTU expects the **four-phase breakdown** ($T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$), not a single lumped number.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion:** $1 \, \text{KB} = 1024$ bytes, not 1000 — examiners in scan-time problems will mark this strictly.
> 5. **Omitting example models** in classification answers: KTU mandates at least **one real model name** (e.g., Siemens LOGO!) to prove the student knows the industry context.
> 6. **Triac cannot switch DC:** Many students write that triacs "switch AC or DC" — this is a **fatal conceptual error** worth -3 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Types of PLC**

- ✅ A **PLC** is a ruggedized industrial computer for real-time control of machines and processes.
- ✅ **Three orthogonal classification axes:**
  1. **Physical architecture** → Compact, Modular, Hybrid.
  2. **Output element** → Relay, Transistor, Triac.
  3. **Size/Application** → Nano, Micro, Small, Medium, Large.
- ✅ **Compact PLC** = integrated, fixed I/O, low cost (e.g., LOGO!).
- ✅ **Modular PLC** = rack-mounted, expandable, high cost (e.g., S7-1500, ControlLogix).
- ✅ **Relay output** = AC/DC, slow (~10 ms), 0 leakage, mechanical wear, ideal for contactors.
- ✅ **Transistor output** = DC only, fast (μs), 0.1–1 mA leakage, **sinking (NPN)** and **sourcing (PNP)** versions exist.
- ✅ **Triac output** = AC only, used for dimming and phase-angle control; cannot commutate DC.
- ✅ **Scan time** $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$.
- ✅ **Sinking vs. Sourcing** is a frequent 3-mark question: sinking = current flows **into** the PLC terminal; sourcing = current flows **out of** the PLC terminal.
- ✅ **Large PLCs** use **redundant CPUs (hot-standby)** for zero-downtime process industries.
- ✅ **IEC 61131-3** is the international standard for PLC programming languages (LD, FBD, ST, IL, SFC).
- ✅ PLC selection is driven by **I/O count, scan time, environment, expandability, and cost** — not brand loyalty.
- ✅ Memorize at least **one example model** per size class to score full marks in classification questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** PLC Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ PLC Modules — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a PLC Module?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **PLC Module** is a *modular, plug-in hardware sub-assembly* housed within a Programmable Logic Controller rack (or chassis) that performs a dedicated function — such as processing, power conditioning, signal acquisition, signal actuation, memory storage, or communication — within the deterministic real-time control loop. Each module is a self-contained printed circuit board that communicates with the central processor through a **backplane bus**.

The PLC is **not a single monolithic box** in modern industrial practice; it is an *assembled system* of interchangeable modules. This modular philosophy allows the control engineer to scale the system (add I/O as the plant grows), service individual components (replace only the failed module), and mix signal types (digital + analog + high-speed counter) on the same backplane.

## 1.2 The "Lego Brick" Analogy 🧱

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a PLC rack as the *chassis of a desktop computer motherboard*, but designed for a hostile factory floor.
> - The **Power Supply** is your SMPS — converts noisy mains AC into clean DC rails.
> - The **CPU Module** is your microprocessor — runs the user logic.
> - The **Memory Module** is your RAM/ROM — stores the operating system and ladder program.
> - The **Input Modules** are your keyboard/mouse ports — they *listen* to the outside world (push-buttons, sensors).
> - The **Output Modules** are your USB/HDMI ports — they *speak* to the outside world (motor starters, solenoids, valves).
> - The **Communication Module** is your Ethernet/Wi-Fi card — talks to other PLCs, SCADA servers, and HMIs.
> - The **Backplane** is your motherboard's PCB traces — the highway on which all data travels.

The *key engineering difference* from a PC is that every module must operate **deterministically**, within a guaranteed **scan cycle time** (typically **1 ms to 20 ms**), and survive **electrical noise, vibration, temperature swings (-20 °C to +60 °C)**, and **24/7 operation for 10+ years**.

## 1.3 The Six Canonical PLC Module Categories

| # | Module Type | Primary Function | Typical Form Factor |
|---|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Power Supply (PS)** | Converts AC mains → regulated DC rails (24 V DC, 5 V DC, ±15 V DC) for backplane | Slot 0 (dedicated) |
| 2 | **Processor / CPU** | Executes scan cycle: *Input Scan → Logic Solve → Output Scan → Housekeeping* | Slot 1 (dedicated) |
| 3 | **Memory** | Stores OS (ROM), user program (RAM/Flash), retentive data (Battery-backed RAM) | Integrated with CPU |
| 4 | **Input (I)** | Isolates, filters, and conditions field signals into logic-level voltages the CPU can read | Slots 2…N |
| 5 | **Output (O)** | Switches/regulates field loads from logic-level CPU commands | Slots 2…N |
| 6 | **Communication (Comms / Network)** | Adds fieldbus / Ethernet / serial ports (Profibus, Modbus, EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet) | Last slots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 1):** Students must be able to *identify, distinguish, and justify the selection* of each module type for a given industrial application. Examiners frequently test the **mapping between field device and module selection** (e.g., "Which module would you use for a 4–20 mA pressure transmitter?" → **Analog Input**).

## 1.4 Why Modularity Matters in Industry

Three engineering drivers justify the modular architecture:

1. **Scalability** — Start with 16 I/O, expand to 2000+ I/O by adding rack-mounted or distributed I/O drops.
2. **Maintainability (MTTR Reduction)** — Mean Time To Repair drops from *hours* (replace whole PLC) to *minutes* (swap one card).
3. **Signal-Type Heterogeneity** — One plant needs 24 V DC discrete inputs, 4–20 mA analog inputs, 230 V AC relay outputs, and 0–10 V analog outputs — different cards handle each safely.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Each Module — Step-by-Step Functional Breakdown

### ⚡ 2.1.1 Power Supply Module (PS)

**Operational Logic:**
1. **EMI Filter Stage** — Suppresses high-frequency noise from the AC mains.
2. **Transformer / SMPS Stage** — Steps down 230 V AC / 120 V AC to a lower AC voltage.
3. **Rectifier + Bulk Capacitor** — Converts AC to pulsating DC, then smooths to a low-ripple DC rail.
4. **Voltage Regulation (Linear or Switch-Mode)** — Holds the output within **±5 %** of nominal despite ±10 % mains variation.
5. **Over-current / Over-voltage Protection** — Crowbar or fold-back current limiting.
6. **Isolation** — Galvanic isolation (typically **1500 V AC for 1 minute**) between field side and logic side.

> **Standard Output Rails of an Industrial PLC:**
> - **24 V DC** — for sourcing discrete inputs and sensor power.
> - **5 V DC** — for backplane logic.
> - **±15 V DC** — for analog signal conditioning (op-amps).

**Real-world utility:** In a refinery, the PLC may be fed from a UPS-backed bus; the power supply must tolerate brief sags without resetting (hold-up time ≥ **20 ms**).

---

### 🧠 2.1.2 Processor / CPU Module

**Operational Logic (the famous *Scan Cycle*):**

1. **Input Scan** — Reads the status of every input module image table into memory.
2. **Program Execution** — Solves the user logic (ladder / FBD / SFC) **rung-by-rung, top-to-bottom**.
3. **Output Scan** — Writes the solved output image table back to the physical output modules.
4. **Housekeeping** — Communication servicing, self-diagnostics, fault logging, time-stamping.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Scan Time Formula** (KTU favourite — appears in almost every past paper):
> $$T_{\text{scan}} = T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{logic}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{hk}}$$
> where $T_{\text{in}}$ ∝ (number of input words), $T_{\text{logic}}$ ∝ (number of rungs + complexity), $T_{\text{out}}$ ∝ (number of output words), and $T_{\text{hk}}$ is fixed overhead (typ. 1–3 ms).

**Determinism Guarantee:** The CPU executes within a **maximum bounded scan time** (e.g., Allen-Bradley CompactLogix = **0.08 ms / 1k instructions**). This is why a PLC — and not a Windows PC — is used for safety interlocks.

---

### 💾 2.1.3 Memory Module

**Memory Map Hierarchy:**

| Memory Region | Volatility | Used For |
|---------------|------------|----------|
| **ROM / Flash** | Non-volatile | Operating system firmware |
| **RAM** | Volatile | Scratchpad / working memory |
| **Battery-backed RAM (BBR)** | Non-volatile (with battery) | Retentive counters, timers, bit memory |
| **EEPROM / Flash User Memory** | Non-volatile | User program download (retained without battery) |

> **Typical Memory Sizing (modern mid-range PLC):** 8 MB user program, 8 MB data, expandable to 32 GB on rack CPUs.

---

### 📥 2.1.4 Input Module (Discrete & Analog)

**Signal Conditioning Pipeline:**

1. **Protection** — TVS diodes, varistors, fuses.
2. **Isolation** — Optocoupler (galvanic isolation, **typically 2.5 kV RMS**).
3. **Filtering** — RC low-pass filter; debounce time for mechanical contacts (typ. **1–20 ms**).
4. **Threshold Detection** — Schmitt trigger converts analog signal to logic 0 / 1.
5. **Status LED Indication** — Per-channel LED for diagnostics.
6. **Multiplexing & ADC (Analog only)** — Shared ADC samples channels sequentially.

**Discrete Input Voltage Standards (IEC 61131-2 Type 1/2/3):**

| Type | Voltage Range | Current @ ON |
|------|---------------|--------------|
| Type 1 | 24 V DC, 0–15 V OFF, 15–30 V ON | 2 mA min |
| Type 2 | 24 V DC (high-noise immune) | 6 mA min |
| Type 3 | 24 V DC (low-current sensors) | 1.5 mA min |

**Analog Input Resolution Formula** (extremely high-yield):

$$\text{Resolution (V/bit)} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

where $V_{\text{FS}}$ is the full-scale voltage (e.g., 10 V) and $n$ is the ADC bit count (typically **12, 14, or 16 bits**).

*Example:* 16-bit ADC over 0–10 V span → $\frac{10}{65535} \approx 152.6 \,\mu V / \text{bit}$.

---

### 📤 2.1.5 Output Module (Discrete & Analog)

**Discrete Output Switching Technologies:**

| Tech | Switching Speed | Surge Current | Application |
|------|-----------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Relay (EMR)** | 10 ms | High inrush ok | Motor starters, solenoids |
| **Transistor (NPN/PNP)** | < 1 µs | Low | Fast DC loads, LEDs |
| **TRIAC** | < 10 µs | High inrush | AC solenoids, heaters |
| **MOSFET** | < 1 µs | Moderate | PWM, proportional valves |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student pitfall:** Forgetting that **relay outputs are dry contacts** (volt-free), whereas **transistor outputs are wet** (switch the 24 V rail). Mixing them up destroys loads.

**Analog Output Resolution:**
$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n} - 1}$$
Same form as the analog input formula. A **4–20 mA current loop** output uses a precision voltage-to-current converter (Howland current pump or op-amp + transistor).

---

### 🌐 2.1.6 Communication / Interface Module

Provides ports for:
- **Fieldbus:** Profibus DP, DeviceNet, Foundation Fieldbus, Modbus RTU.
- **Industrial Ethernet:** EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP.
- **Serial:** RS-232, RS-485.
- **Specialty:** AS-i (Actuator-Sensor Interface), IO-Link, BACnet (building automation).

> **Real-world utility:** In a *Distributed Control System (DCS)*, the comms module is the gateway to the supervisory layer (SCADA / HMI) and to remote I/O drops over fiber-optic ring topologies.

---

## 2.2 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | PLC Scan Time | $T_{\text{scan}} = T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{logic}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{hk}}$ | ms | Add per-rung micro-times for accuracy |
| 2 | I/O Image Update | Word = 16 bits; one *bit* per discrete I/O | bits | 1024 I/O = 64 words |
| 3 | Analog Resolution | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n}-1}$ | V/bit | Use $\vert V_{\text{FS}} \vert$ carefully for bipolar (±10 V) spans |
| 4 | % Quantization Error | $\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{100}{2^{n}}$ | % | n=12 → 0.0244 %; n=16 → 0.00153 % |
| 5 | Backplane Power Budget | $P_{\text{PS}} \ge \sum_{i} P_{\text{CPU}} + \sum_{j} P_{\text{I},j} + \sum_{k} P_{\text{O},k}$ | W | Add 25 % margin |
| 6 | 4–20 mA Engineering Span | $V_{\text{out}} = 4 + \dfrac{16 \cdot (M - M_{\min})}{M_{\max}-M_{\min}}$ | mA | $M$ = measured process variable |
| 7 | Optocoupler Current Transfer Ratio | $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{I_{\text{C}}}{I_{\text{F}}} \times 100$ | % | Derate to ≤ 50 % for reliability |
| 8 | EMI Filter Cut-off | $f_{\text{c}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | Must reject VFD noise (typ. > 2 kHz) |
| 9 | Maximum Discrete Inputs/Outputs | Determined by CPU addressing: e.g., 8192 in / 8192 out for mid-range PLC | I/O count | Address-bound |
| 10 | Battery Hold-up | $t_{\text{hold}} \ge 20$ ms (typical spec) | ms | Survives mains dip without reset |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note:** PLC Module theory is *analytical* rather than purely calculus-heavy. The derivations below focus on the formulae examiners love: scan-time budgeting, analog resolution, and backplane power sizing. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Analog Input Resolution (Full Worked Example)

**Problem Statement:**
A 14-bit ADC is used in an analog input module configured for a unipolar **0 to 10 V DC** range. Determine:
(a) the voltage represented by **1 LSB**,
(b) the smallest detectable change as a percentage of full scale,
(c) the digital output code (in decimal) when the input is **4.823 V**.

### Step 1 — Identify the formula

$$\text{LSB (V/bit)} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}} - V_{\text{Zero}}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

where $V_{\text{FS}}$ = full-scale voltage, $V_{\text{Zero}}$ = zero-scale voltage, $n$ = bit count.

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{FS}} &= 10 \,\text{V}, \\
V_{\text{Zero}} &= 0 \,\text{V}, \\
n &= 14.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSB} &= \frac{10 - 0}{2^{14} - 1} \\
&= \frac{10}{16384 - 1} \\
&= \frac{10}{16383} \\
&= 6.1035 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{V} \\
&= 610.35 \,\mu\text{V / LSB}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each increment of the digital count corresponds to **≈ 0.61 mV**.

### Step 3 — Percent quantization error

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\%} &= \frac{1 \text{ LSB}}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{610.35 \,\mu\text{V}}{10 \,\text{V}} \times 100 \\
&= 0.0061035 \,\%.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Digital code for $V_{\text{in}} = 4.823 \,\text{V}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{Zero}}}{\text{LSB}}\right) \\
&= \text{round}\!\left(\frac{4.823}{6.1035 \times 10^{-4}}\right) \\
&= \text{round}(7902.04) \\
&= 7902.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state the units of LSB (mV/bit or µV/bit) explicitly. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous notation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Backplane Power Budget (Worst-Case Sizing)

**Problem Statement:**
A PLC rack contains:
- 1 × CPU module: **8.5 W**
- 1 × 16-channel DI module: **3.2 W**
- 2 × 16-channel DO (relay) modules: **4.8 W each**
- 1 × 8-channel AI module: **2.7 W**
- 1 × 4-channel AO module: **2.1 W**
- 1 × Ethernet comms module: **3.4 W**

Determine the **minimum power supply rating** required (with engineering margin).

### Step 1 — Sum the loads

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{sum}} &= P_{\text{CPU}} + P_{\text{DI}} + 2 \cdot P_{\text{DO}} + P_{\text{AI}} + P_{\text{AO}} + P_{\text{Comms}} \\
&= 8.5 + 3.2 + 2(4.8) + 2.7 + 2.1 + 3.4 \\
&= 8.5 + 3.2 + 9.6 + 2.7 + 2.1 + 3.4 \\
&= 29.5 \,\text{W}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply engineering margin (25 %)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{PS,min}} &= 1.25 \cdot P_{\text{sum}} \\
&= 1.25 \times 29.5 \\
&= 36.875 \,\text{W}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Round up to commercial rating

The next standard rating is **40 W**. Therefore, a **40 W power supply module** is selected.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget the *inrush current* of relay coils and DC-DC converters on cold-start. Always size with ≥ **25 % margin** to avoid boot-time brown-outs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Scan Time Budgeting for a Conveyor PLC

**Problem Statement:**
A PLC has:
- 32 discrete inputs, 16 discrete outputs.
- I/O scan time per word = **8 µs**.
- Logic program: 250 ladder rungs averaging **0.4 µs/instruction** with an average of **6 instructions/rung**.
- Housekeeping time = **2.5 ms**.
- Communication servicing interrupt = **0.8 ms** every scan.

Compute the **total scan time**.

### Step 1 — Total I/O words

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{words}} &= \left\lceil\frac{32+16}{16}\right\rceil = \left\lceil 3 \right\rceil = 3 \text{ words}.
\end{aligned}
$$

(We use ceiling for safety — incomplete words still occupy a full word slot.)

### Step 2 — I/O scan time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{io}} &= N_{\text{words}} \times 8 \,\mu\text{s} = 3 \times 8 = 24 \,\mu\text{s} = 0.024 \,\text{ms}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Logic execution time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{logic}} &= (\text{rungs}) \times (\text{instr/rung}) \times (\mu s/\text{instr}) \\
&= 250 \times 6 \times 0.4 \,\mu\text{s} \\
&= 600 \,\mu\text{s} = 0.6 \,\text{ms}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Total scan time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= T_{\text{io}} + T_{\text{logic}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{hk}} \\
&= 0.024 + 0.6 + 0.024 + 2.5 + 0.8 \\
&= 3.948 \,\text{ms}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Determinism check

For a conveyor control loop with motor response time ~ 50 ms, a 4 ms scan is **comfortably within determinism**. However, if the application were a *high-speed bottle-filling line* (1 ms response required), the program would have to be **partitioned** into a *periodic task* running at 1 ms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** This derivation is exactly what a control engineer performs during PLC sizing for any production line. An undersized scan budget causes *lost counts*, *jitter*, and *missed interlocks* — root causes of plant downtime.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Module Selection by Field Device

| Field Device | Signal Type | Module Required | Why |
|--------------|-------------|-----------------|-----|
| Push-button, limit switch | 24 V DC contact | **DI (Type 1/3)** | Dry contact → needs wetting voltage |
| Proximity sensor (PNP) | 24 V DC sourcing | **DI (Type 1)** | Sourcing output matches DI wetting |
| RTD / Thermocouple | mV or resistance | **AI (RTD/TC module)** | Needs linearization & cold-junction comp |
| Pressure transmitter | 4–20 mA | **AI (current input)** | Industry-standard current loop |
| Control valve positioner | 4–20 mA demand | **AO (current output)** | Proportional analog control |
| Solenoid valve (24 V DC) | 24 V DC coil | **DO (transistor PNP)** | Fast, solid-state, long life |
| Motor contactor coil | 230 V AC coil | **DO (relay or TRIAC)** | High inrush & galvanic isolation |
| VFD speed reference | 0–10 V or 4–20 mA | **AO** | Analog speed command |
| Encoder | Differential A/B/Z pulses | **High-Speed Counter (HSC) module** | 100 kHz+ pulse rate |
| HMI / SCADA link | Ethernet | **Ethernet comms module** | TCP/IP, Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PLC Rack Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

> The diagram below maps the **physical slot arrangement** of a typical modular PLC rack and the **backplane data-flow paths** between modules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mains["230 V AC MAINS SUPPLY"] --> PS["SLOT 0: POWER SUPPLY MODULE"]
    PS -->|24 V DC + 5 V DC rails| Backplane["BACKPLANE BUS PARALLEL DATA HIGHWAY"]

    Backplane --> CPU["SLOT 1: CPU PROCESSOR MODULE"]
    CPU -->|Image Table write| Backplane
    Backplane -->|Image Table read| CPU

    Backplane --> DI1["SLOT 2: DIGITAL INPUT MODULE 16 CH"]
    Backplane --> DI2["SLOT 3: DIGITAL INPUT MODULE 16 CH"]
    Backplane --> AI1["SLOT 4: ANALOG INPUT MODULE 8 CH RTD"]
    Backplane --> AI2["SLOT 5: ANALOG INPUT MODULE 8 CH 4-20 mA"]

    Backplane --> DO1["SLOT 6: DIGITAL OUTPUT RELAY MODULE 16 CH"]
```
