- Converts the incoming AC mains (**230 V AC, 50 Hz** in India OR **24 V DC** for compact PLCs) into the regulated DC voltages (**+5 V, +15 V, -15 V, +24 V**) required by the CPU, memory, and I/O modules.
- Provides **isolation** between the field wiring and the internal logic — this protects the sensitive electronics from voltage spikes caused by inductive loads (motors, solenoids).
- For critical processes, an **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** of capacity $\geq 30\%$ of the rated load is recommended as backup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The power supply is the **single most failure-prone component** in a PLC. KTU exams often ask why a dedicated power supply is used and not the raw mains.

#### 2.1.2 Central Processing Unit (CPU) / Processor

- The CPU executes three core tasks: **(i) reading inputs, (ii) executing the user program, and (iii) updating outputs** — known collectively as the **scan cycle**.
- Modern CPUs are typically **microprocessor-based** (8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit depending on PLC class) and contain:
  - **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** — performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logic (AND, OR, NOT) operations.
  - **Control Unit (CU)** — decodes instructions and generates timing signals.
  - **Register array** — accumulators, status flags, instruction pointer.
- CPU performance is judged by: **clock speed (MHz)**, **scan time (ms/instruction)**, **bit-handling capability**, and **communication throughput**.

#### 2.1.3 Memory Architecture

PLC memory is **strictly partitioned** into distinct regions. A KTU-favorite diagram and question topic.

| Memory Region | Function | Typical Type | Volatile? |
|---------------|----------|--------------|-----------|
| System ROM / Firmware | Stores OS, scan cycle handler, communication drivers | Read-Only Memory (ROM/Flash) | No |
| User Program Memory | Holds the ladder logic / FBD / ST written by engineer | Flash / EEPROM / Battery-backed RAM | No |
| Data Memory (I/O Image Table) | Stores the **ON/OFF** status of all inputs and outputs | RAM | Yes (backed up by battery) |
| Bit / Word Memory | Internal relays, timers, counters, analog registers | RAM | Yes |
| Retentive Memory | Values preserved across power cycle | Non-volatile RAM / Flash | No |

> [!NOTE]
> The **I/O Image Table** is a critical concept: input statuses are read once at the START of the scan and stored in a memory "image." The program reads from this image, NOT directly from the field. This guarantees **deterministic behavior** during a single scan.

#### 2.1.4 Input Modules

Interface between **field sensors** and the **CPU**. They perform three jobs:
1. **Signal conditioning** — filtering noise, debouncing mechanical contacts.
2. **Isolation** — optocoupler-based electrical separation (typically **1500 V RMS** isolation).
3. **Voltage level conversion** — converting field-level (**24 V DC**, **120 V AC**, **230 V AC**) signals to logic-level (**+5 V DC**) signals.

**Classification of Input Modules:**

- **Discrete (Digital) Input (DI) Module** — accepts ON/OFF signals from switches, limit switches, push-buttons, proximity sensors, photoelectric sensors.
- **Analog Input (AI) Module** — accepts continuously variable signals (e.g., **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V DC**, thermocouple mV, RTD resistance). The module contains an **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** with typical resolution of **12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit**.
- **Specialty Input Module** — high-speed counters, encoders, interrupt inputs, temperature modules (TC/RTD).

#### 2.1.5 Output Modules

Interface between **CPU** and **field actuators** (contactors, solenoid valves, lamps, drives). Provide isolation and current amplification.

**Classification of Output Modules:**

- **Discrete (Digital) Output (DO) Module**:
  - *Relay Output* — electro-mechanical contacts, handles AC and DC, slow ($\approx 10$ ms switching), good for **up to 2 A** per point.
  - *Transistor Output* — solid-state (BJT/MOSFET), DC only, very fast ($\approx 0.1$ ms), leakage current $\leq 0.1$ mA.
  - *TRIAC Output* — solid-state for AC loads, used for frequent switching of heating elements.
- **Analog Output (AO) Module** — generates variable signals (**4–20 mA**, **0–10 V DC**) using a **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)**; used to drive variable-speed drives, control valves, chart recorders.

#### 2.1.6 Programming Device & Communication Interface

- The programming device is typically a **laptop/PC with proprietary software** (e.g., RSLogix 500 for Allen-Bradley, TIA Portal for Siemens, CX-One for Omron, ProWORX for Schneider).
- The communication link is usually **serial (RS-232 / RS-485)**, **Ethernet (TCP/IP)**, or **USB** in modern PLCs.
- Once programmed, the device can be disconnected; the PLC runs autonomously.

#### 2.1.7 Rack / Chassis / Backplane

- Provides the **mechanical frame** for mounting modules and the **electrical bus** for data/address/power distribution.
- **Rack-mounted (modular) PLCs** have slots like Slot 0, Slot 1, … Slot n.
- **Compact (brick) PLCs** have fixed I/O built into the CPU; expandability is via add-on cassettes.

### 2.2 Configuration Topologies — Fixed, Modular, and Distributed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 1 explicitly distinguishes three configuration philosophies. Memorize the differences.

| Configuration Type | Also Known As | I/O Count | Expandability | Application |
|--------------------|---------------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| **Fixed / Compact** | Brick / Shoe-box PLC | 10–40 I/O fixed | Limited (via add-on modules) | Small machines, OEM skid control |
| **Modular** | Rack-mounted PLC | 100–5000+ I/O | Highly expandable | Medium/large process plants |
| **Distributed** | Remote I/O / Field-bus | 5000+ I/O | Essentially unlimited | Large plants (refineries, power plants) |

### 2.3 I/O Addressing Scheme

The addressing scheme defines **how the user identifies each input and output** in the program.

**Common Addressing Formats (KTU-Standard):**

| PLC Brand | Input Address | Output Address | Internal Relay |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|----------------|
| Allen-Bradley (SLC 500) | `I:3/5` | `O:4/2` | `B3:10/0` |
| Siemens (S7) | `I 0.0` | `Q 0.0` | `M 0.0` |
| Schneider (Modicon) | `%I0.0.0` | `%Q0.0.0` | `%M0` |
| Generic (IEC 61131-3) | `%IX0.0` | `%QX0.0` | `%MX0` |

### 2.4 I/O Update Mechanism & Scan Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **PLC Scan Cycle** is the single most important process to understand. KTU questions are routinely asked.

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}
$$

where:
- $T_{scan}$ = Total scan time
- $T_{input}$ = Time to read all inputs into the I/O image table
- $T_{program}$ = Time to execute the user logic
- $T_{output}$ = Time to write outputs from image to field
- $T_{housekeeping}$ = Time for diagnostics, communication, memory checks

Typical values: $T_{scan}$ ranges from **1 ms to 50 ms**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Engineering Use |
|-------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{housekeep}$ | Predicts maximum response latency |
| Scan Frequency | $f_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Determines maximum discrete input frequency |
| Min. Detectable Input Pulse | $T_{pulse,min} \geq T_{scan}$ | For high-speed counting, use HSC module |
| Number of Slots | $N_{slots} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{IO}}{N_{IO,per\,module}} \right\rceil$ | Rack sizing calculation |
| Memory Required (approx.) | $M_{bytes} \approx 0.5 \times N_{instructions}$ | Memory sizing |
| Power Budget | $P_{total} = \sum P_{modules} \leq P_{supply}$ | Power supply sizing |
| Power Dissipation (Watts) | $P = V \times I$ | Thermal design |
| Resolution of ADC | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | Where $n$ = ADC bits |
| Addressable I/O | $N_{IO} = N_{bytes} \times 8$ | Bit-packing memory |

> [!NOTE]
> Always include the **units** in your final answer. KTU examiners deduct marks for "T = 10" without writing "T = 10 ms."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Scan Time for a Modular PLC

**Given Scenario (typical KTU-style problem):**

A Siemens S7-300 PLC has the following modules mounted on the rack:

| Slot | Module | I/O Points | Update Time per Point |
|------|--------|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 16-Pt DI | 16 | 4 µs |
| 2 | 8-Pt AI | 8 | 60 µs (conversion + buffering) |
| 3 | 16-Pt DO | 16 | 3 µs |
| 4 | 8-Pt AO | 8 | 80 µs |

The user program has **1200 Boolean instructions** (avg. execution time = 0.8 µs each) and **75 word-arithmetic instructions** (avg. 4 µs each). Housekeeping and communication overhead = **2.5 ms**.

**Step 1 — Compute $T_{input}$**

The total input read time is the sum of time to read the DI module + AI module:

$$
T_{DI} = 16 \times 4\,\mu s = 64\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{AI} = 8 \times 60\,\mu s = 480\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{input} = T_{DI} + T_{AI} = 64 + 480 = 544\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_{output}$**

$$
T_{DO} = 16 \times 3\,\mu s = 48\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{AO} = 8 \times 80\,\mu s = 640\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{output} = T_{DO} + T_{AO} = 48 + 640 = 688\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_{program}$**

$$
T_{bool} = 1200 \times 0.8\,\mu s = 960\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{word} = 75 \times 4\,\mu s = 300\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{program} = T_{bool} + T_{word} = 960 + 300 = 1260\,\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Add Housekeeping Time**

$$
T_{housekeeping} = 2.5\,ms = 2500\,\mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Total Scan Time**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 544 + 1260 + 688 + 2500
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 4992\,\mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{scan} \approx 4.992\,ms \approx 5\,ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Scan Frequency**

$$
f_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}} = \dfrac{1}{4.992 \times 10^{-3}\,s}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{scan} \approx 200.3\,Hz}
$$

This means the PLC can detect an input change **at most every 5 ms** — any pulse shorter than 5 ms may be **missed** unless a **high-speed counter (HSC)** module is used.

### 3.2 Derivation: ADC Resolution for an Analog Input Module

**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC is used in an analog input module configured for a **0–10 V DC** range. Find:
- (a) Number of discrete steps
- (b) Voltage resolution per LSB
- (c) Digital code corresponding to **4.5 V**

**Step 1 — Number of Discrete Steps**

$$
N = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

So there are **4096 distinct codes** (0 to 4095).

**Step 2 — Voltage Resolution (Q)**

$$
Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{10\,V}{4095}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 2.442\,mV/LSB}
$$

**Step 3 — Digital Code for 4.5 V**

$$
D = \text{round}\left( \dfrac{V_{in}}{Q} \right) = \text{round}\left( \dfrac{4.5}{0.002442} \right)
$$

$$
D = \text{round}(1842.5)
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 1843}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Number of Slots Required

**Problem:** A process requires **128 digital inputs** and **64 digital outputs**. The available modules are:
- DI module: 32 points each
- DO module: 16 points each

How many modules of each type are needed, and what is the minimum rack size (assuming 1 slot for CPU and 1 slot for power supply)?

**Step 1 — Number of DI Modules**

$$
N_{DI} = \left\lceil \dfrac{128}{32} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 4.0 \right\rceil = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Number of DO Modules**

$$
N_{DO} = \left\lceil \dfrac{64}{16} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 4.0 \right\rceil = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Total Module Slots**

$$
N_{slots,total} = 1\,(CPU) + 1\,(PSU) + 4\,(DI) + 4\,(DO) = 10
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{A 10-slot rack is required (with 1 spare for future expansion).}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Fixed vs Modular vs Distributed

| Parameter | Fixed PLC | Modular PLC | Distributed PLC |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| Cost per I/O point | Lowest | Medium | Lowest at scale |
| Expandability | Very limited | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cable length to field | Short (max 5–10 m) | Medium (up to 30 m) | Very long (up to 1–2 km via fiber) |
| Field wiring cost | High (home-run cables) | Medium | Lowest (buses) |
| Single point of failure | CPU failure = total stop | CPU failure = total stop | One node failure ≠ total stop |
| Scan cycle | Very fast (1–2 ms) | Fast (5–20 ms) | Slower due to bus cycle (10–50 ms) |
| Best suited for | Small OEM machines | Discrete manufacturing | Large process plants |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modular PLC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PS[Power Supply Module 230V AC to 24V DC and 5V DC]
    CPU[CPU Module Processor and Memory]
    MEM[Memory ROM RAM and I-O Image Table]
    DI1[DI Module 24V DC 16pt Slot 1]
    DI2[DI Module 24V DC 16pt Slot 2]
    AI1[AI Module 4 to 20 mA 8pt Slot 3]
    DO1[DO Module Relay 16pt Slot 4]
    AO1[AO Module 4 to 20 mA 4pt Slot 5]
    BACK[Backplane Bus Address Data Power]
    PROG[Programming Device PC with TIA Portal]
    HMI[HMI Panel Touchscreen]
    FBUS[Field Bus Profibus or Ethernet IP]
    NET[Remote I-O Stations over Bus]

    PS --> BACK
    CPU --> MEM
    CPU --> BACK
    BACK --> DI1
    BACK --> DI2
    BACK --> AI1
    BACK --> DO1
    BACK --> AO1
    BACK --> FBUS
    FBUS --> NET
    FBUS --> HMI
    PROG --> CPU
    NET --> RI1[Remote DI Station]
    NET --> RI2[Remote DO Station]
    NET --> RI3[Remote AI Station]
```

### 4.2 PLC Scan Cycle — Sequence of Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power ON] --> B[Self Test and Diagnostics]
    B --> C[Read all Physical Inputs into I-O Image Table]
    C --> D[Execute User Program Ladder Logic in Sequential Order]
    D --> E[Update Outputs from Image Table to Physical Modules]
    E --> F[Housekeeping Communication Diagnostics]
    F --> G{Power Still ON}
    G -- YES --> C
    G -- NO --> H[STOP Mode Retain Memory]
```

### 4.3 I/O Addressing Hierarchy (IEC 61131-3 Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[PLC System Level 0]
    ROOT --> RACK[Rack 0]
    RACK --> SLOT1[Slot 1 DI Module]
    RACK --> SLOT2[Slot 2 DO Module]
    RACK --> SLOT3[Slot 3 AI Module]
    SLOT1 --> CH1[Channel 0 Ix 0 0]
    SLOT1 --> CH2[Channel 1 Ix 0 0 1]
    SLOT2 --> CH3[Channel 0 Qx 0 1 0]
    SLOT3 --> CH4[Channel 0 IW0]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow: Field Sensor to PLC to Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FS[Field Sensor Limit Switch] --> WIR1[Shielded Cable]
    WIR1 --> TB1[Terminal Block on DI Module]
    TB1 --> OPT[Optocoupler Isolation 1500V RMS]
    OPT --> FIL[RC Filter and Debounce]
    FIL --> ADC[ADC for AI only]
    ADC --> CPU[CPU I-O Image Table]
    CPU --> DAC[DAC for AO only]
    DAC --> DRV[Output Driver Relay or Transistor]
    DRV --> TB2[Terminal Block on DO Module]
    TB2 --> WIR2[Power Wiring]
    WIR2 --> ACT[Field Actuator Contactor Coil]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set B]**
*List the major components of a PLC and state the function of the I/O image table.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The major components of a PLC are:
1. **Power Supply Module** — converts mains AC to regulated DC voltages and provides isolation.
2. **CPU (Processor)** — executes the user program in scan mode.
3. **Memory** — stores system firmware, user program, data, and the **I/O image table**.
4. **Input Modules (DI/AI)** — accept signals from field sensors.
5. **Output Modules (DO/AO)** — send commands to field actuators.
6. **Programming Device** — PC/handheld used to write and download logic.
7. **Rack and Backplane** — mechanical and electrical bus structure.

**Function of the I/O Image Table:** It is a dedicated region of RAM that stores a "snapshot" of the status of every input and output. Inputs are read once at the start of every scan into the input image; outputs are written once at the end of every scan from the output image. This guarantees **deterministic** behavior, prevents mid-scan jitter, and simplifies the user program because the same input value is used throughout one scan.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set A]**
*Differentiate between a fixed (compact) PLC and a modular PLC. Give one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Fixed / Compact PLC | Modular PLC |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| I/O count | Fixed, typically 10–40 I/O | Configurable, 100–5000+ I/O |
| Expandability | Limited add-on cassettes | Slot-based expansion |
| Size | Single brick / shoe-box | Multiple modules on a rack |
| Cost | Low for small systems | Economical at scale |

- **Application of Fixed PLC:** A small packaging machine controlling a few conveyors and a motor starter.
- **Application of Modular PLC:** An automotive assembly line controlling multiple workstations, robots, and safety interlocks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions. Each carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7**.

---

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a modular Programmable Logic Controller. Discuss the function of each block in detail. Also explain the scan cycle of the PLC with a suitable flowchart.*

#### Part (a) — Architecture Block Diagram and Function of Each Block (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A modular PLC consists of the following interconnected blocks, all mounted on a common **rack** with a **backplane** bus:

1. **Power Supply Module (1 Mark):** Accepts 230 V AC mains (or 24 V DC) and produces isolated +5 V, ±15 V, +24 V DC rails for the CPU, memory, and I/O modules. It also supplies sensor power to the field. Provides **1500 V isolation** between field and logic.

2. **CPU Module (1.5 Marks):** Contains the **ALU, Control Unit, registers, and timers**. The ALU executes Boolean and arithmetic operations; the Control Unit fetches, decodes, and executes instructions from program memory. The CPU runs the **cyclic scan**: read inputs → execute program → update outputs.

3. **Memory Module (1 Mark):** Partitioned into:
   - System ROM (firmware/OS)
   - RAM (I/O image, data, internal relays)
   - Flash/EEPROM (user program, retentive data)
   - The I/O image table resides in battery-backed RAM.

4. **Input Modules (1 Mark):** DI and AI modules with on-board signal conditioning (filter, optocoupler, ADC). Convert field signals (**24 V DC, 120 V AC, 4–20 mA, RTD, TC**) to logic-level digital codes.

5. **Output Modules (1 Mark):** DO (relay, transistor, TRIAC) and AO (DAC-based) modules. Provide isolation and current/voltage amplification to drive field actuators.

6. **Programming Device (0.5 Mark):** PC with vendor software (e.g., TIA Portal, RSLogix 500). Connected via USB/Ethernet/Serial. Used to write, download, monitor, and debug the user program.

7. **Communication / Network Interface (0.5 Mark):** Ethernet, Profibus, Modbus TCP, DeviceNet, etc., enabling HMI, SCADA, and remote I/O connectivity.

8. **Rack and Backplane (0.5 Mark):** Mechanical chassis holding modules; backplane carries **address, data, control, and power** lines between modules and the CPU.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above.)

#### Part (b) — PLC Scan Cycle Flowchart and Explanation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The PLC operates on a **deterministic cyclic scan**, which consists of four main steps:

**Step 1 — Self-Test / Housekeeping (1 Mark):** On power-up, the CPU performs a **diagnostic check** of memory, I/O bus, and watch-dog timer. If all OK, it begins cyclic operation.

**Step 2 — Input Scan (1.5 Marks):** The CPU reads the status of every physical input (DI and AI) and stores the values in the **input image table** (a section of RAM). This snapshot is used for the entire program execution of that scan.

**Step 3 — Program Execution (2.5 Marks):** The CPU executes the user program **sequentially**, rung-by-rung in the case of ladder logic. For each rung, the CPU reads the input bits (from the input image), evaluates the Boolean logic, and stores the result in the **output image table** (or in internal memory). Analog values are processed via arithmetic and comparison instructions.

**Step 4 — Output Scan (1 Mark):** At the end of program execution, the contents of the output image table are written to the **physical output modules** in one parallel update. This simultaneous update prevents intermediate inconsistent output states.

**Step 5 — Housekeeping and Communication (1 Mark):** The CPU services communication requests (HMI, SCADA, peer PLCs), updates internal timers, and prepares for the next scan.

**Flowchart:** (Refer to Section 4.2 above.)

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - ❌ Drawing the input scan and program execution as **parallel** operations — they are **strictly sequential** within one scan.
> - ❌ Forgetting to mention that inputs are read ONCE per scan into the image — students often incorrectly say "the input is checked continuously."
> - ❌ Skipping the housekeeping step — KTU expects it as a separate phase.
> - ❌ Not labeling the diagram with bidirectional arrows for programming/communication — half-mark loss per missing label.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question — Alternative Set]**
*Explain the I/O addressing scheme of a PLC with suitable examples. Differentiate between fixed and modular I/O configurations. A process requires 96 digital inputs, 48 digital outputs, 12 analog inputs, and 4 analog outputs. Select suitable modules (16-pt DI, 16-pt DO, 8-pt AI, 4-pt AO) and determine the total number of slots, total scan time, and total power consumption.*

#### Part (a) — I/O Addressing Scheme and Fixed vs Modular I/O (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**I/O Addressing Scheme (4 Marks):**

Every input and output in a modular PLC is uniquely identified by a multi-level address following the pattern:

$$
\text{Address} = \langle \text{Type} \rangle : \langle \text{Slot} \rangle : \langle \text{Word/Bit} \rangle
$$

**Examples (Allen-Bradley SLC 500 convention):**
- `I:2/7` → Input, Slot 2, Bit 7
- `O:3/0` → Output, Slot 3, Bit 0
- `I:4.5` → Input, Slot 4, Word 5 (analog word)
- `O:5.2` → Output, Slot 5, Word 2 (analog word)

For analog signals, the address refers to a **16-bit WORD** rather than a single bit. The full-scale engineering value (e.g., 4–20 mA) is **linear-scaled** to a raw count (0–32767 for 15-bit signed or 0–4095 for 12-bit).

**Scaling Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{N}{N_{max}} \right) \times \left( E_{max} - E_{min} \right) + E_{min}
$$

where $E$ is the engineering value, $N$ is the raw ADC count, and $N_{max}$ is the maximum count.

**Fixed vs Modular I/O (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed I/O | Modular I/O |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Mounting | Built into CPU | Separate modules on rack |
| Selection | Pre-defined | Engineer selects modules per signal type |
| Hot-swap | Not possible | Possible (with redundant PSU) |
| Cost | Low | Slightly higher per module |

#### Part (b) — Module Selection, Slot Count, Scan Time, and Power (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Number of Each Module Type (2 Marks):**

$$
N_{DI} = \left\lceil \dfrac{96}{16} \right\rceil = 6 \text{ modules}
$$

$$
N_{DO} = \left\lceil \dfrac{48}{16} \right\rceil = 3 \text{ modules}
$$

$$
N_{AI} = \left\lceil \dfrac{12}{8} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ modules (8 pt each, 4 spares)}
$$

$$
N_{AO} = \left\lceil \dfrac{4}{4} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ module}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Slots Required (1.5 Marks):**

$$
N_{slots} = 1_{CPU} + 1_{PSU} + 6_{DI} + 3_{DO} + 2_{AI} + 1_{AO} = 14 \text{ slots}
$$

Select a **16-slot rack** to provide 2 spare slots for future expansion.

**Step 3 — Total Scan Time (2 Marks):**

Assume update time per DI/DO point = **4 µs**, per AI point = **60 µs**, per AO point = **80 µs**, program execution = **2 ms**, housekeeping = **1.5 ms**.

$$
T_{input} = (96 \times 4) + (12 \times 60) = 384 + 720 = 1104\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{output} = (48 \times 4) + (4 \times 80) = 192 + 320 = 512\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{program} = 2000\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{housekeeping} = 1500\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 1104 + 2000 + 512 + 1500 = 5116\,\mu s \approx 5.12\,ms
$$

**Step 4 — Total Power Consumption (1.5 Marks):**

Assume per-point consumption at 24 V:

- DI: 5 mA per point → $96 \times 5\,mA = 480\,mA$
- DO (transistor): 10 mA per point (logic-side) → $48 \times 10 = 480\,mA$
- AI: 20 mA per channel → $12 \times 20 = 240\,mA$
- AO: 30 mA per channel → $4 \times 30 = 120\,mA$

$$
I_{total} = 480 + 480 + 240 + 120 = 1320\,mA \text{ at } 24\,V
$$

$$
P_{total} = 24 \times 1.32 = 31.68\,W
$$

Select a **40 W power supply** to allow **25% spare capacity**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - ❌ Using **floor** instead of **ceiling** (round-up) in the slot count formula — partial-load modules still occupy a full slot.
> - ❌ Forgetting the **CPU and PSU** in the slot count — KTU explicitly includes them.
> - ❌ Mixing up **input scan** and **output scan** in the order of operations in the scan cycle.
> - ❌ Not adding a safety margin (20–30%) to the power supply sizing — a hard-working PSU has a much shorter life.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — must memorize before the exam.**

- ✅ A PLC is configured from **6 building blocks**: Power Supply, CPU, Memory, Input Modules, Output Modules, Programming Device.
- ✅ Three configuration philosophies: **Fixed (compact), Modular, Distributed**.
- ✅ The **scan cycle** is **4-step**: Input scan → Program execution → Output scan → Housekeeping.
- ✅ Scan time formula: $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{housekeep}$.
- ✅ I/O image table ensures **deterministic** behavior — inputs are sampled once per scan.
- ✅ **ADC resolution**: $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1}$ (12-bit ADC for 0–10 V gives ~2.44 mV/LSB).
- ✅ **Slot count formula**: $N_{slots} = \lceil N_{IO} / N_{per\,module} \rceil$ — always round **up**.
- ✅ **DI module** types: AC input, DC input (sinking/sourcing), and Voltage-free contact input.
- ✅ **DO module** types: Relay (slow, universal), Transistor (DC, fast), TRIAC (AC, fast).
- ✅ **Isolation** between field and logic is **1500 V RMS** (typical), provided by **optocouplers**.
- ✅ Power supply sizing: add **25–30%** spare capacity.
- ✅ For high-speed inputs (pulse width < $T_{scan}$), use a **High-Speed Counter (HSC) module**.
- ✅ IEC 61131-3 standardizes **address prefix**: `%I` for input, `%Q` for output, `%M` for memory.
- ✅ **Modular PLCs** use a **backplane** that carries data, address, control, and power signals.
- ✅ Distributed I/O uses **field buses** (Profibus, Profinet, Modbus TCP, DeviceNet, EtherNet/IP).
- ✅ **Local I/O** is in the same rack as the CPU; **Remote I/O** is on a separate chassis connected via a network.
- ✅ A **retain**-attribute variable preserves its value across a power cycle (stored in non-volatile memory).
- ✅ For redundant systems, use **hot-standby CPUs** with **fiber-optic sync links**.
- ✅ Always include **surge protection** (MOV, TVS) on inductive loads wired to relay outputs.
- ✅ Signal types: **DI = bit, AI = word**; **DO = bit, AO = word** in the address space.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scan Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scan Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **PLC Scan Cycle** (also called the *Operating Cycle* or *Sweep Cycle*) is the continuous, sequential, and cyclic procedure by which a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) reads the status of all input devices, executes the user program stored in memory, and updates the status of all output devices. The scan cycle is the heartbeat of the PLC and guarantees *deterministic*, *repetitive*, and *time-bound* execution of control logic.

Mathematically, the total scan time is expressed as:

$$T_{\text{scan}} = T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{program}} + T_{\text{output}} + T_{\text{housekeeping}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OERUT722 - Module 1):**
> The scan cycle is a foundational concept tested under **CO1** (Understand the architecture and operation of PLCs). KTU examiners frequently test the *order* of execution, *worst-case scan time*, and the *effect of scan time on process response*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **security guard** making rounds in a factory every 5 minutes:

1. **Round Start (Housekeeping):** The guard checks his own health (battery, uniform, communication device).
2. **Look at all doors and windows (Input Scan):** He notes whether every door is open or closed, every window broken or intact.
3. **Decide what to do (Program Scan):** Based on his instruction manual (the program), he decides which lights to turn on, which alarms to raise, which doors to lock.
4. **Take action (Output Scan):** He physically flips the switches, raises the alarms, and locks the doors.
5. **Repeat:** After finishing the round, he goes back to step 1.

A PLC behaves **exactly like this guard**, but at electronic speed — typically completing thousands of scans every second. The scan time is the time taken for one complete "round."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a normal computer that runs programs once and stops, a PLC **continuously loops** through the scan cycle for its entire operational life. This is what makes PLCs ideal for real-time industrial control.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Scan Time (T_scan)** | **1 ms – 50 ms** | milliseconds (ms) |
| **Input Scan Time (T_input)** | **0.5 ms – 3 ms** | milliseconds |
| **Program Scan Time (T_program)** | **0.1 ms/instruction – 10 µs/instruction** | ms or µs |
| **Output Scan Time (T_output)** | **0.5 ms – 3 ms** | milliseconds |
| **Housekeeping Time (T_housekeeping)** | **0.5 ms – 5 ms** | milliseconds |
| **I/O Update Method** | **Cyclic (batch) / Immediate / Interrupt-driven** | — |

> [!TIP]
> For slow processes (e.g., conveyor belts, tank level control), a scan time of **20–50 ms** is acceptable. For high-speed processes (e.g., bottling, packaging, motion control), scan times of **1–5 ms** are mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Phases of the Scan Cycle (Detailed Breakdown)

A PLC scan cycle consists of **four distinct sequential phases**. The order is rigid and never changes during normal operation.

#### Phase 1: Input Scan (Reading Inputs)

- The CPU interrogates each **input module** through the input I/O bus.
- The status (ON/OFF) of every physical input (limit switches, sensors, pushbuttons) is read.
- These statuses are stored in a dedicated memory region called the **Process Image Input (PII)** table or **Input Image Table (IIT)**.
- The physical inputs are *frozen* during program execution — even if an actual input changes mid-scan, the program will only see the value latched at the start of the input scan.
- Analog inputs are digitized (via ADC) and stored as integer values in the PII.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why freeze the inputs?** This prevents *race conditions* and ensures *deterministic behavior*. If inputs were read continuously, the same program could produce different outputs within the same scan if an input changed mid-execution.

#### Phase 2: Program Scan (Executing Logic)

- The CPU fetches instructions from program memory (RAM/Flash) **one by one**.
- It executes them in the order they appear in the **ladder logic** (or FBD, STL) program.
- Each instruction operates on the values in the **Input Image Table** and the **internal memory** (M, V, T, C, etc.).
- Results of logic operations that drive physical outputs are *temporarily* stored in the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table — **not** sent to the physical outputs yet.
- Special function blocks (timers, counters, math) are evaluated.

#### Phase 3: Output Scan (Updating Outputs)

- The CPU transfers the contents of the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table to the physical output modules.
- Each output module latches its corresponding output ON or OFF, energizing or de-energizing field devices (contactors, solenoids, valves, lamps).
- Analog outputs are converted back via DAC.
- The outputs remain *latched* until the *next* output scan updates them.

#### Phase 4: Housekeeping / Overhead Tasks

This phase includes critical support functions:

- **Communication processing:** Handling requests from HMI, SCADA, programming terminals (Ethernet, RS-485, Profibus).
- **Self-diagnostics:** RAM check, battery check, module health monitoring, watchdog timer reset.
- **Memory management:** Updating retentive memory, handling password protections.
- **Interrupt servicing:** Acknowledging pending interrupts (high-priority I/O, fault handlers).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Scan Time** | $T_{\text{scan}} = T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{prog}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{ovh}}$ | ms | Sum of all four phases |
| 2 | **Program Scan Time** | $T_{\text{prog}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i$ | ms | Sum over all $N$ executed instructions |
| 3 | **Average Scan Time** | $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_{j=1}^{K} T_{\text{scan},j}$ | ms | Mean over $K$ consecutive scans |
| 4 | **Worst-Case Scan Time** | $T_{\text{worst}} = T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{prog,max}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{ovh,max}}$ | ms | Used for safety-critical sizing |
| 5 | **Maximum Program Throughput** | $P_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}}$ | scans/sec | Inverse of scan time |
| 6 | **Process Response Time (lower bound)** | $T_{\text{resp}} \approx 2 \cdot T_{\text{scan}}$ | ms | Input change → output reaction |
| 7 | **I/O Points Limit (rule of thumb)** | $N_{\text{IO}} \le \dfrac{T_{\text{scan,limit}}}{k \cdot t_{\text{pt}}}$ | points | $k$ = scan safety factor, $t_{\text{pt}}$ = per-point I/O time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Scan time directly determines **process response latency**. In high-speed packaging lines, if $T_{\text{scan}} = 20$ ms, the system may miss a product moving at high speed. Engineers therefore either (a) choose faster PLCs, (b) use **immediate I/O** instructions, or (c) employ **high-speed counters and interrupts** to bypass the standard scan cycle.

### 2.3 Determinism and the Watchdog Timer

- The scan cycle guarantees **deterministic execution** — for a given input pattern and program, the output is always produced within a bounded time.
- A **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** is an internal hardware timer that resets the CPU if a scan cycle takes longer than the preset maximum (default typically **100–500 ms**).
- If the WDT trips, the PLC enters a **fault mode** and typically forces all outputs to a safe (OFF) state.

### 2.4 I/O Updating Methods

| Method | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Cyclic (Batch) Update** | I/O updated once per scan (default) | Most general applications |
| **Immediate I/O** | Specific I/O updated *during* program scan via special instructions (e.g., `REF`, `IOT`, `IIM`, `IOM`) | Time-critical I/O |
| **Interrupt-Driven** | High-priority I/O triggers subroutine, suspending the scan | High-speed counting, alarms |
| **Periodic I/O** | I/O updated at fixed sub-scan intervals | Analog loops, PID |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Scan Time Problems

When solving KTU scan-time numerical problems, follow this **5-step matrix**:

1. **Identify I/O points** (digital input, digital output, analog input, analog output).
2. **Compute $T_{\text{in}}$ and $T_{\text{out}}$** using the manufacturer's per-point scan overhead.
3. **Compute $T_{\text{prog}}$** by summing per-instruction execution times for the *entire* program.
4. **Add $T_{\text{ovh}}$** (housekeeping, given or assumed).
5. **Apply the watchdog constraint:** Check if $T_{\text{scan}} < T_{\text{WDT,set}}$.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Calculating Worst-Case Scan Time

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Pattern):**
A PLC system has the following specifications:

- Number of digital inputs: **64**
- Number of digital outputs: **48**
- Number of analog inputs: **8**
- Number of analog outputs: **4**
- Per digital I/O point scan time: **$t_{\text{D}} = 5 \ \mu s$**
- Per analog I/O point scan time: **$t_{\text{A}} = 100 \ \mu s$**
- Total program instructions: **$N = 1200$**
- Average instruction execution time: **$t_{\text{inst}} = 1.5 \ \mu s$**
- Housekeeping time: **$T_{\text{ovh}} = 3 \ \text{ms}$**
- Watchdog timer setting: **$T_{\text{WDT}} = 50 \ \text{ms}$**

**Find:** Worst-case scan time and verify whether the WDT will trip.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute Input Scan Time $T_{\text{in}}$

The input scan reads all digital inputs and analog inputs:

$$
T_{\text{in}} = \left(N_{\text{DI}} + N_{\text{AI}}\right)_{\text{weighted}}
$$

For mixed digital/analog:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{in}} &= N_{\text{DI}} \cdot t_{\text{D}} + N_{\text{AI}} \cdot t_{\text{A}} \\
&= (64)(5 \ \mu s) + (8)(100 \ \mu s) \\
&= 320 \ \mu s + 800 \ \mu s \\
&= 1120 \ \mu s \\
&= 1.12 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Program Scan Time $T_{\text{prog}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{prog}} &= N \cdot t_{\text{inst}} \\
&= 1200 \cdot 1.5 \ \mu s \\
&= 1800 \ \mu s \\
&= 1.80 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Output Scan Time $T_{\text{out}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{out}} &= N_{\text{DO}} \cdot t_{\text{D}} + N_{\text{AO}} \cdot t_{\text{A}} \\
&= (48)(5 \ \mu s) + (4)(100 \ \mu s) \\
&= 240 \ \mu s + 400 \ \mu s \\
&= 640 \ \mu s \\
&= 0.64 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Add Housekeeping Time $T_{\text{ovh}}$

Given directly as $T_{\text{ovh}} = 3 \ \text{ms}$.

#### Step 5 — Compute Total Worst-Case Scan Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{prog}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{ovh}} \\
&= 1.12 \ \text{ms} + 1.80 \ \text{ms} + 0.64 \ \text{ms} + 3.00 \ \text{ms} \\
&= 6.56 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Compare With Watchdog Timer

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= 6.56 \ \text{ms} \\
T_{\text{WDT}} &= 50 \ \text{ms} \\
\text{Since} \quad 6.56 \ \text{ms} &< 50 \ \text{ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{WDT does NOT trip.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The PLC operates safely. The margin is $50 - 6.56 = 43.44 \ \text{ms}$ (≈ 86.9% margin), so the system can tolerate up to a **~7.6× increase** in scan time before tripping the watchdog.

### 3.3 Derivation of Minimum Process Response Time

**Why minimum response time equals $2 \cdot T_{\text{scan}}$:**

The worst-case scenario for an input change to produce a corresponding output change is:

1. The input changes *just after* the input scan has latched the old value — the change is missed this scan.
2. The next scan catches the new input value at the *next* input scan.
3. The program executes and computes the new output.
4. The output scan at the *end of the same scan* applies the new output.

Total elapsed time = (remaining of current scan) + (full next scan). The *upper bound* of this is:

$$
T_{\text{resp, max}} \approx 2 \cdot T_{\text{scan}}
$$

The *average* response time is:

$$
T_{\text{resp, avg}} \approx 1.5 \cdot T_{\text{scan}}
$$

### 3.4 Margin Analysis — Safety Factor Formula

For safety-critical systems, KTU expects engineers to size the scan with a safety factor:

$$
\text{Safety Factor} = \dfrac{T_{\text{WDT}} - T_{\text{scan}}}{T_{\text{scan}}} \times 100\%
$$

For our example:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SF} &= \frac{50 - 6.56}{6.56} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{43.44}{6.56} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 662.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Rule of Thumb:** Maintain a safety factor of at least **300–500%** to accommodate future program expansion, communication loading, and I/O growth.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The PLC Scan Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Power ON]) --> startB([Initialize CPU])
    startB --> stepA[Housekeeping Phase]
    stepA --> stepB[Self Diagnostics]
    stepB --> stepC[Communication Tasks]
    stepC --> stepD[Memory Check]
    stepD --> stepE[Watchdog Reset]
    stepE --> stepF[Input Scan Phase]
    stepF --> stepG[Read All Digital Inputs]
    stepG --> stepH[Read All Analog Inputs]
    stepH --> stepI[Store in Process Image Input Table]
    stepI --> stepJ[Program Scan Phase]
    stepJ --> stepK[Fetch Instruction 1]
    stepK --> stepL[Execute Ladder Logic]
    stepL --> stepM[Update Timers and Counters]
    stepM --> stepN[Compute Results to Output Image Table]
    stepN --> stepO[Output Scan Phase]
    stepO --> stepP[Transfer Output Image to Modules]
    stepP --> stepQ[Energize Field Devices]
    stepQ --> loopQ{Continue Running?}
    loopQ -- Yes --> stepA
    loopQ -- No --> endA([STOP or Fault])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Scan Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph field1["FIELD DEVICES"]
        s1["Limit Switch"]
        s2["Proximity Sensor"]
        s3["Push Button"]
        a1["Temperature Sensor"]
    end

    subgraph inputm["INPUT MODULES"]
        di["Digital Input Module"]
        ai["Analog Input Module"]
    end

    subgraph cpumem["CPU AND MEMORY"]
        pii["Process Image Input Table"]
        prog["User Program RAM"]
        pio["Process Image Output Table"]
        cpu["CPU Execution Unit"]
    end

    subgraph outputm["OUTPUT MODULES"]
        do["Digital Output Module"]
        ao["Analog Output Module"]
    end

    subgraph field2["FIELD ACTUATORS"]
        m1["Motor Contactor"]
        v1["Solenoid Valve"]
        l1["Indicator Lamp"]
        h1["Heater Element"]
    end

    s1 --> di
    s2 --> di
    s3 --> di
    a1 --> ai

    di --> pii
    ai --> pii

    pii --> cpu
    prog --> cpu
    cpu --> pio

    pio --> do
    pio --> ao

    do --> m1
    do --> v1
    do --> l1
    ao --> h1

    prog -.READS.-> pii
    cpu -.WRITES.-> pio
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Input-to-Output Timing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant F as Field Input
    participant IM as Input Module
    participant PII as Input Image
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant PIO as Output Image
    participant OM as Output Module
    participant A as Actuator

    Note over F,IM: T0 - Input changes in field
    F->>IM: Physical ON/OFF signal
    IM-->>PII: Latched value (Phase 1)
    Note over PII: Input Scan Complete
    PII->>CPU: Read image (Phase 2)
    CPU->>PIO: Write computed outputs
    Note over PIO: Program Scan Complete
    PIO->>OM: Transfer values (Phase 3)
    OM->>A: Energize actuator
    Note over A: T_scan later - Physical reaction
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Watchdog Behavior

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal: Power ON
    Normal --> Scanning: Begin Cycle
    Scanning --> Normal: T_scan < T_WDT
    Scanning --> Fault: T_scan > T_WDT
    Fault --> SafeState: All Outputs OFF
    SafeState --> Normal: Operator Reset
    Normal --> Stop: Operator Command
    Stop --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "PLC Scan Cycle" and list its four phases in the correct order of execution.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PLC Scan Cycle** is the continuous, sequential process by which a PLC reads inputs, executes the control program, and updates outputs in a repeating loop.

The four phases in execution order are:

1. **Housekeeping / Overhead Phase** — Diagnostics, communication, watchdog reset.
2. **Input Scan Phase** — Reads all physical inputs and stores them in the Process Image Input (PII) table.
3. **Program Scan Phase** — Executes the user program (ladder logic) using the PII values, computes output values, and stores them in the Process Image Output (PIO) table.
4. **Output Scan Phase** — Transfers the PIO values to the physical output modules to energize/de-energize field devices.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correct sequence with brief description)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State the role of the Watchdog Timer in a PLC scan cycle. What happens when it trips?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** is an internal hardware timer that monitors the duration of each scan cycle. It is set to a maximum permissible scan time (typically 100–500 ms).

**Role (1 mark):** It ensures the PLC does not enter an infinite loop or hang due to a programming fault, hardware failure, or excessive communication loading — preserving the deterministic nature of control.

**Action on trip (2 marks):**
- The CPU is immediately forced into **FAULT mode**.
- **All outputs are driven to a predefined SAFE state** (usually OFF).
- A fault indicator is set, and the fault code is logged in the diagnostic buffer.
- The PLC requires **operator intervention** (manual reset) to resume operation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a PLC scan cycle**. Clearly describe each phase and its function. Discuss why the input values are *frozen* during the program execution phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLC system has **128 digital inputs**, **96 digital outputs**, **16 analog inputs**, and **8 analog outputs**. The per-point scan time for digital I/O is **$8 \ \mu s$** and for analog I/O is **$120 \ \mu s$**. The user program contains **2500 instructions** with an average execution time of **$2 \ \mu s$** per instruction. Housekeeping time is **$4 \ \text{ms}$** and the Watchdog Timer is set to **$80 \ \text{ms}$**.
**(i)** Calculate the total scan time.
**(ii)** Determine the safety factor and verify whether the WDT will trip.
**(iii)** What is the maximum number of scans per second the PLC can execute? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Architecture & Phases (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Inputs] --> [Input Module] --> [Input Image Table (PII)]
                                          |
                                          v
                                  [CPU executes program]
                                          |
                                          v
                                  [Output Image Table (PIO)]
                                          |
                                          v
[Outputs] <-- [Output Module] <-- [PIO data transferred]
```

Alongside the above, **housekeeping** (diagnostics, comms, watchdog reset) wraps the cycle.

**Phase Description (4 marks):**

1. **Housekeeping (1 mark):** Before each scan, the CPU performs self-tests on memory, modules, and battery; handles communication with HMI/SCADA; services interrupts; resets the watchdog timer.

2. **Input Scan (1 mark):** The CPU reads the ON/OFF status of every digital input and digitizes every analog input via ADC. These values are written into the **Process Image Input Table (PII)**, a reserved memory region.

3. **Program Scan (1 mark):** The CPU executes the user program sequentially. Each ladder rung is evaluated using values from the PII and internal memory (timers, counters, markers). Computed output values are written to the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table, *not yet* to physical outputs.

4. **Output Scan (1 mark):** The PIO contents are transferred to the output modules, which energize/de-energize the physical actuators (contactors, valves, lamps).

**Why inputs are frozen (1 mark):**
- Prevents **race conditions** (an input changing mid-program producing inconsistent outputs).
- Guarantees **deterministic and repeatable** program execution — essential for safety-critical and time-sensitive industrial control.
- Ensures that **all rungs see the same input snapshot**, making logic evaluation consistent.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Total Scan Time (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{in}} &= N_{\text{DI}} \cdot t_{\text{D}} + N_{\text{AI}} \cdot t_{\text{A}} \\
&= (128)(8) + (16)(120) \\
&= 1024 + 1920 \\
&= 2944 \ \mu s = 2.944 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{out}} &= N_{\text{DO}} \cdot t_{\text{D}} + N_{\text{AO}} \cdot t_{\text{A}} \\
&= (96)(8) + (8)(120) \\
&= 768 + 960 \\
&= 1728 \ \mu s = 1.728 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{prog}} &= N \cdot t_{\text{inst}} = 2500 \cdot 2 \ \mu s = 5000 \ \mu s = 5.000 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ovh}} &= 4.000 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= 2.944 + 5.000 + 1.728 + 4.000 \\
&= 13.672 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Safety Factor & WDT Verification (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SF} &= \frac{T_{\text{WDT}} - T_{\text{scan}}}{T_{\text{scan}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{80 - 13.672}{13.672} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{66.328}{13.672} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 485.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $13.672 \ \text{ms} \ll 80 \ \text{ms}$, the **WDT will NOT trip**. A safety factor of ~485% is excellent for future expansion.

**(iii) Maximum Scans Per Second (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{max}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{13.672 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&\approx 73.14 \ \text{scans/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **deterministic execution** in a PLC scan cycle. How does it differ from a normal personal computer's program execution? Discuss the role of **immediate I/O instructions** in reducing scan-related latency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bottle-filling line uses a PLC with a scan time of **$8 \ \text{ms}$**. A photoelectric sensor detects each bottle and the PLC must energize a filling solenoid within **$15 \ \text{ms}$** for proper filling.
**(i)** Determine whether the standard cyclic scan can meet this requirement.
**(ii)** If not, suggest two methods to reduce the response time below 15 ms.
**(iii)** Calculate the maximum allowable scan time to guarantee a 100% safety margin for this application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Determinism and Immediate I/O (7 marks):**

**Deterministic Execution (3 marks):**
A PLC is classified as a **deterministic real-time controller**. This means that for a given input condition, the output response is produced within a **bounded, predictable time** on every scan. The maximum time is fixed by the worst-case scan time $T_{\text{worst}}$. The **Watchdog Timer** enforces this bound.

**Comparison with PC (2 marks):**

| Aspect | PLC | Personal Computer |
|---|---|---|
| Execution model | Cyclic, continuous, deterministic | Event-driven, non-deterministic |
| Priority | Real-time control | User convenience |
| Outputs after scan | Always refreshed | Programs stop on completion |
| WDT enforcement | Yes (hardware) | Yes (software) |
| OS | Real-time OS / bare metal | Windows / Linux (non-RT) |
| Failure mode | Outputs to SAFE state | May hang silently |

**Immediate I/O Instructions (2 marks):**
- Instructions like `IIM` (Immediate Input), `IOM` (Immediate Output), or `REF` allow the CPU to **bypass** the normal PII/PIO update mechanism and read/write a specific I/O point *during* the program scan.
- This drastically reduces the **input-to-output latency** for time-critical I/O without altering the entire scan cycle.
- Used selectively for safety interlocks, high-speed counting, and emergency stops.

---

**Part (b) — Bottle-Filling Application (7 marks):**

**(i) Feasibility Check (2 marks):**

Worst-case response time using cyclic scan:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{resp, max}} &\approx 2 \cdot T_{\text{scan}} \\
&= 2 \cdot 8 \\
&= 16 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $16 \ \text{ms} > 15 \ \text{ms}$ requirement, **the standard cyclic scan CANNOT reliably meet** the 15 ms requirement. There is a **-1 ms deficit** in the worst case.

**(ii) Two Methods to Reduce Response Time (3 marks):**

1. **Use Immediate I/O Instructions:** Replace the standard input read with an `IIM` and the solenoid output with an `IOM`. This forces the input to be read at that exact point in the program and the output to be energized immediately — bypassing PII/PIO latency.

2. **Use a Faster PLC or Reduce Program Size:** Upgrade to a PLC with a higher instruction execution rate (e.g., from 1.5 µs/inst to 0.5 µs/inst) and/or optimize the ladder program to remove redundant rungs. Alternatively, **split the scan** using periodic task classes.

**(iii) Maximum Allowable Scan Time (2 marks):**

For a 100% safety margin, the worst-case response must equal half the requirement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot T_{\text{scan, max allowable}} &= 15 \ \text{ms} \\
T_{\text{scan, max allowable}} &= 7.5 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PLC must be configured (or upgraded) to ensure $T_{\text{scan}} \le 7.5 \ \text{ms}$ to guarantee $T_{\text{resp, max}} = 15 \ \text{ms}$ with zero margin, or smaller to add a positive margin.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Wrong order of phases** — Many students write Input → Output → Program. **Correct order is: Housekeeping → Input → Program → Output.** (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting housekeeping time** — Numerical answers without $T_{\text{ovh}}$ are incomplete. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Unit confusion** — Mixing µs with ms in the final answer. Always convert to a common unit before summing. (Lose 0.5–1 mark.)
> 4. **Confusing scan time with response time** — Scan time is *one* cycle; response time can be up to **2× scan time** in the worst case.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify WDT constraint** — KTU always awards a mark for the final comparison statement ("WDT will/will not trip").
> 6. **Writing PC analogies** — In PLC theory, avoid stating that "PLC is like a computer." Use the correct terminology: *deterministic real-time controller*.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **PLC scan cycle** is the fundamental execution loop consisting of **Housekeeping → Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan**.
- ✅ Total scan time: $T_{\text{scan}} = T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{prog}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{ovh}}$.
- ✅ **Typical scan times:** 1–50 ms depending on PLC class and program complexity.
- ✅ **Inputs are frozen** in the Process Image Input (PII) table at the start of each scan to ensure **deterministic, race-condition-free** execution.
- ✅ **Outputs are latched** in the Process Image Output (PIO) table and updated only at the **end** of the scan via the output scan.
- ✅ **Worst-case response time** $\approx 2 \cdot T_{\text{scan}}$; **average response time** $\approx 1.5 \cdot T_{\text{scan}}$.
- ✅ **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** monitors each scan and forces the PLC to **fault / safe-state** if the cycle exceeds the preset limit.
- ✅ **Safety factor** should be $\geq 300\text{–}500\%$ for industrial systems to accommodate growth.
- ✅ For **time-critical I/O**, use **immediate I/O instructions** or **interrupt-driven routines** instead of relying on cyclic updates.
- ✅ KTU exam trick: Always state the **order of phases** AND define each in **one sentence** to score full marks on theory questions.
- ✅ Numerical problems: Convert all times to a **common unit (ms)** before summing, and end with an explicit **WDT verification statement**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capabilities of PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Capabilities of PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, industrially hardened digital computer specifically designed for the real-time control of industrial automation processes. The term **"Capabilities of a PLC"** refers to the complete range of functional, operational, and performance features that a modern PLC provides to execute deterministic control tasks in harsh industrial environments.

According to the **NEMA ICS 3-240** and **IEC 61131-3** standards, the capabilities of a PLC encompass its ability to perform logical sequencing, timing, counting, arithmetic computation, data manipulation, continuous (PID) control, communication networking, and self-diagnostics, all within a deterministic scan cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to identify, compare, and justify the **Capabilities of PLC** as a distinct feature set differentiating it from conventional relays, microcontrollers, and DCS (Distributed Control System) controllers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The PLC as a "Digital Factory Brain"**

Imagine a factory as a large kitchen. The **input devices** (temperature sensors, limit switches, push buttons) are the *ingredients* arriving at the kitchen. The **output devices** (motors, solenoids, heaters, indicator lamps) are the *dishes* to be served. The **PLC** acts as the *head chef* who:
- Constantly inspects the ingredients (**Input Scanning**)
- Follows a written recipe in a logical order (**Ladder Logic Execution**)
- Serves the prepared dishes in the right sequence (**Output Updating**)
- Keeps track of cooking time and number of portions (**Timing & Counting**)
- Calls the supplier if a rare spice is needed (**Communication & Networking**)
- Tests his own burners before starting (**Self-Diagnostics**)

Just as the chef performs all these tasks **reliably, repeatedly, and rapidly**, the PLC executes the same capabilities in milliseconds inside an industrial plant.

### 1.3 Core Capabilities - KTU Board Perspective

The major capabilities of a PLC, as listed in the KTU 2024 syllabus, are:

- **Sequential Control Capability**
- **Logical Operation Capability** (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, NAND, NOR)
- **Timing and Counting Capability**
- **Arithmetic and Data Handling Capability**
- **Communication and Networking Capability**
- **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) Control Capability**
- **Self-Diagnostic and Fault Detection Capability**
- **Modular and Expandable I/O Capability**
- **High-Speed Counter and Pulse Train Output Capability**
- **Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Integration Capability**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"List the capabilities of PLC"*, always present them as a **classified list** (Control Capabilities, Communication Capabilities, Diagnostic Capabilities) to earn maximum marks. A mere bullet list is considered incomplete by KTU board evaluators.

### 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Scan Time** | **1 ms to 50 ms** | Defines real-time determinism |
| **I/O Capacity** | **32 to 8192 points** | Scalability metric |
| **Memory (RAM)** | **4 KB to 32 MB** | Program storage capacity |
| **Operating Temp** | **0 °C to 60 °C** | Industrial ruggedness |
| **MTBF** | **> 200,000 hours** | Reliability metric |
| **Bit Execution Time** | **0.08 µs to 1 µs** | Processing speed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of PLC Capabilities

#### 2.1.1 Logical Operation Capability

A PLC can execute all fundamental Boolean logic operations. These are implemented at the bit-level within the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** using dedicated logic instruction sets.

- **AND (Series contact logic):** Output is ON only if **all** inputs are ON.
  - *Engineering Utility:* Used in safety interlocks where multiple conditions must hold true (e.g., Guard Door Closed **AND** Start Button Pressed **AND** Emergency Stop Released).
- **OR (Parallel contact logic):** Output is ON if **any** input is ON.
  - *Engineering Utility:* Multi-location start/stop push button stations on long conveyor lines.
- **NOT (Normally Closed contact):** Output is the logical inversion of the input.
  - *Engineering Utility:* Overload relay contact logic for motor protection.
- **XOR (Exclusive OR):** Output is ON only when inputs differ.
  - *Engineering Utility:* Two-pump alternating control systems.
- **NAND / NOR:** Universal logic gates used for combinational logic simplification.
  - *Engineering Utility:* Complex safety circuits and De Morgan's law implementations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept:** Modern PLCs implement these as single instructions executed in **one scan time**, making them far faster than electromechanical relay equivalents.

#### 2.1.2 Timing Capability

PLCs contain dedicated **on-delay (TON)**, **off-delay (TOF)**, and **retentive (RTO)** timer instructions.

- **Time Base:** Standard PLCs use time bases of **1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, and 1 s**.
- **Preset Value (PV):** The user-defined time delay value.
- **Accumulated Value (AC):** The current elapsed time stored in the timer word.
- **Timer Enable Bit:** Set when AC ≥ PV.

The governing equation for a PLC timer is:

$$T_{actual} = T_{base} \times PV$$

where:
- $T_{actual}$ = Actual time delay in seconds
- $T_{base}$ = Time base of the timer (1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, 1 s)
- $PV$ = Preset value (integer count)

#### 2.1.3 Counting Capability

PLCs implement **up-counters (CTU)**, **down-counters (CTD)**, and **up-down counters (CTUD)**.

- **Counter Functionality:** Increments/decrements an accumulator register on every true-to-false transition of the count input.
- **Maximum Count Value:** Determined by the word length of the counter register (typically **16-bit (0 to 32,767)** or **32-bit (0 to 2,147,483,647)**).
- *Engineering Utility:* Counting bottles on a filling line, tracking product batches, position counting on rotary encoders.

#### 2.1.4 Arithmetic and Data Handling Capability

Modern PLCs support integer math, floating-point math, and data transfer operations.

- **Arithmetic Operations:** ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, SQRT, SIN, COS, LOG, EXP.
- **Data Comparison:** EQ, NEQ, GRT, LES, GEQ, LEQ.
- **Data Movement:** MOV, MVM (Masked Move), BTD (Bit Distribute).
- **Data Conversion:** INT-to-REAL, BCD-to-BIN, etc.

The **data word size** typically follows:
- **Bit (1-bit)** → **Byte (8-bit)** → **Word (16-bit)** → **Double Word (32-bit)** → **Long Word (64-bit)** in some high-end PLCs.

#### 2.1.5 Communication and Networking Capability

A PLC can interface with:
- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** panels via serial or Ethernet.
- **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** systems using protocols like **Modbus, Profibus, Ethernet/IP, PROFINET, OPC UA**.
- **Other PLCs** in a peer-to-peer or master-slave configuration.
- **Field Instruments** via HART, Foundation Fieldbus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Hook:** When the topic *Capabilities of PLC* appears in the question paper, examiners often contrast it with **DCS (Distributed Control System)**. Remember: **PLC = Discrete Control** (logic + sequencing), **DCS = Continuous/Process Control** (analog loops + redundancy).

#### 2.1.6 PID Control Capability

Many mid-range and high-end PLCs (e.g., Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Siemens S7-1500) have built-in **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)** instruction blocks for continuous process control.

- The PID algorithm minimizes the error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- The standard parallel form of the PID control law is:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = Controller output (manipulated variable)
- $K_c$ = Proportional gain (dimensionless)
- $T_i$ = Integral time constant (seconds or minutes)
- $T_d$ = Derivative time constant (seconds or minutes)
- $e(t)$ = Error signal
- $SP$ = Setpoint
- $PV$ = Process variable

*Engineering Utility:* Temperature control in furnaces, flow control in pipelines, pressure control in hydraulic systems.

#### 2.1.7 Self-Diagnostic and Fault Detection Capability

PLCs continuously monitor:
- **CPU Watchdog Timer:** Resets the CPU if the scan time exceeds a maximum limit.
- **Memory Integrity:** Checks for parity errors in RAM and checksum errors in ROM.
- **I/O Module Health:** Detects blown fuses, missing modules, short circuits.
- **Battery Status:** Warns of low backup battery for retentive memory.
- **Communication Status:** Reports network errors and broken links.

A status indicator arrangement on the PLC (RUN, FAULT, BATTERY, I/O, COMM) provides real-time visual feedback to the operator.

#### 2.1.8 Modular and Expandable I/O Capability

PLCs are available in:
- **Fixed (Compact) I/O:** Fixed number of I/O points (e.g., 16 in / 16 out).
- **Modular (Rack-Mounted) I/O:** I/O modules are plugged into a backplane rack and can be expanded by adding more racks.

The maximum I/O addressing capacity is governed by:
- **Word Addressing Scheme:** 16 I/O points per word.
- **Total words addressable:** Depends on CPU memory map (e.g., 8 words = 128 points; 256 words = 4096 points).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Standard:** The IEC 61131-3 addressing uses **%I** for input, **%Q** for output, **%M** for memory, and **%DB** for data blocks. Familiarity with this notation earns direct marks.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{scan} = T_{I/O} + T_{prog} + T_{out}$ | Total PLC scan time | ms |
| 2 | $T_{actual} = T_{base} \times PV$ | Timer delay calculation | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{max\,scan} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i + T_{oh}$ | Worst-case scan time | ms |
| 4 | $N_{words} = \lceil N_{points} / 16 \rceil$ | I/O word address calculation | words |
| 5 | $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e\,dt + T_d \frac{de}{dt} \right]$ | PID control law | % output |
| 6 | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Error signal computation | engineering units |
| 7 | $N_{counters} = 2^{n}$ | Maximum count for n-bit counter | counts |
| 8 | $T_{response} = 2 \times T_{scan}$ | Worst-case digital response time | ms |
| 9 | $f_{sampling} \geq \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Sampling rate bound | Hz |
| 10 | $\text{Memory} = n_{instr} \times S_{instr}$ | Program memory estimation | bytes/KB |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

PLC capabilities are not theoretical — they are deployed in:
- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Sequencing robotic welders, conveyors, and torque tools.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Sequential backwash of filters, PID-based chlorine dosing.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum level control, turbine trip interlocks.
- **Food & Beverage Industry:** Batch mixing, bottle filling, capping sequencing.
- **Elevator Control:** Multi-floor logic, door interlocks, overload sensing.
- **Traffic Light Control:** Sequenced timing, count-based phase change.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total PLC Scan Time

The **scan time** is the most critical real-time performance parameter of a PLC. The total scan cycle consists of three sequential phases.

**Phase 1 – Input Scan:**
The CPU reads the status of all physical input devices and stores them in the **Input Image Table (IIT)**.

**Phase 2 – Program Scan (Logic Execution):**
The CPU executes the user program (ladder logic, function block, or structured text) rung by rung, using the current values from the IIT and the Output Image Table (OIT).

**Phase 3 – Output Scan:**
The CPU updates the physical output devices based on the final values stored in the OIT.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} \\
T_{input} &= N_{in} \times t_{bit} \\
T_{output} &= N_{out} \times t_{bit} \\
T_{program} &= N_{rungs} \times t_{instr} + T_{overhead}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the sub-expressions:

$$
T_{scan} = (N_{in} + N_{out}) \cdot t_{bit} + N_{rungs} \cdot t_{instr} + T_{oh}
$$

**Conversion Logic (Step-by-step textual explanation):**
1. **Start with the definition:** Total scan time is the sum of input scan, program execution, and output update times.
2. **Input scan time** is proportional to the number of input points. If each bit is read in $t_{bit}$ microseconds, then for $N_{in}$ inputs, the time is $N_{in} \cdot t_{bit}$.
3. **Output scan time** is similarly proportional to the number of outputs.
4. **Program execution time** depends on the number of rungs and the average instruction execution time $t_{instr}$.
5. **Add the CPU overhead** $T_{oh}$ (memory diagnostics, communication polling, watchdog servicing).
6. **Combine all three phases** to obtain the total scan time.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: I/O Word Addressing Limit

PLC data tables are organized in 16-bit words. The number of words required to address $N$ I/O points is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } N \mod 16 = 0, \quad N_{words} &= N / 16 \\
\text{If } N \mod 16 \neq 0, \quad N_{words} &= \lfloor N / 16 \rfloor + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Unified ceiling formula:

$$
N_{words} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{points}}{16} \right\rceil
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
*Problem:* A PLC has 250 discrete I/O points. How many 16-bit words are required to address them?

**Step 1:** Divide total points by 16.

$$
250 / 16 = 15.625
$$

**Step 2:** Since 15.625 is not an integer, take the ceiling.

$$
N_{words} = 16 \text{ words}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify — 15 full words can address $15 \times 16 = 240$ points, leaving 10 points for the 16th word.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{N_{words} = 16}$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: PID Control Discrete-Time Form

The continuous PID law must be converted to a **discrete-time form** for PLC implementation. We use the **backward difference approximation** for the integral and the **first difference** for the derivative.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau &\;\approx\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k)\,\Delta t \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(Euler's rule for integration)} \\
\dfrac{de(t)}{dt} &\;\approx\; \dfrac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{\Delta t} \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(Backward difference for derivative)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into the continuous PID law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(n) &= K_c \left[ e(n) + \dfrac{\Delta t}{T_i}\sum_{k=0}^{n}e(k) + \dfrac{T_d}{\Delta t}\left(e(n) - e(n-1)\right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

In the **incremental (velocity) form** more commonly used in PLCs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta u(n) &= u(n) - u(n-1) \\
\Delta u(n) &= K_c \left[ \left(e(n) - e(n-1)\right) + \dfrac{\Delta t}{T_i}\,e(n) + \dfrac{T_d}{\Delta t}\left(e(n) - 2e(n-1) + e(n-2)\right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
1. The PLC cannot compute a true integral or derivative — it samples $e(t)$ periodically at interval $\Delta t$.
2. The integral becomes a **running sum** of past errors multiplied by $\Delta t$.
3. The derivative becomes the **difference between the current and previous error**, divided by $\Delta t$.
4. Substituting these into the continuous PID law yields the **discrete-time velocity form** used in PLC PID instruction blocks.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Scan Time vs. PLC Class

| PLC Class | Typical Scan Time | Bit Execution | Memory | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Micro PLC** | **10–20 ms** | **1–5 µs** | **4–16 KB** | Small machines, relay replacement |
| **Compact PLC** | **5–10 ms** | **0.3–1 µs** | **32–128 KB** | OEM machine builders |
| **Modular Mid-Range** | **1–5 ms** | **0.08–0.3 µs** | **256 KB–4 MB** | Factory automation, process skids |
| **High-End / Process PLC** | **0.1–1 ms** | **< 0.08 µs** | **8–32 MB** | DCS integration, safety systems |

### 3.5 Numerical Validation: Scan Time Calculation

**Problem:** A modular PLC has 256 digital inputs, 256 digital outputs, and a program of 1500 rungs. The instruction execution time is 0.1 µs per instruction, and the bit read/write time is 2 µs. The CPU overhead is 0.5 ms. Compute the total scan time.

**Step 1:** Input scan time.

$$
T_{input} = 256 \times 2\,\mu s = 512\,\mu s = 0.512\,ms
$$

**Step 2:** Output scan time.

$$
T_{output} = 256 \times 2\,\mu s = 512\,\mu s = 0.512\,ms
$$

**Step 3:** Program execution time.

$$
T_{program} = 1500 \times 0.1\,\mu s = 150\,\mu s = 0.150\,ms
$$

**Step 4:** Add CPU overhead.

$$
T_{scan} = 0.512 + 0.512 + 0.150 + 0.500
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 1.674\,ms
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{T_{scan} \approx 1.674\,ms}$ — well within the deterministic performance requirement of a mid-range modular PLC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of PLC Capabilities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Modules: Sensors, Switches, Encoders] --> B[Input Image Table IIT]
    B --> C{CPU Scan Cycle Engine}
    C --> D[Program Scan: Ladder Logic FBD ST]
    C --> E[Output Image Table OIT]
    E --> F[Output Modules: Relays Contactors Valves]
    C --> G[Memory: RAM ROM EEPROM]
    C --> H[Timers Counters Latches]
    C --> I[Arithmetic Data Handling Unit]
    C --> J[PID Control Block]
    C --> K[Communication Ports: Ethernet Serial]
    C --> L[Self-Diagnostics: Watchdog I/O Check Battery]
    K --> M[SCADA HMI Other PLCs]
    L --> N[Fault LED Indicators]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: PLC Scan Cycle (Capability Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start of Scan] --> P1[Read Physical Inputs]
    P1 --> P2[Update Input Image Table]
    P2 --> P3[Execute Ladder Logic Rung by Rung]
    P3 --> P4[Update Output Image Table]
    P4 --> P5[Drive Physical Outputs]
    P5 --> P6[Perform Housekeeping: Diagnostics Communication]
    P6 --> P7[Increment Watchdog Timer]
    P7 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: Classification of PLC Capabilities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Capabilities of PLC] --> C1[Control Capabilities]
    R --> C2[Computational Capabilities]
    R --> C3[Communication Capabilities]
    R --> C4[Diagnostic Capabilities]
    C1 --> T1[Sequential Control]
    C1 --> T2[PID Control]
    C1 --> T3[Timing and Counting]
    C2 --> T4[Boolean Logic]
    C2 --> T5[Arithmetic Operations]
    C2 --> T6[Data Handling and Comparison]
    C3 --> T7[Industrial Protocols: Modbus Profinet]
    C3 --> T8[HMI and SCADA Interface]
    C3 --> T9[Peer-to-Peer Networking]
    C4 --> T10[CPU Watchdog]
    C4 --> T11[I/O Module Health]
    C4 --> T12[Memory Integrity Check]
    C4 --> T13[Battery and Power Monitoring]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Interaction between PLC Capabilities and External Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Ext1[Limit Switch] -->|Discrete Input| IM1[Digital Input Module]
    Ext2[Temperature Sensor] -->|Analog 4-20 mA| IM2[Analog Input Module]
    IM1 --> CPU[CPU: Logic and Control Engine]
    IM2 --> CPU
    CPU -->|Boolean Logic| L1[Ladder Logic Program]
    CPU -->|Continuous Control| L2[PID Instruction Block]
    CPU -->|Timing Function| L3[TON TOF RTO Timers]
    CPU -->|Counting| L4[CTU CTD Counters]
    L1 --> OM1[Digital Output Module]
    L2 --> OM2[Analog Output Module]
    L3 --> OM1
    L4 --> OM1
    OM1 --> Dev1[Motor Contactor]
    OM2 --> Dev2[Control Valve Actuator]
    CPU --> Net[Communication Module]
    Net --> SCADA[SCADA Server]
    Net --> HMI[HMI Touch Panel]
    Net --> Peer[Other PLC Peer]
```

### 4.5 Timeline Diagram: Capabilities Active During a Single Scan

```mermaid
gantt
    title Capabilities Activated in a Single PLC Scan Cycle
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s ms
    section Input Phase
    Read Digital Inputs    :a1, 0, 1ms
    Read Analog Inputs     :a2, 0, 1ms
    section Logic Phase
    Execute Ladder Logic   :b1, 1, 4ms
    Update Timers Counters :b2, 2, 3ms
    Compute PID Loop       :b3, 3, 2ms
    section Output Phase
    Update Output Image    :c1, 5, 1ms
    Drive Physical Outputs :c2, 5, 1ms
    section Diagnostic Phase
    Run Watchdog Check     :d1, 6, 1ms
    Communication Polling  :d2, 6, 1ms
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC). List any **four major capabilities** of a modern PLC.

**Model Answer:**

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, industrially hardened digital computer designed for the real-time control of industrial automation processes, operating on a deterministic scan cycle.

The four major capabilities of a PLC are:
1. **Sequential and Logical Control** — Boolean logic execution (AND, OR, NOT, XOR).
2. **Timing and Counting** — On-delay, off-delay, retentive timers, and up/down counters.
3. **Arithmetic and Data Handling** — ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, comparison, and data movement.
4. **Communication and Networking** — Interface with HMI, SCADA, and other PLCs via industrial protocols (Modbus, Profibus, Ethernet/IP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Comment:** Students who write only "PLC controls machines" lose 1.5 marks. You must mention **at least one specific capability** with an example for full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
Explain the **self-diagnostic capability** of a PLC. Mention any **three diagnostic features**.

**Model Answer:**

The **self-diagnostic capability** of a PLC refers to its built-in ability to continuously monitor its own internal and external operational health, detect faults, and alert the operator.

Three diagnostic features are:
1. **CPU Watchdog Timer** — Resets the CPU if the scan cycle exceeds a maximum allowed time, preventing system hang-ups.
2. **I/O Module Health Monitoring** — Detects blown fuses, missing modules, short circuits, and broken field wiring.
3. **Memory Integrity Check** — Verifies RAM parity, ROM checksum, and backup battery status to ensure program and data retention.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a PLC** and describe its **input scan, program scan, and output scan** phases. Discuss how the **scan time** influences the real-time capability of the PLC.

**Model Answer (7 + 7 = 14 Marks):**

**Part (a) — Functional Architecture (7 marks):**

The functional architecture of a PLC consists of the following blocks:

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** The brain of the PLC, containing the microprocessor, memory, and scan engine.
2. **Memory Unit:** Comprises **RAM** (volatile, for program execution and I/O image), **ROM/EEPROM** (non-volatile, for the operating system and retained user program).
3. **Input Interface Module:** Conditions and isolates field input signals (120 VAC, 24 VDC, 4–20 mA) to TTL-level voltages for the CPU.
4. **Output Interface Module:** Amplifies the CPU's low-level signals to drive field actuators (contactors, solenoids, valves).
5. **Programming Device / HMI:** Used to load, monitor, and troubleshoot the user program (laptop, HMI panel, or handheld).
