In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, Structured Text is studied as a **declarative, block-structured language** where the program executes **top-to-bottom, left-to-right** within a logical cycle, making it ideal for **DCS (Distributed Control System)** algorithms, batch process control, and complex analog computations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Cooking Recipe vs. Structured Text**
> Think of a **Structured Text program** as a **written cooking recipe**:
> - **Variables** = Ingredients on your counter (flour, sugar, eggs)
> - **Assignments (`:=`)** = Mixing an ingredient into the bowl (e.g., `bowl := flour + sugar`)
> - **IF-THEN-ELSE** = Decision-making ("If the dough is sticky, add more flour; ELSE proceed")
> - **FOR / WHILE loops** = Repetitive actions ("Stir 10 times" or "Stir until smooth")
> - **Function Blocks** = Pre-measured spice packets you grab and use repeatedly
>
> Just as a chef reads the recipe **line by line, top to bottom**, the PLC scans the ST program in the same order, executing each statement sequentially unless a control structure (loop/branch) redirects the flow.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of ST

| Feature | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Syntax Style** | Pascal/Ada-like with `;` statement terminators | High — frequently tested in ESE |
| **Execution Model** | Sequential, top-to-bottom scan | High — execution order questions |
| **Case Sensitivity** | **Case-insensitive** (`Start` = `START` = `start`) | Medium — common pitfall |
| **Indentation** | Free-form, not syntactically required | Low |
| **Data Types** | Strongly typed (BOOL, INT, REAL, STRING, etc.) | High — type conversion questions |
| **Extension** | `.st` or `.txt` files in CoDeSys / TIA Portal | Low (practical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Structured Text is one of the **five IEC 61131-3 languages** alongside **Ladder Diagram (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), Instruction List (IL), and Sequential Function Chart (SFC)**. ST is recommended for **complex, math-heavy, or algorithmically intensive** control tasks where graphical languages become unmanageable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an ST Program — The "Skeleton"

Every Structured Text program (commonly called a **POU — Program Organization Unit**) follows a strict architectural skeleton. Understanding this hierarchy is **critical for KTU 14-mark questions**.

```
POU Structure (Top to Bottom):
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PROGRAM  (or FUNCTION_BLOCK / FUNCTION) │
│  Name: MyProgram                         │  ← Declaration part
│  VAR_INPUT    : BOOL;                    │     (variable section)
│  VAR_OUTPUT   : INT;                     │
│  VAR          : REAL;                    │
│  END_VAR                                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Statement_1;                            │
│  Statement_2;                            │  ← Instruction part
│  IF condition THEN ... END_IF;           │     (executable code)
│  FOR i := 1 TO 10 DO ... END_FOR;        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  END_PROGRAM                             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2 Data Types — The "Vocabulary" of ST

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Data Types Matter:** ST is **strongly typed**, meaning you cannot assign a `REAL` to an `INT` without explicit conversion. The PLC compiler will reject mixed-type operations unless conversion functions (`REAL_TO_INT`, `INT_TO_REAL`) are used.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Operators & Control Structures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Reference Table** — Memorize this for the 3-mark short-answer section.

#### **A. Assignment Operator**

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `:=` | Assign (right → left) | `Result := A + B;` |

> **Critical Note:** The single `=` is used for **comparison** in `IF` conditions, **NOT** for assignment. This is the **#1 mistake** KTU students make.

#### **B. Arithmetic Operators**

| Operator | Operation | Example |
|---|---|---|

#### **C. Relational (Comparison) Operators**

| Operator | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `=` | Equal to |
| `<>` | Not equal to |
| `>` | Greater than |
| `<` | Less than |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal to |
| `<=` | Less than or equal to |

#### **D. Logical (Boolean) Operators**

| Operator | Operation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `AND` | Logical AND | `A AND B` |
| `OR` | Logical OR | `A OR B` |
| `NOT` | Logical NOT | `NOT A` |
| `XOR` | Exclusive OR | `A XOR B` |

#### **E. Control Structure Cheat Sheet**

| Construct | Syntax Skeleton | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Assignment** | `Var := Expression;` | Store computed values |
| **IF** | `IF cond THEN ... END_IF;` | Single condition |
| **IF-ELSE** | `IF cond THEN ... ELSE ... END_IF;` | Binary decision |
| **IF-ELSIF** | `IF c1 THEN ... ELSIF c2 THEN ... END_IF;` | Multi-way decision |
| **CASE** | `CASE var OF 1: ...; 2: ...; ELSE ...; END_CASE;` | Multi-value dispatcher |
| **FOR** | `FOR i := 1 TO 10 BY 1 DO ... END_FOR;` | Fixed-count loop |
| **WHILE** | `WHILE cond DO ... END_WHILE;` | Pre-test loop |
| **REPEAT** | `REPEAT ... UNTIL cond END_REPEAT;` | Post-test loop |
| **EXIT** | `EXIT;` | Break out of loop |
| **RETURN** | `RETURN;` | Exit the POU early |

#### **F. Standard Functions (Memorize These)**

| Function | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `ABS(x)` | Absolute value | `Y := ABS(-5);` → `5` |
| `SQRT(x)` | Square root | `Y := SQRT(16);` → `4.0` |
| `SIN(x)`, `COS(x)`, `TAN(x)` | Trigonometry (input in **radians**) | `Y := SIN(3.14159/2);` |
| `LN(x)`, `LOG(x)`, `EXP(x)` | Natural/Base-10 log, exponential | `Y := LN(2.718);` |
| `MIN(a,b)`, `MAX(a,b)` | Min/Max of two values | `Y := MAX(T1, T2);` |
| `LIMIT(min, val, max)` | Clamp `val` between `min` and `max` | `Y := LIMIT(0, x, 100);` |
| `MOD(a,b)` | Modulo (remainder) | `Y := MOD(10,3);` → `1` |
| `ROUND(x)`, `TRUNC(x)` | Rounding to integer | `Y := ROUND(3.7);` → `4` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Structured Text is the **workhorse language** in modern industrial automation for:

1. **Batch Process Control** — Pharmaceutical and food industries use ST for **recipe management** (sequential addition of ingredients with timers and conditions).
2. **PID Loop Implementation** — Complex control algorithms with anti-windup, filtering, and auto-tuning are written in ST.
3. **Data Logging & Protocol Handling** — Parsing **Modbus, Profinet, or OPC-UA** payloads.
4. **Mathematical Computations** — **Flow compensation** (e.g., AGA-3 gas flow equations), **enthalpy calculations**, and **linearization** of thermocouple signals.
5. **Motion Control** — Trajectory planning, S-curve profiling in CNC and robotics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> In Structured Text, "derivations" are replaced by **executable program traces**. The following section walks through **complete ST programs** with exhaustive line-by-line analysis, which is the standard KTU examination approach for 14-mark questions.

### 3.1 Example 1 — IF-ELSIF-ELSE Temperature Classifier (Full Trace)

**Problem Statement:** Design an ST program that reads a temperature input `Temp_C` (REAL) and classifies it into one of four categories, activating a corresponding Boolean output.

**Full ST Program with Line-by-Line Annotation:**

```iecst
PROGRAM Temperature_Classifier
    (* ============= DECLARATION SECTION ============= *)
    VAR
        Temp_C       : REAL;    (* Input temperature in Celsius      *)
        Alarm_High   : BOOL;    (* TRUE when Temp > 80 °C            *)
        Alarm_Med    : BOOL;    (* TRUE when 50 < Temp <= 80         *)
        Status_OK    : BOOL;    (* TRUE when 20 <= Temp <= 50        *)
        Alarm_Low    : BOOL;    (* TRUE when Temp < 20 °C            *)
    END_VAR
    (* ============= INSTRUCTION SECTION ============= *)
    
    (* STEP 1: Read input from process *)
    Temp_C := 65.5;   (* Simulated sensor reading — in real PLC this 
                          would be mapped from an analog input word *)
    
    (* STEP 2: Default all outputs to FALSE to avoid stale values *)
    Alarm_High := FALSE;
    Alarm_Med  := FALSE;
    Status_OK  := FALSE;
    Alarm_Low  := FALSE;
    
    (* STEP 3: Evaluate the four mutually exclusive ranges *)
    IF Temp_C > 80.0 THEN
        Alarm_High := TRUE;
    ELSIF Temp_C > 50.0 THEN
        Alarm_Med := TRUE;
    ELSIF Temp_C >= 20.0 THEN
        Status_OK := TRUE;
    ELSE
        Alarm_Low := TRUE;
    END_IF;
    
END_PROGRAM
```

**Exhaustive Trace Table for `Temp_C = 65.5`:**

| Step | Line | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Temp_C := 65.5;` | Assignment | `Temp_C = 65.5` |
| 2 | `Alarm_High := FALSE;` | Clear | `Alarm_High = 0` |
| 3 | `Alarm_Med := FALSE;` | Clear | `Alarm_Med = 0` |
| 4 | `Status_OK := FALSE;` | Clear | `Status_OK = 0` |
| 5 | `Alarm_Low := FALSE;` | Clear | `Alarm_Low = 0` |
| 6 | `IF 65.5 > 80.0` | Test condition 1 | `FALSE` → skip block |
| 7 | `ELSIF 65.5 > 50.0` | Test condition 2 | `TRUE` → enter block |
| 8 | `Alarm_Med := TRUE;` | Execute | `Alarm_Med = 1` |
| 9 | End of `IF` | Exit structure | — |

**Final State:** `Alarm_High = 0, Alarm_Med = 1, Status_OK = 0, Alarm_Low = 0` ✓

### 3.2 Example 2 — FOR Loop: Conveyor Item Count & Sum

**Problem Statement:** Compute the **average mass** of 10 items on a conveyor using a `FOR` loop.

```iecst
PROGRAM Conveyor_Average
    VAR
        Mass_Array  : ARRAY[1..10] OF REAL;  (* 10 sensor readings   *)
        Sum         : REAL := 0.0;            (* Running total         *)
        Average     : REAL;                   (* Computed mean         *)
        i           : INT;                    (* Loop index            *)
    END_VAR
    
    (* Initialize the array with sample values *)
    Mass_Array[1]  := 12.5;  Mass_Array[2]  := 11.8;  Mass_Array[3]  := 13.1;
    Mass_Array[4]  := 12.9;  Mass_Array[5]  := 12.0;  Mass_Array[6]  := 11.5;
    Mass_Array[7]  := 13.4;  Mass_Array[8]  := 12.2;  Mass_Array[9]  := 12.7;
    Mass_Array[10] := 11.9;
    
    Sum := 0.0;   (* Critical: reset accumulator before loop *)
    
    FOR i := 1 TO 10 BY 1 DO
        Sum := Sum + Mass_Array[i];
    END_FOR;
    
    Average := Sum / 10.0;   (* Real division — both operands REAL *)
    
END_PROGRAM
```

**Manual Arithmetic Verification (Sum):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= 12.5 + 11.8 + 13.1 + 12.9 + 12.0 + 11.5 + 13.4 + 12.2 + 12.7 + 11.9 \\
&= 124.0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Average} = \frac{124.0}{10.0} = 12.4 \text{ kg}
$$

**Loop Trace (Iteration Summary):**

| Iteration `i` | `Mass_Array[i]` | `Sum` (after) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.5 | 12.5 |
| 2 | 11.8 | 24.3 |
| 3 | 13.1 | 37.4 |
| 4 | 12.9 | 50.3 |
| 5 | 12.0 | 62.3 |
| 6 | 11.5 | 73.8 |
| 7 | 13.4 | 87.2 |
| 8 | 12.2 | 99.4 |
| 9 | 12.7 | 112.1 |
| 10 | 11.9 | **124.0** |

### 3.3 Example 3 — WHILE Loop with EXIT (Emergency Stop Scan)

**Problem Statement:** Continuously scan a 20-element pressure array and set `Overpressure_Alarm = TRUE` the moment any reading exceeds 50.0 bar. Stop scanning immediately after the first hit.

```iecst
PROGRAM Pressure_Scanner
    VAR
        Pressure_Array   : ARRAY[1..20] OF REAL;
        Overpressure_Alarm : BOOL := FALSE;
        i                 : INT := 1;
    END_VAR
    
    (* Assume array is filled from analog input module *)
    (* ... array population logic here ... *)
    
    Overpressure_Alarm := FALSE;
    i := 1;
    
    WHILE (i <= 20) AND (NOT Overpressure_Alarm) DO
        IF Pressure_Array[i] > 50.0 THEN
            Overpressure_Alarm := TRUE;
            EXIT;   (* Jump directly to END_WHILE *)
        END_IF;
        i := i + 1;
    END_WHILE;
    
END_PROGRAM
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Analysis:** The compound condition `(i <= 20) AND (NOT Overpressure_Alarm)` implements **short-circuit evaluation** — if the alarm is already set, the loop **terminates without further iterations**, saving PLC scan time. The `EXIT;` statement provides an **immediate exit** from the innermost loop, equivalent to `break` in C.

### 3.4 Example 4 — CASE Structure: Motor Speed Selector

```iecst
PROGRAM Motor_Speed_Control
    VAR
        Speed_Selector : INT;     (* Input from HMI: 0,1,2,3 *)
        Motor_Speed    : REAL;    (* Output in RPM         *)
    END_VAR
    
    CASE Speed_Selector OF
        0:  Motor_Speed := 0.0;       (* Stop           *)
        1:  Motor_Speed := 750.0;     (* Low speed      *)
        2:  Motor_Speed := 1500.0;    (* Medium speed   *)
        3:  Motor_Speed := 3000.0;    (* Full speed     *)
        ELSE
            Motor_Speed := 0.0;       (* Safety default *)
    END_CASE;
    
END_PROGRAM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The `ELSE` branch in a `CASE` is a **safety net** — it handles unexpected selector values (e.g., `Speed_Selector = 7` from a faulty HMI) by defaulting the motor to **stopped**. Always include `ELSE` in safety-critical ST code.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — ST Program Execution Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PLC Scan Cycle Start] --> B[Read Process Inputs]
    B --> C[Load ST POU into Memory]
    C --> D[Execute Declaration Section]
    D --> E[Execute Instruction Section]
    E --> F{Control Structure}
    F -->|IF THEN ELSE| G[Evaluate Boolean Condition]
    F -->|CASE OF| H[Match Integer Selector]
    F -->|FOR WHILE REPEAT| I[Loop Iteration Logic]
    G --> J[Execute Selected Branch]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Write Outputs to Process]
    K --> L[Cycle End - Return to A]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — ST POU Structural Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[POU Program Organization Unit] --> Q[Declaration Part VAR END_VAR]
    P --> R[Instruction Part Code Body]
    
    Q --> Q1[VAR_INPUT External Inputs]
    Q --> Q2[VAR_OUTPUT External Outputs]
    Q --> Q3[VAR_IN_OUT Bidirectional]
    Q --> Q4[VAR Local Variables]
    Q --> Q5[VAR_TEMP Scratch Variables]
    Q --> Q6[VAR CONSTANT Immutable Values]
    
    R --> R1[Assignment Statements]
    R --> R2[Selection IF CASE]
    R --> R3[Iteration FOR WHILE REPEAT]
    R --> R4[Function Calls]
    R --> R5[FB Calls]
    
    style P fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Control Structure Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Branch or Loop?] --> A{Number of Conditions?}
    A -->|Single Condition| B[IF THEN END_IF]
    A -->|Two Branches| C[IF THEN ELSE END_IF]
    A -->|Multiple Exclusive| D{Selector Type?}
    A -->|Repeated Action| E{Known Iterations?}
    
    D -->|Integer or Enum| F[CASE OF END_CASE]
    D -->|Range Comparison| G[IF ELSIF ELSE]
    
    E -->|Yes - Fixed Count| H[FOR TO BY DO END_FOR]
    E -->|Unknown - Pre-test| I[WHILE DO END_WHILE]
    E -->|Unknown - Post-test| J[REPEAT UNTIL END_REPEAT]
    
    style Start fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define Structured Text. List any four data types supported by IEC 61131-3 Structured Text.*

**Model Answer:**
Structured Text (ST) is a high-level, text-based programming language defined in the **IEC 61131-3 standard** for PLCs. It uses structured constructs like IF-THEN-ELSE, CASE, FOR, and WHILE loops with Pascal-like syntax, and is executed **sequentially, top-to-bottom** by the PLC scan cycle.

Four data types:
1. `BOOL` — Boolean (TRUE / FALSE), 1 bit
2. `INT` — 16-bit signed integer, range **−32 768 to +32 767**
3. `REAL` — 32-bit floating point, **IEEE 754** single precision
4. `STRING` — Variable-length character string (e.g., `STRING[80]`)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between `WHILE ... DO` and `REPEAT ... UNTIL` loops in Structured Text with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `WHILE ... DO` | `REPEAT ... UNTIL` |
|---|---|---|
| **Test Position** | **Pre-test** (condition checked **before** body) | **Post-test** (condition checked **after** body) |
| **Minimum Executions** | **Zero** (body may never run) | **At least one** (body always runs once) |
| **Termination** | When condition becomes **FALSE** | When condition becomes **TRUE** |
| **Equivalent to** | C's `while` loop | C's `do-while` loop |

**Example `WHILE`:**
```iecst
WHILE Counter < 10 DO
    Counter := Counter + 1;
END_WHILE;
```

**Example `REPEAT`:**
```iecst
REPEAT
    Counter := Counter + 1;
UNTIL Counter >= 10
END_REPEAT;
```

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **selection control structures** in Structured Text (IF-THEN, IF-THEN-ELSE, IF-ELSIF-ELSE, CASE) with appropriate syntax and one industrial example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Structured Text program for a **water tank level control system** with the following specification:
- Inputs: `Level_AI` (REAL, 0–100 %), `Pump_Status` (BOOL)
- Outputs: `Pump_Run` (BOOL), `High_Alarm` (BOOL), `Low_Alarm` (BOOL)
- Logic: Pump ON when Level < 30 %, Pump OFF when Level > 80 %, High Alarm when Level > 90 %, Low Alarm when Level < 10 %.

---

#### **Question A — Model Solutions**

**(a) Model Answer — Selection Structures:**

**1. `IF ... THEN ... END_IF`** — Used when a single conditional action is needed.
- **Syntax:**
  ```iecst
  IF <Boolean_expression> THEN
      <statement(s)>;
  END_IF;
  ```
- **Industrial Example:** Switch on a heater when temperature drops below 40 °C.
  ```iecst
  IF Temp_C < 40.0 THEN
      Heater := TRUE;
  END_IF;
  ```

**2. `IF ... THEN ... ELSE ... END_IF`** — Used for binary (two-path) decisions.
- **Syntax:**
  ```iecst
  IF <condition> THEN
      <statements_A>;
  ELSE
      <statements_B>;
  END_IF;
  ```
- **Industrial Example:** Open/close a valve based on pressure threshold.
  ```iecst
  IF Pressure_Bar > 5.0 THEN
      Valve := FALSE;   (* Close valve *)
  ELSE
      Valve := TRUE;    (* Open valve  *)
  END_IF;
  ```

**3. `IF ... THEN ... ELSIF ... ELSE ... END_IF`** — Multi-way branching with range checks.
- **Syntax:**
  ```iecst
  IF <cond_1> THEN
      <statements_1>;
  ELSIF <cond_2> THEN
      <statements_2>;
  ELSE
      <statements_default>;
  END_IF;
  ```
- **Industrial Example:** Conveyor speed selection based on object weight class.
  ```iecst
  IF Weight > 100.0 THEN
      Conveyor_Speed := 0.3;   (* Slow for heavy items *)
  ELSIF Weight > 50.0 THEN
      Conveyor_Speed := 0.6;   (* Medium *)
  ELSE
      Conveyor_Speed := 1.0;   (* Fast for light items *)
  END_IF;
  ```

**4. `CASE ... OF ... END_CASE`** — Multi-way dispatcher based on a **single integer/selector value**. More readable than long IF-ELSIF chains.
- **Syntax:**
  ```iecst
  CASE <selector_int> OF
      <val_1>: <stmt_1>;
      <val_2>: <stmt_2>;
      ...
      ELSE <default_stmt>;
  END_CASE;
  ```
- **Industrial Example:** Machine mode selection from HMI.
  ```iecst
  CASE Machine_Mode OF
      0: Motor := FALSE;       (* OFF mode  *)
      1: Motor := TRUE;        (* Manual   *)
      2: Auto_Start := TRUE;   (* Auto run *)
      ELSE Motor := FALSE;     (* Safety   *)
  END_CASE;
  ```

---

**(b) Model Answer — Water Tank Level Control Program:**

```iecst
PROGRAM Water_Tank_Control
    (* =============== DECLARATIONS =============== *)
    VAR
        Level_AI   : REAL;     (* Analog input: 0.0 to 100.0 %   *)
        Pump_Status: BOOL;     (* Feedback: TRUE = pump running   *)
        Pump_Run   : BOOL;     (* Output to pump starter          *)
        High_Alarm : BOOL;     (* TRUE when Level > 90 %         *)
        Low_Alarm  : BOOL;     (* TRUE when Level < 10 %         *)
    END_VAR
    (* =============== INSTRUCTION BODY =============== *)
    
    (* Step 1: Read sensor — assume mapped from AI word *)
    (* Level_AI := AI_Register;  -- hardware mapping omitted *)
    
    (* Step 2: Initialize outputs to safe defaults *)
    Pump_Run   := FALSE;
    High_Alarm := FALSE;
    Low_Alarm  := FALSE;
    
    (* Step 3: Implement hysteresis-based pump control *)
    IF Level_AI < 30.0 THEN
        Pump_Run := TRUE;      (* Start pump when too low *)
    ELSIF Level_AI > 80.0 THEN
        Pump_Run := FALSE;     (* Stop pump when full    *)
    END_IF;
    (* Between 30 % and 80 %, the pump retains its last state 
       — this is the hysteresis band preventing chattering. *)
    
    (* Step 4: Generate alarms *)
    IF Level_AI > 90.0 THEN
        High_Alarm := TRUE;
    END_IF;
    
    IF Level_AI < 10.0 THEN
        Low_Alarm := TRUE;
    END_IF;
    
END_PROGRAM
```

**Marking Scheme Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Declaration section: **2 marks**
- Hysteresis pump logic: **3 marks**
- Alarm logic: **2 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **iteration (loop) control structures** in Structured Text: `FOR ... TO ... BY ... DO`, `WHILE ... DO`, and `REPEAT ... UNTIL`. State the conditions under which each is preferred with a one-line industrial example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Structured Text program that computes the **factorial of a positive integer N (N ≤ 8)** stored in variable `N` using a `WHILE` loop, and stores the result in `Factorial`. Show the trace for `N = 5`. (Note: `5! = 120`).

---

#### **Question B — Model Solutions**

**(a) Model Answer — Iteration Structures:**

**1. `FOR ... TO ... BY ... DO ... END_FOR`**
- **Use when** the number of iterations is **known in advance** (counter-driven).
- **Syntax:**
  ```iecst
  FOR <index> := <start> TO <end> BY <step> DO
      <statements>;
  END_FOR;
  ```
- **Industrial Example:** Sum 12 monthly production values.
  ```iecst
  FOR Month := 1 TO 12 BY 1 DO
      Annual_Sum := Annual_Sum + Production[Month];
  END_FOR;
  ```

**2. `WHILE ... DO ... END_WHILE`**
- **Use when** iterations depend on a **runtime condition**; may execute **zero times** (pre-test).
- **Syntax:**
  ```iecst
  WHILE <condition> DO
      <statements>;
  END_WHILE;
  ```
- **Industrial Example:** Read sensor until tank is full.
  ```iecst
  WHILE Level_AI < 100.0 DO
      Valve := TRUE;
      (* Wait for next scan — level updated by I/O refresh *)
  END_WHILE;
  Valve := FALSE;
  ```

**3. `REPEAT ... UNTIL ... END_REPEAT`**
- **Use when** the loop body must execute **at least once** (post-test).
- **Syntax:**
  ```iecst
  REPEAT
      <statements>;
  UNTIL <condition>
  END_REPEAT;
  ```
- **Industrial Example:** Prompt operator for password at least once.
  ```iecst
  REPEAT
      Password_Input := Get_HMI_Input();
  UNTIL Password_Input = Correct_Pwd
  END_REPEAT;
  ```

**Summary Table:**

| Loop | Test Location | Min Iterations | Preferred When |
|---|---|---|---|
| `FOR` | N/A (count-driven) | 0 | Count is known |
| `WHILE` | Pre-test | 0 | Condition-dependent, may skip |
| `REPEAT` | Post-test | 1 | Must execute at least once |

---

**(b) Model Answer — Factorial Program:**

```iecst
PROGRAM Factorial_Calculator
    VAR
        N        : INT := 5;     (* Input number              *)
        Factorial: DINT := 1;     (* Output — DINT for 8! = 40320 *)
        i        : INT;           (* Loop index                *)
    END_VAR
    
    (* Step 1: Input validation — factorial undefined for N < 0 *)
    IF N < 0 THEN
        Factorial := 0;     (* Error sentinel *)
    ELSE
        Factorial := 1;     (* Critical: reset accumulator *)
        i := 1;
        
        WHILE i <= N DO
            Factorial := Factorial * i;
            i := i + 1;
        END_WHILE;
    END_IF;
    
END_PROGRAM
```

**Exhaustive Trace for `N = 5`:**

| Iteration | `i` (before) | `Factorial` (before) | `Factorial` (after) | `i` (after) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 × 1 = **1** | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 × 2 = **2** | 3 |
| 3 | 3 | 2 | 2 × 3 = **6** | 4 |
| 4 | 4 | 6 | 6 × 4 = **24** | 5 |
| 5 | 5 | 24 | 24 × 5 = **120** | 6 |

**Loop Termination:** When `i = 6`, condition `6 <= 5` is `FALSE` → exit.  
**Final Result:** `Factorial = 120` ✓ (matches `5! = 120`)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to reset `Factorial := 1;`** before the loop — loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using `INT` (16-bit) for factorial** — `8! = 40320` **overflows INT** (max 32 767). Must use `DINT` (32-bit) — loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Using `=` instead of `:=` for assignment** — compiler error or wrong semantics — loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing `END_WHILE;` or `END_PROGRAM;`** — syntax error, full question voided.
> 5. **Off-by-one error** (e.g., `i < N` instead of `i <= N`) — produces wrong result, **0 marks** for logic.
> 6. **No input validation** for `N < 0` — loses **1 mark** (industry best practice).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Structured Text**

- [x] **ST is IEC 61131-3 text-based language**, executed **sequentially top-to-bottom** in the PLC scan cycle.
- [x] **`:=` is assignment**, `=` is comparison — **never confuse them**.
- [x] ST is **case-insensitive** (`START` = `Start` = `start`) and **not whitespace-sensitive**.
- [x] Every ST statement ends with a **semicolon `;`**.
- [x] **Data types are strictly enforced** — use `REAL_TO_INT()`, `INT_TO_REAL()` for type conversion.
- [x] **Common data types:** `BOOL`, `SINT`, `INT`, `DINT`, `LINT`, `USINT`, `UINT`, `UDINT`, `REAL`, `LREAL`, `STRING`, `TIME`, `DATE`, `TOD`, `DT`, plus `ARRAY` and user-defined types.
- [x] **IF-ELSIF-ELSE** handles **range-based** decisions; **CASE** handles **discrete value** dispatching.
- [x] **`FOR` = known iterations**, **`WHILE` = pre-test (0+ runs)**, **`REPEAT` = post-test (1+ runs)**.
- [x] **`EXIT;`** breaks out of the **innermost loop**; **`RETURN;`** exits the **entire POU** early.
- [x] Always include **`ELSE`** in `IF` and `CASE` blocks for **safety-critical** code (graceful default).
- [x] Use **hysteresis bands** (e.g., ON at 30 %, OFF at 80 %) in on-off control to **prevent chattering**.
- [x] Comment syntax: `(* single line *)` and `(* multi-line block *)`.
- [x] ST POU structure: **Declaration (`VAR ... END_VAR`) + Instruction (code) + `END_PROGRAM`**.
- [x] Standard functions to memorize: `ABS, SQRT, SIN, COS, TAN, LN, LOG, EXP, MIN, MAX, LIMIT, MOD, ROUND, TRUNC`.
- [x] ST is preferred over Ladder for **complex math, loops, arrays, and data processing** in DCS/SCADA systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instruction List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instruction List (IL)** is a **low-level, assembler-like textual programming language** defined by the international standard **IEC 61131-3** for programmable logic controllers (PLCs). It expresses control sequences as a sequential list of instructions, each composed of an **operator**, an optional **modifier**, and one or more **operands**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, IL is one of the **five standardized PLC programming languages** under IEC 61131-3, sitting alongside:

| Language | Type | Acronym |
|---|---|---|
| Ladder Diagram | Graphical | **LD** |
| Function Block Diagram | Graphical | **FBD** |
| Structured Text | Textual (high-level) | **ST** |
| **Instruction List** | **Textual (low-level)** | **IL** |
| Sequential Function Chart | Graphical | **SFC** |

> [!NOTE]
> Although IEC 61131-3 (Edition 3, 2013) deprecated IL in favor of Structured Text, it is **still retained as a normative annex** and remains a **high-weight topic in KTU ESE examinations** because of its historical importance, compact execution model, and frequent use in legacy industrial PLCs (Siemens STEP 7 STL, Allen-Bradley AOI mnemonics).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of IL as the **"machine code of PLCs"** — much like assembly language for microprocessors.

- Imagine a **factory assembly line** where each worker (instruction) does **one tiny job** and then passes the work-in-progress (the **accumulator** or **current result register**) to the next worker.
- The **first worker** picks up a raw material (an operand loaded via the `LD` instruction).
- **Subsequent workers** modify, combine, or test that material using operators (`AND`, `OR`, `ADD`, `GT`, etc.).
- A **final worker** stores the finished product into a box (a memory location via the `ST` instruction).

This **step-by-step, sequential, register-based paradigm** is exactly the philosophy behind IL. Unlike Ladder Diagram which looks like electrical schematics, IL looks like a **textual "to-do list" for the CPU**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **IEC 61131-3** is the governing standard — published by the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)**.
- IL programs are organized as **POUs (Program Organization Units)** containing a list of statements.
- Each IL statement is **executed sequentially** from top to bottom unless a control flow instruction (`JMP`, `RET`, `CAL`) redirects execution.
- The implicit working register is typically called the **Current Result (CR)** or **Accumulator (ACC)**.
- The standard permits **nested parenthesis** for overriding default left-to-right evaluation order.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an IL Statement

Every IL instruction follows the rigid syntax:

```
LABEL: OPERATOR[MODIFIER] OPERAND1, OPERAND2   (* COMMENT *)
```

| Component | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Label** | Optional jump target identifier followed by `:` | `START:` |
| **Operator** | Mandatory operation code (mnemonic) | `LD`, `AND`, `ST`, `ADD` |
| **Modifier** | Optional suffix altering behavior | `N` (negate), `C` (conditional), `(`, `)` |
| **Operand(s)** | Variable, constant, or address | `I0.0`, `M10`, `TRUE`, `25` |
| **Comment** | Optional, enclosed in `(* … *)` or `//` | `(* start pushbutton *)` |

## 2.2 The Five Logical Categories of IL Operators

### 2.2.1 Load / Store Operators (Foundation)
- `LD` — **Loads** the operand value into the current result (CR) register. Resets the CR.
- `ST` — **Stores** the current result into the operand location.
- `STN` — Stores the **negated** current result.

### 2.2.2 Boolean Logic Operators (Bit-level)
- `AND`, `&` — Logical AND between CR and operand.
- `OR` — Logical OR between CR and operand.
- `XOR` — Exclusive OR.
- `NOT` — Inverts the CR (unary).

### 2.2.3 Arithmetic Operators
- `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV` — Integer arithmetic.
- `MOD` — Modulo.

### 2.2.4 Comparison Operators
- `GT` (greater than), `GE` (greater or equal), `EQ` (equal), `NE` (not equal), `LE`, `LT`.

### 2.2.5 Control Flow Operators
- `JMP` — Unconditional jump to a label.
- `JMPC` — Jump if CR is TRUE (conditional).
- `CAL` — Call a function block or function.
- `RET` — Return from a POU.
- `RETC` — Conditional return.

## 2.3 Modifiers — The Power Multipliers

| Modifier | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `N` | Negate the operand before the operation | `ANDN I0.1` |
| `C` | Execute the operation only if CR is TRUE | `ANDC %IX5` |
| `(` | Open a deferred-operation sub-expression | `AND( %IX3` |
| `)` | Close and merge the sub-expression into CR | `OR %IX4 )` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deferred Operation (Parenthesis Modifier):** When `(` is used after an operator, the operation is **postponed** until the matching `)` is encountered. This is conceptually identical to how a **scientific calculator** evaluates nested expressions — it stores the pending operator stack and resolves them only when the closing parenthesis is found.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

Since IL is a programming paradigm rather than a physics formula, the "cheat sheet" is a **mnemonic-to-operation mapping table** (the single most asked artifact in KTU exams):

| Mnemonic | Operation | Operand Type | Affects CR? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `LD` | Load | Any | Yes (replaces) |
| `LDN` | Load Negated | Boolean | Yes (replaces) |
| `ST` | Store | Any | No |
| `S` | Set (latch) | Boolean | No |
| `R` | Reset (unlatch) | Boolean | No |
| `AND` | Boolean AND | Boolean | Yes |
| `ANDN` | AND with negated operand | Boolean | Yes |
| `OR` | Boolean OR | Boolean | Yes |
| `ORN` | OR with negated operand | Boolean | Yes |
| `XOR` | Exclusive OR | Boolean | Yes |
| `NOT` | Complement CR | — | Yes |
| `ADD` | Integer addition | INT/DINT | Yes |
| `SUB` | Integer subtraction | INT/DINT | Yes |
| `MUL` | Multiplication | INT/DINT | Yes |
| `DIV` | Division | INT/DINT | Yes |
| `MOD` | Modulo | INT/DINT | Yes |
| `GT` | Greater than compare | Any | Yes (BOOL) |
| `GE` | Greater or equal | Any | Yes (BOOL) |
| `EQ` | Equal | Any | Yes (BOOL) |
| `NE` | Not equal | Any | Yes (BOOL) |
| `LE` | Less or equal | Any | Yes (BOOL) |
| `LT` | Less than | Any | Yes (BOOL) |
| `JMP` | Jump | Label | No |
| `JMPC` | Jump if CR = TRUE | Label | No |
| `CAL` | Call FB / function | Instance | No |
| `CALC` | Conditional call | Instance | No |
| `RET` | Return from POU | — | No |
| `RETC` | Conditional return | — | No |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compact code footprint:** IL programs occupy dramatically less memory than equivalent LD programs — critical for early-generation PLCs with limited EPROM/Flash storage.
- **Speed of execution:** Direct, one-to-one mapping to machine cycles makes IL the **fastest executing** IEC 61131-3 language.
- **Migration path:** Industrial sites migrating from legacy relay logic to modern PLCs often transcribe the original logic into IL first, then to LD for visualization.
- **Diagnostic value:** IL listings appear directly in **PLC crash dumps** and **watch windows**, making IL literacy essential for **field maintenance engineers** troubleshooting Siemens S7 (STL), ABB (IL), and Beckhoff (TwinCAT IL) systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — From Boolean Expression to IL

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU style):**  
Convert the following Boolean expression into an equivalent **Instruction List** program and trace the current result (CR) for the input vector `I0.0 = TRUE`, `I0.1 = FALSE`, `I0.2 = TRUE`:

$$
Q = (I0.0 \cdot I0.1) + \overline{I0.2}
$$

### Step-by-Step IL Construction

**Step 1 — Identify the outermost operator.**  
The outermost operator is `+` (OR). We need to compute the **left operand** first, then OR it with the right operand.

**Step 2 — Code the left operand using a deferred parenthesis.**  
Load `I0.0` and use the `AND(` modifier to delay the AND operation.

```
LD    I0.0
AND(  I0.1
)
```

**Step 3 — Code the right operand using `ORN` to handle the NOT.**  
Load `I0.2` and then OR its **negated** value with the pending result.

```
LD    I0.0
AND(  I0.1
)
OR    I0.2     // Since we want OR with negated I0.2, we use ORN
```

**Step 4 — Refine using the `N` modifier for clarity.**

```
LD    I0.0          // CR = TRUE
AND   I0.1          // CR = TRUE AND FALSE = FALSE
OR    I0.2          // CR = FALSE OR TRUE  = TRUE
ST    Q0.0          // Output stored
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization using `ORN`:** The cleaner one-liner version is `LD I0.0 AND I0.1 ORN I0.2 ST Q0.0`. The `N` modifier automatically negates the operand before applying OR, eliminating the need for an explicit `NOT` step.

### Step-by-Step CR Trace Table

| Line | Instruction | CR (Before) | Operation | CR (After) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LD I0.0` | undefined | Load TRUE | TRUE |
| 2 | `AND I0.1` | TRUE | TRUE AND FALSE | FALSE |
| 3 | `ORN I0.2` | FALSE | FALSE OR (NOT TRUE) = FALSE OR FALSE | FALSE |
| 4 | `ST Q0.0` | FALSE | Store to Q0.0 | FALSE (output) |

**Wait** — this trace contradicts the algebraic evaluation. Let us recheck:

$$
Q = (TRUE \cdot FALSE) + \overline{TRUE} = FALSE + FALSE = FALSE
$$

So the IL evaluation correctly yields `FALSE`. The earlier "intuitive" evaluation was wrong. The **IL trace table is the ground truth** and matches Boolean algebra.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Traffic Light Controller Using Timers

**Problem Statement:**  
Write an IL program for a simple traffic light sequence: Red ON for **5 s**, then Green ON for **5 s**, then Yellow ON for **2 s**, then repeat.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 — Declare the timer instances and I/O mapping.**

| Symbol | Type | Address |
|---|---|---|
| `Red_Lamp` | BOOL | Q0.0 |
| `Yellow_Lamp` | BOOL | Q0.1 |
| `Green_Lamp` | BOOL | Q0.2 |
| `T_Red` | TON | T1 |
| `T_Green` | TON | T2 |
| `T_Yellow` | TON | T3 |
| `Step` | INT | MW10 |

**Step 2 — Implement the IL logic with a step counter.**

```
LD    0
ST    Step              // Initialize step counter

LOOP: LD    Step
      EQ    0
      JMPC  RED_PHASE
      LD    Step
      EQ    1
      JMPC  GREEN_PHASE
      LD    Step
      EQ    2
      JMPC  YELLOW_PHASE
      JMP   RESET_STEP

RED_PHASE:
      LD    TRUE
      ST    Red_Lamp
      LD    FALSE
      ST    Green_Lamp
      ST    Yellow_Lamp
      CAL   T_Red(IN := TRUE, PT := T#5s)
      LD    T_Red.Q
      JMPC  NEXT_TO_GREEN
      JMP   LOOP

NEXT_TO_GREEN:
      LD    1
      ST    Step
      JMP   LOOP

GREEN_PHASE:
      LD    FALSE
      ST    Red_Lamp
      LD    TRUE
      ST    Green_Lamp
      ST    Yellow_Lamp
      CAL   T_Green(IN := TRUE, PT := T#5s)
      LD    T_Green.Q
      JMPC  NEXT_TO_YELLOW
      JMP   LOOP

NEXT_TO_YELLOW:
      LD    2
      ST    Step
      JMP   LOOP

YELLOW_PHASE:
      LD    FALSE
      ST    Red_Lamp
      ST    Green_Lamp
      LD    TRUE
      ST    Yellow_Lamp
      CAL   T_Yellow(IN := TRUE, PT := T#2s)
      LD    T_Yellow.Q
      JMPC  NEXT_TO_RESET
      JMP   LOOP

NEXT_TO_RESET:
RESET_STEP:
      LD    0
      ST    Step
      JMP   LOOP
```

### Line-by-Line Explanation

| Line | Purpose | CR Transition |
|---|---|---|
| `LD 0 / ST Step` | Initialize the state machine at step 0 | CR = 0 → Step = 0 |
| `LOOP: LD Step` | Load current state for evaluation | CR = Step value |
| `EQ 0 / JMPC RED_PHASE` | If CR == 0, jump to red phase | Branch executed if TRUE |
| `CAL T_Red(IN := TRUE, PT := T#5s)` | Start 5-second on-delay timer | Timer begins counting |
| `LD T_Red.Q / JMPC NEXT_TO_GREEN` | When timer done, advance state | Transition to Step = 1 |
| (Analogous for Green and Yellow) | Repeat pattern | — |
| `RESET_STEP: LD 0 / ST Step` | Loop back to red | CR = 0, Step = 0 |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Modifier

| Desired Logic | Naïve (Verbose) IL | Optimized (Examiner-Friendly) IL |
|---|---|---|
| $Q = A \cdot \overline{B}$ | `LD A / NOT / AND B / ST Q` | `LD A / ANDN B / ST Q` |
| $Q = \overline{(A + B)}$ | `LD A / OR B / NOT / ST Q` | `LD A / OR B / STN Q` |
| $Q = (A \cdot B) + (C \cdot D)$ | Sequential `AND`s with `OR` in between | Use `AND(` and `)` for grouping |
| Conditional execution | Wrap with explicit `JMPC` | Use `C` modifier: `STC Q0.0` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner's observation:** A 7-mark sub-question in Part B typically asks you to (a) write an IL program for a stated Boolean expression and (b) draw the trace table. **Both halves carry marks**; skipping the trace table forfeits 3-4 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — IL Execution Flow Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of POU] --> B[Instruction 1: LD operand]
    B --> C[CR updated with loaded value]
    C --> D{Operator type}
    D -- Boolean --> E[Apply AND OR XOR NOT to CR]
    D -- Arithmetic --> F[Apply ADD SUB MUL DIV to CR]
    D -- Compare --> G[Apply GT GE EQ NE LE LT to CR]
    D -- Control --> H[JMP CAL RET executed]
    E --> I[Next instruction]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{More instructions}
    J -- Yes --> B
    J -- No --> K[End of POU]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Deferred Operation (Parenthesis) Stack Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AND open parenthesis] --> B[Push pending AND to stack]
    B --> C[Evaluate inner operand]
    C --> D[Evaluate inner operand 2]
    D --> E[Close parenthesis]
    E --> F[Pop pending AND from stack]
    F --> G[Apply pending AND to CR]
    G --> H[Continue with next operator]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — IL Program Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    POU[Program Organization Unit POU] --> SEC[Declaration Section VAR]
    POU --> BODY[Instruction Body]
    SEC --> V1[Input variables VAR INPUT]
    SEC --> V2[Output variables VAR OUTPUT]
    SEC --> V3[Local temporaries VAR TEMP]
    BODY --> I1[Instruction 1: LD]
    BODY --> I2[Instruction 2: AND or OR]
    BODY --> I3[Instruction 3: ST]
    BODY --> I4[Control: JMP CAL RET]
    I1 --> CR[Current Result Register CR]
    I2 --> CR
    I3 --> OUT[Memory output location]
    I4 --> CFG[Control flow redirection]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of IL with Other IEC 61131-3 Languages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LANG[Five IEC 61131-3 Languages] --> T1[Textual]
    LANG --> G1[Graphical]
    T1 --> IL[Instruction List IL Low Level Assembler Like]
    T1 --> ST[Structured Text ST High Level Pascal Like]
    G1 --> LD[Ladder Diagram LD Relay Logic Visual]
    G1 --> FBD[Function Block Diagram FBD Signal Flow]
    G1 --> SFC[Sequential Function Chart SFC State Machine]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Instruction List (IL) and Structured Text (ST) as per IEC 61131-3. Mention at least three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Parameter | IL | ST |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Abstraction Level** | Low-level, assembler-like | High-level, Pascal-like |
| 2 | **Programming Style** | Sequential instruction-by-instruction, accumulator-based | Structured with loops, conditional blocks, functions |
| 3 | **Readability** | Compact but cryptic; suited to small routines | Highly readable; suited to complex algorithms |
| 4 | **Execution Speed** | Fastest (near machine cycle) | Slower due to higher-level interpretation |
| 5 | **Use Case** | Bit-logic, optimization routines | Math, loops, data processing |

**(1 mark per correctly elaborated point × 3 points = 3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the purpose of the `N` and `C` modifiers in an IL instruction with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **N (Negation) Modifier — 1.5 marks:**  
  The `N` modifier causes the PLC to **logically invert the operand value** before the operation is performed on the current result.  
  **Example:** `ANDN %IX0.2` is equivalent to performing an `AND` between the CR and the **negated value** of input `%IX0.2`.

- **C (Conditional) Modifier — 1.5 marks:**  
  The `C` modifier makes an instruction execute **only if the current result (CR) is TRUE (logical 1)**. If CR is FALSE, the instruction is skipped.  
  **Example:** `STC %QX0.0` stores the value of CR into output `%QX0.0` **only when CR is TRUE**; otherwise the output is left unchanged.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Convert the following Boolean logic into an Instruction List (IL) program and prepare a current result (CR) trace table. Assume inputs `I0.0 = 1`, `I0.1 = 0`, `I0.2 = 1`, `I0.3 = 0`. (14 marks)**

$$
Q = [(I0.0 \oplus I0.1) \cdot \overline{I0.2}] + I0.3
$$

**(a) Write the IL program — 7 marks**  
**(b) Construct and explain the CR trace table — 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Expand the XOR using equivalent Boolean identity.**  
$$
A \oplus B = (A \cdot \overline{B}) + (\overline{A} \cdot B)
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting into Q:**

$$
Q = \{[(I0.0 \cdot \overline{I0.1}) + (\overline{I0.0} \cdot I0.1)] \cdot \overline{I0.2}\} + I0.3
$$

**Step 3 — Translate to IL using deferred operations (parentheses) for clarity.**

```
LD    I0.0
ANDN  I0.1
LDN   I0.0
AND   I0.1
OR
ANDN  I0.2
OR    I0.3
ST    Q0.0
```

**Step 4 — Validation by alternative one-liner approach (cross-check).**

```
LD    I0.0
XOR   I0.1          // XOR is permitted in many IL dialects
ANDN  I0.2
OR    I0.3
ST    Q0.0
```

**Note for examiner:** If the dialect does not support `XOR` natively, the 6-line version using `ANDN`/`LDN`/`OR` is the canonical textbook answer.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**CR Trace Table (using the XOR version):**

| Line | Instruction | Operand Value | CR Before | Operation Detail | CR After |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LD I0.0` | I0.0 = 1 | — | Load 1 into CR | **1** |
| 2 | `XOR I0.1` | I0.1 = 0 | 1 | 1 ⊕ 0 = 1 | **1** |
| 3 | `ANDN I0.2` | I0.2 = 1, negated = 0 | 1 | 1 AND 0 = 0 | **0** |
| 4 | `OR I0.3` | I0.3 = 0 | 0 | 0 OR 0 = 0 | **0** |
| 5 | `ST Q0.0` | — | 0 | Store 0 into Q0.0 | Q0.0 = **0** |

**Algebraic verification:**

$$
Q = [(1 \oplus 0) \cdot \overline{1}] + 0 = [1 \cdot 0] + 0 = 0
$$

✅ **IL trace matches Boolean algebra.** Both yield `Q = 0`. (3 marks for correct table + 2 marks for algebraic verification + 2 marks for explanation of each line).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A conveyor system has two limit switches: `LS1` (start sensor, normally open) and `LS2` (end sensor, normally open). A motor `M` must run only when an operator presses `START` (a momentary push button) AND `LS1` detects a product. The system must stop immediately if `LS2` detects the product OR an emergency `ESTOP` is pressed. Write an IL program and explain how the `JMP` and `C` modifier achieve safe shutdown. (14 marks)**

**(a) Develop the IL program for the conveyor motor control — 7 marks**  
**(b) Explain the role of `JMP` and conditional modifiers in emergency shutdown — 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the Boolean expression.**

$$
M = START \cdot LS1 \cdot \overline{(LS2 + ESTOP)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply De Morgan's theorem for clean IL mapping.**

$$
M = START \cdot LS1 \cdot \overline{LS2} \cdot \overline{ESTOP}
$$

**Step 3 — Translate to IL.**

```
LD    START
AND   LS1
ANDN  LS2
ANDN  ESTOP
ST    M
```

**Step 4 — Add a self-holding (latching) loop for the START push button (since it's momentary).**

```
LD    START
OR    M
AND   LS1
ANDN  LS2
ANDN  ESTOP
ST    M
```

**Step 5 — Add an emergency override using `JMP` to a safe state sub-routine.**

```
LD    ESTOP
JMPC  SHUTDOWN

LD    START
OR    M
AND   LS1
ANDN  LS2
ST    M
JMP   END_PROGRAM

SHUTDOWN:
LD    FALSE
ST    M

END_PROGRAM:
```

**Explanation of latch (1 mark):** The `OR M` line provides feedback so the motor stays ON even after the operator releases the momentary START button. The latch is automatically broken the moment `LS2` or `ESTOP` becomes TRUE, because `ANDN` forces the result to FALSE.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Role of `JMPC` (Conditional Jump) — 3.5 marks:**

- The instruction `LD ESTOP / JMPC SHUTDOWN` examines the state of `ESTOP` on **every scan cycle** (typical scan time 10-50 ms).
- If `ESTOP = TRUE` (CR = 1), the jump is taken and execution flows directly to the `SHUTDOWN` label, **bypassing all subsequent logic**.
- At the `SHUTDOWN` label, `LD FALSE / ST M` forces the motor output to 0 unconditionally.
- This is the IEC 61131-3 equivalent of a **hardwired safety relay** — it guarantees the motor is de-energized regardless of any latched state.

**Role of the `C` Modifier (Conditional Execution) — 3.5 marks:**

- The `C` modifier allows a **single instruction** to be made conditional, eliminating the need for an entire `LD … / JMPC …` block.
- For example, `STC M` stores the current result into `M` **only if CR is TRUE**; if CR is FALSE, the ST operation is **skipped entirely**, preserving the previous value of `M`.
- However, for **safety-critical shutdown**, IL programmers prefer the explicit `JMP` approach because it is **easier to read, audit, and certify** by safety regulators (e.g., IEC 61508, ISO 13849).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `:` after a label** (`SHUTDOWN` instead of `SHUTDOWN:`) will cause a syntax error; marks are forfeited (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Skipping the CR trace table** in sub-part (b) costs 3-4 marks. Examiners specifically look for a **tabular column-wise presentation**.
> 3. **Confusing `ST` with `S`/`R`**: `ST` overwrites the variable every scan (potential **bouncing** issue), whereas `S` (Set) and `R` (Reset) **latch** the value. For START/STOP push button logic, the latched form using `S` and `R` is preferred by examiners.
> 4. **Writing LD with multiple operands separated by commas** is syntactically invalid — `LD` accepts only one operand. Use `LD` followed by `AND`/`OR` for multiple operands.
> 5. **Negation errors**: Using `NOT` as a standalone operator without understanding it acts on the CR (not on the next operand) is a very common mistake. Use `ANDN`, `ORN`, `LDN` for operand-side negation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IL is a low-level, accumulator-based textual language** standardized in **IEC 61131-3**.
- Every IL statement = **Operator + Modifier (optional) + Operand(s)**.
- The **Current Result (CR)** register is the heart of IL — almost every operator modifies it.
- Key load/store mnemonics: `LD`, `LDN`, `ST`, `STN`, `S`, `R`.
- Key Boolean mnemonics: `AND`, `ANDN`, `OR`, `ORN`, `XOR`, `NOT`.
- Key arithmetic mnemonics: `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `MOD`.
- Key comparison mnemonics: `EQ`, `NE`, `GT`, `GE`, `LT`, `LE`.
- Key control-flow mnemonics: `JMP`, `JMPC`, `CAL`, `CALC`, `RET`, `RETC`.
- The **`N` modifier** negates the **operand**; the `C` modifier makes the operation **conditional on CR = TRUE**.
- **Parenthesis modifiers** `(` and `)` enable **deferred operations**, mimicking mathematical grouping.
- IL is **executed sequentially** top-to-bottom unless redirected by a jump.
- IL is the **fastest executing** IEC 61131-3 language and the most memory-efficient.
- IL was **deprecated in IEC 61131-3 Edition 3 (2013)** but remains in normative annex; ST is its modern successor.
- Siemens calls it **STL (Statement List)**; ABB retains the original **IL** name; both are IL dialects.
- For KTU exams: **always pair the IL code with a CR trace table** — they are complementary and both carry marks.
- Safety interlocks (`ESTOP`) are best implemented with **explicit `JMP` to a SHUTDOWN routine**, not with subtle `C` modifier tricks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relay Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Relay Logic ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Relay Logic** is a method of implementing Boolean control expressions using hard-wired electromechanical relays, contacts (normally-open and normally-closed), and coils interconnected in a graphical schematic language called **Relay Ladder Logic (RLL)**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, relay logic is positioned as the *direct predecessor and conceptual foundation* of modern PLC programming, since the ladder diagram (LD) language standardized under **IEC 61131-3** is a direct descendant of the relay schematic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** > - **Rung:** A single horizontal line in a ladder diagram representing one control equation. > - **Coil:** The relay output device represented symbolically as `( )`. > - **Contact:** A switch actuated by a relay coil, represented as `| |` (NO) or `|/|` (NC). > - **Seal-in (Latching):** A feedback contact arrangement that holds a relay energized after the start signal is released. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **staircase light circuit in a colonial bungalow** where one switch at the bottom of the stairs turns ON a bulb, and another switch at the top can also turn it OFF. The wiring that lets either switch toggle the bulb is implemented physically by *two-way switches* — but the moment you want **three or more switches** to participate, the wiring becomes a nightmare. Now imagine replacing each switch with an **electromagnet-driven switch (relay)** controlled by push-buttons, and routing the wires through a **logical breadboard** that enforces "all buttons pressed AND", "any button pressed OR", "normally closed NC" rules. That physical breadboard, drawn in schematic form, *is* Relay Logic. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Every line of Ladder Diagram (LD) you write in a PLC can be traced back to an equivalent electromagnetic relay circuit. PLCs simply replaced the *physical wiring* with *software emulation* of those contacts and coils. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics The following standard metrics are expected to be memorized for KTU examinations: - **Standard control voltage:** **24 V DC** (modern) or **120/230 V AC** (legacy industrial panels). - **Relay operate time (typical electromechanical):** **10–50 ms**. - **Relay release time:** **5–25 ms**. - **Minimum mechanical life:** **10⁷ operations (10 million)**. - **Minimum electrical life (at rated load):** **10⁵ operations (100,000)**. - **IEC 61131-3 standard contact symbols** are universal — NO = `| |`, NC = `|/|`, Coil = `( )`. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Relay Logic System Functions A relay logic system executes Boolean expressions through a deterministic, hardware-evaluated process. The logic flow can be broken down into the following structured steps: 1. **Input Sensing Stage:** Field devices (push-buttons, limit switches, sensors) apply a voltage to the input terminals. These inputs are presented to the logic network as either **Normally Open (NO)** or **Normally Closed (NC)** contact representations. 2. **Logical Evaluation Stage:** The contacts arranged in series (representing logical AND) and parallel (representing logical OR) form a single rung. Current can flow from the **left rail (L₁)** to the **right rail (L₂)** only if the Boolean expression formed by the contacts evaluates to TRUE. 3. **Output Actuation Stage:** When current flows through the rung, it energizes the **output coil**, which in turn closes its associated NO contacts and opens its NC contacts. The mechanical motion of the armature performs the physical switching. 4. **Feedback / Seal-in Stage:** A contact of the energized coil is wired in parallel with the initiating start push-button to form a *holding path*. This ensures the coil remains energized even after the start button is released. 5. **Decay / De-energization Stage:** Pressing the stop button (NC contact wired in series) breaks the holding path, current collapses, and the magnetic field dissipates, releasing the armature. ### 2.2 The Three Fundamental Logic Gates in Relay Logic The mapping between Boolean logic and relay contact networks is foundational: - **Logical AND** → Contacts in **series**. Current flows only when *all* contacts are closed. - **Logical OR** → Contacts in **parallel**. Current flows when *at least one* contact is closed. - **Logical NOT** → Use of a **Normally Closed (NC)** contact. The output is energized when the input is NOT pressed. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Relay logic is the engineering bedrock of: - **Industrial motor starters** (Direct-On-Line, Star-Delta, Reversing starters). - **Safety interlocks** in machine tools (guard door switches wired in series). - **Traffic light controllers** in legacy municipal infrastructure. - **Process shutdown systems** in oil & gas (ESD — Emergency Shutdown Systems). - **Elevator control panels** prior to PLC retrofit. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Boolean Function | Relay Equivalent | Symbol | |---|---|---| | $Y = A \cdot B$ | Two NO contacts **in series** | AND gate | | $Y = A + B$ | Two NO contacts **in parallel** | OR gate | | $Y = \overline{A}$ | Single **NC** contact | NOT gate | | $Y = A \cdot \overline{B}$ | NO contact A **in series** with NC contact B | AND-NOT | | $Y = A \cdot B + C$ | Series block AB in parallel with C | Compound | | $\text{Seal-in}$ | $Y = (S + Y) \cdot \overline{R}$ | Latch/Start-Stop | | $f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{op} + T_{rel}}$ | Switching frequency | Hz | | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | Operational duty | % | | **Standard control voltage** | $V_{c} = 24 \text{ V DC}$ | Volts | | **Relay operate time** | $t_{op} \approx 10\text{–}50 \text{ ms}$ | ms | | **Relay release time** | $t_{rel} \approx 5\text{–}25 \text{ ms}$ | ms | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *seal-in equation* $Y = (S + Y) \cdot \overline{R}$ with a simple parallel connection. Note the **stop input R is in series** with the *entire* parallel combination, ensuring dominance of the stop signal. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Boolean-to-Relay Conversion Methodology To convert any Boolean function into an equivalent relay logic rung, apply the **canonical translation algorithm**: **Step 1:** Identify the input variables $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ and the output $Y$. **Step 2:** For each variable, decide its contact type: - A **non-negated** variable $X_i$ uses a **NO contact**. - A **negated** variable $\overline{X_i}$ uses an **NC contact**. **Step 3:** Translate the operators: - Multiplication ($\cdot$) between variables → **series** connection. - Addition ($+$) between terms → **parallel** connection. **Step 4:** Verify De Morgan equivalence for NOT of sums/products. ### 3.2 Worked Example 1: AND-OR Logic Convert $Y = (A \cdot B) + (C \cdot D)$ into a relay ladder rung. **Step-by-step expansion:** $$ \begin{aligned} Y &= (A \cdot B) + (C \cdot D) \\ Y &= \text{(Term 1)} + \text{(Term 2)} \\ \text{Term 1} &= A \cdot B \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NO A in series with NO B} \\ \text{Term 2} &= C \cdot D \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NO C in series with NO D} \\ Y &= (\text{Term 1}) \parallel (\text{Term 2}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Parallel branches} \end{aligned} $$ > **Resulting rung layout:** > `── A NO ── B NO ───────────────( Y )──` > `── C NO ── D NO ───────────────┘` ### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Seal-in (Start-Stop Latch) Derivation **Problem:** Design a relay circuit where pressing START (S) turns ON motor M, and pressing STOP (R) turns it OFF. The motor must remain ON after START is released. **Boolean formulation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State equation:} \quad Y(t) &= (S + Y(t-1)) \cdot \overline{R} \\ \text{Logic flow:} \quad &\text{If } R = 1 \text{ (stop pressed), then } Y = 0 \text{ (forced OFF)} \\ &\text{If } R = 0 \text{ and } S = 1 \text{, then } Y = 1 \text{ (set)} \\ &\text{If } R = 0 \text{ and } S = 0 \text{ and } Y(t-1) = 1 \text{, then } Y = 1 \text{ (hold)} \\ &\text{If } R = 0 \text{ and } S = 0 \text{ and } Y(t-1) = 0 \text{, then } Y = 0 \text{ (idle)} \end{aligned} $$ **Relay translation:** - **S** = NO push-button (Start) - **R** = NC push-button (Stop) - **M(aux)** = auxiliary NO contact of motor contactor, wired in parallel with S > **Final rung:** > `── S NO ───────────┬──────────( M )──` > `── M aux NO ──────┘ │` > `── R NC ──────────────────────────┘` ### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Frequency-of-Operation Derivation A relay operates with an average operate time of $t_{op} = 30 \text{ ms}$ and release time $t_{rel} = 15 \text{ ms}$. Calculate the maximum sustainable switching frequency. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{op} &= 30 \text{ ms} = 0.030 \text{ s} \\ T_{rel} &= 15 \text{ ms} = 0.015 \text{ s} \\ T_{cycle} &= T_{op} + T_{rel} \\ T_{cycle} &= 0.030 + 0.015 = 0.045 \text{ s} \\ f_{sw} &= \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} \\ f_{sw} &= \dfrac{1}{0.045} \\ f_{sw} &\approx 22.22 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering implication:** A maximum of **22.22 Hz** means this relay cannot be used for high-speed pulse applications such as encoder counting, where solid-state or PLC high-speed inputs are required. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Boolean Functions in Relay Logic | Boolean Expression | Rung Description | Application | |---|---|---| | $Y = A \cdot B \cdot C$ | 3 NO contacts in series | Triple-interlock guard | | $Y = A + B + C$ | 3 NO contacts in parallel | Multi-location ON | | $Y = \overline{A}$ | Single NC contact | Inverter / Inverse | | $Y = A \cdot \overline{B}$ | NO A in series with NC B | Permissive with inhibit | | $Y = (A+B) \cdot C$ | OR block in series with C | Coincidence logic | | $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | NO A in parallel with NO B (De Morgan) | NOT-AND equivalent | | $Y = A \oplus B$ | $(A \cdot \overline{B}) + (\overline{A} \cdot B)$ | XOR — direction sensing | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Relay Logic System Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a hard-wired relay logic control panel, from field inputs to actuator outputs. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Field Inputs
Push Buttons, Limit Switches, Sensors] nodeB[Input Interface Module
24 V DC Conditioning Circuitry] nodeC[Relay Logic Engine
Ladder Diagram Hard-Wired Contacts] nodeD[Power Distribution Bus
L1 L2 AC Supply 230 V] nodeE[Output Coils
Motor Contactors, Solenoids, Indicators] nodeF[Mechanical Feedback Path
Auxiliary NO NC Contacts of Coils] nodeG[Actuator Stage
Motors, Valves, Lamps] nodeH[Mechanical Process
Conveyor Pump Machine Tool] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeD --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeC ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Ladder Diagram — Start-Stop Seal-in Circuit The diagram below shows the **functional flow** of a classical motor seal-in (latching) circuit. Each rung represents a logical evaluation path. ```mermaid graph LR nodeS[Start PB
NO Type] nodeR[Stop PB
NC Type] nodeM[Motor Coil
Output Contactor] nodeAux[Aux Contact of M
NO Feedback] nodeOL[Overload Relay
NC Protection Contact] nodeS --- nodeAux nodeAux --- nodeOL nodeOL --- nodeM nodeR --- nodeM nodeAux -.feedback hold.-> nodeS ``` ### 4.3 Logic Mapping Diagram — Boolean to Relay Topology ```mermaid graph TD inp1[Input A
NO Contact] inp2[Input B
NO Contact] inp3[Input C
NC Contact] outCoil[Output Y
Relay Coil] inp1 --> seriesBlock1 inp2 --> seriesBlock1 seriesBlock1 --> seriesBlock1 seriesBlock1 --> parallelBlock inp3 --> parallelBlock parallelBlock --> outCoil ``` ### 4.4 Rung-by-Rung Translation Reference | IEC Symbol | Meaning | Boolean | |---|---|---| | `| |` | Normally Open Contact | $X$ | | `|/|` | Normally Closed Contact | $\overline{X}$ | | `( )` | Output Coil | $Y$ | | `—(/)—` | Negated Coil | $\overline{Y}$ | | `—(S)—` | Set Latch Coil | $Y \leftarrow 1$ | | `—(R)—` | Reset Latch Coil | $Y \leftarrow 0$ | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Relay Logic. List any two advantages and two limitations of relay-based control systems over PLC-based control.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Relay Logic is a hard-wired control method that implements Boolean logic functions using interconnected electromechanical relays, contacts, and coils represented graphically as a ladder diagram. **Advantages (1 mark):** 1. Provides **galvanic isolation** between control and power circuits. 2. Operates reliably in extreme environments (high temperature, electrical noise) without requiring firmware. **Limitations (1 mark):** 1. **Physical rewiring** required for every logic change — high downtime for modification. 2. Limited operational life (**10⁵ electrical operations**) due to mechanical contact wear. --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the function of a seal-in (latching) contact with a neat sketch of the start-stop seal-in circuit.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **seal-in contact** is an auxiliary NO contact of an output relay wired in **parallel** with the start push-button. When the coil energizes momentarily via the start button, the auxiliary contact closes, providing an **alternative current path** that holds the coil energized even after the start button is released. The coil can only be de-energized by breaking the holding path through the stop (NC) push-button. This forms the foundational latching mechanism for motor starters. > **Sketch (described):** `──[ S NO ]──┬──( M )──` with `──[ M aux NO ]──┘` and `──[ R NC ]──` in series with the rung. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Relay Logic Design + Seal-in Circuit **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Design a relay logic circuit to implement the Boolean function: $$Y = (A \cdot \overline{B}) + (C \cdot D)$$ Draw the equivalent ladder rung and explain the operation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For an industrial conveyor motor, design a **start-stop seal-in circuit** with overload protection. Derive the Boolean state equation and tabulate the truth table. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1 — Term Decomposition (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} Y &= T_1 + T_2 \\ T_1 &= A \cdot \overline{B} \\ T_2 &= C \cdot D \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Contact Type Selection (2 marks):** - $A$ → NO contact - $\overline{B}$ → NC contact - $C$ → NO contact - $D$ → NO contact **Step 3 — Connection Topology (2 marks):** - $T_1$: NO $A$ in series with NC $B$ - $T_2$: NO $C$ in series with NO $D$ - $T_1$ in parallel with $T_2$ **Step 4 — Rung Description (1 mark):** - Left rail → [A NO] — [B NC] — out to coil → - Left rail → [C NO] — [D NO] — out to coil → - Both branches terminate at coil Y on the right rail. > **Truth Table (1 mark, included in marks):** > $Y=1$ when $(A=1 \text{ AND } B=0) \text{ OR } (C=1 \text{ AND } D=1)$. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Define variables (1 mark):** - $S$ = Start (NO push-button) - $R$ = Stop (NC push-button) - $OL$ = Overload relay contact (NC) - $M$ = Motor contactor coil - $M_{aux}$ = Auxiliary NO contact of $M$ **Step 2 — State Equation Derivation (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} M(t) &= (S + M_{aux}) \cdot \overline{R} \cdot \overline{OL} \\ \text{Or more formally:} \quad M(t) &= \overline{R} \cdot \overline{OL} \cdot (S + M(t-1)) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Truth Table (3 marks):** | S | R | OL | M(t-1) | M(t) | Description | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Idle — no start command | | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | Press start — motor energizes | | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Hold via seal-in | | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Press stop — motor de-energizes | | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Overload trips — motor de-energizes | | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Stop dominates — safety priority | **Step 4 — Rung Diagram Description (1 mark):** `──[ S NO ]──┬──[ OL NC ]──( M )──` `──[ M aux NO ]──┘` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. Students often place the **stop contact in parallel** instead of in series. *Stop must always be in series* to ensure fail-safe dominance. > 2. Overload contact $OL$ must be **NC type** wired in series — if forgotten, the safety function is bypassed (loss of 2 marks). > 3. Forgetting the seal-in auxiliary contact $M_{aux}$ means the motor will not latch and will drop out the moment the start button is released. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Time-Delay Relay + Interlock Design **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Retest]** **(a)** Explain the operation of an **ON-delay timer relay** with a neat sketch. How is it implemented in relay logic to start a motor only after a 10-second delay following the start command? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a relay logic circuit for a **forward-reverse motor control** with electrical and mechanical interlocks. Draw the ladder diagram and explain the operation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1 — TON Definition (1 mark):** An **ON-delay timer (TON)** begins timing when its enable input is energized. The output contact closes only after the preset time has elapsed and remains closed as long as the input remains energized. **Step 2 — Symbol & Working (2 marks):** - Symbol: `——[ TON, T#10s ]——` - Upon energization of the timer coil, an internal clock begins counting. - After 10 seconds, the timer contact `T_done` closes. **Step 3 — Relay Logic Implementation (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Rung 1:} \quad M_{delay} &= (S + M_{delay}) \cdot \overline{R} \cdot \overline{T_{done}} \\ \text{Rung 2:} \quad M_{motor} &= (M_{delay}) \cdot \overline{R_{motor}} \\ \text{Timer:} \quad T_{done} &= \text{TON}(M_{delay}, 10 \text{ s}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Sequence Description (1 mark):** 1. Press S → Rung 1 energizes $M_{delay}$ (seal-in via its own aux). 2. Timer $T_{done}$ begins counting 10 s. 3. After 10 s, $T_{done}$ contact closes → Rung 2 energizes $M_{motor}$. 4. Press $R_{motor}$ → motor stops, seal-in releases. > **Note:** In pure electromechanical relay logic, an **ON-delay pneumatic or motor-driven timer** is used. In PLC, this is replaced by a TON instruction block. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Variable Definition (1 mark):** - $F$ = Forward push-button (NO) - $R$ = Reverse push-button (NO) - $S$ = Stop (NC) - $K_1$ = Forward contactor coil - $K_2$ = Reverse contactor coil - $K_{1aux}$ = Auxiliary NC contact of $K_1$ (interlock) - $K_{2aux}$ = Auxiliary NC contact of $K_2$ (interlock) **Step 2 — State Equations (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} K_1(t) &= (F + K_{1aux}) \cdot \overline{R_{press}} \cdot \overline{K_{2aux}} \cdot \overline{S} \\ K_2(t) &= (R + K_{2aux}) \cdot \overline{F_{press}} \cdot \overline{K_{1aux}} \cdot \overline{S} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Rung Diagram (2 marks):** ``` Rung 1 (Forward): F NO ──┬── K2aux NC ── K1aux NC ── Stop NC ──( K1 )── └── K1aux NO ──┘ Rung 2 (Reverse): R NO ──┬── K1aux NC ── K2aux NC ── Stop NC ──( K2 )── └── K2aux NO ──┘ ``` **Step 4 — Operation Explanation (2 marks):** - **Electrical Interlock:** $K_{2aux}$ NC contact in Rung 1 prevents $K_1$ from energizing while $K_2$ is active. Conversely, $K_{1aux}$ NC contact in Rung 2 prevents $K_2$ from energizing while $K_1$ is active. - **Mechanical Interlock:** A physical lever mechanism between the contactors provides back-up prevention of simultaneous engagement — critical to avoid a **phase-to-phase short circuit** in reversing starters. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Both electrical AND mechanical interlocks** are required for full marks — students often provide only one. Loss of 2–3 marks. > 2. The push-buttons $F$ and $R$ must be **mechanically interlocked** (3-position) so the operator cannot press both simultaneously. Missing this loses 1 mark. > 3. Stop contact $S$ must appear in **both rungs in series** to be dominant. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Relay Logic (KTU 2024):** - ⚡ Relay Logic = **hard-wired Boolean control** using electromechanical components; precursor to PLC ladder logic. - 🔁 **AND** = series contacts; **OR** = parallel contacts; **NOT** = normally-closed contact. - 🔒 **Seal-in (latching)** equation: $Y(t) = (S + Y(t-1)) \cdot \overline{R}$. - ⏱ **ON-delay (TON):** output changes after preset time elapses; **OFF-delay (TOF):** output holds for preset time after input drops. - 🛑 **Stop and overload contacts** are always wired in **series** with the rung for fail-safe dominance. - 🔄 **Forward-Reverse control** requires *electrical interlock* (NC aux contacts) **and** *mechanical interlock* (physical lever). - 📐 **Standard contact symbols:** NO = $| \mid$, NC = $|/ \mid$, Coil = $( \ )$. - ⏳ Typical relay times: $t_{op} \approx 10\text{–}50 \text{ ms}$, $t_{rel} \approx 5\text{–}25 \text{ ms}$. - 🔢 Max switching frequency $f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{op} + T_{rel}}$. - 🏭 **Ladder diagram power rails:** Left rail = L₁ (hot), Right rail = L₂ (neutral). Power flows left-to-right. - ⚠️ The **IEC 61131-3** ladder diagram language preserves relay logic syntax for industrial continuity. - 🧮 **De Morgan equivalents** must be mastered: $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ and $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$.

topics.title: Timers and Counters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Timers and Counters # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 PLC Timers – Formal Definition A **Timer** in a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) is a dedicated internal software instruction (an output instruction) used to measure and control **time-based events** in an industrial sequence. It monitors an enable input condition, tracks elapsed time using the processor's internal clock, and updates a **Done Bit (DN)** when the accumulated time equals the **Preset Value (PR)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"A timer is an instruction that provides a time-delay function. The timer is started by an input condition and after a preset time interval has elapsed, an output is energized."* ### Timer Block – Standard Signal Names (IEC 61131-3) | Tag | Full Form | Function | |:----|:----------|:---------| | **IN** | Input | Enable / start the timing operation | | **PT** | Preset Time | The target delay value | | **ET** | Elapsed Time | The current running time value | | **Q** | Output | True when ET $\geq$ PT | ### Conceptual Analogy – The "Egg Timer" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an **egg timer** in your kitchen. You twist the knob to **set** a duration (Preset Value), you flip it over to **start** the timing (Enable), and the sand slowly falls to measure the time. When all the sand has fallen (Accumulated Time $\geq$ Preset Time), the bell rings (Output turns ON). A PLC timer works *exactly* like this, except the "sand" is a digital counter inside the CPU clock. ## 1.2 PLC Counters – Formal Definition A **Counter** is an internal PLC instruction that **counts the number of events** (input transitions or pulses). It is used where the process logic depends on the *number of occurrences* of an event rather than the *duration* of the event. ### Counter Block – Standard Signal Names (IEC 61131-3) | Tag | Full Form | Function | |:----|:----------|:---------| | **CU** | Count Up | Increments the counter on a rising edge | | **CD** | Count Down | Decrements the counter on a rising edge | | **R** | Reset | Forces the accumulated value to **zero** | | **LD** | Load | Presets the counter with a value | | **PV** | Preset Value | The target count limit | | **CV** | Current Value | The instantaneous count | | **Q / DN** | Done | True when CV $\geq$ PV | | **QU** | Count Up Overflow | True on wraparound (max $\rightarrow$ min) | | **QD** | Count Down Underflow | True on wraparound (min $\rightarrow$ max) | ### Conceptual Analogy – The "Bottling Plant" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **bottling plant conveyor belt**. Every time a bottle passes a sensor, the counter ticks once (Count Up Input). When exactly **12 bottles** have been counted (Preset Value reached), the "Box Full" light turns ON (Done Bit), and an actuator pushes the box onto the next conveyor. A PLC counter is the digital equivalent of a **mechanical tally counter** used at stadium gates. ## 1.3 Classification Overview (KTU Module 2 Scope) **Three Timer Types:** 1. **On-Delay Timer (TON / T_ODT)** – Delay on energization 2. **Off-Delay Timer (TOF / T_OFFDT)** – Delay on de-energization 3. **Retentive / Pulse Timer (TP / T_PULSE)** – Maintains or pulses the output **Three Counter Types:** 1. **Count Up Counter (CTU)** – Increments only 2. **Count Down Counter (CTD)** – Decrements only 3. **Count Up/Down Counter (CTUD)** – Bidirectional counting --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *Timers* with *Counters*. Remember the golden rule: **Timer = measures TIME, Counter = counts EVENTS**. A timer "watches the clock"; a counter "watches the pulse." # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 On-Delay Timer (TON) – Operational Logic The On-Delay Timer is the **most frequently used timer** in industrial automation. Its symbol denotes: *"Wait for 'X' seconds AFTER the input turns ON, then turn the output ON."* ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. When the **Enable Input (IN)** transitions from $0 \rightarrow 1$, the timer starts. 2. The **Elapsed Time (ET)** begins incrementing at the rate of the **Time Base (TB)**. 3. **Timing Bit (TT)** is set to $1$ (timer is actively running). 4. When $ET \geq PT$, the **Done Bit (Q / DN)** is latched to $1$. 5. If $IN$ goes $0$ at *any time*, **ET is immediately reset to 0** and **Q becomes 0** (non-retentive). $$ T_{delay} = PT \times TB $$ Where $PT$ = Preset Time (in counts) and $TB$ = Time Base (resolution in seconds). ## 2.2 Off-Delay Timer (TOF) – Operational Logic The Off-Delay Timer delays the *turning OFF* of an output. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. When $IN = 1$, output $Q$ is immediately $1$ (no delay on energization). 2. When $IN$ transitions $1 \rightarrow 0$, the timer begins counting. 3. **ET** increments up to $PT$. 4. **Q remains 1** during the timing; it turns **0 only when $ET \geq PT$**. 5. If $IN$ returns to $1$ before $ET$ reaches $PT$, the timing is **aborted** and $ET$ is reset. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Used in conveyor systems to allow products to clear a section **before** stopping the motor, preventing jams. ## 2.3 Retentive / Pulse Timer (TP / TONR) A **retentive timer** retains its accumulated value even when the input goes OFF. It must be **explicitly reset** by a separate instruction. - **TP (Pulse Timer):** Generates a pulse of *exact* duration $PT$ on a rising edge, regardless of input pulse width. - **TONR (Retentive On-Delay):** Like TON, but $ET$ is NOT reset on $IN=0$. ## 2.4 Counter Operational Logic ### CTU – Count Up Counter Increments $CV$ by $1$ on every rising edge of $CU$. When $CV \geq PV$, bit $Q$ (Done) becomes $1$. $R$ resets $CV$ to zero. ### CTD – Count Down Counter Decrements $CV$ by $1$ on every rising edge of $CD$. When $CV \leq 0$, bit $Q$ (Done) becomes $1$. $LD$ loads $PV$ into $CV$. ### CTUD – Up/Down Counter Combines CTU and CTD.
