Both $QU$ and $QD$ flags detect integer overflow / underflow (wraparound). ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates all formula and timing relationships required for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). | # | Quantity | Formula / Relationship | Units | Remarks | |:-:|:---------|:-----------------------|:------|:--------| | 1 | Actual Delay | $T_{d} = PT \times TB$ | seconds | $PT$ in counts, $TB$ in s/count | | 2 | Frequency to Time | $T = 1 / f$ | seconds | $f$ in Hz | | 3 | Total Pulses | $N_{pulse} = f \times T_{window}$ | counts | $T_{window}$ in seconds | | 4 | Counter Final Value | $CV_{final} = CV_{initial} \pm n$ | counts | $n$ = no. of valid edges | | 5 | Timer Resolution | $TB = T_{d} / PT$ | s/count | Standard: 0.01, 0.1, 1.0 | | 6 | Done Bit State | $Q = 1$ when $ET \geq PT$ | Boolean | Latched in TON/TOF | | 7 | Reset Condition | $R = 1 \Rightarrow CV := 0$ | Boolean | Priority over CU/CD | | 8 | CTUD Overflow | $CV = 32767 \rightarrow -32768$ | counts | Sets $QU = 1$ | | 9 | CTUD Underflow | $CV = -32768 \rightarrow 32767$ | counts | Sets $QD = 1$ | | 10 | Scan Time Effect | $T_{error} \leq 2 \times T_{scan}$ | seconds | One scan uncertainty | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Timers and counters are the backbone of **batch process control** (e.g., mixing chemicals for exactly 30 s), **traffic light control** (green phase duration), **packaging machines** (count items per box), and **water treatment** (pump runtime limits). ## 2.6 Memory Type Comparison | Timer/Counter Type | Memory Type | Power Loss Behavior | KTU Example | |:-------------------|:------------|:--------------------|:------------| | TON / TOF | Non-retentive | $ET$ and $Q$ reset | Allen-Bradley SLC | | TONR / Retentive | Retentive | $ET$ retained; $Q$ may reset | Allen-Bradley PLC-5 | | CTU / CTD | Non-retentive (default) | $CV$ reset to 0 | Most modern PLCs | | CTU with retentive flag | Retentive | $CV$ retained | Siemens S7-1500 | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 – Timer Delay Calculation ### Problem Statement A bottling conveyor must run for **45 seconds** after a start button is pressed. A PLC uses an **On-Delay Timer** with a **Time Base of 0.1 s**. Calculate the **Preset Value (PT)** that must be entered in the timer instruction. ### Given Data - Required delay: $T_d = 45$ s - Time Base: $TB = 0.1$ s - Formula: $PT = T_d / TB$ ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Identify the governing relationship. From the formula sheet: $$ T_d = PT \times TB $$ **Step 2:** Rearrange the equation to solve for $PT$: $$ PT = \frac{T_d}{TB} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values: $$ PT = \frac{45 \; \text{s}}{0.1 \; \text{s/count}} $$ **Step 4:** Perform the division: $$ PT = 450 \; \text{counts} $$ **Step 5:** Convert to integer (PLC registers only accept whole numbers): $$ PT = 450 \quad (\text{integer, valid PLC input}) $$ ### Result Verification $$ T_d = 450 \times 0.1 = 45 \; \text{s} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!NOTE] > **Answer for KTU Exam:** Preset Value $PT = 450$ counts with Time Base $0.1$ s gives an exact $45$-second delay. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 – Counter Pulse Calculation ### Problem Statement A photoelectric sensor detects products on a conveyor at a frequency of **4 Hz**. How many products will the CTU counter register in **exactly 2 minutes**? ### Given Data - Pulse frequency: $f = 4$ Hz - Counting window: $T_{window} = 2 \; \text{min} = 120 \; \text{s}$ ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Recall the governing relationship from the formula sheet: $$ N_{pulse} = f \times T_{window} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values (consistent SI units: Hz = 1/s, s): $$ N_{pulse} = 4 \; \text{s}^{-1} \times 120 \; \text{s} $$ **Step 3:** Perform the multiplication: $$ N_{pulse} = 480 \; \text{pulses} $$ **Step 4:** Express the final counter value: $$ CV_{final} = 480 $$ ### Result Interpretation If the Preset Value is set to $PV = 480$, the Done Bit $Q$ will turn ON exactly after 2 minutes of operation. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 – Time Base Selection ### Problem Statement A chemical mixing process requires a delay of **7.5 seconds**. The available PLC has three time-base options: 0.01 s, 0.1 s, and 1.0 s. Determine which time base gives the **exact** preset and compute the corresponding $PT$. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Test Time Base $TB = 1.0$ s: $$ PT = \frac{7.5}{1.0} = 7.5 \quad (\text{not an integer, invalid for PLC}) $$ **Step 2:** Test Time Base $TB = 0.1$ s: $$ PT = \frac{7.5}{0.1} = 75 \quad (\text{exact integer, valid for PLC}) $$ **Step 3:** Test Time Base $TB = 0.01$ s: $$ PT = \frac{7.5}{0.01} = 750 \quad (\text{exact integer, valid for PLC}) $$ ### Selection Logic | Option | Preset $PT$ | Validity | Memory Used | |:-------|:-----------:|:---------|:------------| | $TB = 1.0$ s | 7.5 | **Invalid** | — | | $TB = 0.1$ s | 75 | **Valid** | 7 bits | | $TB = 0.01$ s | 750 | **Valid** | 10 bits | > [!NOTE] > **Answer for KTU Exam:** The optimal choice is $TB = 0.1$ s with $PT = 75$, because it uses the **least memory** while remaining exact. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Timer vs. Counter Side-by-Side | Parameter | Timer (TON) | Counter (CTU) | |:----------|:------------|:--------------| | Measures | Time duration | Number of events | | Trigger | Continuous enable ($IN=1$) | Edge (rising edge of $CU$) | | Increments by | Time Base tick | $1$ per pulse | | Reset | Automatic on $IN=0$ | Manual via $R$ input | | Preset Unit | Seconds (or ms) | Counts (integer) | | Output Action | $Q=1$ after delay | $Q=1$ after count reached | | Typical Use | Delay, dwell time | Tallying, batching | | Formula | $T = PT \times TB$ | $CV = CV_{prev} + 1$ | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram – On-Delay Timer (TON) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input IN
Enable Signal] --> B[Timer TON
Functional Block] B --> C[Time Base TB
0.01s or 0.1s or 1s] B --> D[Preset Value PT
User Defined] C --> E[Accumulator ET
Increments per TB] D --> F{Compare
ET vs PT} E --> F F -->|ET less PT| G[Output Q = 0
Timing Bit TT = 1] F -->|ET greater or equal PT| H[Output Q = 1
Done Bit DN = 1] I[Reset Signal R] --> B ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram – Counter (CTU) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Count Up Pulse CU
Rising Edge] --> B[CTU Counter
Functional Block] J[Load Signal LD] --> B K[Reset Signal R] --> B B --> C[Current Value CV
Integer Register] L[Preset Value PV
Target Count] --> D{Compare
CV vs PV} C --> D D -->|CV less PV| E[Done Bit Q = 0] D -->|CV greater or equal PV| F[Done Bit Q = 1
Count Complete] M[Overflow Flag QU] --> B ``` ## 4.3 Three Timer Types – Comparative Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PLC Timer
Instruction] --> B{Type Selection} B -->|Delay ON| C[TON
On-Delay Timer] B -->|Delay OFF| D[TOF
Off-Delay Timer] B -->|Pulse| E[TP
Pulse Timer] C --> C1[IN goes 1 then Wait PT
then Q becomes 1] D --> D1[IN goes 0 then Wait PT
then Q becomes 0] E --> E1[On rising edge Q becomes 1
for exactly PT seconds] ``` ## 4.4 Counter Type Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P[PLC Counter] --> Q{Type} Q -->|Count Up| R[CTU
CV increments] Q -->|Count Down| S[CTD
CV decrements] Q -->|Up and Down| T[CTUD
Bidirectional] R --> R1[Used for Tallying
Products in a Box] S --> S1[Used for Remaining
Items to Pack] T --> T1[Used for Position
Tracking like Elevator Floor] ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture – Timer-Counter Cascade (Batching System) ```mermaid flowchart LR X1[Start Push Button PB1] --> Y1[Timer TON T1
Fills Tank for 30s] Y1 --> Y2[Sensor LS1
Level Switch Activates] Y2 --> Y3[Counter CTU C1
Counts Bottles] Y3 --> Y4{12 Bottles?} Y4 -->|No| Y3 Y4 -->|Yes| Y5[Conveyor Motor OFF
Box Full Indicator ON] Z1[Reset Button PB2] --> Y3 Z1 --> Y1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** In exam answers, always accompany your ladder logic with a **functional block diagram** and a **timing diagram**. This demonstrates conceptual depth and fetches full marks. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between an On-Delay Timer (TON) and an Off-Delay Timer (TOF) in a PLC. Mention one industrial application of each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Aspect | On-Delay Timer (TON) | Off-Delay Timer (TOF) | |:-------|:---------------------|:----------------------| | Trigger | Delay occurs on **energization** | Delay occurs on **de-energization** | | Output Q | Q turns ON **after** delay $PT$ | Q turns OFF **after** delay $PT$ | | Reset | Automatic when $IN = 0$ | Automatic when $IN = 1$ | | Application | Star-Delta motor starter delay (3 s) | Conveyor overrun time for product clearance | **Industrial Applications:** - **TON:** Used in **motor star-delta starters** to introduce a 3-5 s delay between star and delta switching. - **TOF:** Used in **cooling fans** that must run for 30 s after the motor stops to dissipate residual heat. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Explain the function of a Count Up Counter (CTU) with a neat block diagram. List its key parameters.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): A **CTU counter** increments its internal accumulator (Current Value, $CV$) by **1 unit** on every rising edge of the Count Up input ($CU$). The counter is reset to zero by the Reset ($R$) input. The Done bit ($Q$) becomes logic $1$ when $CV \geq PV$ (Preset Value). **Key Parameters:** - $CU$ – Count Up pulse input - $R$ – Reset input (priority) - $PV$ – Preset Value (target) - $CV$ – Current Value (accumulator) - $Q$ / $DN$ – Done output bit - $QU$ – Overflow flag (when $CV$ exceeds the max limit, e.g., 32767) **Block Diagram:** ``` +-------+ CU --->| | R --->| CTU |--- Q (Done) PV --->| |--- CV (Current Value) +-------+ | | +--QU (Overflow) ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question has 14 marks. The two sub-parts (a) and (b) usually carry 7 marks each. Choose **one** of the two alternatives. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **a)** Explain the working of an **On-Delay Timer (TON)** with the help of a ladder logic diagram and a timing waveform. **(7 Marks)** **b)** A process requires a delay of **25 seconds** using a TON timer. If the available time bases are 0.01 s, 0.1 s, and 1.0 s, determine the **preset value** for each option and justify which is most suitable. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) – 7 Marks Solution:** **Working Principle of TON (3 Marks):** - The TON timer starts timing when the input $IN$ transitions from $0 \rightarrow 1$. - The internal accumulator $ET$ increments by the Time Base value on every PLC scan. - When $ET \geq PT$, the output $Q$ is energized and latched. - When $IN$ returns to $0$, $ET$ resets to $0$ and $Q$ de-energizes. **Ladder Logic Diagram (2 Marks):** ``` PB_Start TON Motor ---| |-------+---[TON]---+----( )--- | PT=50 | | TB=0.1s | +-----------+ | | +---[DN]----+ | Indicator Lamp_Ready ``` **Timing Waveform (2 Marks):** ``` Signal | ___ IN |___| |____________________________________ | t0 t1 Signal | ___________________ Q |_____________| |__________ | t0+PT ET | /| | / | | / | |____/ | | 0 PT ``` At $t_0$, $IN$ goes high → $ET$ starts ramping up. At $t_0 + PT$, $Q$ becomes $1$. **Part (b) – 7 Marks Solution:** **Given:** $T_d = 25$ s, Formula: $PT = T_d / TB$ **Calculation for each Time Base (5 Marks):** **Option 1:** $TB = 1.0$ s $$ PT_1 = \frac{25}{1.0} = 25 \; \text{counts} $$ **Option 2:** $TB = 0.1$ s $$ PT_2 = \frac{25}{0.1} = 250 \; \text{counts} $$ **Option 3:** $TB = 0.01$ s $$ PT_3 = \frac{25}{0.01} = 2500 \; \text{counts} $$ **Justification (2 Marks):** | Option | $PT$ | Validity | Memory | Verdict | |:-------|:-----|:---------|:-------|:--------| | $TB=1.0$ s | 25 | Valid | Smallest | **Best choice** – least memory, exact value | **Conclusion:** $TB = 1.0$ s with $PT = 25$ counts is the most suitable, as it requires the smallest preset (and therefore the least memory) while still producing an exact 25-second delay. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **a)** With a neat ladder diagram and timing diagram, explain the operation of an **Off-Delay Timer (TOF)**. State two industrial applications. **(7 Marks)** **b)** A photo-sensor detects bottles on a conveyor. The PLC counts the bottles using a CTU counter. The system must **stop the conveyor and sound a buzzer when 24 bottles have been counted**. Draw the ladder logic and explain the working. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) – 7 Marks Solution:** **Working of TOF Timer (3 Marks):** - When $IN = 1$, output $Q$ is immediately $1$ (no delay on start). - When $IN$ transitions $1 \rightarrow 0$, the timer begins counting. - $Q$ remains $1$ for the duration $PT$, then turns $0$ when $ET \geq PT$. - If $IN$ returns to $1$ before $PT$ elapses, the timing is aborted and $ET$ resets. **Ladder Logic (2 Marks):** ``` Motor_ON TOF Fan_Run ---| |--------+---[TOF]---+----( )--- | PT=30s | +----------+ ``` **Timing Diagram (2 Marks):** ``` Signal | ___________ _________ IN |___| |________| |___ | t0 t1 t2 Signal | ____________________ Q |___| |__________ | t0 t1+PT ``` The output $Q$ stays ON for $PT$ seconds *after* $IN$ goes OFF. **Two Industrial Applications (incorporated in explanation):** 1. **Cooling fan** in motors (fan runs 30 s after motor stops). 2. **Conveyor overrun** (motor runs briefly to clear products after stop signal). **Part (b) – 7 Marks Solution:** **Ladder Logic Diagram (4 Marks):** ``` Sensor CTU C1 Counter_Done ------| |----------+--[CTU]-----+-------| |--- | PV=24 | | | | +----+ +------------+ | | | | R (Reset) | | | ---(Reset_Button)--+ | | ---[DN]---+---- Buzzer | ---[DN]---+---- Conveyor_OFF ``` **Working Explanation (3 Marks):** - **Rung 1:** The photo-sensor input is the **Count Up input (CU)** to the CTU counter. The counter increments by 1 on every rising edge of the sensor signal (i.e., each detected bottle). - **Rung 2:** When the count $CV$ reaches the **Preset Value $PV = 24$**, the **Done bit (DN)** of the counter becomes logic 1. - **Rung 3:** The DN contact closes, energizing the **Buzzer** output. - **Rung 4:** The DN contact also energizes the **Conveyor_OFF** coil, stopping the conveyor belt. - **Reset:** A **Reset Push Button (PB_Reset)** is wired to the **R** input of the counter, which clears $CV$ back to 0 and de-energizes the outputs, readying the system for the next batch. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Timer/Counter Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** – Always show unit conversions explicitly (e.g., minutes to seconds) in your solution. Showing "$T_d = 2$" without "$2 \; \text{minutes}$" loses **1 mark** outright. > 2. **Non-integer Preset Value** – PLCs only accept integer preset values. If your answer is fractional, you must choose a different time base. > 3. **Confusing TON with TOF waveforms** – A common error is drawing the output turning ON after the delay (which is TON) when the question asked for TOF. Always re-read the question. > 4. **Missing the Reset Rung** – For counter questions, examiners look for an explicit **reset mechanism** in the ladder logic. Omitting this is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. > 5. **No Timing Diagram** – For any timer question, a **timing diagram (waveform)** is mandatory. Without it, expect a **2-3 mark penalty**. > 6. **Wrong Edge Triggering** – Counters respond to **rising edges (0→1)**, not level signals. Stating "the counter counts when the input is ON" is technically incorrect. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist – Timers and Counters** ### A. Timers - A **Timer** measures **elapsed time**; a **Counter** measures **number of events**. - The fundamental relationship is $T_{delay} = PT \times TB$. - **TON (On-Delay):** Delay occurs on **energization** ($IN: 0 \rightarrow 1$). - **TOF (Off-Delay):** Delay occurs on **de-energization** ($IN: 1 \rightarrow 0$). - **TP (Pulse):** Generates a fixed-duration pulse on a rising edge of $IN$. - **Retentive (TONR):** Retains $ET$ across power cycles; requires explicit reset. - Standard Time Bases: **0.01 s, 0.1 s, 1.0 s**. - The **Done Bit (DN/Q)** is latched and becomes $1$ when $ET \geq PT$. ### B. Counters - **CTU (Count Up):** Increments on rising edge of $CU$. - **CTD (Count Down):** Decrements on rising edge of $CD$. - **CTUD:** Bidirectional; sets $QU$ on overflow and $QD$ on underflow. - **Reset ($R$)** has the **highest priority** and forces $CV := 0$. - The **Done Bit (DN/Q)** is set when $CV \geq PV$. - Counters operate on **edges**, not levels. ### C. Key Formulas to Memorize $$ T_{delay} = PT \times TB $$ $$ PT = \frac{T_d}{TB} $$ $$ N_{pulse} = f \times T_{window} $$ $$ CV_{final} = CV_{initial} \pm n $$ ### D. Ladder Logic Must-Haves - For **every timer question** → include a **timing waveform**. - For **every counter question** → include a **reset rung**. - Use proper **rung numbering** and **comment boxes** in exam answers. ### E. Industrial Application Domains - **Timers:** Star-Delta starters, traffic lights, mixing tanks, delay relays. - **Counters:** Bottle filling, parking lot occupancy, batch counting, elevator floor tracking. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip:** When solving a KTU numerical, **always state the formula first, then substitute the values**. Examiners award step marks for *method*, not just the final answer.

topics.title: Simple Programs in PLC: Ladder Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Programs in PLC: Ladder Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ladder Programming (Ladder Logic / Ladder Diagram - LD):** A graphical programming language defined in the **IEC 61131-3** standard, used to develop software for a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC). It represents the control logic as a series of **rungs** between two vertical **rails** (Power Rails), where each rung consists of input contacts and output coils that emulate the schematics of electromechanical relay-based control circuits.

The term *Ladder* is derived from the program's visual appearance: the vertical rails on both sides (representing the **L1 / Hot** and **L2 / Neutral** power supply) and the horizontal rungs in between resemble the rungs of a physical ladder. In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, students are expected to identify the **rung**, **rail**, **contact**, and **coil** nomenclature accurately.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition — Logic Scan:** A ladder program is **NOT** executed rung-by-rung in a static sense. The PLC CPU performs a **cyclic scan** consisting of three phases: **(1) Input Scan**, **(2) Program Scan**, and **(3) Output Scan**. The total cycle time is typically **5 ms to 20 ms** in modern PLCs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an old **electrical control panel** from the 1970s, filled with mechanical relays, timer wheels, and counter drums. An electrician would draw a *wiring diagram* showing how a Start Pushbutton, Stop Pushbutton, and Motor Contactor coil were physically wired together. Now, instead of physically wiring relays, a PLC lets us **draw the same diagram on a computer screen** and the PLC "pretends" the wiring is real. The contacts and coils become **software instructions** that the CPU evaluates thousands of times per second.

- **Rail (Left & Right vertical lines):** Represents the electrical supply (L1 and L2).
- **Rung (Horizontal line):** Represents a single logic path, like one wire in a control panel.
- **Contact (Normally Open / Normally Closed):** Acts like a relay contact that can pass or block current depending on an input's status.
- **Coil (Output):** Acts like a relay coil that energizes an output when current (logic continuity) reaches it.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Tip:** Always read a ladder rung **left to right, top to bottom**. If "electrical continuity" (logic TRUE) reaches the output coil, the output turns ON. This is identical to Kirchhoff's current law applied to a relay ladder.

### 1.3 Why Ladder Logic Dominates Industrial Automation

| Property | Ladder Logic | Other Languages (ST, FBD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Industry Adoption** | ~**70% of installed PLCs** | ~30% |
| **Learning Curve** | Low (electrician-friendly) | Moderate to High |
| **Troubleshooting** | Visual / On-line monitoring | Abstract / Code-based |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High (every semester) | Low |
| **Best For** | Discrete / Boolean control | Math / Data processing |

> [!NOTE]
> Ladder Logic is **not** a general-purpose programming language. It excels at **Boolean logic, timing, counting, and sequencing** but is inefficient for complex mathematics, loops, or string manipulation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Ladder Diagram

Every ladder diagram (regardless of manufacturer — Allen-Bradley / Rockwell, Siemens, Mitsubishi, Schneider) consists of three structural layers:

**Layer 1 — Power Rails**
- **Left Rail (L1):** Symbolizes the "hot" supply (logically TRUE / 1).
- **Right Rail (L2):** Symbolizes the return path (logically 0 / FALSE).
- These are **not physical terminals**; they are a graphical convention.

**Layer 2 — Rungs**
- A rung is one horizontal logic statement.
- Up to **5 to 7 contacts/coils** is typical for readability, though modern PLCs allow more.
- A rung is evaluated as a Boolean expression: if all series conditions AND parallel branches evaluate TRUE → the output coil energizes.

**Layer 3 — I/O Addressing**
- Each contact and coil is bound to a specific memory bit.
- **Allen-Bradley Convention (Rockwell / RSLogix):**
  * `I:1/0` → Input, Slot 1, Terminal 0
  * `O:2/0` → Output, Slot 2, Terminal 0
  * `B3:0/0` → Internal Bit (Binary), File 3, Word 0, Bit 0
- **Siemens Convention (TIA Portal):**
  * `I0.0` → Input, Byte 0, Bit 0
  * `Q0.0` → Output, Byte 0, Bit 0
  * `M0.0` → Merker (Internal Memory)

### 2.2 Core Bit-Level Instructions (The "Big Six")

| Instruction | Symbol | Mnemonic | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normally Open Contact (XIC)** | `—||—` | XIC (Examine If Closed) | TRUE when addressed bit = 1 |
| **Normally Closed Contact (XIO)** | `—|/\|—` | XIO (Examine If Open) | TRUE when addressed bit = 0 |
| **Output Energize (OTE)** | `—( )—` | OTE (Output Energize) | Sets bit to 1 if rung TRUE |
| **Output Latch (OTL)** | `—( L )—` | OTL (Output Latch) | Sets bit to 1, stays latched |
| **Output Unlatch (OTU)** | `—( U )—` | OTU (Output Unlatch) | Resets latched bit to 0 |
| **One-Shot Rising (OSR)** | `—[OSR]—` | OSR | TRUE for ONE scan on rising edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latching Rule (Hold-in Circuit):** An OTL remains ON even after the input condition goes FALSE, until an OTU is executed. This emulates the **seal-in contact** of a motor starter. The standard KTU exam question on this is the **Start-Stop Latch** (also called a *3-wire control* equivalent).

### 2.3 Timer Instructions — The Timekeepers

A PLC timer is a software-implemented timing device with three key elements:

1. **Enable Bit (EN):** The timer is actively timing when EN = 1.
2. **Timer-Timing Bit (TT):** TRUE while accumulated value < preset.
3. **Done Bit (DN):** TRUE when accumulated value ≥ preset.

**Three Timer Types:**

| Type | Mnemonic | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Delay Timer (TON)** | TON | Starts timing when EN=TRUE; DN becomes TRUE **after** preset elapses |
| **Off-Delay Timer (TOF)** | TOF | DN is TRUE immediately when EN=TRUE; turns OFF **after** preset elapses when EN goes FALSE |
| **Retentive Timer (RTO)** | RTO | Like TON but **retains** accumulated value across power loss / EN=FALSE |

### 2.4 Counter Instructions

| Type | Mnemonic | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Count-Up Counter (CTU)** | CTU | Increments accumulator on each false-to-true transition of input |
| **Count-Down Counter (CTD)** | CTD | Decrements accumulator on each false-to-true transition |
| **Up/Down Counter (CTUD)** | CTUD | Combines both |

Counter elements: **CU** (Count Up Enable), **CD** (Count Down Enable), **RES** (Reset), **DN** (Done), **OV** (Overflow), **UN** (Underflow).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> In ladder programming, "formulas" are mostly **timing math** and **scan time** relationships. The following are **KTU 2024 board-favorite equations**:

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{cycle} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output}$ | Total PLC scan cycle time | **ms** |
| 2 | $T_{accumulated} = N \times T_{base}$ | Timer accumulated time = count × time-base | **ms** |
| 3 | $PV = \dfrac{T_{required}}{T_{base}}$ | Preset value calculation (integer rounding) | **counts** |
| 4 | $N_{max} = \dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{base}}$ | Maximum countable events | **counts** |
| 5 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times T_{cycle}}$ | Maximum input frequency the PLC can reliably count (Nyquist rule) | **Hz** |
| 6 | $T_{response(min)} = T_{cycle}$ | Minimum detectable input change | **ms** |
| 7 | $T_{response(max)} = 2 \times T_{cycle}$ | Maximum input-to-output response time | **ms** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation 5 (Nyquist Rule) is a KTU 2024 favorite!** If a PLC has a cycle time of 10 ms, it can reliably count at most **f_max = 1 / (2 × 0.01) = 50 Hz**. Students commonly lose marks by forgetting the factor of 2.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Ladder logic sequences robotic welders, conveyor belts, and safety interlocks.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Pumps, valves, and chemical dosing are coordinated using TON/TOF timers.
- **Packaging Industry:** Counters track bottles, lanes, and rejection rates.
- **Elevator Control:** Latching circuits hold the floor request; counters track position.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Start-Stop Latch with Seal-in (KTU Classic)

**Problem Statement:** A motor (Output `O:2/0`) must start when a normally-open Start Pushbutton (`I:1/0`) is pressed and continue running even after the button is released. The motor must stop when a normally-closed Stop Pushbutton (`I:1/1`) is pressed. Implement the program and derive the Boolean logic equation.

#### Step 1 — Identify the I/O

- **Input 1:** Start PB (NO) → `I:1/0`
- **Input 2:** Stop PB (NC) → `I:1/1`
- **Output:** Motor Contactor → `O:2/0`

#### Step 2 — Develop the Ladder Logic

```
  Rung 1:
         I:1/0         O:2/0          I:1/1           O:2/0
         (NO) ─┐  ┌── (NO seal) ──── (NC) ──────── ( ) MOTOR
                │  │
                └──┘
       └─────[ I:1/0 ]──[/ I:1/1 ]──( O:2/0 )────┐
                                                  │
       └─────[ I:1/0 ]──────[/ I:1/1 ]──[ O:2/0 ]─┘   (Seal-in)
```

#### Step 3 — Derive the Boolean Equation (Exhaustive)

We treat the rung as a series-parallel Boolean network.

**Rung 1, Branch A (Direct Path):**
$$B_A = I_{1/0} \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}}$$

**Rung 1, Branch B (Seal-in Path through the output's own contact):**
$$B_B = I_{1/0} \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}} \cdot O_{2/0}$$

**Total Rung Logic (parallel OR of both branches):**
$$L_{total} = B_A + B_B = (I_{1/0} \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}}) + (I_{1/0} \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}} \cdot O_{2/0})$$

**Output Assignment:**
$$O_{2/0}(t+1) = L_{total}$$

#### Step 4 — Factor Using Boolean Algebra

Using the identity $(A + AB) = A$:
$$L_{total} = I_{1/0} \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}} \cdot (1 + O_{2/0}) = I_{1/0} \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}}$$

Wait — this would imply no latching. This is because the seal-in branch's `I:1/0` must be momentarily TRUE to set the latch. The proper latching formulation uses an OTL/OTU pair OR a feedback that bypasses the start contact:

**Corrected Latching Equation (with feedback bypassing Start PB):**
$$O_{2/0}(t+1) = (I_{1/0} + O_{2/0}) \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}}$$

#### Step 5 — Truth Table Verification

| $I_{1/0}$ (Start) | $I_{1/1}$ (Stop) | $O_{2/0}$ (Prev) | $O_{2/0}$ (Next) | Comment |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| 0 | 1 | 0 | **0** | Idle, motor OFF |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | **1** | Start pressed → motor ON |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | **1** | Start released, seal-in holds motor |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | **0** | Stop pressed → motor OFF |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | **0** | Stop active, start is blocked |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | **0** | Stop wins (NC priority) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Strategy:** Always draw the **Truth Table** after deriving the Boolean equation. KTU examiners award **2 marks** for a clean truth table in a 14-mark question.

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Timer Preset Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A conveyor must run for exactly **45 seconds** after a photoelectric sensor detects a box. The PLC uses a TON timer with a **100 ms time base**. Calculate the Preset Value (PV) required.

#### Step 1 — Identify Knowns

- Required duration: $T_{required} = 45$ s
- Time base: $T_{base} = 100$ ms $= 0.1$ s

#### Step 2 — Apply the Preset Formula

$$PV = \frac{T_{required}}{T_{base}}$$

$$PV = \frac{45 \text{ s}}{0.1 \text{ s}}$$

$$PV = 450 \text{ counts}$$

#### Step 3 — Verify by Inverting

$$T_{accumulated} = PV \times T_{base} = 450 \times 0.1 \text{ s} = 45 \text{ s} \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Maximum Counter Frequency (Nyquist Application)

**Problem Statement:** A PLC has a program scan time of **8 ms**. A high-speed shaft encoder produces **40 pulses per second**. Determine whether the PLC can count the pulses reliably using a standard CTU counter.

#### Step 1 — Compute Maximum Reliable Frequency

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times T_{cycle}}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.008 \text{ s}}$$

$$f_{max} = 62.5 \text{ Hz}$$

#### Step 2 — Compare with Input Frequency

$$f_{input} = 40 \text{ Hz} < f_{max} = 62.5 \text{ Hz}$$

#### Step 3 — Conclusion

Since $f_{input} < f_{max}$, the **standard CTU counter is sufficient**. A High-Speed Counter (HSC) module is **not** required for this application.

> [!NOTE]
> If the encoder produced **>62.5 Hz**, the PLC would **miss pulses** intermittently. This is a common cause of industrial counting errors and is a **guaranteed KTU 2024 question topic**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — All Simple Programs Categorized

| Program Type | Core Instructions | Typical Application | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Start-Stop Latch** | XIC, XIO, OTE (with feedback) | Motor control, pump control | ★★★★★ |
| **Two-Hand Anti-Tie-Down** | Two NC + Two NO contacts in series | Press safety, punch press | ★★★★ |
| **Delayed Start (TON)** | XIC → TON → OTE | Sequential machine startup | ★★★★★ |
| **Delayed Stop (TOF)** | XIC → TOF → OTE | Fan cool-down, lamp dimming | ★★★★ |
| **Flashing Light (Oscillator)** | Timer + own contact feedback | Indicator, alarm beacon | ★★★★★ |
| **Up-Counter Batching** | CTU with RES coil | Bottle counting, batch control | ★★★★ |
| **Sequential Traffic Light** | Multiple TONs cascaded | Intersection control | ★★★ |
| **Forward/Reverse Motor** | Interlocks + contactors | Crane, lathe spindle | ★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Anatomy of a Single Ladder Rung (Mermaid Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Left Rail L1 - Logic 1] --> A[Input Contact 1 XIC]
    A --> B[Input Contact 2 XIO]
    B --> C{Logic Continuity Check}
    C -->|TRUE| D[Output Coil OTE]
    C -->|FALSE| E[Coil remains OFF]
    D --> F[Right Rail L2 - Logic 0]
    E --> F
    style L1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style F fill:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    style D fill:#00C853,color:#FFF
    style E fill:#D50000,color:#FFF
    style C fill:#2196F3,color:#FFF
```

### 4.2 PLC Cyclic Scan Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Power ON]) --> ReadInputs[Phase 1 - Input Scan\nRead all physical I/O into Process Image]
    ReadInputs --> ProgramScan[Phase 2 - Program Scan\nExecute ladder top to bottom left to right]
    ProgramScan --> OutputUpdate[Phase 3 - Output Update\nWrite results to physical output modules]
    OutputUpdate --> Housekeeping[Phase 4 - Housekeeping\nCommunication diagnostics self-test]
    Housekeeping --> ReadInputs
    ProgramScan -.->|Watchdog Fault| Fault[Fault Routine\nStop CPU Enter Safe State]
    Fault --> Stop([Halt All Outputs])
    style Start fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFF
    style Stop fill:#F44336,color:#FFF
    style ReadInputs fill:#2196F3,color:#FFF
    style ProgramScan fill:#FF9800,color:#FFF
    style OutputUpdate fill:#9C27B0,color:#FFF
    style Housekeeping fill:#607D8B,color:#FFF
    style Fault fill:#D32F2F,color:#FFF
```

### 4.3 Start-Stop Seal-in Circuit — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    StartPB[Input I10 - Start Pushbutton NO] --> AND1
    StopPB[Input I11 - Stop Pushbutton NC] --> NOT1[Inverter NC becomes active high]
    NOT1 --> AND1
    AND1{AND Gate} -->|Logic 1| CoilOut
    SealPath[Feedback from Output O20 contact] --> AND1
    CoilOut[Output O20 - Motor Contactor Energized] --> Motor[Three Phase Motor]
    CoilOut -.-> SealPath
    style StartPB fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFF
    style StopPB fill:#F44336,color:#FFF
    style AND1 fill:#FFC107,color:#000
    style CoilOut fill:#2196F3,color:#FFF
    style Motor fill:#9C27B0,color:#FFF
    style SealPath fill:#FF9800,color:#FFF
```

### 4.4 Timer Functional Block (TON)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Enable[Enable Input - XIC] --> TimerT{Timer Block TON}
    Preset[Preset Value PV] --> TimerT
    Base[Time Base 1ms 10ms 100ms] --> TimerT
    TimerT -->|Accumulating| TT[TT Bit - Timer Timing]
    TimerT -->|Complete| DN[DN Bit - Timer Done]
    TT --> OutputCoil1[Output 1 e.g. Pilot Light]
    DN --> OutputCoil2[Output 2 e.g. Process Action]
    style Enable fill:#00BCD4,color:#FFF
    style Preset fill:#FFEB3B,color:#000
    style Base fill:#FFEB3B,color:#000
    style TimerT fill:#3F51B5,color:#FFF
    style TT fill:#FF9800,color:#FFF
    style DN fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFF
```

### 4.5 Counter Functional Block (CTU)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PulseInput[Count Pulse Input - Rising Edge] --> CTUBlock{CTU Counter Block}
    ResetInput[Reset Input RES] --> CTUBlock
    PresetVal[Preset Value PV] --> CTUBlock
    CTUBlock -->|Accumulator up| ACC[Accumulator Value ACC]
    CTUBlock -->|ACC >= PV| DN[DN Bit Done]
    CTUBlock -->|ACC overflow| OV[OV Bit Overflow 16/32 bit wrap]
    ACC --> Display[Optional Numeric Display]
    DN --> Action[Final Count Action e.g. Stop Conveyor]
    style PulseInput fill:#03A9F4,color:#FFF
    style ResetInput fill:#F44336,color:#FFF
    style PresetVal fill:#FFEB3B,color:#000
    style CTUBlock fill:#673AB7,color:#FFF
    style ACC fill:#009688,color:#FFF
    style DN fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFF
    style OV fill:#FF5722,color:#FFF
    style Action fill:#1A237E,color:#FFF
```

### 4.6 Oscillator / Flashing Light Pattern (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Master[Master Start PB] --> Run{Internal Bit B3_0 Run Flag}
    Run --> TON1{TON 1.0s Preset}
    TON1 -->|TT timing| Light1[Output Indicator Light]
    TON1 -->|DN done| ResetTimer[Reset TON via own contact]
    ResetTimer --> TON1
    TON1 -.->|Oscillation| Run
    style Master fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFF
    style Run fill:#FF9800,color:#FFF
    style TON1 fill:#3F51B5,color:#FFF
    style Light1 fill:#FFC107,color:#000
    style ResetTimer fill:#9E9E9E,color:#FFF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the term "rung" in ladder logic programming. How does a rung differ from a network in IEC 61131-3 terminology?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **rung** is a single horizontal line of logic in a ladder diagram, bounded by the two vertical power rails (L1 and L2). It typically represents one output decision and may contain multiple input contacts arranged in series (AND logic) or parallel (OR logic).

In **IEC 61131-3**, the term *network* is the formal equivalent of a rung. A *program organization unit (POU)* may contain multiple networks. The distinction is important because:
- **Rung** is the colloquial Allen-Bradley term.
- **Network** is the formal IEC 61131-3 term (used by Siemens, Codesys, Beckhoff).

**(3 marks awarded: 1 mark for rung definition, 1 mark for network reference, 1 mark for the difference.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the difference between an OTE instruction and an OTL/OTU pair. Give one industrial scenario where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | OTE (Output Energize) | OTL / OTU Pair |
|---|---|---|
| **Retention** | Non-retentive — resets to 0 when rung is FALSE | Latched — holds state until OTU |
| **Memory** | Volatile during scan | Latched bit |
| **Equivalent Relay** | Standard relay coil | Holding relay / bistable |

**Scenario 1 (OTE):** A conveyor belt that should run **only** while a sensor is detecting boxes (no memory required).

**Scenario 2 (OTL/OTU):** An **alarm siren** that must keep sounding after the fault condition clears, until an operator manually acknowledges with a reset pushbutton.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Module 2 (Internal Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat ladder diagram and I/O addressing, explain the operation of a **Start-Stop seal-in circuit** for a three-phase induction motor. Include the Boolean equation and a complete truth table. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — I/O Assignment Table (1 mark)**

| Symbol | Address | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Start_PB | I:1/0 | Input, NO | Start pushbutton |
| Stop_PB | I:1/1 | Input, NC | Stop pushbutton |
| Motor | O:2/0 | Output | Motor contactor coil |
| OL_Relay | I:1/2 | Input, NC | Overload relay contact |

**Step 2 — Ladder Diagram (2 marks)**

```
       I:1/0   Motor O:2/0   I:1/1   I:1/2
         │       (Seal)        │        │        O:2/0
         └──────┐ ┌────────────┘        │         │
                │ │                     │     ┌───┘
                └─┘                     │     │
                                        │     │
       └─────[ I:1/0 ]──[ O:2/0 ]──[/I:1/1]──[/I:1/2]──( O:2/0 )──┐
                                                                       │
       └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 3 — Boolean Equation (2 marks)**

$$O_{2/0}(t+1) = (I_{1/0} + O_{2/0}) \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}} \cdot \overline{I_{1/2}}$$

**Step 4 — Truth Table (2 marks)**

| $I_{1/0}$ | $I_{1/1}$ | $I_{1/2}$ | $O_{2/0}$ (Prev) | $O_{2/0}$ (Next) | State |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | Stopped |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Started |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Sealed-in |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Stop pressed |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Overload trip |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Blocked |

---

**Q. (b)** A PLC uses a **TON timer with a 10 ms time base**. The timer must produce a 1 Hz square wave (50% duty cycle) on an output indicator. Design the ladder program and calculate the Preset Value. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recognize the Square Wave Requirement (1 mark)**

A 1 Hz square wave has a **period T = 1 / f = 1 second**, with 50% duty cycle meaning **0.5 s ON** and **0.5 s OFF**.

**Step 2 — Preset Calculation (2 marks)**

$$PV = \frac{T_{required}}{T_{base}} = \frac{0.5 \text{ s}}{0.01 \text{ s}} = 50 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3 — Ladder Diagram (3 marks)**

```
   Rung 1 (Start the oscillator):
   └────[ Master Start I:1/0 ]──────────────[ Internal_Bit B3:0/0 ]──┐
                                                                       │
   Rung 2 (TON timer):                                                │
   └────[ B3:0/0 ]──────────[ TON T4:0, Preset=50, Base=10ms ]──┐     │
                                                               │     │
   Rung 3 (Output):                                            │     │
   └────[ T4:0/DN ]─────────────────────────────────( O:2/0 )─┘     │
                                                                   │
   Rung 4 (Feedback to reset the timer):                          │
   └────[ T4:0/DN ]──────────────────────[ RES T4:0 ]─────────────┘
```

**Step 4 — Operation Explanation (1 mark)**

When Master Start is pressed, B3:0/0 latches. T4:0 starts timing for 500 ms. After 500 ms, DN bit becomes TRUE → O:2/0 turns ON **and** the RES instruction resets T4:0's accumulator. The DN bit goes FALSE → O:2/0 turns OFF. The cycle repeats, producing a 1 Hz flashing indicator.

---

### Question B — Module 2 (Internal Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Design a **bottle-counting system** on a conveyor using a CTU counter. The system must count bottles passing a sensor, energize a "Batch Complete" lamp when 12 bottles are reached, and reset the counter on a manual reset PB. Draw the ladder logic, I/O list, and Boolean equation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — I/O List (1 mark)**

| Address | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|
| I:1/0 | Bottle sensor (photoelectric) | Input, NO |
| I:1/1 | Manual Reset PB | Input, NO |
| O:2/0 | Batch Complete Lamp | Output |
| C5:0 | Counter (CTU) | Internal |

**Step 2 — Ladder Diagram (3 marks)**

```
   Rung 1 (Count Bottles):
   └────[ I:1/0 ]──────────────────────[ CTU C5:0, Preset=12 ]───┐

   Rung 2 (Batch Complete Lamp):
   └────[ C5:0/DN ]────────────────────( O:2/0 Batch Lamp )──────┤
                                                                   │
   Rung 3 (Reset):                                                │
   └────[ I:1/1 ]──────────────────────[ RES C5:0 ]───────────────┘
```

**Step 3 — Boolean Equation (2 marks)**

For the lamp:
$$O_{2/0} = C_{5/0,DN}$$

For the counter increment (rising-edge detected by PLC firmware):
$$ACC_{C5:0}(t+1) = ACC_{C5:0}(t) + 1 \text{ when } I_{1/0} \text{ transitions } 0 \to 1$$

$$C_{5/0,DN} = 1 \text{ when } ACC_{C5:0} \geq 12$$

**Step 4 — Verification (1 mark)**

Each rising edge of I:1/0 (bottle detected) increments the accumulator. When ACC = 12, DN = TRUE → lamp ON. Pressing I:1/1 resets ACC to 0, lamp turns OFF, and the next batch begins.

---

**Q. (b)** A PLC has a **program scan time of 6 ms**. A proximity sensor produces **80 pulses per second** on a single input. Determine:
   (i) The maximum reliable counting frequency.
   (ii) Whether the standard CTU counter can be used.
   (iii) If not, suggest a hardware alternative. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum Reliable Frequency (2 marks)**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.006 \text{ s}}$$

$$f_{max} = 83.33 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Input Frequency (2 marks)**

$$f_{input} = 80 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{input} = 80 \text{ Hz} < f_{max} = 83.33 \text{ Hz}$$

The input frequency is **technically countable** but only with a **1.3% safety margin**, which is unacceptable in industrial practice (noise, jitter, and temperature drift can easily push pulses over 83.33 Hz).

**Step 3 — Practical Conclusion and Recommendation (3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The textbook calculation says "countable," but industry practice requires a **minimum 20% safety margin**. Therefore $f_{max, practical} = 83.33 \times 0.8 = 66.66$ Hz, which is **less than 80 Hz**. The standard CTU is **NOT reliable** for this application.

**Recommendation:** Use a **dedicated High-Speed Counter (HSC) module** (e.g., Allen-Bradley 1756-HSC, Siemens FM 350-1) capable of counting 100 kHz or more. HSCs count pulses in hardware **independently of the PLC scan cycle**, eliminating the Nyquist limitation entirely.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 2 — Ladder Programming):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the seal-in branch** when drawing a start-stop latch — many students draw a direct OTE without the feedback contact, which is logically incorrect. **(–3 marks)**
> 2. **Using a NO contact for Stop PB** — industry convention is NC, and a NO stop button creates an unsafe circuit (motor cannot be stopped if the wire breaks). **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Ignoring the Nyquist factor of 2** in counter frequency problems. Students often write $f_{max} = 1/T_{cycle}$, but the correct formula has a factor of 2. **(–2 marks)**
> 4. **Confusing OTE with OTL/OTU** — writing a latching equation as $O_{2/0} = I_{1/0} \cdot \overline{I_{1/1}}$ (no memory term). **(–2 marks)**
> 5. **Not showing the I/O addressing** on the diagram — KTU 2024 requires I/O labels on each contact and coil. **(–1 mark per missing label)**
> 6. **Mixing up TON and TOF timing diagrams** — a TON delays the ON state, while a TOF delays the OFF state. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. **(–3 marks)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Simple Programs in PLC: Ladder Programming — KTU 2024 Quick Recap:**

- [x] Ladder logic is defined by **IEC 61131-3** and consists of **two vertical rails** and multiple **horizontal rungs**.
- [x] The PLC executes a **cyclic scan**: Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping.
- [x] The **six core bit instructions** are: **XIC, XIO, OTE, OTL, OTU, OSR**.
- [x] A **seal-in (latch) circuit** requires a feedback contact from the output in parallel with the start condition: $O(t+1) = (I_{start} + O_{prev}) \cdot \overline{I_{stop}}$.
- [x] **Three timer types:** **TON** (On-Delay), **TOF** (Off-Delay), **RTO** (Retentive On-Delay).
- [x] Timer preset formula: $PV = T_{required} / T_{base}$.
- [x] Timer elements: **EN** (Enable), **TT** (Timer-Timing), **DN** (Done).
- [x] **Three counter types:** **CTU** (Count-Up), **CTD** (Count-Down), **CTUD** (Up/Down).
- [x] Counter elements: **CU, CD, RES, DN, OV, UN**.
- [x] **Nyquist rule** for counters: $f_{max} = 1 / (2 \times T_{cycle})$. Always include a 20% safety margin in practice.
- [x] An **OSR (One-Shot Rising)** instruction produces a single-scan TRUE pulse on the rising edge of an input.
- [x] Ladder diagrams are read **left to right, top to bottom** within each rung.
- [x] The **Start-Stop Latch**, **TON Delay**, and **CTU Counter** are the three most frequently asked programs in KTU exams.
- [x] **OTL/OTU** is preferred for **safety-critical** latches (e.g., alarms, emergency stops).
- [x] **Always label I/O addresses** on diagrams; KTU deducts marks for unlabeled contacts/coils.
- [x] **Safety convention:** Stop pushbuttons are **NC** (fail-safe), Start pushbuttons are **NO** (fail-safe).
- [x] High-speed applications (encoders, flowmeters >100 Hz) require **dedicated HSC hardware**, not standard CTUs.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a ladder program," always provide **(1) I/O List, (2) Ladder Diagram, (3) Boolean Equation, (4) Truth Table or Timing Diagram**. This four-part structure is the **gold standard** expected by KTU 2024 examiners and guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Math Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Math Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔢 Math Operations in PLC Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC)** and **Distributed Control Systems (DCS)**, **Math Operations** refer to the set of built-in arithmetic and computational instructions executed by the PLC's CPU within the **ladder logic program**, **Function Block Diagram (FBD)**, or **Structured Text (ST)** to manipulate numerical data stored in **data tables (registers/words)**. These operations form the computational backbone of any process automation strategy where analog measurements must be conditioned, scaled, totaled, or converted into engineering units.

Per the **IEC 61131-3 standard**, math instructions are categorized under the *Standardised Function Block* library and include binary arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`), unary arithmetic (`ABS`, `SQRT`, `LN`, `LOG`, `EXP`, `SIN`, `COS`, `TAN`), conversion functions (`*_TO_***`), and numerical comparator blocks (`GT`, `LT`, `EQ`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OERUT722 Module 2):**
> Math operations are a *core competency* required for implementing **analog signal processing**, **PID control loops**, **flow totalization**, and **recipe management** — all of which are examinable in both **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and **End Semester Examinations (ESE)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **factory floor supervisor** standing between a raw material weighing machine and a packaging machine:

- The weighing machine shouts out a number like **"3824"** (raw load cell counts).
- You have to instantly convert it to **"74.50 kg"** (the real weight) using a calculator.
- Then multiply it by **price per kg** to get the bill.
- Then send it to the printer.

The PLC's **Math Operations** are exactly that calculator — but running **thousands of times per second**, in parallel with all the other factory tasks, without ever getting tired or making a math mistake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The PLC cannot "think" with words — it thinks only with **numbers stored in memory locations (registers)**. Math instructions are the only way to make raw sensor counts into meaningful process variables (PVs).

## 1.3 The Three Numerical Domains in a PLC

A PLC does math in **three distinct number formats**, and choosing the wrong one is the #1 cause of process errors:

| Domain | Bits | Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Integer (INT / 16-bit)** | 16 | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ | Discrete counts, small totals |
| **Double Integer (DINT / 32-bit)** | 32 | $-2{,}147{,}483{,}648$ to $+2{,}147{,}483{,}647$ | Large counters, timer accumulators |
| **Floating Point (REAL / 32-bit IEEE 754)** | 32 | $\pm 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$ | Process variables, PID calculations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Dividing two INT values (e.g., `7 / 2 = 3`) performs *integer truncation* — the decimal is silently discarded. Always use **REAL arithmetic** when decimal precision matters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Math Instruction Set

## 2.1 Classification of Math Instructions

Math operations are broadly grouped into the following families:

### 🔹 A. Basic Binary Arithmetic (Two-Operand)
These take **two source operands** and store the result in a **destination register**:
- `ADD` — Addition
- `SUB` — Subtraction
- `MUL` — Multiplication
- `DIV` — Division

### 🔹 B. Basic Unary Arithmetic (One-Operand)
- `ABS` — Absolute value (magnitude)
- `NEG` — Negation (two's complement)
- `SQRT` — Square root (critical for flow from dP)
- `SQR` — Square (for power/variance calcs)
- `LN` / `LOG` — Natural / base-10 logarithm (for pH, dB)
- `EXP` — Exponential ($e^x$)
- `TRUNC` — Truncate to integer

### 🔹 C. Increment / Decrement
- `INC` — Adds 1 to a register
- `DEC` — Subtracts 1 from a register

### 🔹 D. Trigonometric (in advanced PLCs)
- `SIN`, `COS`, `TAN`, `ASN`, `ACS`, `ATN` — used in motion control

### 🔹 E. Scaling & Compute
- `SCL` — **SCL (Scale)** — Linear scaling: $y = mx + c$
- `SCP` — **Scale with Parameters** (Schneider / Siemens)
- `CPT` — **Compute** — Custom polynomial / expression

### 🔹 F. Statistical / Array Operations
- `AVE` — Moving average
- `STD` — Standard deviation
- `MIN`, `MAX` — Boundary detection
- `SUM` — Sum of an array

## 2.2 The Status Flag System (Under the Hood)

After *every* math operation, the PLC updates four **arithmetic status flags** in the **Status (S) register**:

| Flag | Name | Set When | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S:0/0** | `C` (Carry) | Result overflows unsigned range | Magnitude exceeded |
| **S:0/1** | `V` (Overflow) | Result exceeds signed range | Wrong data type chosen |
| **S:0/2** | `Z` (Zero) | Result equals 0 | Useful for conditional logic |
| **S:0/3** | `S` (Sign) | Result is negative | Direction detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Examiners:** Any well-designed PLC program must check the **`V` flag** after division (because dividing by zero is undefined) and after multiplication (because it can overflow).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains every formula you must memorize for KTU Module 2 Math Operations:

| Operation | Mathematical Form | Typical Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Scaling | $E = \left(\dfrac{R - R_{low}}{R_{high} - R_{low}}\right) \times (E_{high} - E_{low}) + E_{low}$ | Raw ADC count $\rightarrow$ Engineering units |
| SCL Block Form | $y = k \cdot x + b$ | Compact scaling block |
| Flow from dP | $Q = K \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Orifice / Venturi flow meters |
| Power Calculation | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | 3-phase motor monitoring |
| Totalizer | $T_n = T_{n-1} + (F_n \cdot \Delta t)$ | Daily flow / energy total |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Fluid flow regime |
| Resistance (PT100) | $R_t = R_0 \left(1 + A t + B t^2\right)$ | RTD linearization |
| PID Output | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Closed-loop control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants (Memorize):**
> - $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$
> - $e \approx 2.71828$
> - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - $\pi \approx 3.14159$

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

1. **Process Control:** Converting a 4–20 mA signal into 0–100 °C for a temperature controller.
2. **Energy Management:** Computing instantaneous power ($V \times I$) and accumulating energy over time ($\int P \, dt$).
3. **Batch Recipe Management:** Multiplying ingredient counts by batch-size scaling factor.
4. **Flow Compensation:** Correcting orifice flow for temperature/pressure ($Q_{corrected} = Q \cdot \sqrt{\rho/\rho_{ref}}$).
5. **Vessel Volume Calculation:** Mapping a non-linear level-to-volume curve in a horizontal cylindrical tank.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Linear Scaling (Analog Input Conversion)

### Problem Statement
A temperature transmitter outputs a **4–20 mA** signal corresponding to **0–200 °C**. The PLC's analog input card converts this to a **raw integer count (A/D value)** ranging from **0 to 32767** (unipolar 16-bit).
Find the temperature when the raw count is $R = 16500$.

### Derivation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the linear relationship.**
Linear mapping from raw counts to engineering units is:
$$
E = mR + c
$$
where:
- $E$ = Engineering value
- $R$ = Raw count
- $m$ = Slope
- $c$ = Offset

**Step 2 — Compute the slope $m$.**
Using the two endpoint conditions:
- $R = 0 \Rightarrow E = 0\,°\text{C}$
- $R = 32767 \Rightarrow E = 200\,°\text{C}$

$$
m = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta R} = \dfrac{200 - 0}{32767 - 0}
$$

$$
m = \dfrac{200}{32767} \approx 0.006104\,°\text{C}/\text{count}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the offset $c$.**
Substituting $R = 0, E = 0$:
$$
c = E - mR = 0 - (0.006104)(0) = 0
$$

So in this case the equation reduces to:
$$
E = 0.006104 \cdot R
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $R = 16500$.**
$$
E = 0.006104 \times 16500
$$

$$
E \approx 100.72\,°\text{C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLC Ladder Implementation:** Place the raw count `N7:0` in `Source A`, the constant `0.006104` (as REAL) in `Source B`, and store the result `F8:0` in `Destination`. Use `MUL` followed by `MOV` to REAL.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Square Root Extraction for Flow from Differential Pressure

### Problem Statement
An orifice plate generates a differential pressure $\Delta P = 16{,}384$ counts (out of 0–32767) corresponding to a 0–250 kPa differential. Use the **Bernoulli-derived** flow equation:
$$
Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P}
$$
where $K = 0.5$ m³/(min · √kPa).
Find $Q$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the real $\Delta P$ in kPa.**
The raw counts map linearly to 0–250 kPa:
$$
\Delta P_{kPa} = \left(\dfrac{16384}{32767}\right) \times 250
$$

$$
\Delta P_{kPa} = 0.5 \times 250 = 125 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the square root.**
$$
Q = 0.5 \times \sqrt{125}
$$

$$
Q = 0.5 \times 11.1803
$$

$$
Q \approx 5.5902 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification using aligned equations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{125} &= \sqrt{5 \cdot 25} = 5\sqrt{5} \\
&= 5 \times 2.2360679 \\
&= 11.18034 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 0.5 \times 11.18034 \\
  &= 5.59017 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Note:** Real-world PLC implementations often apply a **low-flow cut-off** (e.g., $\Delta P < 1\%$ of range $\Rightarrow Q = 0$) to avoid the square root amplifying noise at zero flow.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Compute (CPT) Instruction — Polynomial Expression

### Problem Statement
A strain gauge bridge has a non-linear output expressed as a third-order polynomial:
$$
y = 0.5x^3 - 4x^2 + 10x + 2
$$
where $x$ is the raw count and $y$ is the force in kN. Evaluate $y$ when $x = 7$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Substitute $x = 7$.**
$$
y = 0.5(7)^3 - 4(7)^2 + 10(7) + 2
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the cubic term.**
$$
7^3 = 343
$$

$$
0.5 \times 343 = 171.5
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the quadratic term.**
$$
7^2 = 49
$$

$$
4 \times 49 = 196
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the linear term.**
$$
10 \times 7 = 70
$$

**Step 5 — Combine all terms with the constant.**
$$
y = 171.5 - 196 + 70 + 2
$$

$$
y = 47.5 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an Allen-Bradley PLC, the **CPT instruction** accepts a single arithmetic expression such as:
> `CPT F8:10 = 0.5*F8:0**3 - 4*F8:0**2 + 10*F8:0 + 2`
> The operator `**` denotes exponentiation. The expression is evaluated in **REAL** arithmetic, which is critical to avoid integer truncation errors.

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Totalizer Implementation (Cumulative Sum)

### Problem Statement
A flowmeter produces a flow rate $F$ in m³/h. Implement a daily totalizer that accumulates the flow every **scan cycle** and is reset at midnight.
Derive the discrete-time accumulation equation.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Continuous-time integral form.**
$$
V_{total}(t) = \int_0^t F(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Discretize using the trapezoidal rule (or simple forward Euler).**
For a scan period $\Delta t$ (in hours):
$$
V_n = V_{n-1} + \left(\dfrac{F_n + F_{n-1}}{2}\right) \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification for constant scan rate.**
If $F$ changes slowly compared to scan time, forward Euler is sufficient:
$$
V_n = V_{n-1} + F_n \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numbers.**
Let $F_n = 12.5$ m³/h, $\Delta t = 0.05$ h (3 min scan), $V_{n-1} = 100$ m³.

$$
V_n = 100 + 12.5 \times 0.05
$$

$$
V_n = 100 + 0.625 = 100.625 \text{ m}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Overflow Trap:** If the flowmeter is in L/s and the totalizer is in INT (16-bit), the value will overflow within hours. Always dimension your totalizer as **DINT** or **REAL**.

---

## 3.5 Example 5 — Division with Remainder & Error Handling

### Problem Statement
Divide $A = 25$ by $B = 7$ using integer and floating-point arithmetic. Compare the results.

### Derivation (Integer Division)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{int} &= 25 \text{ DIV } 7 = 3 \\
R_{int} &= 25 \text{ MOD } 7 = 25 - (3 \times 7) = 25 - 21 = 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation (Floating-Point Division)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{real} &= 25.0 / 7.0 \\
         &= 3.571428571\ldots \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **IEC 61131-3 operator equivalents:**
> - Integer division: `DIV`
> - Floating-point division: `/`
> - Modulo (remainder): `MOD`

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Math Instruction Execution Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how the PLC CPU executes a typical math operation and updates status flags:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rung Energized / Enable Bit ON] --> B[Fetch Source A from Data Table]
    B --> C[Fetch Source B from Data Table]
    C --> D{Type Check INT or REAL}
    D --> E[Integer Math Unit]
    D --> F[Floating Point Unit FPU]
    E --> G[Compute Result]
    F --> G[Compute Result]
    G --> H{Overflow Check}
    H --> I[Set V Flag in Status Register]
    H --> J[Set C Flag if Unsigned Overflow]
    G --> K{Result Equals Zero}
    K --> L[Set Z Flag]
    G --> M{MSB of Result is 1}
    M --> N[Set S Negative Flag]
    I --> O[Write Result to Destination Register]
    J --> O
    L --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[End Instruction Move to Next Rung]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Linear Scaling Pipeline (SCL Instruction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAW[Analog Input Card Raw Count 0 to 32767] --> SCL[SCL Block y = kx + b]
    K[Slope k Constant REAL] --> SCL
    B[Offset b Constant REAL] --> SCL
    SCL --> EU[Engineering Unit e.g. Degrees Celsius]
    EU --> PID[PID Controller Input]
    EU --> HMI[Human Machine Interface Display]
    EU --> ALM[Alarm Limit Comparison]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Math Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to add subtract multiply or divide?} -->|Yes| Q2{Both numbers integers?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Need to extract square root?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| SQRT[Use SQRT or SQR Instruction]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need linear scaling y = mx + c?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| SCLB[Use SCL or SCP Block]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Need custom polynomial?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| CPTB[Use CPT Compute Instruction]
    Q5 -->|No| Q6{Need moving average over N samples?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| AVEB[Use AVE Instruction]
    Q6 -->|No| STD[Use ADD SUB MUL DIV Directly]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q7{Result may exceed 32767?}
    Q7 -->|Yes| DINTB[Use DINT 32-bit Arithmetic]
    Q7 -->|No| INTB[Use INT 16-bit Arithmetic]
    Q2 -->|No| REALB[Convert to REAL and Use Floating Point FPU]
```

## 4.4 Modular Architecture — Math in a DCS Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Level1[Level 1 - Field Instruments]
        TT[Temperature Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
        PT[Pressure Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
        FT[Flow Transmitter Pulse Output]
    end

    subgraph Level2[Level 2 - PLC Controllers]
        AI[Analog Input Module]
        DI[Digital Input Pulse Count]
        MATH[Math Operations Block]
        PID[PID Loop Block]
    end

    subgraph Level3[Level 3 - SCADA HMI]
        TREND[Trend Chart]
        ALARM[Alarm Management]
        RECIPE[Recipe Database]
    end

    TT --> AI
    PT --> AI
    FT --> DI
    AI --> MATH
    DI --> MATH
    MATH --> PID
    PID --> AO[Analog Output to Final Control Element]
    MATH --> TREND
    PID --> ALARM
    RECIPE --> MATH
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
Differentiate between **integer** and **floating-point** arithmetic in a PLC. Why is it important to choose the correct data type before performing division?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Integer (INT/DINT) | Floating Point (REAL) |
|---|---|---|
| Bit Width | 16-bit (INT) or 32-bit (DINT) | 32-bit (IEEE 754) |
| Decimal Support | No | Yes (≈ 7 significant digits) |
| Division Behavior | Truncates (e.g., $7/2 = 3$) | Preserves decimals ($7/2 = 3.5$) |
| Speed | Faster (no FPU needed) | Slower (requires FPU or emulation) |
| Range | Limited, fixed | Very wide ($\pm 10^{38}$) |

**Importance of correct data type:** Integer division silently drops the fractional part, causing cumulative errors in process calculations such as level percentage or flow rate. Choosing REAL ensures precision, especially for ratios and averages. **(1 mark for distinction table, 2 marks for division explanation.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any **four arithmetic status flags** updated by the PLC CPU after a math operation and state their significance.

**Model Answer (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks + 1 mark for explanation):**

1. **C — Carry Flag:** Set when an unsigned arithmetic operation produces a result exceeding the register's unsigned range.
2. **V — Overflow Flag:** Set when a signed result exceeds the positive or negative limit.
3. **Z — Zero Flag:** Set when the result equals zero; useful for conditional branching.
4. **S — Sign Flag:** Set when the most significant bit is 1 (i.e., the result is negative in two's-complement representation).

**Significance:** These flags allow the program to detect errors (overflow, divide-by-zero) and implement conditional logic without extra compare instructions, improving scan time and code clarity.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working of the **Square Root (SQRT) instruction** in a PLC with a suitable industrial application. Derive the mathematical relationship between differential pressure and flow for an orifice plate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4–20 mA signal from a level transmitter corresponds to a tank level of 0–5 metres. The PLC's analog input module converts this to a raw integer between 0 and 32767. Currently the raw input is **12500**. Compute the corresponding level in metres using linear scaling. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Working of SQRT Instruction (4 marks):**
The SQRT instruction takes a single REAL source operand, calculates its non-negative square root, and stores the result in the destination. The CPU invokes the **Floating Point Unit (FPU)** internally.

**Industrial Application (1 mark):**
Flow measurement using a differential pressure (dP) transmitter across an orifice plate, where flow is proportional to $\sqrt{\Delta P}$.

**Mathematical Derivation (2 marks):**
From Bernoulli's equation and continuity:
$$
Q = A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}
$$
where $\beta = D_2/D_1$. Combining constants into $K$:
$$
Q = K\sqrt{\Delta P}
$$
This is the industry-standard **"square root extraction"** application of the SQRT block.

**(b) Linear Scaling Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the linear scaling formula.**
$$
E = \dfrac{(R - R_{low})(E_{high} - E_{low})}{(R_{high} - R_{low})} + E_{low}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**
- $R = 12500$, $R_{low} = 0$, $R_{high} = 32767$
- $E_{high} = 5$ m, $E_{low} = 0$ m

$$
E = \dfrac{(12500 - 0)(5 - 0)}{32767 - 0} + 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the slope.**
$$
m = \dfrac{5}{32767} = 0.00015260 \text{ m/count}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**
$$
E = 12500 \times 0.00015260
$$

$$
E = 1.9076 \text{ m}
$$

**Answer:** The tank level is approximately **1.91 metres**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Linear Scaling (SCL)** instruction in a PLC. State the general equation it implements and how it differs from the manual two-instruction `MUL` + `ADD` approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The flow rate through a chemical pipeline is computed from $Q = K\sqrt{\Delta P}$ where $K = 0.85$ m³/min per √kPa and $\Delta P = 144$ kPa. Calculate the flow rate. If a flow totalizer integrates this value for $\Delta t = 10$ minutes, what is the totalized volume? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) SCL Instruction Explanation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The SCL instruction is a single-block function (not a multi-rung construct). Its three inputs are:
- `Source` — the raw input value (e.g., analog input register)
- `Rate` (or `Slope k`) — multiplier
- `Offset` (or `b`) — additive constant

**Equation (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Destination} = (\text{Source} \times k) + b
$$
In expanded form for 4–20 mA live-zero handling:
$$
E = k \cdot (R - R_{zero}) + E_{zero}
$$

**Comparison with Manual Method (2 marks):**

| Aspect | SCL Block | Manual MUL + ADD |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Rungs | 1 | 2 or more |
| Scan Time | Lower (single op) | Higher |
| Readability | High | Lower |
| Live-zero correction | Built-in | Must be added manually |

**(b) Flow Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the square root formula.**
$$
Q = 0.85 \times \sqrt{144}
$$

$$
\sqrt{144} = 12
$$

$$
Q = 0.85 \times 12 = 10.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 2 — Totalized volume over 10 minutes.**
$$
V = Q \times \Delta t = 10.2 \times 10 = 102 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Answer:** Flow rate = **10.2 m³/min**, Total volume in 10 min = **102 m³**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks 🚨**
>
> 1. **Forgetting data type declaration** when using `MUL` or `DIV` — losing 2 marks if the result is an integer but written as REAL (or vice versa).
> 2. **No unit conversion** in the final answer — Examiner expects `°C`, `kPa`, `m³/min` explicitly.
> 3. **Not stating live-zero correction** for 4–20 mA loops (4 mA ≠ 0 engineering units in some transmitters). Always mention the offset.
> 4. **Skipping the slope calculation step** — write $m = \Delta E / \Delta R$ explicitly.
> 5. **Integer vs REAL confusion in `DIV`** — show that `7/2 = 3` (INT) but `7.0/2.0 = 3.5` (REAL).

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 Math operations in PLCs are executed by the **CPU's ALU** (integer) or **FPU** (floating point).
- 🔹 The **IEC 61131-3** standard defines standardized math FB names (`ADD`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `SUB`, `SQRT`, `ABS`, `CPT`, `SCL`).
- 🔹 **Three numerical domains:** INT (16-bit), DINT (32-bit), REAL (IEEE 754 32-bit).
- 🔹 **Linear scaling equation:** $E = mR + c$ — the #1 most-tested formula in this module.
- 🔹 **Flow from dP:** $Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P}$ — the canonical SQRT application.
- 🔹 **Status flags** — C, V, Z, S — must be monitored after every critical math op.
- 🔹 **Integer division truncates** — beware of `7/2 = 3` vs `7.0/2.0 = 3.5`.
- 🔹 **CPT instruction** allows custom polynomial expressions in one rung.
- 🔹 **Totalizer equation (discrete):** $V_n = V_{n-1} + F_n \cdot \Delta t$.
- 🔹 **Always dimension totalizers** for DINT or REAL to prevent overflow.
- 🔹 **SCL block** is preferred over manual `MUL + ADD` for scaling — saves scan time and rungs.
- 🔹 **Industrial applications:** PID inputs, totalization, recipe scaling, flow compensation, energy monitoring.
- 🔹 **KTU favourite questions:** linear scaling derivation, SQRT for flow, SCL block diagram, data type comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequencers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sequencers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), a **Sequencer** is a specialized, table-driven instruction used to automatically generate a predetermined sequence of binary output patterns or to compare a sequence of input patterns against a reference table. The instruction references a user-defined **data file (or sequencer file)** containing a series of 16-bit words, where each word represents the ON/OFF status of a group of outputs (or the expected state of inputs) for a particular **step** in the sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Sequencers fall under the topic *PLC Instruction Set and Programming*. Students are expected to understand the structure of sequencer files, the role of the **mask word**, **source operand**, **destination operand**, the **control file/element**, the **length** parameter, and the **position pointer**.

The three canonical sequencer instructions found in Allen-Bradley (Rockwell) SLC 500 / MicroLogix families, which form the reference standard for most KTU exam questions, are:

| Instruction | Mnemonic | Function |
|-------------|----------|----------|
| Sequencer Output | **SQO** | Steps through a file and writes each word to a destination one step per scan. |
| Sequencer Compare | **SQC** | Steps through a file and compares each word with a source (input) word. |
| Sequencer Load | **SQL** | Steps through a file and loads a source word into the sequencer file at runtime. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A sequencer is essentially a **time-indexed or event-indexed multiplexer** whose selection pointer is incremented automatically (or by a transition rung), thus replacing dozens of latch/unlatch rungs with a single compact instruction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Music Jukebox

Imagine a **vintage jukebox** in a small-town café. The owner has arranged 12 songs in a fixed playlist. Every time a customer inserts a coin, the jukebox advances one position down the list and plays the next song.

- The **playlist of 12 songs** is the *sequencer file* (data table).
- The **"current song number"** indicator on the front panel is the *position pointer*.
- The **12-song maximum** is the *length* parameter.
- The **coin insertion** is the *enable/transition condition* in the ladder rung.
- The **music being played** is the *destination output pattern* (for SQO) or the *comparison result* (for SQC).

Just as the jukebox would be cumbersome to operate with 12 individual push-buttons wired to 12 relays, a sequencer compresses what would otherwise be hundreds of ladder rungs into a single, easily editable, table-driven instruction.

## 1.3 Why Sequencers Exist — The Engineering Motivation

In many industrial processes (e.g., a **bottle-filling line**, a **car-wash tunnel**, a **traffic light controller**, or a **batch mixing tank**), the outputs follow a strict, repeating, deterministic pattern. Writing the equivalent ladder logic using only contacts and coils would require:

$$
N_{\text{rungs}} \;\approx\; \sum_{k=1}^{S} O_k
$$

where $S$ is the number of distinct steps and $O_k$ is the number of outputs that change state at step $k$. Even for modest systems ($S=10$, $O_k=8$), this can exceed **80 individual rungs**. A single SQO instruction accomplishes the same task with **one rung** and a data file of $S$ words, making the program shorter, easier to debug, and trivially modifiable (just edit the data table, not the logic).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Board-Exam Vocabulary)

The following terms appear verbatim in KTU past papers and must be memorized:

- **File** — The contiguous block of data-table words that holds the sequence. Its base address is supplied in the instruction.
- **Mask** — A 16-bit word in which a **1** means *“use this bit”* and a **0** means *“ignore this bit”*. It is applied bitwise to both the source and the file word.
- **Source** — The real-time address whose bits are to be compared (SQC) or loaded (SQL).
- **Destination** — The address to which the current step word is written (SQO only).
- **Control** — A structured 3-word control element (`R6:0`) holding the status byte, length, and current position.
- **Length** — Number of valid steps in the file. Max **255** for SLC 500, max **256** for some platforms.
- **Position** — The current step pointer (0 → Length, then resets).
- **Step** — One row (one word) of the sequencer file.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 14-mark questions, you will often be given a sequencer file and asked to **draw the output pattern for a given step** or **state the word in the file that contains a particular output bit**. Master the bit-to-word mapping shown in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — What Happens When the Sequencer Goes TRUE

When the rung containing a sequencer instruction transitions from **FALSE → TRUE** (or in many platforms, when it is simply TRUE on a given scan), the following sequence of micro-operations occurs inside the PLC scan cycle:

1. **Mask Application:** The current file word (pointed to by `Position`) is ANDed with the Mask word; the Source word is ANDed with the Mask. This isolates only the relevant bits.
2. **Operation Selection:**
   - **SQO:** The masked file word is written bit-for-bit into the Destination address, overwriting the previous contents.
   - **SQC:** The masked file word is compared (bit-by-bit equality) with the masked source word. A non-equal result sets a fault bit in the control element.
   - **SQL:** The masked source word is written into the masked file word at the current position, allowing the file to be updated on-line.
3. **Position Update:** At the **end** of the rung (just before the next scan), the Position pointer is **incremented by 1** (modulo Length). On the very first execution after power-up, Position initializes to the value pre-loaded in the control element.
4. **End-of-File Behaviour:** When the pointer reaches `Length`, it wraps back to **1** (or 0, depending on the convention), and the **done bit** (DN) in the control element is set TRUE for exactly one scan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Position pointer always advances**, even if the masked comparison fails. This guarantees a deterministic scan-by-scan progression regardless of the state of the controlled process.

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Control Element (R6:0)

The control element is a reserved 3-word block (48 bits). It is the only memory the sequencer needs in addition to the data file.

| Word | Bit | Name | Meaning |
|------|-----|------|---------|
| R6:0 | 15 (MSB) | EN | Enable — rung is TRUE. |
| R6:0 | 14 | EU | Enable Unload (used internally). |
| R6:0 | 13 | DN | Done — position has wrapped to start. |
| R6:0 | 12 | EM | Empty (used with SQL to mark unused file entries). |
| R6:0 | 11 | ER | Error — e.g., length = 0, or position out of range. |
| R6:0 | 10…8 | UL, IN, FD | Unload, Inhibit, Found (platform-specific). |
| R6:0 | 7…0 | — | Reserved / status. |
| R6:1 | — | LEN | Length of the file (number of valid steps). |
| R6:2 | — | POS | Current 0-based position pointer. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **verifiable** from any standard PLC textbook (e.g., Petruzella, *Programmable Logic Controllers*, 5th ed., Ch. 8). The vertical bar symbol for absolute value is rendered as `\vert` to protect the markdown table.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit / Range |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Word address in file** | $W_{n} \;=\; W_{\text{base}} \;+\; (n-1)$ | Address of the $n$-th step word, where $n \in [1,\,L]$ | Word index (integer) |
| 2 | **Bit index in file** (flattened) | $b_{\text{flat}}(n,k) \;=\; (n-1)\cdot 16 \;+\; k$ | Flattened (0-based) bit position of bit $k$ in step $n$ | Integer $\ge 0$ |
| 3 | **Masked bit value** | $y_{k} \;=\; f_{k} \;\land\; m_{k}$ | AND of file bit $f_k$ and mask bit $m_k$ | 0 or 1 |
| 4 | **SQC mismatch flag** | $\varepsilon_{k} \;=\; (s_{k}\land m_{k}) \;\oplus\; (f_{k}\land m_{k})$ | XOR of masked source and masked file — TRUE means **fault** | 0 or 1 |
| 5 | **Any-mismatch** | $E \;=\; \bigvee_{k=0}^{15}\varepsilon_{k}$ | If **any** masked bit differs, the FD bit is set | Boolean |
| 6 | **Position wrap condition** | $P_{\text{next}} \;=\; \bigl(P+1\bigr) \bmod L$ | $L$ = length, $P$ = current position (0-based) | $0 \le P_{\text{next}} \le L-1$ |
