| 7 | **Total number of distinct bit-events per cycle** | $N_{e} \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{L} \mathrm{popcount}\bigl(f^{(n)}\oplus f^{(n-1)}\bigr)$ | Sum of bit-changes across all steps (useful for program-complexity analysis) | Integer |
| 8 | **Memory footprint (bytes)** | $M \;=\; 2L \;+\; 6$ | 2 bytes per 16-bit word + 6 bytes for control | Bytes |
| 9 | **Cycle time per full sequence** | $T_{\text{cyc}} \;=\; L \cdot T_{\text{scan}}$ | Total time for one full pass through the file | Seconds |
| 10 | **Done-bit pulse width** | $T_{\text{DN}} \;=\; T_{\text{scan}}$ | DN is TRUE for exactly one scan | Seconds |

**Notation used:** $\land$ = logical AND, $\oplus$ = logical XOR, $\vee$ = logical OR, $\bmod$ = modulo, $\mathrm{popcount}$ = number of set bits, $L$ = file length, $T_{\text{scan}}$ = PLC scan time (typically **5–20 ms** for mid-range PLCs).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Sequencers

| Industry Sector | Typical Application | Sequencer Type |
|------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Automotive | Paint-shop conveyor gate timing | SQO |
| Bottling | Capper, filler, labeler hand-shaking | SQO + SQC |
| Traffic | Pedestrian-crossing phase table | SQO |
| Material handling | Sortation diverter logic | SQO |
| Process safety | Verifying interlock sequence before machine start | SQC |
| Batch (pharma) | Stepwise recipe progression with operator acknowledgement | SQL |
| HVAC | Chiller staging based on load profile | SQO |

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU 14-mark question, you can earn full marks by drawing a **ladder rung** with the sequencer, a **timing diagram** showing the outputs across steps, and a **data-file excerpt** showing at least three steps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Word Address of an Arbitrary Step

**Problem Statement.** A sequencer file begins at base word address `N7:10` and has length $L=8$. Find the address of the word that holds the output pattern for **step 4**.

**Step 1 — Identify the indexing convention.**

Most Allen-Bradley platforms are **1-based** for the user-facing step number but **0-based** internally. The KTU convention is 1-based; therefore step 1 = first word, step $L$ = last word.

**Step 2 — Write the general formula.**

$$
W_{n} \;=\; W_{\text{base}} \;+\; (n - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
W_{4} \;=\; 10 \;+\; (4 - 1) \;=\; 10 + 3 \;=\; 13
$$

**Step 4 — State the answer.**

The 4th step pattern is stored in word **N7:13**. The masked output of this word is written to the destination each time the pointer reaches step 4.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bit-to-Word Mapping (The Most-Asked KTU Variant)

**Problem Statement.** Output bit `O:5/12` must be set ON at step 3 of a sequencer whose file begins at `B3:0`. Find the exact bit that must be set in the data-file word corresponding to step 3. Also find the word address.

**Step 1 — Decompose the output address.**

`O:5/12` means **slot 5, bit 12** of the output image table. The bit-within-word offset is simply $k = 12$.

**Step 2 — Find the word address for step 3.**

$$
W_{3} \;=\; 0 \;+\; (3 - 1) \;=\; 2
$$

So the step-3 pattern is in word **B3:2**.

**Step 3 — Verify by flattened-bit calculation.**

The flattened (0-based) bit position across the entire file is:

$$
b_{\text{flat}}(3,12) \;=\; (3-1)\cdot 16 \;+\; 12 \;=\; 32 + 12 \;=\; 44
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check by reverse mapping.**

Word index = $\lfloor 44 / 16 \rfloor = 2$ ✓
Bit-within-word = $44 \bmod 16 = 12$ ✓

**Conclusion.** Set bit 12 of word **B3:2** to logic 1 (and apply the corresponding mask bit = 1) to actuate output `O:5/12` at step 3.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SQC Fault-Detection Logic

**Problem Statement.** An SQC has mask = `0000 0011 0000 1100` (binary), source = `0000 0101 0000 1010`, and the current file word = `0000 0010 0000 1100`. Determine whether a fault is flagged and identify the offending bit.

**Step 1 — Mask both operands bitwise.**

$$
\text{Source masked} \;=\; 0000\,0101\,0000\,1010 \;\land\; 0000\,0011\,0000\,1100
$$

$$
\text{Source masked} \;=\; 0000\,0001\,0000\,1000
$$

$$
\text{File masked} \;=\; 0000\,0010\,0000\,1100 \;\land\; 0000\,0011\,0000\,1100
$$

$$
\text{File masked} \;=\; 0000\,0010\,0000\,1100
$$

**Step 2 — Compute XOR of the two masked words.**

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; 0000\,0001\,0000\,1000 \;\oplus\; 0000\,0010\,0000\,1100
$$

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; 0000\,0011\,0000\,0100
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

A **1** anywhere in $\varepsilon$ means a mismatch. We have 1s at bit positions **2, 3, and 10** (counting from LSB=0). Therefore, **three mismatches** exist, and the FD (Found / Fault) bit in the control element is set TRUE.

**Step 4 — Identify the offending input-output pairs.**

A maintenance engineer would cross-check the field wiring for the three flagged bits, because the controller expects one pattern (file word) but is seeing another (source = real inputs).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Position Pointer Wrap and Cycle Time

**Problem Statement.** A sequencer of length $L=12$ is enabled once per second. The PLC scan time is $T_{\text{scan}} = 8\text{ ms}$. Find the time at which the Done bit goes high on the very first pass.

**Step 1 — Determine the scan number on which the wrap occurs.**

Position updates after every enabled rung scan. The first wrap occurs after $L = 12$ advances:

$$
\text{Scan on which wrap occurs} \;=\; 12
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the elapsed time.**

$$
T_{\text{wrap}} \;=\; 12 \;\times\; 1\,\text{s} \;=\; 12\,\text{s}
$$

(The scan time is **negligible** here because the enable event is 1 s, much larger than the 8 ms scan. The Done bit is high for exactly **one scan = 8 ms** at $t = 12\text{ s}$.)

**Step 3 — Alternative case — fast sequencer.**

If the sequencer were enabled **every scan** instead, then:

$$
T_{\text{cyc}} \;=\; L \cdot T_{\text{scan}} \;=\; 12 \times 8\,\text{ms} \;=\; 96\,\text{ms}
$$

The Done pulse would have width $T_{\text{DN}} = 8$ ms and would repeat every **96 ms**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Memory-Footprint Planning

**Problem Statement.** A batch process requires 60 steps, each controlling 12 outputs. The control element occupies 6 bytes. How much data-table memory must be reserved?

**Step 1 — Compute file size in bytes.**

$$
M_{\text{file}} \;=\; 2 \cdot L \;=\; 2 \cdot 60 \;=\; 120\;\text{bytes}
$$

(Each 16-bit word = 2 bytes.)

**Step 2 — Add control element.**

$$
M_{\text{total}} \;=\; M_{\text{file}} + 6 \;=\; 120 + 6 \;=\; 126\;\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check using general formula.**

$$
M \;=\; 2L + 6 \;=\; 2(60) + 6 \;=\; 126 \;\checkmark
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of the Three Sequencer Types

| Property | **SQO** | **SQC** | **SQL** |
|----------|---------|---------|---------|
| Reads from file? | Yes (current step) | Yes (current step) | Yes (current step) |
| Writes to file? | No | No | Yes (writes source into file) |
| Writes to destination? | Yes (masked) | No | No |
| Compares source ↔ file? | No | Yes (masked XOR) | No |
| Typical use | Stepwise output control | Input verification | On-line recipe update |
| Done bit meaning | End of file reached | End of file reached | End of file reached |
| Fault bit | None (FD unused) | FD set on mismatch | EM set if file is empty |
| Forward & reverse? | Forward only (typical) | Forward only | Forward only |
| Requires mask? | Yes | Yes | Yes |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Statement.** A sequencer file is loaded as shown below. The base address is `N7:0`, length $L=5$, and the destination is `O:6`. Mask = `0000 0000 1111 1111` (lower 8 bits active). Find the destination word value at step 3 and state which of `O:6/0` … `O:6/7` are ON.

| Step (n) | N7:(n−1) |
|:--:|:--|
| 1 | `0000 0000 0000 1010` |
| 2 | `0000 0000 0000 1100` |
| 3 | `0000 0000 0000 0011` |
| 4 | `0000 0000 0000 1111` |
| 5 | `0000 0000 0000 0000` |

**Step 1 — Identify the step-3 word.**

$$
W_3 \;=\; 0 + (3-1) \;=\; 2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{N7:2} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,0011
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the mask.**

$$
\text{Masked word} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,0011 \;\land\; 0000\,0000\,1111\,1111
$$

$$
\text{Masked word} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,0011
$$

**Step 3 — Write to destination.**

`O:6` is loaded with `0000 0000 0000 0011`, meaning `O:6/0 = 1` and `O:6/1 = 1`; all others OFF.

**Conclusion.** At step 3, outputs **0 and 1** of slot 6 are energized; the remaining 14 output bits are unaffected by the sequencer (the upper byte is masked off).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SQO Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transition Condition Rung TRUE] --> B[Read File Word at POS]
    M[Mask Word M] --> B
    B --> C[Bitwise AND File AND Mask]
    D[Source Operand] --> E[Bitwise AND Source AND Mask]
    M --> E
    C --> F[Operation Selector SQO]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Write Masked Word to Destination O:x]
    F --> H[SQC Branch Compare XOR]
    H --> I{Fault?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Set FD bit in Control R6:0]
    I -- No --> K[Clear FD bit]
    G --> L[Update POS POS plus 1 mod L]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> P{POS equals L?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Set DN bit for one scan]
    P -- No --> R[Continue Next Scan]
```

## 4.2 Data Flow Through the Three Sequencer Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fileBlock[Sequencer Data File B3 N7 etc]
        F1[Step 1 Word]
        F2[Step 2 Word]
        F3[Step 3 Word]
        F4[Step n Word]
    end
    POS[Position Pointer R6:2] --> fileBlock
    fileBlock --> SEL{Pointer Selects Step n}
    SEL --> SQO_out[Masked Step n AND Mask]
    SEL --> SQC_in[Masked Step n AND Mask]
    SRC[Source Real Time Input] --> SQC_in
    SRC --> SQL_in[Masked Source AND Mask]
    SQC_in --> XOR1[XOR Compare]
    SQC_in --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> FD[FD Fault Bit]
    SQO_out --> DEST[Destination Output O:x]
    SQL_in --> fileBlock
    DEST --> field[Field Actuators]
    FD --> alarm[Alarm or HMI Fault Indicator]
```

## 4.3 Sequencer Scan-Cycle Timing Diagram (Textual Fallback)

The diagram below shows the relationship between the enable input, the position pointer, the destination word, and the done bit across five scans for a length-$L=3$ sequencer.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant EN as Enable Input
    participant POS as Position Pointer
    participant DEST as Destination O6
    participant DN as Done Bit
    Note over EN,DN: Power up POS equals 0
    EN->>POS: Scan 1 TRUE POS becomes 1
    POS->>DEST: Write Step 1 pattern
    EN->>POS: Scan 2 TRUE POS becomes 2
    POS->>DEST: Write Step 2 pattern
    EN->>POS: Scan 3 TRUE POS becomes 3
    POS->>DEST: Write Step 3 pattern
    EN->>POS: Scan 4 TRUE POS wraps to 1
    POS->>DEST: Write Step 1 pattern
    POS->>DN: Done bit TRUE for one scan
```

## 4.4 Control Element Memory Map (SLC 500 Convention)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R60[R6 sub 0 Status Word] --> B15[Bit 15 EN]
    R60 --> B14[Bit 14 EU]
    R60 --> B13[Bit 13 DN]
    R60 --> B12[Bit 12 EM]
    R60 --> B11[Bit 11 ER]
    R60 --> B10[Bit 10 UL]
    R60 --> B9[Bit 9 IN]
    R60 --> B8[Bit 8 FD]
    R61[R6 sub 1 Length Word LEN] --> Ldesc[Holds file length 1 to 255]
    R62[R6 sub 2 Position Word POS] --> Pdesc[Holds current step pointer]
```

> [!TIP]
> When answering a KTU question that supplies a control-element dump, **always read the words in the order R6:0 → R6:1 → R6:2** (status → length → position). This is the convention expected by examiners.

## 4.5 Car-Wash Tunnel — Application Schematic

The figure below shows a car-wash tunnel controlled by an SQO instruction with 7 steps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Vehicle Detected Limit Switch] --> sqo[SQO Instruction File N7:0 Length 7]
    sqo --> m1[Pre Soak Spray Valve O0]
    sqo --> m2[High Pressure Wash O1]
    sqo --> m3[Underbody Wash O2]
    sqo --> m4[Rinse O3]
    sqo --> m5[Hot Wax O4]
    sqo --> m6[Air Dryer Blower O5]
    sqo --> m7[Exit Signal Lamp O6]
    sqo --> dn[Done Bit Conveyor Advance]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a PLC sequencer. List the three commonly used sequencer instructions and state one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
A **PLC sequencer** is a table-driven instruction that automatically indexes through a file of 16-bit words to produce or verify a predetermined sequence of bit patterns. The three standard sequencer instructions are:

- **SQO (Sequencer Output):** Drives outputs step-by-step. *Application:* conveyor paint-shop sequence.
- **SQC (Sequencer Compare):** Compares live inputs with stored references. *Application:* safety interlock verification before machine start.
- **SQL (Sequencer Load):** Writes live inputs into the file at runtime. *Application:* on-line recipe update in a batch process.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the purpose of the MASK word in a sequencer instruction with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
The **mask word** selects which bits of the file word and the source word are **active** during a sequencer step. A **1** in the mask enables the corresponding bit; a **0** disables (ignores) it. The mask prevents unwanted bits from being written to the destination (SQO) or from causing false faults (SQC). *Example:* If only the lower 8 bits of `O:6` must be controlled, the mask is `0000 0000 1111 1111`, isolating the upper byte from sequencer action.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture and operation** of a sequencer output (SQO) instruction. Discuss the role of the **file, mask, source, destination, control element, length, and position pointer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sequencer file begins at `N7:10` and contains 6 steps whose patterns are:

| Step | Word (binary) |
|:--:|:--|
| 1 | `0000 0000 0000 0011` |
| 2 | `0000 0000 0000 1100` |
| 3 | `0000 0000 0011 0000` |
| 4 | `0000 0000 1100 0000` |
| 5 | `0000 0000 0000 1111` |
| 6 | `0000 0000 0000 0000` |

Mask = `0000 0000 1111 1111`, destination = `O:6`. For **step 3**, determine:

1. The word address in the data file.
2. The value written to `O:6` after masking.
3. Which outputs (`O:6/0` … `O:6/15`) are ON.
4. The flattened bit position of `O:6/4` across the whole file. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Internal Architecture and Operation of SQO**

A **Sequencer Output (SQO)** is a single-rung ladder instruction whose block diagram contains the following functional blocks:

1. **File pointer** selects the 16-bit word at `Position` in the user-defined data file.
2. **Mask register** holds a 16-bit mask; only bits where the mask = 1 are propagated forward.
3. **Bitwise AND unit** computes `(File_word) AND (Mask)` → masked step value.
4. **Destination register** receives the masked value and writes it to the addressed output module.
5. **Position counter** (`R6:2`) increments by 1 modulo `Length` at the end of the rung.
6. **Control element** (`R6:0`) updates the status bits (EN, DN, ER) and exposes the length (`R6:1`) and current position (`R6:2`).

On each scan where the rung is TRUE, the SQO writes the current step pattern (masked) to the destination, then advances the pointer. The Done bit (`DN`, `R6:0/13`) is set for one scan each time the pointer wraps from `Length` back to 1.

*Real-world utility:* in a bottling plant, the SQO replaces dozens of latch/unlatch rungs, reducing scan time and making recipe changes a matter of editing a data table rather than rewriting logic.

**(b) — Numerical Solution**

**1. Word address of step 3:**

$$
W_3 \;=\; 10 + (3-1) \;=\; \text{N7:12}
$$

**2. Value written to O:6:**

$$
\text{File word at step 3} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0011\,0000
$$

$$
\text{Mask} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,1111\,1111
$$

$$
\text{Masked word} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0011\,0000 \;\land\; 0000\,0000\,1111\,1111 \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0011\,0000
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\; \text{O:6} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0011\,0000
$$

**3. Outputs that are ON:**

Reading from LSB (bit 0) upward, the 1s occur at **bit 4** and **bit 5**. Therefore:

$$
\text{O:6/4} = 1, \quad \text{O:6/5} = 1, \quad \text{all others} = 0
$$

**4. Flattened bit position of O:6/4:**

$$
b_{\text{flat}}(3,4) \;=\; (3-1)\cdot 16 \;+\; 4 \;=\; 32 + 4 \;=\; 36
$$

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **SQO, SQC, and SQL** instructions. Construct a **ladder-rung** (textual description is acceptable) that uses an SQO to drive 8 conveyor outputs (`O:2/0` … `O:2/7`) through 4 steps, with the sequencer enabled by input `I:1/0`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SQC has mask = `0000 0000 0000 1111`, source = `I:0` whose value is `0000 0000 0000 1010`, and current file word = `0000 0000 0000 1100`. **(i)** Compute the FD (Fault) bit. **(ii)** Identify the specific input bit causing the fault. **(iii)** Suggest two corrective actions a maintenance engineer should take. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Differentiation and Ladder Rung**

**Differentiation Table:**

| Aspect | SQO | SQC | SQL |
|--------|-----|-----|-----|
| Direction of data | File → Destination | Source ↔ File (compare) | Source → File |
| Fault detection | No | Yes (FD bit) | No |
| Use case | Stepwise output | Input verification | Recipe update |

**Ladder Rung (textual):**

```
       I:1/0       SQO
   ---| |------[ SQO  File: N7:0  Mask: 00FFh  Dest: O:2  Control: R6:0  Length: 4 ]---
```

On each FALSE→TRUE transition of `I:1/0`, the SQO writes the current masked step word to `O:2`. Because the mask `00FFh` = `0000 0000 1111 1111` limits activity to the lower 8 bits, outputs `O:2/0` … `O:2/7` are driven. The control element `R6:0` stores length and position; the file holds the 4 step patterns.

**(b) — SQC Fault Computation**

**Step 1 — Mask both operands.**

$$
\text{Source masked} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,1010 \;\land\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,1111 \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,1010
$$

$$
\text{File masked} \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,1100 \;\land\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,1111 \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,1100
$$

**Step 2 — Compute XOR (mismatch map).**

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,1010 \;\oplus\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,1100 \;=\; 0000\,0000\,0000\,0110
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

$\varepsilon$ has 1s at **bit 1** and **bit 2**. Therefore:

(i) **FD = TRUE** (fault is latched in `R6:0/8`).
(ii) **Inputs `I:0/1` and `I:0/2` are the offending bits** (the controller expected both ON, but only `I:0/1` is actually ON).
(iii) **Corrective actions:**
1. Physically inspect the field sensor wired to `I:0/2` (e.g., a photo-eye or proximity switch) for misalignment, broken wiring, or contamination.
2. Verify that the sequencer file word is correct for the current step (perhaps a logic revision inadvertently stored the wrong pattern); if so, edit the file or update the HMI recipe.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing.** Step 1 is the **first** word, not the **second**. The formula is $W_{base} + (n-1)$, **not** $W_{base} + n$.
> 2. **Forgetting the mask.** Many students write the entire 16-bit file word to the destination and lose 2–3 marks. **Always apply the mask first**, then write.
> 3. **Mixing up the control element structure.** Examiners explicitly test `R6:0` (status), `R6:1` (length), `R6:2` (position). Drawing them in the wrong order is a -1 mark penalty.
> 4. **Not drawing a timing diagram.** In 14-mark questions, a 3-row timing diagram (enable / position / output) earns the full 7 marks for part (a) even if the prose is weak.
> 5. **Bit-numbering confusion.** In SLC convention, **bit 0 is the LSB** (right-most). Reading bits left-to-right as 15…0 is the most common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

- [x] A **sequencer** replaces dozens of latch/unlatch rungs with a single, table-driven instruction.
- [x] The three canonical instructions are **SQO, SQC, SQL** — output, compare, load.
- [x] A sequencer operates on a **file of 16-bit words**; each word = one step.
- [x] The **mask word** is bitwise-ANDed with both source and file word; mask 1 = active, mask 0 = ignore.
- [x] The **control element** has 3 words: status (`R6:0`), length (`R6:1`), position (`R6:2`).
- [x] The **position pointer** advances by 1 every time the rung is TRUE, then **wraps to 1** at the end of the file.
- [x] The **Done (DN) bit** is high for exactly **one scan** at the wrap-around.
- [x] **SQC sets the FD bit** when XOR of masked operands has any 1.
- [x] **SQL writes the source into the file**, enabling on-line recipe modification.
- [x] **Word address of step n** = $W_{base} + (n - 1)$.
- [x] **Flattened bit position** = $(n - 1) \cdot 16 + k$.
- [x] Maximum file length $L$ is typically **255** (SLC 500) or **256** (ControlLogix).
- [x] Masking is **mandatory** — never write the unmasked file word to the destination.
- [x] The sequencer is **always-forward** in standard implementations; for reverse, use two sequencers or platform-specific features.
- [x] Use sequencers when outputs follow a **repeating, deterministic pattern** with > 4 steps.
- [x] Combine with **timers** to make each step last a fixed duration; with **counters** to advance per event.
- [x] For a $T_{\text{cyc}}$ full cycle in fast mode: $T_{\text{cyc}} = L \cdot T_{\text{scan}}$.
- [x] Memory footprint: $M = 2L + 6$ bytes (file + control).
- [x] In a 14-mark answer, always include: (i) a ladder rung, (ii) a data-table excerpt, (iii) a timing diagram, (iv) numerical computation with units.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy.** When you see "sequencer" in a KTU question, immediately:
> 1. Identify the **base address** and **length**.
> 2. Compute the **step-n word address** using $W_{base} + (n-1)$.
> 3. **Apply the mask** bitwise.
> 4. Map the result onto the **destination** (SQO) or compute the **XOR** for fault detection (SQC).
> 5. Draw a **timing diagram** for at least 3 scans.
>
> Follow these five steps and you will secure **full marks** on any sequencer problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program Control Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Program Control Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Program Control Instructions** in a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) are a class of advanced ladder logic instructions used to alter the **sequential execution flow** of a program. Unlike standard relay-type instructions (XIC, XOE, OTE) that perform Boolean logic, program control instructions direct the PLC's CPU on *which* rung to execute, *whether* to execute a block of logic, or *when* to jump to alternate program sections.

According to the **Allen-Bradley / RSLogix 5000 (IEC 61131-3 aligned)** classification, these instructions fall into the category of **Program Control / Flow Control Instructions** and include:

1. **Master Control Reset (MCR)** and **Master Control Set (CSL/MCS)** – Zone-based enable/disable of logic zones.
2. **Jump (JMP)** and **Label (LBL)** – Unconditional or conditional skipping of program areas.
3. **Subroutine (SBR/JSR)** and **Return (RET)** – Modular program decomposition.
4. **Temporary End (TND)** – Conditional program scan termination.
5. **Suspend (SUS)** – Software breakpoint / diagnostic halt.
6. **Immediate Input (IIM)** and **Immediate Output (IOM)** – I/O update override.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Program Control Instructions are essential for designing **structured PLC programs**, enabling modularity, scan-time optimization, and safe zone isolation. They form the foundation for building scalable automation in industrial processes such as bottling plants, batch reactors, and conveyor networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a **recipe book** while cooking. The recipe tells you to follow steps 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 in order. Now:

- A **JMP (Jump)** instruction is like the recipe saying *"If you already pre-heated the oven, skip directly to step 4."* You bypass steps 2 and 3.
- A **MCR (Master Control Reset)** is like a master switch on a factory zone: *"Turn OFF the entire assembly line for Zone 2."* All equipment in that zone halts regardless of their individual logic.
- A **SBR (Subroutine)** is like calling a *helper function* — when the recipe says *"prepare the sauce (see page 45),"* you leave the main flow, execute the sauce steps, and return.
- A **TND (Temporary End)** is like saying *"Stop reading the recipe here for now — the oven will beep, and you'll continue later."*
- A **SUS (Suspend)** is a **debugger breakpoint** — the program pauses so a technician can inspect variables.
- An **Immediate I/O** instruction is like a chef who *tastes the dish right now* (force-update the sensor reading) instead of waiting for the next planned tasting moment (next scan cycle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Program control instructions do **not** alter Boolean logic values directly (with the exception of Immediate I/O). They control the **scan path** of the CPU through the ladder program. Misusing them can cause **unpredictable machine behavior** — for this reason, MCR and zone control are safety-critical in KTU lab evaluations.

## 1.3 Scan-Time Significance

The PLC operates on a **cyclic scan**: *Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping*. A typical scan time ranges from **5 ms to 50 ms** depending on program length.

- Standard **non-retentive** outputs (OTE) are updated **only at the end of the program scan**.
- **Immediate I/O** instructions (IIM/IOM) force an I/O update *within* the program scan, providing **sub-millisecond latency** for time-critical applications.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test whether a student understands the difference between **scan-time execution** and **immediate execution**. Always mention the scan cycle in your answers to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Control Reset (MCR) and Master Control Set (MCS / CSL)

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Foundation

The **Master Control Relay concept** originates from hard-wired relay panels where a master relay (often with **force-guided contacts** per **IEC 60947-1**) could de-energize an entire electrical zone. In PLCs, this is emulated via software.

- **MCR (Master Control Reset):** When its rung is **FALSE**, all non-retentive outputs within its *MCR zone* (between a paired MCR and MCS) are **forced OFF (0)**, regardless of their rung logic.
- **MCS (Master Control Set):** Marks the **end** of an MCR zone. The zone is implicitly enabled (outputs function normally) when the MCR input is TRUE.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction (Common Exam Trap):** MCR does **not** disable retentive outputs (e.g., latched outputs OTL/OTU) or **TON** (Timer On-Delay) accumulators. MCR only affects **non-retentive** OTEs. This is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic Steps

1. The CPU evaluates the MCR rung **before** entering the zone.
2. If the MCR instruction is **TRUE (1)** → all rungs inside the zone execute normally.
3. If the MCR instruction is **FALSE (0)** → every **non-retentive OTE** inside the zone is **reset to OFF** (0), and **OTE** instructions in the zone behave as **OTU (Output Unlatch)**.
4. When the CPU encounters the **MCS** instruction, the zone terminates; logic outside resumes normal behavior.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility

- **Safety Zone Isolation:** In a bottling plant, when an emergency stop is pressed, the MCR zone for the "filling station" is disabled, immediately halting valves and motors in that section without cutting power to the entire plant.
- **Maintenance Mode:** MCR allows a technician to disable a machine section via software while keeping conveyor upstream active.

## 2.2 Jump (JMP) and Label (LBL)

### 2.2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The **Jump** instruction causes the CPU to **skip** all instructions between the JMP and its corresponding **LBL** instruction. This is analogous to a `goto` statement in high-level programming (though discouraged in modern software, it remains essential in ladder logic for scan-time reduction).

- **JMP (Jump):** When its input condition is TRUE, the scan jumps forward (or backward) to the specified LBL.
- **LBL (Label):** A target identifier (0–999) acting as the destination for the JMP.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic

1. CPU executes the JMP rung.
2. If the JMP condition is **TRUE** → CPU skips all rungs until it finds the matching **LBL[n]**.
3. If the JMP condition is **FALSE** → CPU executes the next rung sequentially (no jump).
4. The scan continues normally after the LBL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scan-Time Effect:** Jumping *reduces* scan time because skipped rungs are not evaluated. This is the primary reason for using JMP — to optimize cycle time in large programs.

### 2.2.3 Backward Jump Warning

A **backward jump** (JMP to a LBL located earlier in the program) creates a **program loop**. While this can implement iterative behavior, it risks **scan-time overrun** and **watchdog timer faults** in the PLC CPU. KTU examiners may deduct marks for unbounded loops.

## 2.3 Subroutine (SBR / JSR / RET)

### 2.3.1 Theoretical Foundation

A **subroutine** is a self-contained block of ladder logic that can be **called** from the main program or from another subroutine. This supports the **structured programming paradigm** (top-down design, modularity, reusability).

- **JSR (Jump to Subroutine)** — Allen-Bradley terminology, equivalent to **CAL** (Call) in Siemens S7 or **SBR** in some controllers.
- **SBR (Subroutine File)** — The subroutine body itself.
- **RET (Return)** — Marks the end of the subroutine; control returns to the instruction following the JSR.

### 2.3.2 Operational Logic

1. Main program executes a **JSR SubroutineName** instruction.
2. CPU **suspends** the main program scan.
3. CPU begins executing the subroutine from the first rung.
4. When CPU encounters **RET**, scan returns to the main program at the rung following the JSR.
5. If the subroutine calls another subroutine (**nested subroutines**), a **subroutine stack** stores return addresses. Most PLCs allow **nesting depth of 4 to 8 levels** before generating a stack-overflow fault.

### 2.3.3 Real-World Utility

- **Mathematical Operations:** Complex calculations (PID, scaling) placed in a subroutine, called only when needed.
- **Alarm Handling:** A common subroutine processing all alarms called from the main scan.
- **Recipe Management:** Subroutines for different product variants in a batch process.

## 2.4 Temporary End (TND)

### 2.4.1 Operational Logic

- The **TND** instruction causes the CPU to **terminate the current program scan** immediately, mark the scan as complete, and proceed to the **output scan** and **housekeeping**.
- TND does **not** stop the PLC; it only ends the *current scan*. The next scan begins normally.
- Used as a **debugging tool** to isolate program sections during commissioning.

## 2.5 Suspend (SUS)

### 2.5.1 Operational Logic

- The **SUS** instruction halts the PLC's program scan and **freezes all outputs** at their last state.
- The PLC enters **suspend mode**, indicated by a fault (e.g., **Major Fault** in ControlLogix).
- A programmer or HMI must clear the suspend to resume operation.
- Primarily used as a **software breakpoint** for online debugging.

## 2.6 Immediate Input (IIM) and Immediate Output (IOM)

### 2.6.1 Operational Logic

- **IIM (Immediate Input):** Forces the CPU to **bypass the next scheduled input scan** and read a specific input point *immediately*. The value is used for the **single execution** of the next instruction; the input image table is not updated globally.
- **IOM (Immediate Output):** Forces a specific output to be **physically written to the output module immediately**, not waiting for the end-of-scan update.

### 2.6.2 Real-World Utility

- **High-Speed Interlocks:** Emergency stop circuits that must actuate a contactor within **<1 scan cycle** (~5 ms).
- **Closed-Loop Control:** Reading a sensor and updating a valve output within the same scan to minimize loop latency.
- **Anti-Bounce / Debounce:** Reading an input's true state after a delay within a single scan.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Symbol / Notation** | **Value / Range / Rule** | **Unit / Notes** |
|-------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| PLC Scan Cycle Time | $T_{scan}$ | Typically **5–50 ms** | milliseconds (ms) |
| Total Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | $T_{in}$: input scan, $T_{prog}$: program scan, $T_{out}$: output scan, $T_{house}$: housekeeping | ms |
| Immediate I/O Latency | $T_{imm} \leq T_{scan}$ | Always $\leq 1$ scan cycle | ms |
| Subroutine Nesting Depth | $N_{nest}$ | Typically **4–8 levels** | levels |
| JMP Label Range | $n$ | **0 – 999** (Allen-Bradley) | integer |
| MCR Zone Behavior (MCR = 0) | OTE → OFF | Non-retentive outputs only | Boolean |
| MCR Zone Behavior (MCR = 0) | OTL/OTU → Unchanged | Retentive outputs unaffected | Boolean |
| Program Time Saved by JMP | $\Delta T = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{i}$ | $k$ = rungs skipped, $T_i$ = per-rung time | ms |
| Watchdog Timer | $T_{wd}$ | Typically **100–500 ms** | ms |

> [!NOTE]
> The **scan-time formula** is the most-tested equation in KTU Module 2. Memorize: $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Instruction Behavior Under Different Scan Conditions

The following matrix summarizes how each program control instruction modifies the **scan path** and the **I/O image**:

| **Instruction** | **Trigger Condition** | **Effect on Scan** | **Effect on I/O Image** | **Effect on Retentive Tags** | **Side Effect / Pitfall** |
|-----------------|----------------------|--------------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **MCR** (input = TRUE) | Rung TRUE | Zone executes normally | Normal | Unchanged | None |
| **MCR** (input = FALSE) | Rung FALSE | Zone skipped logically | All non-retentive OTE forced OFF | **Unchanged** | Retentive bits retain state |
| **MCS** | Always | Marks zone end | Normal | Unchanged | Must be paired with MCR |
| **JMP** (cond = TRUE) | Rung TRUE | Skips to LBL | Skipped rungs not evaluated | Unchanged | Backward jump = loop risk |
| **JMP** (cond = FALSE) | Rung FALSE | No jump | All rungs evaluated | Unchanged | None |
| **JSR** | Always (when energized) | Calls subroutine | New rung stack frame pushed | Unchanged | Stack overflow if nested deeply |
| **RET** | Last rung of subroutine | Returns to caller | Pops stack | Unchanged | Missing RET = scan fault |
| **TND** | Always (when reached) | Ends current scan | Output scan still occurs | Unchanged | PLC keeps running |
| **SUS** | Always (when reached) | Halts program scan | Outputs **frozen** | Unchanged | Major fault; manual reset needed |
| **IIM** | Always (when reached) | None | Input image updated for one instruction | Unchanged | Does not affect global image |
| **IOM** | Always (when reached) | None | Output point updated immediately | Unchanged | Bypasses output coalescing |

## 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step: MCR Zone Execution

### Step 1 — Evaluate MCR Rung

The CPU reads the input condition of the MCR instruction. Let $M \in \{0, 1\}$ represent the MCR state.

$$
M = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if MCR rung condition is TRUE} \\ 0 & \text{if MCR rung condition is FALSE} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Enter the MCR Zone

If $M = 1$, the CPU enters the MCR zone and **executes each rung** sequentially. Each OTE instruction follows its normal logic:

$$
\text{OTE}_i^{out} = f_i(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)
$$

where $f_i$ is the Boolean logic function of the $i^{th}$ rung inside the zone.

### Step 3 — Apply MCR Override (if M = 0)

If $M = 0$, the CPU **does not skip** the rungs but **overrides** the OTE outputs. Every non-retentive OTE is forced to 0:

$$
\text{OTE}_i^{out} = 0 \quad \forall i \in \text{zone}, \quad \text{when } M = 0
$$

### Step 4 — MCS Termination

When the CPU reaches the **MCS** instruction, the zone ends. Logic outside the zone resumes with $M$ no longer affecting outputs.

### Step 5 — Scan Completion

The PLC proceeds to the **output scan**, where the (possibly overridden) image table values are written to physical outputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Insight:** The MCR override is logically equivalent to an **AND operation** between the rung logic and the MCR flag. For non-retentive OTEs:
> $$\text{Physical Output} = \text{Rung Logic} \cdot M$$
> This is why $M = 0$ forces the output OFF, but $M = 1$ allows the rung logic to pass through unchanged.

## 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step: JMP Forward and Scan-Time Reduction

### Step 1 — Identify Rungs to Skip

Let the JMP instruction be on rung $r_j$ and the LBL be on rung $r_l$, with $r_l > r_j$ (forward jump).

### Step 2 — Evaluate JMP Condition

Let $J \in \{0, 1\}$ represent the JMP trigger state.

### Step 3 — Compute Skipped Rung Count

The number of rungs skipped is:

$$
k = r_l - r_j - 1
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Time Saved

If each rung takes an average execution time $\bar{T}_{rung}$ (typically **0.1–1 ms** per rung depending on instructions), the time saved is:

$$
\Delta T_{saved} = k \cdot \bar{T}_{rung}
$$

### Step 5 — Total Scan Time With vs. Without JMP

$$
T_{scan}^{JMP} = T_{in} + T_{out} + T_{house} + \sum_{i=1}^{r_j} T_i + \sum_{i=r_l}^{N} T_i
$$

$$
T_{scan}^{noJMP} = T_{in} + T_{out} + T_{house} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i
$$

$$
\therefore \Delta T_{saved} = T_{scan}^{noJMP} - T_{scan}^{JMP} = \sum_{i=r_j+1}^{r_l-1} T_i
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A PLC program has **200 rungs** total. A JMP at rung 30 (when TRUE) jumps to LBL at rung 150, skipping rungs 31–149. Each rung averages **0.5 ms** to execute. Compute the scan time saved.

**Step 1:** Calculate skipped rungs:
$$k = 150 - 30 - 1 = 119 \text{ rungs}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate time saved:
$$\Delta T_{saved} = 119 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 59.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3:** If full scan (200 rungs) would be:
$$T_{scan}^{full} = 10 \text{ ms (I/O)} + 200 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 110 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4:** With JMP active:
$$T_{scan}^{JMP} = 10 \text{ ms} + (30 + 51) \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 10 + 40.5 = 50.5 \text{ ms}$$

$$
\therefore \text{Scan time reduction} = \frac{59.5}{100} \times 100\% = 59.5\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** This type of numerical question appears frequently as a **Part A 3-mark problem**. Always show the formula $k = r_l - r_j - 1$ explicitly.

## 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step: Immediate I/O Within a Scan

### Step 1 — Standard Scan Without Immediate I/O

The input image table is read at the **start of the scan** and held constant. The output image table is written to physical outputs at the **end of the scan**.

Let $t_0$ be the start of scan. Input $X$ is read at $t_0$:
$$X_{image}(t) = X_{phys}(t_0) \quad \forall \, t \in [t_0, t_0 + T_{scan}]$$

### Step 2 — With IIM Instruction at Rung $r_i$

When the CPU reaches the IIM instruction at time $t_i$ within the scan, it performs an **immediate read** of the physical input:

$$X_{image}(t_i^+) = X_{phys}(t_i^-) \quad \text{(freshly read)}$$

For the **next instruction only**, this fresh value is used. The rest of the scan reverts to the old image value.

### Step 3 — With IOM Instruction at Rung $r_j$

When the CPU reaches the IOM instruction at time $t_j$ within the scan, it **writes the current output image value to the physical output module immediately**:

$$Y_{phys}(t_j^+) = Y_{image}(t_j^-)$$

This is critical for **time-critical interlocks** where waiting for the end-of-scan output update is unacceptable.

### Step 4 — Latency Comparison

| **Method** | **Latency from Event to Physical Output** |
|------------|------------------------------------------|
| Standard OTE (no immediate) | Up to **$T_{scan}$** (typically 10–20 ms) |
| Immediate Output (IOM) | **< 1 ms** (instruction execution time) |

$$
\text{Latency Reduction Factor} = \frac{T_{scan}}{T_{IOM}} \approx \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{0.5 \text{ ms}} = 40\times
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: MCR Zone Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["PROGRAM SCAN START"] --> node2["INPUT SCAN: Read Image Table"]
    node2 --> node3["EVALUATE MCR RUNG"]
    node3 --> node4{"MCR Condition"}
    node4 -->|TRUE| node5["Enter MCR Zone"]
    node4 -->|FALSE| node6["MCR Zone Override Mode"]
    node5 --> node7["Execute Zone Rungs Normally"]
    node7 --> node8["Non-Retentive OTE follows Rung Logic"]
    node8 --> node9["Encounter MCS Instruction"]
    node6 --> node10["Force All OTE to OFF"]
    node10 --> node11["Retentive Bits UNCHANGED"]
    node11 --> node9
    node9 --> node12["Continue Main Program"]
    node12 --> node13["OUTPUT SCAN: Write Image to Physical"]
    node13 --> node14["HOUSEKEEPING"]
    node14 --> node15["NEXT SCAN CYCLE"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: JMP-LBL Scan Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["RUNG 1"] --> nodeB["RUNG 2"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["RUNG 3"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["JMP Rung - Condition Check"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Jump Trigger"}
    nodeE -->|TRUE| nodeF["SKIP Rungs 4 to 49"]
    nodeE -->|FALSE| nodeG["Execute Rung 4"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["LBL Target Rung 50"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["RUNG 51"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Continue Normal Scan"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["OUTPUT SCAN"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Subroutine Call Stack Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeMain["MAIN PROGRAM SCAN"] --> nodeJSR["JSR Instruction Encountered"]
    nodeJSR --> nodePush["PUSH Return Address onto Stack"]
    nodePush --> nodeSub["Begin Subroutine Execution"]
    nodeSub --> nodeSubR1["Subroutine Rung 1"]
    nodeSubR1 --> nodeSubR2["Subroutine Rung 2"]
    nodeSubR2 --> nodeSubRN["Subroutine Rung N"]
    nodeSubRN --> nodeRET["RET Instruction"]
    nodeRET --> nodePop["POP Return Address from Stack"]
    nodePop --> nodeResume["Resume Main at Next Rung"]
    nodeResume --> nodeMainEnd["MAIN PROGRAM SCAN CONTINUES"]
    nodeMainEnd --> nodeOut["OUTPUT SCAN"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Immediate I/O Override Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Scan Start"] --> nodeIn["Input Scan Reads X_phys"]
    nodeIn --> nodeProg["Program Scan Begins"]
    nodeProg --> nodeRung1["Rung 1: Standard Logic"]
    nodeRung1 --> nodeRung2["Rung 2: IIM Instruction"]
    nodeRung2 --> nodeReadImm["Read X_phys IMMEDIATELY"]
    nodeReadImm --> nodeUseImm["Use Fresh Value for Next Instruction Only"]
    nodeUseImm --> nodeRung3["Rung 3: Standard Logic"]
    nodeRung3 --> nodeRung4["Rung 4: IOM Instruction"]
    nodeRung4 --> nodeWriteImm["Write Y_image to Y_phys IMMEDIATELY"]
    nodeWriteImm --> nodeRungN["Rung N: Standard Logic"]
    nodeRungN --> nodeOutScan["Output Scan: Write Remaining Image"]
    nodeOutScan --> nodeEnd["End of Scan"]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: PLC Program Control Instruction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAIN["MAIN PROGRAM FILE"]
        direction TB
        stepA["Input Scan Block"] --> stepB["MCR Zone 1"]
        stepB --> stepC["JMP-LBL Block"]
        stepC --> stepD["MCR Zone 2"]
        stepD --> stepE["JSR Subroutine A"]
        stepE --> stepF["TND Debug Boundary"]
    end
    stepE -.calls.-> subgraph SUB["SUBROUTINE A"]
    subgraph SUB
        direction TB
        subA["Subroutine Entry"] --> subB["Logic Body"]
        subB --> subC["RET Return"]
    end
    subC -.returns.-> stepF
    stepF --> stepG["Output Scan Block"]
    stepG --> stepH["Housekeeping"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define Master Control Reset (MCR) instruction in a PLC. State one key difference between MCR and a JUMP (JMP) instruction.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Master Control Reset (MCR)** instruction is a program control instruction that enables or disables an entire **zone** of ladder logic based on the state of a preceding input condition. When the MCR input is **FALSE (0)**, all non-retentive outputs (OTE) within the MCR zone are **forced OFF**, while retentive outputs (OTL/OTU) and timer/counter accumulators remain unchanged. The zone is terminated by a paired **MCS (Master Control Set)** instruction.

**Key Difference from JMP:**

| **Aspect** | **MCR** | **JMP** |
|------------|---------|---------|
| Effect on skipped rungs | **Override** outputs to OFF | **Skip** evaluation entirely |
| Effect on retentive bits | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| I/O behavior | Non-retentive OTE forced to 0 | No effect on outputs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Award 2 marks for the MCR definition; 1 mark for the distinction. Mention **"non-retentive"** explicitly.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: What is the purpose of the Temporary End (TND) instruction? How does it differ from the Suspend (SUS) instruction?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Temporary End (TND)** instruction is a program control instruction that **terminates the current PLC program scan** immediately upon execution. The CPU proceeds to the output scan and housekeeping, but the PLC remains **in RUN mode**, and the next scan cycle begins normally.

The **Suspend (SUS)** instruction, in contrast, **halts the entire PLC** and freezes all outputs at their last state. The PLC enters **suspend mode**, generating a **major fault** that requires manual intervention to clear.

| **Aspect** | **TND** | **SUS** |
|------------|---------|---------|
| PLC continues running? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Outputs behavior | Updated normally | **Frozen** |
| Fault generated? | No | Yes (Major Fault) |
| Use case | Debugging / scan segmentation | Online breakpoint |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the function of the **JMP (Jump)** and **LBL (Label)** instructions in a PLC ladder program. With a neat diagram, describe how a forward jump affects the scan cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A PLC program has 250 rungs. A JMP instruction on rung 40 jumps to LBL on rung 180 when its input condition is TRUE. The average rung execution time is 0.6 ms. Calculate:
- (i) The number of rungs skipped.
- (ii) The time saved per scan.
- (iii) The percentage reduction in program scan time, assuming input + output scan + housekeeping = 8 ms. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) JMP and LBL Instructions (7 marks):**

The **JMP (Jump)** instruction causes the PLC CPU to **bypass** the execution of all rungs between the JMP and its corresponding **LBL (Label)** instruction when the JMP input condition is TRUE. The LBL instruction acts as the **destination marker** for the jump.

**Working Principle:**
1. CPU evaluates the JMP rung condition. Let the condition be $J$.
2. If $J = 1$ (TRUE), the CPU **skips** every rung from $r_{JMP}+1$ to $r_{LBL}-1$.
3. The CPU resumes execution at the rung containing the matching LBL.
4. If $J = 0$ (FALSE), the scan proceeds **sequentially** without skipping.

**Scan Cycle Diagram:**

```
[Rung 1] → [Rung 2] → ... → [Rung 39] → [JMP Rung 40 (cond=TRUE)]
                                              ↓ (jump)
[Rung 180: LBL] → [Rung 181] → ... → [Rung 250] → [Output Scan]
```

**Effect on Scan Cycle:**

The program scan time is **reduced** because skipped rungs are not evaluated. The I/O scan and housekeeping steps are unaffected. The total time saved equals the sum of execution times of the skipped rungs.

**Advantages:**
- Reduces scan time in large programs.
- Implements conditional execution of program blocks.
- Enables efficient cyclical process control.

**Disadvantages:**
- Forward jump can cause output "snap-action" issues if the LBL is reached abruptly.
- Backward jump can create loops that risk **watchdog timer faults**.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Total rungs $N = 250$
- JMP at rung $r_j = 40$
- LBL at rung $r_l = 180$
- Average rung time $\bar{T}_{rung} = 0.6$ ms
- Fixed scan time $T_{fixed} = T_{in} + T_{out} + T_{house} = 8$ ms

**(i) Number of rungs skipped:**

$$
k = r_l - r_j - 1 = 180 - 40 - 1 = 139 \text{ rungs}
$$

**(ii) Time saved per scan:**

$$
\Delta T_{saved} = k \times \bar{T}_{rung} = 139 \times 0.6 \text{ ms} = 83.4 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Percentage reduction in program scan time:**

First, compute the full program scan time (without JMP):
$$
T_{prog}^{full} = N \times \bar{T}_{rung} = 250 \times 0.6 = 150 \text{ ms}
$$

Total full scan time:
$$
T_{scan}^{full} = T_{fixed} + T_{prog}^{full} = 8 + 150 = 158 \text{ ms}
$$

Program scan time with JMP active:
$$
T_{prog}^{JMP} = T_{prog}^{full} - \Delta T_{saved} = 150 - 83.4 = 66.6 \text{ ms}
$$

Total scan time with JMP:
$$
T_{scan}^{JMP} = 8 + 66.6 = 74.6 \text{ ms}
$$

Percentage reduction:
$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{T_{scan}^{full} - T_{scan}^{JMP}}{T_{scan}^{full}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{158 - 74.6}{158} \times 100 = \frac{83.4}{158} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ Reduction} \approx 52.78\%}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a suitable ladder logic example, explain how the **MCR and MCS** instructions are used to create a safety isolation zone in an industrial PLC application. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the **Subroutine (JSR/SBR/RET)** mechanism in PLC programming. Discuss the concept of **nesting** and state the consequences of exceeding the maximum nesting depth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) MCR and MCS Safety Isolation Zone (7 marks):**

**Concept:** In industrial automation, certain machine sections (e.g., a robotic welding cell) must be **isolated** during maintenance or emergency conditions without shutting down the entire plant. The **MCR/MCS** pair provides this functionality in software.

**Ladder Logic Example:**

```
Rung 1:  [Emergency_Stop] ─── ( MCR )─────────────────
                                               (MCR zone starts)

Rung 2:     [Conveyor_Run] ───[ ]──────────────────( OTE Conveyor_Motor )
Rung 3:     [Robot_Arm_OK]  ───[ ]──────────────────( OTE Robot_Enable )
Rung 4:     [Coolant_Pump]  ───[ ]──────────────────( OTE Coolant_Valve )

Rung 5:  [Maintenance_Key] ─── ( MCS )─────────────────
                                               (MCR zone ends)
```

**Explanation:**
- When `Emergency_Stop = TRUE`, the MCR instruction is **activated (zone enabled)**, and all rungs execute normally based on their individual logic.
- When `Emergency_Stop = FALSE` (e.g., during E-stop press), the MCR instruction **disables the zone**: all non-retentive OTEs (Conveyor_Motor, Robot_Enable, Coolant_Valve) are **forced OFF**.
- The MCS instruction on Rung 5 marks the **end** of the MCR zone; logic outside the zone (e.g., upstream conveyor) continues normally.
- The `Maintenance_Key` is included to show the zone-end control signal.

**Industrial Use Cases:**
- Bottling plant: isolate the "filling station" while the "labeling station" continues.
- Power plant: disable a boiler feedwater pump zone during sensor calibration.
- Automotive assembly: disable a painting robot during color changeover.

**Advantage over hard-wired MCR:** Software MCR is **programmable**, **auditable**, and supports **multiple zones** with different safety categories per **ISO 13849-1** PL ratings.

---

**(b) Subroutine Mechanism and Nesting (7 marks):**

**Subroutine Definition:** A **subroutine** is a modular block of ladder logic that can be **invoked** from the main program or from another subroutine using a **JSR (Jump to Subroutine)** instruction. The subroutine executes its logic and returns control to the calling program via a **RET (Return)** instruction.

**Mechanism (Step-by-Step):**

1. The CPU executes the main program scan.
2. Upon encountering a **JSR Subroutine_Name** instruction, the CPU **pushes the return address** (the next rung after the JSR) onto an internal **subroutine stack**.
3. The CPU **transfers control** to the first rung of the subroutine (file SBR:n).
4. The subroutine executes its ladder logic **sequentially**.
5. Upon reaching the **RET** instruction, the CPU **pops the return address** from the stack and resumes the main program at the saved location.

**Concept of Nesting:**

A subroutine can call **another subroutine**, creating a **nested call hierarchy**.

**Example (3-level nesting):**

```
Main Program → JSR SubA
SubA → JSR SubB
SubB → JSR SubC
SubC → RET (returns to SubB)
SubB → RET (returns to SubA)
SubA → RET (returns to Main)
```

**Maximum Nesting Depth:**

Most PLC platforms impose a **stack limit**:
- **Allen-Bradley SLC 500:** 8 levels
- **Allen-Bradley ControlLogix:** Program-dependent (no fixed limit, but stack memory bounded)
- **Siemens S7-300/400:** 8 levels (OB/FB/FC nesting)
- **Schneider Modicon:** 8 levels

**Consequences of Exceeding Nesting Depth:**

1. **Stack Overflow Fault:** The CPU generates a **major fault** (e.g., fault code 0028 in SLC 500).
2. **PLC Halts:** The PLC enters **FAULT mode**; all outputs go to their **safe state** (typically OFF for non-retentive).
3. **Process Shutdown:** The controlled process must be restarted manually after fault acknowledgment.
4. **Data Loss:** Any unsaved process variables are lost.

**Real-World Example:**

A **recipe-batch controller** in a pharmaceutical plant may have:
- Level 1: Main recipe (calls mixing subroutine).
- Level 2: Mixing (calls heating subroutine).
- Level 3: Heating (calls temperature-PID subroutine).
- Level 4: PID (calls ADC-read subroutine).

If the developer adds an unintended 5th-level call, a **stack overflow** may occur during a specific recipe, halting the entire batch — potentially wasting expensive ingredients.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse MCR with JMP** — many students write that JMP "turns OFF" outputs. JMP only **skips evaluation**; outputs retain their last value.
> 2. **Do not forget the return address** when explaining subroutines. The **stack push/pop** mechanism is mandatory for full marks.
> 3. **Do not state that TND stops the PLC.** TND ends only the **current scan**.
> 4. **Always state the MCR affects only non-retentive outputs.** Forgetting this loses 2 marks.
> 5. **In numerical problems, show the full substitution** of values into the formula. Skipping the substitution loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Program Control Instructions**

- ✅ **Program Control Instructions** alter the **scan path** of a PLC program, not the Boolean logic values.
- ✅ **MCR/MCS** form a **paired zone**. MCR input TRUE → zone enabled; FALSE → all **non-retentive OTEs forced OFF**. Retentive outputs and timers are **unaffected**.
- ✅ **JMP/LBL** cause **conditional skipping** of program sections. Forward JMP reduces scan time; backward JMP creates loops.
- ✅ **JSR/SBR/RET** implement **modular subroutines** with a **return-address stack**. Maximum nesting is typically **4–8 levels** depending on the PLC platform.
- ✅ **TND** ends the **current scan** only; PLC remains in RUN mode. Used for debugging and program segmentation.
- ✅ **SUS** halts the **entire PLC**, freezes outputs, and generates a **major fault**. Used as a software breakpoint.
- ✅ **IIM/IOM (Immediate I/O)** bypass the **scan-cycle I/O update** and read/write a specific I/O point **within** the program scan, achieving sub-millisecond latency.
- ✅ **Scan Time Formula:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$
- ✅ **JMP Time Saved:** $\Delta T_{saved} = (r_l - r_j - 1) \times \bar{T}_{rung}$
- ✅ **Number of rungs skipped:** $k = r_l - r_j - 1$
- ✅ The PLC scan cycle is **cyclic**: Input → Program → Output → Housekeeping.
- ✅ **MCR** does **not** clear retentive bits; it only resets non-retentive OTEs.
- ✅ **JMP** does **not** force outputs OFF; outputs retain their last state.
- ✅ **Subroutine nesting** beyond platform limits causes a **major stack-overflow fault**.
- ✅ **Immediate Output (IOM)** is critical for **safety interlocks** where latency must be < 1 scan cycle.
- ✅ **Watchdog timer** faults occur if a single scan exceeds ~100–500 ms; avoid unbounded backward jumps.
- ✅ For **KTU board answers**, always draw a **ladder diagram** OR a **scan-path flowchart** to earn full visualization marks.
- ✅ Use **standard metric units** (ms for time) and **integer rung numbers** when solving numerical problems.
- ✅ Memorize the **distinction table** between MCR, JMP, TND, SUS — this is the **#1 short-answer question** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PTO/PWM Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** PTO/PWM Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) and Distributed Control Systems (DCS), **Pulse Train Output (PTO)** and **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** are two specialized high-speed digital output signaling techniques used to interface PLCs with continuous (analog-like) field devices such as stepper motors, servo drives, DC motor drives, proportional valves, and heating elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PTO (Pulse Train Output):** A digital output signal consisting of a **fixed duty cycle (typically 50%)** train of rectangular pulses with **variable frequency (or equivalently, variable period)**, where the **number of pulses** is precisely controlled. Used primarily for **position and velocity control** of stepper and servo motors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation):** A digital output signal consisting of pulses with a **fixed frequency (or fixed period)** but a **variable duty cycle (variable ON-time / pulse width)**. Used primarily to simulate an **analog output** by varying the average energy delivered to a load.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are controlling the speed of a ceiling fan using only a light switch (ON/OFF — no dimmer).

- **PWM Analogy:** You keep flicking the switch ON and OFF very fast, say 100 times per second. If you keep the switch ON for 70% of each cycle and OFF for 30%, the fan receives an *average* of 70% power and spins fast. If you reduce ON-time to 30%, the fan slows down. You are not changing the voltage — you are *chopping* it. The fan's brain (inertia) cannot react to the rapid flickers, so it perceives a smooth "analog" speed. This is exactly how PWM controls DC motor speed, heater temperature, or LED brightness.

- **PTO Analogy:** Imagine a horse pulling a cart. Each step of the horse is one "pulse." If the horse takes 100 steps per minute and each step covers 1 cm, the cart moves 100 cm/min. If the horse takes 200 steps per minute, the cart moves faster. The number of steps determines **distance** (position), and the rate of steps determines **speed**. This is exactly how PTO drives a stepper motor: a precise number of pulses moves the motor shaft to an exact angular position, and the pulse rate controls the rotation speed.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard fixed duty cycle for PTO:** **50%** (square wave) is the universally accepted default.
> - **Typical PWM frequency range in industrial PLCs:** **100 Hz to 20 kHz** (low frequency for thermal/heating loads, high frequency for motor drives to avoid audible noise).
> - **Typical PTO frequency range for stepper/servo drives:** **10 Hz to 200 kHz** depending on PLC hardware.
> - **Resolution parameter:** Expressed as a percentage or as bits (e.g., **8-bit resolution = 256 discrete duty-cycle steps**, **10-bit = 1024 steps**).
> - **Maximum output voltage swing:** Equal to the PLC's digital output voltage, typically **24 V DC** (industry standard per IEC 61131-2).

### 1.4 Why Two Different Techniques?

| Aspect | PTO | PWM |
|---|---|---|
| Variable Parameter | Frequency / Number of Pulses | Duty Cycle (Pulse Width) |
| Fixed Parameter | Duty Cycle (50%) | Frequency (Period) |
| Primary Use | **Position** control | **Power / Energy** control |
| Typical Load | Stepper / Servo motor | DC motor, heater, valve, LED |
| Information Encoded In | Pulse count = position; Pulse rate = speed | ON-time ratio = average power |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pulse Train Output (PTO) — Operational Breakdown

A PTO channel on a modern PLC (e.g., Siemens S7-1200, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix, Schneider M221) is implemented using a **dedicated hardware pulse generator** that bypasses the normal scan cycle. This is because the PLC scan time (typically 5–20 ms) is far too slow to generate accurate high-frequency pulses.

**Step-by-step logic of PTO operation:**

1. **Profile table setup:** The engineer defines a motion profile in a data block — typically containing parameters such as:
   - **Target frequency** $f_{max}$ (in Hz)
   - **Acceleration/deceleration ramp** (in pulses/sec²)
   - **Total pulse count** $N$ (corresponds to desired angular displacement)
   - **Direction bit** (high or low logic level)
2. **Trigger:** A bit in the process image (e.g., `PLS` instruction in Siemens or `PTO` instruction in AB) is set by the user program.
3. **Pulse generation:** The dedicated hardware oscillator produces square-wave pulses at 50% duty cycle, ramping up frequency according to the profile.
4. **Direction output:** A separate digital output is toggled to control motor direction.
5. **Completion interrupt:** When all $N$ pulses have been emitted, a hardware interrupt fires, allowing the CPU to acknowledge completion and trigger the next motion segment.

**The 'Why' Behind the 50% Duty Cycle:**
Stepper motor drivers and servo drives expect a 50% square wave because the leading edge of every pulse advances the motor by one step. A symmetric waveform ensures that the signal has equal rise and fall characteristics, allowing the driver's internal optocoupler and Schmitt-trigger input stage to respond cleanly to every transition. Asymmetric waveforms (e.g., 20% duty) would cause the receiver to see narrow pulses that may be filtered out by the input's noise-rejection filter.

### 2.2 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) — Operational Breakdown

PWM is fundamentally an **energy modulation** technique. The load always sees the full DC supply voltage when the transistor (or FET/IGBT switch) is ON, and zero voltage when OFF.

**Step-by-step logic of PWM operation:**

1. **Period (or frequency) selection:** The PLC engineer sets a fixed period $T$ (e.g., 1 ms = 1 kHz).
2. **Duty cycle programming:** A value between 0% and 100% is written to a control register.
3. **Hardware timing:** The high-speed output hardware divides each period $T$ into a high-time $T_{on}$ and a low-time $T_{off}$ such that:
$$T_{on} = D \cdot T \quad \text{and} \quad T_{off} = (1 - D) \cdot T$$
where $D$ is the duty cycle expressed as a fraction (0 ≤ D ≤ 1).
4. **Output switching:** The output transistor switches at the period boundary with the programmed on/off ratio.
5. **Load integration:** The mechanical (motor inertia) or thermal (heater mass) system averages the chopped waveform, behaving as if it received a constant voltage $V_{avg}$.

**The 'Why' Behind Frequency Selection:**
- For **thermal loads** (heaters, soldering irons), use **low frequency (< 10 Hz)** so the heater element has time to fully heat and fully cool within each cycle, giving smooth temperature control.
- For **motor loads**, use **high frequency (> 20 kHz)** so the switching is above human hearing (prevents the annoying "singing" noise) and well above the motor's electrical time constant (so current ripple is small).
- A practical rule of thumb: PWM frequency should be at least **10× the bandwidth** of the controlled system.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Period | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | seconds (s) | $T > 0$ |
| Frequency | $f$ | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hertz (Hz) | $f > 0$ |
| Duty Cycle (fraction) | $D$ | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq D \leq 1$ |
| Duty Cycle (percent) | $D\%$ | $D\% = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100$ | percent (%) | $0 \leq D\% \leq 100$ |
| Average Output Voltage | $V_{avg}$ | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{max}$ | Volts (V) | $0 \leq V_{avg} \leq V_{max}$ |
| Average Output Current | $I_{avg}$ | $I_{avg} = D \cdot I_{max}$ | Amperes (A) | $0 \leq I_{avg} \leq I_{max}$ |
| Average Power to Load | $P_{avg}$ | $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{R} \cdot D$ | Watts (W) | $0 \leq P_{avg} \leq P_{max}$ |
| Angular Position (Stepper) | $\theta$ | $\theta = N \cdot \theta_{step}$ | radians (rad) | $N \geq 0$ |
| Linear Position | $x$ | $x = N \cdot s$ | meters (m) | $N \geq 0$ |
| Motor Speed (Stepper) | $n$ | $n = \dfrac{f \cdot \theta_{step}}{2\pi}$ | rev/sec (rps) | $f \leq f_{max}$ |
| PWM Resolution | $R_{bits}$ | $R_{bits} = \log_2(N_{steps})$ | bits | $N_{steps} = 2^{R_{bits}}$ |
| Time Constant (RC load) | $\tau$ | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | seconds (s) | $T \ll \tau$ for smoothing |

**Note on notation:** The vertical bar symbol for absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) has been intentionally written using `\vert` in the table to preserve markdown rendering integrity. In LaTeX, $\vert x \vert$ is the standard representation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PTO Applications:** CNC machine tool axis positioning, pick-and-place robots in SMT lines, packaging machine indexing, conveyor synchronization, label printing registration, syringe pump dosing.
- **PWM Applications:** Variable frequency drives (VFDs) for AC induction motors (after rectification and inversion), DC fan speed control in server rooms, proportional hydraulic/solenoid valves, LED dimming, class-D audio amplifiers, induction heating, switched-mode power supplies (SMPS), and even spacecraft thruster valve control.
- **DCS Context:** In a Distributed Control System, individual PLCs generate local PTO/PWM signals, while supervisory control (SCADA) sets the setpoints over the industrial network (e.g., Profibus, Modbus TCP, EtherCAT).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Voltage Delivered by a PWM Signal

A PWM signal is a periodic rectangular waveform. For a resistive (or resistive-dominated) load, the instantaneous power dissipation at any moment is $p(t) = \dfrac{v(t)^2}{R}$. The energy delivered over one complete period $T$ is the integral of instantaneous power over that period. The *average* power is that energy divided by the period.

Starting from the formal definition of the average (mean) value of a periodic function over one period:

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1:** Express the voltage waveform $v(t)$ mathematically. During the ON interval, $v(t) = V_{max}$; during the OFF interval, $v(t) = 0$.

$$v(t) = \begin{cases} V_{max} & \text{for } 0 \leq t < T_{on} \\ 0 & \text{for } T_{on} \leq t < T \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** Split the integral over the two sub-intervals.

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_{max} \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the first integral (the second is trivially zero).

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T} \left[ V_{max} \cdot T_{on} + 0 \right] = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot T_{on}}{T}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\dfrac{T_{on}}{T} = D$, the duty cycle as a fraction.

$$V_{avg} = V_{max} \cdot D$$

**Step 5:** Rewrite in percentage form for engineering use.

$$V_{avg} = V_{max} \cdot \dfrac{D\%}{100}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic summary:** The mean of a rectangular pulse train equals its peak value multiplied by the fraction of time it spends HIGH. This result is the theoretical foundation of every PWM-based power control application.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Resolution of a PWM Channel in Bits

A PLC's PWM output register holds an integer value that is *mapped* to the duty cycle range 0%–100%. If the register is $n$ bits wide, the number of distinct duty-cycle steps is $2^n$.

**Step 1:** Define the smallest increment in duty cycle, called the *least significant bit (LSB)* of duty cycle.

$$D_{LSB} = \dfrac{100\%}{2^n - 1}$$

**Step 2:** Express the duty cycle corresponding to a register value $k$ (where $0 \leq k \leq 2^n - 1$).

$$D\%(k) = k \cdot D_{LSB} = k \cdot \dfrac{100\%}{2^n - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Worked numerical example for an 8-bit PWM register (typical of many PLCs):

$$D_{LSB} = \dfrac{100\%}{2^8 - 1} = \dfrac{100\%}{255} \approx 0.392\%$$

**Step 4:** If the engineer writes a register value $k = 153$:

$$D\%(153) = 153 \times \dfrac{100}{255} = \dfrac{15300}{255} = 60\%$$

**Step 5:** For a 10-bit register:

$$D_{LSB} = \dfrac{100\%}{1023} \approx 0.0977\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic summary:** Higher bit resolution allows finer control but may not be necessary for slow thermal systems. For servo positioning, **at least 10-bit resolution** is recommended.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Angular Displacement from PTO Pulse Count

A stepper motor advances by a fixed mechanical angle $\theta_{step}$ for each incoming pulse. The relationship between total pulse count $N$ and total angular displacement $\theta$ is linear.

**Step 1:** Write the proportionality statement.

$$\theta = N \cdot \theta_{step}$$

**Step 2:** Convert angular displacement to revolutions:

$$N_{rev} = \dfrac{\theta}{2\pi} = \dfrac{N \cdot \theta_{step}}{2\pi}$$

**Step 3:** A common industrial stepper has 200 steps/revolution (1.8° per step). Verify:

$$N_{rev} = \dfrac{N \cdot 1.8°}{360°} = \dfrac{N}{200}$$

**Step 4:** Worked example — to rotate a 200-step/rev motor through exactly 7.5 revolutions:

$$N = 7.5 \times 200 = 1500 \text{ pulses}$$

**Step 5:** If the motor is coupled to a leadscrew with pitch $p$ (mm/rev), the linear travel is:

$$x = N_{rev} \cdot p = \dfrac{N \cdot \theta_{step} \cdot p}{2\pi}$$

For a 5 mm pitch leadscrew and 1500 pulses:

$$x = 7.5 \times 5 \text{ mm} = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Selection Criteria

| Decision Criterion | Choose PTO | Choose PWM |
|---|---|---|
| Load is stepper or servo motor | ✓ | ✗ |
| Need exact position (open-loop steps) | ✓ | ✗ |
| Need to control motor speed only | ✗ | ✓ |
| Need to simulate 0–10 V analog output | ✗ | ✓ |
| Load is resistive heater | ✗ | ✓ |
| Load is proportional solenoid valve | ✗ | ✓ |
| Need trapezoidal velocity profile | ✓ | ✗ |
| Need to vary LED brightness | ✗ | ✓ |
| Pulse count in the thousands required | ✓ | ✗ |
| Continuous energy modulation required | ✗ | ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a PLC PTO/PWM Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU User Program] --> CTRL[PTO PWM Control Register]
    CTRL --> PROFILE[Motion Profile Table]
    PROFILE --> OSC[High Speed Hardware Oscillator]
    OSC --> SW1[Electronic Switch FET or Transistor]
    SW1 --> LOAD[Field Load Stepper Motor or Heater]
    LOAD --> FBK[Feedback Encoder or Thermistor]
    FBK --> HSC[High Speed Counter Input]
    HSC --> CPU
    CTRL -. Frequency and Pulse Count .-> OSC
    CTRL -. Duty Cycle and Period .-> OSC
    OSC --> DIR[Direction Output Bit]
```

### 4.2 PWM Signal Timing Diagram (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWM25[Period T with 25 percent Duty Cycle]
        P25A[OFF for 0 to 0.75T]
        P25B[ON for 0.75T to T]
    end
    subgraph PWM50[Period T with 50 percent Duty Cycle]
        P50A[OFF for 0 to 0.5T]
        P50B[ON for 0.5T to T]
    end
    subgraph PWM75[Period T with 75 percent Duty Cycle]
        P75A[OFF for 0 to 0.25T]
        P75B[ON for 0.25T to T]
    end
    PWM25 --> AVG25[Average V equals 0.25 Vmax]
    PWM50 --> AVG50[Average V equals 0.50 Vmax]
    PWM75 --> AVG75[Average V equals 0.75 Vmax]
```

### 4.3 PTO Trapezoidal Velocity Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start 0 Hz] --> B[Acceleration Ramp]
    B --> C[Cruise Frequency fmax]
    C --> D[Deceleration Ramp]
    D --> E[Stop 0 Hz]
    A -.- N1[Pulse Count N1]
    B -.- N2[Pulse Count N2]
    C -.- N3[Pulse Count N3]
    D -.- N4[Pulse Count N4]
    E -.- NT[Total N equals N1 plus N2 plus N3 plus N4]
```

### 4.4 PTO vs PWM Internal Choice Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Control Requirement] --> Q1{Position or Speed control of stepper or servo motor}
    Q1 -- YES --> PTO[PTO Pulse Train Output]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Energy or average power modulation}
    Q2 -- YES --> PWM[PWM Pulse Width Modulation]
    Q2 -- NO --> HYBRID[Use analog output module or hybrid drive]
    PTO --> APP1[Axis control and CNC and robotics]
    PWM --> APP2[Fan and heater and LED and proportional valve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The flowcharts above are rendered in pure Mermaid syntax using only alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `node1`, `flowA`) and clean uppercase alphanumeric labels, in strict compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between Pulse Train Output (PTO) and Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) as used in PLC systems. List any two industrial applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU Valuation Key Style):**

| Feature | PTO | PWM |
|---|---|---|
| Variable parameter | Frequency / pulse count | Duty cycle |
| Fixed parameter | 50% duty cycle | Frequency (period) |
| Function | Position / speed control of stepper / servo motors | Average power / analog-like control of resistive / inductive loads |

**PTO Applications (1 mark for any two):** (i) Stepper motor axis control in CNC machines; (ii) Servo motor positioning in pick-and-place robotics; (iii) Conveyor indexing in packaging industry.

**PWM Applications (1 mark for any two):** (i) DC motor speed control in fans and pumps; (ii) Heater temperature control in plastic extrusion; (iii) LED dimming in architectural lighting; (iv) Proportional solenoid valve control in hydraulics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Writing "PTO is used for analog output" — this is **wrong** and costs 1 mark. PTO is for **digital pulse-position signaling**, not analog emulation. Conversely, stating "PWM is for position control" is also incorrect.

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**A PLC generates a PWM signal with period $T = 2$ ms and ON-time $T_{on} = 0.6$ ms to drive a 24 V DC heater. Calculate the duty cycle and the average voltage delivered to the heater.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the duty cycle as a fraction and as a percentage.

$$D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T} = \dfrac{0.6 \text{ ms}}{2 \text{ ms}} = 0.3$$

$$D\% = 0.3 \times 100 = 30\%$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the average-voltage formula.

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{max} = 0.3 \times 24 \text{ V} = 7.2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** State engineering conclusion.

The heater dissipates **30% of its rated power**, so it operates at a low, steady temperature suitable for warm-keeping applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation pitfall:** Students often confuse $T$ and $T_{on}$. If the student wrote $D = 2/0.6 = 333\%$, the examiner will award **0 marks** for Step 1. Always check: $T_{on} \leq T$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Model]

**(a)** Explain with a neat functional block diagram how a PLC generates a **Pulse Train Output (PTO)** to control a stepper motor. Describe the role of the motion profile table, the direction bit, and the high-speed hardware oscillator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stepper motor has **200 steps per revolution** and is coupled to a leadscrew of **pitch 4 mm/rev**. The PLC's PTO channel is configured to emit a total of **4,500 pulses** to move a load from point A to point B. Calculate: (i) the number of complete motor revolutions, (ii) the total angular displacement in degrees, and (iii) the linear distance traveled by the load in mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PTO Generation — Functional Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** Define PTO and its purpose: a 50% duty-cycle pulse train where the *number* of pulses determines motor position and the *frequency* determines speed.

2. **(2 marks)** Describe the motion profile table. The table is a structured data block in PLC memory (e.g., DB in Siemens) containing: target frequency, acceleration/deceleration rates, starting/stopping frequency, and total pulse count. The CPU writes to this table, and the dedicated hardware reads from it asynchronously to the scan cycle.

3. **(2 marks)** Describe the high-speed hardware oscillator. It is an independent hardware timer (often implemented using a DDS — Direct Digital Synthesizer — or a PWM timer linked to a counter). It produces the square wave at the precise frequency demanded by the profile, completely independent of the CPU's scan time. This is critical because the PLC scan (5–20 ms) is far too slow for typical motor frequencies (1 kHz–200 kHz).

4. **(1 mark)** Describe the direction bit. A separate digital output is assigned as the DIR signal. The CPU sets DIR = 1 for clockwise rotation and DIR = 0 for counter-clockwise. The motor driver interprets this and reverses the phase excitation sequence accordingly.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** Steps per revolution = 200, Leadscrew pitch $p = 4$ mm/rev, Total pulses $N = 4500$.

**(i) Number of complete revolutions (2 marks):**

$$N_{rev} = \dfrac{N}{\text{steps per rev}} = \dfrac{4500}{200} = 22.5 \text{ revolutions}$$

**(ii) Total angular displacement in degrees (2 marks):**

$$\theta = N_{rev} \times 360° = 22.5 \times 360° = 8100°$$

Equivalently, per pulse: $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{200} = 1.8°$, so $\theta = 4500 \times 1.8° = 8100°$.

**(iii) Linear distance traveled (3 marks):**

$$x = N_{rev} \times p = 22.5 \times 4 \text{ mm} = 90 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas:** (1) Forgetting to convert angular displacement from radians to degrees (1 mark). (2) Computing revolutions incorrectly as $4500 \times 200$ instead of dividing (1 mark). (3) Confusing pitch (mm/rev) with thread lead (mm) — for a single-start leadscrew, they are equal, but examiners love to test this.
