#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Model] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Explain the operating principle of **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** in a PLC. Derive the expression for the average voltage delivered to a resistive load. List any **four advantages** of using PWM over a true analog output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLC generates a PWM signal with **frequency $f = 500$ Hz** to control a 24 V / 60 W DC incandescent lamp. The PLC register is **8 bits wide** and currently holds the value **$k = 128$**. Calculate: (i) the PWM period, (ii) the duty cycle in percent, (iii) the average voltage across the lamp, and (iv) the average power dissipated by the lamp. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PWM Principle & Advantages (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** Definition: PWM is a digital signaling technique in which a load is switched fully ON and fully OFF at a high frequency, with the *ratio* of ON-time to total period (the duty cycle) controlling the average power delivered. The load's inertia or thermal mass integrates the chopped waveform.

2. **(2 marks)** Derivation of average voltage — show steps:

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_{max} \, dt = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot T_{on}}{T} = V_{max} \cdot D$$

3. **(3 marks — 1 mark each, list any four):** Advantages of PWM over analog output:
   - **High noise immunity:** A digital signal is either 0 or $V_{max}$, immune to line-voltage drops.
   - **No DAC required:** Saves cost and complexity; only a timer and switch are needed.
   - **High efficiency:** The switching transistor dissipates very little power (either fully ON with low $V_{DS}$, or fully OFF with zero current).
   - **Easy to generate in software:** A single timer ISR or hardware PWM peripheral suffices.
   - **Easy to transmit over long distances / networks:** Digital pulses are far more robust than millivolt-level analog signals.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 500$ Hz, $V_{max} = 24$ V, $P_{max} = 60$ W, 8-bit register, $k = 128$.

**(i) PWM Period (1 mark):**

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{500} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Duty Cycle (2 marks):**

For an 8-bit register, full scale = $2^8 - 1 = 255$. The duty cycle in percent is:

$$D\% = \dfrac{k}{2^n - 1} \times 100 = \dfrac{128}{255} \times 100 \approx 50.196\%$$

**(iii) Average Voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{avg} = V_{max} \times \dfrac{D\%}{100} = 24 \times 0.50196 \approx 12.05 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Average Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{avg} = P_{max} \times D = 60 \times 0.50196 \approx 30.12 \text{ W}$$

Alternative cross-check: $P_{avg} = \dfrac{V_{avg}^2}{R}$ where $R = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{P_{max}} = \dfrac{24^2}{60} = 9.6 \, \Omega$.

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{12.05^2}{9.6} \approx 15.13 \text{ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical student pitfall:** Note the *two* different formulas for average power. The correct one is $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{max}$ because power is proportional to $D$ for a fixed supply voltage, not to $D^2$. The $V_{avg}^2/R$ formula works only when the load is purely resistive *and* the supply is exactly $V_{avg}$ — in a true chopped PWM waveform, the lamp sees $V_{max}$ for time $T_{on}$ and 0 for time $T_{off}$, so the *rms* heating effect (and perceived brightness) follows $D$, not $D^2$. Examiners test this — use $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{max}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PTO = Position & Speed** control (variable frequency, fixed 50% duty cycle, count matters).
- **PWM = Power & Average Energy** control (fixed frequency, variable duty cycle, on-time ratio matters).
- **Average voltage from PWM:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{max}$, where $D = T_{on}/T$.
- **Average power to resistive load from PWM:** $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{max}$ (linear, NOT quadratic).
- **Angular displacement from PTO:** $\theta = N \cdot \theta_{step}$.
- **Resolution in bits:** $D_{LSB} = 100\% / (2^n - 1)$.
- **PWM frequency rule of thumb:** $> 20$ kHz for motors (above audible), $< 10$ Hz for heaters (allow full thermal cycling).
- **PTO frequency rule of thumb:** Must be $\leq$ the maximum step rate of the motor (otherwise steps are lost — *stall condition*).
- **Hardware isolation:** PTO/PWM are *always* generated by dedicated hardware (not the scan cycle) because the scan time is too slow.
- **Direction bit:** Separate output for PTO, controls motor rotation direction.
- **Open-loop caveat:** Stepper motors driven by PTO are open-loop; missed pulses cause position error with no automatic correction. A closed-loop servo system with encoder feedback avoids this.
- **IEC 61131-2 compliance:** PLC digital outputs are standardized at 24 V DC for industrial use.
- **Common PLC instructions:** Siemens `PLS` / `PWM`, Allen-Bradley `PTO` / `PWM`, Schneider `PLS` / `PWM` — all are functionally similar despite vendor naming differences.
- **Industrial network integration:** PTO/PWM setpoints are often written remotely via Profinet, EtherCAT, or Modbus from a DCS or SCADA supervisory layer.
- **EMI consideration:** Long cables carrying high-frequency PWM act as antennas — use shielded cables and proper grounding per EMC directives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Networking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PLC Networking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — PLC Networking

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **PLC Networking** is defined as the systematic interconnection of multiple Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), Human–Machine Interfaces (HMIs), Remote I/O stations, Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), sensors, actuators, and supervisory computers through standardized **industrial communication protocols** over a shared or switched transmission medium, enabling deterministic data exchange, coordinated process control, distributed I/O handling, and centralized monitoring in real-time automation systems.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Factory as a Smart City"

Imagine a large automated factory as a bustling **smart city**:
- Each **PLC** is a **district headquarters** that controls its own neighborhood (a section of the plant — say, the bottling line, the mixing tank, or the conveyor belt).
- The **network cables** (Profibus, Ethernet, DeviceNet, etc.) are the **roads and highways** connecting the districts.
- The **communication protocol** is the **language and traffic rules** every district must obey to avoid chaos.
- The **master PLC / SCADA HMI** is the **city's central command center** that monitors every district and issues high-level instructions.
- **Field devices** (sensors, valves, motors) are the **citizens and shops** who provide data and respond to commands.

Without networking, each PLC works in **isolation** (like a district with no roads). With networking, the entire factory becomes a **coordinated organism** where every section shares data in milliseconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why Networking?**
> A single PLC can handle only a limited I/O count. **Networking** extends the I/O count to thousands, enables **distributed control** (placing intelligence near the process), provides **redundancy** (if one PLC fails, others take over), and creates the backbone for **Industry 4.0 / IIoT** integration.

### 1.2 Core Necessities Driving PLC Networking

| # | Driver | Engineering Reason |
|---|--------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Scalability** | Single PLCs are limited to 256–2048 I/O. Networks scale to **10,000+** points. |
| 2 | **Distributed Control** | Reduces long analog wiring by placing remote I/O racks near field devices. |
| 3 | **Data Sharing** | Recipes, diagnostics, alarms, and process variables shared between controllers. |
| 4 | **Redundancy** | Hot-standby PLC pairs ensure zero downtime in critical processes (power, pharma). |
| 5 | **Supervisory Integration** | SCADA / MES / ERP systems collect plant-wide data. |
| 6 | **Diagnostics & Maintenance** | Remote programming, firmware updates, and predictive analytics. |

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in PLC Networks

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standardized values** are foundational and frequently tested:
> - **Standard Industrial Ethernet Speed:** **100 Mbps** (Fast Ethernet, IEEE 802.3u) and **1 Gbps** (Gigabit Ethernet, IEEE 802.3ab).
> - **Maximum Profibus DP segment length:** **1200 m at 9.6 kbps** down to **100 m at 12 Mbps** (RS-485 physics).
> - **Token Rotation Time in Profibus:** Target ≤ **T_TR** (Token Target Rotation Time).
> - **Maximum DeviceNet cable length:** **500 m at 125 kbps**, **250 m at 250 kbps**, **100 m at 500 kbps**.
> - **Signal propagation velocity in copper:** ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (≈ 5 ns/m).
> - **Standard cable impedances:** **120 Ω** (RS-485 Profibus termination), **50 Ω** (coaxial), **100 Ω** (twisted-pair Ethernet).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ISO/OSI Reference Model Applied to PLC Networks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every industrial protocol maps to a **subset** of the **7-layer OSI model**. KTU examiners frequently test layer identification, so the table below is **high-yield**.

| OSI Layer | Function | PLC Networking Relevance | Example |
|-----------|----------|--------------------------|---------|
| **7 — Application** | User-level services | Reading/writing process variables, diagnostics | CIP (Common Industrial Protocol), Modbus application |
| **6 — Presentation** | Data formatting, encryption | ASCII ↔ binary conversion, security wrappers | OPC UA encoding |
| **5 — Session** | Dialog control | Connection establishment between PLCs | S7 communication session |
| **4 — Transport** | End-to-end reliability | TCP (reliable) vs UDP (fast) | TCP/UDP over Ethernet |
| **3 — Network** | Routing, logical addressing | IP addressing for multi-subnet plants | IPv4 in PROFINET |
| **2 — Data Link** | Framing, MAC, error detection | Token passing, CSMA/CD, CRC | Profibus MAC, Ethernet MAC |
| **1 — Physical** | Electrical/optical signaling | RS-485, fiber, twisted-pair voltages | EIA-485, 802.3 PHY |

## 2.2 Network Topologies in PLC Systems

A **network topology** is the geometric arrangement of nodes.

### A. **Line / Bus Topology**
- All devices tap onto a **single shared backbone cable** with **termination resistors** (typically **120 Ω**) at both ends to prevent signal reflection.
- Used by: **Profibus DP**, **DeviceNet**, **Foundation Fieldbus H1**, **Modbus RTU (RS-485)**.
- Advantage: **Low cabling cost**, simple to extend.
- Disadvantage: **Single point of failure** — if the backbone breaks, all downstream devices lose communication.

### B. **Star Topology**
- All devices connect to a **central switch/hub**.
- Used by: **Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP)**.
- Advantage: **Fault isolation** — one cable break affects only that node.
- Disadvantage: Switch failure collapses the network; more cable required.

### C. **Ring Topology**
- Devices connected in a **closed loop**; data travels in **one or both directions**.
- Used by: **Media Redundancy Protocol (MRP)** in PROFINET, **Device Level Ring (DLR)** in EtherNet/IP.
- Advantage: **Built-in redundancy** — a single break is bypassed.
- Disadvantage: Latency increases with node count.

### D. **Tree (Hierarchical) Topology**
- Combines star segments connected in a bus/line; reflects the **ISA-95 automation pyramid**.
- Standard in modern plants: Field level → Control level → Supervisory level → Enterprise level.

### E. **Mesh Topology**
- Every node connects to multiple others; maximum redundancy.
- Used in **wireless industrial networks** (WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a) for plant-critical applications.

## 2.3 Communication Architectures

| Architecture | Description | Example Protocol |
|--------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Master–Slave** | One master polls multiple slaves cyclically. Deterministic. | Modbus RTU, Profibus DP, DeviceNet |
| **Multi-Master / Token Passing** | All masters share a logical token; only the token holder transmits. | Profibus FMS, Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) |
| **Producer–Consumer** | Publisher multicasts data; subscribers consume what they need. | EtherNet/IP, PROFINET RT, Foundation Fieldbus |
| **Client–Server** | Client requests data from server. | Modbus TCP, OPC UA, S7 Communication |
| **Peer-to-Peer (P2P)** | All devices have equal status; shared memory or messaging. | PLC-to-PLC links (e.g., Allen-Bradley DH+, Siemens S7) |

## 2.4 Industrial Protocol Comparison — KTU High-Yield Table

| Protocol | Standard | Physical Layer | Speed | Topology | Key Use |
|----------|----------|----------------|-------|----------|---------|
| **Modbus RTU** | Modicon (1979) | RS-485 / RS-232 | Up to 115.2 kbps | Line | Legacy integration |
| **Profibus DP** | IEC 61158 / EN 50170 | RS-485 | 9.6 kbps – 12 Mbps | Line | Siemens factory floor |
| **PROFINET** | IEC 61158 / 61784 | Industrial Ethernet | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | Star/Line/Ring | Siemens real-time control |
| **EtherNet/IP** | ODVA / IEC 61784 | Industrial Ethernet | 10/100/1000 Mbps | Star/DLR/Ring | Rockwell / ODVA ecosystem |
| **DeviceNet** | ODVA / IEC 62026-3 | CAN-based | 125 / 250 / 500 kbps | Line/Trunk-Drop | Discrete sensors/actuators |
| **Foundation Fieldbus H1** | IEC 61158 | 31.25 kbps, bus-powered | 31.25 kbps | Bus | Process industries |
| **Modbus TCP** | Open standard | Ethernet | 10/100 Mbps | Star | Open SCADA integration |
| **AS-i (Actuator-Sensor Interface)** | IEC 62026-2 | Two-wire unshielded | 167 kbps | Tree/Line | Binary sensor/actuator networks |
| **CANopen** | CiA 301 | CAN | 10 kbps – 1 Mbps | Line | Embedded / mobile machines |

## 2.5 Network Components — The Interconnection Toolkit

| Component | OSI Layer | Function |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Repeater** | Layer 1 | Regenerates and amplifies the electrical signal; extends cable length. |
| **Hub** | Layer 1 | Broadcasts incoming signal to all ports (no filtering). |
| **Bridge** | Layer 2 | Forwards frames based on MAC addresses; reduces collisions. |
| **Switch** | Layer 2 | Learns MAC table, forwards only to correct port — full-duplex, no collisions. |
| **Router** | Layer 3 | Routes packets between different IP subnets. |
| **Gateway** | Layers 1–7 | Converts between **dissimilar protocols** (e.g., Profibus ↔ Modbus). |
| **Terminator** | Layer 1 | **120 Ω** resistor absorbs reflections at bus ends. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A **gateway** performs **protocol translation**; a **bridge** performs **frame forwarding**. Mixing them up is a common deduction point.

## 2.6 Transmission Media in Industrial Plants

| Medium | Type | Typical Use | Max Segment |
|--------|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Twisted-Pair Copper (UTP/STP)** | Electrical | Profibus, PROFINET, Modbus | 100 m (Ethernet) |
| **Coaxial Cable** | Electrical | Legacy Ethernet, Foundation Fieldbus | 500 m |
| **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)** | Optical | Noisy environments, long runs | 2 km @ 100 Mbps |
| **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** | Optical | Plant-to-plant, very long runs | 40+ km |
| **Wireless (IEEE 802.15.4 / Wi-Fi)** | RF | Mobile assets, rotating machinery | 100 m – several km |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `\vert vert` characters in the table below to avoid markdown-breaking pipes.** Each formula is graded for KTU frequency.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables | Unit | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------|--------------------|-----------|------|---------------|
| 1 | **Propagation Delay** | $t_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $t_p$ = delay, $d$ = cable length, $v$ = signal velocity (≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** in Cu) | seconds | High |
| 2 | **Bit Rate vs Baud Rate** | $R_b = B \times \log_2(M)$ | $R_b$ = bit rate, $B$ = baud, $M$ = signal levels | bps / symbols/s | High |
| 3 | **Token Rotation Time (Profibus)** | $T_{TR} = \sum t_{msg} + \sum t_{idle}$ | Sum of all message times and idle times | seconds | Medium |
| 4 | **Network Throughput** | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{data}}{T_{data} + T_{overhead}} \times 100\%$ | Useful data time vs total cycle | percent | High |
| 5 | **Frame Efficiency** | $\eta_f = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{header} + L_{trailer}}$ | Payload bits vs total frame bits | ratio (0–1) | High |
| 6 | **Hamming Distance** | $d_{\min} = \min\{H(x,y) : x \neq y\}$ | Minimum bit differences between codewords | integer | Medium |
| 7 | **CRC Generator Polynomial Degree** | Detects all burst errors $\le n$ bits where $n$ = degree of generator | $n$ = degree of polynomial | bits | Medium |
| 8 | **Profibus Segment Length vs Speed** | $L \approx \dfrac{1200 \text{ m} \times 9.6 \text{ kbps}}{f_{bps}}$ | Approximate scaling rule of thumb | meters | Medium |
| 9 | **Modbus Address Capacity** | $2^N$ devices per master | $N$ = address bits (8 for Modbus RTU) | nodes | Low |
| 10 | **CSMA/CD Slot Time (Ethernet)** | $t_{slot} = 512 \text{ bit times}$ | 51.2 μs at 10 Mbps | time | Low |
| 11 | **Maximum Nodes on RS-485 Bus** | 32 unit loads (standard), 256 with repeaters | UL = unit load | devices | Medium |
| 12 | **Cycle Time — Distributed I/O** | $T_{cycle} = T_{scan} + T_{net} + T_{process}$ | PLC scan + network + process reaction | seconds | High |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PLC networks are the **central nervous system** of every modern industry:
> - **Automotive plants** (e.g., Tata, Maruti Suzuki) use **PROFINET** with **MRP rings** for sub-1 ms control of welding robots.
> - **Oil & Gas** uses **FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1** and **PROFIBUS PA** in intrinsically safe (IS) zones.
> - **Power plants** employ **redundant IEC 61850** networks over Ethernet for substation automation.
> - **Pharmaceutical batch plants** rely on **21 CFR Part 11**-compliant **OPC UA** for secure, auditable data.
> - **Water treatment** SCADA uses **Modbus TCP / DNP3** for long-distance telemetry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **quantitative foundations** of PLC networking used in KTU problem statements. Each derivation is shown **step by step** with no logical shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation: Propagation Delay vs Transmission Delay

> [!NOTE]
> These two delays are conceptually different and are **separately tested**.

**Definitions:**
- **Propagation Delay** $t_p$ — time for one bit to physically travel the medium.
- **Transmission Delay** $t_t$ — time to push all bits of a frame onto the wire.

**Derivation of propagation delay:**

The signal travels at velocity $v$ in a medium of length $d$.

$$t_p = \frac{d}{v}$$

**Substituting copper cable constants:** $v \approx 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (≈ 66 % of the speed of light in vacuum $c \approx 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$).

$$t_p = \frac{d}{2 \times 10^8} \text{ s}$$

**Step-by-step numeric example:** Cable length = 100 m, copper medium.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_p &= \frac{100 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} \\
    &= 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\
    &= 500 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of transmission delay:**

If a frame contains $L$ bits and the link bit rate is $R$ (bps), then:

$$t_t = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Combined one-way latency:**

$$t_{latency} = t_t + t_p = \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering implication:** At 1 Gbps, a 1500-byte Ethernet frame still has $t_t = 12 \text{ μs}$, and over 100 m of copper, $t_p = 500 \text{ ns}$, so the total latency is dominated by the frame size, not the distance — a critical insight for real-time PROFINET IRT tuning.

## 3.2 Derivation: Frame Efficiency for Modbus RTU

**Modbus RTU frame structure:**

| Field | Size (bits) |
|-------|-------------|
| Start (silent interval 3.5 char) | 28 |
| Slave Address | 8 |
| Function Code | 8 |
| Data Payload | $8 \times N$ |
| CRC-16 | 16 |
| End (silent interval 3.5 char) | 28 |
| **Total** | $88 + 8N$ |

For reading 10 holding registers ($N=20$ bytes), the payload = 160 bits.

$$L_{payload} = 160 \text{ bits}, \quad L_{total} = 88 + 160 = 248 \text{ bits}$$

**Frame efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_f &= \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{total}} = \frac{160}{248} \\
       &\approx 0.645 \text{ or } 64.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Roughly **35.5 %** of every Modbus RTU transaction is **overhead**. This is why large data blocks are usually packed into a **single Modbus transaction** (e.g., function codes 03/04 with maximum 125 registers per query).

## 3.3 Derivation: Profibus DP Cycle Time

**Profibus DP** uses a **cyclic master–slave polling model**. The cycle time is:

$$T_{cyc} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ t_{req,i} + t_{resp,i} + t_{idle} \right]$$

where $t_{req,i}$ and $t_{resp,i}$ are request and response frame transmission times for slave $i$, and $t_{idle}$ is the inter-message gap.

**Frame transmission time formula:**

$$t_{frame} = \frac{L_{frame} \times 11}{R_b}$$

> The factor **11 bits/byte** accounts for 1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop (UART framing).

**Worked example:** 10 slaves, each with 4-byte request and 8-byte response, at $R_b = 1.5$ Mbps.

- $L_{req} = 4$ bytes → $t_{req} = \dfrac{4 \times 11}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 29.3 \text{ μs}$
- $L_{resp} = 8$ bytes → $t_{resp} = \dfrac{8 \times 11}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 58.7 \text{ μs}$
- Per-slave cycle = $29.3 + 58.7 + 50$ (typical $t_{idle}$) = **138 μs**

**Total cycle for 10 slaves:**

$$
T_{cyc} = 10 \times 138 \text{ μs} = 1.38 \text{ ms}
$$

**Acceptance check:** A Profibus DP cycle below **1 ms** is achievable at high baud rates; for fast motion control, $T_{cyc} < 500 \text{ μs}$ is the target. The derived 1.38 ms is **adequate for process control but marginal for high-speed motion**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Hamming Distance & Error Detection

**Definition (single-bit Hamming distance):** The number of bit positions in which two equal-length binary words differ.

**Example:** Words **1011001** and **1001001** differ only at bit 4 → $H = 1$.

**Parity-check code analysis (Hamming 7,4):**

Generator matrix $G$ produces codewords of length $n = 7$ from data of $k = 4$ bits.

The minimum Hamming distance is:

$$d_{\min} = 3 \text{ for Hamming (7,4)}$$

**Error detection capability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Detect up to } & (d_{\min} - 1) \text{ errors} = 2 \text{ errors} \\
\text{Correct up to } & \left\lfloor \dfrac{d_{\min} - 1}{2} \right\rfloor = 1 \text{ error}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why Modbus RTU uses a **16-bit CRC** (CRC-16/Modbus polynomial **0xA001**), which detects:
- All single-bit errors.
- All double-bit errors.
- All odd-count burst errors.
- All burst errors $\le 16$ bits in length.

## 3.5 Derivation: Network Throughput with Polling Overhead

**Setup:** Master polls $N$ slaves cyclically. Each transaction consumes $T_{msg,i}$ seconds; the master requires $T_{proc}$ seconds for processing between polls.

**Useful data time per cycle:**

$$T_{data} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{payload,i} \text{ bits}$$

**Total cycle time:**

$$T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ T_{req,i} + T_{resp,i} \right] + N \cdot T_{proc}$$

**Aggregate throughput:**

$$
\eta_{net} = \frac{T_{data}}{T_{cycle}} \quad [\text{bits per cycle}]
$$

**Sustained bit rate:**

$$
R_{sustained} = \frac{T_{data}}{T_{cycle}} \times \frac{1}{1 \text{ cycle}} = \frac{\sum L_{payload,i}}{\sum T_{req,i} + \sum T_{resp,i} + N \cdot T_{proc}} \text{ bps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Throughput degrades as the number of slaves grows, the link speed decreases, or the processing time between polls is large. This is the **fundamental scalability limit** of master–slave industrial networks.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Top KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario Type | Given Data | Output | Key Formula |
|---------------|------------|--------|-------------|
| Cable length & speed | $d = 500 \text{ m}$, $v = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ | $t_p$ | $t_p = d/v$ |
| Frame transmission | $L = 256 \text{ bits}$, $R = 1 \text{ Mbps}$ | $t_t$ | $t_t = L/R$ |
| Profibus cycle | $N = 20$ slaves, $R = 12 \text{ Mbps}$, payload 16 B/resp | $T_{cyc}$ | $T_{cyc} = N(t_{req}+t_{resp}+t_{idle})$ |
| Modbus efficiency | 10-register read, Modbus RTU | $\eta_f$ | $\eta_f = L_{payload}/L_{total}$ |
| Error detection | $d_{\min} = 3$ Hamming code | Detects 2, corrects 1 | $d = 2t + 1$ |
| Token rotation | 3 masters, message sizes, baud rate | $T_{TR}$ | $T_{TR} = \sum T_{msg} + \sum T_{idle}$ |
| CSMA/CD collision | 1000 m cable, 10 Mbps | Slot time | $t_{slot} = 51.2 \text{ μs}$ |
| Ascii frame check | N = 10 registers, 11 bits/char | Frame size | $L = 11 \times \text{bytes}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **Mermaid** with strict alphanumeric node IDs (prefixed with letters) and **plain uppercase text inside double-quoted labels** (no bold/italic/HTML tags) to ensure flawless rendering.

## 4.1 The OSI 7-Layer Model in a PLC Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION - MODBUS OPCUA CIP S7"]
    layer6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION - DATA FORMATTING ENCRYPTION"]
    layer5["LAYER 5 SESSION - CONNECTION MANAGEMENT"]
    layer4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT - TCP UDP RELIABLE FAST"]
    layer3["LAYER 3 NETWORK - IP ADDRESSING ROUTING"]
    layer2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK - MAC ADDRESS FRAMING CRC"]
    layer1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL - RS485 ETHERNET FIBER"]

    layer7 --> layer6
    layer6 --> layer5
    layer5 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer1
```

## 4.2 Industrial Network Topology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph busTopo["LINE BUS TOPOLOGY - PROFINET DEVICE NET MODBUS"]
        busMaster["MASTER PLC"]
        busDev1["SLAVE 1"]
        busDev2["SLAVE 2"]
        busDev3["SLAVE 3"]
        busMaster --- busDev1
        busDev1 --- busDev2
        busDev2 --- busDev3
        busTerm1["TERM 120 OHM"]
        busTerm2["TERM 120 OHM"]
        busDev3 --- busTerm1
        busMaster --- busTerm2
    end

    subgraph starTopo["STAR TOPOLOGY - PROFINET ETHERNET IP"]
        starSwitch["MANAGED SWITCH"]
        starNode1["PLC 1"]
        starNode2["PLC 2"]
        starNode3["HMI"]
        starNode4["REMOTE IO"]
        starSwitch --- starNode1
        starSwitch --- starNode2
        starSwitch --- starNode3
        starSwitch --- starNode4
    end

    subgraph ringTopo["RING TOPOLOGY - MRP DLR REDUNDANCY"]
        ringNode1["PLC 1"]
        ringNode2["PLC 2"]
        ringNode3["PLC 3"]
        ringNode4["PLC 4"]
        ringNode1 --- ringNode2
        ringNode2 --- ringNode3
        ringNode3 --- ringNode4
        ringNode4 --- ringNode1
    end
```

## 4.3 Plant-Wide PLC Network Architecture (ISA-95 Pyramid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    level4["LEVEL 4 ENTERPRISE - ERP SAP ORACLE"]
    level3["LEVEL 3 OPERATIONS - MES HISTORIAN OPC UA"]
    level2["LEVEL 2 SUPERVISORY - SCADA HMI DASHBOARDS"]
    level1["LEVEL 1 BASIC CONTROL - PLC CONTROLLER RTU"]
    level0["LEVEL 0 FIELD - SENSORS ACTUATORS VALVES MOTORS"]

    level4 -- ETHERNET TCP IP --> level3
    level3 -- OPC UA MODBUS TCP --> level2
    level2 -- PROFINET ETHERNET IP --> level1
    level1 -- FIELDBUS AS I DEVICENET --> level0
```

## 4.4 Master–Slave Polling Sequence (Modbus RTU)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MasterA as MASTER PLC
    participant SlaveA as SLAVE 1 VFD
    participant SlaveB as SLAVE 2 SENSOR
    participant SlaveC as SLAVE 3 VALVE

    MasterA->>SlaveA: REQUEST 03H READ HOLDING REGISTERS
    SlaveA-->>MasterA: RESPONSE 06 WORDS CRC
    MasterA->>SlaveB: REQUEST 04H READ INPUT REGISTERS
    SlaveB-->>MasterA: RESPONSE 04 WORDS CRC
    MasterA->>SlaveC: REQUEST 06H WRITE SINGLE REGISTER
    SlaveC-->>MasterA: ACK CRC
    Note over MasterA: NEXT POLL CYCLE STARTS
```

## 4.5 PROFINET Real-Time Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION - PROFINET IO DEVICE FUNCTIONS"]
    rpcLayer["RPC PROFINET AC10"]
    dcpLayer["DCP DISCOVERY AND CONFIGURATION PROTOCOL"]
    rtLayer["PROFINET RT REAL TIME - CYCLE 1 MS"]
    irtLayer["PROFINET IRT ISOCHRONOUS REAL TIME - CYCLE 250 US"]
    udpLayer["UDP IP"]
    tcpLayer["TCP IP"]
    ethLayer["FAST ETHERNET 100 MBPS"]

    appLayer --> rtLayer
    appLayer --> irtLayer
    rpcLayer --> tcpLayer
    dcpLayer --> udpLayer
    rtLayer --> ethLayer
    irtLayer --> ethLayer
    udpLayer --> ethLayer
    tcpLayer --> ethLayer
```

## 4.6 Network Component Placement (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fieldBus["FIELD DEVICES SENSORS VALVES"]
    repeaterA["REPEATER LAYER 1 SIGNAL REGEN"]
    switchA["MANAGED SWITCH LAYER 2 MAC TABLE"]
    routerA["ROUTER LAYER 3 IP SUBNET GATEWAY"]
    gatewayA["GATEWAY LAYER 7 PROFINET TO MODBUS"]
    scadaA["SCADA HMI SUPERVISORY"]
    plcMaster["MASTER PLC CONTROLLER"]
    cloudA["ENTERPRISE CLOUD ERP MES"]

    fieldBus --> repeaterA
    repeaterA --> switchA
    switchA --> routerA
    routerA --> gatewayA
    gatewayA --> scadaA
    scadaA --> plcMaster
    scadaA --> cloudA
```

## 4.7 Redundancy Architecture — MRP Ring

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1A["PLC 1 MRM MANAGER"]
    node2A["PLC 2 MRC CLIENT"]
    node3A["PLC 3 MRC CLIENT"]
    node4A["PLC 4 MRC CLIENT"]
    node5A["PLC 5 MRC CLIENT"]

    node1A --- node2A
    node2A --- node3A
    node3A --- node4A
    node4A --- node5A
    node5A --- node1A
    note1["PRIMARY PATH FOR DATA CLOCKWISE"]
    note2["BACKUP PATH ACTIVATED ON LINK FAILURE"]
    node1A -. BLOCKS BACKUP .-> node5A
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme** ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern for **OERUT722 — PLC and Distributed Control Systems**, Module 2. Marks are split as Part A (3 marks × 2 = 6) and Part B (14 marks, with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define PLC networking. List any four advantages of networking PLCs in an industrial automation system.**

**Model Answer:**

> **PLC networking** is the interconnection of multiple PLCs, I/O devices, HMIs, and supervisory systems through **standardized industrial communication protocols** over a shared or switched transmission medium to enable **deterministic data exchange, coordinated control, and centralized monitoring** in real-time automation.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Scalability** — extends I/O count from a single PLC's limit (≈ 2048) to plant-wide 10,000+ points.
2. **Distributed intelligence** — remote I/O and sub-PLCs reduce long analog wiring and improve noise immunity.
3. **Redundancy** — hot-standby controllers and ring topologies (MRP, DLR) eliminate single points of failure.
4. **Data integration** — enables SCADA, MES, and ERP connectivity for Industry 4.0 and IIoT applications.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Compare Profibus DP and PROFINET in terms of physical layer, maximum speed, topology, and typical application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Profibus DP** | **PROFINET** |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| **Physical Layer** | RS-485 (electrical) | Industrial Ethernet (100 Mbps / 1 Gbps) |
| **Maximum Speed** | 12 Mbps | 1 Gbps (Class C / IRT) |
| **Topology** | Line / Bus (terminated) | Star, Line, Ring (with MRP) |
| **Application** | Discrete I/O, drives, sensors | Real-time motion, plant-wide control, IT integration |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic (token + polling) | RT (1 ms), IRT (≤ 250 μs) |
| **Backward Compatibility** | Legacy factory floor | Backward to Profibus via proxy |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **OSI 7-layer model** with reference to PLC industrial networks. Illustrate how the **PROFINET** and **Modbus TCP** protocols map to each layer.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A **Profibus DP** network has **12 slaves** polled cyclically by the master. Each request is **8 bytes** and each response is **24 bytes**. The link runs at **1.5 Mbps**, the inter-message idle time is **40 μs**, and the UART frame uses **11 bits/byte**. Compute the **total cycle time** and the **percentage of overhead** in the cycle.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** reference model (ISO/IEC 7498-1) is a 7-layer abstraction for communication systems.

| Layer | PROFINET Mapping | Modbus TCP Mapping |
|-------|------------------|---------------------|
| **7. Application** | PROFINET IO device functions, GSD file, diagnostics | Modbus Function Codes (01–06, 15, 16, 23) |
| **6. Presentation** | XER encoding (ASN.1), security wrappers | MBAP header decoding, byte-order swap |
| **5. Session** | PROFINET connection RPC session | TCP socket session |
| **4. Transport** | TCP (NRT), UDP (RT) | TCP (reliable) |
| **3. Network** | IPv4 with DCP discovery | IPv4, DHCP/Static |
| **2. Data Link** | Ethernet MAC, VLAN, priority tagging (PCP) | Ethernet MAC, full-duplex switched |
| **1. Physical** | 100BASE-TX (Cat 5e), Fiber | 100BASE-TX (Cat 5e), Wi-Fi |

**Key insight:**
- **PROFINET** uses **three channel types** — **NRT** (non-real-time, TCP/IP), **RT** (real-time, layer 2 cut-through, 1 ms cycle), and **IRT** (isochronous, hardware-scheduled, ≤ 250 μs jitter).
- **Modbus TCP** is purely **client–server** on top of TCP/IP — it inherits **Ethernet's non-determinism** unless the plant uses managed switches with QoS.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $N = 12$ slaves, $L_{req} = 8$ bytes, $L_{resp} = 24$ bytes, $R = 1.5 \text{ Mbps}$, $t_{idle} = 40 \text{ μs}$, 11 bits/byte.

**Step 1: Compute frame transmission times.**

$$
t_{req} = \frac{L_{req} \times 11}{R} = \frac{8 \times 11}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 58.7 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
t_{resp} = \frac{L_{resp} \times 11}{R} = \frac{24 \times 11}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 176 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 2: Per-slave cycle time.**

$$
t_{slave} = t_{req} + t_{resp} + t_{idle} = 58.7 + 176 + 40 = 274.7 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3: Total cycle for 12 slaves.**

$$
T_{cyc} = N \times t_{slave} = 12 \times 274.7 = 3296.4 \text{ μs} \approx 3.30 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Compute overhead percentage.**

Total useful data per cycle (response payload only, ignoring request overhead, addresses, and CRC):

$$
T_{useful} = N \times t_{resp} = 12 \times 176 = 2112 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{cyc}} \times 100\% = \frac{2112}{3296.4} \times 100\% \approx 64.1\%
$$

**Overhead** = $100\% - 64.1\% = 35.9\%$.

**Conclusion:** With 12 slaves, the cycle time of **3.30 ms** is suitable for **process control** but borderline for **high-speed motion control** (which demands < 1 ms).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat **Mermaid/Block diagrams**, explain **star, bus (line), and ring** network topologies as used in PLC systems. For each, give **one industrial protocol example** and **one advantage + one disadvantage**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A **Modbus RTU** master reads **20 holding registers** from a slave over **RS-485** at **115200 bps** with **11 bits/byte**. The Modbus RTU frame consists of: **3.5-char silence (28 bits) + 1 byte address + 1 byte function + 2 bytes start reg + 2 bytes reg count + 1 byte byte-count + 20 bytes data + 2 bytes CRC + 3.5-char silence (28 bits)**. Compute the **total transmission time per transaction** and the **frame efficiency**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

| Topology | Industrial Protocol Example | Diagram | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|----------|----------------------------|---------|-----------|--------------|
| **Star** | PROFINET, EtherNet/IP | Central switch; each device connects via a dedicated cable | Fault isolation; one cable break affects only one node | Switch is SPOF; more cable needed |
| **Bus / Line** | Profibus DP, DeviceNet, Foundation Fieldbus | Single backbone with terminators at both ends | Lowest cable cost; simple to extend | One break disconnects downstream devices |
| **Ring** | PROFINET with MRP, EtherNet/IP with DLR | Each node has two neighbors; data circulates | Built-in redundancy; one break is bypassed | Latency increases with node count |

**Conclusion:** Modern plants favor **star + ring (MRP)** for the best balance of redundancy, scalability, and ease of maintenance.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** 20 holding registers, $R = 115200$ bps, 11 bits/byte.

**Step 1: Compute total frame size in bits.**

Pre/post silence: $28 + 28 = 56$ bits
Header: 1 (addr) + 1 (func) + 2 (start reg) + 2 (reg count) + 1 (byte count) = 7 bytes → $7 \times 11 = 77$ bits
Data: 20 bytes → $20 \times 11 = 220$ bits
CRC: 2 bytes → $2 \times 11 = 22$ bits

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 56 + 77 + 220 + 22 \\
          &= 375 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total transmission time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{L_{total}}{R} = \frac{375}{115200} \\
          &= 3.255 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
          &= 3.255 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute frame efficiency.**

Payload data bits = $20 \times 11 = 220$ bits.

$$
\eta_f = \frac{220}{375} \times 100\% \approx 58.7\%
$$

**Conclusion:** At 115200 bps, a single 20-register Modbus RTU read takes **3.255 ms** with **58.7 %** efficiency. The remaining 41.3 % is overhead from silence intervals, addressing, function code, byte count, and CRC. For high-throughput applications, batch the reads using **function code 23 (Read/Write Multiple Registers)** or use **Modbus TCP** to leverage larger frames.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PLC Networking Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing bit rate and baud rate** — Modbus RTU uses 11 bits/byte (start + 8 data + parity + stop). Forgetting the 11-bit factor yields incorrect transmission times.
> 2. **Using byte count instead of bit count** — frame efficiency must use **bits**, not bytes, when the bit rate is in bps.
> 3. **Mixing up bridge and gateway** — A **bridge** forwards frames at **Layer 2**; a **gateway** translates **protocols** at higher layers.
> 4. **Wrong termination resistance** — RS-485 buses MUST be terminated with **120 Ω** at both ends. Omitting this is a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Ignoring UART framing in Profibus calculations** — Profibus uses **11 bits per character**, not 10.
> 6. **Forgetting propagation delay in long cables** — For fiber runs > 1 km, propagation delay becomes comparable to transmission delay.
> 7. **Confusing RT and IRT in PROFINET** — RT = 1 ms cycle (software); IRT = ≤ 250 μs (hardware-scheduled, motion-grade).
> 8. **No mention of redundancy** when describing ring topology — Always cite **MRP** (PROFINET) or **DLR** (EtherNet/IP) for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: PLC Networking**

- [x] **PLC networking = interconnecting PLCs, I/O, HMIs, SCADA via industrial protocols** for distributed, deterministic control.
- [x] **OSI model** — Always map every protocol to the 7 layers; PROFINET covers all 7; Modbus RTU covers layers 1, 2, 7.
- [x] **Topologies:** **Line/Bus** (Profibus, DeviceNet, Modbus RTU) — terminated with **120 Ω**; **Star** (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP); **Ring** (MRP, DLR).
- [x] **Master–Slave** is the dominant pattern in fieldbus; **Producer–Consumer** dominates Industrial Ethernet.
- [x] **Profibus DP** — RS-485, 9.6 kbps – 12 Mbps, deterministic token + polling.
- [x] **PROFINET** — Industrial Ethernet, RT (1 ms), IRT (≤ 250 μs), uses **DCP** for discovery.
- [x] **Modbus RTU** — RS-485, **11 bits/byte**, master polls 1–247 slaves, function codes 01–06, 15, 16, 23.
- [x] **Modbus TCP** — wraps Modbus PDU in MBAP header on TCP port **502**.
- [x] **DeviceNet** — CAN-based, 125/250/500 kbps, CIP protocol.
- [x] **Components:** Repeater (L1), Hub (L1), Bridge (L2), Switch (L2), Router (L3), Gateway (L7).
- [x] **Termination resistor** = **120 Ω** for RS-485; missing terminator = signal reflections = bus errors.
- [x] **Frame efficiency** $\eta_f = L_{payload} / L_{total}$ — packing more data per transaction improves efficiency.
- [x] **Propagation delay** $t_p = d / v$, with $v \approx 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ in copper.
- [x] **Transmission delay** $t_t = L / R$, where $L$ is in bits and $R$ in bps.
- [x] **Hamming distance $d_{\min}$** — detects $(d_{\min} - 1)$ errors, corrects $\lfloor (d_{\min} - 1)/2 \rfloor$ errors.
- [x] **CRC-16 Modbus** polynomial is **0xA001** (reversed of 0x8005); detects all bursts up to 16 bits.
- [x] **Maximum nodes on standard RS-485** = **32 unit loads**; up to **256** with repeaters.
- [x] **Cycle time for distributed I/O:** $T_{cyc} = N(t_{req} + t_{resp} + t_{idle}) + T_{master\_proc}$.
- [x] **Redundancy mechanisms:** MRP (PROFINET), DLR (EtherNet/IP), PRP / HSR (IEC 62439, substation automation).
- [x] **ISA-95 pyramid:** Field (L0) → Control (L1) → Supervisory (L2) → Operations (L3) → Enterprise (L4).
- [x] **Industry 4.0 enabler:** Industrial Ethernet + **OPC UA** = secure, semantic, multi-vendor data exchange.
- [x] **Remember:** **11 bits/byte** in Modbus RTU and Profibus UART framing — the most-tested numeric factor in KTU.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networking Standards and IEEE Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Networking Standards and IEEE Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Networking Standards in Industrial Automation – Definition

**Networking standards** in the context of PLC and Distributed Control Systems (DCS) are a set of formally documented, internationally ratified technical specifications, protocols, and communication conventions that govern how Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), Remote Terminal Units (RTUs), Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs), Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs), sensors, actuators, and supervisory computers exchange data over a deterministic, real-time industrial communication medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Verbatim):**
> A *networking standard* is a published technical document (e.g., IEEE 802.x, IEC 61158, ISA/ANSI) that defines the **physical medium**, **electrical signaling**, **data link framing**, **medium access control**, **error detection/correction**, and **application-layer semantics** required for interoperability between heterogeneous automation devices in an industrial plant floor network.

In a DCS/PLC network, these standards guarantee that a sensor manufactured by Siemens can communicate with a controller manufactured by Allen-Bradley/Rockwell, and a SCADA server manufactured by Honeywell can supervise them all — provided all of them conform to the same communication standard.

## 1.2 IEEE Standards – Definition

The **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)** is the world's largest technical professional organization responsible for developing consensus-based standards across a vast range of technologies — from power systems and telecommunications to computer networking and industrial automation.

**IEEE Standards** in industrial networking refer specifically to the family of standards developed under the **IEEE 802 LAN/MAN Standards Committee** and other working groups, which define how devices interconnect, transmit, and manage data across local and metropolitan area networks used in automation and control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IEEE Matters in PLC/DCS:**
> Almost every modern industrial network — from the field-level sensor bus (e.g., IO-Link) to the control-level real-time Ethernet (e.g., PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT) — is physically built on top of one or more IEEE 802 standards. Without these foundations, factory-floor interoperability would be impossible.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Universal Language of the Factory"

Imagine a **massive international airport** (your industrial plant) where pilots, air-traffic controllers, ground crew, and passengers all speak different native languages.

- The **IEEE standards** are like the **universal phonetic alphabet, radio frequency bands, and air-traffic-control hand signals** that *every* airport in the world agrees to use.
- The **Networking protocols** (PROFINET, Modbus TCP, EtherCAT, etc.) are the **specific phrasebooks** that allow a French pilot to land safely in Tokyo.
- The **physical medium** (copper cable, fiber optic, wireless RF) is the **runway and radio spectrum** itself.

Without these standards, the pilot and the tower would have no common way to communicate — leading to chaos. In the same way, **without IEEE 802 standards and other networking standards, a Siemens S7-1500 PLC and an ABB AC500 PLC could never exchange a single bit of meaningful data on the same plant floor**.

## 1.4 Classification of Industrial Networking Standards

Industrial networking standards can be classified along several dimensions relevant to the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Dimension | Categories | Example |
|-----------|------------|---------|
| **Scope of Application** | Plant-wide, Cell-level, Field-level | Ethernet TCP/IP, PROFINET, AS-i |
| **Governing Body** | IEEE, IEC, ISA, ISO | IEEE 802.3, IEC 61158, ISA 50.1, ISO/OSI |
| **Physical Medium** | Wired (copper, fiber), Wireless (RF) | 100BASE-TX, 100BASE-FX, IEEE 802.11 |
| **Transmission Type** | Baseband, Broadband | Baseband Ethernet |
| **Topology** | Bus, Star, Ring, Tree, Mesh | PROFIBUS (Bus), PROFINET (Star) |
| **Real-Time Determinism** | Hard RT, Soft RT, Non-RT | EtherCAT (Hard RT), OPC UA (Soft RT) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight for KTU:** The OSI 7-layer model is the *universal conceptual framework* used to position *every* industrial networking standard. Always be prepared to map any standard (IEEE 802.3, PROFIBUS, Modbus, etc.) to the relevant OSI layer in your exam answer.

## 1.5 Historical Context and Evolution

The evolution of industrial networking standards tracks closely with the development of IEEE 802 standards:

1. **1970s** – Proprietary vendor-specific networks (e.g., DECnet, ARCNET)
2. **1985** – **IEEE 802.3 (10BASE5 Ethernet)** and **IEEE 802.4 (Token Bus)** standardized LANs
3. **1990s** – Fieldbus revolution: PROFIBUS (IEC 61158), DeviceNet, Foundation Fieldbus
4. **2000s** – Real-time industrial Ethernet: PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT
5. **2010s–Present** – Time-Sensitive Networking (**IEEE 802.1Qbv, 802.1AS, 802.1CB**), **TSN**, **5G URLLC**, **OPC UA over TSN**

> [!NOTE]
> The 10 Mbps classic Ethernet of **1985** has evolved into today's gigabit industrial Ethernet (1 Gbps, 10 Gbps) running deterministic real-time control loops on the factory floor — a direct result of IEEE's continued refinement of the 802.3 standard.

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **10 Mbps** – Original IEEE 802.3 Ethernet data rate (10BASE-T)
- **100 Mbps** – Fast Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u, 100BASE-TX)
- **1 Gbps** – Gigabit Ethernet (IEEE 802.3ab, 1000BASE-T)
- **2.4 GHz / 5 GHz** – Unlicensed ISM bands for IEEE 802.11 WiFi
- **48-bit** MAC address space (IEEE 802 standard)
- **1500 bytes** – Standard IEEE 802.3 Ethernet MTU
- **100 meters** – Maximum segment length for 100BASE-TX over Cat 5e UTP
- **-40 °C to +85 °C** – Industrial operating temperature range (per IEC 60068)
- **9.6 kbps to 12 Mbps** – Typical PROFIBUS DP data rates
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ISO/OSI Reference Model — Foundation for All Standards

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model, formalized by **ISO/IEC 7498-1**, abstracts network communication into **seven hierarchical layers**. Every IEEE 802 standard is precisely mapped to the lower two layers (Physical and Data Link), with the upper layers defined by other standards bodies (IETF, IEC, ISA, etc.).

| Layer # | Layer Name | Primary Function | IEEE / Industrial Standard Example |
|:-------:|------------|------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **7** | Application | User-facing services, process data exchange | Modbus, OPC UA, EtherNet/IP CIP |
| **6** | Presentation | Data encoding, encryption, compression | OPC UA binary, TLS 1.3 |
| **5** | Session | Dialog control, synchronization | RFC 1006 (ISO-on-TCP) |
| **4** | Transport | Reliable end-to-end delivery | TCP (RFC 793), UDP (RFC 768) |
| **3** | Network | Routing, logical addressing | IPv4/IPv6 (RFC 791/8200) |
| **2** | Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet), IEEE 802.11 (WiFi)** |
| **1** | Physical | Bit transmission over medium | **IEEE 802.3 PHY, RS-485, Fiber** |

## 2.2 IEEE 802 Architecture – The Data Link Subdivision

The **IEEE 802 family** subdivides the OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2) into two sublayers:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2)                  │
├──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────┤
│  Logical Link Control    │  Medium Access        │
│  (LLC) — IEEE 802.2      │  Control (MAC)        │
│                          │  — IEEE 802.3/.4/.5  │
│  • Multiplexing          │  • Framing           │
│  • Flow control          │  • Collision detect  │
│  • Error notification    │  • Address filtering │
└──────────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **LLC sublayer (802.2)** sits *above* the MAC sublayer and provides a common interface to upper-layer protocols. This allows IP, IPX, DECnet, and CIP to run over *any* IEEE 802 MAC (Ethernet, Token Ring, WiFi, etc.) without modification.

## 2.3 Key IEEE 802 Standards — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.3.1 IEEE 802.1 — Bridging, Management, and TSN

- Defines **LAN/MAN bridging** (switches, VLANs, Spanning Tree Protocol — STP/RSTP/MSTP)
- Introduces **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** standards:
  - **IEEE 802.1AS** — Time synchronization (generalized PTP)
  - **IEEE 802.1Qbv** — Time-Aware Shaper (TAS) for scheduled traffic
  - **IEEE 802.1Qbu** — Frame Preemption
  - **IEEE 802.1CB** – Frame Replication and Elimination for Reliability (FRER)
  - **IEEE 802.1Qcc** — Stream Reservation Protocol (SRP) enhancements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TSN is the cornerstone of future industrial Ethernet.** It provides bounded latency (typically < 100 µs) over standard IEEE 802.3 Ethernet, making it suitable for hard real-time control.

### 2.3.2 IEEE 802.2 — Logical Link Control (LLC)

- Provides an interface to the upper layers independent of the MAC
- Defines **LLC Service Access Points (SAPs)**, **Service Access Point Addresses (SAPIs)**, and **LSAPs**

### 2.3.3 IEEE 802.3 — Ethernet (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection — CSMA/CD)

The most widely deployed LAN standard in the world. Original "10BASE5" (Thick Ethernet, 1983) has evolved into 10GBASE-T and beyond.

| Variant | Standard | Speed | Medium | Max Segment |
|---------|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| 10BASE5 | 802.3 (orig) | 10 Mbps | Coax (thick) | 500 m |
| 10BASE-T | 802.3i | 10 Mbps | UTP Cat 3 | 100 m |
| 100BASE-TX | 802.3u | 100 Mbps | UTP Cat 5 | 100 m |
| 1000BASE-T | 802.3ab | 1 Gbps | UTP Cat 5e/6 | 100 m |
| 10GBASE-T | 802.3an | 10 Gbps | UTP Cat 6a/7 | 100 m |
| 100BASE-FX | 802.3u | 100 Mbps | Multimode fiber | 2 km |
| 1000BASE-LX | 802.3z | 1 Gbps | Single-mode fiber | 10 km |

**Frame Format (IEEE 802.3):**
- **Preamble (7 bytes)** + **SFD (1 byte)** + **Destination MAC (6 bytes)** + **Source MAC (6 bytes)** + **Length/Type (2 bytes)** + **Payload (46–1500 bytes)** + **FCS (4 bytes CRC-32)**
- Minimum frame: 64 bytes; Maximum: 1518 bytes (untagged)

**CSMA/CD Operation (for half-duplex legacy Ethernet):**
1. Listen to the medium (Carrier Sense)
2. If idle, begin transmitting
3. If collision detected, jam, back off using Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) algorithm
4. Retransmit after random backoff

> [!WARNING]
> Modern full-duplex switched Ethernet (used in 99% of industrial networks) **does NOT use CSMA/CD** because switches provide dedicated collision domains per port.

### 2.3.4 IEEE 802.4 — Token Bus (Historic)

- **Token Passing** on a bus topology
- Used historically in **MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol)** networks in the 1980s
- Largely obsolete; replaced by Ethernet and Industrial Ethernet

### 2.3.5 IEEE 802.5 — Token Ring (Historic)

- **Token Passing** on a ring topology (4 Mbps / 16 Mbps)
- IBM-led; largely replaced by Ethernet
- Concepts still relevant to **Token Ring** in some PROFIBUS / FDDI systems

### 2.3.6 IEEE 802.11 — Wireless LAN (WiFi)

- Governs WiFi in the **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz** unlicensed ISM bands
- Used in industrial settings for **wireless HMI, AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) control, and mobile worker connectivity**
- Amendments: 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax/be (WiFi 1 through 7)
- Industrial variants: **802.11ah (WiFi HaLow)** for sub-1 GHz long-range IoT

### 2.3.7 IEEE 802.15 — Wireless Personal Area Networks (WPAN)

| Sub-standard | Common Name | Industrial Use |
|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| 802.15.1 | Bluetooth | Wireless sensor commissioning, short-range device config |
| 802.15.4 | ZigBee, WirelessHART, ISA100.11a | Wireless process automation, mesh sensor networks |
| 802.15.4e | TSCH (Time-Slotted Channel Hopping) | Deterministic wireless (WirelessHART) |
| 802.15.6 | Wireless Body Area Networks | Wearable worker safety sensors |

### 2.3.8 IEEE 802.16 — WiMAX (Broadband Wireless)

- Used for long-range (> 10 km) plant-to-plant backhaul
- Largely superseded by 4G/5G for industrial use

## 2.4 The Industrial Ethernet Revolution

**Industrial Ethernet** is the application of IEEE 802.3 Ethernet in automation, with real-time, deterministic, and ruggedized extensions:

| Protocol | Governing Body | Cycle Time | Special Feature |
|----------|----------------|------------|-----------------|
| **PROFINET** | PI (PROFIBUS International) | 31.25 µs (IRT) | Conformance Classes (CC-A/B/C) |
| **EtherNet/IP** | ODVA | 1–10 ms (CIP Sync) | Uses CIP (Common Industrial Protocol) |
| **EtherCAT** | ETG (EtherCAT Technology Group) | 12.5 µs | "Processing on the fly" |
| **Modbus TCP** | Modbus Organization | 5–50 ms | Simple, register-based |
| **SERCOS III** | IG SERCOS | 31.25 µs | Motion control focused |
| **TSN Ethernet** | IEEE 802.1 + IEC/IEEE 60802 | < 100 µs | Standardized real-time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Industrial Ethernet protocols share the same **IEEE 802.3 physical and MAC layers** — they differ only in the upper layers (real-time extensions, application protocols).

## 2.5 Other Critical Standards in PLC/DCS

### 2.5.1 IEEE 488 (GPIB – General Purpose Interface Bus)
- 8-bit parallel digital interface
- Originally HP-IB (Hewlett-Packard Interface Bus)
- Up to 15 devices, max **20 m cable length**
- Max speed: **8 MB/s** (HS488)
- Used to connect instruments to controllers in labs and DCS test benches

### 2.5.2 RS-232 / RS-422 / RS-485 (EIA/TIA Standards)
- **RS-232** – Point-to-point, ±12 V, < 15 m, max 115.2 kbps (legacy)
- **RS-422** – Differential, 10 devices, up to 1200 m, 10 Mbps
- **RS-485** – Differential, 32 devices (or 256 with repeaters), 1200 m, 10 Mbps – **the workhorse of industrial fieldbuses (Modbus RTU, PROFIBUS DP physical layer)**

### 2.5.3 IEEE 1284 – Parallel Port (Parallel Printer Port)
- Legacy; rarely used in modern DCS

### 2.5.4 IEC 61158 – Fieldbus Standard
- Defines **8 types of fieldbus** (Type 1: Foundation Fieldbus, Type 3: PROFIBUS, Type 4: P-NET, etc.)

### 2.5.5 IEC 62443 – Industrial Cybersecurity Standards
- Critical for securing PLC/DCS networks
- Defines Security Levels (SL 1 to SL 4)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Expression / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---------------------|--------|--------------------|------|-------|
| **Frame transmission time** | $T_{frame}$ | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{L_{frame} \times 8}{R_{bps}}$ | seconds | $L_{frame}$ in bits, $R_{bps}$ in bps |
| **Minimum Ethernet frame size** | $L_{min}$ | $L_{min} = 512$ bits (64 bytes) | bits | For collision detection in half-duplex |
| **Maximum Ethernet frame size** | $L_{max}$ | $L_{max} = 12{,}144$ bits (1518 bytes) | bits | Excluding VLAN tag |
| **CSMA/CD slot time** | $T_{slot}$ | $T_{slot} = 512$ bit-times | bit-times | = 51.2 µs at 10 Mbps |
| **Binary Exponential Backoff (max retries)** | $k$ | $0 \le k \le 10$ | — | 16 attempts at 10 Mbps, $2^{k}$ slots |
| **Network throughput** | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{frame}}$ | ratio | Efficiency metric |
| **Propagation delay** | $T_{prop}$ | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_{prop}}$ | seconds | $v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable |
| **WiFi theoretical throughput (802.11n)** | $R_{max}$ | $R_{max} = 600$ Mbps (40 MHz, 4×4 MIMO) | Mbps | |
| **PROFIBUS DP cycle time (typical)** | $T_{cycle}$ | $T_{cycle} \approx 1$ to $10$ ms | ms | 12 Mbps max baud |
| **PROFINET IRT cycle** | $T_{IRT}$ | $T_{IRT} = 31.25$ µs | µs | Isochronous Real-Time |
| **EtherCAT cycle time** | $T_{EC}$ | $T_{EC} = 12.5$ µs (min) | µs | With distributed clocks |
| **Maximum RS-485 cable length** | $L_{max}$ | $L_{max} = 1200$ m @ ≤ 93.75 kbps | m | |
| **Maximum GPIB devices** | $N_{GPIB}$ | $N_{GPIB} \le 15$ | — | +1 controller |
| **Max segment length 100BASE-TX** | $L_{UTP}$ | $L_{UTP} \le 100$ m | m | Cat 5e UTP |
| **5-4-3 Rule (legacy Ethernet)** | — | 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated | — | Applies to 10BASE5/T |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical exam tip:** For latency/jitter calculations in industrial networks, the cycle time formula is $T_{cycle} = T_{transmit} + T_{propagation} + T_{processing}$. The deterministic guarantee of TSN/IRT comes from **bounding each of these three terms**.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application

1. **Process Industries** (Refineries, Chemical): Foundation Fieldbus + WirelessHART over IEEE 802.15.4
2. **Discrete Manufacturing** (Automotive): PROFINET, EtherNet/IP on IEEE 802.3 with TSN
3. **Power Systems**: IEC 61850 (substation automation) over IEEE 802.3
4. **Smart Grid**: IEEE 2030.5 (Smart Energy Profile 2.0)
5. **Pharmaceutical/Batch Control**: ISA-88 + OPC UA over Industrial Ethernet
6. **Plant-wide Backbone**: Gigabit/10 Gigabit Ethernet on fiber (IEEE 802.3z, 802.3ae)
7. **Wireless Sensor Networks**: IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH (Time-Slotted Channel Hopping)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Ethernet Frame Size from CSMA/CD Constraints

The IEEE 802.3 standard mandates a **minimum frame size of 64 bytes (512 bits)** to ensure that **a transmitting station is still transmitting when the first bit reaches the farthest station and any collision signal returns**. This is the cornerstone of the CSMA/CD collision detection mechanism.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the network maximum propagation round-trip time.**

The worst case occurs when two stations at the extreme ends of the maximum network diameter (5 × 500 m segments in 10BASE5 with 4 repeaters) begin transmitting simultaneously.

The one-way propagation delay for 10BASE5 (coax) is:

$$T_{prop,one-way} = \dfrac{d_{max}}{v_{prop}} = \dfrac{2500 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 12.5 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the round-trip propagation time.**

A signal must travel to the far end and a collision signal must return:

$$T_{prop,RT} = 2 \times T_{prop,one-way} = 2 \times 12.5 \text{ µs} = 25 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 3 — Account for repeater and transceiver delays.**

IEEE 802.3 specifies the worst-case path as 25.6 µs (round-trip), broken down as:
- 4 repeaters × 0.5 µs each = 2.0 µs
- 5 cable segments × ~4.6 µs each = 23.0 µs
- Transceiver/MAU delays ≈ 0.6 µs
- **Total = 25.6 µs (round-trip)**, called the **"slot time"**

**Step 4 — Define the slot time in bit-times.**

At 10 Mbps, the bit time is:

$$T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{1}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 100 \text{ ns}$$

The slot time in bit-times is:

$$T_{slot} = \dfrac{25.6 \text{ µs}}{100 \text{ ns/bit}} = 256 \text{ bit-times}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the minimum frame size.**

The minimum frame must be at least 2 × slot time (round-trip) in length to ensure collision detection:

$$L_{min} = 2 \times T_{slot} = 2 \times 256 \text{ bits} = 512 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\boxed{L_{min,802.3} = 512 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bytes}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for Fast/Gigabit Ethernet (100 Mbps, 1 Gbps):** To preserve the same maximum network diameter of 2500 m, the slot time would have to scale to 2.56 µs, requiring a minimum frame of 512 bits *only* if the network diameter is reduced. Hence, full-duplex switched Ethernet (no CSMA/CD) was adopted for 100 Mbps and beyond.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frame Transmission Time Calculation

A practical and frequently-asked question in KTU exams: *Calculate the transmission time of an Ethernet frame on a 100 Mbps network.*

### Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- Frame size: $L_{frame} = 1518$ bytes (maximum IEEE 802.3 frame)
- Data rate: $R = 100$ Mbps
- Preamble + SFD: 8 bytes (typically included in transmission time)
- IFG (Inter-Frame Gap): 12 bytes

**Step 1 — Convert frame size to bits.**

$$L_{bits} = L_{bytes} \times 8 = 1518 \times 8 = 12{,}144 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the on-the-wire transmission time.**

$$T_{tx} = \dfrac{L_{bits}}{R} = \dfrac{12{,}144 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 121.44 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 3 — Add Inter-Frame Gap (IFG) of 96 bit-times.**

$$T_{IFG} = 96 \times T_{bit} = 96 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 960 \text{ ns} = 0.96 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 4 — Total cycle time per frame.**

$$T_{total} = T_{tx} + T_{IFG} = 121.44 + 0.96 = 122.4 \text{ µs}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} \approx 122.4 \text{ µs per max-size frame at 100 Mbps}}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum number of frames per second (pps).**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{122.4 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 8170 \text{ frames/s}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput Efficiency of an Ethernet Frame

**Given:** Payload of 1500 bytes, total on-wire size = 1518 bytes (excluding preamble).

**Step 1 — Express efficiency.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{frame}} = \dfrac{1500}{1518} = 0.9881 = 98.81\%$$

**Step 2 — With preamble (8 B), SFD (1 B) overhead and IFG (12 B):**

$$L_{overhead,total} = 8 + 1 + 12 + 18 = 39 \text{ bytes (preamble, SFD, IFG, header/FCS)}$$

$$L_{total,onwire} = 1500 + 39 = 1539 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\eta_{wire} = \dfrac{1500}{1539} = 0.9747 = 97.47\%$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CSMA/CD Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)

After $i$ collisions, the station waits for a random number of slot times uniformly distributed in:

$$k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{\min(i,10)} - 1\}$$

**Example:** After the 4th collision ($i = 4$):

$$k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 2^4 - 1\} = \{0, 1, \ldots, 15\}$$

So the backoff is $k \times 51.2$ µs, i.e., up to 15 × 51.2 µs = 768 µs.

After 10 collisions, the range is capped at 0 to 1023 slot times. After 16 collisions on 10 Mbps Ethernet, the frame is dropped and an error is reported to the upper layer.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — RS-485 Maximum Cable Length vs. Baud Rate

The empirical relationship for RS-485 is approximately:

$$L_{max} \times R_{baud} \le 10^8 \text{ (units: m × bps)}$$

**For 93.75 kbps (typical PROFIBUS DP):**

$$L_{max} = \dfrac{10^8}{93.75 \times 10^3} \approx 1067 \text{ m (typically specified as 1200 m in practice)}$$

**For 12 Mbps (PROFIBUS DP max):**

$$L_{max} = \dfrac{10^8}{12 \times 10^6} \approx 8.33 \text{ m (typically < 100 m in practice)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real installations, derating factors, stub length, and EMC considerations reduce these numbers further. The PROFIBUS standard specifies 1200 m for 9.6 kbps down to 100 m for 12 Mbps.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Past Year Pattern)

**Question:** A 10BASE-T Ethernet LAN consists of four repeaters in series, each separated by 500 m of cable. The propagation velocity in the cable is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Determine:
(a) The one-way propagation delay across the entire network.
(b) The round-trip delay.
(c) The minimum frame size required to ensure collision detection.
(d) The maximum number of frames per second that can be transmitted at 10 Mbps.

**Solution:**

**(a) One-way propagation delay:**

$$T_{prop,one-way} = \dfrac{4 \times 500 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = \dfrac{2000}{2 \times 10^8} = 10 \text{ µs}$$

**(b) Round-trip delay:**

$$T_{prop,RT} = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ µs}$$

**(c) Minimum frame size (in bits):**

The round-trip delay must be ≥ 1 slot time. At 10 Mbps, $T_{slot} = 51.2$ µs, so:

$$L_{min} = R \times T_{slot} = 10^7 \text{ bps} \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 512 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bytes}$$

**(d) Maximum frames per second (with full 1518-byte frames):**

$$T_{tx} = \dfrac{1518 \times 8}{10^7} = 1214.4 \text{ µs}$$

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{tx}} \approx 823 \text{ frames/s}$$
