# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the IEEE 802.3 standard and explain its relevance to industrial networking. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The **IEEE 802.3 standard**, popularly known as **Ethernet**, is a family of LAN standards defined by the IEEE 802 LAN/MAN Standards Committee. It specifies the **Physical Layer (PHY)** and **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sublayer of the OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2) for **CSMA/CD-based** wired networking over coax, twisted-pair, and fiber optic media.

**Key Specifications:**
- Original data rate: **10 Mbps** (10BASE5, 10BASE-T)
- Modern variants: **100 Mbps (100BASE-TX)**, **1 Gbps (1000BASE-T)**, **10 Gbps (10GBASE-T)**
- Maximum segment length: **100 m** for UTP, up to **40 km** for single-mode fiber
- Minimum frame: **64 bytes**; maximum: **1518 bytes**

**Relevance to PLC/DCS:** IEEE 802.3 is the **physical and data-link foundation** for nearly every modern industrial communication protocol — PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT, Modbus TCP, OPC UA, IEC 61850. It enables **interoperability**, **deterministic Ethernet (TSN)** for hard real-time control, and the use of **standardized IT infrastructure** in plant networks, reducing cost and complexity.

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 pattern]**

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the LLC sublayer (IEEE 802.2) in the IEEE 802 architecture? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The **Logical Link Control (LLC) sublayer**, defined by **IEEE 802.2**, is the upper sublayer of the OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2). It sits **above the MAC sublayer** and provides a **common interface** to upper-layer protocols (such as IP, IPX, CIP, etc.), independent of the underlying MAC technology.

**Functions of IEEE 802.2 LLC:**
1. **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing** of upper-layer protocols using **Service Access Points (SAPs)**.
2. **Flow control** using stop-and-wait or sliding-window mechanisms.
3. **Error detection and recovery notification** to upper layers.
4. **Logical link establishment, maintenance, and termination** between two network nodes.

**Significance:** Because of the LLC, the **same upper-layer protocol (e.g., TCP/IP)** can run transparently over **Ethernet (802.3)**, **Token Ring (802.5)**, **WiFi (802.11)**, or any other IEEE 802 MAC without modification — making it the **"universal glue"** of the IEEE 802 family.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 pattern]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Question on IEEE Standards and Industrial Networking

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OSI 7-layer reference model**. Clearly state which layers are primarily covered by **IEEE 802 standards** and **industrial protocols** like **Modbus TCP** and **PROFINET**. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** Discuss in detail the **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet) standard**, covering:
- Frame format
- CSMA/CD mechanism with Binary Exponential Backoff
- Comparison of 10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, and 1000BASE-T
- Significance in modern industrial Ethernet networks **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. A (a):

**Step 1 — Define OSI:** The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model, standardized as **ISO/IEC 7498-1**, abstracts data communication into **7 hierarchical layers**, each providing services to the layer above and using services from the layer below.

**Step 2 — Layer-wise functions and mappings:**

| Layer | Function | Standard/Protocol |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| 7. Application | User services, process data | Modbus PDU, OPC UA, PROFINET CBA |
| 6. Presentation | Encoding, encryption | TLS, OPC UA binary |
| 5. Session | Dialog control | RPC, RFC 1006 |
| 4. Transport | Reliable delivery | TCP, UDP |
| 3. Network | Routing, IP | IPv4, IPv6 |
| 2. Data Link | Framing, MAC | **IEEE 802.2, IEEE 802.3, IEEE 802.11** |
| 1. Physical | Bit transmission | **IEEE 802.3 PHY, RS-485, Fiber** |

**Step 3 — Mapping Modbus TCP:** Modbus TCP operates on **Layer 7** (Modbus application), **Layer 4** (TCP port 502), **Layer 3** (IP), and **Layers 1–2** (IEEE 802.3).

**Step 4 — Mapping PROFINET:** PROFINET uses **IEEE 802.3** at Layers 1–2, IP at Layer 3, UDP/TCP at Layer 4, and PROFINET IO/IRT at Layer 7.

**Diagram (use the OSI diagram from Section 4.2).**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. A (b):

**Step 1 — IEEE 802.3 Standard Definition:** The **IEEE 802.3** standard, originally published in **1985**, defines Ethernet using **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** at the MAC sublayer.

**Step 2 — Frame Format (with neat diagram):**

| Preamble (7B) | SFD (1B) | Dest MAC (6B) | Src MAC (6B) | Length/Type (2B) | Payload (46–1500B) | FCS (4B) |
|---------------|----------|---------------|--------------|------------------|--------------------|----------|

- **Preamble (7 bytes)**: Alternating 1010... pattern for receiver clock synchronization
- **SFD (1 byte)**: 10101011 — start frame delimiter
- **Destination/Source MAC (6 bytes each)**: 48-bit hardware address
- **Length/Type (2 bytes)**: Payload length (≤ 1500) or EtherType (≥ 1536)
- **Payload (46–1500 bytes)**: User data; padded to 46 bytes if shorter
- **FCS (4 bytes)**: CRC-32 error detection

**Step 3 — CSMA/CD Operation:**

1. Station listens to the medium (Carrier Sense).
2. If idle, transmits.
3. If collision detected, all transmitting stations emit a **jam signal**.
4. Each station waits for a random backoff of $k$ slot times where $k \in [0, 2^{\min(i,10)} - 1]$ and $i$ is the collision count.
5. After 16 failed attempts, the frame is dropped and the error is reported up.

**Step 4 — Comparison of Ethernet Variants:**

| Parameter | 10BASE-T | 100BASE-TX | 1000BASE-T |
|-----------|----------|------------|------------|
| Standard | 802.3i | 802.3u | 802.3ab |
| Data Rate | 10 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 1 Gbps |
| Cable | Cat 3 UTP | Cat 5 UTP | Cat 5e/6 UTP |
| Coding | Manchester | MLT-3 / 4B5B | 5-level PAM / 8B1Q4 |
| Pairs used | 2 | 2 | 4 (all pairs) |
| Max Segment | 100 m | 100 m | 100 m |

**Step 5 — Industrial Significance:**
- All modern **Industrial Ethernet protocols** (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT, Modbus TCP) are built on IEEE 802.3 PHY/MAC.
- With **TSN (IEEE 802.1Qbv, 802.1AS)**, deterministic bounded latency < 100 µs is achievable.
- Replaces proprietary fieldbuses, enabling **IT/OT convergence**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice on Industrial Protocols and TSN

**Q. (a)** Explain the **IEEE 802.11 (WiFi) standard** and its variants. Discuss its **suitability and limitations** for **industrial PLC/DCS** applications. Compare it with **IEEE 802.15.4** used in **WirelessHART** and **ZigBee**. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** With a block diagram, explain the architecture of **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** as defined in the **IEEE 802.1** family. How does TSN enable **deterministic real-time** communication over standard Ethernet, and what are its key standards (802.1AS, 802.1Qbv, 802.1CB)? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. B (a):

**Step 1 — IEEE 802.11 (WiFi) Standard:** Defines wireless LAN (WLAN) operation in **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz** unlicensed ISM bands using **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** with **DIFS, SIFS, ACK, and RTS/CTS** mechanisms.

**Step 2 — Major Variants:**

| Standard | Frequency | Max Rate | Year |
|----------|-----------|----------|------|
| 802.11a | 5 GHz | 54 Mbps | 1999 |
| 802.11b | 2.4 GHz | 11 Mbps | 1999 |
| 802.11g | 2.4 GHz | 54 Mbps | 2003 |
| 802.11n (WiFi 4) | 2.4/5 GHz | 600 Mbps | 2009 |
| 802.11ac (WiFi 5) | 5 GHz | 6.93 Gbps | 2013 |
| 802.11ax (WiFi 6) | 2.4/5/6 GHz | 9.6 Gbps | 2019 |
| 802.11be (WiFi 7) | 2.4/5/6 GHz | 46 Gbps | 2024 |

**Step 3 — Suitability for Industrial PLC/DCS:**
- ✅ Wireless HMI, AGVs, mobile workers, plant-wide WiFi
- ✅ Easy to deploy, mature ecosystem
- ❌ **Not deterministic** — CSMA/CA contention causes variable latency
- ❌ Susceptible to **multipath fading, interference, metal reflection** in industrial environments
- ❌ Limited suitability for **hard real-time** control loops

**Step 4 — IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee/WirelessHART):**

| Parameter | IEEE 802.11 (WiFi) | IEEE 802.15.4 |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Frequency | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 2.4 GHz, 868/915 MHz |
| Max Data Rate | 600 Mbps to 9.6 Gbps | 250 kbps |
| Range | 50–200 m | 10–100 m |
| Power | High | Very low |
| Topology | Star, Mesh | Star, Mesh |
| Determinism | No (CSMA/CA) | Yes (TSCH in 802.15.4e) |
| Use Case | HMI, video, IT | Sensor, process control |

**Step 5 — WirelessHART (IEC 62591):** Built on **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY** with **TSCH (Time-Slotted Channel Hopping)** from **802.15.4e**, providing **deterministic latency, channel hopping for reliability, and 128-bit AES security**. Used widely in process industries.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. B (b):

**Step 1 — TSN Definition:** Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) is a collection of **IEEE 802.1 standards** that add **deterministic, time-bounded, and low-latency** capabilities to standard IEEE 802.3 Ethernet. It is the **foundation of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** and **Industry 4.0** networks.

**Step 2 — TSN Architecture (Block Diagram):**

```
[Time Source (Grandmaster)]
        |
   802.1AS (gPTP)
        |
[TSN End Station] <---> [TSN Bridge] <---> [TSN Bridge] <---> [TSN End Station]
        |                       |                          |
  802.1Qbv TAS           802.1Qci PSFP                802.1CB FRER
  (Scheduled)            (Policing)                    (Replication)
```

**Step 3 — Key TSN Standards:**

| Standard | Function | Benefit |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **IEEE 802.1AS** | Generalized Precision Time Protocol (gPTP) | Sub-µs clock synchronization across network |
| **IEEE 802.1Qbv** | Time-Aware Shaper (TAS) | Scheduled gates for traffic classes, guarantees bounded latency |
| **IEEE 802.1Qbu** | Frame Preemption | Express frames interrupt preemptable ones |
| **IEEE 802.1CB** | Frame Replication and Elimination (FRER) | Zero-loss redundancy |
| **IEEE 802.1Qcc** | Stream Reservation Protocol (SRP) enhancements | Dynamic resource allocation for time-critical streams |
| **IEEE 802.1Qci** | Per-Stream Filtering and Policing | Prevents fault propagation |
| **IEEE 802.1Qcr** | Asynchronous Traffic Shaping (ATS) | Low-latency for non-scheduled streams |

**Step 4 — How TSN Achieves Determinism:**

- All TSN nodes share a **common time base** via **802.1AS** (gPTP), with sub-microsecond accuracy.
- The **Time-Aware Shaper (802.1Qbv)** divides the transmission cycle into **time slots**, dedicating specific slots to time-critical traffic.
- Critical frames are queued and transmitted only during their assigned **time gate windows**, ensuring **bounded worst-case latency** (often < 100 µs).
- **Frame preemption (802.1Qbu)** allows a high-priority frame to interrupt a low-priority one mid-transmission.
- **FRER (802.1CB)** duplicates critical frames across disjoint paths and eliminates duplicates at the receiver — achieving **zero-loss** under single-link failures.

**Step 5 — Application in Industry:**
- **PROFINET over TSN** (PI's Profile for TSN)
- **EtherNet/IP with CIP Motion + TSN**
- **OPC UA Pub/Sub over TSN** (the unified IIoT protocol)
- **IEEE/IEC 60802** — joint profile for Industrial Automation TSN

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mapping mistakes:** A very common error is to assign TCP/IP to **Layer 1 or 2** — TCP is Layer 4, IP is Layer 3, and the IEEE 802 standards cover only Layers 1 and 2.
> 2. **Confusing Ethernet variants:** Students often write "1000BASE-T" for fiber or "100BASE-FX" for copper. Always match the suffix (**T = Twisted Pair, F/SX/LX = Fiber**).
> 3. **Minimum frame size misuse:** The 64-byte minimum is for **half-duplex CSMA/CD only** — full-duplex switched Ethernet has no such constraint. Do not over-apply this rule to modern full-duplex networks.
> 4. **TSN ≠ real-time Ethernet:** TSN is a *suite of IEEE 802.1 standards*, not a single protocol. PROFINET IRT, EtherCAT, and similar are *pre-TSN* real-time Ethernet; TSN standardizes real-time Ethernet across vendors.
> 5. **RS-485 ≠ RS-232:** RS-485 is **differential and multi-drop** (up to 32 nodes); RS-232 is **single-ended and point-to-point**. Conflating these two loses marks.
> 6. **Bit time vs. Byte time confusion:** In all derived formulas, ensure $L$ is in **bits** when $R$ is in **bits/second**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- 📌 **IEEE 802 family** = LAN/MAN standards covering Layers 1 and 2 of the OSI model.
- 📌 **IEEE 802.3** = Ethernet (CSMA/CD), the dominant industrial wired standard.
- 📌 **IEEE 802.11** = WiFi (CSMA/CA), used for wireless HMI and AGVs.
- 📌 **IEEE 802.15.4** = Foundation for ZigBee, WirelessHART, ISA100.11a (industrial wireless sensors).
- 📌 **IEEE 802.1Qbv** = Time-Aware Shaper — the cornerstone of TSN determinism.
- 📌 **IEEE 802.1AS** = gPTP time synchronization for TSN.
- 📌 **CSMA/CD** minimum frame size = **64 bytes (512 bits)** at 10 Mbps.
- 📌 **Slot time** at 10 Mbps = **51.2 µs (512 bit-times)**.
- 📌 **100BASE-TX** max segment = **100 m** over Cat 5 UTP.
- 📌 **RS-485** supports up to **32 nodes, 1200 m @ 93.75 kbps**.
- 📌 **IEEE 802.2 LLC** sits above all IEEE 802 MACs, providing a common interface to upper layers.
- 📌 **PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT, Modbus TCP** all use **IEEE 802.3** at Layers 1–2.
- 📌 **TSN** = collection of IEEE 802.1 standards enabling **bounded latency < 100 µs** over standard Ethernet.
- 📌 **Industrial Ethernet** = IEEE 802.3 + real-time extensions (TSN, IRT, CIP Sync, etc.).
- 📌 **MTU** of standard IEEE 802.3 = **1500 bytes** payload, **1518 bytes** total.
- 📌 **MAC address** = **48 bits (6 bytes)**, globally unique per device.
- 📌 **5-4-3 Rule** (legacy 10BASE5): 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 with end nodes.
- 📌 **Purdue Model** maps enterprise (L4–L5) → operations (L3) → supervisory (L2) → basic control (L1) → process (L0) — each with its own networking standard.
- 📌 **GPIB (IEEE 488)** = 8-bit parallel, 15 devices max, used in lab/DCS test instrumentation.
- 📌 **Industrial temperature range** = **-40 °C to +85 °C** (IEC 60068).
- 📌 **Frame transmission time** $T_{frame} = L \times 8 / R$ (always convert bytes to bits).
- 📌 **Cycle time** for PROFINET IRT ≈ **31.25 µs**; for EtherCAT ≈ **12.5 µs**.
- 📌 **WirelessHART** uses **TSCH** from **802.15.4e** for deterministic channel hopping.
- 📌 **OPC UA over TSN** = the de-facto **IIoT protocol** of the next decade.
- 📌 **IEC 62443** is the leading industrial cybersecurity standard to secure PLC/DCS networks.
- 📌 **Key design intent:** IEEE standards are *open, vendor-neutral, consensus-driven* — they enable **interoperability** in heterogeneous industrial networks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocols: Ethernet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Protocols: Ethernet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: PROTOCOLS — ETHERNET

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Ethernet* is a family of wired computer networking technologies defined by the **IEEE 802.3** standard that specifies the physical layer (PHY) and the Media Access Control (MAC) sub-layer of the data link layer in the OSI model. In the context of Distributed Control Systems (DCS), Ethernet serves as the **backbone communication protocol** for high-speed data exchange between PLCs, SCADA servers, HMI stations, and field instruments using the **CSMA/CD** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) mechanism.

### Key Standard Identifiers
- **IEEE 802.3** — Standard for wired Ethernet (original baseband, 10 Mbps).
- **IEEE 802.3u** — Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps).
- **IEEE 802.3ab / 802.3z** — Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps over copper / fiber).
- **IEEE 802.3ae** — 10 Gigabit Ethernet.
- **IEEE 802.1Q** — VLAN tagging for industrial network segmentation.

---

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy: "The Polite Conversation Rule"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **conference room with one shared microphone** where many engineers (devices) want to speak, but only **one person can talk at a time**:
>
> 1. **Carrier Sense (CS)** — Before speaking, each engineer *listens* to check whether someone is already talking on the wire.
> 2. **Multiple Access (MA)** — Every device has *equal right* to use the medium; there is no central "boss" granting permission.
> 3. **Collision Detection (CD)** — If two engineers accidentally speak at the *exact same instant* (collision), they both immediately *stop*, wait a *random* short time, and try again.
>
> This is exactly how **CSMA/CD** works on classical Ethernet — devices share the bus politely, listen first, and gracefully recover from collisions. In modern **full-duplex switched Ethernet**, the microphone metaphor breaks down because every device has its *own private channel*, making collisions physically impossible.

### Real-World Significance in DCS
Ethernet evolved from an office LAN technology into the **dominant industrial communication backbone** because it offers:
- **High bandwidth** (10 Mbps → 10 Gbps progression).
- **Low cost** of NICs, switches, and CAT-5e/6/7 cabling.
- **Interoperability** with the global **TCP/IP** suite, enabling remote SCADA, IIoT, and cloud integration.
- **Scalability** from small machines to plant-wide DCS networks.

### Physical Layer Variants — Naming Convention

The naming convention **`XBaseY`** decodes as:
- **X** = Nominal data rate in **Mbps**.
- **Base** = Baseband signaling (single frequency band, no modulation).
- **Y** = Cable type or maximum segment length.

| Notation | Rate | Cable Type | Max Segment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **10Base5** | 10 Mbps | Thick Coax ("Yellow Cable") | 500 m |
| **10Base2** | 10 Mbps | Thin Coax ("CheaperNet") | 185 m |
| **10Base-T** | 10 Mbps | Twisted Pair (CAT-3/5) | 100 m |
| **100Base-TX** | 100 Mbps | CAT-5 UTP | 100 m |
| **1000Base-T** | 1 Gbps | CAT-5e/6 UTP | 100 m |
| **10GBase-T** | 10 Gbps | CAT-6a/7 UTP | 100 m |
| **1000Base-LX** | 1 Gbps | Single-mode Fiber | 5–10 km |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CSMA/CD Operational Logic (Classical Half-Duplex Ethernet)

The IEEE 802.3 MAC sub-layer implements **CSMA/CD** using the following structured procedure:

1. **Frame Ready:** The upper layer hands a data packet to the MAC sub-layer.
2. **Carrier Sense:** The MAC checks the physical medium for an ongoing carrier signal.
   - *If idle* → Proceed to transmission.
   - *If busy* → Wait until medium goes idle, then start a **96-bit Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)** delay before transmitting.
3. **Transmit & Monitor:** The station transmits the frame **bit-serially** onto the medium **while simultaneously monitoring** for voltage anomalies on the cable.
4. **Collision Window:** A collision is *guaranteed to be detected* within a time window of **2 × t_prop** (round-trip propagation), where t_prop is the one-way propagation delay across the maximum network span.
5. **Collision Detection:** If voltage exceeds the normal signal envelope, a collision is declared.
6. **Jam Signal:** The transmitter sends a **32-bit jam sequence** to *force* all other stations to recognize the collision.
7. **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):** Both colliding stations compute a random wait:
   - Draw a random integer **k** from the set $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{n}-1\}$, where **n = min(collision_count, 10)**.
   - Wait **k × 512 bit-times**, then attempt retransmission.
8. **Drop Frame:** After **16 consecutive collisions**, the frame is discarded and an error is reported to upper layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 512 bit-times multiplier?** The slot time for 10 Mbps Ethernet equals **51.2 µs** (i.e., 512 bit-times). This fixed quantum ensures all stations are synchronized to a common scheduling unit that is at least as long as the round-trip propagation delay for the maximum legal network diameter.

---

## 2.2 The 802.3 MAC Frame Architecture

An IEEE 802.3 frame has the following exact byte structure:

| Field | Size (Bytes) | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Preamble** | 7 | Alternating 1010... pattern for clock synchronization. |
| **SFD (Start Frame Delimiter)** | 1 | Marks the start of frame (`10101011`). |
| **Destination MAC Address** | 6 | Hardware address of the receiver. |
| **Source MAC Address** | 6 | Hardware address of the sender. |
| **Length / Type** | 2 | Either payload length (≤ 1500) OR EtherType (≥ 1536). |
| **Payload (LLC + Data)** | 46 to 1500 | Carries the user data. |
| **Pad** | 0 to 46 | Padding to enforce 64-byte minimum. |
| **FCS (Frame Check Sequence)** | 4 | **CRC-32** for error detection. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Frame Size Rule:** The frame *must* be **≥ 64 bytes** (excluding preamble/SFD) to ensure collision detection works. Maximum is **1518 bytes** (excluding preamble/SFD).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 2018/2024 scheme university exam question on Ethernet.

| Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bit Time** | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Duration of one bit at data rate $R$. |
| **Slot Time** | $T_{slot} = 512 \times T_b$ | Equals 51.2 µs for 10 Mbps. |
| **Min Frame Size** | $L_{min} = 2 \times t_{prop} \times R$ | Set to 64 bytes in IEEE 802.3. |
| **Max Network Span** | $d_{max} = \dfrac{v \times L_{min}}{2 R}$ | One-way physical reach before collision window breaks. |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | Where $a = \dfrac{t_{prop}}{T_{frame}}$. |
| **Throughput** | $S = \eta \times R$ | Actual useful data rate delivered. |
| **Backoff Delay** | $T_{backoff} = k \times T_{slot}$ | $k \in \{0, \ldots, 2^{n}-1\}$. |
| **Round Trip Time** | $T_{RTT} = 2 \times t_{prop} = \dfrac{2 d}{v}$ | Time for signal to travel to farthest node and back. |
| **Signal Speed (Coax/TP)** | $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | Roughly 0.67 × c (speed of light). |
| **Jamming Time** | $T_{jam} = 32 \times T_b$ | Duration of jam signal = 3.2 µs @ 10 Mbps. |
| **IFG (Inter-Frame Gap)** | $T_{IFG} = 96 \times T_b$ | = 9.6 µs @ 10 Mbps. |
| **5-4-3 Repeater Rule** | 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated | Limits max collision domain diameter. |

---

## 2.4 Why These Matter in Real Industrial Plants

- **Efficiency $\eta$** dictates how much of the theoretical bandwidth is *usable* for I/O scans. A heavily loaded Ethernet with long cables can drop below **30% efficiency**, which is unacceptable for closed-loop control.
- **$T_{RTT}$** dictates the **determinism** of the network. Long propagation times mean longer worst-case latencies — critical for motion control and interlock logic in DCS.
- **5-4-3 Rule** was a hard constraint in shared 10Base5/10Base2 coaxial plants; modern switched Ethernet **eliminates** this constraint.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every step below is fully worked out. KTU examiners reward **explicit algebraic transitions** — do not skip any line in your answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Frame Size From Collision Detection Constraint

**Problem Statement:** Derive the minimum Ethernet frame size required for the sender to detect a collision before it finishes transmitting. Given:
- Maximum one-way propagation delay $t_{prop} = 25.6\ \mu s$ (for a 10Base5 network).
- Data rate $R = 10\ Mbps$.

**Step 1 — Define the collision detection constraint geometrically.**

For the sender to be *guaranteed* to detect a collision, the frame transmission time $T_{frame}$ must be **at least equal to the round-trip propagation time** $2 \cdot t_{prop}$:

$$
T_{frame} \geq 2 \cdot t_{prop}
$$

**Why?** Consider the *worst case*: Station A begins transmitting. Just before its signal reaches Station B (the farthest node), Station B also begins transmitting. The two signals collide. The collision fragment must propagate *back* to Station A within the duration of A's transmission — otherwise A finishes sending and falsely assumes success.

**Step 2 — Express the frame transmission time.**

If the frame has $L$ bits and the data rate is $R$ bits/second:

$$
T_{frame} = \dfrac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for L.**

$$
\dfrac{L}{R} \geq 2 \cdot t_{prop} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L \geq 2 \cdot t_{prop} \cdot R
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in the numerical values.**

$$
L \geq 2 \times (25.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
L \geq 2 \times 25.6 \times 10 = 512\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to bytes.**

$$
L_{min} = \dfrac{512}{8} = 64\ \text{bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly why IEEE 802.3 mandates a **64-byte minimum frame size** (excluding preamble and SFD). The 64 bytes contain 6 (DA) + 6 (SA) + 2 (Length) + 46 (Payload minimum) + 4 (FCS) = 64 bytes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Network Span (Cable Length) for 10Base5 Ethernet

**Given:**
- $L_{min} = 512\ \text{bits}$ (the standard minimum).
- $R = 10\ Mbps$.
- $v = 2 \times 10^{8}\ m/s$ (signal propagation speed in coax).

**Step 1 — Reverse the previous inequality to solve for $t_{prop}$.**

$$
t_{prop} \leq \dfrac{L_{min}}{2 R}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the kinematic relation $t_{prop} = d / v$.**

$$
\dfrac{d_{max}}{v} \leq \dfrac{L_{min}}{2 R}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d_{max}$.**

$$
d_{max} \leq \dfrac{v \cdot L_{min}}{2 R}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
d_{max} \leq \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{8}) \times 512}{2 \times (10 \times 10^{6})}
$$

$$
d_{max} \leq \dfrac{1.024 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
d_{max} \leq 5120\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Account for repeater latency and safety margins.**

Each repeater introduces roughly 5–7 bit-times of delay. With the 5-4-3 rule allowing up to 4 repeaters and practical safety margins, the **standardized 10Base5 maximum end-to-end span is 2500 m**, not 5120 m.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ethernet Channel Efficiency $\eta$

**Definition:** Efficiency is the ratio of the **frame transmission time** to the **total time the medium is occupied** (which includes transmission time, propagation delays, and IFG).

$$
\eta = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{RTT} + T_{IFG}}
$$

**Step 1 — Introduce the parameter $a$ (the ratio of propagation to transmission time).**

$$
a = \dfrac{t_{prop}}{T_{frame}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite the round-trip time in terms of $a$.**

$$
T_{RTT} = 2 \cdot t_{prop} = 2 a \cdot T_{frame}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the efficiency equation.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + 2 a \cdot T_{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

**Step 4 — Limiting case analysis.**

| Case | $a$ value | $\eta$ | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Long frames, short cable | $a \to 0$ | $\eta \to 1$ | Near-ideal, minimal wasted time. |
| Short frames, long cable | $a \to 1$ | $\eta \to 0.333$ | Wasted time dominates. |
| Worst case | $a = 1$ | 33.3% | Floor efficiency. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important insight for **DCS engineers**: pushing larger frames and shorter cable runs (or using **full-duplex switched Ethernet**) directly increases the bandwidth available for I/O data — which is why industrial plants always specify managed switches for control networks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Ethernet Variants

| Variant | Standard | Rate | Medium | Encoding | Max Span | DCS Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 10Base5 | 802.3 | 10 Mbps | Thick Coax | Manchester | 500 m | Legacy backbone. |
| 10Base2 | 802.3 | 10 Mbps | Thin Coax | Manchester | 185 m | Legacy small cells. |
| 10Base-T | 802.3i | 10 Mbps | CAT-3 UTP | Manchester | 100 m | Lowest-cost links. |
| 100Base-TX | 802.3u | 100 Mbps | CAT-5 UTP | 4B/5B + MLT-3 | 100 m | **Most common in PLC racks.** |
| 1000Base-T | 802.3ab | 1 Gbps | CAT-5e/6 UTP | PAM-5 | 100 m | SCADA server uplinks. |
| 10GBase-T | 802.3an | 10 Gbps | CAT-6a/7 UTP | DSQ-128 | 100 m | Plant backbone aggregation. |
| 100Base-FX | 802.3u | 100 Mbps | Multimode Fiber | 4B/5B + NRZI | 2 km | Noisy EMI plant areas. |
| 1000Base-LX | 802.3z | 1 Gbps | Single-mode Fiber | 8B/10B | 10 km | Long-distance inter-plant. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ethernet MAC Frame — Bit-Level Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Preamble 7B] --> B[SFD 1B]
    B --> C[Destination MAC 6B]
    C --> D[Source MAC 6B]
    D --> E[Length or Type 2B]
    E --> F[Payload 46 to 1500B]
    F --> G[Pad 0 to 46B]
    G --> H[FCS CRC32 4B]
    H --> I[IFG 12B]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG) of 12 bytes = 96 bit-times** sits between consecutive frames to allow receivers to recover clock and process the previous frame. It is *not* part of the frame itself.

---

## 4.2 CSMA/CD State Machine — Collision Handling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1([Start: Frame Ready in TX Buffer]) --> S2{Carrier Sense: Medium Idle?}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Defer and Wait]
    S3 --> S2
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Wait 96 bit IFG]
    S4 --> S5[Begin Bit Transmission]
    S5 --> S6{Collision Detected?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Frame Sent Successfully]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Send 32 bit Jam Signal]
    S8 --> S9[Increment Collision Counter n]
    S9 --> S10{n greater than 16?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Drop Frame Report Error to LLC]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Draw Random k in 0 to 2 to the n minus 1]
    S12 --> S13[Wait k times 512 bit times]
    S13 --> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does backoff use $2^n$ but cap at $n = 10$?** The exponential growth ensures that as the medium becomes congested, the *expected* wait time expands rapidly, distributing retransmission attempts. Capping at 10 prevents indefinite stalls on persistently overloaded networks; after 16 attempts, the MAC surrenders and reports an error to higher layers.

---

## 4.3 OSI Model Placement of Ethernet in a DCS Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L7 ["Application Layer"]
        L7A[Modbus TCP, OPC UA, MQTT, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET IO, HTTP]
    end
    subgraph L4 ["Transport Layer"]
        L4A[TCP for reliable control, UDP for real time]
    end
    subgraph L3 ["Network Layer"]
        L3A[IP Addressing and Routing]
    end
    subgraph L2 ["Data Link Layer LLC sublayer"]
        L2A[IEEE 802.2 LLC]
    end
    subgraph L2M ["Data Link Layer MAC sublayer"]
        L2B[IEEE 802.3 MAC with CSMA CD or Full Duplex]
    end
    subgraph L1 ["Physical Layer"]
        L1A[IEEE 802.3 PHY 10Base T, 100Base TX, 1000Base T, Fiber]
    end
    L7A --> L4A --> L3A --> L2A --> L2B --> L1A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a real DCS, **Ethernet is NOT a single-layer protocol** — it is a *family* spanning PHY and MAC (Layers 1 and 2). Industrial protocols (Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET) ride *on top* of Ethernet inside the TCP/IP stack. This layering is a guaranteed KTU exam question topic.

---

## 4.4 Classical Bus vs Modern Star Topology (Used vs Switched)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OLD["Legacy 10Base2/10Base5 Bus Topology"]
        direction LR
        N1[Station A] --- BUS(("Shared Coaxial Bus"))
        BUS --- N2[Station B]
        BUS --- N3[Station C]
        BUS --- N4[Station D]
    end
    subgraph NEW["Modern Switched Star Topology"]
        direction LR
        SW[Managed Industrial Switch]
        SW --- X1[PLC 1]
        SW --- X2[PLC 2]
        SW --- X3[SCADA Server]
        SW --- X4[HMI Station]
        SW --- X5[Remote IO Station]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The shift from **shared bus** to **switched star** is the single biggest reason Ethernet succeeded in industry. In a bus, every device competes for the same medium (CSMA/CD required). In a switched star, every port is a *private full-duplex channel* — CSMA/CD becomes obsolete, collisions are physically impossible, and the network becomes essentially deterministic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students most commonly lose marks by (1) **forgetting to add Preamble, SFD, and IFG** when computing total on-wire bytes; (2) **confusing min frame (64 B) with max frame (1518 B)**; (3) **using speed of light $c$ instead of $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s** for coax propagation; (4) **writing the efficiency formula as $\frac{1}{1+a}$ instead of $\frac{1}{1+2a}$**. Memorize the constants and the $2a$ factor!

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the CSMA/CD protocol used in IEEE 802.3 Ethernet. Why is a minimum frame size enforced?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CSMA/CD stands for **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection**. It is the Media Access Control mechanism used in classical half-duplex Ethernet (IEEE 802.3). Its operation proceeds as follows:

1. A station that wishes to transmit **first senses the carrier** on the medium. If the medium is idle, it waits for the 96-bit Inter-Frame Gap (IFG) and begins transmitting.
2. The station **continues to monitor the medium** during transmission for voltage anomalies that indicate a collision.
3. If a collision is detected, all transmitting stations immediately cease transmission and send a **32-bit jam signal** to reinforce the collision event.
4. Each station then waits a **random backoff time** (Binary Exponential Backoff) and attempts retransmission.

**Why a minimum frame size?** A minimum frame size of **64 bytes (512 bits)** is enforced so that the frame transmission time is always **longer than the round-trip propagation delay** across the maximum legal network diameter. This guarantees that the *sending* station is still transmitting when a collision-induced voltage spike returns from the farthest node, ensuring the collision is *detected* before the sender mistakenly believes the transmission succeeded. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between IEEE 802.3 10Base5, 10Base2, and 10Base-T Ethernet standards. Mention their cable type and maximum segment length.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Standard | Cable Type | Maximum Segment Length |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **10Base5** | Thick coaxial cable (≈ 10 mm dia, "Yellow Cable") | **500 m** |
| **10Base2** | Thin coaxial cable (≈ 5 mm dia, "CheaperNet") | **185 m** (≈ 200 m) |
| **10Base-T** | Twisted Pair (CAT-3 / Voice-grade UTP) | **100 m** |

All three operate at **10 Mbps** using **baseband** Manchester encoding. 10Base5 and 10Base2 use a linear bus topology with terminators at each end, whereas 10Base-T uses a star topology centered on a hub or switch. (3 marks)

---

## 📗 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Pattern]

**A.** *(a)* Draw the IEEE 802.3 MAC frame format and explain the function of **Preamble, SFD, Length/Type, and FCS** fields. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a 10Base5 Ethernet network with a maximum segment length of **2500 m** and a signal propagation speed of $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, calculate:
  (i) The **round-trip propagation delay**.
  (ii) The **minimum frame size** required to guarantee collision detection.
  (iii) The **channel efficiency** for a 1500-byte data frame. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Frame Format (3 marks) + Field Functions (4 marks):**

The IEEE 802.3 MAC frame structure is shown below:

| Field | Size | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Preamble** | 7 bytes | A repeating pattern of `10101010` that allows the receiver's PLL to synchronize its clock with the incoming bit stream. |
| **SFD (Start Frame Delimiter)** | 1 byte | The pattern `10101011` flags the **start** of the actual frame; the receiver begins reading subsequent bits as destination address. |
| **Length / Type** | 2 bytes | If value ≤ 1500 → indicates length of payload. If value ≥ 1536 → indicates the **EtherType** (e.g., 0x0800 = IPv4, 0x0806 = ARP). |
| **FCS (Frame Check Sequence)** | 4 bytes | A **CRC-32** checksum computed over DA + SA + Length + Payload; the receiver recomputes it and discards the frame on mismatch. |

**Additional fields** (for full credit, mention): Destination MAC (6B), Source MAC (6B), Payload (46–1500 B), Pad (0–46 B).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 2500$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $R = 10 \times 10^{6}$ bps.

**(i) Round-trip propagation delay:**

$$
t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{2500}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 12.5\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{RTT} = 2 \times t_{prop} = 2 \times 12.5 = 25\ \mu s
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Minimum frame size:**

$$
L_{min} = 2 \times t_{prop} \times R = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
L_{min} = 250\ \text{bits} \approx 31.25\ \text{bytes}
$$

Since IEEE 802.3 mandates a **standardized minimum of 64 bytes (512 bits)** to provide safety margin, the *enforced* minimum frame size is **64 bytes**.

> [!NOTE]
> The calculated theoretical minimum (250 bits) is *smaller* than the standardized value (512 bits). The extra 262 bits are a **design margin** to absorb repeater delays, safety against ground loops, and clock skew across the entire 5-4-3 rule-compliant network. **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Channel efficiency for a 1500-byte frame:**

First, compute $T_{frame}$:

$$
T_{frame} = \dfrac{1500 \times 8}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 1200\ \mu s
$$

Then compute $a$:

$$
a = \dfrac{t_{prop}}{T_{frame}} = \dfrac{12.5\ \mu s}{1200\ \mu s} \approx 0.01042
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2(0.01042)} = \dfrac{1}{1.02083}
$$

$$
\eta \approx 0.9796 \approx 97.96\%
$$

**(2 marks)** — This high efficiency is why **large frames** are preferred on busy Ethernet segments.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**B.** *(a)* Explain the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm used in IEEE 802.3 after a collision. Why is the maximum collision attempt limited to 16? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 100Base-TX Ethernet segment has a maximum cable length of 100 m and uses CAT-5 UTP with propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.
  (i) Calculate the **slot time** and verify that it exceeds the round-trip propagation delay.
  (ii) Compute the **maximum theoretical efficiency** for a 64-byte minimum frame.
  (iii) If the same network is upgraded to **full-duplex switched Ethernet**, what happens to the efficiency? Justify. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Binary Exponential Backoff (7 marks):**

When a collision is detected, all transmitting stations follow the **BEB algorithm**:
1. Each station sets a **collision counter** $n$ (initialized to 0 after every successful transmission).
2. After the *first* collision, $n = 1$ → the station draws a random integer $k$ from $\{0, 1\}$ and waits $k \times 512$ bit-times.
3. After the $n$-th collision, $k$ is drawn from $\{0, 1, \ldots, 2^{\min(n,10)} - 1\}$ and the wait is $k \times 512$ bit-times.
4. The cap at $n = 10$ means the maximum wait is $1023 \times 512$ bit-times ≈ 52 ms.
5. After **16 consecutive collisions**, the frame is **abandoned** and the MAC reports a transmit error to the upper layer (LLC).

**Why limit to 16 attempts?** (a) Beyond ~10 retries, the network is almost certainly *broken* (cable fault, jabbering NIC, duplex mismatch, or extreme congestion). (b) Continuing to retry wastes bandwidth and risks system-wide throughput collapse. (c) The upper layer can apply its own *application-level* retry policy. (d) It bounds the worst-case latency of the MAC, which is critical for time-sensitive industrial traffic.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R = 100$ Mbps, $d = 100$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**(i) Slot time and RTT:**

Bit time:

$$
T_b = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{1}{10^{8}} = 10\ ns
$$

Slot time:

$$
T_{slot} = 512 \times T_b = 5120\ ns = 5.12\ \mu s
$$

Round-trip propagation delay:

$$
T_{RTT} = \dfrac{2 d}{v} = \dfrac{2 \times 100}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 1\ \mu s
$$

Verification: $T_{slot} = 5.12\ \mu s > T_{RTT} = 1\ \mu s$ ✅

**Conclusion:** The slot time exceeds the round-trip delay, so collisions are reliably detected before the 64-byte minimum frame finishes transmitting. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Maximum theoretical efficiency for 64-byte frame:**

$T_{frame}$ for 64 bytes:

$$
T_{frame} = \dfrac{64 \times 8}{10^{8}} = 5.12\ \mu s
$$

Parameter $a$:

$$
a = \dfrac{t_{prop}}{T_{frame}} = \dfrac{0.5\ \mu s}{5.12\ \mu s} \approx 0.0977
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2(0.0977)} = \dfrac{1}{1.1953} \approx 0.8367
$$

$$
\eta \approx 83.67\%
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Effect of full-duplex switched upgrade:**

In **full-duplex switched Ethernet**, each link between a station and the switch is a **private, collision-free, point-to-point channel**. CSMA/CD is **disabled**, so the $2 \cdot t_{prop}$ wasted time **vanishes**. The efficiency is governed only by the physical encoding and IFG:

$$
\eta_{full-duplex} \to 1\ (\approx 97\text{–}99\%\ \text{with IFG})
$$

The 5-4-3 repeater rule is **also eliminated** — every station gets the full 100 Mbps in *both* directions simultaneously. This is why modern DCS networks use **managed industrial switches** to achieve deterministic, near-lossless communication. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> High-density summary for last-mile revision before KTU ESE. Memorize this verbatim.

- **Ethernet = IEEE 802.3** — PHY + MAC of the OSI Layer 1 and 2.
- **CSMA/CD** — Listen → Transmit → Detect → Jam → Backoff.
- **Minimum frame = 64 bytes; Maximum frame = 1518 bytes** (excluding Preamble + SFD).
- **Slot time = 512 bit-times = 51.2 µs** at 10 Mbps; **5.12 µs** at 100 Mbps; **0.512 µs** at 1 Gbps.
- **Min frame derivation:** $L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_{prop} \cdot R = 512$ bits.
- **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = \dfrac{t_{prop}}{T_{frame}}$.
- **Signal speed in cable:** $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (NOT $3 \times 10^{8}$).
- **5-4-3 rule** applies *only* to shared coaxial Ethernet — obsolete in switched networks.
- **Backoff multiplier:** $k$ drawn from $\{0, \ldots, 2^{\min(n,10)}-1\}$; cap at 16 retries.
- **Naming convention:** `XBaseY` → X = rate in Mbps, Y = cable/length code.
- **Industrial protocols over Ethernet:** Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, OPC UA, MQTT.
- **Full-duplex switched Ethernet = no collisions, no CSMA/CD, no efficiency loss from propagation.**
- **Preamble (7B) + SFD (1B) + IFG (12B)** are NOT part of the official frame byte count.
- **CRC-32** is used for the FCS; errors cause frame discard at the receiver.

---

✅ *End of Module 2 — Protocols: Ethernet Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PROFIBUS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PROFIBUS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**PROFIBUS** (Process Field Bus) is an open, vendor-independent, standardized fieldbus communication protocol widely used in industrial automation. It is defined under the international standards **IEC 61158** and **IEC 61784**, and is governed by the **PROFIBUS Nutzerorganisation e.V. (PNO)**, headquartered in Germany.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, PROFIBUS is positioned as a **real-time, deterministic, digital serial communication system** used to interconnect distributed field devices (sensors, actuators, drives, valves, PLCs, and DCS controllers) over a single shared physical medium, replacing the traditional 4–20 mA analog point-to-point wiring.

The PROFIBUS family consists of three primary variants, each optimized for specific application domains:

| Variant | Full Form | Primary Application | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PROFIBUS DP** | Decentralized Periphery | High-speed discrete and process I/O between controllers (PLCs/DCS) and remote I/O | **Highest** |
| **PROFIBUS PA** | Process Automation | Intrinsically safe communication in hazardous (Ex) areas, typically for process instrumentation | High |
| **PROFIBUS FMS** | Fieldbus Message Specification | General-purpose data communication between controllers (largely obsolete, replaced by PROFINET) | Low (Historical context only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Out of the three variants, **PROFIBUS DP** is the most heavily tested variant in university examinations. Questions on token passing, master-slave communication, RS-485 physical layer, and DP-V0/V1/V2 cyclic services are considered **high-frequency topics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context for Examiners:** PROFIBUS was originally developed in **1989** as part of a German government-funded research project (BMFT), and the first standard was published as **DIN 19245**. It was later harmonized internationally as **EN 50170** and is now part of the **IEC 61158** global fieldbus standard.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multilingual international conference** where delegates from different countries (devices from different vendors like Siemens, ABB, Schneider, Emerson) need to communicate. To make this work, the organizers establish three things:

1. **A common spoken language** (this is the **PROFIBUS Protocol** — IEC 61158).
2. **A strict rule about who speaks and when** (this is the **Token Passing + Master-Slave** access control method).
3. **A physical conference table with a microphone system** (this is the **RS-485 twisted-pair cable** with terminating resistors at both ends).

In this analogy:
- The **Master** is the chairperson who holds the talking stick (Token) and decides when each delegate may speak.
- The **Slaves** are the delegates who only speak when called upon by the chair.
- The **Token** is passed sequentially from one master to the next in a logical ring, ensuring fair and **deterministic** access to the bus.

If you replace the conference room with a factory floor, the delegates with sensors (temperature, pressure, flow), and the chairperson with a PLC or DCS controller — you have a working **PROFIBUS network**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Terminology for KTU 2024 Answer Scripts:**

- **Master (Active Station):** A node that has bus access rights in a defined time window, controls message cycles, and can send unsolicited messages. Class 1 Master (DP-Master) handles cyclic I/O; Class 2 Master is for commissioning/diagnostics.
- **Slave (Passive Station):** A peripheral device (valve, drive, I/O block) that only responds when polled by a master; it never initiates communication.
- **Token:** A special 3-byte telegram (`SD4`, `DA`, `SA` in addressing mode) that grants the right to access the bus for a maximum **Token Holding Time (THT)**.
- **GSD File (General Station Description):** An electronic device datasheet (ASCII or GSDML) that describes a slave's communication parameters, I/O configuration, and supported baud rates.
- **Cyclic Service:** Deterministic, time-bounded, periodic data exchange (used in DP-V0).
- **Acyclic Service:** Non-periodic, event-driven communication used for alarms, diagnostics, and parameterization (used in DP-V1/V2).
- **Baud Rate:** The signalling rate on the physical layer; PROFIBUS DP supports from **9.6 kbps to 12 Mbps**, with common values being **1.5 Mbps** and **12 Mbps**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state the variant (DP/PA/FMS) and the version (V0/V1/V2) in your answer. Saying simply "PROFIBUS" without the variant in a 14-mark question is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** by strict evaluators.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standardized by the PROFIBUS standard and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Maximum number of devices per segment:** **32 stations** (including repeaters).
- **Maximum number of devices per network:** **126 stations** (using 4 repeaters/segments).
- **Maximum segment length (RS-485):** From **100 m at 12 Mbps** to **1200 m at 9.6 kbps** (inverse linear relationship).
- **Standard baud rates (in bps):** **9.6 k, 19.2 k, 45.45 k, 93.75 k, 187.5 k, 500 k, 1.5 M, 3 M, 6 M, 12 M**.
- **Frame overhead:** Each PROFIBUS DP frame has a minimum overhead of **9 bytes** (for short acyclic frames) up to **255 bytes** (for cyclic data with diagnostic fields).
- **PROFIBUS DP cycle time:** Typically **1 ms to 10 ms** at 1.5 Mbps for standard PLC applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PROFIBUS Protocol Architecture (OSI Mapping)

Although PROFIBUS DP does **not** implement all 7 layers of the OSI model (to achieve real-time performance), it follows a streamlined 3-layer architecture (sometimes called the **"Enhanced Performance Architecture"** or **EPA**):

| OSI Layer | PROFIBUS DP Implementation | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 — Application | **DP-V0 / DP-V1 / DP-V2** user interface | Defines cyclic/acyclic services, alarms, diagnostics |
| Layer 2 — Data Link | **FDL (Fieldbus Data Link)** | Bus access (Token + Master-Slave), frame security, error detection |
| Layer 1 — Physical | **RS-485**, **Fiber Optic (FO)**, or **MBP (Manchester Bus Powered)** for PA | Bit encoding (NRZ), signal transmission |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Focus:** The omission of Layers 3, 4, 5, and 6 is **intentional** — it minimizes frame latency, allowing the **single-digit millisecond cycle times** required in modern high-speed discrete manufacturing (e.g., automotive body-in-white lines).

### 2.1.1 Layer 1 — Physical Layer Deep Dive

**RS-485 (EIA-485) — The Most Common Physical Layer**

- Uses a **single twisted-pair copper cable** with a characteristic impedance of **150 Ω**.
- Differential signalling: Logic "1" = `+5 V` to `+200 mV` between lines (A − B); Logic "0" = `−5 V` to `−200 mV`.
- **Two termination resistors (220 Ω each)** must be connected at the **physical ends** of each segment, in series with a failsafe bias network.
- Connector type: **D-sub 9-pin** (DB-9) is the most common; M12 and terminal block variants also exist.

**Manchester Bus Powered (MBP) — For PROFIBUS PA**

- Used in intrinsically safe (IS) applications, especially in chemical and oil & gas plants.
- Data and power are transmitted on a **single pair** using **Manchester II encoding** at a fixed **31.25 kbps**.
- Maximum segment length: **1900 m**.
- Maximum devices per segment: **32** (with 9 mA per device typical current draw).

### 2.1.2 Layer 2 — Fieldbus Data Link (FDL) — The Heart of PROFIBUS

The FDL layer implements **two coexisting bus access methods**:

#### **Method 1: Token Passing (for Multi-Master Systems)**

- Masters are arranged in a **logical ring** defined by the **Highest Station Address (HSA)** parameter.
- The token is passed by address order, from the master with the **lowest TS (Token Sender)** to the master with the next higher **TS (Token Receiver)**.
- Each master may hold the token for a maximum duration called the **Token Holding Time (THT)**. If a master does not pass the token within the **Token Rotation Time (TRT)** or **Target Token Rotation Time (TTRT)**, the bus is assumed faulty and an error recovery routine is initiated.

#### **Method 2: Master-Slave Polling (for Single-Master Systems)**

- The master, while holding the token, **polls each slave sequentially** using **Send-Request (SRD)** telegrams.
- Slaves respond within a fixed **Slot Time (T_sl)** — if no response arrives, the master declares a **"Slave Failure"** diagnostic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Both Methods?** In complex plant networks, multiple controllers (PLCs, DCS stations, HMI servers) must coexist. Token passing ensures that every master gets **guaranteed, deterministic access** without collisions, while master-slave handles the high-frequency cyclic I/O traffic to field devices.

### 2.1.3 Layer 7 — Application Services (DP Versions)

| Version | Services | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **DP-V0** | Cyclic data exchange (Read/Write), diagnostics, module parameterization | **High** |
| **DP-V1** | Acyclic read/write services, alarms, parameter assignment during runtime | Medium |
| **DP-V2** | Isochronous (clock-synchronized) mode, slave-to-slave broadcast, time-stamping | Low |

## 2.2 PROFIBUS DP Frame Structure (SRD Telegram)

The **Send and Request Data (SRD)** telegram is the most common frame on PROFIBUS DP. Its structure (without variable data field) is:

| Field | Abbreviation | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Start Delimiter | **SD** | 1 byte | `0x10` (no data), `0x68` (data), `0xA2` (token), `0xDC` (short ack), `0xD1` or `0xD2` or `0xD3` (variable) |
| Destination Address | **DA** | 1 byte (0–126) | Target station address |
| Source Address | **SA** | 1 byte (0–126) | Sender's address |
| Function Code | **FC** | 1 byte | SRD, SDN, RD, etc. |
| Data Unit | **DU** | 0–244 bytes | Payload (process data) |
| Frame Check Sequence | **FCS** | 1 byte | XOR-based checksum |
| End Delimiter | **ED** | 1 byte | `0x16` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Remember the magic byte `0x16` for ED — students often confuse it with SD. **ED is always `0x16`**, while SD varies based on the telegram type.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all quantitative formulas** you will need for numerical problems in KTU 2024 examinations on PROFIBUS. **Memorize these.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Maximum segment length vs. baud rate** | $L_{max} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{6}}{f_{baud}} \times 0.001$ (approximation for 9.6k–500k range); precise table lookup required for >500k | meters | $L_{max}$ = segment length, $f_{baud}$ = baud rate in bps |
| 2 | **Segment length at 12 Mbps** | $L_{max} = 100$ m | meters | Hard upper limit |
| 3 | **Segment length at 1.5 Mbps** | $L_{max} = 200$ m | meters | Most common in factory automation |
| 4 | **Segment length at 187.5 kbps** | $L_{max} = 1000$ m | meters | Used in process plants |
| 5 | **Segment length at 9.6 kbps** | $L_{max} = 1200$ m | meters | Long-distance, low-speed |
| 6 | **Token Rotation Time** | $T_{TRT} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{master}} T_{THT,i} + T_{idle,i}$ | ms | Sum of all token holding times + idle gaps |
| 7 | **Target Token Rotation Time (TTRT)** | $T_{TTRT} = \max(T_{cycle,req})$ | ms | Set to maximum required cycle time |
| 8 | **Minimum cycle time (single slave)** | $T_{cyc} = T_{bit} \times (N_{frame} + T_{ack} + T_{sl})$ | ms | Per-slave poll time |
| 9 | **Total bus cycle time (N slaves)** | $T_{bus} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} T_{cyc,j} + T_{token}$ | ms | Add token pass overhead |
| 10 | **Bits per frame (SRD with 8 bytes data)** | $N_{frame} = 11 \text{ bytes} \times 11 \text{ bits/byte} = 121$ | bits | UART adds start/stop/parity |
| 11 | **Theoretical bus load** | $Load = \dfrac{T_{bus,actual}}{T_{TTRT}} \times 100$ | % | Should be < 60% for safety margin |
| 12 | **Maximum devices per segment** | $N_{seg} \leq 32$ | — | Including repeaters as one station |
| 13 | **Maximum devices per network** | $N_{net} \leq 126$ | — | With 4 segments + 4 repeaters |
| 14 | **UART encoding factor** | $1 \text{ byte} = 11 \text{ bits}$ | — | 1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Symbol in Tables:** All absolute value notations use `\vert` instead of `|` to keep the markdown table syntax intact. For example, the term `\vert T_{cyc,req} \vert` is used for the absolute value of the required cycle time, not `\vert T_{cyc,req} \vert`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PROFIBUS is the **de-facto fieldbus standard** in:

- **Process Industries:** Chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas refineries (using PROFIBUS PA for Ex-zone instrumentation).
- **Factory Automation:** Automotive assembly lines (BMW, Mercedes, Volkswagen plants use **PROFINET + PROFIBUS DP**).
- **Water & Wastewater Treatment:** Long-distance segment lengths (1200 m at low baud) make it ideal.
- **Power Generation:** Turbine governor controls, boiler balance-of-plant I/O.
- **Building Automation:** HVAC control systems in large commercial complexes.

The reason PROFIBUS dominates these domains is its **determinism** — given a fixed configuration, the **worst-case cycle time is mathematically bounded**, which is critical for safety-instrumented systems (SIS) under IEC 61508 / IEC 61511.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive, line-by-line mathematical derivations** that a KTU 2024 examiner would expect for a 14-mark numerical problem. Every algebraic transition is written explicitly. No shortcuts.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Bus Cycle Time Calculation (Full Derivation)

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Standard Style):**
> A PROFIBUS DP network has **1 Class 1 Master** and **8 Slaves**. The master polls each slave using the SRD service with **4 bytes of input data** and **4 bytes of output data** per slave. The baud rate is **1.5 Mbps**, and the slot time $T_{sl}$ is configured to **100 bit-times**. The token pass time $T_{token}$ is **50 bit-times**. Calculate:
> (a) The duration of a single SRD cycle to one slave.
> (b) The total bus cycle time.
> (c) The bus load if the Target Token Rotation Time is **10 ms**.

### 3.1.1 Part (a): Duration of a Single SRD Cycle

We begin with the **frame structure** for an SRD telegram with 8 bytes of data total (4 in + 4 out).

**Step 1 — Identify each field in the frame and its size in bytes:**

The SRD telegram contains:
- **SD** (Start Delimiter) = 1 byte
- **DA** (Destination Address) = 1 byte
- **SA** (Source Address) = 1 byte
- **FC** (Function Code) = 1 byte
- **DU** (Data Unit, 8 bytes payload) = 8 bytes
- **FCS** (Frame Check Sequence) = 1 byte
- **ED** (End Delimiter) = 1 byte

Summing these fields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{header} &= SD + DA + SA + FC + FCS + ED \\
N_{header} &= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{frame} &= N_{header} + N_{data} \\
N_{frame} &= 6 + 8 = 14 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** Every byte on the RS-485 physical layer is transmitted as a **UART character** consisting of 11 bits (1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 parity bit + 1 stop bit). Therefore, the total number of bits to transmit is:

**Step 2 — Convert bytes to bits and account for UART framing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bits,frame} &= N_{frame} \times 11 \\
N_{bits,frame} &= 14 \times 11 = 154 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the transmission time at the given baud rate:**

The baud rate $f_{baud} = 1.5$ Mbps = $1.5 \times 10^{6}$ bits per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{N_{bits,frame}}{f_{baud}} \\
T_{frame} &= \frac{154}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds} \\
T_{frame} &= 1.0267 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\
T_{frame} &= 102.67 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** The master sends the request frame to the slave, and the slave responds with an acknowledgement frame. For a single SRD transaction, we need to account for **both the request and the response** — both have the same 14-byte size in this configuration (since both carry 4 bytes I/O data).

**Step 4 — Total transmission time for one SRD transaction (request + response):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{tx,total} &= 2 \times T_{frame} \\
T_{tx,total} &= 2 \times 102.67 = 205.33 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert the slot time to microseconds:**

The slot time is given as $T_{sl} = 100$ bit-times. The duration of one bit-time at 1.5 Mbps is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{bit} &= \frac{1}{f_{baud}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} \\
T_{bit} &= 0.6667 \text{ µs/bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{slot} &= T_{sl} \times T_{bit} \\
T_{slot} &= 100 \times 0.6667 = 66.67 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Total SRD cycle time per slave:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{SRD} &= T_{tx,total} + T_{slot} \\
T_{SRD} &= 205.33 + 66.67 \\
T_{SRD} &= 272.0 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer for Part (a):** $\boxed{T_{SRD} = 272 \text{ µs per slave}}$

### 3.1.2 Part (b): Total Bus Cycle Time

**Step 1 — Multiply the per-slave cycle by the number of slaves:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{poll,total} &= N_{slaves} \times T_{SRD} \\
T_{poll,total} &= 8 \times 272.0 = 2176 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Add the token pass time:**

The token pass time is given as $T_{token} = 50$ bit-times, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{token,dur} &= T_{token} \times T_{bit} \\
T_{token,dur} &= 50 \times 0.6667 = 33.33 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total bus cycle time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{bus} &= T_{poll,total} + T_{token,dur} \\
T_{bus} &= 2176 + 33.33 \\
T_{bus} &= 2209.33 \text{ µs} \\
T_{bus} &\approx 2.21 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer for Part (b):** $\boxed{T_{bus} \approx 2.21 \text{ ms}}$

### 3.1.3 Part (c): Bus Load Percentage

The Target Token Rotation Time $T_{TTRT} = 10$ ms = $10000$ µs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bus Load} &= \frac{T_{bus}}{T_{TTRT}} \times 100 \\
\text{Bus Load} &= \frac{2209.33}{10000} \times 100 \\
\text{Bus Load} &\approx 22.1 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer for Part (c):** $\boxed{\text{Bus Load} \approx 22.1\%}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Interpretation:** A bus load of ~22% is well within the **safe operating envelope** (< 60%) recommended by the PROFIBUS standard. This means we have spare bandwidth for diagnostics, acyclic services (DP-V1), and future expansion. A bus load above **70%** would indicate that the network is approaching saturation and cycle times may degrade.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Maximum Segment Length Determination

> **Problem Statement:** A PROFIBUS DP segment uses RS-485 cabling at **500 kbps**. The maximum allowed propagation delay is **$6 \text{ µs/km}$** of cable. The maximum signal rise time allowed is **0.25 bit-times** to avoid reflections. Calculate the maximum recommended segment length using the **PROFIBUS cable length formula**.

### 3.2.1 Standard Cable Length Table (Reference)

The PROFIBUS standard IEC 61158 provides the following relationship (memorize this table for KTU exams):

| Baud Rate | Max Segment Length |
|---|---|
| 9.6 kbps | 1200 m |
| 19.2 kbps | 1200 m |
| 45.45 kbps | 1200 m |
| 93.75 kbps | 1200 m |
| 187.5 kbps | 1000 m |
| 500 kbps | 400 m |
| 1.5 Mbps | 200 m |
| 3 Mbps | 100 m |
| 6 Mbps | 100 m |
| 12 Mbps | 100 m |

### 3.2.2 Derivation from First Principles

**Step 1 — Calculate the bit time at 500 kbps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{bit} &= \frac{1}{f_{baud}} = \frac{1}{500{,}000} \\
T_{bit} &= 2 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — The maximum allowed rise time as a fraction of bit time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rise,max} &= 0.25 \times T_{bit} \\
T_{rise,max} &= 0.25 \times 2 = 0.5 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Using the empirical PROFIBUS relationship (signal propagation at 66% of speed of light in copper, ~6.6 ns/m):**

The maximum cable length is empirically given by:

$$
L_{max} = \frac{T_{rise,max}}{2 \times t_{prop}}
$$

where $t_{prop} \approx 6.66 \text{ ns/m}$ for PROFIBUS cable type A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 6.66 \times 10^{-9}} \\
L_{max} &= \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{13.32 \times 10^{-9}} \\
L_{max} &= \frac{500 \times 10^{-9}}{13.32 \times 10^{-9}} \\
L_{max} &= 37.5 \text{ m (theoretical, but is not the actual practical answer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** The first-principles derivation gives a conservative bound. In practice, the PROFIBUS standard uses an empirically validated table that accounts for additional effects like cable capacitance, jitter, and repeater insertion loss. For 500 kbps, the **standard table value is 400 m**.

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{L_{max} = 400 \text{ m at 500 kbps (per PROFIBUS standard)}}$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PROFIBUS DP vs. PA vs. FMS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Common Question:** "Compare PROFIBUS DP, PA, and FMS." This table is the **definitive answer template**.

| Parameter | PROFIBUS DP | PROFIBUS PA | PROFIBUS FMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Factory automation, discrete I/O | Process automation, hazardous areas | General data exchange (obsolete) |
| **Physical Layer** | RS-485 or Fiber Optic | MBP (Manchester, 31.25 kbps) | RS-485 or Fiber Optic |
| **Baud Rate** | Up to 12 Mbps | Fixed 31.25 kbps | Up to 12 Mbps |
| **Max Segment Length** | 100 m @ 12 Mbps, 1200 m @ 9.6 kbps | 1900 m | 100 m @ 12 Mbps |
| **Power on Bus** | No (separate) | **Yes** (intrinsically safe) | No (separate) |
| **Number of Stations** | 32/segment, 126/network | 32/segment, 126/network | 32/segment, 126/network |
| **Bus Access** | Token + Master-Slave | Master-Slave only | Token + Master-Slave |
| **Cycle Time** | < 1 ms (at 12 Mbps) | ~10 ms (typical) | ~10 ms |
| **Explosion Protection** | No (use barriers) | **Yes (EEx ia/ib)** | No |
| **Current KTU Relevance** | **Highest** | High | Historical only |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Token Passing Algorithm (Logical Ring)

| Step | Action | Involved Field | KTU Exam Phrasing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power-on: all masters listen to bus | — | "Initial state" |
| 2 | First master (lowest address) sends token to next master | TS, LAS (List of Active Stations) | "Token initialization" |
| 3 | Receiving master accepts token and starts its THT timer | THT, TTRT | "Token acceptance" |
| 4 | Master polls all assigned slaves within THT | SRD telegrams | "Cyclic I/O exchange" |
| 5 | Master passes token to the **next higher address** in LAS | TS, PS (Previous Station) | "Token forwarding" |
| 6 | If no response from next master (timeout > slot time), address gap is logged | GAP maintenance | "Gap management" |
| 7 | When token reaches HSA, it loops back to the lowest address | HSA, LAS | "Token ring completion" |
| 8 | After $N_{gap}$ failed attempts, faulty master is removed from LAS | Diagnostic counter | "Fault recovery" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **Highest Station Address (HSA)** with the **actual number of active masters**. HSA is a **configuration parameter** (typically set to 126), not the count of masters. For example, a network may have only **3 masters** but HSA = **126**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PROFIBUS Network Topology — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["MASTER CLASS 1 PLC CONTROLLER"]
    nodeB["MASTER CLASS 2 HMI ENGINEERING STATION"]
    nodeC["REPEATER SEGMENT 1 TO 2"]
    nodeD["SLAVE 1 REMOTE I/O BLOCK 16 DI"]
    nodeE["SLAVE 2 VARIABLE FREQUENCY DRIVE"]
    nodeF["SLAVE 3 PRESSURE TRANSMITTER PA"]
    nodeG["REPEATER SEGMENT 2 TO 3"]
    nodeH["SLAVE 4 VALVE POSITIONER PA"]
    nodeI["SLAVE 5 ANALOG INPUT MODULE"]
    nodeJ["TERMINATING RESISTOR 220 OHM SEGMENT 1"]
    nodeK["TERMINATING RESISTOR 220 OHM SEGMENT 2"]
    nodeL["TERMINATING RESISTOR 220 OHM SEGMENT 3"]
    nodeM["RS485 TWISTED PAIR CABLE TYPE A"]
    nodeN["24 VDC POWER SUPPLY FOR MASTERS"]

    nodeA --- nodeM
    nodeB --- nodeM
    nodeM --- nodeC
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeE
    nodeC --- nodeF
    nodeM --- nodeJ
    nodeC --- nodeG
    nodeG --- nodeH
    nodeG --- nodeI
    nodeG --- nodeK
    nodeF --- nodeL
    nodeN --- nodeA
    nodeN --- nodeB
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - Each rectangle is a **bus station** (master or slave).
> - The lines represent the **RS-485 twisted-pair bus** (single segment, terminated at both ends).
> - Repeaters (nodeC and nodeG) **regenerate the signal** and isolate segments, allowing the network to extend beyond 32 stations or beyond the maximum segment length.
> - The terminating resistors (nodeJ, nodeK, nodeL) are **mandatory at the physical ends of each segment** to prevent signal reflection.

## 4.2 PROFIBUS Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeL7["APPLICATION LAYER 7 DP V0 V1 V2 CYCLIC ACYCLIC ISOCHRONOUS"]
    nodeL2["DATA LINK LAYER 2 FDL FIELD BUS DATA LINK TOKEN PASSING MASTER SLAVE SRD SDN RD"]
    nodeL1A["PHYSICAL LAYER 1A RS 485 TWISTED PAIR 9.6K TO 12 MBPS"]
    nodeL1B["PHYSICAL LAYER 1B FIBER OPTIC POF PCF GLASS"]
    nodeL1C["PHYSICAL LAYER 1C MBP MANCHESTER BUS POWERED 31.25 KBPS INTRINSICALLY SAFE"]

    nodeL7 --- nodeL2
    nodeL2 --- nodeL1A
    nodeL2 --- nodeL1B
    nodeL2 --- nodeL1C
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The diagram clearly shows that PROFIBUS uses only **3 active layers** (Application + Data Link + Physical), omitting OSI Layers 3, 4, 5, and 6. The Physical Layer has **3 optional implementations** (RS-485, Fiber, MBP) selectable based on the deployment environment.

## 4.3 Token Passing Sequence — Logical Ring State Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS1["STATE 1 POWER ON ALL MASTERS LISTEN"]
    nodeS2["STATE 2 LOWEST ADDRESS MASTER CREATES TOKEN"]
    nodeS3["STATE 3 MASTER HOLDS TOKEN THT TIMER STARTS"]
    nodeS4["STATE 4 MASTER POLLS ALL SLAVES CYCLIC DATA EXCHANGE"]
    nodeS5["STATE 5 MASTER PASSES TOKEN TO NEXT HIGHER ADDRESS"]
    nodeS6["STATE 6 NEXT MASTER ACCEPTS TOKEN OR GAP LOGGED"]
    nodeS7["STATE 7 TOKEN LOOPED BACK AFTER HSA REACHED"]
    nodeS8["STATE 8 FAULTY MASTER REMOVED FROM LAS"]

    nodeS1 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6
    nodeS6 --> nodeS4
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7
    nodeS7 --> nodeS3
    nodeS6 --> nodeS8
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** This is a **state machine** of the token passing lifecycle. Note that **States 4 → 5 → 6 → 4** form the main loop (cyclic operation), while **State 7** completes the ring and re-enters State 3, and **State 8** is the fault recovery path.

## 4.4 PROFIBUS DP SRD Frame Structure — Bit-Level View

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSD["SD START DELIMITER 1 BYTE 0x68"]
    nodeDA["DA DESTINATION ADDRESS 1 BYTE 0 TO 126"]
    nodeSA["SA SOURCE ADDRESS 1 BYTE 0 TO 126"]
    nodeFC["FC FUNCTION CODE 1 BYTE SRD 0x10"]
    nodeDU["DU DATA UNIT 0 TO 244 BYTES PROCESS I/O"]
    nodeFCS["FCS FRAME CHECK SEQUENCE 1 BYTE XOR CHECKSUM"]
    nodeED["ED END DELIMITER 1 BYTE 0x16"]

    nodeSD --> nodeDA
    nodeDA --> nodeSA
    nodeSA --> nodeFC
    nodeFC --> nodeDU
    nodeDU --> nodeFCS
    nodeFCS --> nodeED
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The minimum SRD frame (with empty DU) is **7 bytes** (SD + DA + SA + FC + FCS + ED = 6 bytes, plus 1 byte for the DU size byte itself). The maximum frame size is **255 bytes** (limited by the DU length field).
