# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Part A questions carry **3 marks each** (40–60 words expected answer). Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with internal choice; sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically **7 marks each**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Definition of PROFIBUS DP**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define PROFIBUS DP. Mention its primary application domain and the maximum supported baud rate.**

**Model Answer:**
PROFIBUS DP (Decentralized Periphery) is an open, vendor-independent fieldbus protocol defined under **IEC 61158** and used for high-speed, deterministic communication between controllers (PLCs/DCS) and distributed field devices such as remote I/O blocks, variable frequency drives, and valves.

Its primary application is **factory automation** (discrete manufacturing), where it replaces parallel point-to-point wiring with a single digital bus. The maximum supported baud rate on RS-485 is **12 Mbps**, enabling cyclic I/O cycle times below **1 ms**.

---

### **Question 2: Token vs. Master-Slave Access**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: Distinguish between the two bus access methods used in PROFIBUS DP. When is each method employed?**

**Model Answer:**
PROFIBUS DP uses **two coexisting bus access methods** at the FDL (Fieldbus Data Link) layer:

1. **Token Passing:** Used **between masters** in a multi-master network. The token circulates in a logical ring defined by the Highest Station Address (HSA), granting each master exclusive bus access for a defined Token Holding Time (THT). This is employed when **more than one Class 1 master** is present.

2. **Master-Slave Polling:** Used **between a master and its assigned slaves**. The master, while holding the token, sequentially polls each slave using SRD (Send and Request Data) telegrams. Slaves respond only when addressed.

If only one Class 1 master exists, the token passing degenerates into a single-token system and only master-slave polling is effectively used.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A: Comprehensive PROFIBUS Architecture**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **PROFIBUS protocol architecture** based on the OSI model. Clearly identify which layers are implemented and which are omitted, and justify the omission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **PROFIBUS DP SRD frame structure** with the function of each field. A DP slave receives an SRD telegram with 16 bytes of input data. Calculate the **total transmission time** at **1.5 Mbps** baud rate. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The PROFIBUS DP protocol architecture is a **streamlined 3-layer implementation** of the OSI model, intentionally omitting Layers 3 through 6 to achieve real-time performance.

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer:** Implemented using RS-485 (twisted pair, up to 12 Mbps), Fiber Optic (POF/PCF/glass), or MBP (for PROFIBUS PA). This layer handles **bit encoding** (NRZ for RS-485, Manchester for MBP), electrical/optical signal transmission, and connector mechanical specifications (D-sub 9-pin).

**Layer 2 — Data Link Layer (FDL — Fieldbus Data Link):** This is the heart of PROFIBUS. It implements:
- **Bus access control** via Token Passing (between masters) and Master-Slave polling.
- **Frame structure** (SD, DA, SA, FC, DU, FCS, ED).
- **Error detection** (parity + checksum) and **acknowledgement handling** (SC = short acknowledgement).
- **Slot time monitoring** for slave response timeouts.

**Layer 7 — Application Layer:** Provides user services divided into:
- **DP-V0:** Cyclic data exchange (basic function).
- **DP-V1:** Acyclic services, alarms, parameterization during operation.
- **DP-V2:** Isochronous mode, slave-to-slave communication, time-stamping.

**Why Layers 3–6 are Omitted:** The omission of Network, Transport, Session, and Presentation layers eliminates the software processing overhead of routing, segmentation/reassembly, encryption, and data format conversion. This minimizes frame latency, enabling **single-digit millisecond cycle times** essential for high-speed discrete manufacturing (e.g., automotive robotic cells).

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeL7["APPLICATION LAYER 7 DP V0 V1 V2 USER INTERFACE"]
    nodeL2["DATA LINK LAYER 2 FDL TOKEN BUS ACCESS FRAMING ERROR CHECK"]
    nodeL1["PHYSICAL LAYER 1 RS 485 FIBER OPTIC MBP BIT ENCODING"]

    nodeL7 --- nodeL2
    nodeL2 --- nodeL1
```

> **Marking Distribution:** Layer identification (2 marks), Function description (3 marks), Justification of omission (2 marks).

#### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Frame Structure of an SRD Telegram:**

| Field | Size |
|---|---|
| SD (Start Delimiter) | 1 byte |
| DA (Destination Address) | 1 byte |
| SA (Source Address) | 1 byte |
| FC (Function Code) | 1 byte |
| DU (Data Unit, 16 bytes) | 16 bytes |
| FCS (Frame Check Sequence) | 1 byte |
| ED (End Delimiter) | 1 byte |
| **Total** | **21 bytes** |

**Step 2 — Convert bytes to bits using UART framing (11 bits/byte):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bits} &= 21 \times 11 = 231 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the transmission time at 1.5 Mbps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{tx} &= \frac{N_{bits}}{f_{baud}} \\
T_{tx} &= \frac{231}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} \\
T_{tx} &= 1.54 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\
T_{tx} &= 154 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** The total transmission time for the SRD telegram is **$T_{tx} = 154 \text{ µs}$**.

**Function of Each Field (additional 2 marks for explanation):**
- **SD:** Synchronization and telegram type identification.
- **DA/SA:** Addressing — who sends, who receives.
- **FC:** Specifies the service (SRD, RD, SDN).
- **DU:** Carries the actual process I/O data.
- **FCS:** Error detection via XOR checksum.
- **ED:** Marks the end of the telegram.

---

### **Question B: Token Passing and Bus Cycle Time Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **token passing algorithm** used in PROFIBUS DP. Define the terms **HSA, LAS, THT, and TTRT**, and describe the role of the **Gap List** in maintaining the logical ring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PROFIBUS DP network has **2 Class 1 Masters (M1, M2)** and **12 Slaves (6 assigned to M1, 6 to M2)**. Each slave exchanges **2 bytes input + 2 bytes output**. The baud rate is **500 kbps**, slot time $T_{sl} = 100$ bit-times, and token pass time is **40 bit-times**. Calculate the **total bus cycle time** and the **bus load** if TTRT = **20 ms**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Token Passing Algorithm:**

In a multi-master PROFIBUS DP network, only one master can transmit on the bus at a time. To ensure **deterministic, collision-free access**, a **logical token ring** is established. The token is a special 3-byte telegram that grants the right to access the bus.

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. **Initialization:** On power-on, all masters listen on the bus. The master with the **lowest station address** creates the first token (token initialization phase).

2. **Logical Ring Formation:** The token is passed in **ascending address order** through all active masters in the **List of Active Stations (LAS)**. When the token reaches the **Highest Station Address (HSA)**, it loops back to the lowest address.

3. **Token Holding:** The master holding the token may perform all its bus activities — cyclic I/O exchange, acyclic services, diagnostics — within its **Token Holding Time (THT)**.

4. **Token Pass:** Before THT expires, the master passes the token to the next master in the LAS.

**Key Definitions:**

- **HSA (Highest Station Address):** A configuration parameter (default 126) that defines the upper limit of the logical ring. **It is NOT the number of masters.**
- **LAS (List of Active Stations):** A dynamic list maintained by each master, containing the addresses of all masters currently active on the bus.
- **THT (Token Holding Time):** The maximum duration a master may hold the token before passing it. Calculated as $T_{THT} = T_{TTRT} - T_{idle}$.
- **TTRT (Target Token Rotation Time):** The desired time for a complete token rotation around the logical ring, set to the **longest required cycle time** of any master.

**Gap List:**

When a master passes the token to the next address in the LAS and **no response** is received within the slot time (i.e., that address is not an active master), the token is passed to the **next higher address**. The address that failed to respond is added to the **Gap List**. If an address in the Gap List responds to a future token pass attempt (e.g., a master comes online), it is removed from the Gap List and re-inserted into the LAS.

**Role of the Gap List:** It allows the network to **dynamically adapt** to the addition or removal of masters without requiring a complete re-initialization.

> **Marking Distribution:** Algorithm steps (3 marks), Definitions (2 marks), Gap List role (2 marks).

#### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Frame Size for 4 bytes of data (2 in + 2 out):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{frame} &= \text{Header} + \text{Data} = 6 + 4 = 10 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bits with UART encoding (11 bits/byte):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bits,frame} &= 10 \times 11 = 110 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Transmission time per frame at 500 kbps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{110}{500{,}000} = 2.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 220 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total transmission time per SRD transaction (request + response):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{tx,total} &= 2 \times 220 = 440 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert slot time and token pass time to microseconds:**

Bit time at 500 kbps: $T_{bit} = 1/500{,}000 = 2 \text{ µs/bit}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{slot} &= 100 \times 2 = 200 \text{ µs} \\
T_{token} &= 40 \times 2 = 80 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — SRD cycle per slave:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{SRD} &= T_{tx,total} + T_{slot} \\
T_{SRD} &= 440 + 200 = 640 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Total poll time for all 12 slaves:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{poll,total} &= 12 \times 640 = 7680 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Add token pass overhead (each master passes token once, so 2 token passes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{bus} &= T_{poll,total} + 2 \times T_{token} \\
T_{bus} &= 7680 + 2 \times 80 = 7840 \text{ µs} \\
T_{bus} &= 7.84 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Bus load calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bus Load} &= \frac{T_{bus}}{T_{TTRT}} \times 100 \\
\text{Bus Load} &= \frac{7.84}{20} \times 100 = 39.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:**
> $\boxed{T_{bus} = 7.84 \text{ ms}, \text{Bus Load} = 39.2\%}$

> **Engineer's Comment:** A bus load of 39.2% is **healthy** (below 60% safe limit). The network has spare capacity for DP-V1 acyclic services and future device additions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting UART framing:** Many students calculate $N_{bits} = N_{bytes} \times 8$ instead of $\times 11$. This causes a **30% error** in cycle time. **Always use 11 bits/byte.**
> 2. **Forgetting the request AND response:** A single SRD transaction involves **2 frames** (master request + slave response), not 1. Missing this gives half the correct time.
> 3. **Misinterpreting slot time units:** Slot time is given in **bit-times**, not µs. You must multiply by $T_{bit} = 1/f_{baud}$ to convert.
> 4. **Counting token passes incorrectly:** With $N$ masters, the token is passed $N$ times per cycle (including the wrap-around at HSA). For 2 masters, there are **2 token passes**, not 1.
> 5. **Confusing HSA with number of masters:** The token does NOT pass through every address from 0 to HSA — only through active masters in the LAS. Use the number of masters (2), not HSA (126).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep:**

### **Core Concepts**
- ☐ PROFIBUS = **Process Field Bus**, standardized under **IEC 61158 / IEC 61784**, governed by **PNO (Germany)**.
- ☐ Three variants: **DP (Decentralized Periphery)**, **PA (Process Automation)**, **FMS (Fieldbus Message Specification — obsolete)**.
- ☐ PROFIBUS DP uses **3 OSI layers**: Layer 1 (Physical), Layer 2 (FDL), Layer 7 (DP-V0/V1/V2 Application).
- ☐ Layers **3, 4, 5, 6 are omitted** to minimize latency for real-time performance.

### **Physical Layer**
- ☐ **RS-485** is the most common: twisted pair, differential signalling, **150 Ω** impedance, D-sub 9-pin connector.
- ☐ **MBP (Manchester Bus Powered)** is used in PROFIBUS PA for hazardous areas at **31.25 kbps**.
- ☐ Standard baud rates: **9.6k, 19.2k, 45.45k, 93.75k, 187.5k, 500k, 1.5M, 3M, 6M, 12M bps**.
- ☐ Segment length **inversely proportional** to baud rate: **100 m @ 12 Mbps** to **1200 m @ 9.6 kbps**.
- ☐ **220 Ω termination resistors** required at both physical ends of each RS-485 segment.

### **Data Link Layer (FDL)**
- ☐ **Two bus access methods:** Token Passing (between masters) + Master-Slave (between master and slaves).
- ☐ Logical ring defined by **HSA (Highest Station Address)**, default **126**.
- ☐ **LAS (List of Active Stations)** is maintained dynamically.
- ☐ **Gap List** stores addresses that did not respond (inactive masters).
- ☐ **THT (Token Holding Time)** = max time a master can hold the token.
- ☐ **TTRT (Target Token Rotation Time)** = max allowed rotation time = max required cycle time.

### **Frame Structure (SRD)**
- ☐ Frame fields: **SD, DA, SA, FC, DU, FCS, ED** — total overhead **6 bytes** + data.
- ☐ Maximum frame size: **255 bytes** (DU up to 244 bytes).
- ☐ SD values: `0x10`, `0x68`, `0xA2` (token), `0xDC` (short ack).
- ☐ ED is **always `0x16`**.

### **Numerical / Calculation Skills**
- ☐ **1 UART byte = 11 bits** (1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop). **Never use 8 bits/byte.**
- ☐ **SRD transaction time = 2 × frame transmission time + slot time.**
- ☐ **Total bus cycle = (sum of all slave SRD times) + (N_masters × token pass time).**
- ☐ **Bus load = (bus cycle time / TTRT) × 100%** — keep below **60%** for safety.

### **DP Versions**
- ☐ **DP-V0:** Basic cyclic I/O exchange.
- ☐ **DP-V1:** Acyclic services, alarms, parameterization during operation.
- ☐ **DP-V2:** Isochronous mode, slave-to-slave broadcast, time-stamping (clock synchronized to < 1 µs).

### **Network Limits**
- ☐ **32 stations per segment** (including repeaters as 1 station each).
- ☐ **126 stations per network** (using 4 repeaters/segments).
- ☐ **4 repeaters maximum** to extend network to its full capacity.

### **Exam-Specific Tips**
- ☐ Always specify the **variant (DP/PA/FMS)** and **version (V0/V1/V2)** in your answer.
- ☐ For 7-mark calculation questions, show **all 5–6 derivation steps explicitly** — partial marks are awarded for each correct step.
- ☐ Draw **neat block diagrams** with clear labels — a well-labeled diagram can earn **2–3 marks** even if the text explanation is weak.
- ☐ Memorize the **standard baud rate vs. segment length table** — it appears in nearly every KTU cycle time problem.
- ☐ Always convert slot time and token pass time from **bit-times to microseconds** using $T_{bit} = 1/f_{baud}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CANopen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CANopen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CANopen

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CANopen** is a high-level **communication protocol** and **device profile specification** (standardized under **EN 50325-4** and maintained by **CAN in Automation (CiA)**) that operates on top of the underlying **Controller Area Network (CAN) serial bus system** (ISO 11898). It is widely employed in **embedded industrial automation** for connecting devices such as sensors, actuators, motion controllers, and PLCs in a distributed control architecture.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, CANopen is studied as one of the most commercially successful **application-layer protocols** built on the CAN physical and data-link layers. It defines how data is organized, addressed, and exchanged between nodes in a real-time distributed control system.

### Key Standardized Documents (CiA / DS Series)

> [!NOTE]
> - **CiA 301 (DS 301)** — CANopen Application Layer and Communication Profile (the core standard)
> - **CiA 302** — Additional Application Layer Functions (e.g., redundancy, flying master)
> - **CiA 305** — Layer Setting Services (LSS) for bit-rate and Node-ID configuration
> - **CiA 401** — Device Profile for Generic I/O Modules
> - **CiA 402** — Device Profile for Drives and Motion Control
> - **CiA 404** — Device Profile for Measuring Devices and Closed-Loop Controllers
> - **CiA 406** — Device Profile for Encoders
> - **CiA 408** — Device Profile for Hydraulic Drives
> - **CiA 410** — Device Profile for Inclinometers
> - **CiA 413** — Device Profile for Truck Gateways
> - **CiA 414** — Device Profile for Weaving Machines
> - **CiA 415** — Device Profile for Road Construction Machinery
> - **CiA 416** — Device Profile for Building Doors
> - **CiA 417** — Device Profile for Lift (Elevator) Control Systems
> - **CiA 418** — Device Profile for Battery Chargers
> - **CiA 419** — Device Profile for Battery Power Systems
> - **CiA 420** — Device Profile for Extruder Downstream Devices
> - **CiA 421** — Device Profile for Fuel Cell Power Systems
> - **CiA 422** — Device Profile for Municipal Vehicles
> - **CiA 423** — Device Profile for Rail Vehicle Power Drive Systems
> - **CiA 424** — Device Profile for Rail Vehicle Door Control Systems
> - **CiA 425** — Device Profile for Medical Devices
> - **CiA 426** — Device Profile for Outdoor Navigation Systems
> - **CiA 430** — Device Profile for Semiconductor Equipment
> - **CiA 433** — Device Profile for Printing Machines
> - **CiA 434** — Device Profile for Laboratory Automation
> - **CiA 436** — Device Profile for Grids and Storage Systems
> - **CiA 437** — Device Profile for Granulates
> - **CiA 440** — Device Profile for Message Handling
> - **CiA 443** — Device Profile for Water Treatment Systems
> - **CiA 444** — Device Profile for Packaging Machines
> - **CiA 445** — Device Profile for RFID
> - **CiA 446** — Device Profile for Food Processing Machines
> - **CiA 447** — Device Profile for Foundry Machines
> - **CiA 448** — Device Profile for Textile Machines
> - **CiA 449** — Device Profile for Forestry Machines
> - **CiA 450** — Device Profile for Pump Systems
> - **CiA 451** — Device Profile for Beverage Machines
> - **CiA 452** — Device Profile for Plastic Injection Molding Machines
> - **CiA 453** — Device Profile for Engine Coolant Pumps
> - **CiA 454** — Device Profile for Energy Management
> - **CiA 455** — Device Profile for Building Automation
> - **CiA 456** — Device Profile for Solar Power Systems
> - **CiA 457** — Device Profile for Drinking Water Treatment
> - **CiA 458** — Device Profile for Woodworking Machines
> - **CiA 459** — Device Profile for Compressors
> - **CiA 460** — Device Profile for Service Robots
> - **CiA 461** — Device Profile for Agricultural Equipment
> - **CiA 462** — Device Profile for Paper Machines
> - **CiA 463** — Device Profile for Laboratory Information Systems
> - **CiA 464** — Device Profile for Multi-Axis Motion Controllers
> - **CiA 465** — Device Profile for Cleaning Machines
> - **CiA 466** — Device Profile for Firefighting Vehicles
> - **CiA 467** — Device Profile for Heat Meters
> - **CiA 468** — Device Profile for Welding Machines
> - **CiA 469** — Device Profile for Road Weather Information Systems
> - **CiA 470** — Device Profile for Construction Machines
> - **CiA 471** — Device Profile for Municipal Waterworks
> - **CiA 472** — Device Profile for Power Generation Systems
> - **CiA 473** — Device Profile for Lift (Elevator) Drive Systems
> - **CiA 474** — Device Profile for Virtual Devices
> - **CiA 475** — Device Profile for Industrial Robots
> - **CiA 476** — Device Profile for Process Automation
> - **CiA 477** — Device Profile for Road Construction Equipment
> - **CiA 478** — Device Profile for Cranes
> - **CiA 479** — Device Profile for On-Board Diagnostics
> - **CiA 480** — Device Profile for Concrete Mixing Plants
> - **CiA 481** — Device Profile for Industrial Cleaning Equipment
> - **CiA 482** — Device Profile for Tire Manufacturing Machines
> - **CiA 483** — Device Profile for Plastic and Rubber Machines
> - **CiA 484** — Device Profile for Mining Machines
> - **CiA 485** — Device Profile for Beverage Dispensing Equipment
> - **CiA 486** — Device Profile for Power Take-Offs
> - **CiA 487** — Device Profile for Refrigerated Transport
> - **CiA 488** — Device Profile for Tunnel Boring Machines
> - **CiA 489** — Device Profile for Iron and Steel Plants
> - **CiA 490** — Device Profile for Sieving Machines
> - **CiA 491** — Device Profile for Cooking Appliances
> - **CiA 492** — Device Profile for Cooling Appliances
> - **CiA 493** — Device Profile for Home Appliances
> - **CiA 494** — Device Profile for Lifts (Elevators) — General
> - **CiA 495** — Device Profile for Lifts (Elevators) — Drive Systems
> - **CiA 496** — Device Profile for Lifts (Elevators) — Door Systems
> - **CiA 497** — Device Profile for Lifts (Elevators) — Cabin Systems
> - **CiA 498** — Device Profile for Lifts (Elevators) — Control Systems
> - **CiA 499** — Device Profile for Lifts (Elevators) — Safety Systems

The most important for KTU study are **CiA 301** (core), **CiA 305** (LSS), **CiA 401** (I/O), and **CiA 402** (drives/motion).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Multilingual Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen where:
> - **The kitchen bus (CAN bus)** is a long stainless-steel counter where waiters shout orders.
> - **CANopen** is the **structured kitchen order system** — a rule book that says "if a waiter shouts order #1, the soup station responds; if order #2, the grill responds; if order #3, the dessert station responds."
> - **Each cook (node)** has a **numbered station ID (Node ID, 1–127)**.
> - The **Object Dictionary (OD)** is the **cook's personal recipe book** — anyone can ask "What ingredient is in index `0x2000`, sub-index `0x02`?" and the cook will look it up.
> - **PDO (Process Data Object)** is the **quick shout-out** ("Order ready!") used for fast, repeated updates.
> - **SDO (Service Data Object)** is the **detailed written request** ("Could I please have the recipe for index 0x2001?"), slower but reliable.
> - **NMT (Network Management)** is the **head chef** who decides which cook is allowed to talk, and ensures everyone is in sync.

This analogy makes it clear that CANopen is **not** a new physical bus — it is a **rule-book and language** that runs on top of the CAN bus to bring **structured, real-time, plug-and-play communication** to industrial devices.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Data-Rate Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Bit Rates (per ISO 11898-1):** **1 Mbit/s** (max), **800 kbit/s, 500 kbit/s, 250 kbit/s, 125 kbit/s, 50 kbit/s, 20 kbit/s, 10 kbit/s**
> - **Bus Length vs Bit Rate** (mandatory in KTU answers): At **1 Mbit/s**, max cable length is approximately **25–40 m**; at **125 kbit/s**, it can extend to **500 m**; at **50 kbit/s**, up to **1 km**.
> - **Max Nodes:** **127** (Node ID 0 reserved for broadcast; 1–127 valid).
> - **Default Bit Rate (CiA 301):** **125 kbit/s** is often the recommended default.
> - **Identifier length:** **11-bit** CAN standard identifier (CAN 2.0A) is the base; 29-bit is also supported.

## 1.4 Why CANopen is Used in Distributed Control

| Feature | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| Standardized Object Dictionary | Plug-and-play integration of devices from different vendors |
| Predefined Connection Set | No complex network configuration for typical setups |
| PDO Real-Time Broadcast | Deterministic, low-latency I/O exchange |
| SDO Parameter Access | On-the-fly configuration and diagnostics |
| NMT State Machine | Safe start-up, operational control, and shutdown |
| Device Profiles (DS40x) | Vendor-independent interoperability |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CANopen Protocol Stack (Layered View)

CANopen is structured as a **layered protocol** (closely following the OSI model but simplified for embedded use):

### Layer Breakdown

1. **Physical Layer (ISO 11898-2 / ISO 11898-3)**
   - Defines the electrical signalling on the bus (differential, **CAN-H** and **CAN-L** lines).
   - Termination: **120 Ω** at both ends.
   - Bit rates: **10 kbit/s to 1 Mbit/s** as listed above.

2. **Data Link Layer (ISO 11898-1)**
   - Standard **CAN 2.0A** (11-bit identifier) or **CAN 2.0B** (29-bit identifier).
   - Provides non-destructive bitwise arbitration, error detection (CRC-15), acknowledgement, and fault confinement.

3. **Application Layer (CiA 301)**
   - The CANopen-specific layer.
   - Defines the **Object Dictionary**, **Communication Objects (COBs)**, **Network Management**, and **State Machine**.

4. **Device Profile Layer (CiA 4xx)**
   - Defines application-specific behavior of a class of devices (e.g., CiA 402 for drives).
   - Sits *above* the application layer.

5. **Application / User Code**
   - The vendor's or user's actual control program.

## 2.2 The Object Dictionary (OD) — The Heart of CANopen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Object Dictionary** is a **16-bit-indexed, 8-bit-sub-indexed table** that resides in every CANopen device. It is the **central data structure** that makes CANopen device behavior vendor-independent.

### Structure of an OD Entry

- **Index** (16 bits, range **0x0000 to 0xFFFF**)
- **Sub-index** (8 bits, range **0x00 to 0xFF**)
- **Name** (string)
- **Data Type** (e.g., UNSIGNED8, INTEGER16, REAL32, VISIBLE_STRING, DOMAIN)
- **Attribute** (Read-Only / Write-Only / Read-Write / Constant)
- **Value** (the actual data)

### Standardized Index Ranges (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The OD is divided into **standardized regions** defined in CiA 301. The following are mandatory knowledge for KTU:

| Index Range (hex) | Region Name | Content |
|---|---|---|
| 0x0000 | Reserved | Not used |
| 0x0001 – 0x009F | Data Types | Standard data type definitions (e.g., BOOLEAN, INTEGER16) |
| 0x00A0 – 0x0FFF | Reserved | Reserved by CiA |
| 0x1000 – 0x1FFF | Communication Profile Area | Communication parameters (e.g., 0x1000 = Device Type, 0x1017 = Producer Heartbeat Time) |
| 0x2000 – 0x5FFF | Manufacturer-specific Area | Vendor-defined parameters |
| 0x6000 – 0x9FFF | Standardized Device Profile Area | Profile-defined objects (e.g., 0x6000+ for CiA 401 I/O) |

### Most-Asked OD Entries

- **0x1000 — Device Type** (read-only)
- **0x1001 — Error Register** (read-only)
- **0x1003 — Pre-defined Error Field** (read-only, array)
- **0x1005 — COB-ID SYNC message** (read-write)
- **0x1006 — Communication Cycle Period** (read-write, in µs)
- **0x1007 — Synchronous Window Length** (read-write, in µs)
- **0x1008 — Manufacturer Device Name** (VISIBLE_STRING)
- **0x1009 — Manufacturer Hardware Version**
- **0x100A — Manufacturer Software Version**
- **0x100C — Guard Time** (read-write, in ms)
- **0x100D — Life Time Factor** (read-write, integer)
- **0x1010 — Store Parameters** (command to save to non-volatile memory)
- **0x1011 — Restore Default Parameters** (command to factory-reset)
- **0x1012 — COB-ID Time Stamp Object**
- **0x1013 — High Resolution Time Stamp**
- **0x1014 — COB-ID EMCY** (Emergency COB-ID)
- **0x1015 — Inhibit Time EMCY**
- **0x1016 — Consumer Heartbeat Time** (array of entries)
- **0x1017 — Producer Heartbeat Time**
- **0x1018 — Identity Object** (Vendor ID, Product Code, Revision, Serial Number)
- **0x1020 — Verify Configuration**
- **0x1021 — EDS File Version** (read-only)
- **0x1022 — Store EDS File**
- **0x1023 — OS Command**
- **0x1024 — OS Command Mode**
- **0x1025 — OS Debugger Interface**
- **0x1026 — OS Prompt**
- **0x1027 — Module List**
- **0x1028 — Emergency Consumer**
- **0x1029 — Error Behavior**
- **0x102A — DS 302 Data Type**
- **0x102B — DS 302 Error Code**

## 2.3 Communication Models (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CANopen uses **three peer-to-peer communication models** in parallel:

| Model | Direction | Description | Example COB |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Master / Slave** | Point-to-point, command/response | NMT master commands, slaves respond | NMT, SDO |
| **Client / Server** | Confirmed, point-to-point | A client requests; a server replies | SDO (upload/download) |
| **Producer / Consumer** | Unconfirmed, push or pull (broadcast) | Producer pushes; consumers read | PDO, SYNC, EMCY, TIME |

## 2.4 Predefined Connection Set (COB-ID Allocation)

CANopen uses a **Predefined Connection Set** (per CiA 301) so that devices can communicate **without prior configuration**. The 11-bit CAN-ID is split as:

- **Function Code (4 bits)** — defines the type of communication object.
- **Node ID (7 bits)** — identifies the node (1–127).

$$\text{COB-ID (11-bit)} = \underbrace{\text{Function Code (bits 10–7)}}_{4 \text{ bits}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{Node ID (bits 6–0)}}_{7 \text{ bits}}$$

### KTU Predefined Connection Set Table

| Communication Object | Function Code (binary) | Function Code (hex) | Resulting COB-ID | Transmit Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NMT** | 0000 | 0x0 | 0x000 | Master → Slave (broadcast) |
| **SYNC** | 0001 | 0x1 | 0x080 | Producer → Consumers (broadcast) |
| **EMCY** | 0001 | 0x1 | 0x081 + NodeID | Producer → Consumers |
| **TIME** | 0010 | 0x2 | 0x100 | Producer → Consumers (broadcast) |
| **TPDO1 (Transmit PDO 1)** | 0011 | 0x3 | 0x180 + NodeID | Producer → Consumers |
| **RPDO1 (Receive PDO 1)** | 0100 | 0x4 | 0x200 + NodeID | Consumers → Producer |
| **TPDO2** | 0101 | 0x5 | 0x280 + NodeID | Producer → Consumers |
| **RPDO2** | 0110 | 0x6 | 0x300 + NodeID | Consumers → Producer |
| **TPDO3** | 0111 | 0x7 | 0x380 + NodeID | Producer → Consumers |
| **RPDO3** | 1000 | 0x8 | 0x400 + NodeID | Consumers → Producer |
| **TPDO4** | 1001 | 0x9 | 0x480 + NodeID | Producer → Consumers |
| **RPDO4** | 1010 | 0xA | 0x500 + NodeID | Consumers → Producer |
| **SDO (tx) — server → client** | 1011 | 0xB | 0x580 + NodeID | Server → Client |
| **SDO (rx) — client → server** | 1100 | 0xC | 0x600 + NodeID | Client → Server |
| **NMT Error Control (Heartbeat / Guarding)** | 1110 | 0xE | 0x700 + NodeID | Producer → Consumers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** With the predefined set, each node has **4 TPDOs, 4 RPDOs, 1 SDO server channel, 1 SDO client channel, 1 EMCY, 1 TIME consumer, 1 SYNC consumer/producer, 1 Heartbeat producer** already allocated — without any setup. This is the "plug-and-play" essence of CANopen.

## 2.5 Process Data Object (PDO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **PDO** is used for **real-time, cyclic, event-driven data exchange** with **no protocol overhead** beyond the 8-byte CAN payload. It directly maps OD entries to PDO data bytes.

- **TPDO (Transmit PDO)** — produced by the node, consumed by others.
- **RPDO (Receive PDO)** — consumed by the node, produced by others.

### Three Triggering Modes for TPDOs

1. **Event-driven (asynchronous)** — sent on internal data change (with optional **inhibit time** and **event time**).
2. **Synchronous (cyclic)** — sent every Nth SYNC.
3. **Synchronous (acyclic)** — sent only on data change, but only right after a SYNC.

### PDO Communication Parameters (0x1400–0x15FF for RPDO, 0x1800–0x19FF for TPDO)

- **COB-ID** (used)
- **Transmission Type** (0 = acyclic sync, 1–240 = cyclic sync, 252 = synchronous RTR-only, 253 = asynchronous RTR-only, 254 = asynchronous, manufacturer-specific, 255 = asynchronous, device-profile specific)
- **Inhibit Time** (0x1800:0x03) — minimum gap between two TPDOs, in **100 µs units**.
- **Event Time** (0x1800:0x05) — periodic sending, in **ms**.
- **SYNC Start Value** (0x1800:0x06)

### PDO Mapping Parameters (0x1600–0x17FF for RPDO, 0x1A00–0x1BF for TPDO)

Each PDO can map up to **8 bytes** of data. The mapping entry is encoded as:

$$\text{Mapping Value (32 bits)} = \underbrace{\text{Index}}_{16 \text{ bits}} \; \Vert \; \underbrace{\text{Sub-index}}_{8 \text{ bits}} \; \Vert \; \underbrace{\text{Bit length}}_{8 \text{ bits}}$$

For example, mapping OD `0x2000:0x01` (16 bits) and `0x2000:0x02` (16 bits) into TPDO1 is encoded as `[0x20000110, 0x20000210]`.

## 2.6 Service Data Object (SDO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SDO** provides **confirmed, peer-to-peer access to any OD entry** of a remote node. Used for **configuration, parameterization, and diagnostics** rather than real-time I/O.

### Two Operations

- **SDO Download (client → server)**: write a value to the server's OD.
- **SDO Upload (client → server)**: read a value from the server's OD.

### SDO Protocol Variants

1. **Expedited Transfer** (for ≤ 4 bytes of data) — entire value fits in 1 message.
2. **Segmented Transfer** (for > 4 bytes) — data sent in multiple segments, with **toggle bit** for sequencing.
3. **Block Transfer** (for very large data) — bulk transfer of up to **127 segments per block**; significantly faster than segmented.

### SDO Expedited Download Frame (11-bit ID = 0x600 + NodeID)

| Byte 0 | Byte 1–2 | Byte 3 | Byte 4–7 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Command Specifier (e.g., 0x23 = 4-byte download) | Index (LSB first) | Sub-index | Data (up to 4 bytes) |

> [!NOTE]
> The 8 most-significant bits of byte 0 carry: `1` for download, `0` for upload, plus **n** (number of unused data bytes) for expedited transfer. The least-significant 3 bits are the **command specifier**.

### Common SDO Abort Codes (KTU High-Yield)

| Abort Code (hex) | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 0x05040000 | SDO protocol timed out |
| 0x05040001 | Client/server command specifier invalid / unknown |
| 0x05040002 | Invalid block size in block transfer |
| 0x05040003 | Invalid sequence number in segmented transfer |
| 0x05040004 | CRC error during block transfer |
| 0x05040005 | Out of memory |
| 0x06010000 | Unsupported access to an object |
| 0x06010001 | Read access to write-only object |
| 0x06010002 | Write access to read-only object |
| 0x06020000 | Object does not exist in OD |
| 0x06040041 | Object cannot be mapped to PDO |
| 0x06040042 | Number/length of mapped objects exceeds PDO length |
| 0x06040043 | General parameter incompatibility |
| 0x06040047 | General internal incompatibility in device |
| 0x06060000 | Access failed due to hardware error |
| 0x06070010 | Data type mismatch, length of service parameter too high |
| 0x06070012 | Data type mismatch, length of service parameter too low |
| 0x06070013 | Sub-index does not exist |
| 0x06090011 | Sub-index cannot be written, OD is read-only |
| 0x06090030 | Value range of parameter exceeded |
| 0x06090031 | Value of parameter too high |
| 0x06090032 | Value of parameter too low |
| 0x08000000 | General error |
| 0x08000020 | Data cannot be transferred/stored |
| 0x08000021 | Data cannot be transferred/stored because of local control |
| 0x08000022 | Data cannot be transferred/stored because of present device state |
| 0x08000023 | Object dictionary generation failed / no object dictionary present |

## 2.7 Network Management (NMT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> NMT provides **boot-up, state control, and error control** for the entire network. There is **one NMT master** and **NMT slaves**.

### NMT Slave State Machine (KTU High-Yield Diagram in Section 4)

The slave has **four main states** plus a transitional boot-up state:

1. **Initialisation** — power-on, hardware check.
   - Sub-state: **Boot-up** (sends one NMT Boot-up message with COB-ID 0x700 + NodeID, then auto-transitions to Pre-operational).
2. **Pre-operational** — SDO and NMT active; **PDO and SYNC inactive**. Used for configuration.
3. **Operational** — All communication objects active, including PDO and SYNC.
4. **Stopped** — Only NMT is active; device is "quiet" but still monitored.

### NMT Message Format

| COB-ID | DLC | Byte 0 (Command) | Byte 1 (NodeID) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x000 | 2 | 0x01 = Start Remote Node; 0x02 = Stop Remote Node; 0x80 = Enter Pre-operational; 0x81 = Reset Node; 0x82 = Reset Communication | 0x00 = all nodes; 1–127 = specific node |

### Error Control (Two Mechanisms)

1. **Node Guarding** (legacy)
   - Master polls each slave with an **NMT Master RTR** (Remote Transmission Request) on COB-ID 0x700 + NodeID.
   - Slave replies with a **toggle-bit-protected** state byte.
   - If the master misses a configured number of replies, the node is declared **failed**.

2. **Heartbeat Producer / Consumer** (recommended, simpler)
   - Each Heartbeat Producer **autonomously broadcasts** its state byte on COB-ID 0x700 + NodeID at a fixed interval called the **Producer Heartbeat Time** (OD 0x1017, in **ms**).
   - Each Heartbeat Consumer monitors the heartbeat of its peers; if none arrives within the **Consumer Heartbeat Time** (OD 0x1016), the consumer triggers an error reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Heartbeat is preferred over Guarding:** It removes the bus traffic of polling and the dependency on the master.

## 2.8 Other Communication Objects

- **SYNC** (COB-ID 0x080): producer → consumers, triggers synchronous PDOs. Optional.
- **EMCY** (COB-ID 0x081 + NodeID): emergency broadcast, sent on internal error. Format: 8 bytes containing the **Emergency Error Code (16 bits), Error Register (8 bits)**, and 5 manufacturer-specific bytes.
- **TIME** (COB-ID 0x100): optional 6-byte time stamp distribution.
- **LSS (Layer Setting Services, CiA 305)**: for configuring **Node ID and bit rate** of a node when no DIP switches exist. Uses COB-IDs 0x7E5 (LSS master) and 0x7E4 (LSS slave).

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the consolidated, exam-ready formula sheet.

### Equation 1 — COB-ID Predefined Set Construction

$$\text{COB-ID}_{11\text{-bit}} = \text{Function Code} \times 2^{7} + \text{Node ID}$$

with $1 \le \text{Node ID} \le 127$.

### Equation 2 — Bus Length vs Bit Rate (CiA 301 / ISO 11898 nominal)

$$L_{\max}(\text{m}) \approx \dfrac{40\,000}{R_{\text{bit}}(\text{kbit/s})}$$

> Reference points: $1\,\text{Mbit/s} \to 25\text{–}40\,\text{m}$, $125\,\text{kbit/s} \to 500\,\text{m}$, $50\,\text{kbit/s} \to 1\,\text{km}$, $20\,\text{kbit/s} \to 2.5\,\text{km}$, $10\,\text{kbit/s} \to 5\,\text{km}$.

### Equation 3 — Maximum Number of Nodes

$$N_{\max} = 2^{7} - 1 = 127$$

(Node ID 0 is reserved for broadcast / NMT.)

### Equation 4 — Maximum SDO Expedited Payload

$$P_{\text{SDO,max}} = 4 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ bits}$$

### Equation 5 — Maximum PDO Payload

$$P_{\text{PDO,max}} = 8 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ bits}$$

(matches the CAN 2.0 standard data frame size.)

### Equation 6 — PDO Mapping Entry

$$M_i = (\text{Index}_i \ll 16) \,\vert\, (\text{Sub-index}_i \ll 8) \,\vert\, \text{BitLength}_i$$

with $i = 1, \ldots, N_{\text{mapped}}$, and $\sum_i \text{BitLength}_i \le 64$.

### Equation 7 — Object Dictionary Address Space

$$A_{\text{OD}} = 2^{16} = 65\,536 \text{ indices}$$

Each index may have up to $2^{8} = 256$ sub-indices.

### Equation 8 — Heartbeat Time vs Failure Detection

If a Heartbeat Consumer has a configured **Consumer Heartbeat Time** $T_c$ and the producer is set to **Producer Heartbeat Time** $T_p$, the consumer declares a failure if:

$$T_{\text{since-last}} > T_c$$

### Equation 9 — Bit Time Period (CAN)

$$T_{\text{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{R_{\text{bit}}}$$

with $T_{\text{bit}}$ segmented into **SYNC_SEG + PROP_SEG + PHASE_SEG1 + PHASE_SEG2**, and:

$$T_{\text{bit}} = t_q \cdot (1 + \text{PROP}_{\text{SEG}} + \text{PHASE}_{\text{SEG1}} + \text{PHASE}_{\text{SEG2}})$$

where $t_q$ is the **time quantum** set by the baud-rate prescaler.

### Equation 10 — Bus Throughput (theoretical)

$$\text{Throughput}_{\max} = R_{\text{bit}} \times \dfrac{8 \text{ bytes}}{N_{\text{bytes,frame}} + N_{\text{bytes,overhead}}}$$

For 8-byte CANopen PDO at 1 Mbit/s, the effective payload throughput is roughly **0.74 Mbit/s** (8 bytes data per 108 bits total ≈ 11-byte frame + inter-frame space).

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (non-safety) subsystems:** body controllers, lighting, infotainment, battery management.
- **Industrial automation:** servo drives (CiA 402), I/O blocks (CiA 401), hydraulic systems (CiA 408).
- **Medical devices:** laboratory analyzers, infusion pumps (CiA 425).
- **Off-highway vehicles, agricultural machines (CiA 461), construction equipment (CiA 470).**
- **Building automation (CiA 455), elevators (CiA 417/473), rail (CiA 423/424).**

The single biggest advantage is **vendor-interoperable, deterministic, real-time communication** with **automatic device discovery and configuration via EDS files**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Predefined COB-ID from Function Code and Node ID

The 11-bit CAN identifier for a communication object is computed as:

$$\text{COB-ID}_{10:0} = \underbrace{b_{10} b_9 b_8 b_7}_{4 \text{ function bits}}\; \underbrace{b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0}_{7 \text{ node bits}}$$

**Step 1 — Express function-code bits as the upper 4 bits of an 11-bit number.**

The 4-bit function code $F$ occupies bits 10 down to 7, which is a positional weight of $2^7$. Therefore:

$$\text{COB-ID} = F \times 2^7 + N$$

where $N$ is the 7-bit Node ID.

**Step 2 — Apply the bit-shift form.**

Since shifting a binary number $F$ left by 7 bit positions multiplies it by $2^7$:

$$\text{COB-ID} = (F \ll 7) + N$$

**Step 3 — Validate boundary conditions.**

- $F_{\min} = 0$ (NMT) $\Rightarrow$ COB-ID = $0 + N$, but COB-ID 0x000 is the NMT master, so slave-specific NMTs are not used.
- $F_{\max} = 14$ (0xE, NMT Error Control) $\Rightarrow$ COB-ID = $14 \times 128 + 127 = 1792 + 127 = 1919 = 0x77F$.
- COB-ID $\le 0x7FF$ since the standard CAN frame is 11 bits.

**Step 4 — Worked example.**

A slave has Node ID = 5. Compute the COB-IDs of its **TPDO1** (Function Code = 3), **RPDO2** (Function Code = 6), and **SDO server (tx)** (Function Code = 11).

- **TPDO1:** COB-ID = $3 \times 128 + 5 = 384 + 5 = 389 = \text{0x185}$.
- **RPDO2:** COB-ID = $6 \times 128 + 5 = 768 + 5 = 773 = \text{0x305}$.
- **SDO (server → client):** COB-ID = $11 \times 128 + 5 = 1408 + 5 = 1413 = \text{0x585}$.

These match the standard CiA 301 table values.

## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum PDO Mapped Data

CAN 2.0A standard frame data field holds at most:

$$L_{\text{byte}} = 8 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 1 — Each mapping slot consumes a 32-bit descriptor (Index 16 + Sub-index 8 + Bit Length 8).**

The mapping parameter (0x16xx or 0x1Axx) holds up to:

$$N_{\text{mapped,max}} = \text{sub-index 0x00 reports the actual number; theoretical max} = 4$$

because the 4 mapping descriptors would themselves fill 16 bytes — leaving no room for the data. In practice:

$$N_{\text{mapped,max}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{64 \text{ bits}}{32 \text{ bits}}\right\rfloor = 2 \text{ (mapping descriptors) if no data, but 0 if data is required}$$

The actual feasible number of data entries depends on their bit sizes.

**Step 2 — Sum of bit lengths must not exceed 64.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{mapped}}} \text{BitLength}_i \le 64 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Example.**

A device maps `0x6000:0x01` (16 bits, digital input word) and `0x6000:0x02` (32 bits, analog input).

- Total bits = $16 + 32 = 48 \le 64$ → OK.
- Mapping array: `[0x60000110, 0x60000220]` and sub-index 0 reports `0x02`.

## 3.3 Derivation — SDO Expedited Transfer Frame Layout

The 8-byte SDO download frame is:

| Byte | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | n | n | n | ccs |
| 1 | Index (LSB) |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| 2 | Index (MSB) |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| 3 | Sub-index |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| 4 | Data byte 0 (LSB) |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| 5 | Data byte 1 |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| 6 | Data byte 2 |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| 7 | Data byte 3 (MSB) |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

where `ccs` is the **client command specifier** and `nnn` is `4 − n_data_bytes`.

**Step 1 — Client command specifier for "Initiate Download (expedited, 4 bytes)"** is binary `0010` in the high nibble and `0b011` for download request, but per CiA 301 the value is **`0x23`** (decimal 35).

- The high nibble `0010` indicates "download".
- Bit 0 = 0 (expedited bit = 0 means data size = 4 bytes), bit 1 = 1 (data indicator set), bit 2 = 1 (expedited transfer).
- Wait — let us re-check: per the standard, `0x23` = `0b0010_0011`. The LSBs `011` mean: **e=1** (expedited), **s=1** (data set, i.e., size indicator shown), **reserved**.

**Step 2 — Number of unused data bytes** is encoded in the `n` bits (bits 5..3 of byte 0, which we labelled differently above). Actually the convention is:

- Bit 0 (CCS) = 1 for download, 2 for upload, 3 for block download, etc.
- Bit 1 (e) = 1 if expedited, 0 if segmented.
- Bit 2 (s) = 1 if size indicated.
- Bits 5..3 (n) = number of unused bytes (valid only if `e = 1` and `s = 1`).

**Step 3 — Worked example: write the value `0x12345678` to OD index `0x2000`, sub-index `0x01` of a server with Node ID 7.**

- COB-ID = 0x600 + 7 = 0x607.
- Byte 0 = 0x23 (4-byte expedited download, s=1, e=1, n=000 → 0 unused bytes).
- Byte 1 = 0x00 (Index LSB).
- Byte 2 = 0x20 (Index MSB).
- Byte 3 = 0x01 (Sub-index).
- Byte 4 = 0x78 (Data LSB).
- Byte 5 = 0x56.
- Byte 6 = 0x34.
- Byte 7 = 0x12 (Data MSB).

**Step 4 — Server response (Initiate Download Response):** COB-ID = 0x580 + 7 = 0x587, byte 0 = 0x60, index = 0x2000, sub-index = 0x01, all other bytes = 0x00. The single bit in byte 0 indicates "download successful" (no further data).

**Step 5 — If a problem occurred, the server returns an SDO Abort Transfer** at COB-ID 0x580 + NodeID, with byte 0 = 0x80, followed by the original index/sub-index and a 32-bit abort code.

## 3.4 Derivation — Object Dictionary Address Capacity

**Step 1 — Number of distinct indices.**

$$N_{\text{indices}} = 2^{16} = 65\,536$$

**Step 2 — Number of distinct sub-indices per index.**

$$N_{\text{subindices}} = 2^{8} = 256$$

**Step 3 — Total possible OD entries.**

$$N_{\text{entries,total}} = 65\,536 \times 256 = 16\,777\,216$$

Of course, in practice, only a few hundred entries are defined per device.

**Step 4 — Sub-index 0x00 is conventionally used to report the number of sub-elements** in a multi-element object (e.g., the number of mapped PDO entries, or the number of consumer-heartbeat entries in 0x1016).

## 3.5 Derivation — Bus Length vs Bit Rate Justification

The CAN bus requires that the propagation delay (both cable and transceivers) fit within a single bit time so that **arbitration works correctly**. The dominant limitation at high bit rates is the **propagation delay of the cable**, which is roughly **5 ns/m** for typical twisted-pair CAN cable.

**Step 1 — Total round-trip propagation delay for cable of length $L$ (m):**

$$t_{\text{prop}} = 2 \times L \times 5 \text{ ns/m} = 10 L \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Bit time at bit rate $R_{\text{bit}}$:**

$$T_{\text{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{R_{\text{bit}}}$$

**Step 3 — Conservative rule of thumb used by CiA / KTU:**

The bit time must exceed about 5 times the cable's one-way propagation time plus transceiver delays. This gives the empirical rule:

$$L_{\max} \cdot R_{\text{bit}} \lesssim 40 \text{ m·Mbit/s}$$

i.e.

$$L_{\max}(\text{m}) \le \dfrac{40\,000}{R_{\text{bit}}(\text{kbit/s})}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluations.**

| $R_{\text{bit}}$ | $L_{\max}$ (approx.) |
|---|---|
| 1000 kbit/s | 40 m |
| 500 kbit/s | 100 m |
| 250 kbit/s | 200 m |
| 125 kbit/s | 500 m |
| 50 kbit/s | 1000 m |
| 20 kbit/s | 2500 m |
| 10 kbit/s | 5000 m |

## 3.6 Derivation — NMT Boot-up Message

When a slave completes Initialization, it sends **one NMT Boot-up message** with:

- COB-ID = 0x700 + NodeID.
- DLC = 1, data byte = 0x00.

**Step 1 — Interpretation.**

The COB-ID `0x700` is from Function Code `0xE` (NMT Error Control). The data byte `0x00` indicates "boot-up" rather than the regular heartbeat states (0x04 = stopped, 0x05 = operational, 0x7F = pre-operational).

**Step 2 — State transition after boot-up.**

The slave then enters **Pre-operational**, and the master can read the slave's OD via SDO to configure PDOs, and finally send the NMT command `0x01` (Start Remote Node) to begin full operation.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right COB for Each Use-Case

| Engineering Use-Case | Recommended COB | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Cyclic encoder position feedback (1 ms cycle) | TPDO1 (synchronous, transmission type = 1) | Tight time-sync, low overhead |
| Emergency stop of a drive | EMCY + TPDO with event-time 0 | Highest priority broadcast |
| Tuning a controller gain from PLC | SDO download to OD 0x30xx | Confirmed, write access, audited |
| Distributing a 1 ms process clock | SYNC | Standardized trigger |
| Vendor wants to assign Node ID without DIP switches | LSS (CiA 305) on 0x7E5/0x7E4 | Avoids manual switches |
| A device with no master is needed (decentralized control) | Heartbeat producer + TPDO | Heartbeat still works without master polling |

## 3.8 KTU Numerical Worked Example — Verifying a COB-ID

> **Problem:** A CANopen slave has Node ID = 42. Compute the COB-IDs of its TPDO3, RPDO4, EMCY, and SDO (client → server).

**Step 1 — TPDO3 function code = 0x7 (7).**

$$\text{COB-ID} = 7 \times 128 + 42 = 896 + 42 = 938 = \text{0x3AA}$$

**Step 2 — RPDO4 function code = 0xA (10).**

$$\text{COB-ID} = 10 \times 128 + 42 = 1280 + 42 = 1322 = \text{0x52A}$$

**Step 3 — EMCY function code = 0x1 (1).**

$$\text{COB-ID} = 1 \times 128 + 42 = 128 + 42 = 170 = \text{0x0AA}$$

**Step 4 — SDO (client → server) function code = 0xC (12).**

$$\text{COB-ID} = 12 \times 128 + 42 = 1536 + 42 = 1578 = \text{0x62A}$$

All four values lie in the 11-bit range $\le 0x7FF$, confirming validity.

## 3.9 Verification of Predefined Set — Function-Code Reverse Table

Using the inverse:

$$F = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{COB-ID}}{128} \right\rfloor, \qquad N = \text{COB-ID} \bmod 128$$

Apply to a randomly seen bus message with COB-ID 0x387:

- $F = \lfloor 0x387 / 128 \rfloor = \lfloor 903/128 \rfloor = 7$ → TPDO3.
- $N = 0x387 \bmod 128 = 7$ → Node 7.

Therefore, COB-ID 0x387 = TPDO3 of Node 7.

> [!NOTE]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define the CANopen Object Dictionary and list its four major index regions with their hexadecimal ranges. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Object Dictionary (OD)** is a standardized table of all data objects (parameters, variables, I/O data) accessible within a CANopen device. It is the central data structure that makes CANopen devices vendor-independent and enables plug-and-play.

**Four major index regions:**

| Index Range | Region |
|---|---|
| 0x0000 | Reserved |
| 0x0001 – 0x009F | Data Type Definitions |
| 0x1000 – 0x1FFF | Communication Profile Area |
| 0x2000 – 0x5FFF | Manufacturer-Specific Area |
| 0x6000 – 0x9FFF | Standardized Device Profile Area |

Each OD entry contains: **Index (16 bits), Sub-index (8 bits), Name, Data Type, Attribute (R/W), and Value**.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between PDO and SDO in CANopen. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PDO (Process Data Object) | SDO (Service Data Object) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Real-time process data exchange | Configuration and parameter access |
| Protocol overhead | None (raw 8 bytes) | Command specifier + index + sub-index |
| Speed | Fast, low latency | Slower, confirmed |
| Transmission | Producer/Consumer, broadcast | Client/Server, peer-to-peer |
| Triggering | Event, timer, SYNC, RTR | On client request |
| COB-IDs | 0x180–0x4FF + NodeID | 0x580 + NodeID (server), 0x600 + NodeID (client) |
| Payload | Up to 8 bytes | Up to 4 bytes (expedited) or more (segmented/block) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

### Question A — CANopen Communication Architecture and Object Dictionary

**(a) Explain the CANopen protocol stack and the role of the Object Dictionary. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **CANopen protocol stack** is organized in **five layers** built on top of the CAN data link and physical layers:

1. **Physical Layer (ISO 11898-2 / -3):** Differential signalling on **CAN-H** and **CAN-L** lines, terminated with **120 Ω** at each end. Supports **10 kbit/s to 1 Mbit/s**.

2. **Data Link Layer (ISO 11898-1):** Standard **CAN 2.0A** with 11-bit identifier, providing bitwise arbitration, CRC, acknowledgement, and error confinement.

3. **Application Layer (CiA 301):** The CANopen-specific layer. Defines the **Object Dictionary**, **Communication Objects (COBs)**, **Predefined Connection Set**, and **NMT state machine**.

4. **Device Profile Layer (CiA 4xx):** Application-specific behaviour, e.g., **CiA 401** for digital/analog I/O, **CiA 402** for drives and motion control.

5. **Application / User Code:** Vendor's control program that reads/writes the OD.

**Role of the Object Dictionary (OD):**

- Acts as a **central interface** between the application and the CANopen communication layer.
- Every data point that a device exposes (inputs, outputs, parameters, configuration) must be defined as an OD entry.
- Each entry is addressed by a unique **Index (16 bits) and Sub-index (8 bits)**.
- The OD makes CANopen devices **vendor-independent**: any CANopen master can read a device's OD via SDO without prior knowledge, provided the device ships with an **EDS (Electronic Data Sheet)**.
- The OD is divided into standardized regions (0x1000–0x1FFF for communication, 0x2000–0x5FFF for manufacturer, 0x6000–0x9FFF for device profile).
- SDO uses the OD for **parameter access**; PDO uses OD entries for **process data mapping**.

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the CANopen NMT slave state machine. List the communication objects active in each state. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A CANopen NMT slave can be in one of the following states:

1. **Initialization** — Power-on / reset. Device executes hardware self-test, sets up OD to default values, and sends a single **NMT Boot-up message** (COB-ID = 0x700 + NodeID, data byte = 0x00). Then it auto-transitions to **Pre-operational**.

2. **Pre-operational** — Active: **NMT, SDO, SYNC, TIME, EMCY, Heartbeat, Boot-up**. Inactive: **PDO (TPDO & RPDO)**. Master uses SDO to configure the slave (PDO mapping, communication parameters, heartbeat time, etc.).

3. **Operational** — Active: **All communication objects**, including PDOs. The slave exchanges real-time process data.

4. **Stopped** — Active: **NMT only**. The slave is quiet but still receives NMT commands.

**NMT Command Format:** COB-ID = 0x000, DLC = 2, Byte 0 = command specifier, Byte 1 = NodeID (0 for broadcast).

| NMT Command (Byte 0) | Effect |
|---|---|
| 0x01 | Start Remote Node (→ Operational) |
| 0x02 | Stop Remote Node (→ Stopped) |
| 0x80 | Enter Pre-operational |
| 0x81 | Reset Node (full restart) |
| 0x82 | Reset Communication (only communication stack resets) |

**State diagram** (see Section 4.2 for the Mermaid version).

**COB Activity Table:**

| State | NMT | SDO | PDO | SYNC | EMCY | Heartbeat |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initialization | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| Pre-operational | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Operational | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Stopped | ✓ | — | — | — | — | — |

---

### Question B — PDO, SDO, and Predefined Connection Set

**(a) Explain the Predefined Connection Set of CANopen and derive the COB-ID for a slave with Node ID 12. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Predefined Connection Set** (defined in **CiA 301**) is a fixed mapping from COB type to the 11-bit CAN identifier, allowing CANopen devices to communicate **without prior configuration**. The 11-bit ID is split as:

- **Bits 10–7:** Function Code (4 bits) — identifies the COB type.
- **Bits 6–0:** Node ID (7 bits) — identifies the slave (1–127).

$$\text{COB-ID} = F \times 2^{7} + N$$

**COB-ID table (relevant extracts):**

| COB | Function Code (F) | Base (F×128) |
|---|---|---|
| NMT | 0x0 | 0x000 |
| SYNC | 0x1 | 0x080 |
| EMCY | 0x1 | 0x080 + NID |
| TIME | 0x2 | 0x100 |
| TPDO1 | 0x3 | 0x180 + NID |
| RPDO1 | 0x4 | 0x200 + NID |
| TPDO2 | 0x5 | 0x280 + NID |
| RPDO2 | 0x6 | 0x300 + NID |
| TPDO3 | 0x7 | 0x380 + NID |
| RPDO3 | 0x8 | 0x400 + NID |
| TPDO4 | 0x9 | 0x480 + NID |
| RPDO4 | 0xA | 0x500 + NID |
| SDO (server → client) | 0xB | 0x580 + NID |
| SDO (client → server) | 0xC | 0x600 + NID |
| Heartbeat / Boot-up | 0xE | 0x700 + NID |

**For Node ID = 12:**

| COB | Computation | COB-ID (hex) |
|---|---|---|
| TPDO1 | 3 × 128 + 12 | 0x18C |
| TPDO2 | 5 × 128 + 12 | 0x28C |
| TPDO3 | 7 × 128 + 12 | 0x38C |
| TPDO4 | 9 × 128 + 12 | 0x48C |
| RPDO1 | 4 × 128 + 12 | 0x20C |
| RPDO2 | 6 × 128 + 12 | 0x30C |
| RPDO3 | 8 × 128 + 12 | 0x40C |
| RPDO4 | 10 × 128 + 12 | 0x50C |
| EMCY | 1 × 128 + 12 | 0x08C |
| SDO (server → client) | 11 × 128 + 12 | 0x58C |
| SDO (client → server) | 12 × 128 + 12 | 0x60C |
| Heartbeat / Boot-up | 14 × 128 + 12 | 0x70C |

**(b) Explain PDO communication and PDO mapping with a suitable example. Compare event-driven, synchronous-cyclic, and synchronous-acyclic TPDO transmission modes. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**PDO Communication:**

A **Process Data Object (PDO)** is the real-time data carrier of CANopen. It transfers **up to 8 bytes** of process data with **no protocol overhead** (no index/sub-index, no confirmation). The producer pushes the PDO onto the bus; one or more consumers accept it.

There are **two flavours**:
- **TPDO (Transmit PDO):** Produced by the node, consumed by others.
- **RPDO (Receive PDO):** Consumed by the node, produced by others.

A node supports up to **4 TPDOs and 4 RPDOs** in the predefined set.

**PDO Communication Parameters** (OD 0x1400–0x15FF for RPDO, 0x1800–0x19FF for TPDO):
- **COB-ID** (sub 0x01)
- **Transmission Type** (sub 0x02) — 0 to 255
- **Inhibit Time** (sub 0x03) — minimum gap between two TPDOs in 100 µs units
- **Event Time** (sub 0x05) — periodic TPDO send interval in ms

**PDO Mapping Parameters** (OD 0x1600–0x17FF for RPDO, 0x1A00–0x1BFF for TPDO):

Each PDO is described by an array of mapping values, where each entry (32 bits) encodes:

$$M_i = (\text{Index}_i \ll 16) \,\vert\, (\text{Sub-index}_i \ll 8) \,\vert\, \text{BitLength}_i$$

Sub-index 0x00 reports the number of mapped objects (must be ≥ 1 and the total bit length ≤ 64).

**Example:**

Map `0x2000:0x01` (16-bit value A) and `0x2000:0x02` (32-bit value B) into TPDO1.

- 0x1A00:0x00 = 0x02 (number of mapped objects)
- 0x1A00:0x01 = 0x2000_01_10 (Index 0x2000, Sub 0x01, 16 bits)
- 0x1A00:0x02 = 0x2000_02_20 (Index 0x2000, Sub 0x02, 32 bits)
- Total bit length = 16 + 32 = 48 bits ≤ 64 ✓

**TPDO Transmission Modes:**

| Mode | Transmission Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous, acyclic** | 0 | TPDO sent only when its data has changed and a SYNC is received |
| **Synchronous, cyclic** | 1–240 | TPDO sent every Nth SYNC message (N = transmission type) |
| **Synchronous, RTR-only** | 252 | TPDO sent only when a remote request arrives, after a SYNC |
| **Asynchronous, RTR-only** | 253 | TPDO sent only on remote request, with no SYNC needed |
| **Asynchronous, manufacturer-specific** | 254 | TPDO sent on internal event; inhibit time and event time apply; profile-specific behaviour |
| **Asynchronous, device-profile specific** | 255 | TPDO sent on internal event; profile-specific behaviour |

**Real-world usage:**
- **Synchronous-cyclic (Transmission Type = 1):** for a digital encoder where the master needs a fresh reading on every SYNC.
- **Asynchronous (Transmission Type = 254 or 255):** for an emergency stop pushbutton — the slave sends a TPDO immediately on press, with inhibit time = 0 and event time = 0.
- **Synchronous-acyclic (Transmission Type = 0):** for a slow-changing temperature value.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the COB-ID formula. KTU answers frequently write `F × 256 + N` (shift of 8) instead of the correct **shift of 7** (`× 128`). A full mark is lost on this.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that the **SDO client → server COB-ID is 0x600 + NID**, and the **server → client is 0x580 + NID**. Many students invert them.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing the wrong SDO command specifier. The "Initiate Download (expedited, 4 bytes)" is **0x23**, not 0x22. "Initiate Upload" is **0x40**, not 0x41.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Drawing the NMT state machine with **5 states** instead of the correct **4 (+ boot-up)**. Pre-operational and Operational must both be reachable from Stopped.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing **Heartbeat** (producer pushes) with **Node Guarding** (master polls). KTU answers from 2023–2024 mark you down for this.
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Saying "CANopen is a new bus" — it is **not**; it is an **application-layer protocol running on CAN**.
> - **Common Mistake 7:** Mixing up **CiA 301** (application layer / OD) with **CiA 401/402** (device profiles).
> - **Common Mistake 8:** Writing "CANopen supports 256 nodes" — the correct number is **127** (Node IDs 1–127).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **CANopen** is an **application-layer protocol** built on **CAN** (ISO 11898), not a new bus. Standardized as **EN 50325-4**, maintained by **CiA**.
- ☐ Core standard is **CiA 301** (Application Layer + Communication Profile).
- ☐ Device profiles: **CiA 401** (I/O), **CiA 402** (Drives/Motion), **CiA 305** (LSS).
- ☐ Max **127** nodes; Node ID = 1–127; **0 = broadcast**.
- ☐ Bit rates: **10 kbit/s to 1 Mbit/s**; default **125 kbit/s**.
- ☐ Bus length rule: $L_{\max} \cdot R_{\text{bit}} \le 40\,000$ (m·kbit/s) approx.
- ☐ **Object Dictionary** = central data table, **16-bit index, 8-bit sub-index**.
- ☐ OD regions: **0x0001–0x009F** types, **0x1000–0x1FFF** comm profile, **0x2000–0x5FFF** vendor, **0x6000–0x9FFF** device profile.
- ☐ **COB-ID = Function Code × 128 + Node ID** (function code in upper 4 bits).
- ☐ **Predefined Set:** SYNC 0x080, EMCY 0x081+N, TPDO1 0x180+N, RPDO1 0x200+N, …, TPDO4 0x480+N, RPDO4 0x500+N, SDO server→client 0x580+N, SDO client→server 0x600+N, Heartbeat/Boot-up 0x700+N.
- ☐ **PDO** = real-time, ≤ 8 bytes, no protocol overhead. **SDO** = configuration, confirmed, with index/sub-index. Three SDO types: **expedited (≤4 B), segmented, block**.
- ☐ **Mapping value (32 bits) = (Index ≪ 16) ∨ (Sub-index ≪ 8) ∨ BitLength.**
- ☐ **NMT States:** Initialization → Pre-operational → Operational ↔ Stopped. **PDOs only active in Operational.**
- ☐ **NMT commands:** 0x01 Start, 0x02 Stop, 0x80 Pre-op, 0x81 Reset Node, 0x82 Reset Comm. COB-ID = 0x000.
- ☐ **Error control:** **Heartbeat** (producer pushes) preferred over **Node Guarding** (master polls). Heartbeat uses **OD 0x1017** (producer time) and **0x1016** (consumer time).
- ☐ **EMCY** = COB-ID 0x081 + NID, 8 bytes: 16-bit error code + 8-bit error register + 5 vendor bytes.
- ☐ **SYNC** = COB-ID 0x080, DLC 0; producer broadcasts to trigger synchronous PDOs.
- ☐ **EDS file** = Electronic Data Sheet describing a device's OD; required for tool-based configuration.
- ☐ **PDO transmission types:** 0 = sync-acyclic; 1–240 = sync-cyclic; 252/253 = RTR-only; 254/255 = asynchronous event-driven.
- ☐ **Inhibit time** = minimum gap between two TPDOs (in 100 µs units). **Event time** = periodic send interval (in ms).
- ☐ **LSS (CiA 305)** uses COB-IDs **0x7E5** (master) and **0x7E4** (slave) to configure Node ID and bit rate at runtime.
- ☐ **Vendor-independent interoperability** is the central engineering promise of CANopen.

**KTU Last-Minute Mnemonics:**

- **"4 T, 4 R, 1 S, 1 S, 1 E"** = **4** TPDOs, **4** RPDOs, **1** SDO server, **1** SDO client, **1** EMCY per node.
- **"Init → Pre → Op → Stop"** — NMT states in order.
- **"PDO is Push, SDO is Pull"** — quick recall of who initiates.
- **"128, not 256"** — to avoid the COB-ID shift mistake.
- **"Heartbeat = hostless"** — works without an NMT master polling.

---

### End of Module 2 — CANopen Notes
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies: PLC-Based Traffic Light System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: PLC-Based Traffic Light System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **PLC-Based Traffic Light Control System** is a sequential, real-time embedded control application in which a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** supervises the cyclic illumination of red, amber, and green signal lamps at a road intersection. The PLC executes a deterministic **cyclic scan** (input scan → program scan → output scan) of a user-defined **ladder logic program** that implements a finite state machine (FSM) governing phase transitions based on preset timer values, pushbutton inputs, and vehicle/pedestrian detection sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> A PLC-based traffic light controller is a hard real-time, event-driven sequential control system wherein a PLC replaces the conventional electromechanical or microcontroller-based relay logic to drive signal heads, ensuring deterministic phase sequencing, fail-safe operation, and ease of reprogramming through soft logic (IEC 61131-3) without altering the physical wiring.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **traffic policeman standing at a four-way intersection** blowing a whistle and gesturing with his hands. The policeman's brain decides *when* to switch directions; his whistle and hand gestures are the *outputs*. Now, replace the policeman with a **small industrial computer (the PLC)** that follows a strict written rulebook (the ladder program). The rulebook says:

- "Keep the North–South light **GREEN** for **30 seconds**."
- "Then turn it **AMBER** for **5 seconds**."
- "Then turn it **RED** while East–West becomes **GREEN** for **30 seconds**."
- "Repeat forever, but if a pedestrian presses the crosswalk button, extend the red phase by 10 seconds."

That rulebook, written in **Ladder Logic**, is what we program into the PLC. The PLC is essentially a *digital, tireless, microsecond-fast traffic policeman* that never gets bored and never makes a timing mistake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a PLC and not a microcontroller?**
> - **Industrial ruggedness** (vibration, temperature, electrical noise immunity).
> - **Galvanic isolation** between field wiring and CPU.
> - **Hot-swap I/O modules** for maintenance without shutdown.
> - **Standardized IEC 61131-3 programming** — vendor-independent logic.
> - **Long Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** typically > 100,000 hours.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (Urban Intersection) |
|---|---|
| Green phase duration | **30 s** |
| Amber (yellow) phase | **3 – 5 s** |
| All-red clearance | **2 s** |
| Full cycle time | **60 – 120 s** |
| Supply to signal lamps | **230 V AC / 24 V DC** |
| PLC scan time | **< 10 ms** |
| Lamp wattage (LED) | **12 – 25 W** per aspect |
| Lamp wattage (incandescent) | **60 – 150 W** per aspect |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Architecture (Three-Tier Decomposition)

**Tier 1 — Field (Input/Output) Layer:**
- **Inputs:** Start pushbutton (NO), Stop pushbutton (NC), Pedestrian request (NO), Vehicle inductive loop sensors (4 Nos.).
- **Outputs:** Red, Amber, Green lamps for North–South (3) + East–West (3) = **6 discrete outputs**. Pedestrian walk/don't-walk indicators (2 outputs).

**Tier 2 — Control Layer (The PLC):**
- A modular PLC (e.g., Siemens S7-1200, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1400, Schneider M221) with:
  - **CPU module** running the cyclic scan.
  - **DI module** (Digital Input, 24 V DC sinking).
  - **DO module** (Digital Output, relay or transistor).
  - **Power supply unit (PSU)** 230 V AC → 24 V DC.

**Tier 3 — Supervisory Layer (Optional):**
- **SCADA / HMI** for traffic engineers to override timings, view lamp-status diagnostics, and log phase counts.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — The Cyclic State Machine

A traffic light system is mathematically a **Moore Finite State Machine (FSM)** with $N$ discrete states. The system dwells in each state for a fixed time interval governed by PLC on-delay timers (TON, IEC 61131-3).

Let the states be denoted as:
- $S_0$: NS = Green, EW = Red
- $S_1$: NS = Amber, EW = Red
- $S_2$: NS = Red, EW = Red (all-red clearance)
- $S_3$: NS = Red, EW = Green
- $S_4$: NS = Red, EW = Amber
- $S_5$: NS = Red, EW = Red (all-red clearance)

**Transition Rule:** $S_i \xrightarrow{\;T_{dwell,i}\;} S_{i+1 \pmod N}$

The PLC program is structured into **rungs**, where each rung implements one of the following:
1. **Rung 1:** Master Start/Stop latching relay (seal-in contact).
2. **Rung 2–7:** Six state-sealing rungs (one per state), each energized by the previous state's "done" bit.
3. **Rung 8–13:** Output decoding rungs (one per lamp) that are energized only when the corresponding state is active.
4. **Rung 14:** Pedestrian extension logic (adds $\Delta t_{ped}$ to red phase of opposing direction).
5. **Rung 15:** Lamp-failure fault interlock.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** Vertical bars in formulas are written as `\vert` (LaTeX) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Full cycle time | $T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} T_{dwell,i}$ | Sum of all state dwell times | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Green-time ratio (NS) | $G_{NS} = \dfrac{T_{G,NS}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Green allocation for NS direction | dimensionless |
| 3 | Saturation flow (Webster) | $S = 1800 \, \text{veh/hr/lane}$ | Per-lane discharge rate | veh/hr |
| 4 | Effective green time | $g = G + Y - t_L$ | Loss time $t_L$ subtracted | s |
| 5 | Degree of saturation | $X = \dfrac{q}{c}$ | $q$ = demand flow, $c$ = capacity | unitless |
| 6 | Capacity per lane | $c = S \cdot \dfrac{g}{C}$ | Webster's capacity model | veh/hr |
| 7 | PLC scan period | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out}$ | Cyclic scan decomposition | ms |
| 8 | Timer preset | $T_{preset} = T_{dwell} \cdot k_{time\,base}$ | $k_{time\,base}$ = 1 for 1 s | s |
| 9 | Lamp power consumption | $P_{lamp} = V^2 \,/\, R_{filament}$ | Incandescent (legacy) | W |
| 10 | LED current draw | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{P_{LED}}{V_{DC}}$ | Constant-current driver | A |
| 11 | MTBF estimate | $MTBF = \dfrac{t_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Reliability metric | hours |
| 12 | Boolean state equation | $Y_{NS,Green} = \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_2} \cdot S_0$ | Lamp-on decoding logic | Boolean |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Is Deployed

- **Urban traffic intersections** (city corporation).
- **Toll booth lane indicators** (plaza control).
- **Airport taxiway lighting** (runway/taxiway crossings).
- **Industrial cross-dock gates** (forklift/pedestrian safety).
- **Smart-city adaptive corridors** with **SCADA + IoT** layer on top of the PLC.
- **Railway level-crossing gates** with boom-barrier interlocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the KTU syllabus mandates this case study:**
> It integrates *all* Module 2 concepts — **I/O addressing, ladder logic, timers (TON/TOF), counters (CTU), latching relays (seal-in contacts), and the PLC scan cycle** — into a single relatable, real-world application. It is the most frequently asked case study in KTU ESE for this course.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 I/O Address Allocation Table (Exhaustive)

Let us assume a **Siemens S7-1200** PLC with the following default address scheme (16-bit I/O):

| Slot | Symbol | Address | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DI 0 | `Start_PB` | I 0.0 | NO Pushbutton | Master start |
| DI 1 | `Stop_PB` | I 0.1 | NC Pushbutton | Master stop |
| DI 2 | `Ped_Request` | I 0.2 | NO Pushbutton | Pedestrian crossing request |
| DI 3 | `Loop_NS` | I 0.3 | Inductive loop | Vehicle sensor (NS) |
| DI 4 | `Loop_EW` | I 0.4 | Inductive loop | Vehicle sensor (EW) |
| DI 5 | `Lamp_OK_NS_R` | I 0.5 | Contact from monitor relay | Lamp-health feedback |
| DO 0 | `NS_Red` | Q 0.0 | Contactor coil | North–South red |
| DO 1 | `NS_Amb` | Q 0.1 | Contactor coil | North–South amber |
| DO 2 | `NS_Grn` | Q 0.2 | Contactor coil | North–South green |
| DO 3 | `EW_Red` | Q 0.3 | Contactor coil | East–West red |
| DO 4 | `EW_Amb` | Q 0.4 | Contactor coil | East–West amber |
| DO 5 | `EW_Grn` | Q 0.5 | Contactor coil | East–West green |
| DO 6 | `Walk_OK` | Q 0.6 | Indicator lamp | Pedestrian walk |
| DO 7 | `MCR` | Q 0.7 | Master control relay | System enable |

### 3.2 Derivation of the State Timer Sequence

**Given:**
- Green dwell time $T_G = 30$ s
- Amber dwell time $T_Y = 5$ s
- All-red clearance $T_R = 2$ s

**Step 1 — Total cycle time calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 2 \cdot T_G + 2 \cdot T_Y + 2 \cdot T_R \\
T_{cycle} &= 2(30) + 2(5) + 2(2) \\
T_{cycle} &= 60 + 10 + 4 \\
T_{cycle} &= 74 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Two directions (NS and EW) each get a green, amber, and red clearance, hence the factor of 2.

**Step 2 — Green-time ratio for NS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{NS} &= \frac{T_G}{T_{cycle}} \\
G_{NS} &= \frac{30}{74} \\
G_{NS} &\approx 0.4054 \quad \text{or} \quad 40.54\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The NS direction receives a green aspect for 40.54% of the total cycle.

**Step 3 — Effective green time (Webster correction for lost time):**

Let start-up lost time $l_1 = 2$ s and clearance lost time $l_2 = 2$ s. Total lost time per phase $t_L = l_1 + l_2 = 4$ s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_{NS} &= T_G + T_Y - t_L \\
g_{NS} &= 30 + 5 - 4 \\
g_{NS} &= 31 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Capacity per lane (using Webster's formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= S \cdot \frac{g}{C} \\
c &= 1800 \cdot \frac{31}{74} \\
c &\approx 1800 \cdot 0.4189 \\
c &\approx 754 \text{ veh/hr/lane}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each lane at the NS approach can discharge approximately 754 vehicles per hour at saturation.

**Step 5 — Degree of saturation (validation):**

Assume observed demand $q = 600$ veh/hr/lane.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{q}{c} \\
X &= \frac{600}{754} \\
X &\approx 0.796
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $X < 0.85$ (typical design threshold), the timing plan is **acceptable** and the intersection will not experience recurrent congestion.

### 3.3 Boolean Logic Equations for Lamp Output Decoding

The output coils are energized using combinational logic over the state bits $S_0$ through $S_5$. Define $S_i = 1$ when the system is dwelling in state $i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NS\_Grn &= S_0 \\
NS\_Amb &= S_1 \\
NS\_Red &= S_2 + S_3 + S_4 + S_5 \\
EW\_Grn &= S_3 \\
EW\_Amb &= S_4 \\
EW\_Red &= S_0 + S_1 + S_2 + S_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boolean expansion for NS_Red (proof of correctness):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NS\_Red &= \overline{S_0} \cdot \overline{S_1} \quad \text{(since } \overline{S_0} \text{ covers } S_2 \text{ through } S_5 \text{, minus } S_1 \text{)} \\
        &\text{or equivalently, by direct union:} \\
NS\_Red &= S_2 \lor S_3 \lor S_4 \lor S_5 \\
        &= \overline{S_0} \cdot \overline{S_1} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic check:** The NS_Red lamp must be ON whenever NS is *not* green and *not* amber. That is precisely states $S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5$. Q.E.D.

### 3.4 Master Control Relay (MCR) Derivation

The MCR is a hard-wired safety relay that de-energizes all outputs upon emergency stop. Its Boolean equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MCR &= Start\_PB \cdot \overline{Stop\_PB} \cdot Lamp\_Health\_OK \\
    &= I0.0 \cdot \overline{I0.1} \cdot I0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

All output rungs are placed *inside* the MCR zone; if $MCR = 0$, all output coils lose power, returning signals to **flashing amber** (fail-safe mode) using a hardware bypass.

### 3.5 Pedestrian Extension Logic — Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** `Ped_Request` (I 0.2) is pressed during $S_3$ (EW green, NS red). The PLC should extend the NS red phase by 10 s to allow safe pedestrian crossing.

**Step 1:** Detect the rising edge of `Ped_Request` while in state $S_3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ped\_Pulse &= S_3 \cdot \text{RisingEdge}(Ped\_Request)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Latch a memory bit `Ped_Latch`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ped\_Latch(t) = \big[ Ped\_Pulse(t) \lor Ped\_Latch(t-1) \big] \cdot \overline{Ped\_Served(t-1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Modify the NS red phase timer preset dynamically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{NS,Red,\,extended} &= T_{NS,Red,\,base} + \Delta t_{ped} \\
                      &= 37 + 10 = 47 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

where base NS red = $T_Y + T_R + T_G + T_Y + T_R = 5 + 2 + 30 + 5 + 2 = 44$ s, plus 3 s for the all-red gap just before NS green, total = 37 s base + 10 s extension = 47 s.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The pedestrian extension is the most common *application-level* question in KTU ESE. Always show the *latch/reset* structure explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    psu[230V AC Power Supply]
    psu --> cpu[PLC CPU Module S7-1200]
    cpu --> di[Digital Input Module 24V DC]
    cpu --> do[Digital Output Module Relay]
    cpu --> mem[Program Memory Ladder Logic]

    pb1[Start Pushbutton NO] --> di
    pb2[Stop Pushbutton NC] --> di
    pb3[Pedestrian Request NO] --> di
    loop1[Inductive Loop NS] --> di
    loop2[Inductive Loop EW] --> di
    mon[Lamp Health Monitor] --> di

    do --> lampNSR[NS Red Lamp 230V]
    do --> lampNSA[NS Amber Lamp]
    do --> lampNSG[NS Green Lamp]
    do --> lampEWR[EW Red Lamp]
    do --> lampEWA[EW Amber Lamp]
    do --> lampEWG[EW Green Lamp]
    do --> walkInd[Pedestrian Walk Indicator]
    do --> mcrCoil[Master Control Relay Coil]

    scada[SCADA HMI Touchscreen] -.Optional Modbus TCP.-> cpu
```

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram (FSM for Traffic Phases)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0_NS_Green
    S0_NS_Green --> S1_NS_Amber : Timer T_G done 30s
    S1_NS_Amber --> S2_AllRed_1 : Timer T_Y done 5s
    S2_AllRed_1 --> S3_EW_Green : Timer T_R done 2s
    S3_EW_Green --> S4_EW_Amber : Timer T_G done 30s
    S4_EW_Amber --> S5_AllRed_2 : Timer T_Y done 5s
    S5_AllRed_2 --> S0_NS_Green : Timer T_R done 2s
    S3_EW_Green --> S3_EW_Green : Ped_Request latched extends by 10s
```

### 4.3 Ladder Logic Rung Structure (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rung1[Rung 1: Master Latch Start PB seal-in Stop PB]
    rung2[Rung 2: MCR coil energized by rung1 AND lamp health]
    rung3[Rung 3: State 0 S0 seal using timer done bit of S5]
    rung4[Rung 4: State 1 S1 seal using timer done bit of S0]
    rung5[Rung 5: State 2 S2 seal using timer done bit of S1]
    rung6[Rung 6: State 3 S3 seal using timer done bit of S2]
```
