```mermaid
    rung7[Rung 7: State 4 S4 seal using timer done bit of S3]
    rung8[Rung 8: State 5 S5 seal using timer done bit of S4]
    rung9[Rung 9: NS Green coil driven by S0 inside MCR]
    rung10[Rung 10: NS Amber coil driven by S1 inside MCR]
    rung11[Rung 11: NS Red coil driven by S2 OR S3 OR S4 OR S5]
    rung12[Rung 12: EW Green coil driven by S3 inside MCR]
    rung13[Rung 13: EW Amber coil driven by S4 inside MCR]
    rung14[Rung 14: EW Red coil driven by S0 OR S1 OR S2 OR S5]
    rung15[Rung 15: Pedestrian request latch and red-phase extension]
    rung16[Rung 16: Walk indicator driven by Ped_Latch]

    rung1 --> rung2
    rung2 --> rung3 --> rung4 --> rung5 --> rung6 --> rung7 --> rung8
    rung8 --> rung9
    rung8 --> rung10
    rung8 --> rung11
    rung8 --> rung12
    rung8 --> rung13
    rung8 --> rung14
    rung8 --> rung15
    rung15 --> rung16
```

### 4.4 PLC Scan Cycle Timing Diagram (Cyclic Operation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as Field Inputs
    participant DI as DI Module
    participant CPU as PLC CPU
    participant Prog as Ladder Program
    participant DO as DO Module
    participant Lamp as Signal Lamps

    loop Every Scan Cycle approx 8ms
        Sensor->>DI: Read physical signals
        DI->>CPU: Update Process Image Input PII
        CPU->>Prog: Execute ladder top to bottom
        Prog->>CPU: Update Process Image Output PIQ
        CPU->>DO: Write to output latches
        DO->>Lamp: Energize contactors
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight (Diagram 4.4):** The cyclic nature of the PLC means that the *worst-case response time* to a pedestrian button press is one full scan time. For a 5 ms scan, latency is 5 ms — negligible for human perception (< 100 ms threshold).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Explain with a neat block diagram how a PLC is used to implement a traffic light control system. List the inputs and outputs required.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A PLC-based traffic light control system uses a PLC as the central controller replacing conventional relay logic. The **block diagram** consists of:
- **Input devices** (left side): Start pushbutton (I 0.0), Stop pushbutton (I 0.1), pedestrian request (I 0.2), vehicle sensors (I 0.3, I 0.4).
- **PLC unit** (center): CPU + DI module + DO module + 24 V DC power supply.
- **Output devices** (right side): Six signal lamps (NS-Red, NS-Amber, NS-Green, EW-Red, EW-Amber, EW-Green) + pedestrian walk indicator + master control relay.

The PLC executes a ladder program in cyclic scan mode to drive the lamps in a predetermined sequence with on-delay timers. **(3 marks)**

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a PLC-based traffic controller and a microcontroller-based traffic controller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PLC-Based | Microcontroller-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **Programming** | Ladder logic (IEC 61131-3), graphical | Embedded C / Assembly |
| **I/O handling** | Galvanically isolated, modular, hot-swappable | Direct GPIO, limited isolation |
| **Reliability** | Industrial grade, MTBF > 100,000 hrs | Consumer grade |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Maintenance** | Easy, software-only changes | Hardware + firmware re-flash |
| **Scan determinism** | Guaranteed cyclic scan | Interrupt-driven, non-deterministic |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (Module Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

---

#### **Question B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**a)** Design a PLC-based ladder logic program for a four-way traffic intersection. Use **timers and latching contacts** to sequence the lights as follows: NS-Green (30 s) → NS-Amber (5 s) → All-Red (2 s) → EW-Green (30 s) → EW-Amber (5 s) → All-Red (2 s). Draw the **ladder diagram** and explain the operation. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A pedestrian pushbutton is provided. When pressed during the EW-Green phase, the NS-Red phase should be **extended by 10 s** to allow safe crossing. Implement the **pedestrian extension logic** using auxiliary memory bits and timers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Option A — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define state memory bits (1 mark):**
- `M0` = NS Green active
- `M1` = NS Amber active
- `M2` = All-Red (1) active
- `M3` = EW Green active
- `M4` = EW Amber active
- `M5` = All-Red (2) active
- `T1` = 30 s green timer
- `T2` = 5 s amber timer
- `T3` = 2 s all-red timer

**Step 2 — State-sealing rungs (3 marks):**
Each state is latched by the **done bit** of the previous state's timer and sealed-in (i.e., ORed with its own contact). The structure is:

- **Rung for M0:** Contact `M5` in series with contact `T3.DN` in parallel with `M0` (seal) → coil `M0`.
- **Rung for M1:** Contact `T1.DN` in parallel with `M1` → coil `M1`.
- **Rung for M2:** Contact `T2.DN` in parallel with `M2` → coil `M2`.
- **Rung for M3:** Contact `T3.DN` (from M2 timer) in parallel with `M3` → coil `M3`.
- **Rung for M4:** Contact `T1.DN` (from M3) in parallel with `M4` → coil `M4`.
- **Rung for M5:** Contact `T2.DN` (from M4) in parallel with `M5` → coil `M5`.

**Step 3 — Output decoding rungs (2 marks):**

| Lamp | Energized by |
|---|---|
| `Q0.0` (NS-Red) | `M1` OR `M2` OR `M3` OR `M4` OR `M5` |
| `Q0.1` (NS-Amber) | `M1` |
| `Q0.2` (NS-Green) | `M0` |
| `Q0.3` (EW-Red) | `M0` OR `M1` OR `M2` OR `M5` |
| `Q0.4` (EW-Amber) | `M4` |
| `Q0.5` (EW-Green) | `M3` |

**Step 4 — Operation explanation (1 mark):** On power-on, `M0` is preset true, starting the green phase for NS. After 30 s, `T1.DN` energizes `M1`, starting the amber phase. After 5 s, `M2` becomes true (all-red). After 2 s, `M3` starts EW green. The sequence repeats indefinitely until the Stop pushbutton breaks the master seal.

---

#### **Model Solution for Option A — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Pedestrian request detection (2 marks):**
Use a **rising-edge one-shot** (OSR instruction) on `I0.2` while state `M3` is active:

> `Ped_Pulse = M3 · RisingEdge(I0.2)`

**Step 2 — Latch memory bit (2 marks):**
A latch bit `M10` (Ped_Latch) is set by `Ped_Pulse` and reset by a "Ped_Served" done bit:

> `M10 = (M10 + Ped_Pulse) · NOT(Ped_Served)`

**Step 3 — Modify the green-phase timer dynamically (2 marks):**
Use a **movable preset** instruction to alter `T1.PRE` for the NS green phase. If `M10 = 1`, preset changes from 30 s to 40 s (extra 10 s). This is done via:

> `IF M10 THEN T1.PRE := 40; ELSE T1.PRE := 30;`

**Step 4 — Walk indicator activation (1 mark):**
The `Walk_OK` output `Q0.6` is energized by `M10` during the extended red phase, indicating safe crossing.

**Total = 7 marks**

---

#### **Question B — Option B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **various subsystems** of a PLC-based traffic controller — namely, the input subsystem, processing subsystem, output subsystem, and power subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**b)** For a traffic light controller, calculate the **degree of saturation (X)** and **capacity per lane (c)** for the following data: saturation flow $S = 1800$ veh/hr/lane, green time $G = 30$ s, amber time $Y = 5$ s, cycle time $C = 74$ s, lost time $t_L = 4$ s, and demand flow $q = 700$ veh/hr/lane. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Option B — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**Subsystem 1 — Input Subsystem (2 marks):**
Consists of field devices (pushbuttons, inductive loop sensors, pedestrian buttons) wired to the **Digital Input (DI) module**. The DI module provides:
- Optical isolation (galvanic isolation up to 1500 V).
- Debounce filtering (typically 1–10 ms).
- 24 V DC sourcing or sinking logic.
- LED status indication per channel.

**Subsystem 2 — Processing Subsystem (2 marks):**
The **CPU** contains the microprocessor, RAM, ROM, and a **ladder logic interpreter**. The processor executes three phases every scan:
1. **Input scan** → reads PII (Process Image Input) table.
2. **Program scan** → executes ladder rungs sequentially.
3. **Output scan** → writes to PIQ (Process Image Output) table.

**Subsystem 3 — Output Subsystem (1 mark):**
**Digital Output (DO) module** drives lamp contactors or solid-state relays (SSRs). Provides:
- Relay or transistor outputs.
- Short-circuit protection.
- LED status per channel.

**Subsystem 4 — Power Subsystem (1 mark):**
**PSU** converts 230 V AC mains to 24 V DC for PLC operation, plus a battery backup (UPS) for fail-safe flashing-amber mode.

**Subsystem 5 — Communication (1 mark):**
Optional **Ethernet / Profibus / Modbus** port for SCADA/HMI integration.

---

#### **Model Solution for Option B — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Effective green time (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g &= G + Y - t_L \\
g &= 30 + 5 - 4 \\
g &= 31 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Capacity per lane using Webster's formula (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= S \cdot \frac{g}{C} \\
c &= 1800 \cdot \frac{31}{74} \\
c &= 1800 \cdot 0.41892 \\
c &= 754.05 \text{ veh/hr/lane}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Degree of saturation (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{q}{c} \\
X &= \frac{700}{754.05} \\
X &= 0.9284
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (extra credit):** Since $X > 0.85$, the intersection is **oversaturated** and will experience congestion. Recommended action: increase green time to 40 s (recompute $X \approx 0.85$) or add a lane.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the seal-in (latching) contact** — Without it, the state cannot self-sustain. The lamp will only glow for ONE scan cycle (~10 ms) and then turn off. Examiner deducts **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up normally-open (NO) and normally-closed (NC) contacts** on the Stop pushbutton. The Stop PB **must be NC** to ensure fail-safe opening on wire break.
> 3. **Not placing outputs inside the MCR zone** — If MCR logic is missing, the Stop button has no effect. Examiner deducts **1 mark** specifically for "no emergency stop logic shown."
> 4. **Confusing `T1.DN` (done bit) with `T1.TT` (timing bit)** — The transition to the next state should occur on `DN`, not `TT`.
> 5. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — Every time value must carry the unit `s`; every flow value must carry `veh/hr/lane`. Missing units = **0.5 mark penalty per occurrence**.
> 6. **Drawing a ladder diagram but not explaining the timing flow** — Examiner awards marks 60:40 (diagram : explanation). Skipping the verbal explanation forfeits ~**3 marks**.
> 7. **Using microcontroller-style flowcharts instead of ladder logic** — The syllabus explicitly mandates **IEC 61131-3 ladder diagrams**. Other representations are acceptable only as supplements.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize before KTU ESE):**

- ✅ A traffic light controller is a **Moore-type finite state machine** with 6 discrete states.
- ✅ The PLC executes a **cyclic scan** with three phases: Input → Program → Output.
- ✅ **Master Control Relay (MCR)** is mandatory for safety; it must be a hardware + software interlock.
- ✅ **Seal-in (latching) contacts** are used to self-sustain each state's memory bit.
- ✅ **On-delay timers (TON)** generate the `DN` (done) bit that triggers state transitions.
- ✅ **Outputs are decoded combinatorially** from state bits — never energized directly by timer contacts.
- ✅ The **all-red clearance phase** is critical for safety and must be at least **2 seconds** per IRC:93 (Indian Road Congress) standards.
- ✅ **Webster's formula** $C_0 = \dfrac{1.5L + 5}{1 - Y}$ is the optimum cycle time formula (not always required, but KTU may ask for $C_0$).
- ✅ **Degree of saturation** $X < 0.85$ is the design threshold; $X > 1.0$ means oversaturated.
- ✅ **Pedestrian extension** uses rising-edge detection + memory latch + dynamic timer preset.
- ✅ The PLC's **scan time** must be < 10 % of the shortest phase (typically 5 ms scan for 5 s amber is acceptable).
- ✅ **Fail-safe mode**: if the PLC fails, signals default to **flashing amber** on all approaches.
- ✅ **I/O address convention** follows manufacturer pattern: Siemens uses `I/Q` (byte.bit), Allen-Bradley uses `I:0/0`, Mitsubishi uses `X/Y` (octal).
- ✅ **Lamp health monitoring** is a feedback interlock that prevents conflicting greens (e.g., NS and EW both green simultaneously).
- ✅ The **MCR contact must precede all output rungs** in the ladder program (placed in series with the output bus).

---

**End of Module 2 — Case Study: PLC-Based Traffic Light System**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stepper Motor and Servo Motor Control Using PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stepper Motor and Servo Motor Control Using PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stepper Motor – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stepper Motor (Stepping Motor):** A brushless, synchronous electric motor that converts a train of digital input pulses into discrete angular (or linear) mechanical displacements called *steps*. Each electrical pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular increment known as the **step angle (θₛ)**, typically expressed in degrees.

In a PLC context, the stepper motor is treated as an **open-loop positioning actuator** where the PLC acts purely as a *pulse generator*. The number of pulses sent by the PLC directly defines the angular displacement, while the pulse frequency defines the rotational speed.

$$\theta_{total} = N \cdot \theta_s$$

where:
- $\theta_{total}$ = Total angular displacement (degrees)
- $N$ = Number of pulses issued by the PLC
- $\theta_s$ = Step angle (degrees/step)

## 1.2 Servo Motor – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Servo Motor:** A rotary or linear actuator that allows for **precise control of angular or linear position, velocity, and acceleration** using a closed-loop feedback mechanism. It consists of a motor coupled to a position sensor (encoder/resolver) and is driven by a dedicated servo drive/amplifier.

Unlike the stepper motor, the servo system is **closed-loop**. The PLC (or motion controller) commands a *desired position*, and the servo drive internally regulates the actual position by comparing feedback from the encoder with the reference command.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Stepper Motor Analogy – "The Staircase Walker"
> [!TIP]
> Imagine a person walking up a staircase. They **must** place their foot on each individual step — they cannot skip steps or hover between two. Each "command" (placing the foot) makes them move a fixed, identical distance. If the person counts "1, 2, 3, 4..." while walking, the *count* determines their final altitude, and the *speed of counting* determines how fast they climb. **The PLC is the counter; the stepper is the climber.** If the staircase is too steep (high torque load), the climber may slip — *and no one notices because there is no one watching (open loop!)*.

### Servo Motor Analogy – "The Autopilot Driver"
> [!TIP]
> Picture a self-driving car on a highway. The car is told: *"Stay exactly in the center of lane number 2, going 80 km/h."* A camera (encoder) constantly watches the lane markings and reports back to the brain (servo drive). If the car drifts left or right, the brain instantly corrects the steering angle. Even if a strong wind gusts, the car **self-corrects**. The PLC only sets the *destination and speed*; the servo drive handles the *continuous correction*. **This is closed-loop control.**

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step angle (common) | $\theta_s$ | **1.8** | degrees/step |
| Full steps per revolution | $N_{rev}$ | **200** | steps/rev |
| Holding torque | $T_h$ | 0.5 – 50 | N·m |
| Encoder resolution (servo) | $R_{enc}$ | **1024 – 65536** | pulses/rev |
| Detent torque | $T_d$ | 0.01 – 0.1 | N·m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stepper Motor — Operational Logic

A stepper motor is driven by sequentially energizing its stator phase windings in a specific order. There are three principal excitation schemes:

### 2.1.1 Excitation Modes
- **Wave Drive (1-phase-on)**: Only one phase energized at a time. Lowest torque, lowest power consumption.
- **Full Step Drive (2-phase-on)**: Two adjacent phases energized simultaneously. **Highest torque**, standard mode.
- **Half Step Drive**: Alternates between 1-phase and 2-phase energization. **Doubles the resolution** (effectively halves the step angle).

### 2.1.2 Why a Driver is Mandatory
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A PLC discrete output module (typically **24 V DC, 0.5 A**) **cannot** directly energize a stepper motor winding (which may require **2–5 A**). A **stepper driver/amplifier** sits between them. The PLC sends:
> 1. **PULSE (PUL)** signal → step command
> 2. **DIRECTION (DIR)** signal → rotation sense
> 3. **ENABLE (EN)** signal → driver power gating
>
> The driver handles current chopping, microstepping, and thermal protection internally.

## 2.2 Servo Motor — Operational Logic

A servo system is fundamentally a **closed-loop position/velocity regulator**. The PLC interfaces with a **servo drive** (not the motor directly). The drive performs three nested control loops:

| Loop | Variable Controlled | Feedback Sensor | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current loop | Torque (I·Kt) | Shunt resistor / Hall sensor | Highest |
| Velocity loop | Speed (ω) | Encoder / Tachogenerator | Medium |
| Position loop | Angle (θ) | Encoder (high res) | Lowest |

The position loop in many architectures is implemented inside the drive itself, accepting **position pulses** from the PLC. In higher-end systems, the PLC directly executes **PID control**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{total} = N \cdot \theta_s$ | Stepper total angular displacement | degrees |
| 2 | $f_p = \dfrac{v}{\theta_s} \cdot 360$ | Pulse frequency for desired RPM | Hz |
| 3 | $N_{rev} = \dfrac{360}{\theta_s}$ | Steps per revolution | steps |
| 4 | $\omega = f_p \cdot \theta_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180}$ | Angular velocity from pulse rate | rad/s |
| 5 | $T_{out} = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Servo motor torque constant | N·m |
| 6 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Position error (servo control) | pulses / deg |
| 7 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID controller output | Volts |
| 8 | $R_{gear} = \dfrac{\theta_{load}}{\theta_{motor}} = \dfrac{N_m}{N_L}$ | Gear reduction ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{R \cdot J}{K_t \cdot K_b}$ | Mechanical time constant | seconds |
| 10 | $\theta_{eq} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{m}$ | Microstep angle (m = microstep level) | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall warning:** In the formula $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, the absolute value brackets $\vert r(t) - y(t) \vert$ — always render using `\vert ... \vert` in LaTeX to avoid markdown table breaks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stepper motors** dominate in *low-cost, open-loop* positioning: 3D printers, desktop CNC, label printers, camera autofocus, syringe pumps, and textile machines. They are favored when feedback sensors are impractical or too costly.
- **Servo motors** dominate in *high-performance, high-precision* applications: industrial robotics, CNC machining centers, pick-and-place machines, packaging lines, antenna tracking, and electric vehicle traction — wherever dynamic load disturbances exist and **position must be guaranteed** despite external forces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pulse Frequency Required for a Desired RPM (Stepper)

**Problem Statement:** A stepper motor has a step angle $\theta_s = 1.8^\circ$/step. Calculate the pulse frequency $f_p$ (Hz) required to achieve a rotational speed of $N_{rpm} = 120$ RPM.

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to revolutions per second.**

$$N_{rps} = \frac{N_{rpm}}{60} = \frac{120}{60} = 2 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of full steps per revolution.**

$$N_{rev} = \frac{360^\circ}{\theta_s} = \frac{360}{1.8} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the number of pulses issued per second (pulse frequency).**

$$f_p = N_{rps} \times N_{rev}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$f_p = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-verify using angular velocity conversion.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N_{rpm}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 120}{60} = 4 \pi \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_p = \frac{\omega}{\theta_s \cdot (\pi/180)} = \frac{4 \pi}{1.8 \times (\pi/180)} = \frac{4 \pi \times 180}{1.8 \pi} = 400 \text{ Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** $f_p = \mathbf{400 \text{ Hz}}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Number of Pulses to Rotate a Stepper by 270°

**Problem Statement:** With $\theta_s = 0.9^\circ$/step, find the pulse count $N$ for a $270^\circ$ rotation.

**Step 1 — Apply the fundamental displacement equation.**

$$N = \frac{\theta_{total}}{\theta_s}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$N = \frac{270^\circ}{0.9^\circ/\text{step}} = 300 \text{ pulses}$$

**Step 3 — Verification using revolutions.**

$$270^\circ = 0.75 \text{ rev}, \quad N_{rev} = \frac{360}{0.9} = 400 \text{ steps/rev}$$

$$N = 0.75 \times 400 = 300 \text{ pulses} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Servo System Steady-State Error (Position Loop)

**Problem Statement:** A PLC sends a position command $r = 5000$ pulses to a servo drive. The encoder reports $y = 4998$ pulses. Determine the steady-state error and the corrective pulse count needed.

**Step 1 — Define position error.**

$$e = r - y = 5000 - 4998 = 2 \text{ pulses}$$

**Step 2 — Convert error to angular displacement** (assume encoder $R_{enc} = 2500$ pulses/rev, i.e., $0.144^\circ$/pulse).

$$\theta_{e} = e \cdot \frac{360}{R_{enc}} = 2 \times \frac{360}{2500}$$

$$\theta_{e} = \frac{720}{2500} = 0.288^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Compute corrective pulses to be issued at the next PLC scan** (assuming integral action):

$$u_{corr} = K_p \cdot e + K_i \int e \, dt$$

For $K_p = 5$, $K_i = 0.1$ and a sustained error of 2 pulses for $t = 0.5$ s:

$$u_{corr} = 5(2) + 0.1(2)(0.5) = 10 + 0.1 = 10.1 \text{ pulses}$$

**Final Answer:** $e = 2$ pulses, equivalent to $\theta_e = 0.288^\circ$; corrective pulse $u_{corr} \approx 10$ pulses per scan.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Control Type | **Open-loop** | **Closed-loop** |
| Feedback | None (single driver) | Encoder / Resolver |
| PLC Output Type | Digital pulse train (PTO) | Analog ±10 V / Pulse train / Fieldbus |
| Holding at rest | High holding torque (passive) | Active current holding |
| Position accuracy | ±5% (no feedback) | ±0.01% (with encoder) |
| Speed range | Low to medium (<2000 RPM) | Very high (>5000 RPM) |
| Cost | Low | Medium–High |
| Torque at high speed | Drops sharply | Nearly constant (field-weakening region) |
| Failure mode | Silent stall (loss of position) | Detected & flagged by drive |
| Typical driver connection | Step + Direction pins | Command ±10 V + Enable + Fault |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — PLC → Stepper Motor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PLC CPU] -->|Pulse Train PUL| B[Stepper Driver]
    A -->|Direction DIR| B
    A -->|Enable EN| B
    B -->|Phase A current| C[Stepper Motor]
    B -->|Phase B current| C
    B -->|Phase C current| C
    C -->|Mechanical Shaft| D[Load]
    D -->|No Feedback| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted "No Feedback" path emphasizes the **open-loop** nature. A loss of synchronism between PLC pulses and rotor position will **not** be reported to the PLC.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — PLC → Servo Motor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PLC CPU] -->|Position Reference| B[Servo Drive]
    A -->|Enable / Fault Reset| B
    B -->|3-phase PWM| C[Servo Motor]
    C -->|Mechanical Shaft| D[Load]
    C -->|Encoder Signals A B Z| E[Encoder Feedback]
    E -->|Quadrature Pulses| B
    B -->|Fault Output| A
    B -->|In-Position Signal| A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The encoder feedback loop **closes** the position control loop. The PLC receives an *In-Position* flag, confirming the commanded move was completed successfully.

---

## 4.3 Stepper Motor Excitation Sequence (Wave Drive, 4-phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Phase A ON] --> S2[Step 2: Phase B ON]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Phase C ON]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Phase D ON]
    S4 --> S1
```

For a **full-step 2-phase-on** drive, the rotor advances by **θₛ** with *two* phases simultaneously excited at every step, doubling the torque.

---

## 4.4 PID Closed-Loop Control Signal Flow (Servo)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r t] --> E[Error e t]
    Y[Feedback y t] --> E
    E --> P[Proportional Kp]
    E --> I[Integral Ki]
    E --> D[Derivative Kd]
    P --> Sum[Summing Junction]
    I --> Sum
    D --> Sum
    Sum --> U[Control Output u t]
    U --> M[Servo Motor + Drive]
    M --> Y
```

The three branches $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ operate in parallel — this is the classic **non-interactive (parallel) PID form** implemented in nearly all modern PLCs (Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix, Schneider M580, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three differences between open-loop and closed-loop motion control systems.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Feedback:** Open-loop systems (e.g., stepper control) have **no feedback sensor**; closed-loop systems (e.g., servo control) use an **encoder/resolver** to feed back actual position.
2. **Accuracy:** Open-loop accuracy is limited by mechanical tolerance (~±5%); closed-loop accuracy is typically within **±0.01%** of full scale.
3. **Error Detection:** Open-loop cannot detect a **stalled rotor**; closed-loop immediately flags a position deviation through a *following error* alarm.
4. **Cost & Complexity:** Open-loop is cheaper and simpler; closed-loop requires a drive, encoder, and tuning.

*(Any three points — 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define step angle and holding torque of a stepper motor.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Step Angle (θₛ):** The angular displacement of the rotor produced by a *single* pulse applied to the stator windings. For a standard hybrid stepper, $\theta_s = 1.8^\circ$, giving 200 steps per revolution.
- **Holding Torque (Tₕ):** The maximum static torque the motor can sustain on its shaft *without losing synchronism* when a constant current is maintained in the energized phase(s). It is the torque required to forcibly detent the rotor from its rest position.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse** stepper driver inputs (PUL/DIR/EN) with servo drive inputs (±10 V analog + Enable). Examiners frequently deduct **2 marks** for this mix-up.
> - When solving pulse-frequency numericals, always **show the RPM-to-Hz conversion** explicitly. Skipping the $N_{rps} = N_{rpm}/60$ step is the most common **1-mark loss**.
> - In PID numericals, mentioning the *units* of $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ earns the full 7 marks; omitting units may cost 1 mark.
> - Block diagrams drawn in **ESE scripts** must use **rectangles, not circles**, for actuators/sensors. Examiners penalize informal sketches.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **PLC-based open-loop control of a stepper motor**. Describe the function of the **PUL**, **DIR**, and **EN** signals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A stepper motor has $\theta_s = 1.8^\circ$ and is driven in full-step mode. The PLC must move a conveyor belt by **540°** at **90 RPM**. Calculate:
   (i) Total number of pulses required.
   (ii) Pulse frequency.
   (iii) Time to complete the motion. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A PLC controls a stepper motor through a **stepper driver/amplifier** because the PLC's discrete output cannot supply the high winding current. The block diagram is:

`[PLC CPU] → PUL/DIR/EN → [Stepper Driver] → [Stepper Motor] → [Mechanical Load]`

- **PUL (Pulse):** Each rising edge advances the rotor by **one step angle θₛ**. The PLC generates this train using a **PTO (Pulse Train Output)** or **high-speed counter** module.
- **DIR (Direction):** A logic **0** or **1** selects the rotation sense (CW/CCW). The PLC updates DIR *before* issuing the first pulse of a new motion.
- **EN (Enable):** Enables/disables the driver's power stage. Pulling EN *low* removes power and **releases the rotor** (loss of holding torque).

Wiring: PLC outputs are typically **24 V DC**, optically isolated. The driver accepts this and produces 2–5 A pulsed currents on the motor phases A, B, (and C, D for 5-phase motors).

*(Block diagram — 3 marks; Signal descriptions — 4 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Total pulses:**

$$N = \frac{\theta_{total}}{\theta_s} = \frac{540}{1.8} = 300 \text{ pulses}$$

**(ii) Pulse frequency for 90 RPM:**

$$N_{rps} = \frac{90}{60} = 1.5 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$f_p = N_{rps} \times N_{rev} = 1.5 \times 200 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Time to complete motion:**

$$t = \frac{N}{f_p} = \frac{300}{300} = 1.0 \text{ second}$$

**Final Answer:** $N = 300$ pulses, $f_p = 300$ Hz, $t = 1.0$ s.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the **PLC-based closed-loop control of a servo motor** using a **PID controller**. Describe the role of the encoder feedback. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A servo drive receives a position command of **10 000 pulses**. The encoder resolution is **2 500 pulses/revolution**. A proportional gain $K_p = 4$ and a steady error of **12 pulses** is observed. Calculate:
   (i) The angular displacement corresponding to 10 000 pulses.
   (ii) The proportional control output $u_p$ for the 12-pulse error.
   (iii) The angular error in degrees. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

In closed-loop servo control, the PLC sends a **position reference** to the servo drive. The drive internally executes three nested control loops — **current → velocity → position** — using a high-resolution encoder mounted on the motor shaft. The encoder produces quadrature signals (A, B, Z) that are decoded to yield the *actual* rotor position $y(t)$.

The PLC computes the **error** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ and applies **PID control**:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **P (Proportional):** Provides immediate corrective action proportional to current error.
- **I (Integral):** Eliminates steady-state error over time.
- **D (Derivative):** Dampens oscillations and anticipates future error.

The encoder feedback **closes the loop**, ensuring the actual shaft position tracks the commanded position within a sub-arc-minute tolerance, even under external load disturbances. The drive outputs a *In-Position* signal and a *Fault* signal back to the PLC for monitoring.

*(Block diagram — 3 marks; PID explanation — 2 marks; Encoder role — 2 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Angular displacement for 10 000 pulses:**

$$\theta_{ref} = 10\,000 \times \frac{360}{2\,500} = 10\,000 \times 0.144 = 1\,440^\circ = 4 \text{ revolutions}$$

**(ii) Proportional output for 12-pulse error:**

$$u_p = K_p \cdot e = 4 \times 12 = 48 \text{ pulses equivalent}$$

**(iii) Angular error:**

$$\theta_e = 12 \times \frac{360}{2\,500} = 12 \times 0.144 = 1.728^\circ$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_{ref} = 1\,440^\circ$ (4 rev), $u_p = 48$ pulses, $\theta_e = 1.728^\circ$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Stepper & Servo Motor PLC Control**

- ⭐ **Stepper** = Open-loop, digital pulse-driven, **no feedback**, prone to silent stall.
- ⭐ **Servo** = Closed-loop, encoder feedback, **high accuracy**, auto-corrects disturbances.
- ⭐ **Three PLC signals to a stepper driver:** PUL (step), DIR (direction), EN (enable).
- ⭐ **Servo drive inputs:** ±10 V analog command **OR** pulse-train command + Enable + Fault.
- ⭐ **Step angle formula:** $\theta_s = 360 / N_{rev}$; common values: **1.8°, 0.9°, 7.5°**.
- ⭐ **Pulse frequency ↔ RPM:** $f_p = (N_{rpm}/60) \times (360/\theta_s)$.
- ⭐ **Three stepper excitation modes:** Wave (1-phase), Full-step (2-phase, max torque), Half-step (1.5-phase, double resolution).
- ⭐ **Microstepping** further divides $\theta_s$ into 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/256 — reduces resonance and vibration.
- ⭐ **Servo drive** has 3 nested loops: Current (innermost, fastest) → Velocity → Position (outermost, slowest).
- ⭐ **PID output** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \cdot de(t)/dt$.
- ⭐ **Holding torque $T_h$** is the static torque a stepper sustains *with current flowing*; **detent torque $T_d$** is the residual torque *with no current* (PM/hybrid motors only).
- ⭐ **Stepper driver** is **mandatory** — PLC outputs (24 V, 0.5 A) cannot drive motor windings (2–5 A) directly.
- ⭐ **Encoder resolution** $R_{enc}$ directly determines servo position accuracy; typical industrial: 2 048 to 65 536 pulses/rev.
- ⭐ **Gear reduction** multiplies torque by $N_m/N_L$ and divides speed by the same ratio.
- ⭐ **Failure mode difference:** Stepper stalls *silently* (no alarm); Servo drive *trips a fault* and signals the PLC — critical for safety interlock design.
- ⭐ **PLC selection criteria:** Need a **high-speed pulse output (PTO)** or **motion control module** for steppers; need **analog output module** or **servo controller module** for servos.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Sensor Interfacing with PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analog Sensor Interfacing with PLC # Analog Sensor Interfacing with PLC — Foundational Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Analog Sensor Interfacing with a PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** is the systematic process of connecting continuous-variable transducers (sensors whose output varies proportionally with the physical quantity being measured) to the PLC's analog input modules, performing signal conditioning, executing Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC), and translating the resulting raw digital count into scaled engineering units (e.g., °C, bar, L/min) for use in control logic, SCADA/HMI display, and PID closed-loop control algorithms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Analog I/O modules deal with **continuously varying** electrical signals — typically **voltage (0–10 V DC)** or **current (4–20 mA)** — as opposed to discrete digital I/O which only recognises two states (ON/OFF, 0/1). The standard industrial analog signal conventions are: | Signal Standard | Range | Common Use | |---|---|---| | Voltage | **0–10 V DC** | Short-distance, low-noise factory floors | | Current | **4–20 mA** | Long-distance, noisy industrial environments | | Voltage | **1–5 V DC** | Legacy systems, intrinsically safe loops | | Current | **0–20 mA** | Older installations, valve actuators | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Translator at a Press Conference" Imagine a **foreign diplomat** (the **analog sensor** in the field) speaking continuously in their native tongue — slowly, fluidly, expressing every shade of meaning. The **press gallery** (the **PLC CPU**) only understands **binary, digital language**. The **interpreter (the PLC's analog input module)** sits between them. He: 1. **Listens** to the diplomat (reads the continuous voltage/current). 2. **Quantises** the speech into fixed sentence blocks (ADC sampling). 3. **Translates** the meaning into his own language (engineering unit scaling). 4. **Repeats** at regular intervals (sampling rate). Just as a poor interpreter ruins communication, a poorly designed analog interface (wrong resolution, wrong scaling, electrical noise) ruins process control. The **4–20 mA** standard is preferred over voltage because the diplomat can be heard clearly **even when the cable is kilometres long** — current signals are immune to voltage drops over distance. > [!NOTE] > **Why 4 mA and not 0 mA?** > The **4 mA "live zero"** allows the PLC to distinguish between a **true reading of 0% (4 mA)** and a **broken wire / open circuit (0 mA)**. This single feature has saved countless industrial plants from catastrophic misreads. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors - **Transducer** — A device that converts one form of energy into another (e.g., a thermocouple converts temperature to millivolts). - **Sensor** — A transducer whose output is correlated with a specific physical/chemical measurand. - **Transmitter** — A signal-conditioning device that converts the raw transducer output into a **standardised industrial signal** (4–20 mA or 0–10 V). - **Signal Conditioning** — The process of amplifying, filtering, linearising, and isolating the raw sensor signal so that it falls within the input range of the PLC's ADC. - **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — The IC that converts a continuous analog voltage into a discrete binary number. - **Resolution** — The smallest change in the analog input that the ADC can detect, expressed in bits or engineering units. - **Span** — The difference between the maximum and minimum values of the measurement range (e.g., 0–100 °C ⇒ Span = 100 °C). - **Zero** — The lower end of the measurement range (e.g., 4 mA = 0 °C). - **Live Zero** — A non-zero baseline (4 mA) used for fault detection. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Analog Signal Chain — Stage-by-Stage Breakdown The journey of an analog signal from the field to the PLC's CPU consists of **six distinct stages**: ### Stage 1 — Physical Phenomenon Sensing A transducer reacts to a physical quantity (T, P, L, F, pH, etc.) and produces a low-level electrical output. Examples: - **Thermocouple** → microvolts per °C (Seebeck effect). - **RTD (Pt100)** → resistance changes with temperature. - **Strain-gauge load cell** → millivolts per unit strain (Wheatstone bridge). - **Piezoelectric pressure sensor** → charge or voltage proportional to pressure. ### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning (in the Transmitter) Performed by a dedicated **transmitter** (e.g., Rosemount 3051, Yokogawa YTA610). Functions: - **Amplification** of weak signals. - **Cold-junction compensation** (for thermocouples). - **Linearisation** (thermocouples are non-linear by nature). - **Isolation** to break ground loops. - **Output standardisation** to **4–20 mA** or **0–10 V**. ### Stage 3 — Wiring Topology Two common methods are used to connect a 2-wire (loop-powered) or 4-wire transmitter to the PLC: - **Single-ended (uni-polar) input** — signal return tied to common ground. Susceptible to ground-loop noise. - **Differential input** — two signal wires, neither tied to ground. Rejects common-mode noise. **Preferred for long runs.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** For a **4–20 mA loop**, use **shielded twisted-pair cable**, with the shield **grounded at one end only** to avoid ground loops. ### Stage 4 — Analog Input Module Hardware The PLC analog input module performs: 1. **Over-voltage / over-current protection** (TVS diodes, fuses). 2. **Filtering** (RC low-pass, typically 10–100 Hz cutoff to reject 50/60 Hz mains hum). 3. **Multiplexing** (one ADC shared across 4, 8, or 16 channels via an analog mux). 4. **ADC conversion** — Successive Approximation Register (SAR) is the most common architecture in industrial PLCs. ### Stage 5 — Scaling (Software / CPU) The raw digital count (0 to 2ⁿ – 1) is converted to engineering units using the **linear scaling equation**. This is a **mandatory KTU exam topic**. ### Stage 6 — Use in Control Logic The scaled value is used for: - Threshold comparisons (alarm generation). - PID control (continuous output to a variable-speed drive or control valve). - HMI display and trending. - Data logging. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the 4–20 mA Standard | Property | 4–20 mA Advantage | |---|---| | **Live zero** | 0 mA ⇒ wire break is unambiguously detected. | | **Noise immunity** | Current is unaffected by lead resistance and voltage drops. | | **Two-wire loop power** | Transmitter is powered via the same wires that carry the signal. | | **Intrinsic safety** | Current-limited, suitable for hazardous (Ex) areas. | | **Long cable runs** | Signal integrity preserved over **hundreds of metres** with no degradation. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is **exam-critical**. Master every entry. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Resolution of ADC** | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | $n$ = number of bits | | 2 | **LSB (Least Significant Bit) value** | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{\max} - V_{\min}}{2^{n}}$ | Volts per count | | 3 | **Engineering-unit scaling (linear)** | $E = \left(\dfrac{D - D_{\min}}{D_{\max} - D_{\min}}\right) (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}$ | $D$ = raw count, $E$ = EU | | 4 | **Inverse scaling (EU → raw count)** | $D = \left(\dfrac{E - E_{\min}}{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}\right) (D_{\max} - D_{\min}) + D_{\min}$ | Used to write analog outputs | | 5 | **Current-loop to EU** | $E = \left(\dfrac{I - 4}{16}\right) \text{Span} + E_{\min}$ | For 4–20 mA standard | | 6 | **Voltage-loop to EU** | $E = \left(\dfrac{V - 0}{10}\right) \text{Span} + E_{\min}$ | For 0–10 V standard | | 7 | **% of range** | $\%\text{Range} = \left(\dfrac{I_{\text{meas}} - 4}{16}\right) \times 100$ | Always 0 % at 4 mA, 100 % at 20 mA | | 8 | **Span** | $\text{Span} = E_{\max} - E_{\min}$ | Engineering units | | 9 | **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist)** | $f_{\text{sample}} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ | Avoid aliasing | | 10 | **Thermocouple sensitivity (K-type)** | ≈ **41 µV/°C** | Reference junction at 0 °C | | 11 | **Pt100 RTD formula (approx.)** | $R_{T} = R_{0}\left(1 + A\cdot T + B\cdot T^{2}\right)$ | $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$ | | 12 | **dB conversion of signal power** | $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | For voltage ratio | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Analog sensor interfacing is the **lifeline of process industries**: - **Oil & Gas:** Flow, level, and pressure transmitters (4–20 mA with HART protocol overlay) on distillation columns. - **Power Plants:** Drum-level, steam-temperature, and feedwater-flow measurement. - **Pharmaceuticals:** pH, dissolved oxygen, conductivity in bioreactors. - **Water Treatment:** Turbidity, chlorine residual, and flow in SCADA-monitored plants. - **Discrete Manufacturing:** Vision systems, force/torque feedback on robotic arms, temperature control of injection moulding. A mis-designed interface (wrong resolution, improper scaling, no filtering) leads to **oscillating PID loops, false alarms, or undetected process excursions** — all of which cost money and, in safety-critical systems, lives. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Scaling Equation (Generic Case) ### Step 1 — Define the Problem We are given: - A raw digital count $D$ from the ADC. - The ADC's minimum count $D_{\min}$ corresponding to the **lower** analog signal (e.g., 4 mA or 0 V). - The ADC's maximum count $D_{\max}$ corresponding to the **upper** analog signal (e.g., 20 mA or 10 V). - The corresponding engineering-unit range $[E_{\min}, E_{\max}]$. We want to find $E$ as a function of $D$. ### Step 2 — Recognise the Two-Point Form of a Line A line passing through $(D_{\min}, E_{\min})$ and $(D_{\max}, E_{\max})$ has the standard two-point form: $$ \frac{E - E_{\min}}{E_{\max} - E_{\min}} \;=\; \frac{D - D_{\min}}{D_{\max} - D_{\min}} $$ This is the **foundation** of analog scaling and is a guaranteed KTU question. ### Step 3 — Solve for E (Engineering Units Output) Multiply both sides by $(E_{\max} - E_{\min})$ and add $E_{\min}$: $$ E \;=\; \left(\frac{D - D_{\min}}{D_{\max} - D_{\min}}\right)\,(E_{\max} - E_{\min}) \;+\; E_{\min} $$ **Conversion logic explained:** - The ratio $\dfrac{D - D_{\min}}{D_{\max} - D_{\min}}$ is **dimensionless** and represents the **fractional position** of the current count within the ADC range. It is **0** at the bottom and **1** at the top. - Multiplying by $(E_{\max} - E_{\min})$ = **Span** converts this fraction to engineering-unit distance. - Adding $E_{\min}$ shifts the origin from 0 to the lower bound. ### Step 4 — Verify Boundary Conditions - At $D = D_{\min}$: the fraction is 0, so $E = 0 + E_{\min} = E_{\min}$. ✓ - At $D = D_{\max}$: the fraction is 1, so $E = \text{Span} + E_{\min} = E_{\max}$. ✓ - Linearity in between is guaranteed by the straight-line assumption. ✓ ## 3.2 Specialised Scaling for the 4–20 mA Standard The general equation simplifies for the 4–20 mA case because $D$ is linearly proportional to the measured current $I_{\text{meas}}$ (mA). ### Step 1 — Start with the General Equation The current $I$ in mA maps to engineering units as: $$ E = \left(\frac{I_{\text{meas}} - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}\right)\,\text{Span} + E_{\min} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $I_{\min} = 4$ mA, $I_{\max} = 20$ mA $$ E = \left(\frac{I_{\text{meas}} - 4}{20 - 4}\right)\,\text{Span} + E_{\min} $$ The denominator simplifies to **16** (not 20, because we use the live-zero baseline of 4 mA): $$ E = \left(\frac{I_{\text{meas}} - 4}{16}\right)\,\text{Span} + E_{\min} $$ This is the **most-tested equation** in the KTU analog-I/O portion of the syllabus. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Pressure Transmitter **Problem Statement (KTU-style 14-mark sub-question):** A pressure transmitter outputs **4–20 mA** corresponding to **0–10 bar**. The PLC's analog input module has a **12-bit ADC** with an input range of **0–20 mA** (i.e., $D_{\min} = 0$ at 0 mA, $D_{\max} = 4095$ at 20 mA). The current loop measured is **12 mA**. **Find:** 1. The raw digital count. 2. The pressure in bar. 3. The % of range. 4. The LSB resolution in mA and in bar. --- ### Sub-part (i) — Raw Digital Count Using linear proportion (0 mA → 0, 20 mA → 4095): $$ \frac{D - 0}{4095 - 0} \;=\; \frac{12 - 0}{20 - 0} $$ $$ D = 4095 \times \frac{12}{20} = 4095 \times 0.6 $$ $$ D = 2457 $$ So the raw count is **2457** (an integer; the PLC rounds internally). ### Sub-part (ii) — Pressure in bar Using the 4–20 mA scaling equation with $E_{\min} = 0$ bar, Span $= 10$ bar: $$ E = \left(\frac{12 - 4}{16}\right) \times 10 + 0 $$ $$ E = \frac{8}{16} \times 10 = 0.5 \times 10 $$ $$ E = 5\ \text{bar} $$ **Conversion logic:** 12 mA is **halfway between 4 mA and 20 mA**, so the pressure is halfway between 0 and 10 bar, i.e., 5 bar. ✓ ### Sub-part (iii) — Percentage of Range $$ \%\text{Range} = \left(\frac{12 - 4}{16}\right) \times 100 = 50\ \% $$ This matches the 5 bar out of 0–10 bar result. ✓ ### Sub-part (iv) — LSB Resolution For a 12-bit ADC over a 0–20 mA range: $$ \text{LSB} = \frac{20\ \text{mA}}{2^{12}} = \frac{20}{4096} \approx 0.00488\ \text{mA/count} = 4.88\ \mu\text{A/count} $$ In bar: $$ \text{LSB}_{\text{bar}} = \left(\frac{0.00488}{16}\right) \times 10 \approx 0.00305\ \text{bar/count} $$ This is the **smallest detectable pressure change** at the PLC's input. Any pressure change smaller than 3 mbar will not be visible to the CPU. ## 3.4 Resolution Table for Common ADC Bit-Depths | ADC Bits | Levels ($2^{n}$) | 0–10 V LSB (mV) | 4–20 mA LSB (µA) | 0–100 °C Resolution | |---|---|---|---|---| | **8-bit** | 256 | 39.06 | 62.5 | 0.39 °C | | **10-bit** | 1024 | 9.77 | 15.6 | 0.098 °C | | **12-bit** | 4096 | 2.44 | 3.91 | 0.024 °C | | **14-bit** | 16384 | 0.61 | 0.98 | 0.0061 °C | | **16-bit** | 65536 | 0.153 | 0.244 | 0.0015 °C | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** Most industrial PLC analog modules use **12-bit or 14-bit** ADCs. 16-bit is reserved for high-precision applications (e.g., precision weighing, analytical instrumentation). ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Thermocouple Linearisation Concept A K-type thermocouple outputs **41 µV/°C** (Seebeck coefficient). The cold-junction is at **25 °C** and the raw millivolt reading is **10.25 mV**. **Find the hot-junction temperature.** ### Step 1 — Convert mV to °C at the hot junction (ignoring CJC for a moment) $$ T_{\text{hot,raw}} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{10.25 \times 10^{-3}}{41 \times 10^{-6}} = 250\ ^{\circ}\text{C} $$ ### Step 2 — Add the cold-junction temperature (compensation) $$ T_{\text{hot}} = T_{\text{hot,raw}} + T_{\text{CJ}} = 250 + 25 = 275\ ^{\circ}\text{C} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Do not** use a simple linear coefficient for thermocouples across wide ranges — they are non-linear. The above is an **approximation** valid only for small ranges around the calibration point. Industrial transmitters use **polynomial correction** (NIST ITS-90 tables) with coefficients up to 14th order. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Analog Signal Chain (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal journey from the field sensor to the PLC's internal register. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Quantity: T, P, F, L]:::field --> B[Sensor / Transducer] B --> C[Raw mV / Ohms / Charge] C --> D[Signal Conditioning in Transmitter] D --> E[4-20 mA or 0-10 V Standardised] E --> F[Shielded Twisted Pair Cable] F --> G[PLC Analog Input Module] G --> H[Protection and Filtering] H --> I[Multiplexer] I --> J[ADC Conversion] J --> K[Raw Digital Count 0 to 2n-1] K --> L[Scaling in CPU] L --> M[Engineering Units EU] M --> N[PID Control and HMI Display] classDef field fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000 classDef plant fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 classDef plc fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1d4e89,color:#000 class A,B field class C,D,E,F plant class G,H,I,J,K,L,M,N plc ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this:** Follow the arrows left-to-right. The red blocks live in the **field**, the green blocks represent the **plant cabling and instrumentation**, and the blue blocks are **inside the PLC**. ## 4.2 PLC Analog Input Module — Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CHANNELS["Field Wiring (Channels 0-7)"] CH0[CH0: 4-20 mA or 0-10 V]:::io CH1[CH1: 4-20 mA or 0-10 V]:::io CH2[CH2: 4-20 mA or 0-10 V]:::io CH3[CH3: 4-20 mA or 0-10 V]:::io end CH0 --> PRT0[Protection: TVS and Fuse]:::blk CH1 --> PRT1[Protection: TVS and Fuse]:::blk CH2 --> PRT2[Protection: TVS and Fuse]:::blk CH3 --> PRT3[Protection: TVS and Fuse]:::blk PRT0 --> FILT0[RC Low-Pass Filter]:::blk PRT1 --> FILT1[RC Low-Pass Filter]:::blk PRT2 --> FILT2[RC Low-Pass Filter]:::blk PRT3 --> FILT3[RC Low-Pass Filter]:::blk FILT0 --> MUX FILT1 --> MUX FILT2 --> MUX FILT3 --> MUX MUX{Multiplexer}:::mux --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]:::blk AMP --> SAR[SAR ADC 12 or 14 bit]:::adc SAR --> ISO[Galvanic Isolation Barrier]:::blk ISO --> REG[Output Register AIW0 to AIW7]:::reg REG --> CPU[PLC CPU for Scaling]:::cpu classDef io fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a6c00,color:#000 classDef blk fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef mux fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000 classDef adc fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9d4e15,color:#000 classDef reg fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#3c096c,color:#000 classDef cpu fill:#ffadad,stroke:#9d0208,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Architecture Note:** The **multiplexer (MUX)** is the heart of multi-channel analog modules. It sequentially connects one channel at a time to a single shared ADC. This is why analog inputs are **slower** than digital inputs — channels are scanned in rotation, not simultaneously. ## 4.3 Two-Wire vs Four-Wire Transmitter Connection ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TWO["2-Wire Loop-Powered Transmitter"] T1[Sensor Element]:::t1 --> X1[Transmitter Electronics]:::t1 X1 --- L1A(( )):::loop L1A --- L1B(( )):::loop L1B --> R1[250 Ohm Sense Resistor in PLC]:::load R1 --> V1[24 V DC Supply]:::psu V1 --> L1A end subgraph FOUR["4-Wire Self-Powered Transmitter"] T2[Sensor Element]:::t2 --> X2[Transmitter Electronics]:::t2 X2 --> L2S((Signal +)):::sig X2 --> L2Sn((Signal -)):::sig L2S --> R2[250 Ohm Sense Resistor]:::load L2Sn --> R2 X2 --- L2P((Power +)):::psu X2 --- L2N((Power -)):::psu L2P --> V2[24 V DC Supply]:::psu L2N --> V2 end classDef t1 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9d4e15,color:#000 classDef t2 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000 classDef loop fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d3557,color:#000 classDef sig fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#9d0208,color:#000 classDef load fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef psu fill:#ffadad,stroke:#9d0208,color:#000 ``` **Why the 250 Ω resistor?** The PLC measures **voltage across this precision resistor**. For 4–20 mA, the voltage drop is: - At 4 mA: $V = 0.004 \times 250 = 1.0$ V - At 20 mA: $V = 0.020 \times 250 = 5.0$ V This converts the **current loop into a 1–5 V signal** that the ADC can read — the classic **1–5 V standard**. ## 4.4 Scaling Logic Flowchart (CPU Software) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start of Scan Cycle]:::start --> B[Read AIW0 Raw Count 0-4095]:::io B --> C{Raw Count
less than Zero?}:::dec C -- Yes --> D[Set Under-Range Flag
Display -32768]:::err C -- No --> E{Raw Count greater
than 4095?}:::dec E -- Yes --> F[Set Over-Range Flag
Display 32767]:::err E -- No --> G[Apply Scaling Equation]:::calc G --> H[Store in MD or DB
Engineering Units]:::store H --> I[Use in PID or HMI]:::use classDef start fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000 classDef io fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a6c00,color:#000 classDef dec fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d3557,color:#000 classDef err fill:#ffadad,stroke:#9d0208,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9d4e15,color:#000 classDef store fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#3c096c,color:#000 classDef use fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#9d0208,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always include the **over-range and under-range handling** in your scaling block diagram — it earns you easy marks for robustness. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` **List any three advantages of the 4–20 mA current loop standard over the 0–10 V voltage standard for analog sensor interfacing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Live Zero:** The 4 mA baseline allows the PLC to immediately detect a broken wire (0 mA) versus a true zero reading (4 mA). **(1 mark)** 2. **Noise immunity & long-distance fidelity:** Current signals are unaffected by lead resistance and voltage drops, allowing accurate transmission over **hundreds of metres** without degradation. **(1 mark)** 3. **Two-wire loop power:** The transmitter is powered via the same two wires that carry the signal, simplifying cabling and supporting **intrinsic safety** in hazardous areas. **(1 mark)** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` **Define the term "Resolution" of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) and state the formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Resolution is the **smallest change in the analog input signal** that can be detected and represented as a distinct digital output by the ADC. It depends on the number of bits $n$ used by the converter. **(2 marks)** $$ \text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n}} $$ where $V_{\text{FS}}$ is the full-scale input range. **(1 mark)** For example, a 12-bit ADC over a 0–10 V range has a resolution of $10 / 4096 \approx 2.44$ mV per LSB. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Module 2 carries **one full 14-mark question**. You will be given an **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B). Both are provided below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` A temperature transmitter with a range of **0–200 °C** outputs a **4–20 mA** signal. The PLC analog input module uses a **12-bit ADC** with an input range of **0–20 mA** (i.e., 0 mA = 0 counts, 20 mA = 4095 counts). A **250 Ω** sense resistor converts current to voltage internally. #### **(a)** Draw the block diagram of the complete analog signal chain from sensor to scaled engineering unit. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The required block diagram (with explanation) is: | Block | Function | |---|---| | **1. Temperature Sensor (RTD / Thermocouple)** | Converts process temperature into a low-level electrical signal (resistance or mV). **(1 mark)** | | **2. Transmitter / Signal Conditioner** | Performs amplification, linearisation, cold-junction compensation, and converts the signal to a **standardised 4–20 mA** output. **(1 mark)** | | **3. Shielded Twisted-Pair Cable** | Carries the 4–20 mA signal from the field to the control room with **noise immunity**. **(1 mark)** | | **4. Sense Resistor (250 Ω)** | Converts the current loop to a **1–5 V voltage** that the ADC can read. **(1 mark)** | | **5. Protection & Filter (RC LPF)** | Protects against over-voltage and rejects 50/60 Hz mains noise. **(1 mark)** | | **6. Multiplexer + ADC (12-bit SAR)** | Sequentially samples the analog voltage and produces a **raw digital count 0–4095**. **(1 mark)** | | **7. CPU Scaling Block** | Applies the **linear scaling equation** to convert the raw count to engineering units (°C). **(1 mark)** | #### **(b)** The PLC reads a raw count of **2867**. Calculate the corresponding temperature in °C, the percentage of range, and the resolution of the ADC in °C. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert raw count to current (mA).** The ADC maps 0–20 mA to 0–4095. So: $$ I = \frac{D}{4095} \times 20 = \frac{2867}{4095} \times 20 $$ $$ I = 0.7001 \times 20 = 14.002\ \text{mA} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 2 — Apply the 4–20 mA scaling equation.** With $E_{\min} = 0\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$, Span = $200\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$: $$ E = \left(\frac{I - 4}{16}\right) \times 200 + 0 $$ $$ E = \left(\frac{14.002 - 4}{16}\right) \times 200 $$ $$ E = \left(\frac{10.002}{16}\right) \times 200 = 0.6251 \times 200 $$ $$ E \approx 125.0\ ^{\circ}\text{C} $$ **(3 marks)** **Step 3 — Percentage of range.** $$ \%\text{Range} = \left(\frac{14.002 - 4}{16}\right) \times 100 = 62.5\ \% $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Resolution in °C.** For a 12-bit ADC over 0–20 mA, LSB in current is: $$ \text{LSB}_{I} = \frac{20\ \text{mA}}{4096} = 4.883\ \mu\text{A} $$ In °C, each LSB corresponds to: $$ \text{LSB}_{T} = \left(\frac{4.883\ \mu\text{A}}{16\ \text{mA}}\right) \times 200\ ^{\circ}\text{C} $$ $$ \text{LSB}_{T} = \left(\frac{4.883 \times 10^{-3}}{16}\right) \times 200 = 0.0610\ ^{\circ}\text{C/count} $$ **(2 marks)** So the smallest temperature step the PLC can resolve is about **0.061 °C (61 m°C)**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` Differentiate between **single-ended** and **differential** analog input wiring methods. A level transmitter outputs **4–20 mA** for a tank level range of **0–5 metres**. The PLC uses an **8-bit ADC** with 0–20 mA input range. #### **(a)** Explain the working of Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC and its relevance to PLC analog input modules. State its advantages over a flash ADC. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working of SAR ADC (5 marks):** A SAR ADC converts an analog input to a digital output using a **binary search algorithm**: 1. The analog input $V_{\text{in}}$ is held on a sample-and-hold capacitor. **(1 mark)** 2. The internal **DAC** generates a trial voltage starting at half the full-scale value (MSB = 1, all others = 0). **(1 mark)** 3. A **comparator** checks whether $V_{\text{in}}$ is greater than or less than the trial voltage. **(1 mark)** 4. If $V_{\text{in}} > V_{\text{trial}}$, the MSB is kept as 1; otherwise it is reset to 0. The next bit is then tried, halving the search range. **(1 mark)** 5. This process repeats for all $n$ bits, converging on the digital representation in exactly **$n$ clock cycles**. **(1 mark)** **Relevance to PLCs (1 mark):** SAR ADCs offer a **good trade-off between speed (10–500 kSPS) and resolution (12–16 bits)**, making them the **industry standard** for PLC analog input modules. They consume less power and silicon area than flash ADCs of equivalent resolution. **Advantages over Flash ADC (1 mark):** A flash ADC requires $2^{n} - 1$ comparators (e.g., **4095 comparators** for 12 bits), making it power-hungry, expensive, and impractical above 8 bits. SAR uses just **one comparator**, drastically reducing cost, power, and complexity. #### **(b)** Compute the raw count when the tank level is **3.2 m**, and determine the level in metres when the raw count is **180**. Comment on the resolution adequacy. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the current at 3.2 m level.** $$ I = \left(\frac{3.2 - 0}{5 - 0}\right) \times 16 + 4 = 0.64 \times 16 + 4 $$ $$ I = 10.24 + 4 = 14.24\ \text{mA} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 2 — Convert current to raw 8-bit count.** For an 8-bit ADC, $D_{\max} = 2^{8} - 1 = 255$. $$ D = \frac{I}{20} \times 255 = \frac{14.24}{20} \times 255 = 0.712 \times 255 $$ $$ D \approx 181.6 \approx 182 $$ **(2 marks)** **Step 3 — Convert raw count 180 to level (metres).** First find the current: $$ I = \frac{180}{255} \times 20 = 0.7059 \times 20 = 14.118\ \text{mA} $$ **(1 mark)** Then apply scaling: $$ E = \left(\frac{14.118 - 4}{16}\right) \times 5 = \left(\frac{10.118}{16}\right) \times 5 = 0.6324 \times 5 $$ $$ E \approx 3.162\ \text{m} $$ **(2 marks)** **Step 4 — Comment on resolution adequacy (1 mark):** The LSB of the 8-bit ADC corresponds to: $$ \text{LSB}_{\text{level}} = \frac{5\ \text{m}}{256} = 0.0195\ \text{m} = 19.5\ \text{mm} $$ This means the smallest detectable level change is **about 2 cm**. For a 5-metre tank, this is **adequate for general level indication** but **inadequate for fine batching or custody-transfer applications**, where 12-bit or 16-bit ADCs are required. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas (Examiner's Insight):** > 1. **Forgetting the live zero:** Students often use $(I_{\text{meas}}/20) \times \text{Span}$ instead of $\left(\dfrac{I_{\text{meas}} - 4}{16}\right) \times \text{Span}$ — this **loses 2–3 marks** instantly. > 2. **Using $2^{n}$ instead of $2^{n} - 1$** in resolution calculations. The maximum count is $2^{n} - 1$, not $2^{n}$. > 3. **Not showing the formula substitution** in long answers — partial credit is awarded only when each step is shown explicitly. > 4. **Ignoring engineering units** in the final answer. Always write "°C", "bar", "m", "L/min" — the units are worth ½ mark in KTU valuation. > 5. **Confusing 0–20 mA with 4–20 mA:** They are **different standards**. Always clarify the input range of the ADC first. > 6. **Forgetting over/under-range handling** in block diagrams — KTU specifically tests for **robustness**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Exam:** - **Analog I/O** deals with **continuously varying** signals; digital I/O deals with **two states only**. - **4–20 mA** is preferred over 0–10 V due to **live zero, noise immunity, and long-distance fidelity**. - **Live zero = 4 mA** ⇒ 0 mA indicates **broken wire / open circuit**. - The **250 Ω sense resistor** converts 4–20 mA to **1–5 V** for ADC input. - **Linear scaling equation** (most-tested formula): $$ E = \left(\frac{D - D_{\min}}{D_{\max} - D_{\min}}\right) \times \text{Span} + E_{\min} $$ - **ADC resolution** $= V_{\text{FS}} / 2^{n}$, where $n$ = number of bits. - **8-bit** ⇒ 256 levels; **12-bit** ⇒ 4096 levels; **16-bit** ⇒ 65536 levels. - **SAR ADC** is the industry standard for PLC analog modules — good speed/resolution/cost trade-off. - **Nyquist theorem:** Sample rate $\geq 2 \times$ maximum signal frequency to avoid aliasing. - **Single-ended** inputs share a common ground; **differential** inputs reject common-mode noise — **prefer differential** for noisy industrial floors. - **Thermocouples** output ~µV/°C and require **cold-junction compensation**; **RTDs** change resistance linearly with temperature (Pt100 = 100 Ω at 0 °C). - **Transmitter** = sensor + signal conditioner; it standardises the output to 4–20 mA or 0–10 V. - Always include **over-range, under-range, and wire-break detection** in your scaling block — it earns free marks. - **Shielded twisted-pair cable** with **single-end grounding** is the industry-standard practice for analog signal wiring. - **HART protocol** overlays a digital $\pm 0.5$ mA signal on top of the 4–20 mA loop for two-way communication with smart transmitters — a likely 2-mark short question. - **Scaling direction:** Raw count → Engineering Units (analog input) is the **forward** direction; Engineering Units → Raw count (analog output, e.g., to a VFD) is the **inverse** direction.

topics.title: Encoder Interfacing with PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Encoder Interfacing with PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **encoder** is a precision electro-mechanical feedback transducer that converts the angular or linear position of a rotating/linear shaft into an electrical signal train (typically a train of digital pulses or a coded digital word) suitable for direct processing by a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) or other digital controller. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the encoder is classified as the primary **non-contact position feedback sensor** used in closed-loop motion control sub-systems.

When an encoder is electrically and logically connected to a PLC such that the PLC interprets the encoder pulses to determine displacement, velocity, or direction, the system is termed **Encoder Interfacing with PLC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An encoder is a rotary (or linear) sensing device that generates a defined number of electrical pulses per revolution (PPR) or per unit of linear travel. These pulses are routed to **High-Speed Counter (HSC)** input cards/modules of the PLC because the pulse train frequency far exceeds the scan rate of a standard discrete input.

## 1.2 Primary Classification of Encoders

The KTU syllabus (Module 2) categorises encoders into two principal families:

| Classification | Signal Nature | Position Memory on Power Loss |
|---|---|---|
| **Incremental Encoder** | Pulse train (A, B, Z channels) | **No** – requires homing routine |
| **Absolute Encoder** | Unique digital code per shaft angle | **Yes** – retains position |

### 1.2.1 Incremental Encoder
Produces a continuous stream of square-wave pulses whose frequency is proportional to shaft speed. The **relative** change in position is obtained by counting pulses.

### 1.2.2 Absolute Encoder
Produces a unique multi-bit binary (or Gray code) word for **every** shaft angle. The **absolute** position is available instantly on power-up.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Car Odometer**
> Think of an incremental encoder as the **mechanical odometer** of an old car: as the wheels rotate, numbered wheels inside the odometer tick forward one digit at a time. If you cover the odometer with a cloth and then uncover it, you have **no idea** of the absolute distance travelled — you only know the *change* since the cloth was removed. This is exactly how an incremental encoder behaves after a power cycle.
>
> Now think of an **absolute encoder** as a **compass needle**: even after you close your eyes and reopen them, the needle *always* points to a fixed direction (say, North). The system "remembers" its absolute position without any external reference.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantitative parameters must be internalised by every KTU student:

- **PPR (Pulses Per Revolution):** The number of complete pulse cycles generated on Channel A per mechanical revolution of the shaft. Standard values: **100, 360, 500, 1000, 1024, 2048, 4096 PPR**.
- **CPR (Counts Per Revolution):** The number of *resolvable counts* after quadrature decoding (CPR = 4 × PPR).
- **Resolution:** The smallest angular displacement the encoder can resolve. $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{360°}{4 \times \text{PPR}}$.
- **Operating Voltage:** Industrial encoders typically operate at **5 V DC, 8–30 V DC**, or **10–30 V DC**.
- **Output Types:** Push-Pull, NPN (sinking) Open-Collector, PNP (sourcing) Open-Collector, Line Driver (RS-422 differential).
- **Maximum Frequency Response:** Ranges from **20 kHz to 1 MHz** depending on PPR and shaft RPM.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** In KTU answers, **always specify the units** of any encoder quantity. Stating "1000 resolution" will attract partial marking; "1000 PPR" or "4000 counts/rev" will be marked fully correct.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of an Optical Incremental Encoder

An optical incremental encoder is the most common variant tested in KTU exams. Its operation is built on **Moiré fringe optical interruption** and can be broken into the following structured logic steps:

1. **Mechanical Coupling:** The encoder shaft is mechanically coupled (via flexible coupling, belt, or gear) to the motor/load shaft. The shaft drives a **graduated code disc** (usually glass or metal).
2. **Code Disc Geometry:** The disc has a circular track containing **N equally-spaced radial slots** (or opaque/transparent line patterns), where N = PPR.
3. **Optical Source:** An **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** mounted on one side of the disc projects a collimated light beam across the disc.
4. **Photodetector Array:** Two **photodiodes or phototransistors** are mounted on the opposite side. They are physically displaced such that their outputs are **90° out of phase** electrically — these become Channel A and Channel B.
5. **Pulse Generation:** As the disc rotates, slots alternately transmit and block the light. The photodetectors convert this into two **quadrature square waves**.
6. **Index Channel (Z):** A **third photodetector** senses a single slot per revolution, generating one reference pulse per turn — used for **homing and revolution counting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Channels (A & B)?** A single channel can only count pulses (speed/position) but **cannot determine direction**. By displacing the second photodetector 90°, the PLC detects which channel *leads* the other, thereby distinguishing clockwise (CW) from counter-clockwise (CCW) rotation.

## 2.2 Quadrature Decoding — The Core Concept

Quadrature refers to the **90° phase shift** between Channel A and Channel B. This phase relationship encodes direction:

| Channel A leads Channel B | → | **Clockwise (CW)** rotation |
| Channel B leads Channel A | → | **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)** rotation |

A PLC can decode the quadrature signal at three different resolutions:

- **x1 Decoding:** Count on **one edge** of one channel only → resolution = PPR.
- **x2 Decoding:** Count on **both edges** of Channel A (or B) → resolution = 2 × PPR.
- **x4 Decoding:** Count on **all four edges** (rising & falling of A, rising & falling of B) → resolution = 4 × PPR. **This is the standard for most PLC HSC modules.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resolvable counts per revolution (x4) | $C_{rev} = 4 \times \text{PPR}$ | counts/rev | Quadrature mode |
| 2 | Angular resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{4 \times \text{PPR}}$ | degrees | Smallest detectable angle |
| 3 | Angular velocity (RPM) | $N = \dfrac{f \times 60}{\text{PPR}}$ | RPM | f = pulse frequency on Channel A |
| 4 | Angular velocity from counts | $N = \dfrac{\Delta C \times 60}{4 \times \text{PPR} \times \Delta t}$ | RPM | $\Delta C$ = counts in time $\Delta t$ |
| 5 | Linear velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{60}$ | mm/s | D = wheel/shaft diameter |
| 6 | Position (revolutions) | $\theta = \dfrac{\text{Total Counts}}{4 \times \text{PPR}}$ | revolutions | — |
| 7 | Position (degrees) | $\theta = \dfrac{\text{Total Counts} \times 360°}{4 \times \text{PPR}}$ | degrees | — |
| 8 | Linear displacement | $s = \text{Total Counts} \times \text{Pitch}$ | mm | Pitch = mm/count of linear encoder |
| 9 | Pulse frequency (max) | $f_{max} = \dfrac{\text{PPR} \times N_{max}}{60}$ | Hz | Must be $\leq$ HSC max frequency |
| 10 | Direction logic | A leads B → CW, B leads A → CCW | — | Quadrature only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Many KTU students incorrectly write $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{\text{PPR}}$ forgetting the factor of 4 for quadrature. Always derive the *resolvable* resolution, not the *pulse* resolution.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Encoder–PLC systems are the workhorses of modern automation:

- **Packaging Lines:** Synchronising conveyor belts and registration marks at high speed (used in KTU industrial-automation case studies).
- **CNC Machines:** Closed-loop position feedback on servo drives for milling, turning, and grinding.
- **Bottling Plants:** Counting bottles, detecting jams, and synchronising filling heads.
- **Robotics:** Joint position feedback in articulated arms.
- **Elevators:** Carriage position and door synchronisation.
- **Printing Presses:** Web tension and registration control.

The choice of encoder and its interface directly determines **positioning accuracy**, **repeatability**, and **system throughput** in these applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resolvable Counts in Quadrature Mode

**Given:** An incremental encoder has $P$ pulses per revolution on Channel A.

**Step 1 — Understand one full mechanical revolution:**
During one revolution, Channel A produces exactly $P$ complete pulse cycles (one cycle = 1 high + 1 low = 2 edges).

**Step 2 — Count edges in one revolution of Channel A:**
Each cycle contributes 2 edges (1 rising + 1 falling).
Therefore, the number of edges in Channel A per revolution is $2P$.

**Step 3 — Add Channel B edges:**
Channel B is 90° shifted, providing an additional $2P$ edges, each displaced in time from Channel A's edges.

**Step 4 — Combine all four transitions per cycle:**
For each pulse cycle, four distinct state transitions occur:
- A rising,
- A falling,
- B rising,
- B falling.

Thus, total counts in one revolution = $4P$.

$$\boxed{C_{rev} = 4 \times P}$$

**Step 5 — Angular Resolution:**
Since one full revolution = 360°, and we have $4P$ discrete counts:

$$\boxed{\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{4P}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shaft RPM from Pulse Frequency

**Given:** Channel A produces $f$ pulses per second (Hz). The encoder has $P$ PPR.

**Step 1 — Time for one revolution:**
The time taken for the shaft to complete one revolution equals the time for $P$ pulses to be generated.
$$T_{rev} = \frac{P}{f} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Revolutions per second:**
$$N_{rps} = \frac{1}{T_{rev}} = \frac{f}{P} \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to revolutions per minute (multiply by 60):**
$$N_{rpm} = N_{rps} \times 60 = \frac{60 \cdot f}{P}$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{60 \cdot f}{\text{PPR}} \text{ RPM}}$$

**Step 4 — Express frequency in terms of RPM (inverse form, useful for verifying HSC bandwidth):**
$$f = \frac{P \cdot N}{60} \text{ Hz}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed Measurement Using a Time Window

Often the PLC counts encoder pulses over a **fixed time window** $\Delta t$ (in seconds), producing a count difference $\Delta C$.

**Step 1 — Counts represent position change:**
The total number of quadrature counts accumulated in time $\Delta t$ is $\Delta C$.

**Step 2 — Fraction of a revolution completed:**
$$\text{Revolutions in } \Delta t = \frac{\Delta C}{4P}$$

**Step 3 — Revolutions per minute:**
$$N = \frac{\Delta C}{4P \cdot \Delta t} \times 60$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{15 \cdot \Delta C}{P \cdot \Delta t} \text{ RPM}}$$

This is the formula implemented inside the PLC's HSC function block for **speed calculation**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Position (Angle) Tracking

**Given:** The HSC register contains an accumulated count of $C_{total}$ since the last reset/homing.

**Step 1 — Each count corresponds to a discrete angular step:**
The angular equivalent of one count is:
$$\Delta\theta = \frac{360°}{4P}$$

**Step 2 — Total angle traversed:**
$$\theta = C_{total} \times \Delta\theta$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{C_{total} \times 360°}{4 \times \text{PPR}}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Question:** An incremental optical encoder rated at **1000 PPR** is mounted on a motor shaft. The PLC counts **2000 quadrature pulses** in **0.25 seconds**. Calculate:
> (a) The number of resolvable counts per revolution.
> (b) The angular resolution.
> (c) The shaft RPM.
> (d) The total angle rotated in 5 seconds (assuming constant speed).

### Solution

**Part (a) — Counts per revolution:**

$$C_{rev} = 4 \times P = 4 \times 1000 = 4000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Part (b) — Angular resolution:**

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{4P} = \frac{360°}{4000} = 0.09° = 5.4 \text{ arc-minutes}$$

**Part (c) — Shaft RPM:**

We are given $\Delta C = 2000$ counts in $\Delta t = 0.25$ s, and $P = 1000$ PPR.

$$N = \frac{15 \times \Delta C}{P \times \Delta t} = \frac{15 \times 2000}{1000 \times 0.25}$$

$$N = \frac{30000}{250} = 120 \text{ RPM}$$

**Cross-check using pulse frequency:**

$$f = \frac{\Delta C / 2}{2 \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{2000 / 2}{0.5} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$$

(We divide $\Delta C$ by 2 to obtain Channel-A pulses, then by $\Delta t$.)

$$N = \frac{60 \cdot f}{P} = \frac{60 \times 2000}{1000} = 120 \text{ RPM} \checkmark$$

**Part (d) — Total angle in 5 seconds:**

Counts accumulated in 5 s at constant rate:
$$C_{5s} = \frac{2000}{0.25} \times 5 = 40000 \text{ counts}$$

$$\theta = \frac{40000 \times 360°}{4000} = \frac{14400000°}{4000} = 3600°$$

Equivalent revolutions:
$$\frac{3600°}{360°} = 10 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Verification via RPM:**
In 5 seconds, revolutions = $\frac{120 \text{ RPM}}{60} \times 5 = 10$ revolutions ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Pattern:** The most common KTU numerical carries 7 marks with 3–4 sub-parts. They typically test (a) CPR, (b) RPM, and (c) an angle/displacement calculation. Memorise the four master formulas and you will score full marks.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Encoder Types vs. Application Suitability

| Encoder Type | Direction Sense | Position on Power-Up | Wiring Complexity | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Incremental (A only) | ❌ | ❌ (needs homing) | 3 wires | Simple speed only |
| Incremental (A + B) | ✅ | ❌ (needs homing) | 4–5 wires | Position + direction |
| Incremental (A + B + Z) | ✅ | ✅ (with homing once) | 6–8 wires | High-precision motion |
| Absolute (Single-turn) | ✅ | ✅ Instant | 8–14 wires | Robot joints |
| Absolute (Multi-turn) | ✅ | ✅ Instant (4096 × N turns) | 14+ wires | Machine tools, elevators |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
