| Network cycle time (master-slave) | $T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{slave,i} + N \cdot T_{overhead}$ | seconds |
| HART update rate | $f_{update} = 2$ updates/sec | typical for process vars |
| Network availability | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The formula $L \cdot f_{baud} \le 10^8$ is a critical rule of thumb: as baud rate increases, maximum cable length must decrease proportionally to maintain signal integrity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Cable Length vs. Baud Rate for RS-485

The RS-485 standard specifies that the round-trip propagation delay of the cable must not exceed approximately **50% of one bit period** to ensure proper signal sampling at receivers.

### Step 1: Define the bit period
The bit period (time duration of one bit) is given by:

$$T_b = \frac{1}{f_{baud}}$$

**Explanation:** If a signal is transmitted at 9600 bps, each bit occupies $T_b = 1/9600 \approx 104.17 \, \mu s$.

### Step 2: Define the propagation velocity in a twisted-pair cable
Standard twisted-pair cables have a velocity of propagation (VoP) of approximately 66% the speed of light, but for calculation simplicity in industry:

$$v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^8 \, m/s \quad \text{(typical industry approximation)}$$

**Explanation:** This accounts for the dielectric constant of polyethylene insulation used in industrial-grade cables.

### Step 3: One-way propagation delay
For a cable of length $L$, the one-way propagation delay is:

$$t_{prop} = \frac{L}{v_{prop}}$$

### Step 4: Round-trip delay
Signal must travel from transmitter to far receiver and the reflection (or response) back. Round-trip delay is:

$$t_{rt} = 2 \cdot t_{prop} = \frac{2L}{v_{prop}}$$

### Step 5: Apply the 50% bit period constraint
For reliable detection:

$$t_{rt} \le 0.5 \cdot T_b$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{2L}{v_{prop}} \le 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{f_{baud}}$$

### Step 6: Solve for L
Rearranging to isolate L:

$$L \le \frac{0.5 \cdot v_{prop}}{2 \cdot f_{baud}} = \frac{v_{prop}}{4 \cdot f_{baud}}$$

Substituting $v_{prop} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$L \le \frac{2 \times 10^8}{4 \cdot f_{baud}} = \frac{5 \times 10^7}{f_{baud}}$$

### Step 7: Final rule of thumb
This yields the canonical industrial rule:

$$L \cdot f_{baud} \le 10^8 \quad \text{to} \quad 5 \times 10^7$$

The slightly relaxed form $L \cdot f_{baud} \le 10^8$ accounts for jitter, attenuation, and termination mismatches.

**Verification example:** At $f_{baud} = 10^6$ bps (1 Mbps), $L \le 100$ m, which matches the RS-485 datasheet specification for 1 Mbps operation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Modbus RTU Frame Transmission Time

Modbus RTU uses 11 bits per byte: 1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop. For a frame containing $N_{bytes}$ total:

### Step 1: Total bits in the frame
Modbus RTU frame structure is: 1 address byte + 1 function code byte + $N_{data}$ data bytes + 2 CRC bytes

$$N_{total\_bytes} = 4 + N_{data}$$

$$N_{total\_bits} = 11 \cdot (4 + N_{data}) = 11 N_{total\_bytes}$$

**Explanation:** Each byte contributes 11 bits including start, parity, and stop bits.

### Step 2: Frame duration
The time to transmit this frame at baud rate $f_{baud}$ is:

$$T_{frame} = \frac{11 \cdot (4 + N_{data})}{f_{baud}}$$

### Step 3: Example calculation
For reading 10 holding registers (20 data bytes) at 9600 bps:

$$T_{frame} = \frac{11 \cdot (4 + 20)}{9600} = \frac{11 \cdot 24}{9600} = \frac{264}{9600} \approx 27.5 \, ms$$

**Explanation:** The master must wait at least 3.5 character times (silence interval) between frames for RTU mode, which is:

$$T_{silence} = 3.5 \cdot \frac{11}{9600} \approx 4.01 \, ms$$

So the minimum cycle time per slave is approximately **31.5 ms**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Communication Standards

| Parameter | RS-232 | RS-422 | RS-485 | Ethernet | Profibus DP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Point-to-point | Multi-drop (1 driver) | Multi-drop bus | Star/Bus | Bus |
| **Max Devices** | 1 pair | 1 driver + 10 receivers | 32 (256 w/ repeaters) | Limited by switches | 127 (w/ repeaters) |
| **Max Distance** | 15 m | 1200 m | 1200 m | 100 m (copper) | 1200 m |
| **Max Speed** | 115.2 kbps | 10 Mbps | 35 Mbps | 1 Gbps+ | 12 Mbps |
| **Duplex** | Full | Full | Half/Full | Full | Half |
| **Noise Immunity** | Low | High | High | Medium-High | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Low-Medium | Medium | High |
| **Industrial Use** | HMI port | Legacy DCS | PLC networks | Modern DCS | Factory floor |

## 3.4 Communication Cycle Time in a Master-Slave Network

For a master polling $N$ slaves sequentially:

$$T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( T_{request,i} + T_{response,i} + 2T_{prop,i} + T_{silence} \right)$$

**Where:**
- $T_{request,i}$ = master request frame time
- $T_{response,i}$ = slave response frame time
- $T_{prop,i}$ = cable propagation delay to slave $i$
- $T_{silence}$ = 3.5 character inter-frame silence (Modbus RTU)

**Engineering insight:** As $N$ increases, $T_{cycle}$ grows linearly. For a process requiring **100 ms** update rate with 20 slaves, the average per-slave budget is only 5 ms. This is why critical loops are placed on dedicated high-speed networks (e.g., EtherCAT), and slower monitoring data is segregated to separate sub-networks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Industrial Communication Hierarchy (Purdue Model)

The Purdue Reference Model organizes industrial networks into hierarchical levels. Below is the Mermaid block diagram of the communication architecture used in modern PLC/DCS systems:

```mermaid
graph TB
    L0["Level 0 Process Field Devices"] --> L1["Level 1 Basic Control PLC and DCS Controllers"]
    L1 --> L2["Level 2 Supervisory HMI and SCADA"]
    L2 --> L3["Level 3 Operations Manufacturing Execution System"]
    L3 --> L4["Level 4 Enterprise ERP and Cloud"]
    L0 --> P1["Sensor Transmitter Actuator"]
    L1 --> P2["Modbus Profibus Foundation Fieldbus"]
    L2 --> P3["OPC UA Modbus TCP Ethernet IP"]
    L3 --> P4["SQL ODBC REST API"]
    L4 --> P5["ERP SAP Cloud Analytics"]
```

## 4.2 RS-485 Multi-Drop Bus Network Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    M["Master PLC"] -->|Tx Plus Tx Minus| BUS["RS-485 Bus Twisted Pair"]
    S1["Slave 1 VFD"] --> BUS
    S2["Slave 2 Flow Meter"] --> BUS
    S3["Slave 3 Temperature Transmitter"] --> BUS
    S4["Slave 4 HMI"] --> BUS
    BUS --> R1["Termination Resistor 120 Ohm A"]
    BUS --> R2["Termination Resistor 120 Ohm B"]
    BUS --> SH["Cable Shield Grounded at One End Only"]
```

## 4.3 Modbus Master-Slave Communication Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master PLC
    participant S1 as Slave 1 VFD
    participant S2 as Slave 2 Sensor
    M->>S1: Request Read Holding Register
    S1-->>M: Response with Data CRC
    M->>S2: Request Read Input Register
    S2-->>M: Response with Data CRC
    Note over M: 3.5 char silence between frames
    M->>S1: Write Single Register
    S1-->>M: Acknowledgment
```

## 4.4 OSI Layer Mapping for Industrial Protocols

```mermaid
graph TB
    L7["Layer 7 Application Modbus HART OPC UA"] --> L6["Layer 6 Presentation Data Encoding"]
    L6 --> L5["Layer 5 Session Transaction Control"]
    L5 --> L4["Layer 4 Transport TCP UDP"]
    L4 --> L3["Layer 3 Network IP"]
    L3 --> L2["Layer 2 Data Link Ethernet MAC Profibus FDL"]
    L2 --> L1["Layer 1 Physical RS-485 Fiber Ethernet Cable"]
```

## 4.5 Communication Facility Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Start Identify Need"] --> B{"Distance Greater Than 15m"}
    B -->|No| C["Use RS-232 HMI Port"]
    B -->|Yes| D{"Multiple Devices on Same Bus"}
    D -->|No| E["Use RS-422 Point to Point"]
    D -->|Yes| F{"Real Time Determinism Required"}
    F -->|Yes Critical| G["Use EtherCAT PROFINET IRT"]
    F -->|Medium| H["Use Profibus DP or Profinet"]
    F -->|No Best Effort| I["Use Modbus RTU RS-485"]
    F -->|Yes Process Industry| J["Use Foundation Fieldbus or HART"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:**
> The above block diagrams intentionally abstract the **physical layout** into **functional architecture** since Mermaid cannot render actual cable drawings. The arrows represent logical data flow rather than physical wiring topology. For actual wiring diagrams, refer to manufacturer datasheets (e.g., Allen-Bradley, Siemens, Schneider).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
**Differentiate between Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex communication modes with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Description | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | Data flows in **one direction only**; no return channel | Field transmitter sending 4–20 mA signal to PLC input card |
| **Half-Duplex** | Data flows in **both directions, but one at a time** | RS-485 2-wire Modbus network (PLC and VFD share same pair) |
| **Full-Duplex** | Data flows in **both directions simultaneously** | RS-232 (3-wire Tx, Rx, Gnd) communication between PC and PLC |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
**State any three advantages of RS-485 over RS-232 for industrial PLC networking.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Multi-drop capability:** RS-485 supports up to **32 devices on a single bus** (256 with repeaters), whereas RS-232 is point-to-point only.
2. **Longer distance:** RS-485 operates reliably up to **1200 m**; RS-232 is limited to 15 m.
3. **Superior noise immunity:** RS-485 uses **differential signaling** (twisted pair), rejecting common-mode EMI from motors, VFDs, and relays; RS-232 uses single-ended signaling vulnerable to ground loops.
4. (Bonus) Higher data rates (up to 35 Mbps vs. 115.2 kbps for RS-232).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**  
**(a)** Explain the RS-485 communication standard in detail. Discuss its electrical specifications, topology, maximum device count, and the role of termination resistors. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical DCS communication network showing the levels (field, control, supervisory, operations, enterprise). Explain one protocol used at each level. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — RS-485 Communication Standard

**Electrical Specifications:**
- **Differential voltage signaling:** A and B lines, with logic defined as $V_{A} - V_{B} > 200$ mV = MARK (1), $< -200$ mV = SPACE (0)
- **Common-mode voltage range:** −7 V to +12 V (allows operation across ground potential differences)
- **Driver output:** Minimum 1.5 V differential
- **Receiver sensitivity:** ±200 mV

**Topology:** Multi-drop bus. Single twisted pair (2-wire, half-duplex) or two twisted pairs (4-wire, full-duplex). Daisy-chain only; stubs must be kept short (< 1 m at high speeds).

**Maximum Device Count:** 32 "unit loads" (1 UL ≈ 12 kΩ input impedance). With repeaters, this extends to 256 devices.

**Termination Resistors:** A **120 Ω resistor** is placed at **each end** of the bus (matching the characteristic impedance of the cable) to **prevent signal reflection**. Without termination, high-speed signals would echo back, corrupting data. The 120 Ω also forms a voltage divider with the driver output, ensuring valid differential levels.

**Cable Specifications:** Twisted pair, 24 AWG typical, with overall shield. Shield is grounded at **one end only** to prevent ground loops.

**Biasing Resistors:** Often, a fail-safe bias network (pull-up on A, pull-down on B) is added at the master to force a known idle state.

#### Part (b) — DCS Communication Architecture

A typical DCS follows the **Purdue Reference Model** with five levels:

| Level | Function | Protocol Example | Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L0 Field** | Sensor/actuator connection | 4–20 mA + HART | Transmitters, valves |
| **L1 Basic Control** | Continuous and discrete control | Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus PA | DCS Controllers |
| **L2 Supervisory** | Operator HMI, alarming | OPC UA, Modbus TCP | Operator Stations |
| **L3 Operations** | Plant-wide data, MES | SQL/ODBC, Ethernet | Engineering Workstations |
| **L4 Enterprise** | Business integration | ERP interface, REST API | SAP, Cloud Analytics |

**Block Diagram:** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Key Concept:** Each level uses protocols appropriate for its determinism and bandwidth needs. The field level prioritizes determinism (Foundation Fieldbus); the enterprise level prioritizes data volume and IT integration (TCP/IP, cloud).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Alternate]**  
**(a)** Explain the Modbus RTU protocol. Describe its frame format, addressing, and how master-slave communication is handled. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Compare the features of Profibus DP, Modbus RTU, and HART protocols. Identify two industrial scenarios where each would be the preferred choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Modbus RTU Protocol

**Protocol Nature:** Modbus is an **open, royalty-free**, master-slave (client-server) application layer protocol. It is the **most widely deployed industrial protocol** globally.

**Physical Layer:** Typically RS-485 2-wire (also supports RS-232, TCP/IP).

**Frame Format (Modbus RTU):**

| Field | Size | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Silence interval | 3.5 char times | Frame delimiter |
| Slave Address | 1 byte (1–247) | 0 = broadcast; 1–247 = specific slave |
| Function Code | 1 byte | Specifies action (1=Read Coils, 3=Read Holding Reg, etc.) |
| Data | N bytes | Function-specific payload |
| CRC-16 | 2 bytes | Error detection (cyclic redundancy check) |
| Silence interval | 3.5 char times | Frame delimiter |

**Key Function Codes:**
- **0x01** Read Coils
- **0x03** Read Holding Registers
- **0x06** Write Single Register
- **0x10** Write Multiple Registers

**Master-Slave Operation:**
1. Master sends request frame addressed to specific slave.
2. All slaves receive but only the addressed slave processes.
3. Slave responds after processing delay (turnaround time).
4. Master verifies response and moves to next slave.
5. If no response within timeout, master declares communication failure.

**Data Encoding:** Big-endian (most significant byte first). RTU uses binary encoding for compactness; ASCII variant is human-readable but 2x larger.

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Profibus DP, Modbus RTU, HART

| Feature | Profibus DP | Modbus RTU | HART |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | 9.6 kbps – 12 Mbps | Up to 115.2 kbps typical | 1200 bps |
| **Topology** | Bus with terminators | Multi-drop bus | Point-to-point or multi-drop |
| **Max Devices** | 127 (with repeaters) | 32 (per RS-485) | 15 in multi-drop |
| **Determinism** | High | Low (polled) | Low |
| **Data Bandwidth** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Power on Cable** | No (separate) | No | Possible (intrinsic safety) |
| **Backward Compatible** | No | No | **Yes (with 4–20 mA)** |

**Preferred Scenarios:**

- **Profibus DP:** High-speed discrete factory automation (e.g., automotive assembly line with 50+ I/O stations needing < 1 ms update).
- **Modbus RTU:** Retrofit of existing RS-485 infrastructure in a water treatment plant with simple monitoring (slow scan, low device count).
- **HART:** Refinery process instrumentation where existing 4–20 mA wiring must be preserved while adding digital diagnostics (e.g., smart pressure transmitter on a distillation column).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Communication Facilities:**
> 1. **Confusing RS-232 with RS-485 voltage levels:** Students often write that RS-485 uses ±12 V. It does NOT. RS-485 is differential, ±1.5 V minimum, with common-mode range −7 V to +12 V. Marks deducted for confusing TIA/EIA standards.
> 2. **Mixing up Modbus RTU and Modbus TCP:** RTU is over RS-485 with CRC error detection; TCP is over Ethernet using TCP/IP stack. Saying "Modbus uses RS-232" is incorrect in modern context.
> 3. **Forgetting termination resistors:** Any answer about RS-485 that does not mention 120 Ω termination at bus ends will lose at least 1 mark. Termination is a **favourite KTU question**.
> 4. **Incorrect baud rate-distance relationship:** Students must not state that RS-485 always works at 1200 m. The actual distance depends on baud rate: $L \cdot f_{baud} \le 10^8$.
> 5. **Treating HART as purely digital:** HART is a **hybrid** protocol. The 4–20 mA analog signal is preserved; digital communication is superimposed via FSK. Failing to mention Bell 202 modulation costs marks.
> 6. **No mention of protocol determinism:** Industrial examiners expect students to recognize that **determinism** (worst-case latency) is a critical criterion distinguishing protocols like EtherCAT from best-effort Modbus.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Communication facilities** in PLC/DCS integrate physical layer (RS-232/422/485, Ethernet), data link protocols (Modbus, Profibus, HART), and application services (OPC UA, SCADA).
- **RS-232** is short-distance (15 m), point-to-point, single-ended; **RS-485** is multi-drop, differential, 1200 m, up to 32 devices — the **PLC network workhorse**.
- **Duplex modes:** Simplex (one-way), Half-duplex (alternating), Full-duplex (simultaneous).
- **Modbus RTU frame** = Address(1) + Function(1) + Data + CRC(2), separated by 3.5 char silence.
- **HART** uniquely combines analog 4–20 mA with digital 1200 bps FSK modulation, allowing smart instrumentation upgrades on existing wiring.
- **Profibus DP** offers high-speed deterministic communication (up to 12 Mbps) for factory automation.
- **Industrial Ethernet** (Profinet, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT) is replacing legacy fieldbuses in modern Industry 4.0 systems.
- **Termination resistors (120 Ω)** are mandatory at both ends of any RS-485 bus to prevent signal reflection.
- **The rule of thumb** $L \cdot f_{baud} \le 10^8$ defines the maximum cable length at a given baud rate.
- **Purdue Model** organizes networks into 5 levels: Field, Control, Supervisory, Operations, Enterprise.
- **Determinism** is the critical metric for safety-critical loops; best-effort protocols (Modbus TCP) are unsuitable for fast closed-loop control.
- **Shield grounding** at one end only is essential to avoid ground loops in industrial RS-485 installations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Function Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Various Function Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) and Distributed Control Systems (DCS), a **Function Block (FB)** is a standardized, reusable program organization unit (POU) defined by the **IEC 61131-3** standard. Function blocks encapsulate both **algorithmic data** (the procedure) and **internal state variables** (memory), producing one or more output values when executed with given input parameters. Unlike plain functions, FBs retain their internal state between successive executions, making them ideal for modelling continuous industrial processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** *Various Function Blocks* includes the study of timers, counters, comparators, mathematical functions, data transfer, type conversion, analog scaling, PID control, and sequencers in both PLC and DCS architectures. This topic is heavily tested in Part A (3-mark) and forms a sub-part in Part B (14-mark) questions.

The IEC 61131-3 standard defines **three programmable body types**:
1. **Functions (F):** Stateless; same input always yields same output (e.g., `SIN`, `ADD`).
2. **Function Blocks (FB):** Stateful; retain internal data between calls (e.g., `TON`, `CTU`).
3. **Programs (PRG):** Top-level logical containers for an FB or function network.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Industrial Black-Box Analogy"**

Imagine a worker in a factory assigned to one specific repetitive job — say, *measuring the temperature of a furnace every 5 seconds and turning ON a cooling fan if it exceeds 80°C*. Now imagine you write down this worker's entire job description, hand it to a new worker, and that new worker remembers the last reading and the elapsed time even after lunch. That "remembering" capability is what makes a **Function Block** different from a plain **Function**.

- A **Function** is like a calculator: `2 + 3` always gives `5`, no memory.
- A **Function Block** is like a *cumulative scoreboard* — it remembers the previous score, so the next "score" is built on the last one. This is critical for **timers** (remembering elapsed time) and **counters** (remembering accumulated count).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PT** | **Preset Time** — target duration (ms or s) | IEC 61131-3 |
| **ET** | **Elapsed Time** — current accumulated time | IEC 61131-3 |
| **PV** | **Process Variable** — feedback from field sensor | ISA-88 / DCS |
| **CV** | **Control Variable** — output to final element | ISA-88 / DCS |
| **SP** | **Setpoint** — desired target value | Industrial Standard |
| **K_c, T_i, T_d** | **PID tuning constants** (gain, integral, derivative time) | Ziegler-Nichols |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Function Blocks (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Function blocks in PLC/DCS are broadly classified into the following categories:

### A. Timer Function Blocks
Timer FBs measure elapsed time between events. The IEC 61131-3 defines three primary timer types:

#### i. **TON (On-Delay Timer)**
- Starts timing the moment the input `IN` becomes TRUE.
- Output `Q` becomes TRUE only **after** `ET ≥ PT`.
- Used for "start after delay" logic.

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Scan input `IN`. If `IN = FALSE`, reset internal clock: `ET := 0`, `Q := FALSE`.
2. If `IN = TRUE`, start incrementing `ET` every scan cycle.
3. If `ET ≥ PT`, set `Q := TRUE`. Otherwise, `Q := FALSE`.
4. `ET` is clamped: it never exceeds `PT` while `IN` is TRUE.

#### ii. **TOF (Off-Delay Timer)**
- The inverse of TON. Output `Q` remains TRUE while `IN` is TRUE.
- When `IN` transitions to FALSE, timer starts; `Q` stays TRUE until `ET ≥ PT`, then becomes FALSE.
- Used for "keep running after stop" logic (e.g., cooling fan after motor stop).

#### iii. **TP (Pulse Timer)**
- On any rising edge of `IN`, output `Q` is set TRUE for exactly `PT` duration, regardless of input changes.
- Used for fixed-width pulse generation.

### B. Counter Function Blocks

#### i. **CTU (Count-Up Counter)**
- Increments internal `CV` on every rising edge of `CU`.
- Resets to zero when `R = TRUE`.
- Output `Q` becomes TRUE when `CV ≥ PV`.

#### ii. **CTD (Count-Down Counter)**
- Decrements `CV` on every rising edge of `CD`.
- Loads `PV` when `LD = TRUE`.
- Output `Q` becomes TRUE when `CV ≤ 0`.

#### iii. **CTUD (Count-Up/Down Counter)**
- Combines both; has separate `CU`, `CD`, `R`, `LD` inputs and `QU`, `QD` outputs.

### C. Mathematical Function Blocks
- **ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, ABS, SQRT, EXPT, LN, LOG, SIN, COS, TAN**
- Operate on `REAL`, `LREAL`, `INT`, `DINT`, `LINT` data types.

### D. Comparison Function Blocks
- **EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE** — return BOOL based on input comparison.
- Critical in alarm management and interlock logic.

### E. Data Manipulation Blocks
- **MOVE (:=), BLKMOV, FILL, SWAP, SHL, SHR, ROL, ROR** — used to transfer, copy, or shift bit patterns between memory locations.

### F. Type Conversion Blocks
- **INT_TO_REAL, REAL_TO_INT, BOOL_TO_INT, WORD_TO_DWORD**, etc.
- Essential when interfacing analog inputs (REAL) with discrete logic (INT).

### G. Analog Scaling Function Block
- A custom or built-in FB (e.g., `N7_SCL` in Allen-Bradley, `FC106` in Siemens) that converts raw 16-bit ADC value (0–27648 typical) into engineering units (e.g., 0–100 °C).

The standard linear scaling formula is:

$$
\text{Out} = \left( \frac{\text{In} - \text{In}_{\min}}{\text{In}_{\max} - \text{In}_{\min}} \right) \times (\text{Out}_{\max} - \text{Out}_{\min}) + \text{Out}_{\min}
$$

### H. PID Function Block
- Implements Proportional-Integral-Derivative control on a process variable.
- The standard ISA form of PID output is:

$$
u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

Where `e(t) = SP - PV`. In discrete-time (PLC scan-based), the derivative is approximated by backward difference and the integral by trapezoidal sum.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Timer Elapsed Time update | $ET_{k} = ET_{k-1} + \Delta t$ | $ET, \Delta t$ in seconds |
| 2 | Counter Increment | $CV_{k} = CV_{k-1} + 1$ | Integer count |
| 3 | Counter Decrement | $CV_{k} = CV_{k-1} - 1$ | Integer count |
| 4 | Analog Scaling | $\text{Out} = \text{Out}_{\min} + \dfrac{(\text{In} - \text{In}_{\min}) \times (\text{Out}_{\max} - \text{Out}_{\min})}{\text{In}_{\max} - \text{In}_{\min}}$ | Engineering Units |
| 5 | Raw ADC % Span | $\%\text{span} = \dfrac{\text{Raw} - 0}{27648 - 0} \times 100$ | Percent |
| 6 | PID Output (ISA) | $u(t) = K_c\,e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{T_i}\int e\,dt + K_c T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Control signal % |
| 7 | PID Output (incremental / velocity form) | $\Delta u_k = K_c\!\left[\Delta e_k + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i}e_k + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}\Delta^2 e_k \right]$ | Incremental |
| 8 | Deadband for Quantization Error | $\text{Error}_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{\text{Range}}{2^{n}-1}$ | Volts or Units |
| 9 | Boolean to Bit weight | $\text{Word} = \sum_{i=0}^{15} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | Decimal weight |
| 10 | Number of bits in 16-bit register | $n = 16$ | — |
| 11 | Number resolution | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Range}_{\max}}{2^{n}-1}$ | Units / LSB |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Deployment Examples:**
> - **TON** in conveyor belt sequential start-up: Each motor is delayed by 5 s from the previous to avoid inrush current peaks.
> - **CTU** in bottle-filling lines: Count 24 bottles per crate; when count = 24, fire a cylinder to push the crate.
> - **PID** in a temperature oven: Maintain $SP = 200 \pm 1\,^\circ C$ for curing rubber.
> - **Analog Scaling** in tank-level control: Convert 4–20 mA signal (corresponding to 0–10 m water level) into 0–100% in HMI.
> - **SHL/SHR** in traffic light sequencers: Walk a single 1-bit through a word to drive LEDs in sequence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Analog Scaling Function Block

**Problem Context (KTU-style):** A pressure transmitter outputs a 4–20 mA signal corresponding to 0–10 bar. The PLC's analog input card converts this current to a **raw integer** between 0 and 27648 (12-bit unipolar, sign-corrected). Derive the linear scaling formula that maps the raw input `Raw_In` to engineering pressure in bar.

### Step 1 — Identify the Input-Output Mapping

At $I = 4\,\text{mA}$, $\text{Raw\_In} = 0$ and the pressure $P = 0\,\text{bar}$.
At $I = 20\,\text{mA}$, $\text{Raw\_In} = 27648$ and $P = 10\,\text{bar}$.

So we have two coordinate pairs:
$$
(\text{Raw}_{\min}, P_{\min}) = (0, 0) \quad \text{and} \quad (\text{Raw}_{\max}, P_{\max}) = (27648, 10)
$$

### Step 2 — State the Linear Interpolation Principle

For a linear sensor, output is proportional to input. The two-point form of a line is:

$$
\frac{P - P_{\min}}{\text{Raw} - \text{Raw}_{\min}} = \frac{P_{\max} - P_{\min}}{\text{Raw}_{\max} - \text{Raw}_{\min}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Known Values

$$
\frac{P - 0}{\text{Raw} - 0} = \frac{10 - 0}{27648 - 0}
$$

$$
\frac{P}{\text{Raw}} = \frac{10}{27648}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for P

$$
P = \text{Raw} \times \frac{10}{27648}
$$

$$
P = \text{Raw} \times 3.6177 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{bar}
$$

### Step 5 — Generalize to Arbitrary Input/Output Ranges

Now let us derive the general formula. Define span in raw units and span in engineering units.

$$
\Delta \text{Raw} = \text{Raw}_{\max} - \text{Raw}_{\min}
$$
$$
\Delta \text{Eng} = \text{Eng}_{\max} - \text{Eng}_{\min}
$$

Then the scaling factor is:

$$
K = \frac{\Delta \text{Eng}}{\Delta \text{Raw}}
$$

The final scaled engineering output is:

$$
\text{Eng\_Out} = (\text{Raw\_In} - \text{Raw}_{\min}) \times K + \text{Eng}_{\min}
$$

### Step 6 — Verification

Check both endpoints:
- $\text{Raw\_In} = 0$: $P = (0 - 0) \times 3.6177\times10^{-4} + 0 = 0$ bar ✓
- $\text{Raw\_In} = 27648$: $P = 27648 \times 3.6177\times10^{-4} = 10.000$ bar ✓
- $\text{Raw\_In} = 13824$ (mid-scale): $P = 13824 \times 3.6177\times10^{-4} = 5.000$ bar ✓

### Step 7 — Offset Compensation for 4–20 mA Live-Zero

In some cards, $4\,\text{mA}$ maps to $\text{Raw} = 0$ but $0\,\text{mA}$ is treated as fault. If raw input is $R$, then engineering output with live-zero is:

$$
\text{Eng\_Out} = \text{Eng}_{\min} + \frac{R \times 27648 - 0}{27648 - 0} \times (\text{Eng}_{\max} - \text{Eng}_{\min})
$$

This is equivalent to:

$$
\text{Eng\_Out} = \text{Eng}_{\min} + R \times \frac{\Delta \text{Eng}}{1.0}
$$

if $R$ is already normalized to a per-unit fraction $[0, 1]$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Timer Behavior

We construct a complete timing analysis for a **TON timer** with $\text{PT} = 5\,\text{s}$. The PLC scan time is $\Delta t = 100\,\text{ms}$.

| Scan # | Time (s) | Input `IN` | $ET$ before | $ET$ after | $Q$ Output | Remarks |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 0.1 | 0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | FALSE | Input low → no counting |
| 2 | 0.2 | 1 | 0.0 | 0.1 | FALSE | IN rises, ET starts |
| 3 | 0.3 | 1 | 0.1 | 0.2 | FALSE | Counting up |
| 4 | 0.4 | 1 | 0.2 | 0.3 | FALSE | Counting up |
| … | … | … | … | … | … | … |
| 51 | 5.1 | 1 | 4.9 | 5.0 | **TRUE** | $ET \geq PT$ achieved |
| 52 | 5.2 | 0 | 5.0 | 0.0 | FALSE | Input falls, ET reset |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Counter Behavior

Let $\text{PV} = 4$ and rising edges arrive at $t = 1, 2, 3, 5, 6\,\text{s}$.

| Edge # | Time (s) | $CV$ before | $CV$ after | $Q$ Output | Remarks |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 1.0 | 0 | 1 | FALSE | $CV < PV$ |
| 2 | 2.0 | 1 | 2 | FALSE | $CV < PV$ |
| 3 | 3.0 | 2 | 3 | FALSE | $CV < PV$ |
| 4 | 5.0 | 3 | 4 | **TRUE** | $CV \geq PV$ |
| 5 | 6.0 | 4 | 5 | TRUE | $CV$ keeps incrementing |

## 3.4 Derivation: Discrete-Time PID Output (Position Form)

The continuous-time PID is:

$$
u(t) = K_c\,e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

### Step 1 — Discretize the Integral via Backward Rectangular Approximation

$$
\int_{0}^{k T_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \approx T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e_i
$$

### Step 2 — Discretize the Derivative via Backward Difference

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt}\Big|_{t=k T_s} \approx \frac{e_k - e_{k-1}}{T_s}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine

$$
u_k = K_c\,e_k + \frac{K_c\,T_s}{T_i}\sum_{i=0}^{k} e_i + \frac{K_c\,T_d}{T_s}(e_k - e_{k-1})
$$

This is the **position-form** PID. It is called "position" because $u_k$ is the absolute valve position (e.g., 0–100%).

### Step 4 — Derive the Velocity Form (Incremental)

Subtracting $u_{k-1}$ from $u_k$ cancels the integral accumulator term (which depends on the entire history):

$$
\Delta u_k = u_k - u_{k-1}
$$

This gives the **velocity form**:

$$
\Delta u_k = K_c\bigl[\,e_k - e_{k-1}\,\bigr] + \frac{K_c\,T_s}{T_i}\,e_k + \frac{K_c\,T_d}{T_s}\,\bigl[e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2}\bigr]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the velocity form matters in KTU exams:** The velocity form is *bumpless* on controller restart and does not require integral wind-up protection — a common viva question.

### Step 5 — Anti-Windup (Clamp on Integral Term)

A common practical implementation is to clamp the integral sum:

$$
I_k = \begin{cases} I_{k-1} + e_k, & \text{if } u_{\min} \leq u_k \leq u_{\max} \\ I_{k-1}, & \text{otherwise (saturated)} \end{cases}
$$

This prevents the integrator from accumulating error while the valve is saturated, which would cause a large overshoot on recovery.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** A 12-bit unipolar ADC is used to read a signal in the range 0–10 V. Find the resolution and the quantization error. If a reading is 2050 (decimal), what voltage does it represent?

### Step 1 — Compute Resolution

The ADC has $n = 12$ bits, so total levels = $2^{12} = 4096$.

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{\max} - V_{\min}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{10 - 0}{4095} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 2.442\,\text{mV/LSB}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Quantization Error (Maximum)

$$
\text{Error}_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.442\,\text{mV} = 1.221\,\text{mV}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Voltage for Raw = 2050

$$
V = \text{Raw} \times \text{Resolution} = 2050 \times 2.442 \times 10^{-3} = 5.006\,\text{V}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** "1 LSB is resolution. Half LSB is the maximum quantization error." This is a favourite viva question and appears in nearly every KTU paper.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Function Block Lifecycle in a PLC Scan

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the call cycle of a function block during one PLC scan.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Scan Cycle] --> B[Read All Physical Inputs]
    B --> C[Execute Program: Function Block Network]
    C --> D[FB1 TON Timer]
    C --> E[FB2 CTU Counter]
    C --> F[FB3 PID Controller]
    D --> G[Update Internal State ET CV]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Evaluate Output Q of each FB]
    H --> I[Write All Physical Outputs]
    I --> J[End of Scan Cycle]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Analog Scaling Function Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor 4 to 20 mA] --> B[Analog Input Module]
    B --> C[Raw Integer 0 to 27648]
    C --> D[Scaling FB]
    D --> E[Engineering Value 0 to 10 bar]
    E --> F[HMI Display]
    E --> G[PID Controller Input]
    D --> H[Alarm High or Low FB]
    H --> I[Siren or Lamp]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: PID Control Loop in DCS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Summer Node e = SP minus PV]
    PV[Process Variable PV] --> SUM
    SUM --> PID[PID Function Block]
    PID --> CV[Control Variable CV]
    CV --> VALVE[Control Valve Actuator]
    VALVE --> PROC[Industrial Process Tank]
    PROC --> SENSOR[Temperature or Pressure Sensor]
    SENSOR --> TRANSMIT[Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
    TRANSMIT --> AI[Analog Input Card]
    AI --> SCALE[Scaling Function Block]
    SCALE --> PV
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Counter-Based Bottle Crating Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Proximity Sensor] --> B[CTU Counter]
    B --> C{Is CV greater or equal to PV 24}
    C -- No --> D[Continue Counting]
    C -- Yes --> E[Set Output Q TRUE]
    E --> F[Trigger Pusher Cylinder]
    F --> G[Reset Counter via R]
    G --> B
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: TON Timer Conveyor Sequencing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[Master Start Pushbutton] --> T1[TON 5 seconds]
    T1 --> M1[Conveyor 1 Motor]
    M1 --> T2[TON 5 seconds]
    T2 --> M2[Conveyor 2 Motor]
    M2 --> T3[TON 5 seconds]
    T3 --> M3[Conveyor 3 Motor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU papers:** Draw clear block diagrams with **inputs on the left, outputs on the right, and internal state in the middle**. Always label signal lines and add a legend.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a Function and a Function Block as per IEC 61131-3.**

**Model Answer:**
- A **Function** is a POU that, when called with the same input arguments, always produces the same output. It has no internal memory (stateless). Example: `SIN(x)`, `ADD(a,b)`.
- A **Function Block (FB)** is a POU that retains its internal state variables between successive calls. The output depends on both the inputs *and* the stored internal state (stateful). Example: `TON`, `CTU`, `PID`.
- Functions can be called within FBs; FBs cannot be called inside functions.
- FBs require an instance name and a backing data block; functions do not.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Explain the operation of a TOF (Off-Delay) timer with a neat timing diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
A TOF (Off-Delay) timer is used to keep its output `Q` TRUE for a preset time `PT` *after* the input `IN` goes FALSE. The operating logic is:

1. When `IN` becomes TRUE, output `Q` is set to TRUE immediately and `ET` remains at zero.
2. When `IN` transitions from TRUE to FALSE, the timer starts counting up: `ET` increments every scan.
3. Output `Q` remains TRUE until `ET ≥ PT`.
4. When `ET ≥ PT`, `Q` becomes FALSE, and `ET` is reset to 0.

**Timing Diagram (textual representation):**

| Signal | t=0 to 3s | t=3 to 8s | t=8 to 10s | t=10s onward |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Input IN | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | LOW |
| Output Q | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH (still) | LOW (after PT=5s) |
| ET | 0 | 0 | 0 to 5s | Reset to 0 |

**Application:** Used to keep a cooling fan running for 30 seconds after a motor stops, to dissipate residual heat.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the various types of timer function blocks used in PLC programming. Compare ON-delay, OFF-delay, and Pulse timers with timing diagrams.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A temperature sensor outputs 4–20 mA corresponding to 0–200 °C. The PLC receives a raw value `R` in the range 0–27648. Derive the scaling equation and calculate the temperature when R = 13824, 6912, and 20736.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Timer Classification (IEC 61131-3 / KTU 2024):**

1. **TON (On-Delay Timer)**
   - Input `IN`, output `Q`, inputs `PT`, output `ET`.
   - When `IN` transitions from FALSE to TRUE, `ET` starts incrementing.
   - `Q` becomes TRUE only when `ET ≥ PT`.
   - When `IN` goes FALSE, `ET` is reset to 0 and `Q` becomes FALSE immediately.

2. **TOF (Off-Delay Timer)**
   - `Q` becomes TRUE the moment `IN` becomes TRUE.
   - On the falling edge of `IN`, `ET` starts counting.
   - `Q` falls to FALSE only when `ET ≥ PT`.
   - Used to extend an action after the trigger is removed.

3. **TP (Pulse Timer)**
   - On a rising edge of `IN`, `Q` is set TRUE for a duration of exactly `PT`, regardless of further changes in `IN`.
   - Common in valve-stroking and brake-engagement logic.

**Block Diagram (textual):**

```
       ┌──────────────┐
  IN ─►│   TIMER FB   │──► Q
  PT ─►│ (TON/TOF/TP) │
       └──────┬───────┘
              ▼
              ET ──► (read for monitoring)
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | TON | TOF | TP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trigger on rising edge | Starts timing | Sets Q=TRUE | Starts fixed pulse |
| Trigger on falling edge | Resets ET, Q=FALSE | Starts timing | No effect |
| Output behaviour | TRUE after PT delay | TRUE until PT after IN falls | TRUE for exactly PT |
| Application | Conveyor start delay | Fan run-down | Brake engagement |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify Scaling Pairs**

$$
(R_{\min}, T_{\min}) = (0, 0\,^\circ C) \quad \text{and} \quad (R_{\max}, T_{\max}) = (27648, 200\,^\circ C)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Linear Scaling Formula**

$$
T = T_{\min} + \frac{(R - R_{\min}) \times (T_{\max} - T_{\min})}{R_{\max} - R_{\min}}
$$

$$
T = 0 + \frac{R \times 200}{27648} = R \times 7.234 \times 10^{-3}\,^\circ C
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Three Values**

- For $R = 13824$:
$$
T = 13824 \times 7.234 \times 10^{-3} = 100.0\,^\circ C
$$

- For $R = 6912$:
$$
T = 6912 \times 7.234 \times 10^{-3} = 50.0\,^\circ C
$$

- For $R = 20736$:
$$
T = 20736 \times 7.234 \times 10^{-3} = 150.0\,^\circ C
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - Students often forget to subtract the offset and write $T = R \times \frac{200}{27648}$ even when $R_{\min} \neq 0$. If the sensor range is 4–20 mA and the card maps this to 0–27648, the lower bound is correctly 0, but in 0–10 V or bipolar ranges, the offset is non-zero. Always state both endpoints explicitly.
> - A common mistake: writing the formula as $T = \frac{R}{27648} \times 200$ without clarifying the assumption $T_{\min} = 0$. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing assumptions.
> - Students frequently confuse TON with TOF in diagrams. Always include the legend on the timing diagram.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the operation of the CTUD (Count-Up/Down) counter with its block diagram. Explain how it differs from CTU and CTD counters. Give one industrial application.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a PID function block in a DCS environment. Derive the discrete-time position form of the PID algorithm and state the significance of anti-windup.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**CTUD Block Diagram (textual):**

```
                ┌────────────────────┐
    CU ────────►│                    │──► QU (CV ≥ PV)
    CD ────────►│   CTUD COUNTER FB  │
    R  ────────►│   (Bidirectional)  │──► QD (CV ≤ 0)
    LD ────────►│                    │
    PV ────────►│                    │──► CV (current value)
                └────────────────────┘
```

**Operating Logic:**
1. On every rising edge of `CU`, internal `CV := CV + 1`.
2. On every rising edge of `CD`, internal `CV := CV - 1`.
3. If `R` (Reset) is TRUE, `CV := 0`.
4. If `LD` (Load) is TRUE, `CV := PV`.
5. `QU := TRUE` if `CV ≥ PV`.
6. `QD := TRUE` if `CV ≤ 0`.

**Difference from CTU and CTD:**

| Feature | CTU | CTD | CTUD |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Count direction | Up only | Down only | Both |
| Number of counter inputs | 1 (CU) | 1 (CD) | 2 (CU, CD) |
| Outputs | 1 (Q) | 1 (Q) | 2 (QU, QD) |
| Load input | No | Yes (LD) | Yes (LD) |
| Reset input | Yes (R) | No | Yes (R) |

**Industrial Application:**
In a parking-lot management system, two photoelectric sensors are used:
- Sensor A at the entry increments `CV` when a car enters.
- Sensor B at the exit decrements `CV` when a car exits.
- A display shows the number of available slots. When `CV ≥ PV` (lot full), a "FULL" sign is lit.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**PID in DCS — Functional Block Description:**

A PID function block in a DCS is a self-contained software object that:
- Accepts `SP` (setpoint) and `PV` (process variable) inputs.
- Internally computes error $e = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$.
- Outputs a control signal `CV` to the final control element (e.g., a valve).
- Has tuning parameters $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$ configurable by the operator.
- Implements anti-windup, output clamping, and bumpless transfer as built-in features.

**Derivation of Discrete Position Form (already shown in §3.4):**

$$
u_k = K_c\,e_k + \frac{K_c\,T_s}{T_i}\sum_{i=0}^{k} e_i + \frac{K_c\,T_d}{T_s}(e_k - e_{k-1})
$$

where $T_s$ is the scan period (sampling time).

**Significance of Anti-Windup:**

When the actuator saturates (e.g., valve at 100%), the error `e` continues to be non-zero. In a pure integrator, this causes the integral term to keep growing unbounded — the "windup" effect. When the setpoint finally changes, the controller overshoots dangerously before recovering.

Anti-windup mechanisms include:
1. **Clamping:** Stop updating the integral sum when output saturates.
2. **Back-calculation:** Feed back the difference between the unconstrained and constrained output into the integrator with gain $T_t$ (tracking time constant).
3. **Conditional integration:** Integrate only when error is within a deadband.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> - For 7-mark questions, students must show the **integral** and **derivative** discretization steps explicitly. Skipping these steps costs 2–3 marks.
> - A common error is writing $u_k = K_p + K_i + K_d$ without defining what $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are. Always relate them back to $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$, and $T_s$.
> - In the CTUD diagram, students often forget to label the `LD` and `R` inputs separately, which causes 1-mark deduction.
> - Examiners look for "real-world application" — always state at least one industrial example.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Various Function Blocks**

- ☐ **IEC 61131-3 POUs:** Functions (stateless) vs Function Blocks (stateful) vs Programs.
- ☐ **Timers:** TON (delay-on), TOF (delay-off), TP (pulse). Remember the order: $IN$ rising edge behaviour, $IN$ falling edge behaviour, $Q$ transition condition.
- ☐ **Counters:** CTU (counts up, resets via R), CTD (counts down, loads PV via LD), CTUD (both, with QU and QD).
- ☐ **Mathematical FBs:** ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, ABS, SQRT, SIN, COS — operate on REAL/INT data types.
- ☐ **Comparison FBs:** EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE — all return BOOL.
- ☐ **Data Movement:** MOVE (`:=`), BLKMOV (block), FILL (fill with constant), SHL/SHR (shift), ROL/ROR (rotate).
- ☐ **Type Conversion:** INT_TO_REAL, REAL_TO_INT, BOOL_TO_INT — critical for analog-to-discrete interface.
- ☐ **Analog Scaling:** $Eng = Eng_{min} + \frac{(R - R_{min})(Eng_{max} - Eng_{min})}{R_{max} - R_{min}}$ — know by heart.
- ☐ **ADC Resolution:** $\frac{V_{max}}{2^n - 1}$ and quantization error = $\frac{1}{2} \times$ resolution.
- ☐ **PID Position Form:** $u_k = K_c e_k + \frac{K_c T_s}{T_i}\sum e_i + \frac{K_c T_d}{T_s}(e_k - e_{k-1})$.
- ☐ **PID Velocity Form:** $\Delta u_k = K_c[(e_k - e_{k-1}) + \frac{T_s}{T_i}e_k + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2})]$.
- ☐ **Anti-Windup:** Clamp integrator when actuator saturates; back-calculate; conditional integration.
- ☐ **Sequencer FBs:** DRUM, SFC (Sequential Function Chart) — for state-based process control.
- ☐ **Bit-Logic FBs:** SR (Set-Reset) and RS (Reset-Set) flip-flop blocks; latch/unlatch.
- ☐ **Edge Detection:** R_TRIG (rising edge), F_TRIG (falling edge).
- ☐ **Draw clean block diagrams** with inputs on the left, outputs on the right, internal state in the middle. **Always label signal lines.**
- ☐ **Industrial context earns you 1–2 bonus marks** — always cite at least one real application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Inspection Automation System Using Sensors, PLC and SCADA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Inspection Automation System Using Sensors, PLC and SCADA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inspection Automation System (IAS)** is an integrated electro-mechanical-cybernetic arrangement that combines **sensors** (for data acquisition), a **Programmable Logic Controller** (for real-time decision making), and a **SCADA** (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) interface (for centralized monitoring, visualization, and archival) to automatically verify the physical, dimensional, or functional conformity of a product against a predefined quality standard — without continuous human intervention.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *OERUT722 – PLC and Distributed Control Systems* (Module 4), the design of an inspection automation system constitutes the practical integration of the three pillars of industrial automation — **Sensing, Control, and Supervision** — into a single, deterministic, fault-tolerant loop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **security checkpoint at an airport**:
- The **metal detector door** and **X-ray scanner** are the *sensors* — they "see" the luggage and the passenger.
- The **security officer** standing at the monitor is the *PLC* — he instantly decides: "Pass," "Stop," or "Inspect Manually."
- The **big wall-mounted screen in the control room** showing live camera feeds from all checkpoints is the *SCADA* — supervisors can see the entire airport's status from one place and pull up historical logs.

Replace the airport with a *bottle-filling plant*, the metal detector with a *vision camera checking the fill level*, the security officer with a *PLC deciding Accept / Reject*, and the wall screen with a *SCADA HMI showing OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) dashboards* — and you have a textbook inspection automation system.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following parameters are universally used in industry to characterize any inspection automation system:
> - **Cycle Time (T_c)**: Total time from sensor trigger to actuator response, typically **0.1 s to 5 s**.
> - **False Accept Rate (FAR)**: Probability of accepting a defective unit — industrial target: **< 0.1%**.
> - **False Reject Rate (FRR)**: Probability of rejecting a good unit — industrial target: **< 0.05%**.
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**: System reliability metric, typically **≥ 50,000 hours**.
> - **Inspection Resolution**: Smallest defect detectable, measured in **mm** or **pixels/mm**.
> - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)**: Quality metric benchmark = **85% (World-Class)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of an Inspection System

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats inspection automation as a **hierarchical, closed-loop system** consisting of three distinct functional layers:

**Layer 1 — Field Level (Sensors & Actuators)**
- Responsible for converting physical phenomena (presence, dimension, color, weight, temperature) into electrical signals.
- Operates on **24 V DC** standard industrial voltage.
- Response time is critical; sensors must be **5x faster** than the conveyor speed's dwell time.

**Layer 2 — Control Level (PLC)**
- The "brain" of the system. Executes a deterministic scan cycle:

$$T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{comm}$$

- Inputs: discrete (24 V) and analog (4–20 mA / 0–10 V).
- Outputs: discrete (relay/transistor) and analog (PWM / ±10 V).
- Typical scan times: **1 ms to 20 ms**.

**Layer 3 — Supervisory Level (SCADA)**
- Provides **HMIs, trending, alarming, and historical archiving**.
- Communicates with PLCs via industrial protocols: **Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA**.
- Polling cycle: typically **100 ms to 1 s**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Step

- **Why sensors first?** Because no control decision is possible without reliable data — *garbage in, garbage out* is the golden rule of automation.
- **Why PLC, not a PC?** Because PLCs are deterministic, rugged, and immune to OS crashes. They guarantee execution within a bounded cycle time — a hard requirement for safety.
- **Why SCADA on top?** Because operators need a *bird's-eye view*; a single screen must show the status of 200 inspection stations without overwhelming the operator.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula and standard metric you must memorize for KTU 2024 examination purposes on this topic.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Standard Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------------------|---------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | PLC Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$ | ms | Total cycle duration |
| 2 | Conveyor Dwell Time | $T_{dwell} = \dfrac{L_{insp}}{v_{belt}}$ | s | Time product is under sensor |
| 3 | Sensor Selection Rule | $T_{response} \leq 0.2 \cdot T_{dwell}$ | s | Avoids missed triggers |
| 4 | Throughput Rate | $R = \dfrac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$ | units/hr | Pieces inspected per hour |
| 5 | OEE Formula | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % (0–100) | Overall Equipment Effectiveness |
| 6 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{T_{run}}{T_{planned}}$ | ratio | Uptime proportion |
| 7 | Performance | $P = \dfrac{T_{ideal} \times N_{total}}{T_{run}}$ | ratio | Speed efficiency |
| 8 | Quality | $Q = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | ratio | First-pass yield |
| 9 | Reject Rate | $RR = \dfrac{N_{reject}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Rejection percentage |
| 10 | Resolution (Vision) | $R_{px} = \dfrac{FOV}{N_{pixels}}$ | mm/px | Smallest detectable defect |
| 11 | Bandwidth Requirement | $BW = N_{tags} \times U_{tag}$ | bps | SCADA network load |
| 12 | Bit Packing (Integer) | $V = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$ | integer | Binary value decoding |

> **Where:**
> - $L_{insp}$ = Length of inspection zone on conveyor
> - $v_{belt}$ = Belt linear velocity
> - $T_{cycle}$ = Time between two successive products
> - $N_{tags}$, $U_{tag}$ = Number and size of SCADA data tags
> - $FOV$ = Camera Field of View
> - $N_{pixels}$ = Total pixels across FOV

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inspection automation systems are the backbone of:
- **Pharmaceutical packaging** (blister pack integrity, tablet count).
- **Automotive final assembly** (torque, paint defect, weld seam).
- **Food & Beverage** (fill level, cap torque, label alignment, foreign object detection).
- **Electronics SMT** (solder joint inspection — AOI).
- **Semiconductor wafer fabrication** (sub-µm defect classification).

In Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing, these systems are now extended with **Machine Learning-based vision classifiers**, **edge analytics**, and **digital twins** — all running atop the classical Sensor → PLC → SCADA stack.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Design Methodology — The 7-Stage KTU Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes a structured design procedure for inspection automation systems. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step analytical methodology.

---

#### **Stage 1: Define the Inspection Specification**

Step 1.1 — Identify the **physical parameter** to be inspected (presence, dimension, color, weight, etc.).

Step 1.2 — Quantify the **acceptance limits**:

$$x_{lower} \leq x_{measured} \leq x_{upper}$$

Step 1.3 — Define the **defect classification matrix** (which defect type causes which outcome):

$$\text{Defect Map}: D_i \;\mapsto\; \{ \text{Accept}, \text{Reject}, \text{Rework} \}$$

---

#### **Stage 2: Determine the Throughput and Timing Budget**

Step 2.1 — Given the production rate $R$ (units/hr), compute the per-unit cycle time:

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{3600}{R} \quad [\text{seconds/unit}]$$

**Conversion logic:** Since 1 hour = 3600 seconds, the per-unit time is obtained by simple division.

Step 2.2 — Compute the **conveyor dwell time** (the window during which the product is physically under the sensor):

$$T_{dwell} = \frac{L_{insp}}{v_{belt}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Length divided by velocity yields time (units: m ÷ m/s = s).

Step 2.3 — The sensor response time must satisfy the **5x faster rule** for reliability:

$$T_{sensor} \leq \frac{T_{dwell}}{5}$$

---

#### **Stage 3: Sensor Selection & Justification**

Step 3.1 — Build a **selection matrix** mapping inspection parameter → sensor technology:

Step 3.2 — Apply the **resolution constraint**:

$$R_{px} = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}} \leq \frac{x_{min}}{2}$$

**Conversion logic:** To resolve a defect of size $x_{min}$, the smallest addressable pixel must be at most half of $x_{min}$ (Nyquist sampling principle).

Step 3.3 — Verify the **analog signal conditioning** for 4–20 mA loops:

$$I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \quad [\text{mA}]$$

**Conversion logic:** A 4 mA output represents the lower range value (LRV) and 20 mA represents the upper range value (URV); the linear interpolation above gives the current proportional to the measured value.

---

#### **Stage 4: PLC I/O Allocation**

Step 4.1 — Compile the **Input/Output Address List (IOL)**:

For each physical signal, assign a unique PLC address. Standard addressing convention:

| Type | Convention | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| Digital Input | I:0/X or %IX0.X | I:0/0 (Start PB) |
| Digital Output | O:0/X or %QX0.X | O:0/2 (Reject Solenoid) |
| Analog Input | IW:X or %IW | IW:2 (Load Cell) |
| Analog Output | QW:X or %QW | QW:1 (VFD Speed Ref) |

Step 4.2 — Compute **total I/O count** to size the PLC rack:

$$N_{total} = N_{DI} + N_{DO} + N_{AI} + N_{AO} + 20\%\;\text{spare}$$

**Conversion logic:** Always reserve 20% spare channels for future expansion, retrofitting, and troubleshooting.

---

#### **Stage 5: Ladder Logic Development**

Step 5.1 — The PLC executes a **cyclic scan**:

$$T_{scan} = T_{read\;inputs} + T_{execute\;program} + T_{write\;outputs} + T_{housekeeping}$$

Step 5.2 — Logic is organized into **rungs** representing the inspection decision:

**Pseudo-logic representation (NOT executable code — conceptual only):**

```
Rung 1: |  Sensor_OK   Sensor_Trigger   Product_Present   |
        |  ----------- ---------------- ------------------ |
        |                                               |
        |   ( ) Inspection_Active                       |
        |-----------------------------------------------|

Rung 2: |  Inspection_Active   ( x_meas > x_upper )     |
        |  ------------------------------------         |
        |                ( ) Reject_Actuator            |
        |-----------------------------------------------|

Rung 3: |  Inspection_Active   ( x_lower <= x_meas )    |
        |  ( x_meas <= x_upper )                        |
        |  -----------------------------------          |
        |                ( ) Accept_Indicator           |
        |-----------------------------------------------|
```

Step 5.3 — Verify **worst-case scan time** stays below 70% of $T_{dwell}$:

$$T_{scan,\;max} \leq 0.7 \cdot T_{dwell}$$

---

#### **Stage 6: SCADA Tag Database & Screen Design**

Step 6.1 — Build the **tag dictionary** (minimum mandatory tags):

Step 6.2 — Compute network bandwidth:

$$BW = N_{tags} \times U_{tag} \times f_{poll} \quad [\text{bits/sec}]$$

**Conversion logic:** Each tag is polled at frequency $f_{poll}$ (Hz), with payload $U_{tag}$ bits. The total bandwidth must remain under **70% of the link capacity** (good engineering practice).

Step 6.3 — Design **operator screens**:
- **Overview Screen**: Plant-wide mimic with color-coded status.
- **Detail Screen**: Per-station live values, trends, and alarms.
- **Alarm Summary**: Active, acknowledged, and historical alarms.
- **Recipe Screen**: Product changeover parameters.

---

#### **Stage 7: Sorting / Reject Mechanism Integration**

Step 7.1 — Calculate the **timing offset** between defect detection point and reject actuator:

$$t_{offset} = \frac{d_{sensor \to actuator}}{v_{belt}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The PLC must delay the reject command by exactly $t_{offset}$ so that the defective product — not the previous or next one — arrives at the reject station.

Step 7.2 — Implement a **shift register / FIFO buffer** of length $n$:

$$n = \left\lceil \frac{t_{offset}}{T_{scan}} \right\rceil$$

**Conversion logic:** $n$ is the smallest integer ≥ the ratio, so that the reject pulse is delayed by exactly the required offset time.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Bottle Fill-Level Inspection

**Given:**
- Bottle fill volume must be between $V_{min} = 490$ mL and $V_{max} = 510$ mL.
- Conveyor speed $v_{belt} = 1.0$ m/s.
- Inspection zone length $L_{insp} = 0.2$ m.
- Vision camera FOV = 200 mm, pixel array = 1024 px.
- Distance from vision station to reject pusher = 0.5 m.

**Step A — Compute the per-unit cycle time:**

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2 \;\text{s}$$

**Step B — Compute the sensor response requirement:**

$$T_{sensor} \leq \frac{0.2}{5} = 0.04 \;\text{s} = 40\;\text{ms}$$

**Step C — Compute the camera resolution:**

$$R_{px} = \frac{200}{1024} = 0.195\;\text{mm/px} \approx 0.2\;\text{mm/px}$$

**Step D — Verify defect detectability** (smallest acceptable defect = 1 mm fill deviation in height ≈ 5 mm horizontal image displacement):

$$\text{Displacement in pixels} = \frac{5}{0.2} = 25\;\text{px} \quad \checkmark \;\text{(well above 2-pixel threshold)}$$

**Step E — Compute the reject delay:**

$$t_{offset} = \frac{0.5}{1.0} = 0.5\;\text{s}$$

**Step F — Compute the FIFO buffer length (assuming $T_{scan} = 10$ ms):**

$$n = \left\lceil \frac{0.5}{0.01} \right\rceil = \lceil 50 \rceil = 50\;\text{steps}$$

**Interpretation:** The PLC must maintain a 50-step shift register so that the reject signal, triggered when the vision camera detects an under/over-filled bottle, is delayed by exactly 0.5 s — the time it takes the bottle to travel from camera to pusher.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a generic inspection automation system, showing the precise interactions between the sensor layer, control layer, and supervisory layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PRODUCT ON CONVEYOR] --> B[SENSOR LAYER]
    B --> B1[PROXIMITY SENSOR]
    B --> B2[VISION CAMERA]
    B --> B3[LOAD CELL]
    B --> B4[COLOR SENSOR]
    B1 --> C[PLC INPUT MODULE]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[CPU EXECUTE SCAN CYCLE]
    D --> E[PLC OUTPUT MODULE]
    E --> F1[ACCEPT DIVERTER]
    E --> F2[REJECT PUSHER]
    E --> F3[REJECT BIN]
    E --> F4[STACK LIGHT]
    D --> G[SCADA SERVER]
    G --> H1[HMI OVERVIEW SCREEN]
    G --> H2[TREND AND HISTORY]
    G --> H3[ALARM MANAGEMENT]
    G --> H4[RECIPE DATABASE]
    H1 --> I[OPERATOR]
    I --> J[CONTROL COMMANDS]
    J --> D
```

> **Figure Interpretation:**
> - **A → B → C → D → E → F1/F2/F3/F4** is the **real-time closed control loop** (millisecond timescale).
> - **D → G → H1/H2/H3/H4 → I → J → D** is the **supervisory loop** (second-to-minute timescale).
> - The two loops operate at different speeds, which is the fundamental reason PLCs and SCADAs are *separate* subsystems.

---

### 4.2 Detailed Inspection Decision Flow

The diagram below shows the **internal logic flow** executed by the PLC for each product that enters the inspection zone.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[START OF CYCLE] --> CK1{Is Product In Zone?}
    CK1 -- NO --> ST
    CK1 -- YES --> TRG[Trigger All Sensors]
    TRG --> CAP[Capture Vision Image]
    TRG --> W1[Read Load Cell Value]
    TRG --> W2[Read Color Sensor Output]
    CAP --> ANA[Run Image Processing]
    ANA --> ANA1[Edge Detection]
    ANA --> ANA2[Blob Analysis]
    ANA1 --> DEC
    ANA2 --> DEC
    W1 --> DEC
    W2 --> DEC
    DEC{Within Tolerance?}
    DEC -- YES --> ACC[Activate Accept Indicator]
    DEC -- NO --> FLG[Set Reject Flag In Shift Register]
    FLG --> DLY[Wait For t offset]
    DLY --> RJ[Fire Reject Pusher]
    ACC --> LOG[Log Result To SCADA]
    RJ --> LOG
    LOG --> ST
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** Every product entering the zone triggers a *parallel* capture of all sensor values. The PLC's decision logic combines these values using AND/OR gates (implemented as ladder rungs) to declare Accept or Reject. The shift register delays the Reject pulse by the precomputed $t_{offset}$.

---

### 4.3 SCADA Screen Hierarchy (Logical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[LEVEL 0 LOGIN AND SECURITY] --> L1[LEVEL 1 PLANT OVERVIEW MIMIC]
    L1 --> L2A[LEVEL 2A STATION DETAIL]
    L1 --> L2B[LEVEL 2B ALARM SUMMARY]
    L1 --> L2C[LEVEL 2C TRENDS]
    L1 --> L2D[LEVEL 2D RECIPE MANAGER]
    L2A --> L3A[LEVEL 3A LIVE SENSOR VALUES]
    L2A --> L3B[LEVEL 3B ACTUATOR STATUS]
    L2A --> L3C[LEVEL 3C MANUAL OVERRIDE]
    L2C --> L3D[LEVEL 3D HISTORICAL DATA EXPORT]
    L2D --> L3E[LEVEL 3E PRODUCT DATABASE]
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** SCADA follows a **3-level hierarchy** of Operator screens. Level 1 is for situational awareness, Level 2 is for diagnosis, Level 3 is for engineering access. This pyramid structure prevents operator overload.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> - Students frequently **confuse PLC scan time with conveyor dwell time**. Always state the *units* explicitly: scan time in **ms**, dwell time in **s**.
> - When asked to design an inspection system, students often *describe components* instead of *justifying selections* using formulas. Marks are awarded for **justification with numerical evidence**.
> - In FIFO/shift register problems, forgetting to **round UP** the buffer size loses 2 marks. Always use the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$.
> - SCADA questions demand **tag table + screen design**, not just the word "SCADA".

---

### **Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**List the three functional layers of an inspection automation system and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three functional layers are:
1. **Sensor / Field Layer** — Acquires physical parameters (dimension, presence, color, weight) and converts them to electrical signals (24 V DC discrete or 4–20 mA analog). Example: photoelectric sensor, vision camera.
2. **Control Layer (PLC)** — Executes deterministic logic in a cyclic scan to make Accept/Reject decisions within the dwell time. Example: Siemens S7-1200.
3. **Supervisory Layer (SCADA)** — Provides centralized HMI visualization, trending, alarm management, and historical data archiving. Example: WinCC, Ignition.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define OEE and state the industry benchmark value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is a composite metric that measures the *true productive effectiveness* of a manufacturing system. It is the product of three ratios:

$$OEE = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

- *Availability* = Uptime / Planned Production Time
- *Performance* = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Run Time
- *Quality* = Good Pieces / Total Pieces

**World-class OEE benchmark = 85%**. Typical Indian manufacturing plants operate at **60–70%**.

---

### **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)**

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **ONE** of the two alternatives below.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Design an inspection automation system for an automatic **bottle capping station** in a beverage plant. The system must verify **(i)** cap presence, **(ii)** cap color (must be red), and **(iii)** cap tightness (torque between 12 Nm and 18 Nm). Production rate is 3600 bottles/hour. Conveyor speed is 0.5 m/s and the inspection zone is 0.15 m long.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute timing parameters:**

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{3600}{3600} = 1.0\;\text{s/unit}$$

$$T_{dwell} = \frac{0.15}{0.5} = 0.3\;\text{s}$$

$$T_{sensor} \leq \frac{0.3}{5} = 0.06\;\text{s} = 60\;\text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Sensor Selection (3 marks for justification):**

| Parameter | Sensor Selected | Justification |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| Cap presence | Inductive proximity sensor | Detects metallic cap on bottle mouth |
| Cap color | RGB color sensor (e.g., SICK KT5) | Output: 3-channel digital or analog |
| Cap tightness | Rotary torque transducer (0–25 Nm) | Provides 4–20 mA proportional to torque |

**Step 3 — PLC I/O Allocation (2 marks):**

| Address | Signal | Type |
|---------|--------|------|
| I:0/0 | Start PB | DI |
| I:0/1 | Cap presence sensor | DI |
| I:0/2 | Color sensor (Red OK) | DI |
| IW:1 | Torque transducer (4–20 mA) | AI |
| O:0/0 | Accept diverter solenoid | DO |
| O:0/1 | Reject pusher | DO |
| O:0/2 | Stack light (Red) | DO |
| O:0/3 | Stack light (Green) | DO |

**Step 4 — Ladder Logic Outcome (2 marks):**
The PLC computes: `IF (Cap_Present == ON) AND (Color_Red == ON) AND (12 ≤ Torque ≤ 18) THEN Accept ELSE Reject`.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
With reference to the system designed in part (a), explain the **SCADA tag database** you would build, list any **four alarm conditions** with their priority levels, and sketch the **operator overview screen** layout in words.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — SCADA Tag Table (3 marks):**

| Tag Name | Data Type | PLC Address | Scan Class | Engineering Units |
|----------|-----------|-------------|------------|--------------------|
| Conv_Running | Boolean | I:0/0 | Fast (100 ms) | — |
| Cap_Present | Boolean | I:0/1 | Fast (100 ms) | — |
| Color_Red_OK | Boolean | I:0/2 | Fast (100 ms) | — |
| Torque_Actual | Integer | IW:1 | Fast (100 ms) | Nm |
| Reject_Counter | Integer | MW10 | Slow (1 s) | count |
| Accept_Counter | Integer | MW12 | Slow (1 s) | count |
| OEE_Quality | Real | MD20 | Slow (1 s) | % |

**Step 2 — Alarm Conditions (2 marks):**

| Alarm Tag | Trigger Condition | Priority | Class |
|-----------|--------------------|----------|--------|
| Torque_High | Torque > 18 Nm | High (1) | Process |
| Torque_Low | Torque < 12 Nm | High (1) | Process |
| Sensor_Fault | Cap_Present = 0 for > 5 s | Medium (2) | Diagnostic |
| PLC_Battery | Battery low flag from CPU | Critical (0) | System |

**Step 3 — Operator Overview Screen Layout (2 marks):**
- **Top banner**: Plant name, current shift, time/date, logged-in operator.
- **Center mimic**: Conveyor line drawn horizontally; capping station shown as a rectangle with three colored LED icons (Cap, Color, Torque) — green if OK, red if NG.
- **Right panel**: Counters (Accept/Reject), OEE %, current production rate.
- **Bottom banner**: Active alarm ticker; Acknowledge button.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
A vision-based inspection system is required to detect a **minimum defect size of 0.5 mm** on a 100 mm × 100 mm PCB moving at 200 mm/s under a camera. The camera FOV is 120 mm and the camera has a 1024 × 768 pixel sensor. Compute:
(i) The camera resolution in mm/px.
(ii) The exposure time required.
(iii) Whether the chosen camera meets the **Nyquist criterion** for defect detection.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Camera resolution (3 marks):**

$$R_{px} = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}} = \frac{120}{1024} = 0.117\;\text{mm/px}$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist check (2 marks):**
Per Nyquist, the defect must be covered by at least 2 pixels:

$$\text{Pixels covering 0.5 mm defect} = \frac{0.5}{0.117} = 4.27\;\text{px} \;>\; 2\;\text{px}\quad \checkmark$$

The criterion is satisfied; defect is detectable.

**Step 3 — Exposure / dwell time (2 marks):**
If the PCB is illuminated under the camera for the full FOV traversal:

$$T_{dwell} = \frac{120}{200} = 0.6\;\text{s}$$

A standard industrial camera with a global shutter of **1 µs exposure** easily freezes the motion; hence exposure time is **not the limiting factor** here.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
For a PLC controlling a reject mechanism located **0.75 m downstream** of a vision station, with conveyor speed 1.5 m/s and PLC scan time of 5 ms, calculate:
(i) The **time offset** between detection and rejection.
(ii) The **shift register length** required.
(iii) Explain what happens if the shift register length is *under-sized* by 5 steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time offset (2 marks):**

$$t_{offset} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{0.75}{1.5} = 0.5\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Shift register length (3 marks):**

$$n = \left\lceil \frac{t_{offset}}{T_{scan}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{0.5}{0.005} \right\rceil = \lceil 100 \rceil = 100\;\text{steps}$$

**Step 3 — Consequence of under-sizing (2 marks):**
If the register holds only 95 steps, the actual delay becomes:

$$t_{actual} = 95 \times 0.005 = 0.475\;\text{s}$$

This corresponds to a *shorter* physical distance:

$$d_{actual} = 0.475 \times 1.5 = 0.7125\;\text{m}$$

The reject pusher would fire **0.0375 m too early** — i.e., the *next* bottle in line would be pushed off, not the defective one. This is a **critical quality failure** that would pass good bottles into the reject bin and allow defective ones to remain in the accepted stream.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024**

- ☐ **Three layers**: Sensor → PLC → SCADA. Remember the timescale separation (ms vs. s).
- ☐ **Sensor rule**: $T_{sensor} \leq T_{dwell} / 5$ (always justify numerically).
- ☐ **Camera Nyquist**: minimum 2 pixels must cover the smallest defect.
- ☐ **PLC scan time formula**: $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$.
- ☐ **OEE = A × P × Q**; world-class benchmark = **85%**.
- ☐ **Shift register size**: $n = \lceil t_{offset} / T_{scan} \rceil$ — *always ceiling, never floor*.
- ☐ **I/O sizing**: include 20% spare for future expansion.
- ☐ **SCADA hierarchy**: 3 levels (Overview → Detail → Engineering).
- ☐ **Tag polling**: keep total SCADA bandwidth under 70% of link capacity.
- ☐ **Reject timing**: distance/speed → offset time → register steps.
- ☐ **Industrial protocols**: Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA.
- ☐ **Current loop**: 4 mA = LRV, 20 mA = URV, linear in between.
- ☐ **Conveyor dwell time**: $L_{insp} / v_{belt}$ — *this is the master timing budget*.
- ☐ **Design sequence**: Spec → Timing → Sensor → I/O → Logic → SCADA → Reject.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Simple Water Management System Using PLC, SCADA and Electrical Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT722 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Simple Water Management System Using PLC, SCADA and Electrical Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Water Management System (WMS)** in the PLC/SCADA context is an integrated automation architecture that monitors, controls, and optimizes the storage, transfer, and distribution of water using a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** for real-time sequential and continuous control, a **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** station for centralized human-machine interface (HMI), and **electrical drives** (typically Variable Frequency Drives / VFD coupled to AC induction motors driving centrifugal pumps) for energy-efficient flow regulation.

The system essentially forms a **closed-loop industrial automation pyramid** (per the ISA-95/IEC 62264 hierarchical model), spanning from field instrumentation (Level 0) through control (Level 1) to supervisory operations (Level 2).

### 1.2 System Components — Taxonomy

| Layer | Device | Function |
|-------|--------|----------|
| Field | Float switches, Ultrasonic/Radar level sensors, Pressure transmitters, Flow meters (magnetic / vortex), Limit switches on valves | Primary measurement & actuation feedback |
| Control | PLC (Allen-Bradley MicroLogix, Siemens S7-1200, Schneider M221) | Sequential & PID logic execution |
| Drive | VFD (e.g., ABB ACS150, Danfoss VLT) controlling 3-φ induction motor | Variable-speed pump control |
| Supervisory | SCADA (Wonderware InTouch, WinCC, iFIX, Ignition) | Visualization, trending, alarms, historian |
| Communication | Industrial protocols: Modbus RTU/TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP | Data exchange between layers |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Smart Building Water Tank"**
> Imagine a multi-storey building's overhead water tank with three pumps, two sump tanks, and a maintenance crew. Without automation, a watchman must climb up every hour, check the level with a stick, manually start/stop pumps, and write down readings in a register. Now replace the watchman with a **PLC** (the brain that decides when to start/stop), give him **sensors** (eyes and ears), a **VFD-controlled motor** (his muscles, but with adjustable strength instead of fixed on/off), and a **SCADA dashboard** (a giant wall-mounted TV showing him real-time levels, alarms, and graphs). The result: faster response, no human error, reduced power consumption (because the pump runs at **50 % speed when demand is low**), and an automatic SMS to the engineer when something fails.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

Water management is the **canonical industry-relevant case study** for KTU Module 4 because it integrates every concept taught previously: PLC I/O configuration, ladder logic, analog signal handling, PID, SCADA tag mapping, drive parameterization, and fail-safe design. Expect at least one **14-mark Part B question** on a similar integrated design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Architecture — Hierarchical View

The architecture follows the classical **three-tier industrial automation model**:

**Tier 1 — Field Level:**
- Sump tank with **level transmitter (LT)** — typically 4-20 mA output proportional to 0-100 % level
- Overhead tank (OHT) with another LT
- Pressure transmitter (PT) at pump discharge line
- Flow transmitter (FT) — magnetic flow meter
- Motor starters / contactors
- Solenoid-operated control valves

**Tier 2 — Control Level:**
- PLC rack with:
  - CPU module
  - Digital Input (DI) module: 16-ch, 24 VDC
  - Digital Output (DO) module: 16-ch, relay / transistor
  - Analog Input (AI) module: 4-ch, 4-20 mA / 0-10 V
  - Analog Output (AO) module: 2-ch, 4-20 mA (for VFD speed reference)
- VFD with **speed reference input (AI)** and **run/stop command (DI)**

**Tier 3 — Supervisory Level:**
- SCADA PC with Ethernet/IP or Modbus TCP link to PLC
- Operator screen, alarm log, trend window, recipe database

### 2.2 Operational Modes

A typical WMS has three operational modes (hand-off-auto or HOA):

- **Manual Mode:** Operator starts pump from SCADA; PLC interlocks still active.
- **Auto Mode (Level-based):** PLC starts pump when OHT level falls below **LLL (Low-Low Level, e.g., 20 %)** and stops when level reaches **HH (High-High, e.g., 90 %)**.
- **Auto Mode (PID/Pressure-based):** VFD speed modulated to maintain constant discharge pressure (closed-loop).

### 2.3 Pump Affinity Laws & Drive Selection (KTU High-Yield)

When the system uses a VFD to vary pump speed, the following **affinity laws** govern hydraulic performance:

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

$$
H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/h)
- $H$ = head developed by pump (m)
- $P$ = shaft power (W or kW)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Reducing pump speed to 80 % of rated reduces power consumption to roughly **51.2 %** — this is the primary energy-saving justification for using a VFD instead of a throttling valve.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|-------|
