| Analog signal scaling | $E_{eng} = \left(\frac{E_{raw} - E_{min}}{E_{max} - E_{min}}\right) \times (S_{max} - S_{min}) + S_{min}$ | Engineering units | $E_{min}=4$ mA, $E_{max}=20$ mA for current loop |
| Current to count | $I = 4 + \left(\frac{16}{2^{n}-1}\right) \times N_{ADC}$ | mA | $n$ = ADC resolution (typically 12-bit = 4095) |
| Pump Affinity — Flow | $Q \propto N$ | m³/h | Linear with speed |
| Pump Affinity — Head | $H \propto N^{2}$ | m | Quadratic with speed |
| Pump Affinity — Power | $P \propto N^{3}$ | kW | Cubic — dominates savings |
| Motor synchronous speed | $N_{s} = \frac{120 \cdot f}{p}$ | rpm | $f$ = supply freq, $p$ = number of poles |
| Motor slip | $s = \frac{N_{s} - N_{r}}{N_{s}} \times 100$ | % | $N_{r}$ = rotor speed |
| VFD output frequency | $f_{out} = f_{rated} \times \frac{N_{desired}}{N_{rated}}$ | Hz | 0–50 Hz typically |
| Tank volume (cylindrical) | $V = \pi r^{2} h$ | m³ | For vertical storage |
| Tank filling time | $t = \frac{V}{Q_{net}}$ | hours | $Q_{net}$ = inflow - outflow |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing formulas in your KTU answer script, **always specify units** next to numerical values. Marks are specifically deducted for unit-less answers under the "Engineering Knowledge" CO criterion.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water supply** (Corporation pumping stations): reduces energy bills by **20–40 %**.
- **Industrial process plants**: ensures uninterrupted boiler feedwater.
- **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)**: coordinates multiple pumps and aerators.
- **High-rise buildings**: rooftop tank level control with two-pump alternation.
- **Irrigation systems**: precision water delivery based on soil moisture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Analog Level Signal Scaling

**Given:** A radar level transmitter outputs **4-20 mA**, with 4 mA = 0 % (empty) and 20 mA = 100 % (full). The PLC's analog input module has a **12-bit resolution** (range 0–4095 counts).

**Find:** The engineering level (%) corresponding to an ADC reading of **2458 counts**.

**Step 1 — Convert ADC counts to current:**

$$
I_{mA} = 4 + \left(\frac{20 - 4}{4095 - 0}\right) \times N_{ADC}
$$

Substituting $N_{ADC} = 2458$:

$$
I_{mA} = 4 + \left(\frac{16}{4095}\right) \times 2458
$$

$$
I_{mA} = 4 + 0.003907 \times 2458
$$

$$
I_{mA} = 4 + 9.604
$$

$$
I_{mA} = 13.604 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert current to engineering units (linear interpolation):**

$$
L_{\%} = \left(\frac{I_{mA} - 4}{20 - 4}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
L_{\%} = \left(\frac{13.604 - 4}{16}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
L_{\%} = \left(\frac{9.604}{16}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
L_{\%} = 0.60025 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\%} \approx 60.03 \text{ \%}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to absolute height (if tank height H = 5 m):**

$$
h_{m} = \frac{L_{\%}}{100} \times H = \frac{60.03}{100} \times 5 = 3.00 \text{ m}
$$

**Interpretation:** The overhead tank is 60 % full, equivalent to 3.00 m of water column.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: VFD Frequency for Desired Pump Speed

**Given:** A 4-pole, 3-phase induction motor is rated at **N_rated = 1440 rpm** at **50 Hz**. The PLC PID controller commands **70 % speed** to maintain constant pressure.

**Find:** The required VFD output frequency $f_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Compute rated synchronous speed:**

$$
N_{s,rated} = \frac{120 \cdot f_{rated}}{p} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute rated slip:**

$$
s_{rated} = \frac{N_{s,rated} - N_{r,rated}}{N_{s,rated}} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = 0.04 = 4 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute desired rotor speed (assuming slip scales proportionally with frequency, common V/F assumption):**

$$
N_{r,desired} = 0.70 \times 1440 = 1008 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute desired synchronous speed:**

$$
N_{s,desired} = \frac{N_{r,desired}}{1 - s_{rated}} = \frac{1008}{1 - 0.04} = \frac{1008}{0.96} = 1050 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute VFD output frequency:**

$$
f_{out} = \frac{N_{s,desired} \cdot p}{120} = \frac{1050 \times 4}{120}
$$

$$
f_{out} = \frac{4200}{120}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{out} = 35 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The VFD must output 35 Hz to drive the motor at 1008 rpm (70 % of rated speed). The PLC's AO module sends the corresponding 4-20 mA reference: $I_{ref} = 4 + 0.7 \times 16 = 15.2$ mA.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Saving Calculation Using Affinity Laws

**Given:** A centrifugal pump operating at rated condition delivers $Q_{rated} = 100$ m³/h at $H_{rated} = 30$ m and consumes $P_{rated} = 15$ kW. The system is throttled down to 80 % flow using a control valve (method 1) versus speed reduction via VFD (method 2). Cost of electricity = ₹8 per kWh; operating hours = 4000 h/year.

**Step 1 — Throttled case (no speed change):**
Pump still rotates at rated speed, so:

$$
Q_{2} = 0.8 \times 100 = 80 \text{ m³/h}
$$

$$
P_{2} = P_{rated} = 15 \text{ kW (approximately, valve causes minor loss)}
$$

Energy per year:

$$
E_{throttle} = 15 \times 4000 = 60000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — VFD case (speed reduced):**

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \implies \frac{80}{100} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

$$
N_2 = 0.8 \times N_1
$$

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = 15 \times (0.8)^{3}
$$

$$
P_2 = 15 \times 0.512 = 7.68 \text{ kW}
$$

Energy per year:

$$
E_{VFD} = 7.68 \times 4000 = 30720 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual savings:**

$$
\Delta E = 60000 - 30720 = 29280 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\text{Money saved} = 29280 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 234240 \text{ per year}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Annual saving} \approx \text{₹ } 2.34 \text{ lakh}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even though both methods achieve the same flow (80 m³/h), the **VFD consumes nearly half the energy** because the cubic relationship between power and speed dominates. This is the foundational KTU 2024 answer for "justify the use of VFD in water management systems."

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — PLC-Based Pump Selection Logic

| OHT Level (%) | Pump A | Pump B | VFD Speed (%) | Comments |
|---------------|--------|--------|---------------|----------|
| 0–20 | OFF | OFF | 0 | Dry-run protection active |
| 20–50 | ON | OFF | 100 | Single pump, full speed |
| 50–75 | ON | OFF | 70 | VFD reduces speed, saves power |
| 75–90 | ON | ON | 100 | Both pumps on, peak demand |
| > 90 | OFF | OFF | 0 | Overflow alarm triggered |

**Lead/Lag Alternation Logic:** After every cycle, the role of Pump A and Pump B is swapped using a flip-flop (e.g., a TON/TOF timer or a single-bit toggle). This ensures **equal wear distribution**, doubling the effective life of pump seals and bearings.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three advantages of using a PLC-SCADA based water management system over a conventional relay-based control panel.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Flexibility & Re-programmability:** Control logic can be modified by simply editing the ladder program, no rewiring required (1 mark).
2. **Remote Monitoring & Data Logging:** SCADA enables centralized visualization, real-time trending, and historical data archiving across multiple sites (1 mark).
3. **Energy Efficiency through VFD Integration:** PLC analog outputs drive VFDs to modulate pump speed using affinity laws, reducing power consumption by up to 50 % versus throttling (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the pump affinity laws and explain their significance in VFD-based pump control.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pump affinity laws state (2 marks):

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** Because power consumption varies with the cube of speed, reducing pump speed to 80 % cuts energy consumption to 51.2 %, providing substantial operational cost savings in continuous-duty water pumping applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a PLC-SCADA based water management system for a multi-storey building having two sump tanks and one overhead tank. Explain the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the ladder logic program (explain rung-by-rung) for automatic alternation of two pumps (Pump A and Pump B) based on overhead tank level, with the following conditions:
- Pump ON when level ≤ 25 %, OFF when level ≥ 90 %
- Pumps alternate every cycle
- Dry-run protection via sump low-level float
- Fault lockout with manual reset **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Description:**

The system follows a **three-tier hierarchical architecture** (1 mark):

**Tier 1 — Field Instrumentation Layer:**
- **Two sump tanks** with float switches (LS-1, LS-2) for low-level detection and ultrasonic level transmitters (LT-1, LT-2) for continuous level measurement (4–20 mA).
- **One overhead tank (OHT)** with radar level transmitter (LT-3) providing 4–20 mA proportional to 0–5 m water column.
- **Pressure transmitter (PT)** at pump discharge manifold.
- **Flow meter (FT)** on common delivery line.
- **Motor contactors** with thermal overload relays as final switching elements.

**Tier 2 — Control Layer (PLC):**
- **CPU module** (e.g., Siemens S7-1214C) with integral DI/DO and AI/AO capability (1 mark).
- **DI module:** receives pushbutton commands, selector switch (Hand/Auto), float switches, motor contactor auxiliary contacts.
- **DO module:** drives motor contactors, alarm horn, indicator lamps.
- **AI module:** 4–20 mA inputs from LT-1, LT-2, LT-3, PT, FT.
- **AO module:** 4–20 mA output to VFD as speed reference (0–100 %).
- The PLC executes scan-cycle based ladder logic at ~10 ms cycle time, enabling deterministic response.

**Tier 3 — Supervisory Layer (SCADA):**
- Industrial PC running **WinCC/Ignition** software, connected to PLC via **Ethernet/IP** or **Modbus TCP** (1 mark).
- Provides animated mimic diagram of sump and OHT, real-time trends, alarm summary, historical reports.
- Operator can switch modes, acknowledge alarms, and override setpoints from SCADA.

**Electrical Drive Layer:**
- **VFD** (e.g., ABB ACS150 or Danfoss VLT Micro) controlling 5 HP, 3-phase, 4-pole induction motor coupled to centrifugal pump.
- PLC sends analog speed reference; VFD ramps motor up/down according to programmed acceleration/deceleration times to prevent water hammer (1 mark).

**Communication Links:**
- RS-485 Modbus RTU between VFD and PLC (1 mark).
- Ethernet TCP/IP between PLC and SCADA PC.
- Optional: GSM modem for SMS alerts to maintenance engineer.

(1 mark for concluding sentence on integrated operation: PLC senses → decides → drives → reports to SCADA.)

---

### 5.4 Model Solution — Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Ladder Logic Rung-by-Rung Design:**

Assume internal memory bits: `M0.0` = Auto Mode, `M0.1` = Pump A Selected, `M0.2` = Pump B Selected, `M1.0` = Lead Pump Flag (toggles each cycle), `M2.0` = Dry-Run Lockout, `M3.0` = Fault Latch.

**Rung 1 — Auto Mode Master Enable (1 mark):**

```
|  AUTO_MODE  |  NOT(DRY_RUN_LOCK)  |  NOT(FAULT_LATCH)  |   ( M_RUN_EN  )
   M0.0             M2.0                   M3.0
```

**Rung 2 — Level Comparison using Internal Tags (1 mark):**
Pre-computed boolean tags from analog scaling: `LT3_LL` (level ≤ 25 %), `LT3_HH` (level ≥ 90 %).

```
|  M_RUN_EN  |  LT3_LL  |   ( START_PUMP_REQ )
   M0.0          (boolean derived from AI)
```

**Rung 3 — Stop Condition (1 mark):**

```
|  LT3_HH  |  NOT(MANUAL_STOP)  |   ( STOP_PUMP_REQ )
              I0.2 (PB_STOP)
```

**Rung 4 — Alternation Logic using Flip-Flop (2 marks):**

```
|  M_RUN_EN  |  LT3_LL  |  NOT(M1.0)  |   ( M0.1  )   --- Pump A latched
   M0.0                                    Pump A selected

|  M_RUN_EN  |  LT3_LL  |  M1.0       |   ( M0.2  )   --- Pump B latched
   M0.0                                    Pump B selected
```

After each complete fill cycle (level reaches LT3_HH), a one-shot pulse toggles `M1.0`:

```
|  LT3_HH_RISE_EDGE  |   ( M1.0  )   --- toggle bit using XOR or alternate instruction
```

**Rung 5 — Dry-Run Protection (1 mark):**

```
|  FSW_DRY  |   ( M2.0  )   --- latched dry-run lockout
   I0.0
|  M2.0  |   ( ALARM_HORN )  --- audible + SCADA alarm
```

**Rung 6 — Fault Latch with Manual Reset (1 mark):**

```
|  MTR1_FAULT  |  MTR2_FAULT  |   ( M3.0  )   --- OR of motor fault contacts
   I0.4              I0.5
|  PB_RESET  |  NOT(M3.0)  |   ( M3.0_RST )  --- manual reset
   I0.6
```

**Rung 7 — Final Motor Output to VFD (1 mark):**

```
|  M0.1  |  NOT(M2.0)  |  NOT(M3.0)  |   ( MTR1_RUN  )
|  M0.2  |  NOT(M2.0)  |  NOT(M3.0)  |   ( MTR2_RUN  )
```

The analog AO `VFD_REF` is set to 100 % (20 mA) when any pump is running, else 0 %.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with diagrams the principle of operation of a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD). How is it interfaced with a PLC to control a pump in a water management system? Show the signal flow and I/O wiring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump rated at 30 m³/h at 30 m head draws 7.5 kW at 1450 rpm. Using affinity laws, calculate:
   (i) The new flow and head when speed is reduced to 1160 rpm.
   (ii) The new power consumption.
   (iii) The annual energy saving (in kWh and ₹) if the pump operates 12 hours/day for 300 days/year at electricity cost of ₹7.50/kWh, compared to a throttle valve delivering the same reduced flow. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.5 Model Solution — Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**VFD Principle (4 marks):**
A VFD converts fixed-frequency, fixed-voltage AC mains into variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC output to control motor speed. It has three sections:
1. **Rectifier:** Converts 3-phase 50 Hz AC to DC using a 6-pulse diode bridge; output is uncontrolled DC bus (~540 VDC nominal for 415 V input).
2. **DC Bus / Filter:** Large electrolytic capacitors smooth DC; optional braking resistor for regenerative energy dissipation.
3. **Inverter:** Uses **IGBT switches** with **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** to synthesize a quasi-sinusoidal AC output at desired frequency (0–50/60 Hz) and voltage (V/Hz ratio maintained constant up to 50 Hz, then field weakening above).

VFD block diagram reference:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mains["3 PHASE AC MAINS 415 V 50 Hz"] --> Rect["DIODE RECTIFIER 6 PULSE"]
    Rect --> DCbus["DC BUS CAPACITOR"]
    DCbus --> Inv["IGBT INVERTER SPWM"]
    Inv --> Motor["3 PHASE INDUCTION MOTOR"]
    DCbus --> Brk["BRAKING RESISTOR"]
```

**PLC Interfacing (3 marks):**
- **Digital Inputs to VFD:** Start/Stop, Forward/Reverse, Multi-speed selection (preset frequencies).
- **Digital Output from VFD:** Run signal, Fault relay, At-frequency.
- **Analog Input to VFD:** 4–20 mA or 0–10 V speed reference (from PLC AO module).
- **Analog Output from VFD:** Motor current, frequency feedback (to PLC AI module for closed-loop PID).
- **Communication:** RS-485 Modbus for parameter reading/writing (acceleration time, deceleration time, motor nameplate data).

**Signal Flow Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PLCai["PLC AI LT3 OHT LEVEL"] --> PID["PLC PID FUNCTION BLOCK"]
    PID --> PLCao["PLC AO VFD REFERENCE 4 to 20 mA"]
    PLCao --> VFDin["VFD ANALOG INPUT AI1"]
    VFDin --> VFDinv["VFD INVERTER SECTION"]
    VFDinv --> Motor["INDUCTION MOTOR"]
    Motor --> Pump["PUMP"]
    Pump --> PT["PT DISCHARGE PRESSURE"]
    PT --> PLCai2["PLC AI PT FEEDBACK"]
    PLCai2 --> PID
```

---

### 5.6 Model Solution — Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $Q_1 = 30$ m³/h, $H_1 = 30$ m, $P_1 = 7.5$ kW, $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $N_2 = 1160$ rpm.

**(i) New flow and head (3 marks):**

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 30 \times \frac{1160}{1450}
$$

$$
Q_2 = 30 \times 0.80 = 24 \text{ m³/h}
$$

$$
H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} = 30 \times (0.80)^{2}
$$

$$
H_2 = 30 \times 0.64 = 19.2 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) New power consumption (2 marks):**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = 7.5 \times (0.80)^{3}
$$

$$
P_2 = 7.5 \times 0.512 = 3.84 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Annual saving (2 marks):**

Total operating hours: $12 \times 300 = 3600$ h/year.

VFD energy:

$$
E_{VFD} = 3.84 \times 3600 = 13824 \text{ kWh}
$$

Throttle valve energy (pump still at 1450 rpm, minor loss ignored):

$$
E_{throttle} = 7.5 \times 3600 = 27000 \text{ kWh}
$$

Energy saved:

$$
\Delta E = 27000 - 13824 = 13176 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

Cost saved:

$$
\text{Saving} = 13176 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 98820
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Annual saving} \approx \text{₹ } 98{,}820}
$$

---

### 5.7 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Water Management Design Questions:**
> 1. **Missing I/O List:** Students often draw the block diagram but forget to tabulate digital and analog I/O with addresses. Examiners allocate **2 marks exclusively for the I/O allocation table** — do not omit it.
> 2. **No Safety Interlocks:** Listing only start/stop logic without dry-run protection, overload trip, and fault latch loses up to **2 marks** under the "Safety & Environment" CO.
> 3. **Wrong Affinity Law Application:** Some students apply the cubic relation to flow instead of power. Memorize: **Flow ∝ N, Head ∝ N², Power ∝ N³.**
> 4. **Unit Omission:** Final answers without units (m³/h, m, kW, ₹) will be penalized **0.5 mark per error**.
> 5. **No SCADA Tag Mapping:** When asked for SCADA design, students often describe the screen but forget the **tag database** (e.g., LT_001 → DB10.DBD0 with engineering range 0–100).
> 6. **Ignoring Communication Protocol:** Simply saying "PLC and SCADA are connected" is incomplete. Always specify **Ethernet/IP, Modbus TCP, or Profinet** for **1 mark**.

---

### 5.8 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **System Tiers:** Field (sensors/actuators) → Control (PLC + VFD) → Supervisory (SCADA). Always diagram this hierarchy first.
- **Pump Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^{2}$, $P \propto N^{3}$. Cubic power relation is the **energy-saving justification**.
- **Motor Speed Formula:** $N_s = 120f/p$. For 50 Hz, 4-pole: $N_s = 1500$ rpm; typical slip 2–5 %.
- **Analog Scaling:** 4–20 mA → 0–100 %. Use $E = ((I - 4) / 16) \times (S_{max} - S_{min}) + S_{min}$.
- **VFD Reference Signal:** PLC AO 4–20 mA → VFD AI terminal; 20 mA = 100 % speed = rated frequency.
- **Alternation Logic:** Use a toggle flip-flop after each fill cycle to alternate lead pump; equalizes wear.
- **Dry-Run Protection:** Mandatory sump low-level float switch interlock; latch until manual reset.
- **VFD V/Hz Ratio:** Maintain constant V/f below base frequency for constant torque operation.
- **SCADA Tags:** Each PLC variable must have a unique tag name, address, type (BOOL/INT/REAL), and engineering range.
- **Safety:** E-Stop (hardwired, Category 1 per ISO 13849) overrides all software logic; include in ladder.
- **Communication:** Modbus TCP for PLC–SCADA, Modbus RTU (RS-485) for VFD–PLC is the most common KTU-expected answer.
- **Cost Justification:** Always quantify energy savings using affinity laws + annual operating hours + tariff.
- **Drawing Conventions:** Use standard symbols (IEC 60617) for level switches, motors, contactors; label all signals.
- **VFD Wiring Order:** Contactor → VFD Input → VFD Output → Motor. Never put contactor between VFD and motor (damages IGBTs).
